This is the phase where we collect user problems

01:03.990 --> 01:06.120
We brainstorm solutions.

01:06.120 --> 01:08.100
We're saying, "All right, we wanna make a product,

01:08.100 --> 01:10.380
but we want to figure out what we should make."

01:10.380 --> 01:13.380
We identify the focus areas we wanna work on.

01:13.380 --> 01:16.530
The biggest source of ideas for this phase is

01:16.530 --> 01:18.750
usually inside of a company

01:18.750 --> 01:21.510
from the employees of that company themselves.

01:21.510 --> 01:23.700
Phase number two, plan.

01:23.700 --> 01:27.690
In this phase, we do market research.

01:27.690 --> 01:29.220
So, we've already got our ideas

01:29.220 --> 01:31.440
and we do the research on those ideas.

01:31.440 --> 01:33.000
We look at the business case.

01:33.000 --> 01:35.040
Is this something that's gonna make us money?

01:35.040 --> 01:36.570
We do customer interviews,

01:36.570 --> 01:39.420
and see if people will find this to be a problem.

01:39.420 --> 01:42.900
We also roadmap out what we think we could make,

01:42.900 --> 01:45.644
how long it might take, and at what points

01:45.644 --> 01:48.570
we should have particular features

01:48.570 --> 01:50.130
if we're actually gonna do this.

01:50.130 --> 01:52.080
So, we move on to the next phase.

01:52.080 --> 01:53.460
The third phase is develop.

01:53.460 --> 01:55.380
This is where we get our hands dirty.

01:55.380 --> 01:57.900
So, we want to make timelines,

01:57.900 --> 01:59.730
and we want to write out features

01:59.730 --> 02:01.350
that we're gonna have in this product.

02:01.350 --> 02:03.990
We're gonna make our user stories and our specs,

02:03.990 --> 02:05.340
and we're gonna talk to development

02:05.340 --> 02:09.090
about estimations on very specific details

02:09.090 --> 02:12.570
of how long will this particular thing need

02:12.570 --> 02:14.640
to take to be developed.

02:14.640 --> 02:16.830
By the time we begin developing,

02:16.830 --> 02:19.110
the requirements should be set,

02:19.110 --> 02:20.340
and they really shouldn't change

02:20.340 --> 02:22.170
until we get some sort of validation

02:22.170 --> 02:24.990
on the early parts of the completed product,

02:24.990 --> 02:28.020
our MVP, which we'll talk about a little bit later.

02:28.020 --> 02:31.200
Then, we move on to the fourth phase, iterate.

02:31.200 --> 02:33.234
So, this is when we finish the MVP,

02:33.234 --> 02:35.190
our very early prototype,

02:35.190 --> 02:37.950
or the early minimum viable product

02:37.950 --> 02:40.020
of this new product we're developing.

02:40.020 --> 02:42.330
We get early feedback from users.

02:42.330 --> 02:44.250
We test the assumptions that we made

02:44.250 --> 02:47.490
with the original idea for the product.

02:47.490 --> 02:50.340
We don't wait until we are all the way done,

02:50.340 --> 02:51.630
because we want to get the testing

02:51.630 --> 02:53.730
in as early as possible from the users.

02:53.730 --> 02:57.540
So, we use tools like having an Alpha and a Beta.

02:57.540 --> 02:59.430
You've probably seen these sorts of things out there,

02:59.430 --> 03:01.980
where you can join Beta programs for software.

03:01.980 --> 03:04.560
This is because these things are not complete,

03:04.560 --> 03:05.640
and they wanna make sure they're heading

03:05.640 --> 03:08.010
in the right direction before they do a full launch.

03:08.010 --> 03:10.410
Again, those first four phases,

03:10.410 --> 03:11.550
conceive,

03:11.550 --> 03:12.420
plan,

03:12.420 --> 03:13.620
develop,

03:13.620 --> 03:14.580
iterate,

03:14.580 --> 03:15.510
and we're gonna talk about

03:15.510 --> 03:17.520
the next three major phases

03:17.520 --> 03:19.980
of the product development process

03:19.980 --> 03:21.390
in the next lecture.

03:21.390 --> 03:22.440
I will see you there.

response.description:
We’ve covered a good chunk of the product development process, but there’s always more to learn. Every product has a common journey, and decisions have to be made at every stage in that journey. How you make your product decisions will have repercussions further down the line. In other words, know this stuff inside and out! Do you want to know what Braveheart, Seinfeld, and product development have in common? You’ll have to watch this video to find out.

Covered in this lecture:

  • How to make decisions about your product that benefit the company.
  • What to do if your product doesn’t work out.
response.title: Getting Deeper Into the Product Development Process

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.150 --> 00:01.260
-: All right, so in the last lecture,

00:01.260 --> 00:03.000
we discussed the first four phases

00:03.000 --> 00:04.890
of the product development process.

00:04.890 --> 00:06.870
That company conceives of a new product,

00:06.870 --> 00:08.790
they plan it out, they develop it,

00:08.790 --> 00:11.910
and then they iterate on it before you even launch it.

00:11.910 --> 00:15.030
And you do this using the early test feedback

00:15.030 --> 00:17.310
or the alphas and the betas

00:17.310 --> 00:18.600
and there's a few other types of things

00:18.600 --> 00:20.550
we can do to get that feedback.

00:20.550 --> 00:21.990
But after that's done,

00:21.990 --> 00:23.880
we wanna do three more things.

00:23.880 --> 00:26.250
We want to launch, then we wanna hold it

00:26.250 --> 00:28.740
in a steady state, and then, after that,

00:28.740 --> 00:30.330
we decide if we wanna maintain it

00:30.330 --> 00:31.830
or we kill the product.

00:31.830 --> 00:33.699
So let's talk about launch.

00:33.699 --> 00:34.815
(explosion)

00:34.815 --> 00:36.930
Controller: Liftoff, we have a liftoff.

00:36.930 --> 00:39.300
-: We want to work with the marketing team

00:39.300 --> 00:41.730
and the legal team, the PR team,

00:41.730 --> 00:42.720
the sales team.

00:42.720 --> 00:44.430
And we wanna position this product

00:44.430 --> 00:45.900
for public launch.

00:45.900 --> 00:47.550
Then, we actually send it out there

00:47.550 --> 00:50.127
to the public, we see what kind of reaction we get.

00:50.127 --> 00:53.490
And the sixth major phase is called steady state

00:53.490 --> 00:56.610
or just basically hold it in either a steady state

00:56.610 --> 00:58.269
or continue iterating on it.

00:58.269 --> 00:59.102
(dramatic music)

00:59.102 --> 00:59.944
(yelling)

00:59.944 --> 01:00.777
-: Steady.

01:00.777 --> 01:01.610
(dramatic music)

01:01.610 --> 01:02.443
(people yelling and horse hooves clopping)

01:02.443 --> 01:03.803
Hold.

01:03.803 --> 01:05.130
(dramatic music)

01:05.130 --> 01:05.963
-: During this time,

01:05.963 --> 01:08.400
we're collecting metrics on how people are using it,

01:08.400 --> 01:10.080
how often are people buying it.

01:10.080 --> 01:13.200
We analyze the product and we optimize our metrics,

01:13.200 --> 01:16.200
we try to get the maximum return for our investment

01:16.200 --> 01:17.033
on it.

01:17.033 --> 01:18.510
During this time, we also have marketing

01:18.510 --> 01:20.040
continuing to market it

01:20.040 --> 01:22.500
and sales people continuing to sell it.

01:22.500 --> 01:25.590
We assess our continued efforts here

01:25.590 --> 01:28.770
to basically see how likely are we

01:28.770 --> 01:30.960
to continue moving forward with this.

01:30.960 --> 01:33.870
Then, we move on to the seventh and final phase

01:33.870 --> 01:37.170
of the product development process, maintain or kill.

01:37.170 --> 01:38.730
At this point, we decide,

01:38.730 --> 01:40.710
using all the data we've already gathered,

01:40.710 --> 01:43.230
and especially from the last phase

01:43.230 --> 01:45.420
how frequently are people purchasing this,

01:45.420 --> 01:47.100
are we staying on top of the market,

01:47.100 --> 01:49.290
are we competitive with this product anymore.

01:49.290 --> 01:51.120
And we look at, more importantly,

01:51.120 --> 01:54.420
how much money are we spending to maintain it,

01:54.420 --> 01:57.300
to see is it even paying for itself?

01:57.300 --> 01:59.280
How's our return on investment?

01:59.280 --> 02:00.660
If it's not doing so well,

02:00.660 --> 02:02.640
then maybe we decide to kill it

02:02.640 --> 02:06.356
and we decide to move onto something new.

02:06.356 --> 02:08.370
(beeping)

02:08.370 --> 02:10.295
-: I'm gonna disconnect your air machine.

02:10.295 --> 02:11.550
(air hissing)

02:11.550 --> 02:13.380
And you're gonna go to sleep.

02:13.380 --> 02:15.240
-: And one thing that's important to mention here

02:15.240 --> 02:18.270
is that it may not have anything to do with the revenue

02:18.270 --> 02:20.460
of that product or maybe even the usage.

02:20.460 --> 02:23.880
It could just not fit our company vision anymore.

02:23.880 --> 02:26.790
Our executives and the people that are leading the company

02:26.790 --> 02:30.090
might decide just based on market circumstance

02:30.090 --> 02:32.400
that the company is now going in a different direction

02:32.400 --> 02:35.310
so we need to go ahead and cut our losses on that product

02:35.310 --> 02:36.840
or go ahead and put it to bed.

02:36.840 --> 02:38.673
-: It's not you, it's me.

02:39.990 --> 02:43.160
-: You're giving me the "it's not you, it's me" routine?

02:43.160 --> 02:44.220
(audience laughs)

02:44.220 --> 02:47.100
I invented "it's not you, it's me." (audience laughs)

02:47.100 --> 02:48.570
-: If we decide to kill the product,

02:48.570 --> 02:50.850
then we do what's called sunsetting.

02:50.850 --> 02:52.590
You've probably heard this before.

02:52.590 --> 02:55.230
This is a slow transition to the end of life

02:55.230 --> 02:56.250
of the product.

02:56.250 --> 02:57.630
We message our user base,

02:57.630 --> 02:59.040
we tell them what's going on,

02:59.040 --> 03:01.650
and we have an end of life plan for the users

03:01.650 --> 03:03.690
and the people that may rely on it.

03:03.690 --> 03:06.090
So if we have a company or a product

03:06.090 --> 03:08.820
that is storing data for people, for instance,

03:08.820 --> 03:11.190
then we may allow them to back up their data

03:11.190 --> 03:12.870
before we shut down the product.

03:12.870 --> 03:16.620
Everyone has probably seen a product that they love to use,

03:16.620 --> 03:19.380
you know, be killed, and it's always very sad

03:19.380 --> 03:22.380
but unfortunately, sometimes there's not much

03:22.380 --> 03:23.370
you can do about it.

03:23.370 --> 03:25.800
But I have had a lot of good experiences

03:25.800 --> 03:27.270
with products that are dying

03:27.270 --> 03:30.540
and being killed out but they still allow you

03:30.540 --> 03:32.550
to bring out your data and something like that.

03:32.550 --> 03:34.200
This is really good practice.

03:34.200 --> 03:37.380
All right, so those are the seven main phases:

03:37.380 --> 03:42.380
conceive, plan, develop, iterate, launch, steady state,

03:42.690 --> 03:45.660
and then finally, we maintain it or kill it.

03:45.660 --> 03:47.100
One thing to mention here

03:47.100 --> 03:50.160
is that this might sound a little bit academic to you

03:50.160 --> 03:53.523
but it is because it is actually pretty academic.

03:54.450 --> 03:56.550
You know, this is a process

03:56.550 --> 03:58.890
that every product goes through

03:58.890 --> 04:01.860
but you're not gonna be actually using these terms,

04:01.860 --> 04:03.360
you know, in the office every day.

04:03.360 --> 04:05.430
It's just very important as a product manager

04:05.430 --> 04:09.270
to understand that every type of product or company

04:09.270 --> 04:11.520
goes through these types of phases.

04:11.520 --> 04:13.680
And there's things to consider about each.

04:13.680 --> 04:15.720
So without any further ado,

04:15.720 --> 04:17.460
let's go on to the next section.

04:17.460 --> 04:18.813
I will see you there.

04:19.971 --> 04:22.471
(jolly music)

04:31.875 --> 04:35.208
(jolly music continues)

response.description:
If you want to get lean, you’ve got to cut out the fat!

In this lecture, we'll learn what it means to get your organization lean. And when it comes to product management, getting lean is definitely in. So what is “lean” when it comes to our process and how can we do it? 

Covered in this lecture:

  • What it means to make your product development process lean.
  • Why we want to have a lean process.
  • Real world examples of what it would look like to go lean.
  • How to be responsible about going lean.
response.title: What is "Lean Product Development"?

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.310 --> 00:02.893
(upbeat music)

00:04.170 --> 00:05.430
-: So what is Lean?

00:05.430 --> 00:08.160
Well Lean is a product development philosophy

00:08.160 --> 00:11.820
that revolves around cutting out all of the unnecessary work

00:11.820 --> 00:14.400
or effort until you're absolutely sure you need to do it

00:14.400 --> 00:16.050
for your users or customers.

00:16.050 --> 00:18.930
Let's say I have an idea for a food delivery business

00:18.930 --> 00:20.970
where someone can just text on their phone,

00:20.970 --> 00:23.190
their order to a cell phone number

00:23.190 --> 00:25.830
and we bring 'em the food, like that.

00:25.830 --> 00:28.680
What would you need to make this business a reality?

00:28.680 --> 00:30.780
Seriously, put yourself in the position

00:30.780 --> 00:33.060
of someone that's going to be starting this business

00:33.060 --> 00:35.100
and think about what you would need

00:35.100 --> 00:37.230
to get it to be a real thing.

00:37.230 --> 00:40.410
Normally, we'd incorporate, we'd hire some drivers,

00:40.410 --> 00:41.760
we would build a website,

00:41.760 --> 00:43.800
we would put out some advertisements,

00:43.800 --> 00:45.259
and we would buy a cell phone number

00:45.259 --> 00:47.370
for people to text for the food.

00:47.370 --> 00:49.050
So to better use resources here

00:49.050 --> 00:51.300
I could have done some work up front myself,

00:51.300 --> 00:53.070
by doing things like

00:53.070 --> 00:55.140
having people text my own cell phone number,

00:55.140 --> 00:57.167
or putting out flyers myself,

00:57.167 --> 01:00.570
or driving the food to people's houses myself,

01:00.570 --> 01:02.160
once I got a text message.

01:02.160 --> 01:05.250
This would require a lot more work for me up front

01:05.250 --> 01:08.370
but it guarantees that I'm not spending money

01:08.370 --> 01:10.860
on stuff until I know I actually have to.

01:10.860 --> 01:13.770
So at the point that I get enough business

01:13.770 --> 01:15.960
where I can no longer do these things myself,

01:15.960 --> 01:18.870
where I can no longer drive to the people's houses myself,

01:18.870 --> 01:22.740
or take the orders myself, then I start hiring the drivers.

01:22.740 --> 01:24.390
This is a Lean approach.

01:24.390 --> 01:27.000
So Lean is really just about building something

01:27.000 --> 01:31.380
in an intelligent way where I'm not using the resources

01:31.380 --> 01:33.720
until I know I actually have to.

01:33.720 --> 01:35.340
And this way we save a ton of money.

01:35.340 --> 01:39.000
Lean is not specifically for software, though.

01:39.000 --> 01:40.710
There is a process called Agile

01:40.710 --> 01:42.630
that is specifically for software.

01:42.630 --> 01:45.180
But I do recommend reading more about Lean

01:45.180 --> 01:48.030
and if you'd like, there's a whole book on the topic

01:48.030 --> 01:50.910
called "Lean Startup" by Eric Ries,

01:50.910 --> 01:53.250
and it looks a lot like that.

01:53.250 --> 01:56.073
So in the next lecture we're gonna talk about Agile.

response.description:
Now that you’re convinced that you need to be a lean mean product development machine, it's time to talk about "agile". In order to best serve stakeholders and conserve resources, we need to become adept at being agile. 

So what is the relationship between lean and agile and why are they important to a product manager? Explore these implications as you discover how to be agile.

Covered in this lecture:

  • What it means to be agile.
  • Why a product manager needs to know the difference between lean and agile.
  • Practical examples of what it means to be agile.
  • Why being agile can save you tons of resources.
response.title: What is "Agile"?

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.110 --> 00:01.830
-: So we know that Lean is a way

00:01.830 --> 00:05.280
of building a product or business without wasting resources,

00:05.280 --> 00:07.650
without spending the money until we have to.

00:07.650 --> 00:08.760
But what is Agile?

00:08.760 --> 00:10.320
Aren't they the same thing?

00:10.320 --> 00:11.640
Well, a little bit.

00:11.640 --> 00:13.020
In this lecture we're gonna talk

00:13.020 --> 00:15.300
about Agile and why it's so important

00:15.300 --> 00:17.160
for a product manager to understand.

00:17.160 --> 00:18.690
So, what is Agile?

00:18.690 --> 00:19.860
Well, it's pretty easy.

00:19.860 --> 00:21.120
Agile is just a way

00:21.120 --> 00:24.450
of applying that Lean mindset to software development.

00:24.450 --> 00:26.760
And agile, as you know, is an adjective.

00:26.760 --> 00:30.522
You can say something is agile, but when people, especially

00:30.522 --> 00:34.080
in the software or tech industry, say the word agile

00:34.080 --> 00:37.020
they're usually meaning Agile software development.

00:37.020 --> 00:38.430
Which is just a sort

00:38.430 --> 00:42.900
of a project management framework for software development.

00:42.900 --> 00:44.580
Agile is an iterative method

00:44.580 --> 00:47.100
to developing software where we group things

00:47.100 --> 00:48.870
into small batches and do them

00:48.870 --> 00:51.030
in order to not waste resources.

00:51.030 --> 00:53.610
So, let's imagine that you are writing

00:53.610 --> 00:57.000
down the features that you think an app should have.

00:57.000 --> 01:00.270
So, you're making an app, write 10 features down

01:00.270 --> 01:02.550
and you think these are absolutely necessary

01:02.550 --> 01:05.100
for the users to want your app.

01:05.100 --> 01:06.926
If we are doing this in an Agile way

01:06.926 --> 01:10.980
we would probably only research and develop the

01:10.980 --> 01:14.460
first two to five features that we think are most important.

01:14.460 --> 01:15.990
So, we would take those features

01:15.990 --> 01:18.090
we say these are the most important ones.

01:18.090 --> 01:21.810
We design them, you know, we research 'em, we develop 'em

01:21.810 --> 01:24.660
we release them, and we see what the user feedback is

01:24.660 --> 01:26.580
on those three to five features

01:26.580 --> 01:29.670
before we go on and develop the rest of the features.

01:29.670 --> 01:30.960
In next lecture, we're gonna talk

01:30.960 --> 01:33.180
about two very specific frameworks inside

01:33.180 --> 01:36.810
of the agile development process called Kanban and Scrum.

01:36.810 --> 01:38.410
All right, see you next lecture.

response.description:
While "scrum" may sound like something you feed your pet shark, it’s actually a highly effective tool in the product manager’s toolbox. Coming off the last lecture, you might be wondering how exactly does one implement "agile"?

Well, scrum is one of the more popular ways. Learn why you need to scrum, why you should stand up when you’re having meetings, and how to keep your product development process running smoothly. If you’re unsure about the step-by-step process of embracing agile, scrum is your first step.

Covered in this lecture:

  • How scrum can help you be a more effective product manager.
  • What scrum looks like in a day-to-day context.
  • How you can implement scrum in your operation.
response.title: What is "Scrum" and How Does it Work?

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.240 --> 00:01.260
-: Welcome back, everyone.

00:01.260 --> 00:04.410
In this lecture, we're gonna talk about Scrum and Kanban,

00:04.410 --> 00:08.220
which are two agile development philosophies or frameworks.

00:08.220 --> 00:10.710
So we know now that agile is a philosophy

00:10.710 --> 00:13.560
for developing software in a lean and iterative way

00:13.560 --> 00:16.680
with plenty of collaboration between the team members,

00:16.680 --> 00:19.740
but what are the guidelines for putting it in place,

00:19.740 --> 00:21.480
how does it actually look?

00:21.480 --> 00:23.130
There are two very popular

00:23.130 --> 00:26.700
agile software development methodologies or structures,

00:26.700 --> 00:27.960
one is called Scrum,

00:27.960 --> 00:29.550
which you've probably heard of before,

00:29.550 --> 00:31.530
and the other is called Kanban.

00:31.530 --> 00:34.380
Some people pronounce it Kanban, but whatever.

00:34.380 --> 00:36.120
You can think of both of these methods

00:36.120 --> 00:39.900
as merely guides to developing software in an agile way.

00:39.900 --> 00:41.310
So let's start with Scrum.

00:41.310 --> 00:44.460
It's probably the most common of the two.

00:44.460 --> 00:46.260
Let's walk through how to implement it

00:46.260 --> 00:48.480
or what it looks like in four easy steps.

00:48.480 --> 00:51.120
Now, there are a lot of things about Scrum

00:51.120 --> 00:52.950
but these are the four main ones.

00:52.950 --> 00:56.520
Step one, the sprint planning meeting.

00:56.520 --> 00:59.010
So you start off with a product backlog,

00:59.010 --> 01:00.600
which is a big prioritized list

01:00.600 --> 01:02.700
of all the things you've either decided to work on

01:02.700 --> 01:04.560
or may work on.

01:04.560 --> 01:07.590
Your team holds a sprint planning meeting,

01:07.590 --> 01:10.080
which is where you take the most important feature

01:10.080 --> 01:10.950
or several,

01:10.950 --> 01:12.870
from the top of your product backlog

01:12.870 --> 01:15.450
and you move it to your sprint backlog.

01:15.450 --> 01:18.120
Then you talk about all the work you're going to need to do

01:18.120 --> 01:19.980
in order to implement it.

01:19.980 --> 01:22.530
Together, your team writes out what work needs to be done

01:22.530 --> 01:24.750
in order to make that feature a reality.

01:24.750 --> 01:28.050
You put the work into a project management software

01:28.050 --> 01:30.060
into things called tickets.

01:30.060 --> 01:32.250
Step two, this is where you start developing.

01:32.250 --> 01:33.660
The core tenet of Scrum

01:33.660 --> 01:35.070
is that the work your team does

01:35.070 --> 01:37.650
is boxed into a timeframe called a sprint,

01:37.650 --> 01:39.210
usually two weeks.

01:39.210 --> 01:41.850
Some companies do three week sprints and some do four,

01:41.850 --> 01:43.200
but two is the most common.

01:43.200 --> 01:44.400
Throughout the two weeks,

01:44.400 --> 01:45.900
your team works on the things

01:45.900 --> 01:49.380
by taking them off the top of the sprint backlog

01:49.380 --> 01:51.960
and moving them to in progress

01:51.960 --> 01:53.910
and then eventually to done.

01:53.910 --> 01:55.980
These are the tickets that they're moving

01:55.980 --> 01:57.690
from column to column.

01:57.690 --> 01:59.280
At the end of the two weeks,

01:59.280 --> 02:02.550
you should have completed everything in the sprint backlog.

02:02.550 --> 02:05.820
If not, those things go to the next sprint.

02:05.820 --> 02:08.520
So step three is standup meetings,

02:08.520 --> 02:10.980
and these are very common and very helpful.

02:10.980 --> 02:13.590
Standup meetings are these small daily meetings

02:13.590 --> 02:15.180
usually hold in the mornings.

02:15.180 --> 02:16.830
Why are they called standup meetings?

02:16.830 --> 02:17.790
Well, the theory here

02:17.790 --> 02:20.490
is that if you remain standing as a team

02:20.490 --> 02:21.870
and you're not sitting down,

02:21.870 --> 02:24.390
then the meeting will be very brief

02:24.390 --> 02:26.580
and just concise in general.

02:26.580 --> 02:28.860
And we find that to be pretty true.

02:28.860 --> 02:30.390
So the goal of the standup meeting

02:30.390 --> 02:32.250
is for every team member to talk about

02:32.250 --> 02:33.330
what they worked on last,

02:33.330 --> 02:34.560
what they're working on now,

02:34.560 --> 02:36.600
and then what they're gonna work on next.

02:36.600 --> 02:37.470
Most importantly,

02:37.470 --> 02:40.080
they should bring up any questions or requests for help

02:40.080 --> 02:41.730
during the standup meeting.

02:41.730 --> 02:44.580
These daily meetings, usually every morning again,

02:44.580 --> 02:47.190
keep everything moving really smoothly.

02:47.190 --> 02:50.760
So step four and another big component of implementing Scrum

02:50.760 --> 02:53.250
is retrospective meetings.

02:53.250 --> 02:55.470
A retrospective is kind of the opposite

02:55.470 --> 02:57.000
of a sprint planning meeting.

02:57.000 --> 02:58.890
The retrospective is a meeting you have

02:58.890 --> 03:00.720
at the end of each sprint with your team.

03:00.720 --> 03:02.070
You get everyone in a room,

03:02.070 --> 03:04.770
yourself, the developers, the designers, et cetera,

03:04.770 --> 03:06.840
and you talk about three main things

03:06.840 --> 03:08.100
about the last sprint,

03:08.100 --> 03:09.060
what went well,

03:09.060 --> 03:10.260
what did not go well,

03:10.260 --> 03:12.390
and what questions people have.

03:12.390 --> 03:14.340
It's usually your job as the product manager

03:14.340 --> 03:16.410
to lead the team through this.

03:16.410 --> 03:18.270
These meetings ensure that everyone

03:18.270 --> 03:20.640
that has questions or concerns about the team

03:20.640 --> 03:22.740
or the structure or your process

03:22.740 --> 03:24.330
is getting these things heard,

03:24.330 --> 03:26.640
and it keeps everything moving along very smoothly.

03:26.640 --> 03:29.040
All right, so let's recap on Scrum

03:29.040 --> 03:31.350
and the big four major components.

03:31.350 --> 03:33.780
The first one is that we have a sprint planning meeting

03:33.780 --> 03:35.880
where we take everything from the product backlog,

03:35.880 --> 03:37.620
we put it in the sprint backlog.

03:37.620 --> 03:39.750
Secondly, we have the sprint itself,

03:39.750 --> 03:41.730
which is a time box period of time,

03:41.730 --> 03:44.490
like two weeks, three weeks, or four,

03:44.490 --> 03:46.500
but the most common is two.

03:46.500 --> 03:49.710
Then we have the daily standup meetings during the sprint.

03:49.710 --> 03:51.750
These are 10 or 15 minute meetings

03:51.750 --> 03:54.090
where we keep standing up

03:54.090 --> 03:56.340
so that people don't take too long

03:56.340 --> 03:58.710
and we just talk about what you're doing yesterday,

03:58.710 --> 04:00.060
what you're doing today,

04:00.060 --> 04:01.860
and what you're doing tomorrow

04:01.860 --> 04:05.910
and clarify any issues or any collaboration needed.

04:05.910 --> 04:08.280
Finally, we have the retrospective meeting

04:08.280 --> 04:11.160
where we talk about what went well during the sprint,

04:11.160 --> 04:12.660
what did not go well,

04:12.660 --> 04:15.360
and any outstanding questions that people have.

04:15.360 --> 04:16.193
All right, everyone.

04:16.193 --> 04:17.720
We'll see you in the next lecture.

response.description:
Just when you thought you knew everything about scrum and agile, here comes scrum’s older, more lucid brother. Kanban represents a framework of product management that differs from scrum in a few key ways. 

What are the major differences between kanban and scrum and how can you use this knowledge to your advantage? You’ll only know on the other side.

Covered in this lecture:

  • How to pronounce kanban.
  • The difference between scrum and kanban.
  • Which one of these two frameworks is best.
response.title: What is "Kanban" and How Does it Work?

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.042 --> 00:02.625
(upbeat music)

00:04.230 --> 00:05.670
-: What is up, everybody?

00:05.670 --> 00:08.640
So what's Kanban and how does it compare to Scrum?

00:08.640 --> 00:11.850
By the way, is it Kanban or Kanban?

00:11.850 --> 00:13.800
I'm almost positive it's Kanban,

00:13.800 --> 00:16.530
but if I am wrong, tweet at me or something.

00:16.530 --> 00:19.980
So like Scrum, Kanban is just a framework

00:19.980 --> 00:22.470
for implementing Agile software development

00:22.470 --> 00:24.810
except it's not as strict as Scrum

00:24.810 --> 00:27.060
in terms of meetings and times.

00:27.060 --> 00:29.550
So you may have actually seen what's referred to

00:29.550 --> 00:32.490
as a Kanban board before.

00:32.490 --> 00:34.770
It's just a bunch of columns with cards on it

00:34.770 --> 00:37.380
that you can move from one column to the other one

00:37.380 --> 00:39.300
to reflect a state of that item

00:39.300 --> 00:42.210
like to do, in progress, and then done.

00:42.210 --> 00:45.720
However, just because something has a Kanban style board

00:45.720 --> 00:48.810
does not mean that that is the Kanban process.

00:48.810 --> 00:52.050
Instead, the key concepts with Kanban are

00:52.050 --> 00:53.970
that it does not use sprints.

00:53.970 --> 00:56.460
So you don't time box the work of your team

00:56.460 --> 00:58.800
into these two or four-week periods.

00:58.800 --> 01:02.130
Also, since there's no sprints, there's no sprint backlog,

01:02.130 --> 01:03.090
which we'll learn about,

01:03.090 --> 01:05.820
but only the product backlog itself.

01:05.820 --> 01:09.000
So what happens is the team just works on their tickets,

01:09.000 --> 01:11.490
they do the work, they move it to done,

01:11.490 --> 01:13.560
and then they take the next task

01:13.560 --> 01:15.870
off the top of the product backlog

01:15.870 --> 01:17.940
and that backlog just goes on forever.

01:17.940 --> 01:19.020
It's just endless.

01:19.020 --> 01:21.360
Kanban is also different from Scrum

01:21.360 --> 01:25.260
in that it does not prescribe any particular meeting types

01:25.260 --> 01:27.570
like Scrum does with standup meetings,

01:27.570 --> 01:29.100
and sprint planning meetings,

01:29.100 --> 01:30.660
and retrospective meetings.

01:30.660 --> 01:32.460
And there's one more big difference.

01:32.460 --> 01:34.800
Kanban operates off of the theory

01:34.800 --> 01:38.670
that only a certain amount of items can be in progress

01:38.670 --> 01:40.890
or in any given state at one time.

01:40.890 --> 01:43.110
How many items can be in each particular state

01:43.110 --> 01:45.720
is up for you and your team to decide.

01:45.720 --> 01:47.970
Some software teams use Kanban,

01:47.970 --> 01:50.610
but it tends to work best with teams

01:50.610 --> 01:54.210
that are not very concerned with things like estimation

01:54.210 --> 01:56.370
and are continuously moving tasks

01:56.370 --> 01:59.250
through the same number of steps pretty quickly

01:59.250 --> 02:00.210
until they're complete.

02:00.210 --> 02:02.220
For instance, customer service teams

02:02.220 --> 02:04.890
often use a Kanban style of working.

02:04.890 --> 02:07.740
All right, so we are familiar with Scrum and Kanban.

02:07.740 --> 02:09.870
Then the big question is, which one is best?

02:09.870 --> 02:10.860
Sorry to disappoint you

02:10.860 --> 02:12.960
but there's no definitive answer here

02:12.960 --> 02:15.930
as it depends on what your team prefers to use.

02:15.930 --> 02:18.840
The best process is the one that you like using.

02:18.840 --> 02:19.673
As you can tell,

02:19.673 --> 02:22.980
Kanban is a more relaxed way of doing Agile development

02:22.980 --> 02:24.840
but it has a big disadvantage.

02:24.840 --> 02:26.280
Since it doesn't use sprints

02:26.280 --> 02:27.540
it's going to be more difficult

02:27.540 --> 02:29.190
to project the amount of time

02:29.190 --> 02:31.470
it's going to take to complete work.

02:31.470 --> 02:33.240
Don't worry too much about that right now

02:33.240 --> 02:35.160
'cause we're going to talk about things

02:35.160 --> 02:37.830
like estimations and velocity in a later lecture.

02:37.830 --> 02:38.910
So let's recap.

02:38.910 --> 02:41.130
Kanban is just another way to implement

02:41.130 --> 02:43.860
an Agile type of software development workflow.

02:43.860 --> 02:46.080
It's a little less prescriptive than Scrum

02:46.080 --> 02:47.820
and it does not use sprints.

02:47.820 --> 02:48.653
All right, everybody.

02:48.653 --> 02:50.170
I will see you in the next lecture.

response.description:
When you think of a waterfall, isn’t product management the first thing on your mind?

No? Well, after this lecture you’ll start thinking about products with every cascading fountain of water you see. Waterfall development is a well known development strategy that differs quite a bit from agile. 

Is one method better than the other? We'll discuss.

Covered in this lecture:

  • How to distinguish the waterfall method from the agile method.
  • The driving philosophy behind waterfall.
  • Dangers to watch out for.
response.title: What is Waterfall Development?

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.120 --> 00:01.800
-: In the Agile app example,

00:01.800 --> 00:04.320
we took those 10 features we thought we wanted

00:04.320 --> 00:06.180
and we only selected a few that we thought

00:06.180 --> 00:07.590
were the most important.

00:07.590 --> 00:10.170
And we took those and we researched them,

00:10.170 --> 00:12.780
designed them, developed them, and then released them

00:12.780 --> 00:15.150
and validated whether or not those were the things

00:15.150 --> 00:17.190
that users were interested in.

00:17.190 --> 00:20.520
Waterfall looks a little bit more something like this.

00:20.520 --> 00:23.160
We take all 10 of those features that we think we want,

00:23.160 --> 00:25.650
and we develop them all at the same time.

00:25.650 --> 00:28.170
So we research, design, develop,

00:28.170 --> 00:30.660
and release every single one of them all at once.

00:30.660 --> 00:32.160
Doing things in a waterfall way

00:32.160 --> 00:33.540
is sometimes pretty risky,

00:33.540 --> 00:37.320
because your software product might have a ton of features

00:37.320 --> 00:38.700
and when you have a ton of features,

00:38.700 --> 00:41.820
it's going to take a long time to develop them all at once.

00:41.820 --> 00:44.400
During that time, your competitors may do something,

00:44.400 --> 00:46.740
or you may find something else about the user needs,

00:46.740 --> 00:50.040
or market that makes it much harder to adapt.

00:50.040 --> 00:52.500
You also might find that after you've developed it,

00:52.500 --> 00:54.420
your users wanted something else

00:54.420 --> 00:56.160
that you've spent little time on,

00:56.160 --> 00:58.230
and you spent the most time on stuff

00:58.230 --> 00:59.700
that they're not actually gonna be using.

00:59.700 --> 01:03.180
So after all of this trash talking on waterfall development,

01:03.180 --> 01:05.970
you might be thinking, when is it ever useful?

01:05.970 --> 01:08.370
Well, there's actually a few specific use cases

01:08.370 --> 01:10.800
where waterfall development is pretty useful,

01:10.800 --> 01:13.403
and we're gonna talk about those in the next lecture.

response.description:
Pretend that you’re working in product development for a shiny new company that’s making the world’s greatest app. Are you going to use agile or waterfall as your management methodology? Whatever you answer, it’s useful to SEE the real world applications of both methods in action- beforehand. Each one has strengths and weaknesses, and as a product manager, you need to have a good grasp of what they are.

Covered in this lecture:

  • How to use agile in the real world.
  • Why agile can sometimes spell disaster for your company.
  • When you should be embrace waterfall.
response.title: Real World Examples of Waterfall and Agile

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:04.132 --> 00:05.430
-: In the last lecture, we learned

00:05.430 --> 00:08.160
that Agile software development is a methodology

00:08.160 --> 00:10.260
for applying that lean mindset

00:10.260 --> 00:12.000
to software development inside a company.

00:12.000 --> 00:14.850
So what does it look like when we actually apply it

00:14.850 --> 00:16.980
inside a development environment?

00:16.980 --> 00:18.783
What does it look like day-to-day?

00:22.860 --> 00:25.530
Let's say we work at a music streaming company

00:25.530 --> 00:28.290
and we've identified that the most important thing

00:28.290 --> 00:33.290
to our users is to have a ability to build a playlist.

00:33.360 --> 00:36.270
So using the Agile development methodology,

00:36.270 --> 00:38.790
we can get everyone in a room, the engineers,

00:38.790 --> 00:41.970
the designers, and us, the product managers,

00:41.970 --> 00:45.090
and we can decide on the very core features

00:45.090 --> 00:48.090
of building a playlist and what that entails.

00:48.090 --> 00:50.250
So if we talk to the designers and engineers

00:50.250 --> 00:53.010
we can be pretty certain that the most basic requirement

00:53.010 --> 00:54.900
for building playlist functionality

00:54.900 --> 00:57.270
is going to be adding a bunch of tracks,

00:57.270 --> 01:00.720
or songs to a list called a playlist.

01:00.720 --> 01:02.580
With this knowledge, the engineers can go ahead

01:02.580 --> 01:05.130
and start developing the database that we know

01:05.130 --> 01:07.860
we'll need to build the playlist functionality.

01:07.860 --> 01:10.140
In the meantime, the designer and us,

01:10.140 --> 01:13.680
the product managers, can get together and start talking

01:13.680 --> 01:17.100
about how will the playlists act, and how will they look

01:17.100 --> 01:19.350
and how will they feel inside of the app?

01:19.350 --> 01:22.800
So us, the product managers, and the designers can go off

01:22.800 --> 01:25.260
and wireframe some potential designs.

01:25.260 --> 01:27.060
The designers can make 'em look really nice.

01:27.060 --> 01:29.220
We can test 'em with users and we can

01:29.220 --> 01:31.860
make changes based on user feedback.

01:31.860 --> 01:33.570
We can do this process a couple of times

01:33.570 --> 01:35.850
while the engineers are working on the database.

01:35.850 --> 01:37.890
By the time we've got the perfect design,

01:37.890 --> 01:41.460
and look and feel ready, the engineers can put it inside

01:41.460 --> 01:43.590
of the app because they're done with the database.

01:43.590 --> 01:45.270
So now, with the engineers done

01:45.270 --> 01:48.330
with the backend and the database they start implementing

01:48.330 --> 01:50.910
the actual playlist functionality in the app.

01:50.910 --> 01:54.150
Then, the product manager and the designer can go on

01:54.150 --> 01:56.310
and start talking to users about

01:56.310 --> 01:58.290
the next features they should build

01:58.290 --> 02:00.840
and then start doing the entire process over.

02:00.840 --> 02:02.640
So while the engineers are actually implementing

02:02.640 --> 02:05.460
this feature into the app, the product manager, us,

02:05.460 --> 02:09.030
and the designers can go talk to other users and look

02:09.030 --> 02:12.390
at metrics to decide what feature we should build next.

02:12.390 --> 02:15.870
Then, we go and do wireframes again and design

02:15.870 --> 02:18.570
and we get some more feedback from users

02:18.570 --> 02:20.520
and we just complete the cycle over again.

02:20.520 --> 02:23.550
When companies use the Agile development methodology,

02:23.550 --> 02:26.370
usually, everyone is in very close communication

02:26.370 --> 02:29.610
and collaboration, engineers, designers

02:29.610 --> 02:32.070
and product managers are usually, in the same room

02:32.070 --> 02:34.110
or office and they're always talking.

02:34.110 --> 02:36.390
So how about Waterfall, when is it useful

02:36.390 --> 02:39.570
and what are some examples of it being a useful process?

02:39.570 --> 02:43.530
Well, think about Microsoft Windows or Mac OS X,

02:43.530 --> 02:46.710
which are operating systems for your computers.

02:46.710 --> 02:50.190
These are operating systems that have literally thousands

02:50.190 --> 02:52.560
of features, it would be really, really hard

02:52.560 --> 02:55.560
to pick a couple of features that stand on their own.

02:55.560 --> 02:58.380
All of these features have dependencies on others

02:58.380 --> 03:01.710
and you really just release a few to the public.

03:01.710 --> 03:05.010
Another example of Waterfall being a little bit more useful

03:05.010 --> 03:09.420
than Agile is in mission critical software applications,

03:09.420 --> 03:13.650
like braking, or engine control systems in cars.

03:13.650 --> 03:16.200
Do you think that using Agile development

03:16.200 --> 03:18.660
and developing only a few few features at a time

03:18.660 --> 03:22.140
inside of a car for the braking systems is a good idea?

03:22.140 --> 03:22.973
Definitely not.

03:22.973 --> 03:25.050
Well, what about things outside of software?

03:25.050 --> 03:28.200
Waterfall is used in other places where it's pretty useful.

03:28.200 --> 03:30.720
Let's imagine we're building a skyscraper.

03:30.720 --> 03:33.090
Do you think we'd wanna build five floors at a time

03:33.090 --> 03:34.290
and then put a bunch of people

03:34.290 --> 03:36.270
in it and see if it still stands?

03:36.270 --> 03:39.120
Probably not, we're gonna build all 100 floors together

03:39.120 --> 03:41.160
and we're gonna do it from a spec that we know

03:41.160 --> 03:43.650
from the beginning has everything we need.

03:43.650 --> 03:46.230
When the Waterfall software development process is used

03:46.230 --> 03:49.560
the team does not collaborate quite as closely as the team

03:49.560 --> 03:52.140
that's doing an Agile software development process.

03:52.140 --> 03:56.250
You can have the research and product requirements team

03:56.250 --> 03:59.760
in one city, the designers in another city and time zone,

03:59.760 --> 04:03.060
and the developers in an entirely other city and time zone.

04:03.060 --> 04:05.640
This is because everything is defined up front

04:05.640 --> 04:07.110
and it gets passed from one team

04:07.110 --> 04:09.540
to the other and nothing changes.

04:09.540 --> 04:12.060
So I hope everyone has a pretty decent understanding

04:12.060 --> 04:14.970
of the differences between Agile and Waterfall,

04:14.970 --> 04:18.900
and what sorts of real world examples these are used in.

04:18.900 --> 04:21.030
For your purposes as a product manager

04:21.030 --> 04:23.850
it'll be focused mostly on Agile, but don't think

04:23.850 --> 04:26.793
that Waterfall is completely useless because it's not.

response.description:
Welcome to the land of ideas and user needs! Have you ever wondered where a product actually starts? Does it come from a room full of bigwigs and “idea guys” who just sit around and spout dribble all afternoon? Whether or not you think that’s how products start to exist, there’s a whole world to explore around the ideas and needs we and our users have. Your job as a product manager can also feel pretty broad, so it's time to start feeling out the edges of your responsibilities.

Covered in this lecture:

  • Where products come from.
  • What your job as a product manager is.
  • Why your role is so important in an organization.
  • How to figure out where your role boundaries are.
response.title: Introduction to Ideas and User Needs

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.240 --> 00:02.100
-: What's up, everybody?

00:02.100 --> 00:04.410
Everything in life has to start somewhere.

00:04.410 --> 00:06.990
Now, I'm not gonna lecture you on philosophy

00:06.990 --> 00:07.823
or something like that,

00:07.823 --> 00:09.480
but when you're a product manager,

00:09.480 --> 00:11.550
products also start somewhere.

00:11.550 --> 00:14.970
And that means that features and new functionality,

00:14.970 --> 00:16.650
it all comes from some place.

00:16.650 --> 00:20.730
Everything that you see or use in your life, any product,

00:20.730 --> 00:24.150
at one point it was either an idea in someone's head

00:24.150 --> 00:27.030
or it was a need, like a requirement.

00:27.030 --> 00:30.210
Someone said, "Hey, I need this team to build this for me."

00:30.210 --> 00:31.890
And then they built it.

00:31.890 --> 00:34.920
So, there's this theory out there that product managers

00:34.920 --> 00:37.440
and executives, they all get in a room

00:37.440 --> 00:38.670
and they're the idea people.

00:38.670 --> 00:41.520
They just sit there and come up with these fantastic ideas,

00:41.520 --> 00:43.890
and then the developers and the designers, you know,

00:43.890 --> 00:45.690
make it look pretty and build it.

00:45.690 --> 00:47.880
And then the executives get rich,

00:47.880 --> 00:51.390
and that's actually so far from the truth,

00:51.390 --> 00:52.800
I don't even want to go there.

00:52.800 --> 00:56.790
Your job as a product manager is not to be the idea person.

00:56.790 --> 00:58.680
We're gonna talk about that in a little bit,

00:58.680 --> 01:02.280
but it's really just about getting this huge influx

01:02.280 --> 01:06.300
of ideas and requirements from all over the place.

01:06.300 --> 01:09.007
Requirements are other people coming up to you and saying,

01:09.007 --> 01:10.616
"Hey, I need you to build this."

01:10.616 --> 01:14.320
♪ Do you wanna build a snowman? ♪

01:14.320 --> 01:16.417
-: Maybe a sales team or marketing team,

01:16.417 --> 01:18.870
"Hey, we need this functionality," that sort of thing.

01:18.870 --> 01:20.100
They have all of this stuff.

01:20.100 --> 01:22.140
And your job is really just to say,

01:22.140 --> 01:24.870
of all of these things, which ones are good ideas,

01:24.870 --> 01:26.007
which ones are bad?

01:26.007 --> 01:28.765
(alarm sounds)
(man crashes)

01:28.765 --> 01:30.960
(extinguisher hisses)

01:30.960 --> 01:32.370
Why are they good ideas?

01:32.370 --> 01:34.560
And when is the best time to do them,

01:34.560 --> 01:38.010
given all of the other stuff that's going on at the time

01:38.010 --> 01:41.640
in the company, with technology, and in the industry?

01:41.640 --> 01:44.760
Anyways, that's what this section is all about.

01:44.760 --> 01:49.710
So, get ready to take a dive into the land of ideas,

01:49.710 --> 01:52.530
and user needs, and false user needs,

01:52.530 --> 01:54.210
and users thinking they need this thing

01:54.210 --> 01:55.890
when they actually need this thing.

01:55.890 --> 01:57.480
Anyways, I'm getting ahead of myself,

01:57.480 --> 02:00.843
but this is all coming up, so I will see you in there.

response.description:
Just like your five-year old self asking where babies come from, we now turn to the age-old product management question, “Where do ideas come from?” Specifically, whose job is it to come up with awesome ideas for you to develop? And when ideas are developed, whose job is it to filter through them? A lot of questions come up once you start digging into the details, and we aim to answer some of those.

Covered in this lecture:

  • Where your product ideas come from.
  • How to handle different types of ideas.
  • Why you probably don’t have to worry about running out of ideas.
  • How to keep track of all the ideas.
response.title: Where Ideas Come From as a PM

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.300 --> 00:01.650
-: What's up everybody?

00:01.650 --> 00:03.150
In this lecture we're gonna talk

00:03.150 --> 00:07.050
about where ideas come from as a product manager.

00:07.050 --> 00:09.921
So as a product manager, your entire job revolves

00:09.921 --> 00:13.320
around deciding what you need to do next.

00:13.320 --> 00:16.311
This requires a list of possible features to build

00:16.311 --> 00:18.060
and also a method

00:18.060 --> 00:21.030
of deciding which of those is the most important.

00:21.030 --> 00:22.500
Now, later on in the course

00:22.500 --> 00:23.370
we're going to talk about

00:23.370 --> 00:25.770
prioritization of features and epics

00:25.770 --> 00:27.390
which you'll learn about, but for now,

00:27.390 --> 00:28.560
we're just gonna need to focus

00:28.560 --> 00:31.950
on getting that list of features in the first place.

00:31.950 --> 00:34.530
So, where do ideas come from?

00:34.530 --> 00:36.930
Where is the top of the funnel for the Product Manager

00:36.930 --> 00:39.300
in terms of what you might build in the future?

00:39.300 --> 00:41.790
Well, I wanna start by saying that any company you work

00:41.790 --> 00:44.610
for as a Product Manager, is gonna have a long list

00:44.610 --> 00:48.540
of requests, and feature ideas that need to be developed.

00:48.540 --> 00:52.170
If this is not the case, then you should feel really lucky.

00:52.170 --> 00:54.240
Even if you do have a really big list

00:54.240 --> 00:55.620
of stuff that needs to be built,

00:55.620 --> 00:58.770
there's always going to be instances where new ideas come

00:58.770 --> 01:01.920
in and take precedence over existing ones

01:01.920 --> 01:05.010
based on things like user feedback volume,

01:05.010 --> 01:07.320
what the competition is doing, et cetera.

01:07.320 --> 01:08.370
All right, So let's go ahead

01:08.370 --> 01:11.190
and assume you're a Product Manager at a new company

01:11.190 --> 01:12.870
and there's already a few things your company

01:12.870 --> 01:15.750
says you need to build with your team,

01:15.750 --> 01:18.180
but you also want to make sure there's plenty of other ideas

01:18.180 --> 01:21.540
in the backlog to think about and develop after that.

01:21.540 --> 01:22.860
Where do you get these ideas?

01:22.860 --> 01:26.400
Despite popular belief, the Product Management job is not

01:26.400 --> 01:29.190
about being the idea guy or idea girl.

01:29.190 --> 01:32.310
Ideas and requests come from everywhere,

01:32.310 --> 01:33.510
and you need to collect

01:33.510 --> 01:35.640
and organize them as a Product Manager.

01:35.640 --> 01:38.190
Feature ideas and requests usually come

01:38.190 --> 01:40.710
from four main places that we can easily

01:40.710 --> 01:44.737
remember using an acronym that is E M U C.

01:44.737 --> 01:45.570
"EMUC"

01:45.570 --> 01:47.520
So let's go through each one of those.

01:47.520 --> 01:49.620
Let's start with "E", Employees.

01:49.620 --> 01:51.690
This is just ideas coming in

01:51.690 --> 01:55.740
from your coworkers, management, and you, yourself.

01:55.740 --> 01:58.860
The second one is "M", and it stands for Metrics.

01:58.860 --> 02:00.570
This is just problems

02:00.570 --> 02:03.720
and inefficiencies you find when you're looking

02:03.720 --> 02:06.270
into how users use your product.

02:06.270 --> 02:08.550
For example, I may be looking at some data

02:08.550 --> 02:12.942
on how users navigate through an app, and I might notice

02:12.942 --> 02:16.410
that a lot of people just spend one second

02:16.410 --> 02:20.370
on a particular area and immediately go somewhere else.

02:20.370 --> 02:22.350
Well, that might give me the idea

02:22.350 --> 02:25.860
that this is an unclear section of the app,

02:25.860 --> 02:27.829
and we need to do some sort of redesign,

02:27.829 --> 02:29.250
or, at least go figure

02:29.250 --> 02:32.130
out what's going on, and then try to fix it.

02:32.130 --> 02:33.840
All right, so now let's move on to "U",

02:33.840 --> 02:35.550
which is Users.

02:35.550 --> 02:38.173
This is user feedback from forums,

02:38.173 --> 02:41.490
or emails to your company or yourself

02:41.490 --> 02:42.870
and social media.

02:42.870 --> 02:45.750
It's just everyone out there that uses your product.

02:45.750 --> 02:48.000
So the last one is see "C", Clients

02:48.000 --> 02:51.810
and this is a special case that applies primarily

02:51.810 --> 02:53.550
to Product Managers that are

02:53.550 --> 02:55.080
Business to Business,

02:55.080 --> 02:57.660
because it's not always the case

02:57.660 --> 03:00.300
that a User is the same as a Client,

03:00.300 --> 03:03.120
a client meaning the person that paid for the product.

03:03.120 --> 03:06.570
So, depending on what type of Product Manager you are,

03:06.570 --> 03:09.892
the importance and volume of requests may change.

03:09.892 --> 03:13.740
Do you remember the three types of Product Management roles?

03:13.740 --> 03:15.120
Consumer Product Manager,

03:15.120 --> 03:16.710
Internal Tools Product Manager,

03:16.710 --> 03:18.720
and Business to Business Product Manager?

03:18.720 --> 03:20.520
Let's do three examples really quickly

03:20.520 --> 03:23.370
of the PM types and where the ideas might come from.

03:23.370 --> 03:25.440
So if you're an Internal Product Manager,

03:25.440 --> 03:27.630
most of the ideas are probably going to be coming

03:27.630 --> 03:29.120
from your primary stakeholders,

03:29.120 --> 03:30.374
which are your coworkers

03:30.374 --> 03:32.460
and employees that you work with.

03:32.460 --> 03:35.250
If you are a Business Consumer Product Manager,

03:35.250 --> 03:38.490
most requests are probably going to come from the users,

03:38.490 --> 03:41.280
but you should also look for problems in metrics, and listen

03:41.280 --> 03:45.060
to new ideas from fellow coworkers, especially management.

03:45.060 --> 03:47.640
Now, if you're a Business to Business Product Manager,

03:47.640 --> 03:48.473
it means you're working

03:48.473 --> 03:51.300
on a product that is being sold to other companies.

03:51.300 --> 03:53.190
In this case, requests and ideas

03:53.190 --> 03:55.220
for features are going to come primarily

03:55.220 --> 03:58.200
from employees and clients.

03:58.200 --> 04:00.667
Like I said, a User is not always the same

04:00.667 --> 04:02.792
as a Customer or a Client.

04:02.792 --> 04:06.180
If you're a Business to Business Product Manager,

04:06.180 --> 04:08.940
but we will talk a little bit more about that later.

04:08.940 --> 04:10.650
All right, let's sum it up really quick.

04:10.650 --> 04:12.570
You're going to have ideas and feedback coming in

04:12.570 --> 04:14.310
from all sorts of places,

04:14.310 --> 04:16.009
depending on the type of Product Manager you are,

04:16.009 --> 04:20.280
some of the volume of feedback is going to be different

04:20.280 --> 04:21.420
from those different groups,

04:21.420 --> 04:23.460
but you can remember these groups

04:23.460 --> 04:25.020
by the acronym, "EMUK",

04:25.020 --> 04:29.670
Employees, Metrics, Users, and Clients or Customers.

04:29.670 --> 04:30.503
All right, everyone.

04:30.503 --> 04:31.930
I will see you in the next lecture.

04:34.073 --> 04:36.656
(upbeat music)

response.description:
What are some of the biggest challenges you will face as a product manager? If you said, “being a mind reader” you’re only slightly wrong. A product manager needs a sixth sense of what’s really going on. When your users tell you they need something, is your first response to automatically give it to them or to dig deeper to find the reasoning behind their request? As we continue to flesh out what a product manager is responsible for, these questions need to be answered in order for us to move forward.

Covered in this lecture:

  • How to understand a need.
  • The difference between a real need and a fake one.
  • How product managers should handle requests for features.
  • Examples of how to dig deeper to find the real answer.
response.title: Getting to the Real User Needs

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.351 --> 00:02.934
(upbeat music)

00:04.380 --> 00:05.520
-: Welcome back, everyone.

00:05.520 --> 00:08.610
So in the last lecture, we talked about all of the places

00:08.610 --> 00:11.460
where you can receive user feedback and ideas.

00:11.460 --> 00:15.570
Once we have this list of ideas or feature requests,

00:15.570 --> 00:17.010
do we just go build them?

00:17.010 --> 00:19.980
Let's say that a user has given you some feedback

00:19.980 --> 00:22.650
and they want you to build a native mobile app

00:22.650 --> 00:25.020
for your website, so you get that feedback,

00:25.020 --> 00:26.580
and you just go out and build it, right?

00:26.580 --> 00:29.850
No, being a product manager is about a lot more

00:29.850 --> 00:33.210
than just learning a few skills and applying those skills.

00:33.210 --> 00:34.500
One of the most important things

00:34.500 --> 00:35.850
about being a product manager

00:35.850 --> 00:38.880
is having a product manager mindset.

00:38.880 --> 00:40.500
And a big component of that

00:40.500 --> 00:44.250
is being able to understand the core pain points,

00:44.250 --> 00:48.480
the real problems behind what people ask you to do.

00:48.480 --> 00:50.490
So in this lecture, that's what we're gonna learn,

00:50.490 --> 00:52.410
how to get that essential skill.

00:52.410 --> 00:53.820
Let's start off with an example.

00:53.820 --> 00:55.920
Let's say that I have a friend,

00:55.920 --> 00:57.330
and I don't really have any friends

00:57.330 --> 01:01.110
so this is an unrealistic example, but let's say that I do

01:01.110 --> 01:03.000
and they're standing outside in the rain

01:03.000 --> 01:04.290
and they're getting all wet.

01:04.290 --> 01:06.757
And they're upset about being wet, and they say,

01:06.757 --> 01:08.670
"Cole, can you give me a towel?"

01:08.670 --> 01:11.370
And I give them a towel and they dry off

01:11.370 --> 01:13.260
and they get dry for a few minutes

01:13.260 --> 01:15.600
or maybe a few seconds and it's still raining,

01:15.600 --> 01:17.130
so they keep getting wet.

01:17.130 --> 01:18.810
Did I really solve their problem?

01:18.810 --> 01:20.130
Well, of course not.

01:20.130 --> 01:22.680
They asked for a towel, so that's what I gave them.

01:22.680 --> 01:24.480
That was their request to me.

01:24.480 --> 01:26.970
But is that the actual problem?

01:26.970 --> 01:30.870
No, the actual problem here is that they're in the rain.

01:30.870 --> 01:33.960
So the actual solution would be to get them out of the rain.

01:33.960 --> 01:36.840
So I know that example sounds pretty ridiculous,

01:36.840 --> 01:39.060
but this sort of thing happens all the time

01:39.060 --> 01:40.830
in the world of product management.

01:40.830 --> 01:44.730
People are always asking you to build things or add features

01:44.730 --> 01:47.940
and those things, at first glance, seem reasonable,

01:47.940 --> 01:50.430
but actually, they're merely symptoms

01:50.430 --> 01:53.280
of a real problem the user is having behind the scenes.

01:53.280 --> 01:54.420
Being a product manager

01:54.420 --> 01:58.530
is all about finding a solution to a problem

01:58.530 --> 02:01.890
instead of trying to fit a problem to a solution

02:01.890 --> 02:04.320
that you or someone else has thought of in the first place.

02:04.320 --> 02:06.630
Given any request or feature idea,

02:06.630 --> 02:09.187
the first thing I want you to do is think to yourself,

02:09.187 --> 02:11.850
"Is this solving an actual problem?"

02:11.850 --> 02:13.867
The second thing I want you to do is ask yourself,

02:13.867 --> 02:16.230
"If we do this exactly as they ask

02:16.230 --> 02:17.820
without looking any deeper,

02:17.820 --> 02:20.010
is it gonna have any unintended side effects?"

02:20.010 --> 02:22.740
I'll give you an example of an unintended side effect.

02:22.740 --> 02:24.330
Everyone's familiar with Twitter, right?

02:24.330 --> 02:26.550
They've got several hundred million users.

02:26.550 --> 02:29.280
The way Twitter works is you can type some characters

02:29.280 --> 02:30.660
and send it out as a tweet

02:30.660 --> 02:32.220
and that appears on your profile

02:32.220 --> 02:33.960
and it also appears in a newsfeed

02:33.960 --> 02:35.640
for everyone that follows you.

02:35.640 --> 02:38.910
On Twitter, you can also retweet, which is clicking a button

02:38.910 --> 02:42.240
on their tweet, and it puts it on your profile.

02:42.240 --> 02:45.090
And all of that also feeds into the newsfeed.

02:45.090 --> 02:48.210
So if you are following a ton of people,

02:48.210 --> 02:52.050
then your newsfeed can understandably get pretty busy.

02:52.050 --> 02:54.570
One of the most common things people ask for on Twitter

02:54.570 --> 02:57.840
is the ability to just filter out retweets.

02:57.840 --> 03:00.090
So the idea is that if the product managers

03:00.090 --> 03:02.580
and developers at Twitter just made a button

03:02.580 --> 03:05.700
to where you wouldn't see retweets on your timeline anymore,

03:05.700 --> 03:07.710
it would make things a lot less messy.

03:07.710 --> 03:09.210
Sounds pretty reasonable, right?

03:09.210 --> 03:11.340
Now, Twitter as a business model

03:11.340 --> 03:14.220
is a company that really just tries to show

03:14.220 --> 03:17.280
everyone out there what is really popular right now.

03:17.280 --> 03:19.470
So what are people around the world talking about?

03:19.470 --> 03:21.030
Think about retweets for a moment.

03:21.030 --> 03:23.467
When I retweet something, it's like saying,

03:23.467 --> 03:24.870
"I really like this.

03:24.870 --> 03:25.980
I endorse it.

03:25.980 --> 03:26.940
I like it so much

03:26.940 --> 03:29.280
that I'm willing to put it on my own profile."

03:29.280 --> 03:32.910
So if we built this feature and it got pretty popular,

03:32.910 --> 03:36.750
then we could have, say, 20 to 40% of people

03:36.750 --> 03:38.880
not seeing retweets at all.

03:38.880 --> 03:42.210
What ends up happening here is less and less people retweet

03:42.210 --> 03:44.610
because there's not as large of an audience.

03:44.610 --> 03:46.200
And then Twitter has less

03:46.200 --> 03:49.350
of an idea what people really strongly like

03:49.350 --> 03:51.150
or really strongly endorse.

03:51.150 --> 03:52.980
This makes it even more difficult for them

03:52.980 --> 03:57.240
to separate out signals on what's globally super popular.

03:57.240 --> 03:59.280
This affects normal users as well.

03:59.280 --> 04:00.870
So with less people retweeting,

04:00.870 --> 04:03.660
it's more difficult for them to tell what is super popular

04:03.660 --> 04:05.550
or what people really, really like.

04:05.550 --> 04:07.530
So remember, the request here

04:07.530 --> 04:10.260
was to allow us to filter out retweets

04:10.260 --> 04:11.910
on the Twitter timeline,

04:11.910 --> 04:14.820
and we've just covered that might not be the best idea

04:14.820 --> 04:17.280
because it has some unintended consequences.

04:17.280 --> 04:20.670
I'll give you example from my career of a feature request

04:20.670 --> 04:23.400
that did not actually address the user problem.

04:23.400 --> 04:25.950
A while ago, I was a product manager

04:25.950 --> 04:29.220
at an e-commerce website, and we were working

04:29.220 --> 04:32.070
on building a new content management system

04:32.070 --> 04:33.390
for the marketing team

04:33.390 --> 04:35.520
because they had outgrown the old one.

04:35.520 --> 04:38.280
This is basically a system by which they can go

04:38.280 --> 04:41.520
and edit the photos and the text and that sort of thing

04:41.520 --> 04:44.040
on the website while the website is live.

04:44.040 --> 04:45.750
So I'm meeting with the marketing team

04:45.750 --> 04:49.687
and I'm writing down requirements and they're saying,

04:49.687 --> 04:51.750
"We need it to do these things."

04:51.750 --> 04:54.300
One of the things they mentioned was they said

04:54.300 --> 04:57.000
they needed the ability to put up a small message

04:57.000 --> 05:01.470
on a specific product webpage to tell the users something

05:01.470 --> 05:02.910
and they wanted to be able to put that up

05:02.910 --> 05:04.470
and take it down pretty quickly.

05:04.470 --> 05:06.690
At that point, I could have said, "Okay,

05:06.690 --> 05:07.830
I'll take that into account

05:07.830 --> 05:09.870
and I'll make sure that whatever we do,

05:09.870 --> 05:11.340
we'll give you that feature."

05:11.340 --> 05:14.167
But instead what I did was ask them,

05:14.167 --> 05:15.270
"What's behind this request?"

05:15.270 --> 05:16.800
What do they actually want it to do?

05:16.800 --> 05:19.470
So I asked why this was actually a requirement

05:19.470 --> 05:21.270
and for what reason do they need this?

05:21.270 --> 05:24.090
So it turns out that the merchandising department

05:24.090 --> 05:26.310
told the marketing department to tell me

05:26.310 --> 05:28.800
that this was a requirement, and the reason was,

05:28.800 --> 05:31.350
it turns out, that they would hold sales

05:31.350 --> 05:32.820
on particular product pages.

05:32.820 --> 05:36.210
And on this shorts page in particular,

05:36.210 --> 05:40.080
the sizing and pictures were a little bit misleading.

05:40.080 --> 05:42.120
So when a lot of traffic went there,

05:42.120 --> 05:44.250
it ends up that a lot of people left

05:44.250 --> 05:45.660
because they were confused.

05:45.660 --> 05:47.850
So what they wanted was a little,

05:47.850 --> 05:50.610
basically a message where users would know

05:50.610 --> 05:53.880
to call our customer service line to better understand it.

05:53.880 --> 05:57.480
So after hearing all of this, it was pretty clear to me

05:57.480 --> 06:01.440
that the real problem wasn't that they needed an ability

06:01.440 --> 06:04.080
to put up a message really quickly for customers,

06:04.080 --> 06:07.380
the real problem was that the page was extremely misleading

06:07.380 --> 06:09.360
and causing problems in the first place.

06:09.360 --> 06:10.740
So after hearing this,

06:10.740 --> 06:13.080
I went back to the designers and developers

06:13.080 --> 06:16.350
and told them about this and we went to the drawing board

06:16.350 --> 06:18.900
and talked about how we might make this

06:18.900 --> 06:20.700
a lot clearer for customers.

06:20.700 --> 06:22.740
Once we had solved this on the webpage,

06:22.740 --> 06:24.540
it was not really a problem anymore

06:24.540 --> 06:26.070
for the merchandising department

06:26.070 --> 06:27.510
and the customers didn't need to call

06:27.510 --> 06:29.070
into customer service anymore.

06:29.070 --> 06:33.180
So problem solved, but the real problem was solved,

06:33.180 --> 06:35.520
not the initial idea or requirement

06:35.520 --> 06:37.860
given to me by another team.

06:37.860 --> 06:40.830
I wanna show you a really easy way to get to the core issue

06:40.830 --> 06:43.770
of a request and see what the actual problem is.

06:43.770 --> 06:47.190
And this is a technique that a lot of sales people use

06:47.190 --> 06:49.020
and have for a really long time.

06:49.020 --> 06:50.130
So it's really easy.

06:50.130 --> 06:51.750
You just ask, "Why?"

06:51.750 --> 06:54.690
But then you ask it two more times at least.

06:54.690 --> 06:58.800
So if someone asks you to do something, you say, "Why?"

06:58.800 --> 06:59.910
They'll tell you.

06:59.910 --> 07:02.580
You say, "Why?" again, and they'll tell you

07:02.580 --> 07:05.100
a reason that's a little bit closer to the truth.

07:05.100 --> 07:08.100
And then you say, "Why?" again, and by the end of it,

07:08.100 --> 07:10.380
they'll probably have illustrated to you

07:10.380 --> 07:12.090
what the real pain point is.

07:12.090 --> 07:14.940
It seems really obvious, but in fact, it's not,

07:14.940 --> 07:16.170
and there are so many people

07:16.170 --> 07:18.690
that just skip over this simple step.

07:18.690 --> 07:19.830
Ask why.

07:19.830 --> 07:23.730
I once took a sales class back after college,

07:23.730 --> 07:25.800
it was really, really good.

07:25.800 --> 07:30.390
And this guy that taught the course was in his early 50s.

07:30.390 --> 07:32.970
He had done some of the best sales jobs in the world

07:32.970 --> 07:35.970
and his number one piece of advice was to do this.

07:35.970 --> 07:37.320
And this technique works

07:37.320 --> 07:39.450
with product management just as well.

07:39.450 --> 07:41.880
So in this lecture, we learned that

07:41.880 --> 07:44.670
the product manager's job is not only to listen

07:44.670 --> 07:47.280
to the request the person is giving us,

07:47.280 --> 07:50.640
but to find the real pain behind the request.

07:50.640 --> 07:53.010
What is the real reason they're asking for this?

07:53.010 --> 07:54.570
We also learned that the easiest way

07:54.570 --> 07:57.807
to get to that is to continually ask, "Why?"

response.title: [ACTIVITY]: Separating the Signal from the Noise

response.description:
Think about the users of your product for a second. You want to create something for them that makes their lives better, right? Now think about your customers. Are they the same people? And if they’re not the same, do they have the same needs?  It can get confusing if you’re uncertain about the answers to these questions!

Covered in this lecture:

  • What kinds of companies might have customers that are not users.
  • The two kinds of feedback you can get and what to do with each.
  • Real world examples of users and customers.
response.title: Users vs. Customers

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.150 --> 00:01.260
-: Welcome back, everyone.

00:01.260 --> 00:03.480
So do you remember where I said that sometimes

00:03.480 --> 00:04.860
the people that pay for your product

00:04.860 --> 00:07.140
are not actually the users of your product?

00:07.140 --> 00:08.160
We're gonna talk a little bit more

00:08.160 --> 00:09.150
about that right now.

00:09.150 --> 00:10.350
So there's a difference between

00:10.350 --> 00:12.150
users and customers.

00:12.150 --> 00:14.820
And if you work as a B2B,

00:14.820 --> 00:16.770
or business-to-business product manager

00:16.770 --> 00:18.480
this might actually be pretty common.

00:18.480 --> 00:20.970
So, one of my first product management jobs

00:20.970 --> 00:22.560
was at a company that sold

00:22.560 --> 00:24.990
social media monitoring software

00:24.990 --> 00:26.790
to companies that were interested

00:26.790 --> 00:28.860
in identifying trends on social media.

00:28.860 --> 00:30.810
So the company was called Mass Relevance,

00:30.810 --> 00:32.490
and this product we sold,

00:32.490 --> 00:34.410
the ability to log into a platform

00:34.410 --> 00:36.240
and check out all these social media trends,

00:36.240 --> 00:40.200
was something that cost like two to $500,000.

00:40.200 --> 00:41.370
So it was not cheap.

00:41.370 --> 00:42.810
So the people that bought this

00:42.810 --> 00:43.890
were major companies,

00:43.890 --> 00:45.690
and the people that signed the checks for this

00:45.690 --> 00:49.380
were directors of marketing or social media,

00:49.380 --> 00:50.490
and when they bought it

00:50.490 --> 00:52.260
they were not the ones using it,

00:52.260 --> 00:53.670
but they would give the logins

00:53.670 --> 00:55.200
to their underlings,

00:55.200 --> 00:56.880
like the social media managers

00:56.880 --> 00:59.940
or the marketing managers,

00:59.940 --> 01:01.350
and you can see how these

01:01.350 --> 01:03.300
are two totally different people.

01:03.300 --> 01:05.190
So when we were getting feedback,

01:05.190 --> 01:07.230
we would get feedback from two different groups.

01:07.230 --> 01:09.270
We'd get macro-level feedback

01:09.270 --> 01:11.580
on what our products should be able to do

01:11.580 --> 01:12.450
in general.

01:12.450 --> 01:13.830
This was from the higher ups,

01:13.830 --> 01:15.270
like the directors of marketing

01:15.270 --> 01:16.170
and that sort of thing.

01:16.170 --> 01:18.840
And then we had really granular feedback

01:18.840 --> 01:20.250
from the users themselves.

01:20.250 --> 01:21.083
These are the people

01:21.083 --> 01:22.560
that were logged in every day.

01:22.560 --> 01:23.707
And they'd have feedback like,

01:23.707 --> 01:25.230
"Oh, where's the button for this?

01:25.230 --> 01:27.480
I would like to do this inside the platform."

01:27.480 --> 01:29.130
Two separate user groups

01:29.130 --> 01:30.960
and two separate sets of feedback.

01:30.960 --> 01:32.490
All right.
So, to sum it up,

01:32.490 --> 01:34.500
the users and the clients

01:34.500 --> 01:36.810
are sometimes two completely different groups

01:36.810 --> 01:38.220
with different sets of feedback,

01:38.220 --> 01:39.840
but it's important to keep in mind.

01:39.840 --> 01:41.433
Got it. All right.
Moving on.

response.description:
Say you've always thought that donuts should be stuffed with superfoods. You've wondered if there are enough garlic and spirulina lovers out there - but those who are also ok with fried donuts. If these people exist, then cha-ching. If they don't, then you'll join the ranks of the other fantastic-but-fatally-flawed-flops. But how will you know?

Sizing the market is the answer. In this lecture, we'll discuss how to count your strong-stomached-spirulina-and-donut fans and figure out whether they're likely to pay for something which is probably going to make them feel... well... gross. 

Covered in this lecture:

  • Why you need to know the size of your market.
  • The difference between top-down and bottom-up market sizing.
  • How to do top-down market sizing.
  • How to do bottom-up market sizing.
  • How to use Google to find market data.
  • Learn about a tool to help you analyze competing website traffic.
  • Why Adwords is helpful in market sizing research.
  • The benefits of using social media in your research.
response.title: Market Research - Sizing the Market

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.330 --> 00:01.163
-: Hey, everyone.

00:01.163 --> 00:01.996
Welcome back.

00:01.996 --> 00:05.550
This section is all about competitive and market analysis.

00:05.550 --> 00:06.840
A big part of product manager

00:06.840 --> 00:10.080
is making sure that the market you're attempting to address

00:10.080 --> 00:12.990
is large enough to make it worth going into.

00:12.990 --> 00:16.170
This applies if you're a product manager at a company

00:16.170 --> 00:19.560
and you're looking at building a feature or product

00:19.560 --> 00:21.810
that will put you in another market

00:21.810 --> 00:24.210
or if you're starting a new business on your own.

00:24.210 --> 00:25.980
There are two common approaches

00:25.980 --> 00:27.900
to thinking about market sizing,

00:27.900 --> 00:31.710
one of them is top down and the other one is bottom up.

00:31.710 --> 00:33.120
The top down analysis

00:33.120 --> 00:35.850
is calculated by finding the total market

00:35.850 --> 00:38.940
and then estimating what your share of the market is.

00:38.940 --> 00:40.710
Let's say that we're selling an iPhone app

00:40.710 --> 00:43.620
for, let's say ice cream connoisseurs.

00:43.620 --> 00:45.967
Top down estimate would be something like,

00:45.967 --> 00:48.450
"Okay, we know there are 100,000

00:48.450 --> 00:51.630
ice cream connoisseurs with iPhones in the United States.

00:51.630 --> 00:53.730
So if we can just get 10% of them

00:53.730 --> 00:56.340
to download our app at $5 a piece,

00:56.340 --> 00:58.140
we're gonna make $50,000."

00:58.140 --> 00:59.340
The problem with this approach

00:59.340 --> 01:01.170
is that you need to make assumptions

01:01.170 --> 01:03.990
on how much market penetration you will have

01:03.990 --> 01:05.010
inside that market.

01:05.010 --> 01:06.720
Even though it sounds reasonable,

01:06.720 --> 01:08.700
it's not the most respected way

01:08.700 --> 01:10.560
of estimating the potential market.

01:10.560 --> 01:12.270
A bottom up analysis is done

01:12.270 --> 01:15.180
by thinking about the current sales of similar products

01:15.180 --> 01:18.750
and then estimating how much of those sales you can capture.

01:18.750 --> 01:20.460
This is a much more accurate approach

01:20.460 --> 01:23.610
because it looks at patterns that exist in sales today

01:23.610 --> 01:25.200
rather than the total market.

01:25.200 --> 01:27.630
This is a better way to do it as a product manager,

01:27.630 --> 01:29.040
but it takes a little more effort

01:29.040 --> 01:30.600
and takes a little bit longer.

01:30.600 --> 01:33.000
Again, let's pretend we have an iPhone app

01:33.000 --> 01:34.560
for ice cream connoisseurs.

01:34.560 --> 01:36.120
First, we must ask ourselves

01:36.120 --> 01:38.160
where people currently buy apps

01:38.160 --> 01:40.500
related to ice cream or food.

01:40.500 --> 01:43.800
Obviously, this would be the Apple App store in this case.

01:43.800 --> 01:46.350
Next, we think about the volume of sales

01:46.350 --> 01:48.330
for existing similar apps.

01:48.330 --> 01:50.640
You may think that there's nothing similar to app,

01:50.640 --> 01:51.630
but in reality,

01:51.630 --> 01:54.630
there's probably things at least slightly similar.

01:54.630 --> 01:57.450
If there are no other ice cream connoisseur apps,

01:57.450 --> 01:58.620
we could look at other apps

01:58.620 --> 02:01.260
that have to do with other types of desserts or foods.

02:01.260 --> 02:03.420
We do it a little bit of online research

02:03.420 --> 02:06.510
or we look at websites like App Annie,

02:06.510 --> 02:08.280
and we look at the sales volumes

02:08.280 --> 02:10.950
and popularity for the existing apps out there.

02:10.950 --> 02:12.510
So let's say that the similar apps

02:12.510 --> 02:15.960
are downloaded about a thousand times per month globally.

02:15.960 --> 02:18.360
At that point, we can make a conservative assumption

02:18.360 --> 02:21.180
and say that if we get 5% of the sales

02:21.180 --> 02:22.710
that they do per month,

02:22.710 --> 02:25.050
it comes out to be about 50 downloads per month.

02:25.050 --> 02:27.210
So do you see the difference between these two approaches?

02:27.210 --> 02:30.840
The top down approach thinks about the total market size

02:30.840 --> 02:32.640
or the potential number of users,

02:32.640 --> 02:34.560
which is always quite optimistic.

02:34.560 --> 02:37.860
The bottom up market looks at the current trends and data

02:37.860 --> 02:39.300
to make sure our assumptions

02:39.300 --> 02:41.280
stay a little closer to reality.

02:41.280 --> 02:43.020
It is much better to be conservative

02:43.020 --> 02:45.450
when it comes to starting a product or business,

02:45.450 --> 02:46.710
or adding a new feature

02:46.710 --> 02:48.630
than it is to be overly optimistic.

02:48.630 --> 02:50.520
Lastly, I want to give you some tips

02:50.520 --> 02:52.440
on tools and techniques you can use

02:52.440 --> 02:54.150
to look at some market data.

02:54.150 --> 02:55.590
One of the first things you can do

02:55.590 --> 02:58.980
is just Google industry reports for X,

02:58.980 --> 03:01.740
or industry or market size for X,

03:01.740 --> 03:02.790
just using Google.

03:02.790 --> 03:06.690
This is a really good way to find already existing reports

03:06.690 --> 03:09.300
that industry research groups have put out.

03:09.300 --> 03:12.930
Secondly, if you are looking at websites of competitors

03:12.930 --> 03:14.580
or other similar apps,

03:14.580 --> 03:17.460
then you can use a website like compete.com

03:17.460 --> 03:19.320
and look at the amount of traffic

03:19.320 --> 03:21.480
the different competitive websites get.

03:21.480 --> 03:24.690
You can compare this to other ones or other industries

03:24.690 --> 03:26.840
