01:54.143 --> 01:56.670
Why? Because I'm assuming a lot of our customers

01:56.670 --> 01:59.790
are broke surfers, who don't wanna spend too much

01:59.790 --> 02:01.050
if they don't have to.

02:01.050 --> 02:02.370
Another one I think might be important

02:02.370 --> 02:04.140
is aesthetics.

02:04.140 --> 02:07.380
Why? Well, because with an action camera,

02:07.380 --> 02:09.450
you're someone who does extreme things,

02:09.450 --> 02:11.790
perhaps you're a vain person.

02:11.790 --> 02:12.810
I'm making judgements.

02:12.810 --> 02:14.070
Maybe not true.

02:14.070 --> 02:16.350
I think it's probably important that

02:16.350 --> 02:18.390
there's a cool factor to it.

02:18.390 --> 02:19.350
You really don't want it looking

02:19.350 --> 02:20.790
like the Microsoft Zune.

02:20.790 --> 02:22.740
The next one I'm gonna put is weight.

02:22.740 --> 02:24.030
Kind of spitballing on this one,

02:24.030 --> 02:25.890
but I'm gonna guess since the idea

02:25.890 --> 02:27.180
is that they're small and portable,

02:27.180 --> 02:29.130
you might wear it on your head or your chest,

02:29.130 --> 02:30.810
or something like that,

02:30.810 --> 02:33.210
that you don't want it to infringe

02:33.210 --> 02:35.220
on your ability to do whatever it is

02:35.220 --> 02:36.150
that you're trying to do.

02:36.150 --> 02:37.860
So, I'm gonna guess you don't want something

02:37.860 --> 02:39.450
that has the weight of a bowling bowl.

02:39.450 --> 02:41.100
I'm gonna put 4K, as well.

02:41.100 --> 02:43.200
That is the new, kind of, cutting edge

02:43.200 --> 02:44.370
way of filming.

02:44.370 --> 02:46.560
We are not shooting in 4K right now.

02:46.560 --> 02:48.360
If we were shooting in 4k,

02:48.360 --> 02:51.270
you would be able to zoom in and get into

02:51.270 --> 02:52.170
my pores.

02:52.170 --> 02:53.310
But for people that are doing this,

02:53.310 --> 02:54.510
I would imagine they want that.

02:54.510 --> 02:55.980
Why? Because higher resolution makes

02:55.980 --> 02:58.680
your pictures look better in general.

02:58.680 --> 03:01.230
So, I would imagine if you're gonna do

03:01.230 --> 03:02.640
something extreme or cool,

03:02.640 --> 03:04.620
you want it to look as cool as possible.

03:04.620 --> 03:07.200
The next one I'm gonna put is max FPS,

03:07.200 --> 03:09.840
which means frames per second in camera speak.

03:09.840 --> 03:12.690
What I'm getting at is can it shoot in slow motion?

03:12.690 --> 03:13.899
Right?

03:13.899 --> 03:14.940
So right now we are shooting in

03:14.940 --> 03:17.490
24 frames per second.

03:17.490 --> 03:20.250
If I shot this in 60 frames per second,

03:20.250 --> 03:23.070
I would be moving really slowly

03:23.070 --> 03:26.490
and it might look like some of those sequences

03:26.490 --> 03:28.260
you see on commercials.

03:28.260 --> 03:31.290
So, I wanna see how slow these can go.

03:31.290 --> 03:32.850
I think that's gonna be interesting because,

03:32.850 --> 03:34.950
again, people use these for action shots.

03:34.950 --> 03:37.770
They wanna slow down and watch the coolest parts.

03:37.770 --> 03:39.540
The last thing I'm gonna put is accessories.

03:39.540 --> 03:40.980
Now, accessories I think are important, right?

03:40.980 --> 03:43.950
Because a small action camera, it's small,

03:43.950 --> 03:45.090
it's kind of awkward.

03:45.090 --> 03:47.670
You need to have an easy way of putting it

03:47.670 --> 03:50.460
on your wrist, on your head, on your chest,

03:50.460 --> 03:53.070
and using a lot of tools to get

03:53.070 --> 03:54.030
what you need done.

03:54.030 --> 03:55.560
You're not gonna go grab a fork

03:55.560 --> 03:57.000
and duct tape it to it.

03:57.000 --> 03:58.260
That's just not gonna cut it.

03:58.260 --> 04:00.270
Now, there are obviously some things I ignored

04:00.270 --> 04:01.920
and these are just decisions I made.

04:01.920 --> 04:03.810
I ignored things like battery life.

04:03.810 --> 04:06.090
Ignored things like the ability

04:06.090 --> 04:08.790
to edit on the actual camera,

04:08.790 --> 04:10.380
which is apparently a feature amongst

04:10.380 --> 04:12.000
the action cameras.

04:12.000 --> 04:13.740
I also decided on camera controls

04:13.740 --> 04:15.780
probably weren't that important.

04:15.780 --> 04:17.580
I don't really know how important it is

04:17.580 --> 04:20.520
to be able to scrub through what you're filming

04:20.520 --> 04:22.320
on the little box.

04:22.320 --> 04:23.880
It just seems like all you need to do is

04:23.880 --> 04:25.710
press record and go.

04:25.710 --> 04:26.670
Now, I'm making these judgements

04:26.670 --> 04:28.710
because GoPro users probably want

04:28.710 --> 04:30.300
the cutting edge.

04:30.300 --> 04:32.880
They want to film really cool stuff

04:32.880 --> 04:35.160
in short periods of time.

04:35.160 --> 04:36.540
That's why I don't think battery life

04:36.540 --> 04:37.830
is gonna be tops for them.

04:37.830 --> 04:39.540
I could totally be wrong.

04:39.540 --> 04:41.250
And I don't think editing on the thing

04:41.250 --> 04:42.270
is gonna make sense

04:42.270 --> 04:44.370
because people use GoPros probably want

04:44.370 --> 04:46.770
to make a montage or a video that looks

04:46.770 --> 04:47.760
as good as possible.

04:47.760 --> 04:48.990
That's not something you're gonna get editing

04:48.990 --> 04:50.190
on a tiny little box.

04:50.190 --> 04:51.960
Okay? I was lucky to get all this information

04:51.960 --> 04:53.879
just from individual websites and

04:53.879 --> 04:58.290
a Top 10 Competitors comparison website

04:58.290 --> 04:59.550
relatively easy.

04:59.550 --> 05:01.350
For you guys, it's all gonna be easy as well.

05:01.350 --> 05:03.450
Just go to the actual competitor's website.

05:03.450 --> 05:04.890
They'll tell you everything you need to know.

05:04.890 --> 05:06.840
Again, in reality, what I would probably do

05:06.840 --> 05:09.570
is go out, read consumer reviews,

05:09.570 --> 05:12.630
talk to customers, see what they experienced.

05:12.630 --> 05:14.700
And most importantly, I would play with

05:14.700 --> 05:16.440
the actual product.

05:16.440 --> 05:18.960
There's no substitute for you actually

05:18.960 --> 05:21.270
using their product and making a judgment.

05:21.270 --> 05:22.960
All right, let's fill it out for the GoPro.

05:22.960 --> 05:25.008
The GoPro cost close to $500.

05:25.008 --> 05:26.010
499.

05:26.010 --> 05:28.740
Aesthetics, they're all kind of ugly.

05:28.740 --> 05:30.630
I think there's some improvement.

05:30.630 --> 05:31.860
But for the most part,

05:31.860 --> 05:34.590
I think it's probably like a seven out of 10.

05:34.590 --> 05:36.240
It's better than a lot of them.

05:36.240 --> 05:39.660
Weight 89 grams, that means nothing to me right now.

05:39.660 --> 05:40.650
Does it shoot in 4k?

05:40.650 --> 05:42.360
Yes, it actually does shoot in 4k.

05:42.360 --> 05:45.006
And I'm gonna put a secondary piece of information,

05:45.006 --> 05:46.950
which is that it can shoot up to

05:46.950 --> 05:48.780
30 frames per second when it's in that

05:48.780 --> 05:50.010
high resolution format.

05:50.010 --> 05:53.400
Max FPS, I just put yes, because it can

05:53.400 --> 05:55.320
go above the normal FPS.

05:55.320 --> 05:57.240
And it can go up to 120 if you're shooting

05:57.240 --> 05:58.440
in 1080p.

05:58.440 --> 05:59.910
For those of you who don't know what that means,

05:59.910 --> 06:01.290
just don't worry about it.

06:01.290 --> 06:02.910
Accessories, I put a hundred plus

06:02.910 --> 06:04.170
because when you search on Amazon,

06:04.170 --> 06:06.930
there are an enormous amount of accessories

06:06.930 --> 06:08.130
for GoPros.

06:08.130 --> 06:08.963
Okay, cool.

06:08.963 --> 06:10.860
So, now let's just start filling them out.

06:10.860 --> 06:11.730
TomTom Bandit.

06:11.730 --> 06:13.650
This is what I gathered from their website.

06:13.650 --> 06:16.020
They cost $349.

06:16.020 --> 06:18.960
Pretty expensive, but cheaper than the GoPro.

06:18.960 --> 06:20.820
Aesthetics, I would give them a four out of 10.

06:20.820 --> 06:24.270
Again, I said they look like a plastic hot dog

06:24.270 --> 06:27.720
or a submarine with a big eyeball in the front.

06:27.720 --> 06:29.430
I honestly don't know what they're going for.

06:29.430 --> 06:31.290
You just run around with it like this.

06:31.290 --> 06:33.300
And just point it at people like a laser pointer.

06:33.300 --> 06:35.970
Weight, 190 grams.

06:35.970 --> 06:37.320
I'm not sure what that means,

06:37.320 --> 06:39.630
but in relative comparison to the GoPro,

06:39.630 --> 06:41.610
that's a big honking item.

06:41.610 --> 06:43.260
4k? Yes, it does do it.

06:43.260 --> 06:46.170
But it only shoots in 12 frames per second.

06:46.170 --> 06:47.340
That's questionable.

06:47.340 --> 06:51.780
The max FPS is 60 frames per second versus our 120.

06:51.780 --> 06:53.070
And does it have accessories?

06:53.070 --> 06:54.330
Yes, but not very many.

06:54.330 --> 06:55.860
Only about 10 I could find.

06:55.860 --> 06:57.194
Moving on to the Veho.

06:57.194 --> 06:59.187
The Veho is cheap. It's $200.

06:59.187 --> 07:01.650
The aesthetics are very similar to the GoPro.

07:01.650 --> 07:03.840
It's just a little bit worse.

07:03.840 --> 07:05.520
So, I give it a six out of 10.

07:05.520 --> 07:07.440
The weight is 84 grams.

07:07.440 --> 07:08.850
Can it shoot in 4k?

07:08.850 --> 07:09.930
No, it cannot.

07:09.930 --> 07:11.310
The max FPS is, again,

07:11.310 --> 07:13.140
like our favorite plastic hotdog,

07:13.140 --> 07:15.180
it's about 60 frames per second.

07:15.180 --> 07:16.470
Does it have accessories?

07:16.470 --> 07:20.370
More than the hot dog but still not a ton.

07:20.370 --> 07:22.260
I think I found around 15-ish.

07:22.260 --> 07:23.093
And the last one,

07:23.093 --> 07:25.375
the Sony Franklin Delano Roosevelt.

07:25.375 --> 07:27.600
That one is probably the closest competitor.

07:27.600 --> 07:30.600
This one is $399.

07:30.600 --> 07:32.940
Aesthetics, I give it a six out of 10.

07:32.940 --> 07:34.200
Doesn't look as good as the GoPro,

07:34.200 --> 07:35.220
but it's not that bad.

07:35.220 --> 07:37.380
Weight, it's 114 grams.

07:37.380 --> 07:39.270
It can shoot in 4K and it can shoot

07:39.270 --> 07:40.500
up to 30 frames per second,

07:40.500 --> 07:42.930
compared to the GoPro, do the exact same thing.

07:42.930 --> 07:45.180
The max FPS is 120.

07:45.180 --> 07:47.010
And the number of accessories I could find

07:47.010 --> 07:49.560
was over the 25 range.

07:49.560 --> 07:50.610
Okay, there you have it.

07:50.610 --> 07:53.280
Now, what we could do is internalize this

07:53.280 --> 07:55.980
and understand are we beating our

07:55.980 --> 07:56.813
direct competitors?

07:56.813 --> 07:58.650
Are we the obvious choice?

07:58.650 --> 08:02.010
Or are we only appealing to one segment?

08:02.010 --> 08:03.870
Are there areas we should focus our energy

08:03.870 --> 08:05.520
on improving?

08:05.520 --> 08:08.160
Are there areas that we should just entirely ignore?

08:08.160 --> 08:10.440
I would love to hear you guys' thoughts on this.

08:10.440 --> 08:11.273
Answer that question.

08:11.273 --> 08:12.106
What do you think?

08:12.106 --> 08:14.340
What should GoPro do based off of

08:14.340 --> 08:15.450
what we just filled out?

08:15.450 --> 08:16.740
Post it in the group discussion.

08:16.740 --> 08:17.640
We'll talk about it.

08:17.640 --> 08:19.620
In the next lecture, we're gonna do this again

08:19.620 --> 08:22.380
but we're gonna do it in a more granular way.

08:22.380 --> 08:23.550
We're gonna make a feature table

08:23.550 --> 08:26.670
for one specific proposed feature

08:26.670 --> 08:28.170
and we're gonna use a platform that

08:28.170 --> 08:29.670
everyone's familiar with, Udemy.

08:29.670 --> 08:31.373
All right, see you in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.000 --> 00:01.200
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:01.200 --> 00:04.320
So in this lecture we're gonna do more feature tables

00:04.320 --> 00:05.700
but we're gonna do it this time

00:05.700 --> 00:07.950
from a different angle in a different way.

00:07.950 --> 00:09.240
Now watching the last two lectures

00:09.240 --> 00:12.060
you probably think that feature tables are only helpful

00:12.060 --> 00:16.980
for comparing large products on everything they do

00:16.980 --> 00:20.280
to other products and everything they do.

00:20.280 --> 00:21.570
And that's not actually the case.

00:21.570 --> 00:22.440
The other feature tables

00:22.440 --> 00:24.630
they kind of look like post-mortems.

00:24.630 --> 00:27.930
They look like after you've built your product, you know

00:27.930 --> 00:29.760
what do we need to work on,

00:29.760 --> 00:33.420
improve, or deprioritized compared

00:33.420 --> 00:35.880
to what our other competitors are doing?

00:35.880 --> 00:37.860
And that's really important as a product manager

00:37.860 --> 00:39.420
you're constantly doing that

00:39.420 --> 00:43.634
and you will have a large product to hold on to,

00:43.634 --> 00:47.250
maintain, and make sure that you're not losing ground.

00:47.250 --> 00:49.800
But how do we use this technique going forward?

00:49.800 --> 00:52.470
Let's say we're proposing a feature,

00:52.470 --> 00:54.690
proposing a new product.

00:54.690 --> 00:58.410
Can we use this as a way to determine what we should put

00:58.410 --> 01:01.260
into our product or know whether or not it's feasible?

01:01.260 --> 01:03.210
So let's do just that.

01:03.210 --> 01:06.300
We're gonna use this now for a proposed feature.

01:06.300 --> 01:08.910
And we'll go back to our other example, not GoPro.

01:08.910 --> 01:09.750
We're gonna use Udemy.

01:09.750 --> 01:11.160
Now you're using Udemy

01:11.160 --> 01:14.040
online marketplace for education.

01:14.040 --> 01:16.680
Anybody can create a course, anybody can take one.

01:16.680 --> 01:19.380
And for the most part, they're expensive

01:19.380 --> 01:21.780
but they also discount very cheaply.

01:21.780 --> 01:24.660
Let's say you're a product manager at Udemy, okay?

01:24.660 --> 01:26.880
And we're gonna imagine that we're gonna add the

01:26.880 --> 01:31.530
feature that we had previously, which is group discussions.

01:31.530 --> 01:35.460
We think it should be interesting for students to interact

01:35.460 --> 01:38.490
with the course in a different way, post discussions.

01:38.490 --> 01:41.130
And that way the instructor can post back

01:41.130 --> 01:43.380
or other students can post to it.

01:43.380 --> 01:44.850
Now, what would you think

01:44.850 --> 01:47.130
would be important to that feature?

01:47.130 --> 01:51.510
So answer those at home while I make my feature table.

01:51.510 --> 01:53.370
It's gonna take a little bit.

01:53.370 --> 01:54.708
Cause this time I wanna make sure

01:54.708 --> 01:57.690
that the lines are actually somewhat straight.

01:57.690 --> 01:59.340
Okay, on the left, let's start with some

01:59.340 --> 02:01.890
of the aspects that we think would be important

02:01.890 --> 02:05.610
in a group discussion or a lecture discussion feature.

02:05.610 --> 02:06.960
What we're gonna do is we're gonna fill these

02:06.960 --> 02:09.630
out and then we're gonna fill out our competitors

02:09.630 --> 02:12.210
and then we're going to see what they have

02:12.210 --> 02:14.610
and offer before deciding what we're gonna do.

02:14.610 --> 02:16.380
So the first thing I'd like to ask is

02:16.380 --> 02:19.830
can you actually post a discussion for a lecture?

02:19.830 --> 02:22.530
I'm assuming some competitors might not actually have that.

02:22.530 --> 02:25.418
Another aspect I think would be important is time markers.

02:25.418 --> 02:28.800
If I put a group discussion, ask a question

02:28.800 --> 02:30.450
can I tie that specifically

02:30.450 --> 02:33.030
to one specific piece of the lecture?

02:33.030 --> 02:34.470
So it's not just generic,

02:34.470 --> 02:35.700
you don't know what you're referring to.

02:35.700 --> 02:36.810
The next one I think is important

02:36.810 --> 02:38.820
are probably engagement features.

02:38.820 --> 02:39.653
If I'm posting

02:39.653 --> 02:42.060
it's a good thing to get other students to post.

02:42.060 --> 02:44.940
And what better way to do that than have things like likes

02:44.940 --> 02:48.420
and shares and different ways that people can engage

02:48.420 --> 02:50.790
and say whether or not they like the content.

02:50.790 --> 02:53.070
The next one I think might be important is location.

02:53.070 --> 02:54.675
It might be important

02:54.675 --> 02:57.060
for our group discussion that everyone knows where it is

02:57.060 --> 02:59.968
and it's prominent, otherwise people might not use it.

02:59.968 --> 03:01.740
And the last one I'm gonna put is post images.

03:01.740 --> 03:02.820
Can you post images?

03:02.820 --> 03:03.990
I think that would be interesting.

03:03.990 --> 03:06.840
It could make the discussions more enriched.

03:06.840 --> 03:08.100
Something I would guess that's probably

03:08.100 --> 03:10.260
par for the course for a group discussion tool.

03:10.260 --> 03:11.340
Okay, And so I'm just gonna fill

03:11.340 --> 03:13.260
out some of the competitors based off

03:13.260 --> 03:16.830
of the list that I made before looking for Udemy.

03:16.830 --> 03:19.110
And they're the usual suspects.

Skillshare is probably the first one that comes to mind.

Then we probably have Coursera

03:25.256 --> 03:27.480
which is quite a bit different, but still a competitor.

03:27.480 --> 03:28.650
Then we have Udacity.

03:28.650 --> 03:31.830

Again, solutions different, but they go

11:22.650 --> 11:24.180
do we need to make changes to the feature

11:24.180 --> 11:25.500
we've already implemented?

11:25.500 --> 11:26.760
Is it important?

11:26.760 --> 11:31.380
How far away are we from becoming the best in this category?

11:31.380 --> 11:32.760
And should we care?

11:32.760 --> 11:33.593
Alright, guys.

11:33.593 --> 11:34.426
In the next couple lectures we're gonna talk

11:34.426 --> 11:36.420
about analyzing your competitors.

11:36.420 --> 11:38.550
Things to keep in mind as a product manager to

11:38.550 --> 11:41.280
make sure you're looking in the right direction.

11:41.280 --> 11:42.680
See you in the next lecture.

response.description:
Wunderlist does wunders (get it?) for many things from recording to-do lists and setting repeat reminders to keeping your project team on track. Launched in 2011, they had over 13 million users before being acquired by Microsoft in 2015 (and quietly killed off, sadly)

But before they were "old yeller-ed", Wunderlist had a classic Product Management dilemma. 

This dilemma happens CONSTANTLY all over the world to companies of all different stripes.

The dilemma:

They had a free version and a paid version. In order to continue to grow their user base, they needed to have a competitive feature set for their free version. But in order to make money and sustain their business, they needed to be able to convert those users into Paid customers, which most likely meant withholding features from their free version.

Competition in the space was always red hot, with apps like Evernote and Trello constantly one-upping themselves with more and more free features. Wunderlist was being squeezed at both ends of their customer funnel.

In this case study, I want you to put on your PM hat, travel back in time, and pretend you're making the call for Wunderlist's product team. Could a different strategy have ended with a different outcome? Use the data and the feature table provided to make the call - what would you have done in their situation?

Choose wisely.
response.title: [ACTIVITY] Wunderlist and Their Not-So-Wunderful Dilemma

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.360 --> 00:01.193
-: Hey guys.

00:01.193 --> 00:02.026
Welcome back to the course.

00:02.026 --> 00:04.410
So in the previous lectures in this section

00:04.410 --> 00:07.710
we covered how to find competitors,

00:07.710 --> 00:09.300
gather information on them,

00:09.300 --> 00:11.880
figure out if they're direct or indirect,

00:11.880 --> 00:13.830
and then compare them.

00:13.830 --> 00:15.240
Now, those are all crucial skills

00:15.240 --> 00:18.150
for gathering the information you need to make judgments

00:18.150 --> 00:19.560
on your competitors.

00:19.560 --> 00:23.730
However, competitors are always going to be there

00:23.730 --> 00:26.670
if not forever, at least for a while

00:26.670 --> 00:29.370
and like you, competitors will change.

00:29.370 --> 00:32.220
Competition is an ever changing landscape

00:32.220 --> 00:34.380
and as a good product manager

00:34.380 --> 00:37.050
which is what you want to be, right?

00:37.050 --> 00:40.080
You want to keep tabs on your competition

00:40.080 --> 00:43.140
so that as soon as they make a change,

00:43.140 --> 00:45.840
you can redirect or adjust.

00:45.840 --> 00:47.760
Now remember the five dimensions

00:47.760 --> 00:50.498
that we judge our competitors on.

00:50.498 --> 00:52.260
We have the product team.

00:52.260 --> 00:55.170
How good are they or how bad are they?

00:55.170 --> 00:57.030
We have design, is it pretty?

00:57.030 --> 00:58.260
We have brand.

00:58.260 --> 01:00.180
How important is their brand?

01:00.180 --> 01:02.850
Is it a positive asset for them?

01:02.850 --> 01:07.020
Is it so strong it prevents you from doing things?

01:07.020 --> 01:09.300
How big is their user size?

01:09.300 --> 01:11.850
Is it big or is it just girthy?

01:11.850 --> 01:13.200
And the last one is speed.

01:13.200 --> 01:15.600
How much of it are they on?

01:15.600 --> 01:18.270
Now, those are the five criteria that we wanna keep

01:18.270 --> 01:20.548
in our minds constantly about our competitors

01:20.548 --> 01:23.580
and it's always going to change.

01:23.580 --> 01:25.530
They're gonna make changes

01:25.530 --> 01:29.970
and take initiatives that will increase certain ones

01:29.970 --> 01:32.970
of the five, and sometimes they'll make mistakes

01:32.970 --> 01:36.930
and they'll lose some ground on some of those five

01:36.930 --> 01:40.050
and you need to be there to know when it happens.

01:40.050 --> 01:41.220
So in this lecture, we're gonna cover

01:41.220 --> 01:44.310
three different events that you should

01:44.310 --> 01:46.470
constantly be looking for

01:46.470 --> 01:48.630
and we're gonna cover three different ways

01:48.630 --> 01:52.710
that you can keep tabs on if those events happen.

01:52.710 --> 01:53.637
All right.

01:53.637 --> 01:54.470
Now there obviously a ton

01:54.470 --> 01:57.420
of tiny little things that can happen with your competitors

01:57.420 --> 01:59.040
and it's worthwhile to know

01:59.040 --> 02:00.840
maybe they move a button over here,

02:00.840 --> 02:03.870
maybe they make a big change to their web app.

02:03.870 --> 02:06.150
Whatever it is, it's all worth knowing.

02:06.150 --> 02:08.638
But there are three things as a product manager

02:08.638 --> 02:11.580
that I think stick out more than the others

02:11.580 --> 02:14.700
because they have the ability and the potential

02:14.700 --> 02:17.040
to change all of those five criteria

02:17.040 --> 02:18.420
we previously mentioned.

02:18.420 --> 02:21.030
So the first one is funding.

02:21.030 --> 02:23.730
Now, this is not really something I've talked a ton

02:23.730 --> 02:27.000
about before, but a lot of companies receive money.

02:27.000 --> 02:28.470
If you're in the startup space,

02:28.470 --> 02:31.200
a lot of companies receive venture capital.

02:31.200 --> 02:33.150
The amount of money that's in their bank account

02:33.150 --> 02:35.100
can make an enormous difference

02:35.100 --> 02:38.070
for a number of our five variables.

02:38.070 --> 02:40.410
More money means they can hire more people.

02:40.410 --> 02:44.490
More money means they can spend more money on ads,

02:44.490 --> 02:46.837
means that they can hire more contractors,

02:46.837 --> 02:49.410
they can pay for more press,

02:49.410 --> 02:53.760
so that means that they can dramatically change their speed.

02:53.760 --> 02:55.290
More money also could mean

02:55.290 --> 02:59.250
that they now have more to work with for recruiting

02:59.250 --> 03:00.240
for their product team.

03:00.240 --> 03:02.280
And so you should keep your eye out

03:02.280 --> 03:05.070
in case their product team improves.

03:05.070 --> 03:07.350
It does small changes to brand.

03:07.350 --> 03:09.150
It doesn't necessarily change their design

03:09.150 --> 03:10.530
but it could allow them to bring on

03:10.530 --> 03:14.670
people who can add more design and usability

03:14.670 --> 03:16.080
and it also could

03:16.080 --> 03:18.360
or could not have an effect with user size.

03:18.360 --> 03:21.540
Oftentimes, especially with startups, when they raise money

03:21.540 --> 03:22.950
they do spend a lot

03:22.950 --> 03:27.510
of it on advertisement and growing their user base.

03:27.510 --> 03:30.480
Alright, but how do you know when they get funding?

03:30.480 --> 03:32.220
Where are you supposed to put your ear

03:32.220 --> 03:34.470
to the ground for this?

03:34.470 --> 03:35.970
Well, actually it's pretty easy.

03:35.970 --> 03:37.620
Generally when companies get funded,

03:37.620 --> 03:40.680
there's a website that keeps track of all of that for us,

03:40.680 --> 03:41.760
and it's incredibly useful.

03:41.760 --> 03:43.963
It's called Crunch Base, So let's check it out.

03:43.963 --> 03:45.450
This is what Crunch Base looks like.

03:45.450 --> 03:46.710
Just crunchbase.com.

03:46.710 --> 03:49.290
All they do is keep track of

03:49.290 --> 03:51.660
how much money people have raised.

03:51.660 --> 03:53.040
All you have to do is search up here

03:53.040 --> 03:57.060
for the organization that you're trying to keep tabs on.

03:57.060 --> 04:01.290
Let's say I worked for Spotify, sorry again, Cole.

04:01.290 --> 04:03.630
I wanted to keep track of SoundCloud.

04:03.630 --> 04:06.090
SoundCloud honestly is the least of their worries.

04:06.090 --> 04:08.250
I'd be more worried about Apple Music,

04:08.250 --> 04:10.500
but let's say that we wanted to keep tabs on them.

04:10.500 --> 04:13.290
Right here we can see information about them.

04:13.290 --> 04:15.840
All this stuff is really nice

04:15.840 --> 04:18.622
but the most important thing is it gives us a timeline

04:18.622 --> 04:21.660
of their funding right here.

04:21.660 --> 04:25.140
So not only can we get historical information,

04:25.140 --> 04:28.170
we can also see when this gets updated,

04:28.170 --> 04:30.060
and that'll tell us that they just received

04:30.060 --> 04:31.260
a new round of funding.

04:31.260 --> 04:33.240
So how do I get notified when this happens?

04:33.240 --> 04:34.073
Well, it's pretty easy.

04:34.073 --> 04:34.980
You just hit follow

04:34.980 --> 04:37.230
and then it'll ask you to create an account.

04:37.230 --> 04:40.800
Do that, and then you'll get emails directly

04:40.800 --> 04:44.160
to your inbox every time something major happens

04:44.160 --> 04:47.220
with your competitors, specifically, they're funding.

04:47.220 --> 04:48.053
There you go.

04:48.053 --> 04:52.110
Another thing that Crunch Base is really, really helpful for

04:52.110 --> 04:55.230
happens to be our number two thing

04:55.230 --> 04:59.550
that we want to look out for, and that is acquisitions.

04:59.550 --> 05:01.140
Acquisitions, if you're not familiar,

05:01.140 --> 05:03.360
is when a company buys another company.

05:03.360 --> 05:06.090
Imagine a really big fish and a really small fish

05:06.090 --> 05:08.280
and then the big fish eats the small fish.

05:08.280 --> 05:12.060
Except in this scenario, that small fish actually attaches

05:12.060 --> 05:14.580
to the big fish and makes it larger.

05:14.580 --> 05:16.590
Kind of like the angler fish.

05:16.590 --> 05:18.300
I know that's random, but look it up.

05:18.300 --> 05:19.950
It's the creepiest thing in the world.

05:19.950 --> 05:21.840
What a company buys another company.

05:21.840 --> 05:24.750
They're doing it for a number of reasons

05:24.750 --> 05:26.100
and it usually comes down

05:26.100 --> 05:29.370
to the five reasons that we care about.

05:29.370 --> 05:31.620
Oftentimes, companies will buy another company

05:31.620 --> 05:34.350
because they have a good product team

05:34.350 --> 05:37.655
and they want to integrate that into their own team.

05:37.655 --> 05:39.840
Sometimes they wanna buy a company

05:39.840 --> 05:42.270
because they have a lot of users

05:42.270 --> 05:44.430
and they would like to integrate those users

05:44.430 --> 05:45.960
into their own user base or

05:45.960 --> 05:48.930
at least cross sell them information

05:48.930 --> 05:51.720
or gather information on their users.

05:51.720 --> 05:53.490
Either way, bad for you.

05:53.490 --> 05:56.070
They could also buy them because of design.

05:56.070 --> 05:57.330
There are examples

05:57.330 --> 05:59.730
of companies that have bought other companies to

05:59.730 --> 06:02.460
absorb their design team.

06:02.460 --> 06:04.080
Remember how we talked about Path?

06:04.080 --> 06:07.230
Path got bought for pretty much that reason.

06:07.230 --> 06:08.063
The only other two things,

06:08.063 --> 06:10.260
it doesn't necessarily affect our brand

06:10.260 --> 06:12.360
unless acquisitions are prestigious,

06:12.360 --> 06:15.180
I mean they kind of are, or speed.

06:15.180 --> 06:16.770
Speed is tough.

06:16.770 --> 06:19.260
Oftentimes companies will buy another company

06:19.260 --> 06:23.400
because that company seems to get stuff done really quickly.

06:23.400 --> 06:26.760
But you kind of run into a problem

06:26.760 --> 06:29.220
where larger organizations as a whole

06:29.220 --> 06:34.020
as they get larger and larger, get slower and slower.

06:34.020 --> 06:36.720
So it's ah, I don't know, it's kind of a wash.

06:36.720 --> 06:39.420
How we find acquisitions is you just go here.

06:39.420 --> 06:42.450
That gives us all their acquisitions, gives us the date.

06:42.450 --> 06:43.800
We can actually click through

06:43.800 --> 06:46.920
and read about the company if we want to

06:46.920 --> 06:50.580
and since we're already following it, we will get an update.

06:50.580 --> 06:52.290
It'll even give us the terms of it.

06:52.290 --> 06:53.520
That's pretty cool.

06:53.520 --> 06:56.280
And the last event that we really care about

06:56.280 --> 06:59.518
are feature or new product launches.

06:59.518 --> 07:00.480
Why?

07:00.480 --> 07:03.390
Well, because those are major maneuvers

07:03.390 --> 07:05.190
that your competitors are making

07:05.190 --> 07:08.880
and you absolutely have to know if they're doing it

07:08.880 --> 07:12.990
especially if they make a product or if new feature that is

07:12.990 --> 07:16.620
in some way competing directly with you or copying you.

07:16.620 --> 07:19.050
The best way of keeping tabs on that and a number

07:19.050 --> 07:22.170
of other things, it's a website called mention.com.

07:22.170 --> 07:25.920
Now, it's not free but it is incredibly useful

07:25.920 --> 07:28.080
and it has a free trial, more importantly.

07:28.080 --> 07:30.810
What mention.com does is it

07:30.810 --> 07:34.830
tracks all of your competitor's social media accounts.

07:34.830 --> 07:39.830
It looks at chatter between customers and the company.

07:39.900 --> 07:42.180
It looks for major updates

07:42.180 --> 07:46.920
on the company's blogs, any large seismic shifts.

07:46.920 --> 07:48.840
They will email you and you can even

07:48.840 --> 07:52.350
set your preferences to tell it how often to email.

07:52.350 --> 07:53.940
Only email you when big things happen

07:53.940 --> 07:57.030
or email me anytime anything moves at all.

07:57.030 --> 07:59.100
Let's check out Mention really quickly.

07:59.100 --> 08:00.330
Sign up, it's free

08:00.330 --> 08:03.030
and then you can create your first alert.

08:03.030 --> 08:06.330
So all you wanna do is tell it what you're trying

08:06.330 --> 08:08.700
to train their listening system to.

08:08.700 --> 08:10.620
So we're looking for a competitor.

08:10.620 --> 08:12.330
Tell 'em the name of your competitor.

08:12.330 --> 08:14.010
We'll say SoundCloud.

08:14.010 --> 08:15.010
Oh, there we go.

08:15.010 --> 08:16.740
And move on to the next step.

08:16.740 --> 08:20.130
Here we can set our priorities, what we care about the most.

08:20.130 --> 08:21.210
If you're more concerned

08:21.210 --> 08:24.150
about what they say in their social channels

08:24.150 --> 08:27.570
you can make sure to listen to that more, add more of them.

08:27.570 --> 08:28.590
But we're just looking

08:28.590 --> 08:30.690
for feature changes, product launches.

08:30.690 --> 08:33.540
So when those happen, it's gonna be a seismic shift.

08:33.540 --> 08:34.860
They're gonna have a blog post about it.

08:34.860 --> 08:36.240
They're gonna have a Twitter post

08:36.240 --> 08:38.760
and probably a Facebook post as well.

08:38.760 --> 08:39.963
You'll get one of them.

08:40.830 --> 08:42.060
So just move on.

08:42.060 --> 08:44.640
You can pick what all you want to pull in.

08:44.640 --> 08:47.790
This is incredibly useful for the news setting.

08:47.790 --> 08:48.623
Why?

08:48.623 --> 08:51.480
Because a lot of times you might not see the feature launch

08:51.480 --> 08:54.150
but news will pick up on it

08:54.150 --> 08:57.429
or news will pick up on it way before it launches.

08:57.429 --> 08:58.680
That's really helpful.

08:58.680 --> 08:59.970
Also, for tracking things

08:59.970 --> 09:02.250
like perception which affect their brand.

09:02.250 --> 09:05.010
Move on to check out your results.

09:05.010 --> 09:05.970
We're almost done.

09:05.970 --> 09:07.290
And there we go.

09:07.290 --> 09:08.760
Our account is already set up.

09:08.760 --> 09:11.850
Look, we have 76 new mentions for the alert SoundCloud.

09:11.850 --> 09:12.683
Alright, so it went out

09:12.683 --> 09:15.510
and gathered quite a lot of information about SoundCloud.

09:15.510 --> 09:17.670
It's going back in time.

09:17.670 --> 09:19.680
If I wanted to look at the mentions

09:19.680 --> 09:22.320
I would just go here and then I can just scroll

09:22.320 --> 09:26.460
down this feed and I can check it out.

09:26.460 --> 09:28.800
Here I can search and I can filter through it.

09:28.800 --> 09:30.660
There are a lot of really cool tools

09:30.660 --> 09:34.260
for picking out the signal and the noise, so to speak.

09:34.260 --> 09:35.310
You can set things

09:35.310 --> 09:38.640
like keywords you're looking for, or even exclude keywords.

09:38.640 --> 09:42.000
So certain things don't ever show up in your feed.

09:42.000 --> 09:43.200
You can also, in your settings

09:43.200 --> 09:45.069
change how often they email you

09:45.069 --> 09:47.580
and what type of interactions

09:47.580 --> 09:50.130
they consider important and will notify you about.

09:50.130 --> 09:50.963
Alright, cool.

09:50.963 --> 09:52.440
Mention as a very powerful tool

09:52.440 --> 09:54.540
check out what you can use it for.

09:54.540 --> 09:57.270
You can find 1,000,001 things that you can do with it.

09:57.270 --> 10:01.020
This is fantastic for figuring out when they get money,

10:01.020 --> 10:04.290
when they get acquisitions, and when they launch new things.

10:04.290 --> 10:07.170
And the last thing we're gonna cover is Google alerts.

10:07.170 --> 10:10.680
Google Alerts is not as powerful as Mention

10:10.680 --> 10:13.260
but it's a broader blanket.

10:13.260 --> 10:14.220
All you have to do is type

10:14.220 --> 10:16.200
in the company name that you wanna stock.

10:16.200 --> 10:19.110
And what it's gonna do is it's going to

10:19.110 --> 10:22.080
frequently email you and notify you.

10:22.080 --> 10:23.720
If you wanna do desktop notifications

10:23.720 --> 10:28.680
of anytime new search results show up for your competitor.

10:28.680 --> 10:31.140
That is the broadest sweep you can use.

10:31.140 --> 10:34.770
Incredibly useful though to see how their SEO changes,

10:34.770 --> 10:38.910
to see how anything is popping up, conversations,

10:38.910 --> 10:41.520
things way out there you might not have seen.

10:41.520 --> 10:43.170
All this information, really helpful.

10:43.170 --> 10:44.003
Alright guys.

10:44.003 --> 10:45.750
Keep track of your competitors 'cause if you don't

10:45.750 --> 10:47.160
they might get outta your sights

10:47.160 --> 10:49.305
and it'll come back to bite you later.

10:49.305 --> 10:51.333
Alright, see you in the next lecture.

response.title: What Do We Ultimately Care About as a PM?

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.270 --> 00:01.140
-: Hey, everyone.

00:01.140 --> 00:05.070
This section was all about competition and market analysis.

00:05.070 --> 00:06.690
What you learned from Evan's lectures

00:06.690 --> 00:09.120
is stuff that's really vital to understand

00:09.120 --> 00:11.880
if you're developing a new product or company from scratch.

00:11.880 --> 00:14.430
However, if you are a product manager at a company

00:14.430 --> 00:16.590
or looking to get a job as one,

00:16.590 --> 00:18.990
this stuff will be extremely important to understand

00:18.990 --> 00:20.460
from a theoretical level.

00:20.460 --> 00:21.810
Like we've mentioned before,

00:21.810 --> 00:24.210
if you're working as a product manager at a company

00:24.210 --> 00:26.610
and they don't know who their competitors are,

00:26.610 --> 00:27.810
then that's a red flag.

00:27.810 --> 00:30.210
The people you work with and, of course, the executives

00:30.210 --> 00:33.300
will know exactly who the competitors currently are,

00:33.300 --> 00:35.430
or if there are none yet then they will know

00:35.430 --> 00:38.730
who is in a position to start competing with you soon.

00:38.730 --> 00:41.340
The biggest tip I can give you as a product manager

00:41.340 --> 00:43.920
when it comes to competition and market analysis

00:43.920 --> 00:46.620
is to stay on top of industry news

00:46.620 --> 00:49.290
and pay very close attention to the features

00:49.290 --> 00:50.700
that competitors build.

00:50.700 --> 00:53.160
It's also great to look across industries

00:53.160 --> 00:54.600
at similar patterns,

00:54.600 --> 00:57.360
even if the company does not directly compete with you.

00:57.360 --> 00:58.530
Here's an example of that.

00:58.530 --> 01:00.120
If I'm building social features

01:00.120 --> 01:03.450
for an app that lets people read books on their smartphone,

01:03.450 --> 01:06.510
I don't only want to look at other smartphone book apps.

01:06.510 --> 01:09.600
I should also look at other apps that are social in general,

01:09.600 --> 01:12.060
like Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram

01:12.060 --> 01:13.650
to see what they have in common,

01:13.650 --> 01:15.570
how users are interacting with them,

01:15.570 --> 01:17.550
and what I might learn from the lessons

01:17.550 --> 01:18.750
they've already learned,

01:18.750 --> 01:20.400
since they've already been doing social.

01:20.400 --> 01:22.350
Overall, if you're always up to date

01:22.350 --> 01:23.700
on what competitors are doing

01:23.700 --> 01:26.580
and have informed opinions about where they will go next,

01:26.580 --> 01:28.500
you will gain respect from coworkers

01:28.500 --> 01:31.200
and go really far in the role of product management.

01:31.200 --> 01:32.160
So, that's it for this one.

01:32.160 --> 01:34.010
I'll see you all in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.150 --> 00:02.370
-: Hey guys, welcome to the next section.

00:02.370 --> 00:04.380
Super proud of you for making it this far.

00:04.380 --> 00:05.250
Now, in this section,

00:05.250 --> 00:08.520
we're gonna cover a very prominent concept

00:08.520 --> 00:11.130
in startups and large businesses today

00:11.130 --> 00:13.230
called customer development.

00:13.230 --> 00:15.780
Customer development is one of the number one tools

00:15.780 --> 00:19.410
a product manager will use to get any sense

00:19.410 --> 00:21.840
of whether or not you're building the right thing,

00:21.840 --> 00:23.520
what the market will accept,

00:23.520 --> 00:26.130
and what the market will absolutely just reject.

00:26.130 --> 00:27.840
So it's something you have to know.

00:27.840 --> 00:30.067
Now a lot has changed since this book

00:30.067 --> 00:32.130
"The Lean Startup" came out.

00:32.130 --> 00:34.740
Companies, startups, even small businesses

00:34.740 --> 00:36.630
are looking at their markets

00:36.630 --> 00:38.580
and looking at their customers

00:38.580 --> 00:42.360
as well as their business ideas in a radically new way.

00:42.360 --> 00:44.160
If you're unfamiliar with "The Lean Startup",

00:44.160 --> 00:47.550
we cover that later in the course so don't worry.

00:47.550 --> 00:49.563
But the old way of thinking

00:49.563 --> 00:52.050
was that you would sit around,

00:52.050 --> 00:53.730
come up with the best idea

00:53.730 --> 00:55.860
for what you think your market will accept,

00:55.860 --> 00:58.320
then what you would do is you would build that

00:58.320 --> 01:00.240
and push it out to the market.

01:00.240 --> 01:03.120
The attitude in general was that some products

01:03.120 --> 01:06.570
and some new features succeed and some fail.

01:06.570 --> 01:07.920
Such is life,

01:07.920 --> 01:12.240
you go from concept to production to promotion

01:12.240 --> 01:14.670
and that's the entire life cycle.

01:14.670 --> 01:15.960
Now if there's one thing we've learned

01:15.960 --> 01:17.850
from "The Lean Startup",

01:17.850 --> 01:21.810
it's that you can't come up with product ideas in a vacuum.

01:21.810 --> 01:24.180
There's a lot more that goes

01:24.180 --> 01:26.640
into what makes a product successful

01:26.640 --> 01:29.010
and what makes a product unsuccessful.

01:29.010 --> 01:31.890
And that information nugget that you wish you had

01:31.890 --> 01:34.290
before you started building your product

01:34.290 --> 01:35.760
and before you launched it,

01:35.760 --> 01:38.220
is actually held by the customer.

01:38.220 --> 01:40.140
So customer development in a nutshell

01:40.140 --> 01:44.220
is the practice of establishing a continuous

01:44.220 --> 01:48.780
and iterative communication line with your customers

01:48.780 --> 01:51.240
so that you can come up with ideas,

01:51.240 --> 01:53.280
come up with hypotheses,

01:53.280 --> 01:56.850
try them out with your customers, get feedback,

01:56.850 --> 01:58.080
and use that feedback

01:58.080 --> 02:00.813
to inform your product decisions going forward.

02:02.790 --> 02:06.030
Development is that by establishing a line of communication

02:06.030 --> 02:07.950
with your customers,

02:07.950 --> 02:10.530
you can on a constant basis

02:10.530 --> 02:13.710
test and validate your product ideas

02:13.710 --> 02:15.870
with regards to who's gonna buy it

02:15.870 --> 02:17.490
and the market in general.

02:17.490 --> 02:18.600
Now, I know a lot of you guys out there

02:18.600 --> 02:19.950
have heard of customer development

02:19.950 --> 02:21.480
and you probably are thinking,

02:21.480 --> 02:23.100
well, that's part of it, right?

02:23.100 --> 02:25.680
Well, let's talk about customer development really quickly

02:25.680 --> 02:26.940
from an academic sense.

02:26.940 --> 02:29.190
Now customer development is actually a framework

02:29.190 --> 02:31.710
and it was developed by a man named Steve Blank.

02:31.710 --> 02:33.450
He is a professor at Stanford

02:33.450 --> 02:35.340
and a thought leader in startups

02:35.340 --> 02:40.340
and how small companies can disrupt large industries.

02:40.530 --> 02:42.420
The concept as he teaches it

02:42.420 --> 02:44.340
was expounded in his book

02:44.340 --> 02:46.470
called "The Four Steps to the Epiphany".

02:46.470 --> 02:47.990
If that's any hint,

02:47.990 --> 02:52.020
there are four steps to his customer development framework.

02:52.020 --> 02:53.130
Step one is discovery.

02:53.130 --> 02:54.690
Step two is validation.

02:54.690 --> 02:56.580
Step three is creation

02:56.580 --> 02:58.140
and step four is building.

02:58.140 --> 03:00.660
Now he teaches the customer development mindset

03:00.660 --> 03:04.080
as something that permeates every single section

03:04.080 --> 03:06.090
of your product life cycle,

03:06.090 --> 03:07.530
from coming up with the idea

03:07.530 --> 03:10.350
all the way to growing your business.

03:10.350 --> 03:12.478
And this is absolutely true

03:12.478 --> 03:16.110
and product managers do use customer development

03:16.110 --> 03:18.510
in every single section of their business.

03:18.510 --> 03:20.910
But for our purposes in this course,

03:20.910 --> 03:22.410
we're gonna focus on the parts

03:22.410 --> 03:23.910
of the customer development framework

03:23.910 --> 03:26.520
that are actionable and are the most useful,

03:26.520 --> 03:28.320
and that is the customer interview.

03:28.320 --> 03:29.880
As you manage a growing product

03:29.880 --> 03:32.220
or even a budding brand new product,

03:32.220 --> 03:35.460
you're gonna want to increase your product IQ

03:35.460 --> 03:39.900
by understanding the real reasons why your customers

03:39.900 --> 03:42.630
use or do not use your product.

03:42.630 --> 03:44.070
And by doing customer interviews,

03:44.070 --> 03:45.840
we can hear it in their own words

03:45.840 --> 03:47.880
which is incredibly valuable.

03:47.880 --> 03:51.960
So for a product manager or a founding entrepreneur,

03:51.960 --> 03:55.320
customer development is going to look like this.

03:55.320 --> 03:57.750
Now the first step in the process

03:57.750 --> 03:59.850
is that you're gonna come up with an idea.

03:59.850 --> 04:01.500
This is the core nugget

04:01.500 --> 04:04.980
that you're gonna build your enterprise or your product on

04:04.980 --> 04:07.050
and that means that you're gonna instantly go

04:07.050 --> 04:10.500
into the process of validation, trying to figure out

04:10.500 --> 04:13.050
whether or not this is a good idea to build.

04:13.050 --> 04:13.883
At this stage,

04:13.883 --> 04:16.020
you're gonna use customer interviews to figure out

04:16.020 --> 04:18.060
whether or not your product is needed,

04:18.060 --> 04:20.040
whether or not the problem you think you're solving

04:20.040 --> 04:20.934
is a real problem,

04:20.934 --> 04:25.800
and whether or not customers actually have that problem

04:25.800 --> 04:27.900
and are interested in your solution for it.

04:27.900 --> 04:30.090
You're gonna constantly reintroduce this feedback

04:30.090 --> 04:32.850
that you've received into your validation process,

04:32.850 --> 04:35.520
eventually landing at the MVP stage,

04:35.520 --> 04:36.990
and then onto the next stage

04:36.990 --> 04:38.850
which is the actual development

04:38.850 --> 04:41.190
of your first version one product.

04:41.190 --> 04:42.780
Now during the development phase,

04:42.780 --> 04:44.220
you're gonna be using customer interviews

04:44.220 --> 04:45.360
and customer development

04:45.360 --> 04:47.160
as a way of figuring out

04:47.160 --> 04:50.069
what features are the correct ones to build

04:50.069 --> 04:53.070
and how should you prioritize

04:53.070 --> 04:55.020
what goes into your version one

04:55.020 --> 04:57.150
so that you can be efficient

04:57.150 --> 04:58.950
and save resources.

04:58.950 --> 05:00.060
After your version one,

05:00.060 --> 05:01.350
you go into a process

05:01.350 --> 05:03.720
whereby you're gonna start iterating on your product.

05:03.720 --> 05:06.510
You're gonna start improving it, adding features,

05:06.510 --> 05:08.280
changing things that don't work out.

05:08.280 --> 05:09.113
And at this stage,

05:09.113 --> 05:11.880
you're gonna use customer development constantly

05:11.880 --> 05:13.920
to figure out things like,

05:13.920 --> 05:15.690
are customers enjoying the product?

05:15.690 --> 05:17.910
Are they using it correctly?

05:17.910 --> 05:20.400
Who exactly is using the product

05:20.400 --> 05:22.080
and getting the most out of it?

05:22.080 --> 05:24.090
Is it the group that we thought it was?

05:24.090 --> 05:25.680
You'll use this as a tool for figuring out

05:25.680 --> 05:28.560
whether or not you're targeting the right people,

05:28.560 --> 05:30.900
figure out if there's anything that you are missing,

05:30.900 --> 05:32.520
and to give you clues

05:32.520 --> 05:36.180
as to what new features or new avenues

05:36.180 --> 05:37.320
might be open to you.

05:37.320 --> 05:39.930
Customer development is primarily a tool for two things:

05:39.930 --> 05:44.610
one, risk mitigation and two, opportunity recognition.

05:44.610 --> 05:47.400
And that is what you are gonna use custom development for

05:47.400 --> 05:48.720
as a product manager.

05:48.720 --> 05:50.400
So in this section, we're gonna focus

05:50.400 --> 05:53.340
on how do we do that iterative process,

05:53.340 --> 05:54.960
how do we do it correctly?

05:54.960 --> 05:56.790
How do we know if we're getting the right feedback?

05:56.790 --> 05:59.280
Who exactly do we talk to?

05:59.280 --> 06:02.760
And what insights can we get from our interviews?

06:02.760 --> 06:03.960
All right, guys, hope you're excited.

06:03.960 --> 06:07.530
This is an incredibly useful skill you will use

06:07.530 --> 06:10.350
regardless of whether or not you become a product manager.

06:10.350 --> 06:11.750
See you in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.150 --> 00:01.890
-: Hey, guys. Welcome back to the course.

00:01.890 --> 00:03.930
So in this lecture, we're gonna start talking

00:03.930 --> 00:06.840
about the actual customer interviews.

00:06.840 --> 00:07.673
In this lecture,

00:07.673 --> 00:10.860
we're gonna cover four different types of interviews,

00:10.860 --> 00:15.420
talk about why we will be engaging with customers,

00:15.420 --> 00:17.430
what we're trying to get out of them,

00:17.430 --> 00:20.550
and how, in general, these interviews are conducted.

00:20.550 --> 00:21.600
Now, as a product manager,

00:21.600 --> 00:25.380
there are numerous reasons why you will want to engage

00:25.380 --> 00:28.410
with your customers in an interview setting.

00:28.410 --> 00:30.180
Entrepreneurs, on the other hand,

00:30.180 --> 00:32.430
generally will talk to their customers

00:32.430 --> 00:34.560
at the beginning, at the outset,

00:34.560 --> 00:36.930
just to figure out whether or not they're on to something.

00:36.930 --> 00:38.760
And they'll then use that information

00:38.760 --> 00:40.920
to figure out how to build an MVP.

00:40.920 --> 00:44.250
But there are other reasons why you might engage

00:44.250 --> 00:46.230
with a customer, and it kinda just depends

00:46.230 --> 00:50.970
on what you need as an organization, as a product owner,

00:50.970 --> 00:54.000
and where you are in your product's progress.

00:54.000 --> 00:55.620
Now, the first type of interview

00:55.620 --> 00:57.930
is the exploratory interview.

00:57.930 --> 00:59.430
Now, an exploratory interview

00:59.430 --> 01:02.910
is the most free-form interview there is.

01:02.910 --> 01:06.450
The idea here is you're really just waiting out to see,

01:06.450 --> 01:09.660
talking to your customers, trying to establish

01:09.660 --> 01:12.120
whether or not they have certain pain points,

01:12.120 --> 01:15.390
whether or not they're open to certain solutions,

01:15.390 --> 01:18.540
and in general, what do they feel like,

01:18.540 --> 01:21.090
what do they look like, and how do they operate?

01:21.090 --> 01:24.870
The idea here is that you are exploring. You're fishing.

01:24.870 --> 01:27.960
You're trying to find things your customers

01:27.960 --> 01:31.560
have a problem with, are excited about.

01:31.560 --> 01:32.910
You're trying to find things

01:32.910 --> 01:35.610
that they do differently than other people.

01:35.610 --> 01:38.580
You're looking for any little clue or hint

01:38.580 --> 01:40.800
that can give you some type of insight

01:40.800 --> 01:42.360
about the product you already have

01:42.360 --> 01:43.740
or the one that you're building.

01:43.740 --> 01:45.390
This is often something you will do

01:45.390 --> 01:47.700
when you're trying to come up with ideas,

01:47.700 --> 01:51.210
ways of changing your product, ways of expanding it.

01:51.210 --> 01:53.130
You will have a backlog,

01:53.130 --> 01:57.000
but you do have to come up with new ideas.

01:57.000 --> 01:58.800
When you talk to an actual customer,

01:58.800 --> 02:00.420
talk to them about their day,

02:00.420 --> 02:02.580
the context in which they use your product

02:02.580 --> 02:04.530
or could use your product.

02:04.530 --> 02:07.200
They'll give you a million different things

02:07.200 --> 02:08.880
that you could build a solution for.

02:08.880 --> 02:10.500
And the best part about doing this in person

02:10.500 --> 02:14.730
is you can visually see how badly they want this solution

02:14.730 --> 02:17.220
and whether or not they would pay for it.

02:17.220 --> 02:19.710
Here's some example exploratory questions

02:19.710 --> 02:21.990
that you might ask a customer.

02:21.990 --> 02:23.220
Let's say we're using an app

02:23.220 --> 02:25.800
that has to do with getting directions.

02:25.800 --> 02:29.040
I might ask, "What's the worst part of your commute?"

02:29.040 --> 02:30.690
It's an open-ended question,

02:30.690 --> 02:33.810
fishing for some type of pain

02:33.810 --> 02:37.470
and to understand the hierarchy of problems they have

02:37.470 --> 02:39.750
within the context of a certain situation.

02:39.750 --> 02:40.777
Another question I might ask is,

02:40.777 --> 02:43.890
"When you get lost driving, what do you do?"

02:43.890 --> 02:48.890
I'm fishing for substitutes for solving their problem,

02:49.530 --> 02:53.070
and again, they'll color in kind of my picture

02:53.070 --> 02:57.240
of what normal consumers do when they're lost.

02:57.240 --> 02:58.290
Now, the second type of interview

02:58.290 --> 03:00.480
is the validation interview,

03:00.480 --> 03:02.790
and this is by far the most common one.

03:02.790 --> 03:04.410
The purpose of a validation interview

03:04.410 --> 03:09.300
is that you have a theory and you want to test it out.

03:09.300 --> 03:12.180
Your theory might be that users

03:12.180 --> 03:15.420
will go bananas for feature X,

03:15.420 --> 03:16.980
or you might be pre-product

03:16.980 --> 03:21.480
and your theory is that my solution solves their problem.

03:21.480 --> 03:25.170
That is always a theory until you launch,

03:25.170 --> 03:26.640
until you get validation,

03:26.640 --> 03:29.370
and until you reach product-market fit.

03:29.370 --> 03:30.480
Now let's talk about a little bit more

03:30.480 --> 03:31.710
about validation interviews

03:31.710 --> 03:34.800
because they are the biggest deal of them all.

03:34.800 --> 03:36.660
You will do them as a product manager,

03:36.660 --> 03:40.080
and you will do them as an entrepreneur, unavoidable.

03:40.080 --> 03:41.910
Generally, validation interviews are run

03:41.910 --> 03:44.220
in a very specific way.

03:44.220 --> 03:46.050
Because you have a theory

03:46.050 --> 03:49.140
and you want it to be proven or disproven,

03:49.140 --> 03:52.260
you kind of have to run it in a scientific way.

03:52.260 --> 03:54.837
Now, do you think that in a validation interview,

03:54.837 --> 03:57.307
you just walk right in there and say,

03:57.307 --> 03:59.070
"This is my product,

03:59.070 --> 04:01.050
or this is the feature I'm adding.

04:01.050 --> 04:02.850
What do you think? Do you like it?"

04:02.850 --> 04:05.340
If I were standing right in front of your face,

04:05.340 --> 04:07.650
like I am now but coming through the screen,

04:07.650 --> 04:10.740
and I asked you, "Do you like my website,

04:10.740 --> 04:12.930
I spent six months working on it,"

04:12.930 --> 04:16.350
do you think you're more likely to say yes or say no?

04:16.350 --> 04:18.150
You're probably more likely to say yes

04:18.150 --> 04:20.640
because you're probably a decent person

04:20.640 --> 04:22.170
and you want to please me,

04:22.170 --> 04:24.870
and that's generally how human nature works.

04:24.870 --> 04:28.320
Validation interviews are hyper-sensitive to bias,

04:28.320 --> 04:31.500
so much so that they're run in a way

04:31.500 --> 04:34.920
that you don't even introduce your idea,

04:34.920 --> 04:38.100
your theory, your product, your new feature,

04:38.100 --> 04:40.320
till the very, very end.

04:40.320 --> 04:42.600
You'll spend the majority of the interview

04:42.600 --> 04:46.650
talking around the problem your product solves.

04:46.650 --> 04:49.590
When you do introduce it, you don't hype the idea.

04:49.590 --> 04:54.360
You don't defend it. You don't exaggerate what it is.

04:54.360 --> 04:59.360
You simply present it and then collect honest feedback

04:59.670 --> 05:02.820
and try to chart how they react

05:02.820 --> 05:05.520
and how they think after it.

05:05.520 --> 05:09.870
The problem is that if you bias their answers in any way,

05:09.870 --> 05:14.220
if you infringe on their ability to think freely,

05:14.220 --> 05:16.620
even if it's very subtle,

05:16.620 --> 05:20.220
you have essentially ruined the interview.

05:20.220 --> 05:24.150
Any data you got from that is junk. It's poison.

05:24.150 --> 05:28.110
So learn to zip it and let them do the talking.

05:28.110 --> 05:32.370
Let them, in their own words, describe the problem they have

05:32.370 --> 05:35.490
and what they think of your proposed solution.

05:35.490 --> 05:36.750
You'll spend the majority of these interviews

05:36.750 --> 05:37.920
not pushing your customer,

05:37.920 --> 05:40.830
but corralling them in the right direction,

05:40.830 --> 05:43.260
trying your best to get them to talk

05:43.260 --> 05:46.590
about the problem you are trying to solve in their own words

05:46.590 --> 05:50.130
and to see if they themselves find it on their own.

05:50.130 --> 05:53.880
Let's give some examples of a validation-type question.

05:53.880 --> 05:57.990
I might ask you, "Do you ever lose things in your house?"

05:57.990 --> 06:01.920
Followed up by, "Do you ever lose your keys?"

06:01.920 --> 06:03.840
What I'm doing there is I'm asking them

06:03.840 --> 06:05.310
about things they lose in their house,

06:05.310 --> 06:07.800
seeing if they say anything about their keys,

06:07.800 --> 06:12.570
and if they don't, I try to narrow the focus down to keys.

06:12.570 --> 06:15.150
So then I ask them if they lose their keys often

06:15.150 --> 06:18.750
to see if that is actually something they do.

06:18.750 --> 06:20.760
Now, that's a good pre-product question,

06:20.760 --> 06:23.220
but what about a post-product question?

06:23.220 --> 06:26.467
Well, a post-product validation question could be like,

06:26.467 --> 06:28.590
"When you're using our app

06:28.590 --> 06:31.290
and you wanna collaborate with your coworkers,

06:31.290 --> 06:32.670
what do you end up doing?"

06:32.670 --> 06:35.310
This might be on the heel of a feature

06:35.310 --> 06:38.070
that allows them to collaborate within your app,

06:38.070 --> 06:42.030
and you're trying to chart how they solve that problem,

06:42.030 --> 06:43.440
whether or not they have that problem

06:43.440 --> 06:45.480
or it's even something they care about.

06:45.480 --> 06:46.470
The third type of interview

06:46.470 --> 06:49.140
is a satisfaction-oriented interview.

06:49.140 --> 06:50.310
And satisfaction interviews,

06:50.310 --> 06:52.020
they're exactly what they sound like.

06:52.020 --> 06:55.290
You're trying to dig out what parts of your products

06:55.290 --> 06:56.730
are working for your customers

06:56.730 --> 06:59.430
and what parts of your product are not.

06:59.430 --> 07:02.160
You're also trying to gauge, in general,

07:02.160 --> 07:03.660
what they think about your product.

07:03.660 --> 07:04.500
As a product manager,

07:04.500 --> 07:08.100
you need to know whether or not opinions of your product

07:08.100 --> 07:09.180
are going south,

07:09.180 --> 07:12.300
if they're jumping ship and going to your competitors.

07:12.300 --> 07:15.210
But more importantly than just knowing that,

07:15.210 --> 07:17.280
you need to understand why they are.

07:17.280 --> 07:19.860
So get at the root of their dissatisfaction,

07:19.860 --> 07:22.800
or figure out what exactly you are doing right.

07:22.800 --> 07:24.577
Some example questions might be,

07:24.577 --> 07:26.580
"What should we stop doing?"

07:26.580 --> 07:28.110
A great open-ended question,

07:28.110 --> 07:29.880
trying to get them to tell us

07:29.880 --> 07:32.070
what exactly simply is not working.

07:32.070 --> 07:33.150
If something's abrasive,

07:33.150 --> 07:35.820
if something is just going against the grain

07:35.820 --> 07:38.310
for their experience, they'll tell you.

07:38.310 --> 07:39.337
Another one I like to ask is,

07:39.337 --> 07:40.650
"What's one thing I could do

07:40.650 --> 07:42.330
to make this better for you?"

07:42.330 --> 07:44.970
If they have something that is lacking,

07:44.970 --> 07:46.350
they will bring it up.

07:46.350 --> 07:49.260
It's a great way of digging out the number one thing

07:49.260 --> 07:51.720
on the top of their mind

07:51.720 --> 07:54.090
and whatever is most important to them.

07:54.090 --> 07:57.060
Now, the last type of interview is an efficiency interview.

07:57.060 --> 07:58.140
In an efficiency interview,

07:58.140 --> 08:01.050
you're trying to figure out how exactly your product

08:01.050 --> 08:03.630
ingrains into the life of your customer,

08:03.630 --> 08:05.220
and as a function of that,

08:05.220 --> 08:09.300
how can you improve it to better serve its purpose?

08:09.300 --> 08:13.110
You're trying to get an idea for who uses your product.

08:13.110 --> 08:15.480
What do they do on it? When do they use it?

08:15.480 --> 08:17.550
Under what circumstances?

08:17.550 --> 08:19.110
And from that contextual learning,

08:19.110 --> 08:21.570
you can figure out what parts are serving them well,

08:21.570 --> 08:25.080
what parts are unnecessary or inefficient,

08:25.080 --> 08:28.290
and what parts are actually degrading

08:28.290 --> 08:29.820
from what they're trying to do.

08:29.820 --> 08:31.560
You'll fish for things like,

08:31.560 --> 08:34.440
are they using X, Y, and Z feature?

08:34.440 --> 08:38.100
And what are they using X, Y, and Z feature for?

08:38.100 --> 08:42.270
It's very common you'll find people not using your features

08:42.270 --> 08:44.850
or your product exactly like you thought.

08:44.850 --> 08:47.250
When you do find that, that is generally a sign

08:47.250 --> 08:50.400
that something is broken in your process.

08:50.400 --> 08:52.470
Either something's too hard to understand,

08:52.470 --> 08:55.530
or the process is too inefficient,

08:55.530 --> 08:57.840
or you're just targeting the wrong people.

08:57.840 --> 08:59.940
Some example questions you might ask

08:59.940 --> 09:02.857
in an efficiency-type interview is,

09:02.857 --> 09:06.480
"How easy is it for you to use feature X?"

09:06.480 --> 09:08.280
You're just trying to figure out, in their own words,

09:08.280 --> 09:10.920
is this something they enjoy using?

09:10.920 --> 09:13.230
Is it easy for them to use?

09:13.230 --> 09:16.350
Is it easy to understand all of it?

09:16.350 --> 09:18.817
Another question might be something along the lines of,

09:18.817 --> 09:21.210
"If you wanted to do blank,

09:21.210 --> 09:23.730
how would you do it in our product?"

09:23.730 --> 09:25.260
It's always interesting to ask customers

09:25.260 --> 09:27.960
to show you how they would do something,

09:27.960 --> 09:31.770
and you can see at what point in time they got confused,

09:31.770 --> 09:34.440
misdirected, did something wrong,

09:34.440 --> 09:37.020
or figured out something you never thought of.

09:37.020 --> 09:40.440
Okay, so those are the four different types of interviews,

09:40.440 --> 09:42.060
and I know you're asking the question,

09:42.060 --> 09:44.640
well, aren't there interviews that do both

09:44.640 --> 09:45.900
or a combination of them?

09:45.900 --> 09:47.460
And yes, absolutely.

09:47.460 --> 09:49.110
So don't necessarily think of them

09:49.110 --> 09:51.330
as just types of interviews.

09:51.330 --> 09:53.130
They're more the purposes

09:53.130 --> 09:55.470
that you would use an interview for

09:55.470 --> 09:58.830
or whatever your focus is on an interview.

09:58.830 --> 10:02.250
You can definitely do a validation interview

10:02.250 --> 10:06.660
and an efficiency interview in the same setting.

10:06.660 --> 10:07.530
All right, in the next lecture,

10:07.530 --> 10:09.330
we're gonna talk about best practices

10:09.330 --> 10:11.850
when you are talking to your customers.

10:11.850 --> 10:14.460
How can you make good questions?

10:14.460 --> 10:18.180
How can you tell when you're asking the wrong questions?

10:18.180 --> 10:21.210
And what, in general, do you wanna shy away from?

10:21.210 --> 10:22.943
All right, see you in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.180 --> 00:01.470
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:01.470 --> 00:03.510
Hey, so I wanna make a quick pit stop

00:03.510 --> 00:06.930
to talk to you a little bit about how customer development

00:06.930 --> 00:11.640
is going to defer in your career as a product manager.

00:11.640 --> 00:14.850
And this will help you understand how we teach this section.

00:14.850 --> 00:16.080
So one thing you need to understand

00:16.080 --> 00:18.600
about customer development is that it evolves

00:18.600 --> 00:21.360
throughout the life cycle of your product.

00:21.360 --> 00:22.770
You as a product manager,

00:22.770 --> 00:25.050
your role is going to evolve as well

00:25.050 --> 00:28.830
as your product goes from baby, to teenager, to adult.

00:28.830 --> 00:30.480
Now, what you need to be aware of though

00:30.480 --> 00:33.960
in customer development is that there's a very large chasm

00:33.960 --> 00:38.960
or divide between when you're validating a very new product

00:39.360 --> 00:41.370
and reaching out to customers.

00:41.370 --> 00:44.310
Or when you're trying to improve an existing product

00:44.310 --> 00:46.350
with an existing customer base.

00:46.350 --> 00:48.330
Those are two roles that every product manager

00:48.330 --> 00:50.700
will actually have to tackle.

00:50.700 --> 00:53.070
However, we treat them a little bit differently.

00:53.070 --> 00:54.630
Now, when you don't have a product

00:54.630 --> 00:55.530
and you're trying to figure out whether

00:55.530 --> 00:57.480
or not you're going to build a product,

00:57.480 --> 00:59.310
you're primarily going to be running

00:59.310 --> 01:00.390
your customer development

01:00.390 --> 01:02.820
and running your customer development interviews

01:02.820 --> 01:05.100
with potential customers.

01:05.100 --> 01:07.230
And when you have a product, you're gonna be running it

01:07.230 --> 01:09.150
with existing customers.

01:09.150 --> 01:10.860
Now, it doesn't sound like there's a huge difference

01:10.860 --> 01:13.110
but it's actually an enormous difference.

01:13.110 --> 01:15.360
Potential customers do not know you.

01:15.360 --> 01:20.130
They probably don't know of what you're trying to do.

01:20.130 --> 01:21.120
The information you're gonna try

01:21.120 --> 01:22.590
to get out of them is different.

01:22.590 --> 01:25.560
The way that you have to reach out to them is different.

01:25.560 --> 01:28.890
And in the second case, it's also very, very different.

01:28.890 --> 01:30.660
Your customers you're gonna be talking to,

01:30.660 --> 01:32.880
you already know who they are.

01:32.880 --> 01:34.950
Reaching out to them is generally easier.

01:34.950 --> 01:37.650
And when you get them on the line or in person,

01:37.650 --> 01:39.330
it's an entirely different script.

01:39.330 --> 01:43.380
Now, keep this in mind as we go through this section.

01:43.380 --> 01:47.730
Customer development will vary in terms of purpose

01:47.730 --> 01:50.520
but very rarely in terms of form.

01:50.520 --> 01:53.760
We still will use the same guiding principles

01:53.760 --> 01:57.060
we learn in this section for either pre-product

01:57.060 --> 01:59.940
or post product interviews.

01:59.940 --> 02:02.850
The primary difference is the type of information

02:02.850 --> 02:04.740
you're trying to get out of them.

02:04.740 --> 02:06.300
So as we go through this section,

02:06.300 --> 02:08.220
I'm gonna be dividing everything

02:08.220 --> 02:10.800
as pre-product potential customer

02:10.800 --> 02:13.620
and post product existing customer.

02:13.620 --> 02:16.140
And in a couple cases, I'm gonna explain one.

02:16.140 --> 02:18.000
Next lecture, explain the next one.

02:18.000 --> 02:20.280
I suggest you learn both techniques.

02:20.280 --> 02:22.890
But if you only wanna learn the type of situation

02:22.890 --> 02:24.540
you're in right now, say you're an entrepreneur,

02:24.540 --> 02:25.560
and you're launching a new product,

02:25.560 --> 02:29.490
then you can focus on pre-product potential customers.

02:29.490 --> 02:31.680
And if you're someone who knows that you wanna manage

02:31.680 --> 02:33.390
a product that's already existing

02:33.390 --> 02:34.260
you can focus on the other.

02:34.260 --> 02:35.670
But again, I suggest both.

02:35.670 --> 02:37.403
All right, see you in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.424 --> 00:03.007
(upbeat music)

00:04.290 --> 00:06.570
-: Hey, guys, welcome back to the course.

00:06.570 --> 00:08.580
So let's, in this lecture,

00:08.580 --> 00:12.810
start the process of customer development.

00:12.810 --> 00:13.800
All right, so in this lecture,

00:13.800 --> 00:15.750
we're gonna cover the first step

00:15.750 --> 00:18.720
of the customer development process.

00:18.720 --> 00:21.210
There are four steps to this,

00:21.210 --> 00:23.430
and I will fill you in as we go.

00:23.430 --> 00:27.180
So step one in the process is figuring out

00:27.180 --> 00:31.260
who exactly we want to talk to in the first place.

00:31.260 --> 00:32.093
Now, you obviously

00:32.093 --> 00:34.410
don't wanna talk to just literally anyone.

00:34.410 --> 00:38.700
You wanna make sure it's either a potential customer

00:38.700 --> 00:41.400
or a customer you already have

00:41.400 --> 00:43.560
that is relevant to the new features

00:43.560 --> 00:45.450
or new products you're trying to launch.

00:45.450 --> 00:47.640
Now, as I explained in the last lecture,

00:47.640 --> 00:49.020
pretty much everything we're gonna do

00:49.020 --> 00:50.970
is divided down the middle

00:50.970 --> 00:53.460
between pre-product and post-product.

00:53.460 --> 00:55.710
So first I'm gonna explain the process

00:55.710 --> 00:58.380
by which we can figure out who we are targeting

00:58.380 --> 01:00.900
and who to interview for pre-product,

01:00.900 --> 01:02.640
and then at the end of the lecture,

01:02.640 --> 01:04.140
we'll cover post-product.

01:04.140 --> 01:06.120
Okay, for the pre-product scenario,

01:06.120 --> 01:08.250
you have not yet created it.

01:08.250 --> 01:12.480
You are probably in the validation section of development.

01:12.480 --> 01:14.460
You're still trying to figure out what should go in it.

01:14.460 --> 01:15.990
Do people actually want it?

01:15.990 --> 01:17.460
Is there a market?

01:17.460 --> 01:20.850
Now, in 90% of circumstances,

01:20.850 --> 01:23.700
you probably in your head

01:23.700 --> 01:27.300
have already thought of a target group of people

01:27.300 --> 01:29.970
that you think this new product is going to appeal to.

01:29.970 --> 01:33.270
If that's the case, then you're a step ahead.

01:33.270 --> 01:36.510
Take the idea that you have and write it down.

01:36.510 --> 01:38.070
We're gonna try to get to

01:38.070 --> 01:42.840
at least three potential groups of people to talk to.

01:42.840 --> 01:44.070
Now, if you're sitting there right now,

01:44.070 --> 01:46.133
and you have absolutely no idea who

01:46.133 --> 01:50.910
a potential customer for your new product is going to be,

01:50.910 --> 01:52.410
I would be worried.

01:52.410 --> 01:53.280
You're in a situation

01:53.280 --> 01:57.720
where we call a solution looking for a problem,

01:57.720 --> 02:01.140
and I hope the next exercise we go through

02:01.140 --> 02:02.160
will be beneficial.

02:02.160 --> 02:04.680
Now, I want you to write down at least a minimum

02:04.680 --> 02:08.340
of three different types of customers or customer groups

02:08.340 --> 02:11.370
that you think would find your product appealing.

02:11.370 --> 02:12.360
Why do I ask for three?

02:12.360 --> 02:14.220
Well, because any product out there

02:14.220 --> 02:18.030
is going to appeal to not just one type of customer.

02:18.030 --> 02:21.090
It's going to have appeal to other types as well.

02:21.090 --> 02:24.810
It might not be as strong, but it still has an appeal.

02:24.810 --> 02:26.130
Now, write down the customer groups

02:26.130 --> 02:28.140
you think answer that question.

02:28.140 --> 02:30.360
Once you have three, then we're gonna decide

02:30.360 --> 02:33.000
which of these three is worth targeting first.

02:33.000 --> 02:34.980
And we're gonna do that by playing a game

02:34.980 --> 02:36.630
called three question stud.

02:36.630 --> 02:37.560
I just made it up.

02:37.560 --> 02:39.120
Three question stud is based

02:39.120 --> 02:41.790
off of the game five card stud.

02:41.790 --> 02:44.610
And if anyone plays poker and has played five card stud,

02:44.610 --> 02:47.220
you know it's the dumbest game in poker.

02:47.220 --> 02:49.710
But for our purposes of figuring out who to target,

02:49.710 --> 02:51.360
this metaphor works perfectly.

02:51.360 --> 02:52.920
Now, the reason I call this three card stud

02:52.920 --> 02:55.920
is that we're gonna judge our potential customer groups

02:55.920 --> 02:58.350
on three different criteria,

02:58.350 --> 03:01.410
and much like a game of stud,

03:01.410 --> 03:05.700
you get three criteria, three ratings, and that is it.

03:05.700 --> 03:07.950
If you're playing three card stud in real life,

03:07.950 --> 03:08.940
like the poker game,

03:08.940 --> 03:10.350
you would get three cards,

03:10.350 --> 03:12.210
your opponent would get three cards.

03:12.210 --> 03:13.560
You would look at them.

03:13.560 --> 03:18.360
Whichever one adds up to more or as a pair or whatever wins.

03:18.360 --> 03:19.200
That's it.

03:19.200 --> 03:20.520
Now, the first step in this game

03:20.520 --> 03:22.320
is to ask yourself a question

03:22.320 --> 03:24.870
and then answer it to yourself.

03:24.870 --> 03:27.930
Your product solves a problem for someone,

03:27.930 --> 03:31.470
and that is the case with 100% of new products.

03:31.470 --> 03:32.820
If you think about it long enough,

03:32.820 --> 03:36.030
you will think of a problem that it solves.

03:36.030 --> 03:37.710
Google Map solves the problem

03:37.710 --> 03:40.530
of I don't know how to get where I'm going.

03:40.530 --> 03:42.420
I get lost easily.

03:42.420 --> 03:46.320
And even frivolous things like mobile app games,

03:46.320 --> 03:47.520
those solve the problem

03:47.520 --> 03:50.610
of being bored during short periods of time

03:50.610 --> 03:52.950
where you can't engage in other things.

03:52.950 --> 03:56.070
So once you figured out what the problem is,

03:56.070 --> 03:59.130
ask yourself the question, who has this problem?

03:59.130 --> 04:00.570
Now, if you dwell on that,

04:00.570 --> 04:03.990
you should be able to come up with at least a couple answers

04:03.990 --> 04:05.250
to that question.

04:05.250 --> 04:06.750
Now, they might be customer groups

04:06.750 --> 04:09.120
you've never even considered,

04:09.120 --> 04:10.560
and obviously it'll be the ones

04:10.560 --> 04:13.350
that you probably had in the back of your mind.

04:13.350 --> 04:15.240
All right, so grab a pen and a piece of paper

04:15.240 --> 04:18.570
or an Excel sheet or a Word document,

04:18.570 --> 04:20.070
Evernote doc, whatever it is

04:20.070 --> 04:23.190
that you're using to capture your ideas and your thoughts.

04:23.190 --> 04:28.190
Draw a table and make it three columns and three rows.

04:28.290 --> 04:31.020
Now on the top, we're gonna write our customer groups,

04:31.020 --> 04:32.700
customer group one, customer group two,

04:32.700 --> 04:34.050
and customer group three.

04:34.050 --> 04:35.220
Fill in the actual names

04:35.220 --> 04:36.720
of the ones you previously came up with.

04:36.720 --> 04:37.553
Now on the left side,

04:37.553 --> 04:39.990
we're gonna write down the three criteria,

04:39.990 --> 04:42.420
and this is pretty much the name of the game.

04:42.420 --> 04:45.210
So the number one we're gonna write is size,

04:45.210 --> 04:48.597
the second one we're gonna write is called pain to payment,

04:48.597 --> 04:50.940
and the third one we write is accessibility.

04:50.940 --> 04:54.120
Together, these criteria are probably the fastest

04:54.120 --> 04:57.420
and the most relevant criteria to use

04:57.420 --> 05:01.560
to determine who is actually worth going after right now.

05:01.560 --> 05:03.510
Now, size might not be obvious.

05:03.510 --> 05:06.060
When I say size, I mean market size.

05:06.060 --> 05:09.120
Think of the customer group you are talking about.

05:09.120 --> 05:11.550
How big is your customer group?

05:11.550 --> 05:13.800
Is it something that has thousands of people

05:13.800 --> 05:15.480
or millions of people?

05:15.480 --> 05:16.920
Let's say you were building a system

05:16.920 --> 05:21.510
that helped property developers and real estate agents

05:21.510 --> 05:23.700
and those were the two that you were going after,

05:23.700 --> 05:27.521
people who run property development companies, like CEOs,

05:27.521 --> 05:30.129
and real estate agents.

05:30.129 --> 05:32.986
How many CEOs in the world are there

05:32.986 --> 05:37.230
that run real estate or property development companies?

05:37.230 --> 05:40.410
And conversely, how many real estate agents are there?

05:40.410 --> 05:41.910
So in the first case, there's not a lot of them.

05:41.910 --> 05:45.510
So that customer group would rate low on this criteria.

05:45.510 --> 05:47.010
And the second one, real estate agents,

05:47.010 --> 05:50.280
there's a bajillion of them, so this would rate high.

05:50.280 --> 05:53.850
Now, the second criteria, pain to payment, this is a ratio.

05:53.850 --> 05:56.550
Now I want you to think about each customer group

05:56.550 --> 06:00.030
and think about how much pain do they have

06:00.030 --> 06:03.060
associated with the problem you are trying to solve.

06:03.060 --> 06:06.690
Someone who constantly gets lost in the city

06:06.690 --> 06:10.410
