probably has a decent level of pain,

06:10.410 --> 06:14.340
and someone who is bored waiting for their doctor,

06:14.340 --> 06:16.830
that's not a huge level of pain.

06:16.830 --> 06:20.220
Now, the second part of this criteria is payment.

06:20.220 --> 06:23.918
How likely are they to pay you for this service?

06:23.918 --> 06:26.760
And if they are willing to pay you,

06:26.760 --> 06:28.650
are they willing to pay you a lot of money

06:28.650 --> 06:30.330
or a little bit of money?

06:30.330 --> 06:32.340
And based off of those two,

06:32.340 --> 06:36.870
you can kind of come up with a good ratio or a bad ratio.

06:36.870 --> 06:39.720
High pain, high payment is very good.

06:39.720 --> 06:43.170
Low pain, low payment is quite poor.

06:43.170 --> 06:45.238
In the Google Maps example, we have high pain,

06:45.238 --> 06:46.980
but we have very low payment,

06:46.980 --> 06:49.350
because there's tons and tons of free options out there,

06:49.350 --> 06:51.570
so it actually would rate somewhere in the middle,

06:51.570 --> 06:53.970
and with a mobile app game,

06:53.970 --> 06:58.740
again, we have low pain, and payment is also quite low.

06:58.740 --> 07:02.640
You rarely pay more than, say, $5 for a mobile app game.

07:02.640 --> 07:04.200
And the last one is accessibility.

07:04.200 --> 07:05.700
And accessibility just means

07:05.700 --> 07:09.420
how easily can you get in contact with these people?

07:09.420 --> 07:10.590
Are they easy to find?

07:10.590 --> 07:13.650
Do they have their contact information posted somewhere?

07:13.650 --> 07:17.370
Are they used to receiving queries from the outside world,

07:17.370 --> 07:18.450
and responding to them?

07:18.450 --> 07:20.030
To go back to that first example,

07:20.030 --> 07:24.750
CEOs of property development companies, very inaccessible.

07:24.750 --> 07:26.220
Their information's not gonna be public,

07:26.220 --> 07:28.830
and if you email them, they're probably not gonna respond.

07:28.830 --> 07:30.900
They are trained to do that.

07:30.900 --> 07:33.720
They get too much email, and that's not their job.

07:33.720 --> 07:37.380
Whereas real estate agents publicly post their information.

07:37.380 --> 07:39.210
They do that on purpose,

07:39.210 --> 07:42.870
and they are very used to responding quickly

07:42.870 --> 07:45.210
to people who inquire from the outside world,

07:45.210 --> 07:46.530
because that is part of their job.

07:46.530 --> 07:49.740
All right, and quickly try to think about each criteria

07:49.740 --> 07:50.880
and each customer group.

07:50.880 --> 07:53.790
Give them a reading from one to 10,

07:53.790 --> 07:56.190
or if you want to do it based on the card metaphor,

07:56.190 --> 08:00.570
one through 10, or jack, queen, king, ace.

08:00.570 --> 08:02.130
And then at the bottom,

08:02.130 --> 08:05.730
I want you to add up the value of your hand

08:05.730 --> 08:08.310
or the value of your one through 10 total.

08:08.310 --> 08:10.680
One of those will have a higher number than the other two,

08:10.680 --> 08:13.470
and that's how we know this customer group

08:13.470 --> 08:15.139
has the most potential,

08:15.139 --> 08:18.180
and we're probably gonna be the most successful

08:18.180 --> 08:19.290
reaching out to them.

08:19.290 --> 08:20.123
All right, you got it?

08:20.123 --> 08:21.030
Just answer the question

08:21.030 --> 08:23.850
of what problem does your product solve?

08:23.850 --> 08:24.990
Who has that problem?

08:24.990 --> 08:27.210
And once you have three groups,

08:27.210 --> 08:30.000
try to evaluate them on three criteria,

08:30.000 --> 08:33.150
size, pain to payment, and accessibility.

08:33.150 --> 08:35.280
All right, now for the post-product people,

08:35.280 --> 08:36.480
if you are post-product,

08:36.480 --> 08:39.660
that means you already have an established customer base.

08:39.660 --> 08:41.380
You probably have people who are paying

08:41.380 --> 08:43.320
and engaging with your product,

08:43.320 --> 08:46.980
and you probably also have a lot of prospective leads

08:46.980 --> 08:48.270
and prospective customers

08:48.270 --> 08:49.830
that have already established themselves

08:49.830 --> 08:51.360
as being interested in your product.

08:51.360 --> 08:53.040
In this case, who do you interview?

08:53.040 --> 08:55.140
Well, it's actually relatively simple.

08:55.140 --> 08:58.080
Think about the product that you are proposing to launch

08:58.080 --> 09:00.870
or the feature you're trying to add

09:00.870 --> 09:04.020
or the problems you were trying to smooth out

09:04.020 --> 09:06.360
in your existing product line.

09:06.360 --> 09:08.730
Try to think of it in terms of your user base.

09:08.730 --> 09:10.890
Try to apply basic demographics.

09:10.890 --> 09:14.130
Are they older people that you're going to be targeting?

09:14.130 --> 09:15.510
Are they younger people?

09:15.510 --> 09:16.710
Are they married people?

09:16.710 --> 09:19.350
Are they people that have slow internet?

09:19.350 --> 09:21.810
Try to think of basic demographic data.

09:21.810 --> 09:23.010
Use that information,

09:23.010 --> 09:25.230
and then filter out your customer group

09:25.230 --> 09:26.760
and reach out to them.

09:26.760 --> 09:28.650
That one is infinitely easier,

09:28.650 --> 09:30.780
because you have a big pile of customers

09:30.780 --> 09:32.580
and you just need to decide which ones

09:32.580 --> 09:34.650
are probably gonna give you better information.

09:34.650 --> 09:36.120
Whatever problem you're trying to fix

09:36.120 --> 09:38.160
or whatever initiative you're trying to run

09:38.160 --> 09:41.190
probably pertains to some defined group

09:41.190 --> 09:42.750
within that customer base,

09:42.750 --> 09:44.850
and it's your job to pick them out and contact them.

09:44.850 --> 09:45.683
All right, guys.

09:45.683 --> 09:48.240
In the next lecture, we're gonna do a example

09:48.240 --> 09:50.310
of playing three card stud with a real idea.

09:50.310 --> 09:53.070
If you guys don't care, you think you got the idea,

09:53.070 --> 09:56.010
skip over that into step two,

09:56.010 --> 10:00.060
where we start talking about finding potential interviewees.

10:00.060 --> 10:01.260
All right, see you then.

WEBVTT

00:00.057 --> 00:02.040
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:02.040 --> 00:04.080
All right, we're moving on to step two

00:04.080 --> 00:06.510
of the customer development process

00:06.510 --> 00:09.420
finding your potential interviewees.

00:09.420 --> 00:11.940
Now, you can't obviously gather feedback about your product

00:11.940 --> 00:13.770
or your proposed product

00:13.770 --> 00:16.950
without actually talking to a potential

00:16.950 --> 00:18.660
or an existing customer.

00:18.660 --> 00:19.680
In this lecture, we're gonna talk

00:19.680 --> 00:22.380
about pre-product scenarios

00:22.380 --> 00:25.530
when we're looking for new customers

00:25.530 --> 00:29.340
or people that are not already aware of us to interview.

00:29.340 --> 00:33.330
Now, there are a lot of different places online

00:33.330 --> 00:36.630
or offline that you can go to connect with people

00:36.630 --> 00:40.590
that might be interested in your product, but for our sake,

00:40.590 --> 00:43.920
I've reduced it down to four different places

00:43.920 --> 00:48.210
or types of places to look that consistently can find you

00:48.210 --> 00:50.160
people that are interested.

00:50.160 --> 00:52.530
Now, in the resources of this lecture

00:52.530 --> 00:55.320
I actually attached a great article

00:55.320 --> 01:00.320
called 96 Different Ways to Find People to Interview.

01:00.540 --> 01:04.020
And they go through literally every single little thing

01:04.020 --> 01:05.010
you can think of.

01:05.010 --> 01:06.510
If you wanna read that, check it out.

01:06.510 --> 01:08.190
It's a little overkill for us.

01:08.190 --> 01:09.510
Now at this stage of the game

01:09.510 --> 01:12.180
you know which target customer group

01:12.180 --> 01:13.650
you would like to talk to

01:13.650 --> 01:15.540
because we played three questions stud

01:15.540 --> 01:17.784
and you identified one of the three

01:17.784 --> 01:22.560
as probably more worthwhile to go after

01:22.560 --> 01:25.050
and one that you'll have more success reaching out to.

01:25.050 --> 01:25.920
Now, the first place

01:25.920 --> 01:27.960
and I think probably the best place

01:27.960 --> 01:30.303
to start reaching out to potential customers

01:30.303 --> 01:32.670
that you can talk to is LinkedIn.

01:32.670 --> 01:35.040
I'm sure most of you guys are familiar with LinkedIn,

01:35.040 --> 01:37.170
it's an online social network

01:37.170 --> 01:39.420
that is primarily for networking.

01:39.420 --> 01:43.590
It holds a lot of information about the people that sign up

01:43.590 --> 01:45.210
and a lot of that information is public.

01:45.210 --> 01:48.150
We generally get to see where people go to school,

01:48.150 --> 01:49.200
what their job is,

01:49.200 --> 01:51.840
what they've done in the past, where they live.

01:51.840 --> 01:53.790
We also know generally interests they're in

01:53.790 --> 01:56.029
and groups they participate in.

01:56.029 --> 01:59.592
We also have endorsements that show us what skills they have

01:59.592 --> 02:02.460
so that it's a treasure chest of different ways

02:02.460 --> 02:06.456
that you can narrow down on exactly the target customer

02:06.456 --> 02:07.890
you have in mind.

02:07.890 --> 02:09.840
Now, for those of you out there that have a LinkedIn account

02:09.840 --> 02:12.630
and you have lots of connections, this works extremely well.

02:12.630 --> 02:14.220
For those of you that have an account,

02:14.220 --> 02:15.480
but don't have a lot of connections,

02:15.480 --> 02:18.060
it still works very well.

02:18.060 --> 02:19.830
And for those of you that don't have an account,

02:19.830 --> 02:21.450
I suggest creating one

02:21.450 --> 02:23.670
and connecting with some of the people you know.

02:23.670 --> 02:26.910
It really will only take you 10, 15 minutes

02:26.910 --> 02:29.220
and by this time tomorrow,

02:29.220 --> 02:31.440
you can actually use it to find people.

02:31.440 --> 02:33.300
Now, the best way to use LinkedIn

02:33.300 --> 02:35.610
is actually just to use their search feature.

02:35.610 --> 02:37.050
Now, what the search feature is going to do

02:37.050 --> 02:39.942
is it's gonna search based off of your connections

02:39.942 --> 02:42.390
and then your connections connections.

02:42.390 --> 02:43.223
That's why I say,

02:43.223 --> 02:45.900
if you only have a medium amount of connections,

02:45.900 --> 02:47.700
only 50 people, 20 people,

02:47.700 --> 02:50.670
it still works because as long as you're connected to people

02:50.670 --> 02:54.240
that have a lot of them, then the search

02:54.240 --> 02:56.730
has quite a large group of people to look through.

02:56.730 --> 02:58.980
So if you define your customer group by,

02:58.980 --> 03:01.440
let's say their job type, this is perfect.

03:01.440 --> 03:04.200
You define it based off of their education,

03:04.200 --> 03:06.870
where they went to school, what location they live in.

03:06.870 --> 03:08.250
This also works very well.

03:08.250 --> 03:11.340
So let's use the example of the real estate agents.

03:11.340 --> 03:12.600
If we were building a product

03:12.600 --> 03:15.150
that went after real estate agents or appealed to them,

03:15.150 --> 03:16.830
all they have to do is in the search bar

03:16.830 --> 03:21.510
just type in real estate agent and right at the top it says,

03:21.510 --> 03:24.120
I can pick between people, jobs, and groups.

03:24.120 --> 03:26.220
Those are all very helpful,

03:26.220 --> 03:27.960
but I'm probably just gonna pick people

03:27.960 --> 03:29.970
cause these are actually gonna show me people

03:29.970 --> 03:31.740
that have it in their title,

03:31.740 --> 03:35.490
meaning right now they are a real estate agent

03:35.490 --> 03:38.910
and it'll give me an extensive list of people.

03:38.910 --> 03:40.020
All right, so right off the bat,

03:40.020 --> 03:43.860
I have a tons and tons of people that I could reach out to.

03:43.860 --> 03:48.360
If I then wanted to add some criteria about where they live,

03:48.360 --> 03:50.670
I can use filtering criteria over here.

03:50.670 --> 03:53.520
So maybe I only want to interview people

03:53.520 --> 03:57.180
that are near where I live in San Francisco.

03:57.180 --> 03:59.700
All right, now I have a very good list of people

03:59.700 --> 04:00.960
that I could start to connect with.

04:00.960 --> 04:03.570
I could add in more criteria if I wanted to,

04:03.570 --> 04:05.520
but for our sake, let's not.

04:05.520 --> 04:07.680
Now, if I wanted to contact these people,

04:07.680 --> 04:09.000
the way that you would use LinkedIn

04:09.000 --> 04:10.470
is through one of two means.

04:10.470 --> 04:12.900
One is you can send them what's called an InMail.

04:12.900 --> 04:14.160
An InMail is a message

04:14.160 --> 04:17.400
that goes directly to their message system.

04:17.400 --> 04:19.220
Now, this is a paid option.

04:19.220 --> 04:22.560
I don't think you pay a lot of money for it.

04:22.560 --> 04:24.630
You probably have to do a subscription package

04:24.630 --> 04:27.150
and it gives you a certain amount of InMails.

04:27.150 --> 04:28.740
If this is something you frequently use,

04:28.740 --> 04:30.540
then it's definitely worth doing.

04:30.540 --> 04:33.630
So the second option is to just connect with them

04:33.630 --> 04:34.890
because when you connect with them,

04:34.890 --> 04:37.031
it'll give you the option of writing a message.

04:37.031 --> 04:39.780
So click through to the profile

04:39.780 --> 04:41.370
and then you can hit connect.

04:41.370 --> 04:43.500
And in the message down here,

04:43.500 --> 04:46.590
I can write something along the lines of,

04:46.590 --> 04:48.390
this is what I'm doing and I would like to talk to you.

04:48.390 --> 04:49.890
We covered that in the next lecture.

04:49.890 --> 04:52.200
How do we get them to actually talk to us?

04:52.200 --> 04:53.490
If they don't see this,

04:53.490 --> 04:56.220
they still will probably connect with you.

04:56.220 --> 04:59.100
At which point you can then send them a direct message

04:59.100 --> 05:00.420
and that doesn't cost any money.

05:00.420 --> 05:01.420
All right.

05:01.420 --> 05:04.530
So the number two place that we're gonna cover are forums.

05:04.530 --> 05:06.180
Now, what do I mean by forums?

05:06.180 --> 05:09.060
Well, forums are generally just online communities

05:09.060 --> 05:12.480
that are all built around some kind of theme.

05:12.480 --> 05:15.690
Pretty much any type of affiliation or job

05:15.690 --> 05:19.530
or interest you can think of has a forum

05:19.530 --> 05:22.347
or an online community dedicated to it.

05:22.347 --> 05:24.930
And the reason forums work so well

05:24.930 --> 05:26.280
is because it's a bunch of people

05:26.280 --> 05:30.396
talking about whatever subject or topic you picked,

05:30.396 --> 05:33.900
and it's very easy to search them or find people

05:33.900 --> 05:37.080
that have a problem that you are trying to solve,

05:37.080 --> 05:40.503
have experience in the area you're trying to go into.

05:41.490 --> 05:42.780
It's pretty much easy pickings

05:42.780 --> 05:44.880
cause people will talk about what they're interested in

05:44.880 --> 05:46.530
and they'll self-identify themselves.

05:46.530 --> 05:50.160
The first place I like to start as a website called Reddit.

05:50.160 --> 05:52.800
If you ever heard of Reddit, it's an online forum

05:52.800 --> 05:54.930
and it's absolutely massive.

05:54.930 --> 05:57.030
It's an online community, and the way that it works

05:57.030 --> 05:59.970
is it's actually tons and tons of forums

05:59.970 --> 06:01.860
inside of one forum.

06:01.860 --> 06:04.590
Reddit calls these sub forums, subreddits

06:04.590 --> 06:08.340
and every subreddit in this website

06:08.340 --> 06:11.070
is going to be based off of some type of theme

06:11.070 --> 06:12.810
or topic or subject.

06:12.810 --> 06:14.460
So if you can identify some of the things

06:14.460 --> 06:17.160
that your target customer group have in common,

06:17.160 --> 06:21.319
are interested in or anything that they would affiliate with

06:21.319 --> 06:24.030
you can find a subreddit for that.

06:24.030 --> 06:25.260
The best way of doing that

06:25.260 --> 06:27.420
is to search through their search box

06:27.420 --> 06:29.850
and it'll give you list of subreddits

06:29.850 --> 06:32.820
or what you can do is actually search through Google

06:32.820 --> 06:36.690
and if you use the code site:reddit.com

06:36.690 --> 06:39.990
and then followed by searching for the topic you want,

06:39.990 --> 06:41.310
you can do it that way as well.

06:41.310 --> 06:43.440
Sometimes Google's actually better than Reddit.

06:43.440 --> 06:46.080
So let's see how this works for the real estate idea.

06:46.080 --> 06:49.020
All I'd have to do is say real estate agents right there

06:49.020 --> 06:50.460
and see if it shows me.

06:50.460 --> 06:53.490
And then right off the bat, we have subreddits right here

06:53.490 --> 06:56.940
and it gives you some gauge of how many people are in it.

06:56.940 --> 06:57.773
For the most part,

06:57.773 --> 07:01.830
I can dive into any one of these, start looking around,

07:01.830 --> 07:04.560
start interacting, and then start reaching out to people.

07:04.560 --> 07:07.110
Another great option is a website called Quora.

07:07.110 --> 07:11.863
Now Quora, spelled Q-U-O-R-A.com

07:13.260 --> 07:16.320
is essentially Yahoo answers, but for adults

07:16.320 --> 07:18.720
it's a website where people will ask questions

07:18.720 --> 07:21.000
and then people who have expertise in that subject

07:21.000 --> 07:22.020
will answer them.

07:22.020 --> 07:25.080
And it's a website where people kind of trip over each other

07:25.080 --> 07:26.700
to give the best answer.

07:26.700 --> 07:28.080
But because so many people

07:28.080 --> 07:30.030
have asked so many different questions,

07:30.030 --> 07:32.970
what you can do is look for specific topics

07:32.970 --> 07:36.150
or groups of questions and then look at the people

07:36.150 --> 07:39.240
that are constantly talking in them, answering questions,

07:39.240 --> 07:40.920
interacting and replying.

07:40.920 --> 07:43.299
So I could search for real estate agents

07:43.299 --> 07:46.560
and then right there I can pick the actual topic.

07:46.560 --> 07:48.210
So it's gonna show me all the questions

07:48.210 --> 07:50.520
related to real estate agents.

07:50.520 --> 07:54.330
So all it had have to do is just jump into any one of these

07:54.330 --> 07:57.210
and see if the people that are asking the question

07:57.210 --> 07:58.889
or answering the question

07:58.889 --> 08:01.920
are people that fit my target demographic.

08:01.920 --> 08:04.560
If they are, I can then message them

08:04.560 --> 08:06.030
and try to make a connection.

08:06.030 --> 08:07.249
All right.

08:07.249 --> 08:08.520
The third place that I suggest using

08:08.520 --> 08:12.480
to find potential customers to interview is Twitter.

08:12.480 --> 08:16.830
Twitter is fantastic as a resource for finding people

08:16.830 --> 08:18.480
that might be interested in your service.

08:18.480 --> 08:21.000
The reason why it's so effective at this

08:21.000 --> 08:24.000
is similar to the reason why forums are

08:24.000 --> 08:27.420
in that people are constantly talking on it

08:27.420 --> 08:30.090
and people are constantly self-identifying themselves

08:30.090 --> 08:32.400
with things they're interested in,

08:32.400 --> 08:34.680
things they affiliate themselves with

08:34.680 --> 08:36.810
and what they do for a living.

08:36.810 --> 08:40.320
Twitter's also just a gigantic, complaining fest

08:40.320 --> 08:42.240
which is great because you can find people

08:42.240 --> 08:44.790
that potentially have the problem you're trying to solve

08:44.790 --> 08:45.930
and reach out to them.

08:45.930 --> 08:47.670
What you're actually searching through

08:47.670 --> 08:51.630
when you use this feature is what people are saying

08:51.630 --> 08:54.660
and not specific details about the person.

08:54.660 --> 08:56.490
So if I said real estate agent,

08:56.490 --> 08:59.370
it might not actually be as effective.

08:59.370 --> 09:00.203
Why?

09:00.203 --> 09:01.800
Because real estate agents on Twitter

09:01.800 --> 09:04.500
probably aren't saying real estate agent over

09:04.500 --> 09:06.150
and over and over again.

09:06.150 --> 09:08.550
It might still work, but it's more effective

09:08.550 --> 09:11.250
to think about things they might be saying

09:11.250 --> 09:12.450
and then search for that.

09:12.450 --> 09:14.880
So a real estate agent might be saying something

09:14.880 --> 09:17.710
along the lines of looking to buy a home

09:19.020 --> 09:20.490
and then we can scroll down

09:20.490 --> 09:23.250
and it looks like right off the bat,

09:23.250 --> 09:25.290
here's someone that says, looking for a home?

09:25.290 --> 09:26.883
Are you looking for a home?

09:26.883 --> 09:30.690
Lots of people here are self-identifying

09:30.690 --> 09:32.820
and the first one we captured right here,

09:32.820 --> 09:35.310
he works as a real estate agent.

09:35.310 --> 09:36.270
See how that worked?

09:36.270 --> 09:38.670
Now that we know who they are, we can tweet at them

09:38.670 --> 09:40.440
or we can try to follow them

09:40.440 --> 09:42.690
in the hopes that they follow us back,

09:42.690 --> 09:44.610
at which point we can send them a direct message.

09:44.610 --> 09:46.770
All right, and the last go-to place

09:46.770 --> 09:48.780
for finding potential customers

09:48.780 --> 09:51.630
is it's actually your competitors.

09:51.630 --> 09:52.463
Okay.

09:52.463 --> 09:54.570
90% of the time when you're launching a new product,

09:54.570 --> 09:57.990
they're probably going to be competitors already there.

09:57.990 --> 09:59.670
And if they're not doing the exact same thing,

09:59.670 --> 10:01.230
they're probably doing

10:01.230 --> 10:04.200
at least part of what you propose to do

10:04.200 --> 10:07.523
or they're just servicing the same target customer group.

10:07.523 --> 10:10.230
If you can identify your potential competitors

10:10.230 --> 10:12.600
right out of the gate, well, then it's relatively easy

10:12.600 --> 10:16.410
to just go to their social media pages, see who is engaging

10:16.410 --> 10:17.880
and you can then figure out

10:17.880 --> 10:20.220
that those people probably are customers

10:20.220 --> 10:23.700
or are interested customers for your competitor

10:23.700 --> 10:24.870
and you can contact them.

10:24.870 --> 10:27.360
Let's say we were doing that real estate business

10:27.360 --> 10:28.320
never said what it was,

10:28.320 --> 10:30.840
but let's say one of our competitors was Opendoor.

10:30.840 --> 10:34.290
It's a relatively well known startup.

10:34.290 --> 10:35.640
We can find their page on Twitter

10:35.640 --> 10:38.610
and then just look for who is interacting with it.

10:38.610 --> 10:40.590
We can see who is liking their page.

10:40.590 --> 10:42.330
There's someone who likes it, there's someone who likes it

10:42.330 --> 10:45.390
and these are people that interact with their page.

10:45.390 --> 10:48.600
We can then go through these people's pages

10:48.600 --> 10:50.160
and contact 'em in the same way.

10:50.160 --> 10:52.680
Now, this works the same for Facebook.

10:52.680 --> 10:54.450
This works the same for Twitter.

10:54.450 --> 10:58.170
If your competitor has a blog, you can also go on there

10:58.170 --> 11:01.020
and find people who are commenting on their blog.

11:01.020 --> 11:02.400
That is also a gold mine

11:02.400 --> 11:04.950
for finding potential people to talk to.

11:04.950 --> 11:06.090
All right, so as of today,

11:06.090 --> 11:08.460
those are the four main categories

11:08.460 --> 11:11.040
product managers are probably going to use

11:11.040 --> 11:15.330
to reach out to potential customers that they can interview.

11:15.330 --> 11:19.050
If you need more or are interested, check out the resources.

11:19.050 --> 11:21.600
Otherwise, we're gonna move on to step three,

11:21.600 --> 11:25.260
which is how exactly do you get these people to talk to you?

11:25.260 --> 11:26.460
All right, see you then.

WEBVTT

00:00.150 --> 00:02.010
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:02.010 --> 00:06.510
So, last lecture we talked about where can you find

00:06.510 --> 00:10.380
and drill down on potential customers that you can interview

00:10.380 --> 00:13.860
if you're in a scenario where you are pre-product

00:13.860 --> 00:14.910
or pre-launch.

00:14.910 --> 00:16.470
So the other side of the coin

00:16.470 --> 00:20.670
to this is, what do you do when you are post product?

00:20.670 --> 00:24.450
Let's say you have some ideas for things to add

00:24.450 --> 00:26.220
to a product you already have.

00:26.220 --> 00:29.490
You have a feature list, you have a roadmap,

00:29.490 --> 00:30.660
you have a backlog.

00:30.660 --> 00:32.370
The customer development framework tells us

00:32.370 --> 00:34.410
that we need to get out of the building

00:34.410 --> 00:36.660
and start talking to our customers.

00:36.660 --> 00:40.020
But, where do we find the customers?

00:40.020 --> 00:41.670
Now all you at home are probably thinking,

00:41.670 --> 00:44.220
well isn't this a little more obvious?

00:44.220 --> 00:46.470
And you're right, it is more obvious, presumably

00:46.470 --> 00:51.470
if you're post product, you have a list of customers,

00:51.750 --> 00:56.340
a list of free users, and potentially a list of people

00:56.340 --> 00:59.070
that have demonstrated interest in your product.

00:59.070 --> 01:02.970
Even if you're relatively new, you only launched not

01:02.970 --> 01:05.850
that long ago you'll still have what are called,

01:05.850 --> 01:09.810
early evangelists which are people that use it as

01:09.810 --> 01:12.930
if it were a version for polished product

01:12.930 --> 01:13.890
and they just love it.

01:13.890 --> 01:15.000
Regardless of where you are

01:15.000 --> 01:16.770
in this product development process,

01:16.770 --> 01:19.290
there are customers you can interview.

01:19.290 --> 01:22.560
Now, the most obvious thing is that why don't you just go

01:22.560 --> 01:27.560
and look at the list of customers and just go that route?

01:27.570 --> 01:29.190
Now, you can do that.

01:29.190 --> 01:30.990
You can just tap someone on the shoulder

01:30.990 --> 01:33.840
and say, "Hey, I need the list of all the emails

01:33.840 --> 01:36.960
of all of our users and let me contact them."

01:36.960 --> 01:39.030
Well, there's two downsides to doing that.

01:39.030 --> 01:42.720
One is that, it comes off very spammy

01:42.720 --> 01:46.110
and it actually can break your terms and agreement.

01:46.110 --> 01:48.840
So you need to make sure that that's within the bounds

01:48.840 --> 01:52.440
of spam and what's just not gonna annoy the crap out

01:52.440 --> 01:53.273
of people.

01:53.273 --> 01:54.106
The other thing is

01:54.106 --> 01:57.840
that you're gonna have a harder time getting people to talk

01:57.840 --> 02:01.350
to you just because this is a cold email.

02:01.350 --> 02:04.440
This is as a cold as it would get.

02:04.440 --> 02:07.050
You're just emailing them 'cause you saw their email

02:07.050 --> 02:07.950
on a list.

02:07.950 --> 02:10.440
It'd be hard even to customize it for them.

02:10.440 --> 02:12.360
So you can by all means, just send

02:12.360 --> 02:14.610
out an email blast to your user base

02:14.610 --> 02:16.500
and you will get interviews that way.

02:16.500 --> 02:18.210
But, in this lecture we're gonna focus

02:18.210 --> 02:23.210
on four ways to get warm leads that will save you some time

02:23.640 --> 02:27.270
with regards to finding them and getting them to talk to you

02:27.270 --> 02:30.240
and is probably going to get you better customers

02:30.240 --> 02:31.073
to talk to.

02:31.073 --> 02:34.680
In general, some of the best customers to talk to are ones

02:34.680 --> 02:38.760
that have already pointed themselves out as people

02:38.760 --> 02:43.740
that are enthusiastic or interested or have a problem

02:43.740 --> 02:45.420
with something in your product.

02:45.420 --> 02:48.240
People that have opinions and people that are invested

02:48.240 --> 02:50.280
in your product enough to have one

02:50.280 --> 02:52.590
of those two emotions are going

02:52.590 --> 02:55.917
to give you a much bigger bounty of information.

02:55.917 --> 02:58.110
Now the first way of going about this is

02:58.110 --> 03:01.860
by using your company's live chat feature.

03:01.860 --> 03:03.834
Now, a lot of companies,

03:03.834 --> 03:08.670
startups even large businesses use live chat systems.

03:08.670 --> 03:11.610
Live chat systems are exactly what they sound like.

03:11.610 --> 03:14.610
You can chat with someone in the company live, by clicking

03:14.610 --> 03:17.100
on a box, usually in the bottom right of your screen.

03:17.100 --> 03:19.860
Whether it's Olark or Tawk to or one

03:19.860 --> 03:24.210
of the more complicated ones like intercom.io.

03:24.210 --> 03:27.480
If you have them, then you have a wealth of information

03:27.480 --> 03:30.900
from which you can choose ideal customers to reach out to.

03:30.900 --> 03:33.840
So if you have a live chat system, great, continue on.

03:33.840 --> 03:36.000
Now, there's two ways of going about this.

03:36.000 --> 03:38.880
One is the passive and one is the active.

03:38.880 --> 03:42.720
In the passive approach, what you wanna do is essentially go

03:42.720 --> 03:46.980
and ask to see the chat logs from your customers

03:46.980 --> 03:50.100
and what they're saying and what questions they're asking.

03:50.100 --> 03:52.380
It's really interesting stuff on its own

03:52.380 --> 03:54.480
but, what you can do is you can look

03:54.480 --> 03:58.800
at what people are saying and try to find individual people

03:58.800 --> 04:00.720
that meet your target demo.

04:00.720 --> 04:03.120
Then when you message them you can pull information

04:03.120 --> 04:05.460
from their chat log, make it sound

04:05.460 --> 04:07.530
like a continuation of the conversation.

04:07.530 --> 04:10.290
People are much more likely to respond to that

04:10.290 --> 04:12.750
and you get plus points for customer service.

04:12.750 --> 04:16.470
Now the other approach is the more active version.

04:16.470 --> 04:19.350
A lot of live chat systems, the vast majority

04:19.350 --> 04:22.230
of them have the functionality that allows them

04:22.230 --> 04:25.080
to force themselves on your screen.

04:25.080 --> 04:27.390
So, you might have seen this before.

04:27.390 --> 04:29.730
If you're ever on a website and you have something pop up

04:29.730 --> 04:31.770
as you go to another subpage

04:31.770 --> 04:33.300
and it usually says something

04:33.300 --> 04:35.040
like, do you need help finding something?

04:35.040 --> 04:36.570
You say yes or no.

04:36.570 --> 04:38.490
You say yes, it automatically connects you.

04:38.490 --> 04:39.323
If you have

04:39.323 --> 04:42.300
that functionality, you can definitely find people

04:42.300 --> 04:46.350
that appear to be doing things that you think would fit

04:46.350 --> 04:48.870
into your target demo or you can look

04:48.870 --> 04:52.110
for specific users when they get online

04:52.110 --> 04:53.700
or you can just do it at random.

04:53.700 --> 04:55.830
When you force a conversation then you can pop up,

04:55.830 --> 04:58.920
you can talk to them and go that route.

04:58.920 --> 05:01.410
Alternatively, if you don't have that capability,

05:01.410 --> 05:02.243
that's fine.

05:02.243 --> 05:05.970
A much simpler version of this active approach is either

05:05.970 --> 05:09.480
to sit in on the customer service live chats,

05:09.480 --> 05:12.570
if you're in a small organizations is very easy.

05:12.570 --> 05:15.480
Or ask someone who is in customer service to

05:15.480 --> 05:17.580
at the very end of their chats,

05:17.580 --> 05:20.700
once they've solved the problem just simply ask them

05:20.700 --> 05:22.636
if they would be willing to talk

05:22.636 --> 05:26.850
to someone in a product management role for 10 minutes.

05:26.850 --> 05:30.030
It's very similar to when you call the bank and they say,

05:30.030 --> 05:31.710
after your phone call would you like

05:31.710 --> 05:33.750
to take a five minute survey?

05:33.750 --> 05:35.100
Yes or no?

05:35.100 --> 05:37.860
A lot of people say no, but a lot of people say yes.

05:37.860 --> 05:40.500
So don't ignore the live chat. This is some potent stuff.

05:40.500 --> 05:43.080
These people have self-identified themselves

05:43.080 --> 05:45.690
as being engaged with your product.

05:45.690 --> 05:48.600
The second treasure trove where you can go

05:48.600 --> 05:52.140
to get people to interview is your blog.

05:52.140 --> 05:55.230
Now, most companies have blogs.

05:55.230 --> 05:56.760
If you don't have a blog nowadays it's kind

05:56.760 --> 05:58.800
of the exception.

05:58.800 --> 06:01.410
It's kind of standard practice it seems like.

06:01.410 --> 06:05.880
So, if you have a blog, a lot of blogs allow commenting.

06:05.880 --> 06:07.380
So, it's rather simple.

06:07.380 --> 06:09.990
Look at the people who are commenting on your blog.

06:09.990 --> 06:12.720
If you don't have a blog, look for people who are commenting

06:12.720 --> 06:15.630
on your outside external press.

06:15.630 --> 06:18.180
Those people have something to say about your product.

06:18.180 --> 06:23.180
They're probably users and also you have this advantage.

06:24.090 --> 06:26.370
They want to be heard, okay?

06:26.370 --> 06:29.400
And that's the reason why they're posting on your blog

06:29.400 --> 06:30.233
to begin with.

06:30.233 --> 06:31.290
They have something to say.

06:31.290 --> 06:34.380
No one posts a comment, it's entirely passive

06:34.380 --> 06:35.940
that says nothing.

06:35.940 --> 06:38.070
They always want to be heard.

06:38.070 --> 06:40.140
And if you message those people, email them

06:40.140 --> 06:41.820
and say "Would you like to talk

06:41.820 --> 06:43.770
for 10 minutes about the product?"

06:43.770 --> 06:46.650
They will feel special and it will convert.

06:46.650 --> 06:50.310
Now a third area to consider are power users.

06:50.310 --> 06:53.640
So we previously established that you probably have a list

06:53.640 --> 06:55.860
of all of your customers and

06:55.860 --> 06:58.590
if your organization even slightly knows what they're doing

06:58.590 --> 07:02.070
they're probably collecting information on each user.

07:02.070 --> 07:07.070
So, all you have to do is look specifically for power users.

07:07.560 --> 07:10.200
Now you can segment power users

07:10.200 --> 07:11.970
by a number of different things.

07:11.970 --> 07:14.730
You can look for them based off of frequency.

07:14.730 --> 07:16.650
How often do they use your application?

07:16.650 --> 07:19.950
And that's generally a very good metric.

07:19.950 --> 07:22.170
People who are only casual about your product,

07:22.170 --> 07:23.700
probably don't log in very often

07:23.700 --> 07:26.310
and they probably don't use the features very often.

07:26.310 --> 07:28.350
These are very easy things to track.

07:28.350 --> 07:29.250
Be very surprised

07:29.250 --> 07:31.050
if your organization was not tracking them.

07:31.050 --> 07:35.070
You could also look for things like high engagement

07:35.070 --> 07:37.380
and every organization has a different definition

07:37.380 --> 07:38.580
of engagement.

07:38.580 --> 07:40.830
It could be buying things often,

07:40.830 --> 07:45.830
it could be sending messages often, it could just be people

07:46.530 --> 07:51.530
that use it frequently and message your own company often.

07:51.540 --> 07:53.490
So don't neglect power users.

07:53.490 --> 07:57.000
They are in general more informed about your product.

07:57.000 --> 07:58.530
They spend more time using it.

07:58.530 --> 08:02.490
And here's the catch, they're more invested in your product.

08:02.490 --> 08:03.780
So if you say

08:03.780 --> 08:06.540
to them, "Let's have a conversation about improving it."

08:06.540 --> 08:09.300
There's a very good chance they'll be interested in that.

08:09.300 --> 08:11.070
And the last one is Twitter.

08:11.070 --> 08:13.710
I love using Twitter for talking to customers

08:13.710 --> 08:15.600
because it's fantastic for that.

08:15.600 --> 08:18.270
It's just an online machine for conversations.

08:18.270 --> 08:20.760
Twitter is a gold mine for reaching out to these people

08:20.760 --> 08:22.230
and it's really simple.

08:22.230 --> 08:23.700
You guys probably have a Twitter account.

08:23.700 --> 08:27.390
Just look at who is tweeting at you, who is replying,

08:27.390 --> 08:30.720
who is liking, and who is sharing your posts.

08:30.720 --> 08:32.370
Chances are they already follow you,

08:32.370 --> 08:34.890
which means you can direct message them.

08:34.890 --> 08:38.460
Those messages get answered almost all of the time.

08:38.460 --> 08:40.290
So they've essentially self-identified themselves

08:40.290 --> 08:43.560
as saying, "Hey, we like your product enough

08:43.560 --> 08:46.710
to spend time talking about it or engaging with you."

08:46.710 --> 08:47.543
They have their foot

08:47.543 --> 08:49.410
in the door, they're probably going to talk to you.

08:49.410 --> 08:51.960
Okay guys, so those were four different ways

08:51.960 --> 08:54.840
that you within an organization and

08:54.840 --> 08:56.490
with a preexisting product,

08:56.490 --> 09:01.287
can pick out customers who are most likely to respond

09:01.287 --> 09:05.070
to you and give you better information than

09:05.070 --> 09:06.600
if you pick someone at random.

09:06.600 --> 09:10.620
So use those four tricks yourself live chat, commenters,

09:10.620 --> 09:13.200
Twitter, and look for power users.

09:13.200 --> 09:14.670
In the next lecture, we're moving on

09:14.670 --> 09:19.290
to step three, which is getting them to talk to us

09:19.290 --> 09:21.630
and it's a very weird dance.

09:21.630 --> 09:22.830
All right, see you then.

response.title: How to Get Them to Talk

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.150 --> 00:01.500
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:01.500 --> 00:04.980
So now that we've identified who you want to interview,

00:04.980 --> 00:07.530
we're gonna talk about how do you get them to talk?

00:07.530 --> 00:10.650
Now a lot of people think if I just message someone

00:10.650 --> 00:14.670
out of the blue, why would they respond to me?

00:14.670 --> 00:18.060
Why would they give me their time just because?

00:18.060 --> 00:19.920
So in this lecture, we're gonna address that.

00:19.920 --> 00:21.900
We're gonna go over three principles

00:21.900 --> 00:25.560
to live by when asking to interview a customer

00:25.560 --> 00:29.700
and some two bonus tips that'll put you over the edge.

00:29.700 --> 00:31.320
To those of you that nodded

00:31.320 --> 00:35.040
when I said why would they actually contact us?

00:35.040 --> 00:36.900
Well, you know, let's talk about it.

00:36.900 --> 00:40.380
It's rather obvious that if you're cold emailing someone,

00:40.380 --> 00:42.450
even if you use the techniques that we covered

00:42.450 --> 00:43.830
in the last two lectures,

00:43.830 --> 00:46.260
it's still a relatively cold email.

00:46.260 --> 00:48.330
They have no personal connection to you

00:48.330 --> 00:50.940
and we all know how we treat emails

00:50.940 --> 00:53.760
from people we don't know, right?

00:53.760 --> 00:55.410
You probably don't remember them.

00:55.410 --> 00:57.000
They go to spam,

00:57.000 --> 01:00.300
or you delete them faster than you can even notice.

01:00.300 --> 01:02.850
The thing is, in general with cold emailing,

01:02.850 --> 01:05.520
you have to think of it kind of from

01:05.520 --> 01:07.320
the perspective of investment.

01:07.320 --> 01:09.030
If someone doesn't know you,

01:09.030 --> 01:11.670
they may be willing to read your email,

01:11.670 --> 01:14.760
but they are not going to be willing

01:14.760 --> 01:18.420
to invest the time to read the entire thing.

01:18.420 --> 01:19.710
If they're talking to a friend

01:19.710 --> 01:21.750
and a friend let's say, emails them,

01:21.750 --> 01:24.420
they'll probably read a page right before spacing out.

01:24.420 --> 01:27.060
But if someone emails 'em, they have no idea who you are,

01:27.060 --> 01:30.360
and they're not referring to any previous conversation,

01:30.360 --> 01:32.130
they might give you a couple sentences.

01:32.130 --> 01:35.520
This can be overcome and it's relatively easy.

01:35.520 --> 01:38.640
So, let's talk about three things to live by,

01:38.640 --> 01:40.830
three tenants of cold emailing.

01:40.830 --> 01:42.540
If you follow these three,

01:42.540 --> 01:45.090
you will have a dramatic increase

01:45.090 --> 01:47.580
in the number of people that respond.

01:47.580 --> 01:50.710
Side note though, to put this in perspective,

01:50.710 --> 01:54.540
we generally have a ratio three to one

01:54.540 --> 01:57.508
which we use to reference how many people you contact

01:57.508 --> 01:59.430
and how many people respond.

01:59.430 --> 02:01.410
Now, obviously this is a generalization,

02:01.410 --> 02:04.500
but set your expectations like that.

02:04.500 --> 02:07.860
If you message 10 people and three people respond,

02:07.860 --> 02:09.360
don't get mad at the other seven.

02:09.360 --> 02:11.460
It's just the case that

02:11.460 --> 02:13.350
you generally have to message three times

02:13.350 --> 02:14.790
as many people that you want to interview.

02:14.790 --> 02:16.080
And also keep in mind,

02:16.080 --> 02:18.450
let's say you want to interview 10 people,

02:18.450 --> 02:20.040
how many people should you message?

02:20.040 --> 02:22.800
Probably 30, maybe more to be safe.

02:22.800 --> 02:23.633
Anyway, enough of that.

02:23.633 --> 02:25.710
Let's back to the three main tenants.

02:25.710 --> 02:28.890
Okay, so the number one tenant when contacting someone

02:28.890 --> 02:33.660
in a cold connection type situation is be short.

02:33.660 --> 02:34.493
You probably figured this out

02:34.493 --> 02:35.864
'cause I was just talking about

02:35.864 --> 02:40.099
how people aren't gonna give you a lot of time investment.

02:40.099 --> 02:44.010
So, being short is incredibly important.

02:44.010 --> 02:47.760
It's one of the biggest mistakes I see people making.

02:47.760 --> 02:50.010
They'll message someone that they want to contact

02:50.010 --> 02:53.580
and they'll send pages and pages.

02:53.580 --> 02:55.170
This happens to me all the time.

02:55.170 --> 02:57.389
Somehow people get my email address

02:57.389 --> 03:01.950
and send me something, and it's three pages long.

03:01.950 --> 03:03.090
It's ridiculous.

03:03.090 --> 03:06.360
No one under any circumstance is going to read that.

03:06.360 --> 03:09.390
So focus yourself on brevity.

03:09.390 --> 03:12.450
Now, what do I mean specifically when I say short?

03:12.450 --> 03:13.410
How short is short?

03:13.410 --> 03:17.700
Should it be like a tweet or is short like four paragraphs?

03:17.700 --> 03:21.120
Short generally means closer to the tweet

03:21.120 --> 03:22.661
than the five paragraphs.

03:22.661 --> 03:27.661
I like to think of it in terms of four to seven sentences.

03:28.050 --> 03:31.260
If you can get it away in four, that's great,

03:31.260 --> 03:33.510
but seven is the absolute maximum.

03:33.510 --> 03:34.350
Anything over that

03:34.350 --> 03:37.290
and you'll see your response rates just plummet.

03:37.290 --> 03:39.300
Number two, be personal.

03:39.300 --> 03:43.380
People do not want to be emailed from a robot.

03:43.380 --> 03:45.733
We don't like talking to robots.

03:45.733 --> 03:50.224
If I even sniff or have the tiniest little inkling

03:50.224 --> 03:52.890
that this is an autogenerated email,

03:52.890 --> 03:56.700
that dramatically lowers the chances of me responding to it.

03:56.700 --> 04:00.330
Now, you're not going to be messaging millions of people

04:00.330 --> 04:01.950
or even thousands of people,

04:01.950 --> 04:04.352
so there's really no excuse for not adding

04:04.352 --> 04:07.170
at least a tiny bit of personalization.

04:07.170 --> 04:09.270
The most effective way of being personal

04:09.270 --> 04:13.200
is by responding to how you found them, okay?

04:13.200 --> 04:14.520
If this is a person you found

04:14.520 --> 04:16.380
on Twitter complaining about something,

04:16.380 --> 04:19.320
you need to immediately make a connection to that.

04:19.320 --> 04:22.590
Say that you saw that they had this problem,

04:22.590 --> 04:24.600
you recognize that it's a problem,

04:24.600 --> 04:27.780
and then you go into the asking them to talk to you.

04:27.780 --> 04:30.780
That email cannot possibly be autogenerated.

04:30.780 --> 04:33.090
People are much more likely to respond

04:33.090 --> 04:34.898
if they actually think you typed it out

04:34.898 --> 04:38.100
and you don't have to type every single one out.

04:38.100 --> 04:39.810
You can make a semi template

04:39.810 --> 04:42.720
where you're only modifying one or two sentences

04:42.720 --> 04:44.347
of the four to seven,

04:44.347 --> 04:47.430
but you have to write those one to two.

04:47.430 --> 04:49.650
If you found someone who wrote a blog piece

04:49.650 --> 04:50.700
and you're connecting with them,

04:50.700 --> 04:52.950
then you need to say something about their piece

04:52.950 --> 04:57.950
that you had a connection with, that you responded to.

04:57.960 --> 04:59.760
Point out some kind of connection

04:59.760 --> 05:01.170
between something they mentioned

05:01.170 --> 05:03.120
and something you've experienced yourself.

05:03.120 --> 05:08.100
And the last tenant of cold emailing is be valuable.

05:08.100 --> 05:11.130
Yes, you are correct in saying that everyone out there

05:11.130 --> 05:14.670
for the most part will consider their own self-interest

05:14.670 --> 05:17.640
when answering your email and thinking about

05:17.640 --> 05:19.860
whether or not they want to spend time talking to you.

05:19.860 --> 05:21.630
Now, I'm not saying that you have to bribe

05:21.630 --> 05:23.640
every single person that you talk to,

05:23.640 --> 05:26.944
but you need to show that having this conversation with you

05:26.944 --> 05:29.640
is valuable in some way to them.

05:29.640 --> 05:31.650
Now that could be abstract things

05:31.650 --> 05:35.130
as simple as it would help our organization out.

05:35.130 --> 05:37.950
A lot of people want to feel altruistic.

05:37.950 --> 05:39.784
They wanna feel like they're helping.

05:39.784 --> 05:42.060
That ultimately is valuable to them.

05:42.060 --> 05:44.550
Telling them that you value their input

05:44.550 --> 05:47.910
and there's a high likelihood that you'll act on it,

05:47.910 --> 05:49.350
makes them feel like they actually

05:49.350 --> 05:52.170
have an effect on the organization.

05:52.170 --> 05:54.323
It's not like voting for the president.

05:54.323 --> 05:55.740
This actually matters.

05:55.740 --> 05:58.830
And yes, of course, you can offer to incentivize them,

05:58.830 --> 06:01.373
and that's something that you're almost always

06:01.373 --> 06:04.230
gonna try at some point.

06:04.230 --> 06:07.830
It is very effective and it is something

06:07.830 --> 06:12.555
you can use to quickly get people to agree to talk to you.

06:12.555 --> 06:16.110
But it's not the case that you have to grease the wheel

06:16.110 --> 06:17.580
for every single person.

06:17.580 --> 06:20.370
You can appeal to their better self.

06:20.370 --> 06:21.545
Okay, got it.

06:21.545 --> 06:23.850
You open up that email client,

06:23.850 --> 06:27.219
you better make sure you are four to seven sentence short,

06:27.219 --> 06:29.160
short, short, short.

06:29.160 --> 06:32.340
You better make sure that you sound personal,

06:32.340 --> 06:36.030
not like a robot that just autogenerated this.

06:36.030 --> 06:39.540
You need to connect it to something they did

06:39.540 --> 06:42.960
or something you've experienced that is similar to them.

06:42.960 --> 06:46.260
It needs to be customized and it needs to be valuable.

06:46.260 --> 06:49.620
Why should they spend time talking to you?

06:49.620 --> 06:51.120
You can butter them up with money

06:51.120 --> 06:52.770
and gift cards if you want,

06:52.770 --> 06:55.277
but you can also just appeal to the fact that

06:55.277 --> 06:57.780
they could help you out

06:57.780 --> 06:59.940
and a lot of people are okay with that.

06:59.940 --> 07:01.560
Now, before you run out there

07:01.560 --> 07:04.320
and just start willy-nilly emailing people

07:04.320 --> 07:06.120
to see if they'll talk to you,

07:06.120 --> 07:08.220
there's two other tips that I want to give you

07:08.220 --> 07:11.340
that'll really put you over the top as a product manager.

07:11.340 --> 07:14.040
Two other things that I really insist on

07:14.040 --> 07:17.275
trying to fit into cold emails is, one,

07:17.275 --> 07:21.090
make sure that you mention that you're not from sales.

07:21.090 --> 07:23.640
That is an amazing advantage to you.

07:23.640 --> 07:26.550
I would say 60% of the people that don't respond

07:26.550 --> 07:29.790
just think that it's because if they talk to you,

07:29.790 --> 07:31.170
you're gonna try to sell them something.

07:31.170 --> 07:34.170
So if you say you're not in sales,

07:34.170 --> 07:36.660
you're in a different department or a different role,

07:36.660 --> 07:39.870
the title of product manager is very innocuous.

07:39.870 --> 07:43.440
No one's gonna think that at the end of this conversation,

07:43.440 --> 07:47.070
you're gonna pitch them on a timeshare condo in Florida.

07:47.070 --> 07:50.040
This is an advantage that is specific to you

07:50.040 --> 07:53.670
and even founders of companies can't do this.

07:53.670 --> 07:55.560
They can't say I'm not in sales,

07:55.560 --> 07:58.050
because if they're the ones emailing people,

07:58.050 --> 08:00.090
that's because they don't have any salespeople

08:00.090 --> 08:04.740
or have product managers and founders do a lot of selling,

08:04.740 --> 08:06.360
so it doesn't really work that way.

08:06.360 --> 08:08.460
The second thing that'll really increase your chances

08:08.460 --> 08:11.910
of connecting are make them feel special.

08:11.910 --> 08:14.310
You really need to emphasize that

08:14.310 --> 08:18.060
you want to understand their specific problems,

08:18.060 --> 08:21.600
and build products that fix their problems,

08:21.600 --> 08:24.540
and that they can help you get there.

08:24.540 --> 08:27.390
That sort of Neo you are the only one

08:27.390 --> 08:31.942
type kind of conversation makes them feel like, well,

08:31.942 --> 08:34.680
I'm the only one who can actually help them.

08:34.680 --> 08:37.170
This is really a waste if I don't.

08:37.170 --> 08:39.150
Alternatively, you can make it sound like the reason

08:39.150 --> 08:40.170
why you're reaching out to them

08:40.170 --> 08:43.290
is because they have done something right.

08:43.290 --> 08:45.420
Give them the VIP treatment.

08:45.420 --> 08:47.310
Make it sound like, you know,

08:47.310 --> 08:50.670
we listen to you more than we listen to other people.

08:50.670 --> 08:54.300
I bet you didn't know this, but Udemy actually does this.

08:54.300 --> 08:57.990
They use the VIP treatment to get feedback from people.

08:57.990 --> 09:02.100
They actually have an account manager system

09:02.100 --> 09:07.100
that is only offered to a very select group of instructors.

09:07.290 --> 09:09.780
So make them feel special.

09:09.780 --> 09:12.780
Make it sound like you are giving them special treatment.

09:12.780 --> 09:13.800
They have information

09:13.800 --> 09:16.230
that is more valuable than anybody else.

09:16.230 --> 09:19.530
They will melt for it, especially if they're power users.

09:19.530 --> 09:21.240
All right guys, we've covered the concepts

09:21.240 --> 09:22.740
of how to get them to talk to you.

09:22.740 --> 09:25.950
In the next lecture, let's actually start doing it.

09:25.950 --> 09:28.320
I'll open up an email client

09:28.320 --> 09:32.220
and show you how these three principles to tips

09:32.220 --> 09:35.790
translate into actual email communication.

09:35.790 --> 09:36.990
All right, see you then.

WEBVTT

00:00.180 --> 00:02.313
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:04.920 --> 00:08.790
how do you reach out and get these potential customers

00:08.790 --> 00:10.710
or customers you already have

00:10.710 --> 00:13.320
to connect with you and give you feedback.

00:13.320 --> 00:15.450
It's a bit of an ask to get someone

00:15.450 --> 00:18.900
either on the phone or in person.

00:18.900 --> 00:22.830
So need to have some skill with it.

00:22.830 --> 00:26.010
So let's get some practice in the actual realm

00:26.010 --> 00:29.460
where you're going to be contacting them, your email client.

00:29.460 --> 00:33.060
So I just kinda whipped open this email right here

00:33.060 --> 00:35.670
and we can use this as a practicing space.

00:35.670 --> 00:36.503
I wrote at the bottom

00:36.503 --> 00:40.440
remember the three things, short, personable, and valuable.

00:40.440 --> 00:41.580
We need to be short,

00:41.580 --> 00:45.390
four to seven sentences, less is more.

00:45.390 --> 00:48.150
Personal, we need to make sure that whatever we say

00:48.150 --> 00:50.640
to the person is specific to them

00:50.640 --> 00:52.320
doesn't feel like a robot is saying it.

00:52.320 --> 00:54.450
And valuable, we need to highlight

00:54.450 --> 00:58.500
that they can get some sort of benefit from talking to us.

00:58.500 --> 01:01.260
Alright, building off of what we said in the last lecture

01:01.260 --> 01:04.186
on how in general people are probably gonna read only one

01:04.186 --> 01:07.140
or two sentences of what you send them.

01:07.140 --> 01:08.610
Since this is a cold email

01:08.610 --> 01:11.610
we need to front load our emails and make sure

01:11.610 --> 01:15.035
that whatever we write that's personal to them

01:15.035 --> 01:17.100
we make sure that's at the beginning.

01:17.100 --> 01:18.451
You can make a crucial mistake

01:18.451 --> 01:22.590
by using your template at the beginning of your email.

01:22.590 --> 01:23.730
That's what they read.

01:23.730 --> 01:25.770
They think it's an auto system sending it

01:25.770 --> 01:27.240
to them and they bail.

01:27.240 --> 01:29.040
So the first thing you want to do is write

01:29.040 --> 01:32.310
one to two sentences of an introduction.

01:32.310 --> 01:33.330
Now, when I say introduction

01:33.330 --> 01:37.095
I don't mean introduce yourself necessarily.

01:37.095 --> 01:40.680
One of the things that people generally dislike is

01:40.680 --> 01:42.660
that in the first couple of sentences

01:42.660 --> 01:47.059
they hear you talk about yourself and not them.

01:47.059 --> 01:49.200
That's another crucial mistake people make

01:49.200 --> 01:50.610
when reaching out to people.

01:50.610 --> 01:53.070
They make it all about me, me, me, me, me.

01:53.070 --> 01:54.390
When in reality it needs to be

01:54.390 --> 01:56.520
about the person who's receiving it

01:56.520 --> 01:58.050
because they're the ones that are, you know

01:58.050 --> 01:59.700
you're asking them to respond.

01:59.700 --> 02:01.140
This is where we get personal

02:01.140 --> 02:04.408
and whatever you write here, it needs to be specific.

02:04.408 --> 02:07.380
I read your blog post on blank.

02:07.380 --> 02:09.150
It was great.

02:09.150 --> 02:11.550
I've noticed that you are struggling

02:11.550 --> 02:14.850
with X feature and our product.

02:14.850 --> 02:18.120
I saw that you had a problem with this.

02:18.120 --> 02:20.970
A lot of people have that problem and I'd like to help you.

02:20.970 --> 02:23.100
The next thing you want to do is generally

02:23.100 --> 02:26.370
write two sentences of why you want to talk.

02:26.370 --> 02:30.090
Now this is your opportunity to insert the value portion

02:30.090 --> 02:31.470
of your email.

02:31.470 --> 02:33.360
Once they get to this section

02:33.360 --> 02:35.880
this is where you kind of hook them in

02:35.880 --> 02:39.298
and give them an incentive to actually respond.

02:39.298 --> 02:44.298
Now, two classic go-to ways of adding value

02:44.610 --> 02:48.780
in these these cold emails is to do one of two things.

02:48.780 --> 02:50.793
One, you can say, you know

02:50.793 --> 02:54.780
I think I can help you use our product better,

02:54.780 --> 02:58.770
in the case of a post-product customer

02:58.770 --> 03:01.950
or in the case of a pre-product potential customer

03:01.950 --> 03:04.230
you say something along the lines

03:04.230 --> 03:08.970
of how you specifically want to solve their problem.

03:08.970 --> 03:13.020
Those always seem to be the go-to sentences.

03:13.020 --> 03:14.820
You see this pretty much everywhere.

03:14.820 --> 03:17.308
If you've ever received an email, they probably said

03:17.308 --> 03:21.074
that I would like to solve this problem for you

03:21.074 --> 03:25.590
or I can show you how you can get more out of our product.

03:25.590 --> 03:27.990
Alternatively, if you didn't wanna use those two

03:27.990 --> 03:30.240
you can go a different route.

03:30.240 --> 03:32.580
Now, there are three different avenues

03:32.580 --> 03:36.120
or paths I think you can use that are quite effective.

03:36.120 --> 03:39.060
One is instead of just saying generically

03:39.060 --> 03:43.230
you have a problem, I wanna solve it, you appeal to pride.

03:43.230 --> 03:48.035
Pride is a very, very useful thing to use

03:48.035 --> 03:50.400
when you want someone to respond to you.

03:50.400 --> 03:54.360
What I mean is you have the opportunity to say things like

03:54.360 --> 03:57.850
you appear to be an expert on blank.

03:57.850 --> 04:01.920
You would be able to give us so much valuable information.

04:01.920 --> 04:05.910
Everyone likes to hear that someone has recognized

04:05.910 --> 04:09.120
you know more information or you are better

04:09.120 --> 04:11.130
in some way than everybody else.

04:11.130 --> 04:12.810
If you wanna appeal to the pride of

04:12.810 --> 04:14.340
someone who's an existing customer

04:14.340 --> 04:15.930
you could say things like

04:15.930 --> 04:18.510
you have been a customer so much longer

04:18.510 --> 04:21.180
than all of our other customers

04:21.180 --> 04:24.600
or it seems like you understand our product much better

04:24.600 --> 04:26.160
than other customers.

04:26.160 --> 04:27.000
And that relates back

04:27.000 --> 04:28.980
to what we talked in the previous lecture.

04:28.980 --> 04:30.630
Make them feel special.

04:30.630 --> 04:33.750
That is one technique that is always open to you

04:33.750 --> 04:36.780
as a product manager and it is very effective.

04:36.780 --> 04:41.010
The second avenue you can go down is appeal to money.

04:41.010 --> 04:43.110
You can use this section,

04:43.110 --> 04:48.000
this two sentence section to say why you want to talk

04:48.000 --> 04:51.690
and then incentivize them by saying that there's some sort

04:51.690 --> 04:54.840
of monetary reward at the end of that rainbow.

04:54.840 --> 04:56.490
Who doesn't like money?

04:56.490 --> 04:59.905
The third thing you can do is imply association.

04:59.905 --> 05:04.740
So what I mean by association is you can imply

05:04.740 --> 05:09.740
that you know people or are well situated

05:10.950 --> 05:14.730
in a space that the person you're emailing cares about.

05:14.730 --> 05:16.380
Oftentimes what you can do is you can look

05:16.380 --> 05:19.500
at their LinkedIn profile, see what space they're in

05:19.500 --> 05:23.100
and you can imply that by connecting with you

05:23.100 --> 05:26.910
you know a lot of other people, that in itself is valuable.

05:26.910 --> 05:27.960
Some people do that.

05:27.960 --> 05:29.910
Some people are more than willing to talk just

05:29.910 --> 05:32.940
because they think you're a valuable contact.

05:32.940 --> 05:35.910
After that we have one last thing that you want to do

05:35.910 --> 05:38.340
and this one is amazingly crucial.

05:38.340 --> 05:40.590
The last thing you wanna do is schedule a time.

05:40.590 --> 05:45.030
Now do you say, are you willing to talk?

05:45.030 --> 05:45.863
No.

05:45.863 --> 05:49.200
Do you say, do you have any time next week?

05:49.200 --> 05:50.033
No.

05:50.033 --> 05:53.100
What you say is, are you available

05:53.100 --> 05:57.690
Wednesday at 3:30 PM Pacific Standard Standard time?

05:57.690 --> 06:00.131
What you're doing here is you're reducing the friction

06:00.131 --> 06:04.020
in between them responding and talking to you.

06:04.020 --> 06:06.090
If you say next week and they're interested

06:06.090 --> 06:08.220
the next time they'll say yes, which day

06:08.220 --> 06:09.450
and you'll say, how about Tuesday?

06:09.450 --> 06:11.910
And they'll say, great, propose a time.

06:11.910 --> 06:13.890
It just goes on and on and on.

06:13.890 --> 06:15.780
Went from one email to six emails.

06:15.780 --> 06:17.730
If you just pick a time and say it

06:17.730 --> 06:19.994
they're much more likely to say yes

06:19.994 --> 06:22.710
and you don't run the risk of losing them

06:22.710 --> 06:25.500
throughout that process of trying to schedule them.

06:25.500 --> 06:26.880
Don't forget that one.

06:26.880 --> 06:30.180
You've probably seen that technique in your inbox.

06:30.180 --> 06:32.190
Another pro tip you can use

06:32.190 --> 06:34.410
in conjunction with this is use some

06:34.410 --> 06:38.700
of those online scheduling is like Calendly.

06:38.700 --> 06:40.140
There's a ton of them

06:40.140 --> 06:41.640
where you don't even have to suggest a time

06:41.640 --> 06:44.602
you just let them click through and pick a time.

06:44.602 --> 06:48.720
All right, so let's look at a well crafted email

06:48.720 --> 06:51.660
that follows that same template we just went over.

06:51.660 --> 06:52.770
All right, so it says, hi Sam.

06:52.770 --> 06:53.610
I read your article

06:53.610 --> 06:56.430
on volunteering your professional skills in Guatemala.

06:56.430 --> 06:57.570
It was really inspiring.

06:57.570 --> 06:59.850
I'm looking to travel more and you've got me thinking

06:59.850 --> 07:02.400
about incorporating volunteering when I do.

07:02.400 --> 07:04.200
So right there, that is two sentences.

07:04.200 --> 07:05.400
It's personal.

07:05.400 --> 07:10.200
It connects it to his article on volunteering in Guatemala.

07:10.200 --> 07:12.450
You showed that you read their article

07:12.450 --> 07:15.660
and you understood it and you were responding because of it.

07:15.660 --> 07:19.710
Now, the next sentence is what we call the value section.

07:19.710 --> 07:22.860
It's just divided for purposes of reading it.

07:22.860 --> 07:26.040
It looks like they still used two sentences

07:26.040 --> 07:27.810
but they said, I have a software company trying to

07:27.810 --> 07:30.780
improve remote medical record coding, and then it says

07:30.780 --> 07:31.890
I'm not looking to sell anything.

07:31.890 --> 07:34.770
But since you have so much expertise with remote coding

07:34.770 --> 07:36.300
I'd love to get your advice on our products

07:36.300 --> 07:38.036
so we don't build the wrong thing.

07:38.036 --> 07:41.700
This is a masterful set of sentences.

07:41.700 --> 07:44.310
Whenever someone sees, I have a software company

07:44.310 --> 07:46.260
dot, dot, dot, they usually cringe.

07:46.260 --> 07:47.100
But if it's followed

07:47.100 --> 07:50.880
by trying to improve remote record coding

07:50.880 --> 07:55.260
which is something you do and probably are frustrated with,

07:55.260 --> 07:57.150
it instantly sounds like

07:57.150 --> 08:00.030
this could be something that is game changing

08:00.030 --> 08:00.863
then they move on.

08:00.863 --> 08:03.870
And we use that technique where they said, I'm not in sales.

08:03.870 --> 08:06.120
I'm not trying to sell you something.

08:06.120 --> 08:07.020
And then they appeal

08:07.020 --> 08:10.927
to their pride by saying, you have expertise.

08:10.927 --> 08:13.680
So it's a value of responding to this email.

08:13.680 --> 08:17.580
Well, it is the fact that one, you can help them.

08:17.580 --> 08:21.300
Two, you have a specific expertise that would benefit them

08:21.300 --> 08:22.560
more than talking to someone else.

08:22.560 --> 08:24.930
And three, and this is the big one, they're building

08:24.930 --> 08:27.660
something that could actually solve your problems.

08:27.660 --> 08:29.970
And then the last one is, if you're available

08:29.970 --> 08:32.010
I'd love to chat for just 20 minutes Thursday

08:32.010 --> 08:33.556
or Friday morning.

08:33.556 --> 08:36.180
You know what I'm gonna complain about in that one?

08:36.180 --> 08:39.970
They didn't specifically say a time or a set of times

08:39.970 --> 08:42.330
but that's pretty good.

08:42.330 --> 08:45.780
You also noticed that this came in at six sentences

08:45.780 --> 08:49.873
so it's very well within our four to seven guideline.

08:49.873 --> 08:51.030
All right, guys

08:51.030 --> 08:55.560
go out there and shoot off some emails in into the wild.

08:55.560 --> 08:59.190
Just make sure that you follow this format

08:59.190 --> 09:01.950
or at least you stick to the core principles

09:01.950 --> 09:04.830
of being short, personal, and valuable.

09:04.830 --> 09:06.067
If you want to use this template

09:06.067 --> 09:09.570
start with an introduction, then add value,

09:09.570 --> 09:13.205
then schedule them, make it short, make it valuable.

09:13.205 --> 09:15.405
All right guys, see you in the next lecture.

response.title: How to Run a Customer Interview Correctly

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.000 --> 00:02.040
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:02.040 --> 00:04.710
So in this lecture, we're gonna talk about

00:04.710 --> 00:07.140
what are some of the best practices

00:07.140 --> 00:09.630
with regards to talking to your customers

00:09.630 --> 00:11.610
and running customer interviews.

00:11.610 --> 00:14.310
Now, you need to talk to your customers

00:14.310 --> 00:16.530
in order to make great products.

00:16.530 --> 00:18.600
That is unavoidable.

00:18.600 --> 00:21.270
But in order to collect the right information

00:21.270 --> 00:24.780
and get the right signals for your product,

00:24.780 --> 00:28.680
you need to know how to talk to your customers

00:28.680 --> 00:30.240
and what not to do.

00:30.240 --> 00:35.240
Now, customer interviews are not as intuitive as you think.

00:35.520 --> 00:38.430
You might think that the point of a customer interview

00:38.430 --> 00:41.970
is to just sit down, have a conversation,

00:41.970 --> 00:44.010
talk to them about your product,

00:44.010 --> 00:48.870
see how they react, pitch them on whatever new feature

00:48.870 --> 00:51.540
or idea you're rolling around in your head.

00:51.540 --> 00:54.150
Now, that's not even remotely true.

00:54.150 --> 00:55.830
The only thing that was true in that

00:55.830 --> 00:57.870
is that you are sitting down with the customer.

00:57.870 --> 01:00.480
I would hardly call it a conversation.

01:00.480 --> 01:02.100
It is an interview.

01:02.100 --> 01:03.630
Now, what's the actual purpose

01:03.630 --> 01:05.340
of running a customer interview?

01:05.340 --> 01:08.220
Well, it's to get the most real

01:08.220 --> 01:12.180
and accurate information as you can from your customer.

01:12.180 --> 01:16.200
If you just sit down and start rattling off pitches

01:16.200 --> 01:19.230
and if you domineer the conversation

01:19.230 --> 01:22.050
or act the same way as you would

01:22.050 --> 01:24.060
in any other normal conversation,

01:24.060 --> 01:27.870
you will not get accurate or real information.

01:27.870 --> 01:31.890
So I'm gonna go over four applicable best practices

01:31.890 --> 01:35.010
to help you understand how to run a customer interview

01:35.010 --> 01:36.870
and what you should do.

01:36.870 --> 01:38.910
Now, first of all, let's tackle the misconception

01:38.910 --> 01:41.130
that when you run a customer interview,

01:41.130 --> 01:43.650
your topic is going to be your product.

01:43.650 --> 01:45.420
It's actually not.

01:45.420 --> 01:46.770
When you talk to your customers,

01:46.770 --> 01:51.150
you wanna talk to them about their problems and their needs.

01:51.150 --> 01:55.800
You rarely, if ever, will talk about the solution.

01:55.800 --> 01:57.930
If you're talking about your solution directly

01:57.930 --> 02:00.090
with the customer, the information you're gonna get,

02:00.090 --> 02:01.650
it's going to be limited,

02:01.650 --> 02:05.160
and it's not going to help you understand the why,

02:05.160 --> 02:09.060
the deeper level of why they make certain decisions,

02:09.060 --> 02:11.340
why they think a certain way.

02:11.340 --> 02:13.590
There's a great quote about talking to your customers

02:13.590 --> 02:17.010
and that's, "Customers might not know what they want,

02:17.010 --> 02:19.320
but they can't hide what they need."

02:19.320 --> 02:20.700
So when you talk about the solution,

02:20.700 --> 02:23.160
you're talking about what they want,

02:23.160 --> 02:26.430
and they often don't know what exactly they want

02:26.430 --> 02:30.720
or how, in an accurate way, to process that information

02:30.720 --> 02:31.950
or describe it.

02:31.950 --> 02:35.550
But their needs are as simple as it gets.

02:35.550 --> 02:38.370
Anyone can talk about what problems they have

02:38.370 --> 02:42.000
or what they need, and we care because those conversations

02:42.000 --> 02:45.090
are much, much richer than a conversation

02:45.090 --> 02:48.180
about the surface of a solution

02:48.180 --> 02:50.520
and whether or not it looks nice.

02:50.520 --> 02:51.810
The second thing you need to understand

02:51.810 --> 02:54.540
is that you are here to ask questions

02:54.540 --> 02:58.890
and not really direct conversation

02:58.890 --> 03:02.700
or give your own opinions or your own thoughts.

03:02.700 --> 03:05.610
In general, the ratio between how much a customer

03:05.610 --> 03:07.230
is going to talk and how much you talk

03:07.230 --> 03:08.850
in one of these interviews,

03:08.850 --> 03:13.850
it should be 95% or 90% to 10% or 5%.

03:13.950 --> 03:16.410
It is very, very skewed.

03:16.410 --> 03:19.890
You wanna set this up so that you ask one question

03:19.890 --> 03:22.320
and that question leads to your customer

03:22.320 --> 03:26.640
talking about multiple things in detail.

03:26.640 --> 03:29.610
The point here is that you wanna get them to talk.

03:29.610 --> 03:32.130
One of my favorite rules about customer development

03:32.130 --> 03:36.810
or customer interviews is the point is to get off point.

03:36.810 --> 03:39.390
Basically, what that means is that don't worry

03:39.390 --> 03:41.610
about whether or not they're answering the question

03:41.610 --> 03:43.980
the exact way that you expected them to

03:43.980 --> 03:46.920
or going onto the topics that you wanted them to.

03:46.920 --> 03:49.530
You have to embrace the idea of tangents.

03:49.530 --> 03:51.720
Sometimes you'll ask a customer a question,

03:51.720 --> 03:53.760
they'll answer it or they might not,

03:53.760 --> 03:55.920
and then they'll go off on their own

03:55.920 --> 03:58.650
and start telling a story or an anecdote

03:58.650 --> 04:01.260
or something that's entirely not related.

04:01.260 --> 04:03.060
And you gotta understand that tangents are good.

04:03.060 --> 04:04.950
Sometimes they're even gold mines.

04:04.950 --> 04:08.490
Sometimes the best ideas come from your customers

04:08.490 --> 04:11.850
going off the cuff, off the record,

04:11.850 --> 04:15.780
saying things that you never would've thought to ask.

04:15.780 --> 04:18.180
The third thing is make sure that you create

04:18.180 --> 04:21.810
an environment where they feel comfortable talking.

04:21.810 --> 04:24.630
That means creating a rapport with them.

04:24.630 --> 04:27.300
Don't run a customer interview where you

04:27.300 --> 04:28.320
just sit down with them

04:28.320 --> 04:31.590
and immediately start asking them detailed questions.

04:31.590 --> 04:33.510
Make sure that they are comfortable with you.

04:33.510 --> 04:35.730
Make sure that you at least feign some effort

04:35.730 --> 04:40.530
that you are interested in them outside of this interview.

04:40.530 --> 04:42.870
Make sure that you're comfortable in conversation

04:42.870 --> 04:44.370
before you actually start.

04:44.370 --> 04:46.080
Along those lines, don't ask them questions

04:46.080 --> 04:48.660
that would be hard for them to answer in person

04:48.660 --> 04:53.220
or make them feel awkward or nervous to answer in person.

04:53.220 --> 04:55.080
Also, when they give you negative feedback,

04:55.080 --> 04:56.790
don't react negatively.

04:56.790 --> 04:59.940
Don't defend the product or the idea

04:59.940 --> 05:02.430
that you were projecting.

05:02.430 --> 05:05.370
When they give you negative feedback, they're testing you,

05:05.370 --> 05:07.140
and if you respond negatively,

05:07.140 --> 05:09.870
that has a chilling effect on the rest of the interview.

05:09.870 --> 05:13.650
They're much less likely to give you truthful,

05:13.650 --> 05:15.780
honest information in the future

05:15.780 --> 05:18.630
because they don't want to have the confrontation

05:18.630 --> 05:19.710
or the argument.

05:19.710 --> 05:23.670
When you respond in a neutral, nonjudgmental way,

05:23.670 --> 05:27.060
they'll then realize that it's okay to tell the truth.

05:27.060 --> 05:30.420
And the last thing is don't force these conversations,

05:30.420 --> 05:32.460
try to guide them.

05:32.460 --> 05:34.650
Depending on what type of interview you're doing,

05:34.650 --> 05:39.030
whether it's exploratory, validation, satisfaction,

05:39.030 --> 05:43.290
or efficiency, you probably have a focus in mind.

05:43.290 --> 05:45.540
You probably have some questions you want to answer

05:45.540 --> 05:47.850
and some topics that you wanna spend time on.

05:47.850 --> 05:52.320
But don't be forceful in forcing them onto that subject.

05:52.320 --> 05:55.200
If you allow them to graze

05:55.200 --> 05:57.600
in areas that they actually care about

05:57.600 --> 06:00.300
and then naturally guide them in the direction

06:00.300 --> 06:03.300
of what you wanna talk about, you'll end up in a situation

06:03.300 --> 06:05.010
where they give you a lot more information

06:05.010 --> 06:07.560
and they feel a lot more comfortable.

06:07.560 --> 06:09.990
You also don't want to risk the chance

06:09.990 --> 06:13.350
that in the process of getting to the topic

06:13.350 --> 06:14.940
that you wanted to talk about,

06:14.940 --> 06:18.720
they could have brought up many, many interesting things

06:18.720 --> 06:20.220
you never would've thought of.

06:20.220 --> 06:22.170
So if you keep those things in mind,

06:22.170 --> 06:24.270
you will have a much better chance

06:24.270 --> 06:26.940
of running a successful interview.

06:26.940 --> 06:28.290
And if you get to a point

06:28.290 --> 06:31.350
where you're having trouble talking to the customer,

06:31.350 --> 06:33.540
you don't know what to ask,

06:33.540 --> 06:35.850
you don't really know what's gonna get them

06:35.850 --> 06:40.350
to respond in a more open, fruitful way,

06:40.350 --> 06:42.690
use this one that I use all the time.

06:42.690 --> 06:44.940
My favorite sentence, anytime I'm stuck,

06:44.940 --> 06:46.140
I don't even know what to say,

06:46.140 --> 06:49.170
is "That's interesting, tell me more."

06:49.170 --> 06:52.500
And if you ever find yourself lost in this process,

06:52.500 --> 06:56.280
not sure what direction is up and what direction is down,

06:56.280 --> 06:59.790
just keep in mind the five W framework.

06:59.790 --> 07:00.810
I'm sure you've heard of it,

07:00.810 --> 07:02.430
but you probably haven't heard of it used

07:02.430 --> 07:04.740
in the context of product management,

07:04.740 --> 07:09.630
which is what, why, who, when, and where.

07:09.630 --> 07:12.720
That is, you are always, as a product manager,

07:12.720 --> 07:17.070
striving to answer the five questions

07:17.070 --> 07:20.850
and get a better, deeper understanding

07:20.850 --> 07:23.130
of what they mean for your product and your customer.

07:23.130 --> 07:25.410
And those questions are, who are your customers?

07:25.410 --> 07:27.840
What are their habits?

07:27.840 --> 07:30.630
When do they use your product?

07:30.630 --> 07:32.910
Where do they need your product?

07:32.910 --> 07:34.860
The last and most important one,

07:34.860 --> 07:37.890
why do they need your product?

07:37.890 --> 07:40.380
Customer development and customer interviews

07:40.380 --> 07:43.680
are really just a process of filling in the gaps

07:43.680 --> 07:46.560
of your knowledge of the five Ws.

07:46.560 --> 07:49.560
The more conversations you have with your customers,

07:49.560 --> 07:52.440
the more you'll be able to fill in the five Ws

07:52.440 --> 07:55.710
and have a better, more holistic understanding

07:55.710 --> 07:58.650
of what your product is, its impact,

07:58.650 --> 08:00.300
and why people are using it.

08:00.300 --> 08:01.800
All right, guys, now that you know

08:01.800 --> 08:03.180
some of the bigger picture

08:03.180 --> 08:06.300
of what makes these interviews successful,

08:06.300 --> 08:09.150
what are they like, what are you thinking about,

08:09.150 --> 08:11.760
and how to keep your eye on the prize,

08:11.760 --> 08:14.310
we're gonna talk a little bit more about the specifics,

08:14.310 --> 08:16.110
where we're gonna talk about good questions

08:16.110 --> 08:17.850
and bad questions.

08:17.850 --> 08:21.300
They make a huge difference in your interviews.

08:21.300 --> 08:22.450
All right, see ya then.

response.title: Putting the GO in Pokemon Go

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.248 --> 00:02.831
(upbeat music)

00:04.200 --> 00:05.940
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:05.940 --> 00:07.890
In this lecture, we're gonna talk about

00:07.890 --> 00:10.080
good questions and bad questions.

00:10.080 --> 00:12.060
Now, anytime you talk to a customer,

00:12.060 --> 00:16.350
you run the risk of accidentally biasing the information

00:16.350 --> 00:20.280
