they give you, causing them to lie to you,

00:20.280 --> 00:23.550
or the information they give you is hard to understand,

00:23.550 --> 00:25.440
or worse, misleading.

00:25.440 --> 00:29.820
A good question can bring out so much out of a customer,

00:29.820 --> 00:32.370
and it can fill in so much of your understanding

00:32.370 --> 00:34.740
of your product and how to improve it.

00:34.740 --> 00:38.880
Bad questions, on the other hand, are very pernicious.

00:38.880 --> 00:42.330
Sometimes you don't know if you're asking them,

00:42.330 --> 00:44.040
and worse, sometimes you don't know

00:44.040 --> 00:45.990
if you're getting bad answers

00:45.990 --> 00:49.080
and incorporating that into your product decisions.

00:49.080 --> 00:50.010
Let's simplify it.

00:50.010 --> 00:52.290
You ask good questions, you'll make good products.

00:52.290 --> 00:53.370
You ask bad questions,

00:53.370 --> 00:56.340
and you're gonna probably gonna make a bad product.

00:56.340 --> 00:59.760
So how should we ask questions to our customers?

00:59.760 --> 01:02.730
Well, there are some rules that are worth following

01:02.730 --> 01:05.190
to make sure that you're probably asking

01:05.190 --> 01:07.860
good questions and not the bad ones.

01:07.860 --> 01:12.090
The number one rule is always ask open-ended questions.

01:12.090 --> 01:13.830
An open-ended question is a question

01:13.830 --> 01:17.190
that cannot be answered with a simple yes or no

01:17.190 --> 01:20.760
or a specific piece of information.

01:20.760 --> 01:22.260
When you ask someone an open-ended question,

01:22.260 --> 01:26.400
it gives them the latitude and the room to answer it

01:26.400 --> 01:30.090
and to give the relevant information they see fit,

01:30.090 --> 01:32.220
which is exactly what we want from them.

01:32.220 --> 01:35.400
If I just ask someone, "Is it raining outside?"

01:35.400 --> 01:37.800
All I get is a yes or no answer.

01:37.800 --> 01:39.660
It's one tiny little data point.

01:39.660 --> 01:44.310
But if I ask someone, "What do you do on rainy days?"

01:44.310 --> 01:47.910
That could be answered in 1,000,001 different ways.

01:47.910 --> 01:48.743
Who knows?

01:48.743 --> 01:51.120
They could talk for an hour after that.

01:51.120 --> 01:52.380
And you remember from the last lecture,

01:52.380 --> 01:54.750
we talked about the fact that we want people

01:54.750 --> 01:58.350
to go on tangents, we want them to explain.

01:58.350 --> 02:00.450
That's where we get the good stuff.

02:00.450 --> 02:01.830
So that one's pretty simple.

02:01.830 --> 02:04.740
That's what you should do, ask open-ended questions.

02:04.740 --> 02:07.500
If you do that, you can't really fail.

02:07.500 --> 02:09.780
So rule number two is start with the things

02:09.780 --> 02:12.180
that you should not do.

02:12.180 --> 02:15.990
Rule number two, don't ask binary questions.

02:15.990 --> 02:18.240
What do I mean by binary questions?

02:18.240 --> 02:20.377
An example of a binary question might be,

02:20.377 --> 02:22.290
"Have you heard of Uber?"

02:22.290 --> 02:25.500
That question can only be answered with yes or no.

02:25.500 --> 02:27.960
Binary, there's only two options.

02:27.960 --> 02:31.890
You don't do that because one, it doesn't draw them out.

02:31.890 --> 02:35.040
It doesn't entice them to tell you more information

02:35.040 --> 02:37.380
or feel more comfortable with you.

02:37.380 --> 02:38.460
And the second reason is that

02:38.460 --> 02:40.590
you're just robbing yourself of information.

02:40.590 --> 02:44.130
They could have gone on and on and on about a subject

02:44.130 --> 02:45.810
adjacent to that question,

02:45.810 --> 02:47.760
but instead you ask them something

02:47.760 --> 02:50.040
that just ends with a hard landing.

02:50.040 --> 02:53.820
And rule number three is don't ask hypothetical questions.

02:53.820 --> 02:54.653
Now, I know a lot of people

02:54.653 --> 02:56.160
like to ask hypothetical questions.

02:56.160 --> 02:57.930
They get ridiculous and they're things you just

02:57.930 --> 03:00.240
should not do in a customer interview.

03:00.240 --> 03:01.837
An example of a hypothetical might be,

03:01.837 --> 03:03.487
"If you were a painter,

03:03.487 --> 03:07.117
"would you buy a subscription service for canvases

03:07.117 --> 03:09.727
"if it was reasonably priced

03:09.727 --> 03:11.760
"and the canvases were high quality?"

03:11.760 --> 03:14.400
That question is absolutely ridiculous.

03:14.400 --> 03:15.660
And I wouldn't be surprised

03:15.660 --> 03:19.200
if I saw someone ask this in a customer interview,

03:19.200 --> 03:21.180
and I would shake my head vigorously.

03:21.180 --> 03:23.610
Hypotheticals fail for a number of different reasons.

03:23.610 --> 03:25.950
But the biggest reason is that people operate

03:25.950 --> 03:30.950
and often are illogical in fictitious environments,

03:31.140 --> 03:32.970
hypothetical environments.

03:32.970 --> 03:36.120
And if you bring those questions down to earth,

03:36.120 --> 03:37.980
they will actually give you information

03:37.980 --> 03:40.890
that's easier to understand, easier for them to connect with

03:40.890 --> 03:42.210
and it's gonna be more accurate.

03:42.210 --> 03:45.750
Rule number four is don't ask leading questions.

03:45.750 --> 03:47.820
Anybody's watched "Law & Order",

03:47.820 --> 03:50.010
you know what a leading question is.

03:50.010 --> 03:52.230
A leading question is a question that biases

03:52.230 --> 03:56.010
or influences the person that's supposed to answer you.

03:56.010 --> 03:58.260
Leading questions are often questions

03:58.260 --> 04:01.770
that sound more like they're just telling you something.

04:01.770 --> 04:03.727
An example of a leading question might be,

04:03.727 --> 04:06.757
"If you could save 40% on your auto bill,

04:06.757 --> 04:08.310
"would you be interested?"

04:08.310 --> 04:09.540
Yes, I would, Geico.

04:09.540 --> 04:13.440
But let's be honest, who is gonna say no to that question?

04:13.440 --> 04:15.510
It's almost rhetorical.

04:15.510 --> 04:19.110
If I just ask you plainly, "Would you like to save money?"

04:19.110 --> 04:21.030
You'd probably say yes.

04:21.030 --> 04:24.390
So I know the answer to that before I asked it.

04:24.390 --> 04:26.310
So why am I asking the question?

04:26.310 --> 04:29.940
Leading questions get the answer that you want,

04:29.940 --> 04:33.810
but the answer you want might not be the truth.

04:33.810 --> 04:36.120
Rule number five, don't ask questions

04:36.120 --> 04:39.690
that might make the customer lie.

04:39.690 --> 04:42.000
And I mean, lie in the sense of questions

04:42.000 --> 04:44.550
that put them in an awkward position

04:44.550 --> 04:48.240
and will unduly influence them to give a certain answer.

04:48.240 --> 04:50.047
So for instance, if I ask them,

04:50.047 --> 04:52.260
"Have you ever cheated on a test?"

04:52.260 --> 04:54.810
That might be something that they might not wanna tell me,

04:54.810 --> 04:58.170
or questions, let's say about, of personal nature.

04:58.170 --> 05:00.397
Another example question would be,

05:00.397 --> 05:03.990
"Do you think I did a good job with this design?"

05:03.990 --> 05:06.180
It's very awkward sitting next to someone,

05:06.180 --> 05:07.590
especially when you don't know,

05:07.590 --> 05:10.860
to tell them what they made is terrible.

05:10.860 --> 05:13.020
It's the reason why you don't tell your kids

05:13.020 --> 05:16.200
that their kid art is really quite bad.

05:16.200 --> 05:18.840
If they lie, then the data's gonna be junk.

05:18.840 --> 05:22.440
But more importantly, if you put them in a scenario

05:22.440 --> 05:25.980
where they could lie, you'll never know if they did.

05:25.980 --> 05:27.330
And so, it doesn't even matter

05:27.330 --> 05:29.880
because that ambiguity makes your data

05:29.880 --> 05:33.090
and makes your information pretty much junk.

05:33.090 --> 05:33.923
So that's it.

05:33.923 --> 05:35.367
Focus on open-ended questions,

05:35.367 --> 05:38.010
and get the most information out as possible,

05:38.010 --> 05:41.670
and make your customers feel as comfortable as they can.

05:41.670 --> 05:43.170
Don't ask binary questions.

05:43.170 --> 05:44.460
Don't ask hypotheticals.

05:44.460 --> 05:47.460
Don't put them in awkward situations where they might lie.

05:47.460 --> 05:49.380
And don't ask leading questions.

05:49.380 --> 05:53.220
There's really no excuse for breaking some of these rules.

05:53.220 --> 05:56.220
Bad questions can almost always be transformed

05:56.220 --> 05:57.750
into good questions.

05:57.750 --> 05:59.130
Have you heard of Uber?

05:59.130 --> 06:00.667
You could easily just say,

06:00.667 --> 06:04.110
"If you need to get across town, how do you do it?"

06:04.110 --> 06:06.630
That'll give you way more information.

06:06.630 --> 06:08.340
And if they do use Uber

06:08.340 --> 06:10.500
or have heard of it, they will mention it.

06:10.500 --> 06:13.260
If you could save 40% on your auto bill,

06:13.260 --> 06:14.160
would you be interested?

06:14.160 --> 06:17.520
Should be changed to probably multiple questions.

06:17.520 --> 06:21.307
But you could ask a question that's better by saying,

06:21.307 --> 06:24.060
"If you could save 40% on your auto bill by,"

06:24.060 --> 06:26.700
and then explain the downside,

06:26.700 --> 06:29.730
then ask them if they would do it, that's a better question.

06:29.730 --> 06:31.140
Maybe you save 40%

06:31.140 --> 06:33.690
because you have to carpool with everyone every day.

06:33.690 --> 06:35.220
Or maybe it's 40%

06:35.220 --> 06:38.190
because you can't drive on Fridays or Saturdays.

06:38.190 --> 06:40.080
I don't know, give them the whole picture.

06:40.080 --> 06:41.520
The last thing I'm gonna leave you with

06:41.520 --> 06:45.930
is my favorite tip for asking good questions.

06:45.930 --> 06:48.480
Or at least if you ever get stuck in a situation

06:48.480 --> 06:50.640
where you don't know what to ask,

06:50.640 --> 06:53.100
always use this one or have it stored

06:53.100 --> 06:55.560
in the back of your head, ready to go.

06:55.560 --> 06:57.217
It's, "That's interesting.

06:57.217 --> 06:58.290
"Tell me more."

06:58.290 --> 06:59.430
I hate your product.

06:59.430 --> 07:00.330
That's interesting.

07:00.330 --> 07:01.350
Tell me more.

07:01.350 --> 07:03.150
I'm not sure if I would pay for this.

07:03.150 --> 07:04.200
That's interesting.

07:04.200 --> 07:05.340
Tell me more.

07:05.340 --> 07:07.410
Why are you blocking the exits?

07:07.410 --> 07:08.340
That's interesting.

07:08.340 --> 07:09.330
Tell me more.

07:09.330 --> 07:11.243
All right, guys, see you in the next lecture.

response.title: Building User Personas Off Your Interviews

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.300 --> 00:01.470
-: What's up everyone?

00:01.470 --> 00:04.470
This is all about building user personas.

00:04.470 --> 00:06.690
And you can build them from the interviews

00:06.690 --> 00:08.040
that Evan just talked about.

00:08.040 --> 00:10.860
First, I want to say, this is a bit of a buzzword alert.

00:10.860 --> 00:13.350
Because what I'm going to talk about in this lecture

00:13.350 --> 00:16.410
is of course user personas and how to make them.

00:16.410 --> 00:19.620
But I want you to know that if you go to many companies

00:19.620 --> 00:20.940
and become a product manager,

00:20.940 --> 00:25.200
they will likely already have a good idea of user personas.

00:25.200 --> 00:27.840
And you will likely not be the one making them.

00:27.840 --> 00:29.430
If you do have to make them,

00:29.430 --> 00:31.770
it's something you will do with the design team.

00:31.770 --> 00:34.920
In any case, understanding what a user persona is

00:34.920 --> 00:37.860
and what they're used for is really useful

00:37.860 --> 00:39.000
to being a product manager

00:39.000 --> 00:40.350
and it's something you need to know.

00:40.350 --> 00:44.190
So have you ever heard of the term user persona before?

00:44.190 --> 00:46.740
If not, you will when you become a product manager

00:46.740 --> 00:48.990
because designers are talking about them all the time.

00:48.990 --> 00:52.890
User personas are just aggregates of observed user behavior.

00:52.890 --> 00:55.350
So let's say that I'm a product manager at eBay.

00:55.350 --> 00:56.850
For those of you that don't know,

00:56.850 --> 00:58.860
eBay is an online auction website

00:58.860 --> 01:00.930
where you can buy and sell products

01:00.930 --> 01:02.460
that people put up for sale.

01:02.460 --> 01:04.260
As a product manager at EBay,

01:04.260 --> 01:06.960
if we talked or interviewed a whole bunch of users,

01:06.960 --> 01:09.360
we would likely find out that there are groups

01:09.360 --> 01:12.690
of certain users that behave in similar ways.

01:12.690 --> 01:14.790
For instance, there are people who shop

01:14.790 --> 01:16.560
once every few months on eBay

01:16.560 --> 01:18.720
for a certain type of collectible item.

01:18.720 --> 01:20.580
There are also people that come to eBay

01:20.580 --> 01:23.340
to look at reviews and prices on certain items,

01:23.340 --> 01:25.380
but very rarely purchase anything.

01:25.380 --> 01:27.240
And then finally, there are probably

01:27.240 --> 01:30.152
some heavily engaged users that log in everyday

01:30.152 --> 01:33.480
to look for a specific item to come available

01:33.480 --> 01:36.270
and they snatch it up really quick and try to resell it.

01:36.270 --> 01:39.240
Then on the other side, we have the seller side.

01:39.240 --> 01:42.330
So these types of users are sellers on eBay

01:42.330 --> 01:44.280
and there are groups of them as well.

01:44.280 --> 01:47.790
So people will sell something once every few months

01:47.790 --> 01:49.200
when they need extra cash.

01:49.200 --> 01:51.660
And then there's another group of people probably

01:51.660 --> 01:53.790
who use eBay all day every day.

01:53.790 --> 01:56.580
And make a actual living off of selling items

01:56.580 --> 01:59.610
over the internet that they purchased in person.

01:59.610 --> 02:02.280
You could imagine that if you were building a product

02:02.280 --> 02:06.000
or feature that is targeted at a specific user behavior,

02:06.000 --> 02:08.880
it would be really convenient to have a user

02:08.880 --> 02:10.980
to refer to as an example.

02:10.980 --> 02:13.230
Rather than this, say, I don't know.

02:13.230 --> 02:15.480
This feature is targeted to all people

02:15.480 --> 02:18.810
between ages of 25 and 40 who sell

02:18.810 --> 02:20.940
a lot of stuff on eBay everyday

02:20.940 --> 02:23.580
and make a living off of our site, right?

02:23.580 --> 02:25.770
This is where user personas come in.

02:25.770 --> 02:28.290
To make a user persona, we interview or observe

02:28.290 --> 02:30.240
a large number of users.

02:30.240 --> 02:33.630
Then you find a user behavior that is observed frequently

02:33.630 --> 02:37.050
and pretend that it is one fictional user doing that thing.

02:37.050 --> 02:40.770
Then you name that fictional user with a real name.

02:40.770 --> 02:42.690
You give them a description like

02:42.690 --> 02:45.480
how they like to use your product and what matters to them.

02:45.480 --> 02:48.990
And then you even give them a bit of background information.

02:48.990 --> 02:51.990
You're actually kind of creating a fake person here.

02:51.990 --> 02:54.930
The purpose of user personas is to make it more convenient

02:54.930 --> 02:57.150
to talk about certain user behaviors

02:57.150 --> 02:58.710
that you're building for,

02:58.710 --> 03:01.950
but also to increase empathy towards your users.

03:01.950 --> 03:03.630
If you're looking at a bunch of metrics

03:03.630 --> 03:06.120
on a screen and see a whole bunch of numbers

03:06.120 --> 03:07.830
that are describing a certain way

03:07.830 --> 03:09.870
that a large user base behaves,

03:09.870 --> 03:12.840
it's sometimes hard to keep in mind

03:12.840 --> 03:14.700
that these are real people out there

03:14.700 --> 03:16.920
in real life doing these things.

03:16.920 --> 03:19.890
User personas make behavior in metrics

03:19.890 --> 03:21.630
more relatable as a human.

03:21.630 --> 03:23.790
All right, so head on over to the next lecture

03:23.790 --> 03:24.840
and we're going to go over

03:24.840 --> 03:28.713
what an actual real life user persona might look like.

response.title: Real World Example of a User Persona

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.000 --> 00:01.080
-: Hey everyone.

00:01.080 --> 00:04.320
So just so you can see a true real life example,

00:04.320 --> 00:08.760
here is an actual user persona that we use at SoundCloud.

00:08.760 --> 00:12.510
Her name is Becky and she's a mainstream music listener.

00:12.510 --> 00:14.370
So you can see that we have her name.

00:14.370 --> 00:15.570
We have a little bit about her,

00:15.570 --> 00:17.040
like what type of phone she uses

00:17.040 --> 00:19.800
and what type of music listening habits she has.

00:19.800 --> 00:21.870
And then we have some preferences

00:21.870 --> 00:24.090
on how much she likes technology

00:24.090 --> 00:27.270
or what type of listening she really likes to do.

00:27.270 --> 00:30.270
So if we were doing this with our eBay example

00:30.270 --> 00:32.910
we would do something like this, but we would focus it

00:32.910 --> 00:36.330
on selling things on eBay rather than listening to music.

00:36.330 --> 00:39.300
For instance, we may want to make a persona for users

00:39.300 --> 00:42.360
that find cool stuff at local garage sales

00:42.360 --> 00:45.120
and sell those items on eBay for a living.

00:45.120 --> 00:47.910
We could call this user persona, Jessica maybe,

00:47.910 --> 00:50.370
and say that Jessica is typically between

00:50.370 --> 00:52.620
the ages of 20 and 50,

00:52.620 --> 00:55.350
loves antique shopping and garage sales,

00:55.350 --> 00:57.870
and relies on eBay to make a living.

00:57.870 --> 01:00.150
The most important thing to her could be

01:00.150 --> 01:03.712
that she is able to quickly list multiple items

01:03.712 --> 01:07.470
that she found at antique shows on eBay,

01:07.470 --> 01:10.560
and then manage all of those multiple listings at once

01:10.560 --> 01:11.790
easily from her computer.

01:11.790 --> 01:15.272
So that's an overview of user personas and like I mentioned,

01:15.272 --> 01:18.390
UX designers are going to be the people that are

01:18.390 --> 01:20.460
making these most of the time in your company.

01:20.460 --> 01:22.470
But you need to be very aware of these people

01:22.470 --> 01:23.730
as a product manager.

01:23.730 --> 01:25.350
When you make your user personas,

01:25.350 --> 01:28.650
make sure they have a name, a sketch or a photo,

01:28.650 --> 01:32.070
and a description about what is most important to them

01:32.070 --> 01:35.820
about your product, what they want to be able to do easily,

01:35.820 --> 01:37.620
and anything else you think is useful.

01:37.620 --> 01:39.090
So if you want take a moment

01:39.090 --> 01:41.520
and make a few simple user personas yourself

01:41.520 --> 01:44.100
and share the personas with the rest of the students

01:44.100 --> 01:45.960
in the course forum below.

01:45.960 --> 01:47.070
All right, that's it for this one.

01:47.070 --> 01:48.920
I'll see you all in the next lecture.

response.title: The Product Manager & The Data Diet

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.083 --> 00:02.666
(upbeat music)

00:04.530 --> 00:05.363
-: Hey guys.

00:05.363 --> 00:06.450
Welcome back to the course.

00:06.450 --> 00:07.500
This lecture, we're gonna talk

00:07.500 --> 00:11.772
about a concept in product management called The Data Diet.

00:11.772 --> 00:15.060
Now, what do I mean by data diet?

00:15.060 --> 00:17.790
Well, what I mean and what I'm getting at

00:17.790 --> 00:21.840
is that when you are a product manager or an entrepreneur

00:21.840 --> 00:24.270
you do not make decisions in a vacuum.

00:24.270 --> 00:27.660
You also are not going to make product decisions

00:27.660 --> 00:30.540
with only one single source of information.

00:30.540 --> 00:33.510
Let's say that you wanted to go see a movie.

00:33.510 --> 00:38.510
Would you be satisfied in just pulling people over randomly

00:38.610 --> 00:41.100
in the street, asking them what they thought of the movie,

00:41.100 --> 00:43.110
and then basing your decision about whether

00:43.110 --> 00:45.120
or not to see it off what they said?

00:45.120 --> 00:47.820
Well, no, and if you said yes

00:47.820 --> 00:50.850
that's because it's a low risk maneuver

00:50.850 --> 00:54.060
or you have really low standards, shame on you.

00:54.060 --> 00:56.100
Building products compared to going

00:56.100 --> 00:57.870
to movies are a little bit different.

00:57.870 --> 01:01.320
Building products are a much bigger risk.

01:01.320 --> 01:04.080
When you're building a product, there are no do-overs,

01:04.080 --> 01:07.740
and if you make a mistake in building your product,

01:07.740 --> 01:11.820
that can haunt your company's legacy for years.

01:11.820 --> 01:15.660
That movie, in this terrible metaphor, would instead last,

01:15.660 --> 01:18.150
let's say a year or at least one

01:18.150 --> 01:20.460
of those ridiculously long four hour movies

01:20.460 --> 01:22.140
and you can't leave to go pee.

01:22.140 --> 01:24.780
So you better figure out for sure

01:24.780 --> 01:27.540
whether or not you're watching the Shawshank Redemption

01:27.540 --> 01:31.650
or Eternal Sunshine for the Spotless Mind.

01:31.650 --> 01:32.670
God, that was awful.

01:32.670 --> 01:33.930
When the stakes are that high

01:33.930 --> 01:37.320
you probably will look for other sources of information.

01:37.320 --> 01:41.490
You might ask your friends or your family, people you trust.

01:41.490 --> 01:43.290
You might read reviews online.

01:43.290 --> 01:46.050
You might even preview it by watching the trailer.

01:46.050 --> 01:47.370
You get the point.

01:47.370 --> 01:50.430
And the same goes for product decisions.

01:50.430 --> 01:53.250
When you make a product decision, you're gonna

01:53.250 --> 01:57.570
be using a mix of different information sources.

01:57.570 --> 02:01.170
You might use internal feedback from your company.

02:01.170 --> 02:04.170
You might use data you got from user testing.

02:04.170 --> 02:07.440
You might use feedback that was submitted online.

02:07.440 --> 02:09.300
You might use the analytics

02:09.300 --> 02:13.320
and the engagement stats from visitors on your page.

02:13.320 --> 02:15.690
You'll even base your decisions off the news

02:15.690 --> 02:19.350
and market trends and watching what your competitors do.

02:19.350 --> 02:21.360
There are a lot of different inputs.

02:21.360 --> 02:23.280
Customer interviews, and everything we just learned

02:23.280 --> 02:25.950
in this section are a crucial part

02:25.950 --> 02:28.590
of making decisions as a product manager,

02:28.590 --> 02:31.860
but they are only one dish in your data diet.

02:31.860 --> 02:35.580
Customer interviews are great, but they do have a pair

02:35.580 --> 02:38.190
of vulnerabilities, and that's why it is crucial

02:38.190 --> 02:41.940
that you supplement this information with other sources.

02:41.940 --> 02:45.390
The first vulnerability is that customer interviews,

02:45.390 --> 02:46.795
they don't really scale.

02:46.795 --> 02:50.280
It's only you talking to a customer.

02:50.280 --> 02:52.410
You can have one, maybe you can have 10,

02:52.410 --> 02:55.140
maybe you're crazy, you have 50 interviews.

02:55.140 --> 02:57.030
That's a lot, but you could be working

02:57.030 --> 02:59.190
with an application that has thousands of users,

02:59.190 --> 03:02.019
so it's actually quite risky to take

03:02.019 --> 03:03.840
three or four interviews

03:03.840 --> 03:06.726
and extrapolate that for the entire group.

03:06.726 --> 03:09.960
Who knows if those three people you talk to

03:09.960 --> 03:11.190
were the crazy ones?

03:11.190 --> 03:14.160
The second vulnerability is that this is qualitative data

03:14.160 --> 03:16.650
and qualitative data is very important

03:16.650 --> 03:19.470
but you also need quantitative data.

03:19.470 --> 03:22.140
Qualitative is the fluffy stuff.

03:22.140 --> 03:23.850
The why, the what.

03:23.850 --> 03:24.870
I like this.

03:24.870 --> 03:26.070
I like that.

03:26.070 --> 03:30.540
Quantitative is the data that are, it's hard numbers.

03:30.540 --> 03:34.140
0.25% of people clicked on this button.

03:34.140 --> 03:35.610
People stayed on your page

03:35.610 --> 03:39.750
for four minutes and 25 seconds, et cetera, et cetera.

03:39.750 --> 03:43.290
Qualitative data is nice and it helps you understand,

03:43.290 --> 03:46.110
but it's kind of left out to dry because

03:46.110 --> 03:48.480
it's not precise enough,

03:48.480 --> 03:51.090
and that's why you need quantitative as well.

03:51.090 --> 03:53.220
Think of qualitative information more like

03:53.220 --> 03:56.328
a barometer of temperature and quantitative data

03:56.328 --> 03:59.610
like an actual thermostat, so keep that in mind.

03:59.610 --> 04:01.560
You have a data diet.

04:01.560 --> 04:06.270
Custom interviews are just one piece of your dinner meal.

04:06.270 --> 04:09.600
You as a product manager will have to make hard decisions

04:09.600 --> 04:12.540
about what inputs to use in your decisions,

04:12.540 --> 04:17.100
which information sources are relevant and which are junk.

04:17.100 --> 04:18.630
In the rest of the course we cover a lot

04:18.630 --> 04:21.660
of these different input signals and we also cover

04:21.660 --> 04:25.320
in more depth how you can synthesize this information

04:25.320 --> 04:27.990
and make good judgements for your product.

04:27.990 --> 04:29.963
All right guys, see you in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.210 --> 00:01.620
Instructor: Hey guys, welcome back to the course

00:01.620 --> 00:03.690
and welcome to the new section.

00:03.690 --> 00:06.660
We're gonna talk in this section about MVPs.

00:06.660 --> 00:08.880
First off, we're gonna talk about MVPs,

00:08.880 --> 00:11.040
what they mean in definition,

00:11.040 --> 00:15.810
and how the Lean Startup framework wants us to think

00:15.810 --> 00:16.920
about them.

00:16.920 --> 00:19.110
Then after we're done talking about the theory, we'll talk

00:19.110 --> 00:23.820
about how do MVPs get used in actuality by product managers.

00:23.820 --> 00:25.560
Now, MVP, if you don't know

00:25.560 --> 00:28.410
this stands for Minimum Viable Product.

00:28.410 --> 00:29.670
The term is originally coined

00:29.670 --> 00:32.670
in a book called the Lean Startup Framework written

00:32.670 --> 00:33.503
by

00:33.503 --> 00:34.336
Eric

00:34.336 --> 00:35.169
Ries

00:35.169 --> 00:38.550
and for the most part, it's extremely popular.

00:38.550 --> 00:42.780
Startups nowadays often follow this religiously

00:42.780 --> 00:44.940
and even big companies have taken a lot

00:44.940 --> 00:48.750
of the core concepts and integrated it into their workflow.

00:48.750 --> 00:50.880
All right, let's look at the actual definition of MVP,

00:50.880 --> 00:53.220
as the book says it. Pull it up on the screen.

00:53.220 --> 00:54.510
You guys can read it.

00:54.510 --> 00:57.093
(gentle music)

01:04.140 --> 01:05.283
All right, when we're back. (gabbles)

01:05.283 --> 01:06.540
That was ridiculous

01:06.540 --> 01:11.220
and I'm sorry for how long that definition is.

01:11.220 --> 01:13.170
I don't know why it's so bloated.

01:13.170 --> 01:16.380
MVP just means the smallest amount

01:16.380 --> 01:20.610
of a proposed product that you can build, push out there

01:20.610 --> 01:23.070
and then get real feedback from users

01:23.070 --> 01:26.220
about whether or not they are actually interested in it.

01:26.220 --> 01:30.120
MVPs are primarily designed to test hypotheses

01:30.120 --> 01:32.970
and test assumptions that you have.

01:32.970 --> 01:34.650
Now, if you wanna be more accurate, you would call an MVP

01:34.650 --> 01:39.270
an MVP experiment, because actually an MVP is a

01:39.270 --> 01:40.290
process,

01:40.290 --> 01:42.510
not necessarily a thing

01:42.510 --> 01:45.270
and that process is very much like a science experiment.

01:45.270 --> 01:49.230
You'll start with things like assumptions, a hypothesis,

01:49.230 --> 01:51.510
a minimum criteria for success

01:51.510 --> 01:54.510
and then you'll go about testing your hypothesis

01:54.510 --> 01:56.970
in a very scientific measured way.

01:56.970 --> 01:58.500
MVP experiments rely heavily

01:58.500 --> 02:01.200
on a concept called validated learning

02:01.200 --> 02:02.520
and they draw the distinction

02:02.520 --> 02:05.130
between learning and validated learning.

02:05.130 --> 02:06.900
Validated learning is anything you learn

02:06.900 --> 02:10.110
from a customer that's done in a test environment

02:10.110 --> 02:11.400
in a way that you know

02:11.400 --> 02:13.890
for a fact you're not biasing your customer

02:13.890 --> 02:16.830
and you're studying them in an environment where

02:16.830 --> 02:20.040
they have no undue pressure and they act naturally.

02:20.040 --> 02:21.000
Learning, on the other hand

02:21.000 --> 02:24.780
is just pretty much anything else you do that's not designed

02:24.780 --> 02:26.760
like an experiment, not done well

02:26.760 --> 02:29.310
and you can't trust the results you get from it.

02:29.310 --> 02:34.230
Think of MVPs in the metaphor of walking in a park

02:34.230 --> 02:37.320
and you're on a path and you reach a fork in the road,

02:37.320 --> 02:40.410
except fork is three different ways you can go

02:40.410 --> 02:41.790
and you don't know where to go.

02:41.790 --> 02:42.750
Why are you doing this?

02:42.750 --> 02:44.520
I-- why do people hike in general?

02:44.520 --> 02:45.630
Probably cause they love trees.

02:45.630 --> 02:46.770
Now, in this metaphor,

02:46.770 --> 02:50.190
you have to pick which path to go down

02:50.190 --> 02:51.690
and I'll give you a little bit more information.

02:51.690 --> 02:53.700
Right at that intersection, there's a town.

02:53.700 --> 02:54.780
And this town has people

02:54.780 --> 02:57.360
and these people presumably know which path is

02:57.360 --> 02:58.740
the right one to take.

02:58.740 --> 03:00.150
So how do you decide?

03:00.150 --> 03:03.330
Well, most people would say, ask someone in the town

03:03.330 --> 03:06.480
but actually with an MVP experiment,

03:06.480 --> 03:08.280
you don't wanna ask the town's people.

03:08.280 --> 03:09.113
Why?

03:09.113 --> 03:11.280
Because even if they swear

03:11.280 --> 03:13.020
that they know which path it is,

03:13.020 --> 03:16.590
we don't know for a hundred percent certainty

03:16.590 --> 03:18.570
that they've any idea what they're talking about.

03:18.570 --> 03:20.610
Can you really trust those townspeople

03:20.610 --> 03:22.560
with their quaint way of life?

03:22.560 --> 03:23.550
No, you can't

03:23.550 --> 03:27.180
because it's entirely subjective and qualitative.

03:27.180 --> 03:29.430
So the only way to actually tell for sure

03:29.430 --> 03:32.130
is to walk down each individual path

03:32.130 --> 03:34.110
until you can see enough

03:34.110 --> 03:37.320
of the path to know if it's the right direction.

03:37.320 --> 03:38.610
With an MVP experiment,

03:38.610 --> 03:41.100
it is the art of walking down those paths,

03:41.100 --> 03:43.140
putting in the minimum amount of effort

03:43.140 --> 03:45.780
to get to the point where you can say, "Ah, wrong path."

03:45.780 --> 03:47.100
If you're gonna build a website

03:47.100 --> 03:49.080
that sold shoes online and you weren't sure

03:49.080 --> 03:52.170
if people wanted to buy your shoes online

03:52.170 --> 03:53.010
what would you do?

03:53.010 --> 03:54.130
Would you

03:55.020 --> 03:58.320
go all in, build out a fancy website,

03:58.320 --> 04:00.000
hire some people to program it,

04:00.000 --> 04:02.130
buy some inventory, which is,

04:02.130 --> 04:06.122
this is the equivalent of just sprinting down one path

04:06.122 --> 04:09.480
or would you try to do the absolute minimum

04:09.480 --> 04:10.860
just to figure out first

04:10.860 --> 04:12.240
whether or not this is the right path

04:12.240 --> 04:14.970
and whether or not people wanna buy your shoes online?

04:14.970 --> 04:17.280
That's actually a classic example of the MVP.

04:17.280 --> 04:18.990
If you guys are familiar with Zappos, the way

04:18.990 --> 04:21.780
that they started was exactly what I'm talking about.

04:21.780 --> 04:23.700
They wanted to know if people would buy shoes online

04:23.700 --> 04:25.740
and this is back in the day when buying shoes online

04:25.740 --> 04:27.270
was kind of crazy.

04:27.270 --> 04:30.420
So what they did is they made a very basic website.

04:30.420 --> 04:32.820
They didn't buy any inventory whatsoever.

04:32.820 --> 04:34.680
They walked across the street to a shoe store,

04:34.680 --> 04:37.560
took photos of the shoes and then when people bought them,

04:37.560 --> 04:39.360
they physically walked over, bought the shoes

04:39.360 --> 04:40.200
and then shipped them.

04:40.200 --> 04:41.760
Once that had happened enough

04:41.760 --> 04:43.170
they knew this is the right path.

04:43.170 --> 04:44.580
They knew that this was validated.

04:44.580 --> 04:46.830
Now, a common misconception with MVPs is

04:46.830 --> 04:49.530
that an MVP is some sort of prototype

04:49.530 --> 04:51.690
and when I say MVP, it means, you know

04:51.690 --> 04:53.640
a really basic version of what you're trying to build

04:53.640 --> 04:55.200
and that's just not the case.

04:55.200 --> 04:58.110
MVPs are about idea validation at the end

04:58.110 --> 05:01.920
of the day and what it takes to get to that point.

05:01.920 --> 05:03.540
Well, that's your MVP.

05:03.540 --> 05:04.410
In most situations,

05:04.410 --> 05:05.490
you can actually get away

05:05.490 --> 05:08.100
with not building much of anything,

05:08.100 --> 05:10.500
just anything you can use to figure out whether

05:10.500 --> 05:13.440
or not people are interested in what you are about to build.

05:13.440 --> 05:16.260
The other core reason we make MVPs is

05:16.260 --> 05:20.220
that we use them as a way to mitigate risk.

05:20.220 --> 05:23.340
If you're on the option of building a big fancy platform

05:23.340 --> 05:27.360
that created forms or just using a Google form,

05:27.360 --> 05:30.900
or a basic platform to validate your idea beforehand,

05:30.900 --> 05:34.980
why wouldn't you go with the faster, cheaper, easier option?

05:34.980 --> 05:37.020
By picking that simpler version,

05:37.020 --> 05:40.140
by walking only part way down the path,

05:40.140 --> 05:42.540
we save an enormous amount of resources.

05:42.540 --> 05:44.940
Time resources, money resources

05:44.940 --> 05:46.860
and things like opportunity cost.

05:46.860 --> 05:48.300
The last thing I'll say about MVPs

05:48.300 --> 05:50.190
in the actual lean framer, 'cause that are,

05:50.190 --> 05:53.040
there's a very strong focus on speed

05:53.040 --> 05:56.310
and that's where the mantra of fail fast comes from.

05:56.310 --> 05:58.050
If you can can fail fast

05:58.050 --> 06:01.470
and build your MVP experiments, run them quickly,

06:01.470 --> 06:05.280
then you can run more MVP experiments in the same amount

06:05.280 --> 06:09.210
of time and collect exponentially more data, which means

06:09.210 --> 06:11.640
that you're more likely to find a product

06:11.640 --> 06:15.600
or find a feature that fits, works and is successful.

06:15.600 --> 06:17.940
All right guys, so that's maybe that sounds familiar to you,

06:17.940 --> 06:19.110
maybe it doesn't.

06:19.110 --> 06:22.800
That is the academic way of explaining MVPs.

06:22.800 --> 06:24.840
And then next lecture we're gonna talk more

06:24.840 --> 06:29.026
about the relationship between product managers and MVPs.

06:29.026 --> 06:31.410
MVPs are different depending

06:31.410 --> 06:33.600
on who you are as a product manager

06:33.600 --> 06:36.390
and what company you are working for, right?

06:36.390 --> 06:38.040
See you guys in the next lecture.

response.title: How do product managers think about MVPs?

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.300 --> 00:01.133
-: Hey guys.

00:01.133 --> 00:01.966
Welcome back to the course.

00:01.966 --> 00:04.020
So this lecture, we're gonna get even deeper

00:04.020 --> 00:06.690
into the concept of an MVP.

00:06.690 --> 00:10.020
We now know the academic sense of what an MVP is.

00:10.020 --> 00:11.190
Now we're gonna ask the question,

00:11.190 --> 00:13.890
what is an MVP to a product manager?

00:13.890 --> 00:14.723
Well, let's first start

00:14.723 --> 00:16.890
with what we established in the previous lecture.

00:16.890 --> 00:20.370
MVPs are minimum viable products, which are

00:20.370 --> 00:24.120
basically experiments that we're gonna run to simulate

00:24.120 --> 00:26.340
whether or not people are interested

00:26.340 --> 00:30.810
in a new product, or feature or proposing to build.

00:30.810 --> 00:32.760
This primarily is used

00:32.760 --> 00:36.450
as a way to mitigate risk and to save us

00:36.450 --> 00:38.490
from wasting tons of resources

00:38.490 --> 00:40.470
building out the full thing.

00:40.470 --> 00:43.530
Risk is defined here on a number of different dimensions,

00:43.530 --> 00:47.490
but the main ones are risk of losing resources.

00:47.490 --> 00:50.670
Resources like time, resources like money.

00:50.670 --> 00:53.250
Also things like opportunity cost.

00:53.250 --> 00:56.460
So time, money, and opportunity cost.

00:56.460 --> 00:58.290
As a product manager,

00:58.290 --> 01:02.370
are those three things going to be your top priority?

01:02.370 --> 01:04.440
Now, this is where MVPs don't really match

01:04.440 --> 01:08.940
up perfectly with the role of a product manager.

01:08.940 --> 01:11.400
Now, it's easy to understand why lean

01:11.400 --> 01:14.250
and MVPs are so important to startups.

01:14.250 --> 01:16.420
Startups in general don't have a lot

01:17.415 --> 01:20.670
of resources and they don't have a huge tolerance for risk.

01:20.670 --> 01:21.503
Now, you might say to yourself,

01:21.503 --> 01:24.390
"Whoa, I thought startups were super risky."

01:24.390 --> 01:26.820
Well yeah, startups do take risks

01:26.820 --> 01:28.170
on building their products,

01:28.170 --> 01:31.770
but what happens if that product they build

01:31.770 --> 01:33.030
is adopted by no one?

01:33.030 --> 01:33.990
No one shows up.

01:33.990 --> 01:35.790
No one uses it, no one buys it.

01:35.790 --> 01:40.590
Well, the vast majority of cases, the startup just dies.

01:40.590 --> 01:42.180
So as far as tolerance goes,

01:42.180 --> 01:44.550
if you've launched the wrong product

01:44.550 --> 01:47.850
and that means that you die, I would say

01:47.850 --> 01:50.850
that you have a pretty low tolerance for risk.

01:50.850 --> 01:52.710
And so that's why startups hug

01:52.710 --> 01:55.560
the leanness of the MVP pillow.

01:55.560 --> 01:57.870
It's what protects them from the monsters

01:57.870 --> 01:59.400
at night that wanna throw them

01:59.400 --> 02:02.100
into the dead pool of other failed startups.

02:02.100 --> 02:03.660
Now remember what we said when we talked

02:03.660 --> 02:05.730
about what a product manager is.

02:05.730 --> 02:10.020
Well, product managers primarily exist to manage products

02:10.020 --> 02:12.870
and that inherently suggests that they're part

02:12.870 --> 02:16.440
of something that is a little bit more established.

02:16.440 --> 02:19.860
In general, if you're a product manager at a startup,

02:19.860 --> 02:21.480
you might have all the roles

02:21.480 --> 02:23.640
and responsibilities of a product manager,

02:23.640 --> 02:25.800
but your title is gonna be a little bit different.

02:25.800 --> 02:28.140
It probably needs something like co-founder.

02:28.140 --> 02:30.300
Now, the traditional product managers exist

02:30.300 --> 02:33.210
in organizations that are a little bit larger,

02:33.210 --> 02:34.560
and a little bit more established,

02:34.560 --> 02:37.950
and therefore have a higher tolerance for risk.

02:37.950 --> 02:40.230
Assuming that you already have a product,

02:40.230 --> 02:44.010
and it's validated, and it's has product market fit,

02:44.010 --> 02:46.348
it's potentially making money.

02:46.348 --> 02:49.920
Any experiment or MVP you run

02:49.920 --> 02:53.490
that's tangential to that, if it fails

02:53.490 --> 02:57.630
it often doesn't endanger the main product line.

02:57.630 --> 03:00.270
The main source of life blood.

03:00.270 --> 03:01.103
It's not to say that

03:01.103 --> 03:04.080
a failed MVP experiment might not critically

03:04.080 --> 03:08.430
endanger the company, but when you're a product manager

03:08.430 --> 03:10.890
you're probably in an organization that has

03:10.890 --> 03:12.930
at least a little bit of cushion.

03:12.930 --> 03:15.780
The larger the organization, the higher the cushion,

03:15.780 --> 03:18.750
the higher the tolerance for risk, and the more

03:18.750 --> 03:22.710
likely you are to be working on a product that's farther

03:22.710 --> 03:25.380
and farther away from their core product.

03:25.380 --> 03:27.360
Now, imagine you're at Google and you're a product manager.

03:27.360 --> 03:30.300
Congratulations. They get paid a ton of money.

03:30.300 --> 03:33.660
Now imagine you run an experiment on a product,

03:33.660 --> 03:35.520
you launch it and it fails miserably

03:35.520 --> 03:37.230
and you blow a bunch of money.

03:37.230 --> 03:39.690
Do you think that's going to endanger Google?

03:39.690 --> 03:42.270
In very few conceivable scenarios

03:42.270 --> 03:44.310
could it ever endanger that company.

03:44.310 --> 03:45.720
As long as it doesn't have to do

03:45.720 --> 03:48.510
with that oil derrick of money coming

03:48.510 --> 03:51.990
from their search ads, they'll probably be fine.

03:51.990 --> 03:54.540
So as a PM, when you run an MVP experiment

03:54.540 --> 03:56.970
you're gonna try to do it quickly

03:56.970 --> 03:59.010
and you're gonna try to not be wasteful,

03:59.010 --> 04:02.460
but you are probably going to de-emphasize

04:02.460 --> 04:05.070
resources as a hard constraint.

04:05.070 --> 04:09.030
If I were to draw this as a graph, on the left hand side

04:09.030 --> 04:11.280
the Y axis you would have risk tolerance.

04:11.280 --> 04:14.040
On the bottom you would have the size of the organization.

04:14.040 --> 04:17.700
As it gets larger, they have higher risk tolerance

04:17.700 --> 04:20.340
and as their risk tolerance gets higher and higher,

04:20.340 --> 04:22.740
they start replacing the things that you're

04:22.740 --> 04:25.110
traditionally concerned with in Lean and

04:25.110 --> 04:29.280
in MVP with other metrics and aspects.

04:29.280 --> 04:31.260
They start caring about other things

04:31.260 --> 04:34.440
such as, their brand or opportunity cost.

04:34.440 --> 04:36.060
Bigger organizations care about things

04:36.060 --> 04:37.770
like brand and opportunity cost,

04:37.770 --> 04:40.590
because they're a bigger deal at a big organization.

04:40.590 --> 04:43.500
Brands for big organizations are big.

04:43.500 --> 04:45.750
There's a lot of inherent value

04:45.750 --> 04:47.490
in the brand that they've built up.

04:47.490 --> 04:50.640
Startups generally do not have that problem.

04:50.640 --> 04:53.100
And opportunity cost is another thing

04:53.100 --> 04:54.600
that's big for a company.

04:54.600 --> 04:58.050
A company that has lots of resources, lots of talent

04:58.050 --> 05:02.100
has the option to pursue a lot of different avenues.

05:02.100 --> 05:03.450
For a large organization,

05:03.450 --> 05:06.330
they're gonna care a lot less about wasted resources

05:06.330 --> 05:08.370
and a lot more about things

05:08.370 --> 05:09.930
that startups can't even imagine,

05:09.930 --> 05:12.630
such as not being the first company onto

05:12.630 --> 05:16.800
a brand new platform, or missing a big trend

05:16.800 --> 05:18.300
with one of their product lines.

05:18.300 --> 05:20.700
That's a big deal for large organizations.

05:20.700 --> 05:22.800
Now, the last point I'm gonna make is a product manager,

05:22.800 --> 05:25.111
is that you don't have to think

05:25.111 --> 05:26.820
like a scrappy two person startup.

05:26.820 --> 05:30.300
If you make a new product or feature and it doesn't work,

05:30.300 --> 05:33.785
you will survive to live another day.

05:33.785 --> 05:36.510
And because you are still there, also keep

05:36.510 --> 05:40.170
in mind that failed products can actually benefit you

05:40.170 --> 05:42.900
in other ways, other parts of your organization

05:42.900 --> 05:44.820
can absorb the benefit.

05:44.820 --> 05:47.700
So for as a class activity, I'm gonna ask you a question.

05:47.700 --> 05:50.550
Post in the group discussion with what you would do.

05:50.550 --> 05:53.220
Let's go back to the Google example.

05:53.220 --> 05:54.780
Let's talk about Google Glass.

05:54.780 --> 05:55.860
You remember that one?

05:55.860 --> 05:59.520
Google launched the notorious face wear called Google Glass,

05:59.520 --> 06:02.280
which either turns you into an object

06:02.280 --> 06:04.350
of envy or a target of derision.

06:04.350 --> 06:06.780
Now, as a product manager at Google,

06:06.780 --> 06:09.840
would you have launched Google Glass?

06:09.840 --> 06:11.430
Now, some food for thought,

06:11.430 --> 06:14.520
a lot of you guys would say no.

06:14.520 --> 06:18.270
It was a big waste of money, waste of time.

06:18.270 --> 06:20.490
There was the whole glass hole phenomenon.

06:20.490 --> 06:23.430
People really just didn't adopt it.

06:23.430 --> 06:25.230
But I'm gonna ask you to think just a little bit

06:25.230 --> 06:26.850
about the other benefits

06:26.850 --> 06:29.760
that could have came from Google Glass.

06:29.760 --> 06:30.960
All that money they spent,

06:30.960 --> 06:34.110
it barely even dented their balance sheet.

06:34.110 --> 06:38.160
It made no difference to the company as a whole,

06:38.160 --> 06:41.010
but they did get a ton of buzz,

06:41.010 --> 06:43.650
a ton of press and they enhanced their brand

06:43.650 --> 06:45.240
because they're pushing the envelope

06:45.240 --> 06:47.760
showing themselves as innovative, and at least, hey

06:47.760 --> 06:50.130
they're swinging for the fences and trying something.

06:50.130 --> 06:51.570
So just put us in the group discussion.

06:51.570 --> 06:53.190
State your case.

06:53.190 --> 06:55.050
Do you agree with me or do you agree

06:55.050 --> 06:58.020
with the state standard response, which is no.

06:58.020 --> 07:00.293
All right guys, I'll see you in the next lecture.

response.title: 7 steps to running an MVP experiment

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.240 --> 00:02.340
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the chorus.

00:02.340 --> 00:04.410
All right, so now you are pretty much

00:04.410 --> 00:06.270
up to your neck in information

00:06.270 --> 00:09.450
about how PMs look at MVPs

00:09.450 --> 00:12.420
and what they are from an academic sense.

00:12.420 --> 00:16.920
You understand now that MVPs, they change depending

00:16.920 --> 00:18.840
on the size of the organization

00:18.840 --> 00:22.860
because organizations have vastly different amounts

00:22.860 --> 00:24.360
of resources to work with.

00:24.360 --> 00:27.630
So in the next set of lectures in this section

00:27.630 --> 00:30.570
we're gonna go down step by step the process

00:30.570 --> 00:33.180
that a product manager would take

00:33.180 --> 00:36.060
to come up with an MVP experiment

00:36.060 --> 00:38.310
and run it and then learn from it.

00:38.310 --> 00:39.720
And now I'm gonna explain briefly

00:39.720 --> 00:44.250
how the general flow of an MVP experiment is going to go.

00:44.250 --> 00:47.010
There are seven steps, and we'll start with the first one.

00:47.010 --> 00:49.500
The first step in running an MVP experiment

00:49.500 --> 00:52.170
is just figuring out what exactly

00:52.170 --> 00:55.110
your problem and solution set is.

00:55.110 --> 00:58.230
We're not gonna jump on this in this section

00:58.230 --> 01:01.680
because you should have done that in the last two sections

01:01.680 --> 01:05.700
where we talked about figuring out what problem your product

01:05.700 --> 01:08.220
or feature is actually aiming to solve.

01:08.220 --> 01:09.780
Now you don't obviously need to know

01:09.780 --> 01:12.120
for a fact that that problem exists

01:12.120 --> 01:14.670
and that this solution is the best solution.

01:14.670 --> 01:17.580
That's what we are gonna use an MVP experiment for

01:17.580 --> 01:20.730
to figure out whether or not this is the right combo.

01:20.730 --> 01:22.530
But once you do have on the table

01:22.530 --> 01:25.137
what you want to try the next step.

01:25.137 --> 01:28.050
And step number two is we need to identify

01:28.050 --> 01:32.970
the assumptions associated with this problem solution set.

01:32.970 --> 01:34.650
Anytime you launch a new product,

01:34.650 --> 01:35.550
come up with a new feature.

01:35.550 --> 01:38.340
You are assuming a lot of things,

01:38.340 --> 01:41.370
there are a lot of things you just take for granted,

01:41.370 --> 01:42.540
and there's a lot of different things

01:42.540 --> 01:45.210
that go into the success of a product.

01:45.210 --> 01:48.060
So we're gonna systematically sit down and try to think

01:48.060 --> 01:51.720
of what are all the things that have to go right

01:51.720 --> 01:53.430
for this to be successful.

01:53.430 --> 01:54.540
And then at the end of that process,

01:54.540 --> 01:56.580
we're gonna figure out which of these assumptions

01:56.580 --> 01:57.540
is the riskiest.

01:57.540 --> 02:01.950
Which is the gunpowder and the bottom deck of your ship.

02:01.950 --> 02:04.200
That can just sink the whole thing.

02:04.200 --> 02:05.820
The next step in step number three

02:05.820 --> 02:07.440
is once we have our assumptions,

02:07.440 --> 02:09.360
we're gonna work on building testable

02:09.360 --> 02:11.670
hypotheses out of them.

02:11.670 --> 02:16.670
It's a relatively simple, straightforward technique.

02:16.980 --> 02:18.210
We'll take the assumptions

02:18.210 --> 02:22.260
and we'll figure out a way of stating what exactly we think

02:22.260 --> 02:25.830
they are and then establish criteria for testing them.

02:25.830 --> 02:28.650
Building a hypothesis around a assumption

02:28.650 --> 02:30.720
is relatively easy.

02:30.720 --> 02:34.050
All we're doing is building statements

02:34.050 --> 02:37.560
that we are confident about and we want to test

02:37.560 --> 02:38.670
based off the assumption.

02:38.670 --> 02:40.920
In the next step, step number four, we're gonna set up

02:40.920 --> 02:43.770
what's called a minimum criteria for success.

02:43.770 --> 02:44.940
We're gonna run an experiment

02:44.940 --> 02:49.110
and try to test our assumptions and prove our hypotheses

02:49.110 --> 02:51.990
we need to know what constitutes success,

02:51.990 --> 02:53.880
what constitutes failure.

02:53.880 --> 02:55.890
If you don't do this step

02:55.890 --> 02:58.710
you have no idea if you succeeded or you failed.

02:58.710 --> 03:00.900
We gotta draw a line in the sand

03:00.900 --> 03:02.670
and then later we can look and say,

03:02.670 --> 03:05.040
ah, no, he fell right before the line.

03:05.040 --> 03:06.900
Or, you know, he actually made it past it.

03:06.900 --> 03:08.670
The next step is picking

03:08.670 --> 03:11.340
what type of MVP you're going to run.

03:11.340 --> 03:12.960
There's a lot of different ways

03:12.960 --> 03:16.620
of getting validation from your customers

03:16.620 --> 03:18.630
or potential customers.

03:18.630 --> 03:22.080
It's just gonna depend on what suits you

03:22.080 --> 03:25.170
what suits the feature that you're gonna propose

03:25.170 --> 03:27.240
and you're trying to try to build.

03:27.240 --> 03:29.580
And it's also gonna really matter on

03:29.580 --> 03:31.290
how big your organization is.

03:31.290 --> 03:35.040
The last two steps are executing the MVP experiment

03:35.040 --> 03:38.610
running it, getting out there, getting some real life data

03:38.610 --> 03:42.600
then iterating on that, collecting the data

03:42.600 --> 03:45.660
and figuring out whether or not you succeeded,

03:45.660 --> 03:48.810
what worked, what didn't, and what you would change

03:48.810 --> 03:50.610
if you were gonna redo this experiment.

03:50.610 --> 03:53.520
At that point you'll have a hard yes or no

03:53.520 --> 03:56.250
about whether or not this is a viable option

03:56.250 --> 03:57.900
and it's worth your time

03:57.900 --> 04:00.420
compared to other opportunities you might have.

04:00.420 --> 04:01.830
All right guys, hope you're excited.

04:01.830 --> 04:04.650
In the next lecture, we're gonna start with step number two

04:04.650 --> 04:07.080
which is taking a problem solution set

04:07.080 --> 04:10.500
and identifying assumptions that we are making about it.

04:10.500 --> 04:11.700
All right, see you then.

WEBVTT

00:00.300 --> 00:01.770
-: Hey guys, welcome to the course.

00:01.770 --> 00:02.610
All right, so we're gonna start

00:02.610 --> 00:06.150
down the MVP journey and we're gonna start

00:06.150 --> 00:09.720
with identifying the assumptions that we're making

00:09.720 --> 00:12.240
about our problem solution set.

00:12.240 --> 00:14.220
Now, in case you didn't catch me before when I said this

00:14.220 --> 00:15.630
and at this stage of the game

00:15.630 --> 00:19.770
you probably have an idea of what you are trying to build.

00:19.770 --> 00:22.020
We covered that in the other section.

00:22.020 --> 00:24.923
So you're probably have a decent idea

00:24.923 --> 00:29.580
of what type of user or potential customer you're targeting

00:29.580 --> 00:31.620
and what is the new feature

00:31.620 --> 00:34.230
or the new solution you're gonna be proposing.

00:34.230 --> 00:38.520
Now, every single time you come up with a new idea, whether

00:38.520 --> 00:42.780
or not it's a new business idea or a new feature idea

00:42.780 --> 00:46.620
you are taking on board enormous amount of assumptions.

00:46.620 --> 00:48.240
Like almost anything in life,

00:48.240 --> 00:52.110
we think we know things, and that is built

00:52.110 --> 00:57.090
up of a lot of small things that we assume to be true.

00:57.090 --> 01:00.000
You get into a car and you think

01:00.000 --> 01:03.000
that it's going to be able to start

01:03.000 --> 01:07.260
you're assuming that the engine is actually working.

01:07.260 --> 01:10.740
You're assuming that it has gas in the tank.

01:10.740 --> 01:13.020
You're assuming it's not cold enough

01:13.020 --> 01:14.561
outside that the spark plugs

01:14.561 --> 01:17.190
things get stuck together and it can't start.

01:17.190 --> 01:21.360
There's a lot of assumptions into that even basic idea.

01:21.360 --> 01:24.090
And since all these assumptions have the possibility

01:24.090 --> 01:28.470
of not being true and that creating a problem for us

01:28.470 --> 01:32.820
we need to write out as many of our assumptions that we can.

01:32.820 --> 01:33.653
All right?

01:33.653 --> 01:34.890
So when you start identifying the assumptions

01:34.890 --> 01:38.940
that you are making, it's kind of an abstract process.

01:38.940 --> 01:40.950
There's not generally a step

01:40.950 --> 01:43.134
by step process you'll go through.

01:43.134 --> 01:45.870
Really, you just need to sit down

01:45.870 --> 01:50.870
and think hard about what goes into

01:51.210 --> 01:52.080
you thinking

01:52.080 --> 01:55.410
that your users are gonna like whatever you're gonna build.

01:55.410 --> 01:56.250
You need to recognize

01:56.250 --> 01:58.620
that almost every single time you have one of these ideas

01:58.620 --> 01:59.670
it's gonna be based off

01:59.670 --> 02:04.200
of some type of observation or some type of intuition.

02:04.200 --> 02:06.690
In either case, you just assume

02:06.690 --> 02:09.540
and take for granted many, many things.

02:09.540 --> 02:11.408
Now, there are two different ways I like to try to get

02:11.408 --> 02:13.680
into kind of the mindset that'll help me

02:13.680 --> 02:15.959
identify these tricky little things.

02:15.959 --> 02:20.370
The first one is to try to think about the lean mantra

02:20.370 --> 02:22.140
which is that you we're running this

02:22.140 --> 02:25.140
like a science experiment and any science experiment

02:25.140 --> 02:27.270
we're challenging ourselves with regards

02:27.270 --> 02:29.460
to what we know is true.

02:29.460 --> 02:31.999
And so what we need to do to be successful is to get

02:31.999 --> 02:34.920
in kind of a Lean Zen mode

02:34.920 --> 02:38.493
and keep saying to ourselves, We don't know anything.

02:39.540 --> 02:41.440
-: You know nothing, John Snow

02:44.460 --> 02:47.580
-: Anything you think you know is an assumption.

02:47.580 --> 02:49.800
So what do you think you already know

02:49.800 --> 02:50.880
before you've even gotten

02:50.880 --> 02:53.040
out there and tried to build something?

02:53.040 --> 02:54.630
Now, the second way

02:54.630 --> 02:56.610
and I think this one's probably more effective

02:56.610 --> 02:58.601
for most people, and I like to write this one out

02:58.601 --> 03:03.601
is in order for this problem solution

03:03.780 --> 03:05.700
set to be successful

03:05.700 --> 03:07.830
the following must be true.

03:07.830 --> 03:11.010
And then just try to fill in all of the factors you think go

03:11.010 --> 03:14.280
into this potentially being successful.

03:14.280 --> 03:15.113
Google Glass.

03:15.113 --> 03:16.320
What do you think has to be true

03:16.320 --> 03:18.450
for that to have been successful?

03:18.450 --> 03:21.000
Well, you probably have to assume

03:21.000 --> 03:24.360
that people will wear a camera on their face.

03:24.360 --> 03:29.070
What has to be true in order for Udemy to be successful?

03:29.070 --> 03:29.903
It has to be true

03:29.903 --> 03:32.370
that people are gonna be comfortable paying money

03:32.370 --> 03:34.530
for online courses.

03:34.530 --> 03:37.500
Whether or not we're at the stage in our development

03:37.500 --> 03:40.710
that we trust online courses enough to pay money.

03:40.710 --> 03:42.390
That's a legitimate question.

03:42.390 --> 03:43.530
If you're having natural regrets

03:43.530 --> 03:46.200
about buying this course, I don't blame you.

03:46.200 --> 03:47.610
I do blame Cole.

03:47.610 --> 03:49.260
Now, in most cases, there are enormous amount

03:49.260 --> 03:51.780
of assumptions that you could write out for this.

03:51.780 --> 03:55.440
All we wanna do is focus on the big ones

03:55.440 --> 03:59.340
and I'm gonna give you some examples of common assumptions

03:59.340 --> 04:02.340
that almost everybody makes, and then hopefully

04:02.340 --> 04:05.400
this can be a template for you going forward

04:05.400 --> 04:09.630
and it'll help you find a whole batch of other assumptions.

04:09.630 --> 04:10.463
Now, the first assumption

04:10.463 --> 04:11.820
that almost everyone seems to make is

04:11.820 --> 04:14.970
My user has X, Y, Z problem.

04:14.970 --> 04:18.180
Remember, we had the problem solution set.

04:18.180 --> 04:19.770
You're probably trying to build something

04:19.770 --> 04:22.050
that targets someone's problem

04:22.050 --> 04:24.600
but how do you know if that problem is real?

04:24.600 --> 04:28.020
How do you know if the customer considers it a problem?

04:28.020 --> 04:30.480
That is an assumption, and it's an assumption

04:30.480 --> 04:33.450
in pretty much anything you ever build.

04:33.450 --> 04:36.390
If you're building Bluetooth headphones, you're assuming

04:36.390 --> 04:39.270
that people really are fed up with cords.

04:39.270 --> 04:40.560
If you're building this Snuggie,

04:40.560 --> 04:44.940
that big one piece wizard suit, you're assuming

04:44.940 --> 04:49.940
that it is too inconvenient to just put a blanket on you.

04:50.190 --> 04:52.380
Now, the number two most common assumption I

04:52.380 --> 04:55.620
see is blank matters to my customers.

04:55.620 --> 04:58.320
Anytime you do a new feature or business idea

04:58.320 --> 05:01.800
or product idea, you are assuming it's gonna be successful

05:01.800 --> 05:05.850
because you're gonna take some kind of angle with it

05:05.850 --> 05:08.880
and that angle is something your customers care about.

05:08.880 --> 05:10.350
If I'm building something that's specifically

05:10.350 --> 05:12.000
going to be cheaper than the competition

05:12.000 --> 05:14.670
I'm assuming people care about it being cheaper.

05:14.670 --> 05:17.310
If I'm trying to build something that is more convenient

05:17.310 --> 05:18.240
than the competition,

05:18.240 --> 05:21.960
I'm assuming that convenience is a big factor

05:21.960 --> 05:24.270
in what they care about with the service.

05:24.270 --> 05:26.730
So don't assume what your customers care about

05:26.730 --> 05:28.560
but do write it out.

05:28.560 --> 05:32.160
Number three is that users will pay

05:32.160 --> 05:36.450
or sign up for this service or product.

05:36.450 --> 05:38.730
Now, that's kind of an optional assumption.

05:38.730 --> 05:41.490
This is assuming you have a product that charges

05:41.490 --> 05:46.490
or signs people up in some way to eventually make money.

05:46.680 --> 05:48.450
Eventually, we all wanna make money

05:48.450 --> 05:50.010
and all products will eventually get

05:50.010 --> 05:52.770
to a point where it makes money.

05:52.770 --> 05:53.910
So either have to go one

05:53.910 --> 05:56.550
of those avenues, charging or signing them up.

05:56.550 --> 05:57.900
So that's always, always an assumption.

05:57.900 --> 06:01.050
Maybe the problem is a big problem, but do we know

06:01.050 --> 06:03.870
if it's a big enough problem for them to say pay money

06:03.870 --> 06:06.960
for it or give you their contact details?

06:06.960 --> 06:11.070
If I'm the genius behind the Hot Pocket, I'm assuming

06:11.070 --> 06:13.710
that people will enjoy a

06:13.710 --> 06:17.250
big bean burrito of lava

06:17.250 --> 06:20.070
so much so that they would pay money for it.

06:20.070 --> 06:20.903
And number four is

06:20.903 --> 06:23.610
that there are no satisfactory substitutes.

06:23.610 --> 06:27.180
A lot of times we just assume that

06:27.180 --> 06:30.275
our solution is better than what's available, but it's

06:30.275 --> 06:33.720
it's often the case that customers have already figured

06:33.720 --> 06:35.520
out a way to solve that problem

06:35.520 --> 06:37.860
and there's really no reason to switch.

06:37.860 --> 06:38.820
I'll give you an example.

06:38.820 --> 06:41.310
Let's say we are making an app that told me what

06:41.310 --> 06:43.710
the temperature is right now.

06:43.710 --> 06:46.410
Now are there satisfactory substitutions for that?

06:46.410 --> 06:48.330
Absolutely. Not only could I go

06:48.330 --> 06:53.010
to weather.com or any of the other hundred weather services

06:53.010 --> 06:56.940
I could just look out the window or just walk outside.

06:56.940 --> 06:57.960
What a concept.

06:57.960 --> 06:59.700
So keep that one in mind

06:59.700 --> 07:04.170
and try to test whether or not there are alternatives

07:04.170 --> 07:06.810
out there that are already meeting their needs.

07:06.810 --> 07:07.643
All right, guys.

07:07.643 --> 07:08.476
In the next section

07:08.476 --> 07:10.950
we're gonna talk about risky assumptions

07:10.950 --> 07:13.231
and then after that we'll do some practice

07:13.231 --> 07:16.410
with this so you guys can see some real life examples.

07:16.410 --> 07:17.410
All right, See then.

response.title: FOLLOW ALONG: Let's identify the assumptions for Zirx

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.270 --> 00:01.980
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:01.980 --> 00:05.580
Hey, I totally lied at the end of the last lecture.

00:05.580 --> 00:07.110
We're actually just gonna jump in

00:07.110 --> 00:10.020
and start working with a real example

00:10.020 --> 00:13.980
and then go over what are some example assumptions

00:13.980 --> 00:17.160
that we can think of for this product.

00:17.160 --> 00:18.960
Yes, I know, I lied, you'll get over it.

00:18.960 --> 00:20.550
I'm over it already, let's start.

00:20.550 --> 00:23.820
All right, so for our example we're gonna use a service

00:23.820 --> 00:28.800
that is somewhat well known in the San Francisco Bay Area.

00:28.800 --> 00:33.800
It's not really outside of New York and San Francisco,

00:34.110 --> 00:38.460
I think, right now, it might be in Chicago and Boston,

00:38.460 --> 00:43.200
but it's enough of an an idea that's a little bit out there

00:43.200 --> 00:47.250
that we can come up with some assumptions relatively easily.

00:47.250 --> 00:49.320
And it's a good example to use.

00:49.320 --> 00:53.640
It's an example that's called Zirx, that's Z-I-R-X.

00:53.640 --> 00:57.330
And what they do is they build a mobile app

00:57.330 --> 01:00.510
that allows you when you are driving your car

01:00.510 --> 01:04.710
to hail an on-demand valet person

01:04.710 --> 01:09.690
who picks up your car and then takes it to storage

01:09.690 --> 01:13.140
and then you can request your car back at any time.

01:13.140 --> 01:16.860
So basically what they're trying to do is on-demand valet.

01:16.860 --> 01:20.850
And the idea is that if you're driving, let's say downtown,

01:20.850 --> 01:23.310
you're going out to eat, you have a car,

01:23.310 --> 01:27.330
in any big city, parking is just a disaster.

01:27.330 --> 01:30.090
And garages are typically inconvenient.

01:30.090 --> 01:32.520
They're hard to get into, they're hard to find,

01:32.520 --> 01:33.480
especially when you're trying to do

01:33.480 --> 01:38.310
a hundred different things at once and they're expensive.

01:38.310 --> 01:40.530
So for all the same reasons you would use a valet,

01:40.530 --> 01:42.180
you would use an on-demand valet.

01:42.180 --> 01:44.550
But the advantage of being an on-demand valet

01:44.550 --> 01:47.670
is that I can call a valet person

01:47.670 --> 01:50.790
and they can take that car literally anywhere.

01:50.790 --> 01:53.730
They don't have to park it in a garage near me.

01:53.730 --> 01:55.380
They can take it out to the suburbs

01:55.380 --> 01:58.530
where it costs nothing to store.

01:58.530 --> 02:00.660
And then all I have to do is just tell them

02:00.660 --> 02:02.340
a little bit before when I need it

02:02.340 --> 02:04.590
and it works the exact same way.

02:04.590 --> 02:05.580
It's an interesting service.

02:05.580 --> 02:07.860
Check it out if you guys are ever in San Francisco

02:07.860 --> 02:09.780
and have a car and want to do it.

02:09.780 --> 02:10.980
They have a lot of funding

02:10.980 --> 02:13.740
and they are launching in a lot of different cities

02:13.740 --> 02:17.070
and they have a lot of competitors, mainly copycats.

02:17.070 --> 02:20.220
There's one called Luxe and there's one called Caarbon.

02:20.220 --> 02:21.630
All right, so get familiar with them

02:21.630 --> 02:23.040
'cause we're gonna do some assumptions.

02:23.040 --> 02:25.620
Now, the core problem solution set

02:25.620 --> 02:27.840
for Zirx is the problem, right,

02:27.840 --> 02:32.400
is that it's hard to park your car in big cities

02:32.400 --> 02:36.060
and or it's hard to find garages

02:36.060 --> 02:39.360
which are inconvenient and expensive.

02:39.360 --> 02:40.800
And then the solution to that

02:40.800 --> 02:44.910
is by using flexible on-demand valets

02:44.910 --> 02:48.510
through a mobile application to take advantage

02:48.510 --> 02:52.260
of technology for speed and for proximity

02:52.260 --> 02:54.360
so they can save money on the storage costs.

02:54.360 --> 02:55.830
All right, so we're building that product.

02:55.830 --> 02:58.950
What did we assume? Well, a whole lot.

02:58.950 --> 03:03.300
Pretty much every sentence I said to you about why they work

03:03.300 --> 03:06.870
or why they're appealing, was just loaded with assumptions.

03:06.870 --> 03:08.670
Now, let's use the technique that we learned before

03:08.670 --> 03:11.490
and say the following must be true

03:11.490 --> 03:13.710
in order for our idea to be successful.

03:13.710 --> 03:15.300
So what has to be true?

03:15.300 --> 03:17.820
Well, we'll start off with one of the more obvious ones.

03:17.820 --> 03:18.653
Remember how I said

03:18.653 --> 03:21.570
that parking is hard to find in big cities?

03:21.570 --> 03:22.830
That's an assumption.

03:22.830 --> 03:25.620
I do not know that for an absolute fact.

03:25.620 --> 03:27.360
So I'm gonna write that out.

03:27.360 --> 03:31.260
Parking is bad or difficult in big cities.

03:31.260 --> 03:34.620
Maybe it's the case that people don't find parking

03:34.620 --> 03:39.620
to be an issue or maybe most cities don't have this issue.

03:40.560 --> 03:42.420
So what are some other examples that we can think of?

03:42.420 --> 03:44.370
Well, let's use those examples

03:44.370 --> 03:45.900
that we learned in the last lecture.

03:45.900 --> 03:47.640
Well, most times we're making assumptions

03:47.640 --> 03:49.440
about the problem we think we're solving.

03:49.440 --> 03:50.910
So remember how I said

03:50.910 --> 03:53.790
that people find parking garages inconvenient?

03:53.790 --> 03:55.020
That is an assumption.

03:55.020 --> 03:57.510
Do people really find them inconvenient?

03:57.510 --> 04:00.840
It could be the case that people have no issue with them.

04:00.840 --> 04:02.640
They're everywhere, they're so easy to spot,

04:02.640 --> 04:05.430
they have signs and maybe those guys who twirl

04:05.430 --> 04:07.830
their signs in the middle of the road, whatever.

04:07.830 --> 04:09.240
And then the corollary to that

04:09.240 --> 04:12.540
was that people find them expensive, right?

04:12.540 --> 04:15.210
I said that, you know, there's a cost advantage

04:15.210 --> 04:17.970
by using an on-demand valet system,

04:17.970 --> 04:21.240
but do people really think the alternative,

04:21.240 --> 04:24.690
which are garages, are too expensive?

04:24.690 --> 04:26.610
It might be the case that people who use garages

04:26.610 --> 04:27.960
are used to those prices

04:27.960 --> 04:30.720
and so they don't actually think of it as a problem.

04:30.720 --> 04:31.860
Let's move on to the other one.

04:31.860 --> 04:34.170
What are things we are assuming

04:34.170 --> 04:37.380
that matter to our potential user?

04:37.380 --> 04:38.213
Well, I mean,

04:38.213 --> 04:40.950
think about the value proposition of this app, right?

04:40.950 --> 04:43.800
We're trying to save them money and make it more convenient.

04:43.800 --> 04:45.750
Those are the two biggest things.

04:45.750 --> 04:47.280
So we're kind of assuming

04:47.280 --> 04:49.410
that someone who's driving their car downtown

04:49.410 --> 04:52.950
and wants to park it for dinner or work or whatever

04:52.950 --> 04:55.770
cares about price and convenience.

04:55.770 --> 04:57.240
And we're also assuming

04:57.240 --> 05:00.480
that they don't really care about speed, right?

05:00.480 --> 05:02.790
Because if we're using the on-demand system,

05:02.790 --> 05:04.560
it might actually be slower

05:04.560 --> 05:07.710
than if you're using a garage where you know where it is

05:07.710 --> 05:09.870
or a valet service that's already embedded

05:09.870 --> 05:11.370
into the restaurant you're going to.

05:11.370 --> 05:13.590
Now, are there any satisfactory substitutes?

05:13.590 --> 05:14.790
Well, we kind of already addressed it

05:14.790 --> 05:17.370
when we said parking on the street is a substitute

05:17.370 --> 05:19.050
or parking in a garage is a substitute.

05:19.050 --> 05:21.600
But we could just say that people are not satisfied

05:21.600 --> 05:24.270
with parking their car in a garage.

05:24.270 --> 05:25.410
Now let's think about some things

05:25.410 --> 05:27.600
that we're assuming about the market.

05:27.600 --> 05:28.980
We're assuming that the market

05:28.980 --> 05:30.630
that we're gonna be able to serve,

05:30.630 --> 05:34.710
let's say, has enough drivers with smartphone.

05:34.710 --> 05:37.500
That is actually an important assumption.

05:37.500 --> 05:40.470
If you're going into a market and you build a smartphone app

05:40.470 --> 05:43.200
and the vast majority of people don't have smartphones,

05:43.200 --> 05:46.200
then it could potentially be a venture

05:46.200 --> 05:48.390
that's not nearly as lucrative or interesting.

05:48.390 --> 05:50.640
We're also assuming in a weird way

05:50.640 --> 05:53.460
that the driver can operate the smartphone

05:53.460 --> 05:55.980
while they're driving the car, right?

05:55.980 --> 05:57.720
I mean, texting and driving is terrible,

05:57.720 --> 05:59.520
but I can't even imagine what it's like

05:59.520 --> 06:01.920
trying to place a pin on the phone

06:01.920 --> 06:04.860
while you're like navigating traffic 'cause you're downtown.

06:04.860 --> 06:06.390
So it might be the case that, you know,

06:06.390 --> 06:09.630
everyone who uses a service has someone else in the car.

06:09.630 --> 06:12.150
Maybe solo drivers, not a great idea.

06:12.150 --> 06:15.480
Another area that we're making assumptions about

06:15.480 --> 06:17.640
actually is the other side of the market, right?

06:17.640 --> 06:19.680
We have to hire people to be the valet.

06:19.680 --> 06:21.180
So we're actually assuming

06:21.180 --> 06:24.090
that people want to be on-demand valets.

06:24.090 --> 06:25.500
And then the last thing I would say

06:25.500 --> 06:27.810
is just trust and safety.

06:27.810 --> 06:31.140
We are assuming that people are going to be comfortable

06:31.140 --> 06:35.250
giving their car to what they call a Zirx agent.

06:35.250 --> 06:37.650
Seriously, Zirx's a terrible, terrible name,

06:37.650 --> 06:38.880
but interesting service.

06:38.880 --> 06:41.400
All right guys, so we got 10 assumptions right there.

06:41.400 --> 06:43.890
Those are all big ones.

06:43.890 --> 06:47.070
If you guys have any other ones you wanna add,

06:47.070 --> 06:48.660
post actually in the group discussion,

06:48.660 --> 06:50.370
tell me which ones I left out.

06:50.370 --> 06:53.190
And if you disagree with some of these being assumptions,

06:53.190 --> 06:54.660
I would love to hear it.

06:54.660 --> 06:57.480
We can fight it out and then we'll see who's right.

06:57.480 --> 06:58.890
All right, so in the next lecture what we're gonna do

06:58.890 --> 07:00.810
is we're gonna take these assumptions

07:00.810 --> 07:03.630
and organize them by risk.

07:03.630 --> 07:05.580
All right, see you in the next lecture.

response.title: Finding the riskiest assumption of them all

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.090 --> 00:02.673
(upbeat music)

00:04.290 --> 00:06.270
-: Hey, guys, welcome back to the course.

00:06.270 --> 00:07.860
So in this lecture, we're gonna talk about

00:07.860 --> 00:09.720
how do we take our assumption list

00:09.720 --> 00:14.280
and identify which of them are the really risky ones,

00:14.280 --> 00:16.650
and which ones are not as risky.

00:16.650 --> 00:18.390
Now, the reason why we do this

00:18.390 --> 00:21.450
is because it's a function of being lean,

00:21.450 --> 00:24.960
and it's a function of running an MVP experiment.

00:24.960 --> 00:28.410
Now remember, it's all about mitigating risk

00:28.410 --> 00:30.600
and saving resources.

00:30.600 --> 00:34.710
So, if we have a lot of factors that have to be true

00:34.710 --> 00:38.040
in order for our product or feature to be successful,

00:38.040 --> 00:41.970
it makes sense to focus first on the ones

00:41.970 --> 00:44.040
that could potentially sink the ship

00:44.040 --> 00:46.744
and make the entire thing unviable,

00:46.744 --> 00:50.730
versus some of the less risky things

00:50.730 --> 00:53.370
that are not really a big deal.

00:53.370 --> 00:54.690
You don't wanna get into a situation

00:54.690 --> 00:58.560
where you spend a lot of time validating assumptions

00:58.560 --> 01:01.230
that were very, very small and unrisky,

01:01.230 --> 01:04.200
only to come up to let's say your sixth experiment

01:04.200 --> 01:07.500
where you test a risky one and you fail miserably,

01:07.500 --> 01:09.900
and you completely scrap the project.

01:09.900 --> 01:11.460
That's gonna happen all the time,

01:11.460 --> 01:13.590
but you wasted so many resources

01:13.590 --> 01:16.252
that you didn't have to waste, had you just started

01:16.252 --> 01:19.980
the opposite direction with the riskiest one first.

01:19.980 --> 01:21.360
In this section, we're gonna come up with

01:21.360 --> 01:24.960
an MVP experiment structure that's gonna target

01:24.960 --> 01:27.990
as many of the riskiest ones as we can,

01:27.990 --> 01:30.390
but we first gotta figure out which ones are risky.

01:30.390 --> 01:33.000
So let's look at the template of examples

01:33.000 --> 01:34.290
that we used before.

01:34.290 --> 01:35.760
Number one was the assumption

01:35.760 --> 01:38.880
that our customer has x, y, z problem.

01:38.880 --> 01:42.360
Number two is that we assume our customer cares

01:42.360 --> 01:44.340
about whatever we think they care about.

01:44.340 --> 01:46.320
Number three, we're assuming that the solution

01:46.320 --> 01:50.130
we're about to create is valuable enough to the user,

01:50.130 --> 01:53.010
or potential user, for them to either pay you,

01:53.010 --> 01:57.360
or give you their email address or some form of information.

01:57.360 --> 02:00.180
And then the last one on the template was that we assume

02:00.180 --> 02:02.507
that there are no satisfactory substitutes,

02:02.507 --> 02:05.940
meaning that there's no other way of doing this

02:05.940 --> 02:08.070
that people find to be okay.

02:08.070 --> 02:10.380
So now, just out of these examples,

02:10.380 --> 02:13.620
let's say you just had a theoretical product idea,

02:13.620 --> 02:15.516
which of these four do you think

02:15.516 --> 02:18.180
is probably the biggest deal?

02:18.180 --> 02:22.260
As in, if one of these things turned out to be false,

02:22.260 --> 02:26.203
we assume this and it's not true,

02:26.203 --> 02:29.970
which of them is probably going to be the biggest deal?

02:29.970 --> 02:33.330
Which one could potentially just blow up

02:33.330 --> 02:35.370
and make all the other ones not matter?

02:35.370 --> 02:37.140
So let's just start from the bottom.

02:37.140 --> 02:40.380
No satisfactory substitutions.

02:40.380 --> 02:42.030
Well, there are plenty of products

02:42.030 --> 02:43.800
and plenty of services out there
