02:43.800 --> 02:47.610
that competed with satisfactory substitutions.

02:47.610 --> 02:49.650
People didn't even realize they had a problem with it

02:49.650 --> 02:51.037
until someone popped up and said,

02:51.037 --> 02:53.970
"Hey, here's a better way of doing it."

02:53.970 --> 02:56.580
People were pretty satisfied with their feature phones.

02:56.580 --> 02:58.230
I mean, they had a lot of issues,

02:58.230 --> 03:00.510
but for the most part, if you asked someone

03:00.510 --> 03:03.690
who had a feature phone you know, before 2006,

03:03.690 --> 03:06.630
they'd probably say, "Yeah, I'm pretty satisfied with it."

03:06.630 --> 03:09.930
and that was before they came out with the iPhone.

03:09.930 --> 03:13.530
So people can typically get dislodged from substitutions

03:13.530 --> 03:16.530
if you show them there's a better way on some dimension.

03:16.530 --> 03:19.740
Number three, they will pay for it or sign up for it.

03:19.740 --> 03:22.710
Well, that's a problem if they won't

03:22.710 --> 03:25.470
because it's an issue for your business model.

03:25.470 --> 03:28.083
But is it absolutely a game changer,

03:28.083 --> 03:31.050
something that you know, just drops the curtain,

03:31.050 --> 03:32.670
you can't possibly go forward?

03:32.670 --> 03:33.750
Well, no, not really

03:33.750 --> 03:36.270
'cause there's plenty of other ways of monetizing,

03:36.270 --> 03:38.430
or growing a successful product,

03:38.430 --> 03:42.510
that don't rely on the customer paying you directly.

03:42.510 --> 03:44.910
For instance, you can work with ads.

03:44.910 --> 03:46.470
Ads don't require any sign up,

03:46.470 --> 03:49.320
and they don't require anyone paying you any money.

03:49.320 --> 03:52.710
It might not be ideal but it's still a viable avenue.

03:52.710 --> 03:55.320
Number two, blank matters to my customers.

03:55.320 --> 03:56.580
You know, if you're trying to build something,

03:56.580 --> 03:58.620
let's say that is super fast,

03:58.620 --> 03:59.700
and you find out that people

03:59.700 --> 04:02.190
don't really care how fast it is.

04:02.190 --> 04:03.187
Let's say we were, you know, Domino's Pizza, and we

04:03.187 --> 04:08.187
came out with the 30 minutes or less delivery strategy.

04:09.210 --> 04:11.100
We found out that no one cares.

04:11.100 --> 04:12.469
It doesn't matter if they get it in 30 minutes

04:12.469 --> 04:15.090
or two hours or three hours.

04:15.090 --> 04:16.590
Now, would it be a game ender?

04:16.590 --> 04:20.850
Well, no, but it would be a a pretty significant setback.

04:20.850 --> 04:23.610
The thing is that we have a thing called marketing,

04:23.610 --> 04:25.080
and the job of marketing is to

04:25.080 --> 04:27.420
convince people that they care about things

04:27.420 --> 04:29.250
they didn't previously care about.

04:29.250 --> 04:31.200
So it's very plausible that dominoes

04:31.200 --> 04:34.017
could just run an ad convincing people

04:34.017 --> 04:37.680
that you want pizza and pizza's better if you eat it sooner.

04:37.680 --> 04:41.287
Before I bought a 1080p HDTV, if you had asked me,

04:41.287 --> 04:43.650
"Do you care about the resolution of your TV?"

04:43.650 --> 04:45.960
I'd say, What are you talking about?

04:45.960 --> 04:47.310
What is resolution?

04:47.310 --> 04:50.730
But after I bought one and I saw what they could do,

04:50.730 --> 04:52.620
yeah all of a sudden I started caring

04:52.620 --> 04:54.540
because that was really crisp.

04:54.540 --> 04:56.850
This is actually shot in 1080p, not 4k,

04:56.850 --> 04:59.443
and you can thank us for that cause if it was shot in 4k,

04:59.443 --> 05:01.110
you wouldn't be paying attention.

05:01.110 --> 05:05.730
You'd be lost in every contour and bump on my face.

05:05.730 --> 05:08.670
Now the last one is, my customer has x, y, and z problem.

05:08.670 --> 05:12.060
That actually is a big, big deal.

05:12.060 --> 05:14.850
If we're trying to build something that solves a problem,

05:14.850 --> 05:16.470
and that problem does not exist,

05:16.470 --> 05:19.950
we are building a solution in search of a problem,

05:19.950 --> 05:21.300
and that never goes well.

05:21.300 --> 05:22.950
So I would actually say the lack

05:22.950 --> 05:27.090
of a problem in this scenario is by far the riskiest.

05:27.090 --> 05:29.490
And as a product manager, what I would do then

05:29.490 --> 05:34.020
is try to design my MVP experiment to target that one.

05:34.020 --> 05:36.630
After I proved that one to be true, I would then

05:36.630 --> 05:40.380
move on to the next riskiest, which is probably number two,

05:40.380 --> 05:42.450
and then the next one is probably number four,

05:42.450 --> 05:44.100
and then the next one is probably number three.

05:44.100 --> 05:45.540
In the next lecture we're going to

05:45.540 --> 05:48.870
find the riskiest assumption of our example Zercs.

05:48.870 --> 05:50.610
You guys can skip this one if you want.

05:50.610 --> 05:54.450
Totally not mandatory, but if you do care, watch it.

05:54.450 --> 05:56.950
Otherwise, we'll see you in the next next lecture.

response.title: Making decisions: The risk / difficulty square

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.210 --> 00:01.043
-: Hey guys.

00:01.043 --> 00:01.876
Welcome back.

00:01.876 --> 00:04.290
All right, let's talk product management strategy

00:04.290 --> 00:06.990
as it relates to testing out your assumptions.

00:06.990 --> 00:08.460
Now, as you remember,

00:08.460 --> 00:10.830
and as we established MVP,

00:10.830 --> 00:12.630
it's just a technique for testing whether

00:12.630 --> 00:14.425
or not a new product or feature

00:14.425 --> 00:17.070
is gonna be adopted by your user base

00:17.070 --> 00:18.750
or potential customers.

00:18.750 --> 00:21.960
Remember this, the next step when you have a list

00:21.960 --> 00:25.200
of things you want to add or test or build,

00:25.200 --> 00:27.330
is to break it down into assumptions

00:27.330 --> 00:30.120
and build hypotheses around them.

00:30.120 --> 00:32.220
That's where we're at right now.

00:32.220 --> 00:35.160
Now you have a list of everything you think.

00:35.160 --> 00:36.630
How do you decide which one you should

00:36.630 --> 00:39.480
direct your effort towards proving first?

00:39.480 --> 00:43.200
Well, we know which one is the riskiest, right?

00:43.200 --> 00:47.490
So wait, why don't we just jump right into testing that?

00:47.490 --> 00:49.680
Well, it's complicated,

00:49.680 --> 00:52.170
and if you were an entrepreneur

00:52.170 --> 00:54.270
I would say absolutely the first thing you should do

00:54.270 --> 00:56.910
is just tackle the riskiest assumption you have.

00:56.910 --> 00:58.770
But as a product manager

00:58.770 --> 01:01.680
it's a little bit more nuanced than that.

01:01.680 --> 01:03.060
There are other things you have to take

01:03.060 --> 01:06.630
into consideration to make the right decision.

01:06.630 --> 01:08.310
That's what we're gonna talk about in this lecture.

01:08.310 --> 01:10.920
So I'm gonna introduce to you a structured way

01:10.920 --> 01:13.590
of viewing your test and your hypotheses.

01:13.590 --> 01:15.690
The reality of any situation is going to be

01:15.690 --> 01:17.340
that you have assumptions

01:17.340 --> 01:20.220
and God only knows if they're true.

01:20.220 --> 01:22.800
You need to figure out whether or not they are.

01:22.800 --> 01:25.770
You also have to contend with the fact that

01:25.770 --> 01:28.740
as an organization you probably have

01:28.740 --> 01:31.620
limited resources for these experiments.

01:31.620 --> 01:35.610
Now companies have to focus on a million different things,

01:35.610 --> 01:38.250
and chances are this little test you're running

01:38.250 --> 01:41.100
is probably not the biggest thing in the universe to them.

01:41.100 --> 01:43.410
So your design team and your dev team are really

01:43.410 --> 01:46.320
gonna have to ration their time based

01:46.320 --> 01:50.070
on how important the task at hand is going to be.

01:50.070 --> 01:52.350
That's exactly the mentality we're gonna use to

01:52.350 --> 01:54.780
pick what to test first.

01:54.780 --> 01:57.300
Now product assumptions generally will have

01:57.300 --> 01:59.280
two main criteria:

01:59.280 --> 02:00.660
one is their risk,

02:00.660 --> 02:05.580
and two is the difficulty associated with executing on them.

02:05.580 --> 02:08.670
Now, risk in this case means how risky they are

02:08.670 --> 02:13.320
to a potential feature or product as a whole.

02:13.320 --> 02:15.510
Now remember we talked about how some can

02:15.510 --> 02:19.170
sink the ship and others just change its course.

02:19.170 --> 02:20.610
Difficulty on the other hand,

02:20.610 --> 02:22.740
refers to how hard it would be to figure

02:22.740 --> 02:26.310
out whether or not this assumption is real,

02:26.310 --> 02:30.090
as in how much effort would you have to channel

02:30.090 --> 02:31.470
into testing this?

02:31.470 --> 02:34.050
It's often thought of in a graph.

02:34.050 --> 02:36.810
On the Y axis we have risk,

02:36.810 --> 02:39.600
and on the X axis we have difficulty.

02:39.600 --> 02:42.870
Now, since we have risk and we have difficulty

02:42.870 --> 02:46.050
we can divide this up into four different quadrants.

02:46.050 --> 02:47.130
You obviously have things that are

02:47.130 --> 02:49.230
low risk, low difficulty.

02:49.230 --> 02:51.960
Then we have a quadrant that's low risk, high difficulty.

02:51.960 --> 02:54.600
Another one that's high risk, low difficulty,

02:54.600 --> 02:57.120
and another one that's high risk, high difficulty.

02:57.120 --> 03:00.150
Now an example of a high risk, low effort assumption:

03:00.150 --> 03:01.650
let's say that you were gonna make

03:01.650 --> 03:05.010
a subscription business for Airbnb hosts.

03:05.010 --> 03:08.580
What it does is it mails them gifts

03:08.580 --> 03:10.650
that they give to their guests.

03:10.650 --> 03:12.750
They sign up, they say their preferences,

03:12.750 --> 03:15.240
and it sends them out the gifts.

03:15.240 --> 03:16.920
Now, it's really risky that guests

03:16.920 --> 03:21.696
might not actually want gifts from their hosts,

03:21.696 --> 03:23.700
and that's something that's easy to find out

03:23.700 --> 03:25.860
because all you have to do to figure out

03:25.860 --> 03:28.440
that assumption is get one owner

03:28.440 --> 03:30.240
to give out a couple of gifts,

03:30.240 --> 03:32.850
or you know just ask a couple guests,

03:32.850 --> 03:34.950
get them on the phone.

03:34.950 --> 03:37.620
Very risky, very easy to figure out

03:37.620 --> 03:39.510
whether or not that assumption is true.

03:39.510 --> 03:41.190
Now, let's look at the inverse.

03:41.190 --> 03:43.160
What's something that's low risk?

03:43.160 --> 03:45.630
As in, it's not gonna sink the ship,

03:45.630 --> 03:47.220
it's not a huge deal,

03:47.220 --> 03:49.200
but to answer that question,

03:49.200 --> 03:51.060
it'd be pretty difficult.

03:51.060 --> 03:53.010
Let's stick with the same example.

03:53.010 --> 03:56.640
Subscription service that sends gifts to Airbnb host.

03:56.640 --> 03:58.740
Well, I could think of one.

03:58.740 --> 04:02.520
It's that hosts might not buy gifts

04:02.520 --> 04:04.530
or buy items that they would have

04:04.530 --> 04:07.680
otherwise had access to in their own city.

04:07.680 --> 04:10.770
Well, how exactly would you test whether or not

04:10.770 --> 04:14.070
they'll buy these items from you

04:14.070 --> 04:15.390
that they have access to?

04:15.390 --> 04:18.210
Well, you probably have to build a website,

04:18.210 --> 04:20.640
find things they already know they can buy,

04:20.640 --> 04:22.350
and then stock those things

04:22.350 --> 04:24.480
and see if they buy those things.

04:24.480 --> 04:25.620
You couldn't just ask them

04:25.620 --> 04:28.050
on the phone whether or not they'd buy it.

04:28.050 --> 04:29.760
That's not a scenario where

04:29.760 --> 04:31.710
what they tell you off the cuff,

04:31.710 --> 04:33.870
what they tell you, subjectively,

04:33.870 --> 04:35.610
it's probably not gonna be true.

04:35.610 --> 04:37.500
You'll need to see it in practice.

04:37.500 --> 04:38.580
Now, as a product manager,

04:38.580 --> 04:39.930
you're gonna wanna focus your effort

04:39.930 --> 04:42.840
on what gets you the most bang for your buck.

04:42.840 --> 04:44.790
Since you don't drive

04:44.790 --> 04:47.940
the singular focus of your organization,

04:47.940 --> 04:50.520
which by the way this is entirely different

04:50.520 --> 04:52.170
than when you are an entrepreneur

04:52.170 --> 04:54.510
cuz as an entrepreneur you are everything.

04:54.510 --> 04:58.590
You'll have to really pick what is most worth it.

04:58.590 --> 05:00.450
Generally, the best category to target

05:00.450 --> 05:01.680
is kind of obvious right?

05:01.680 --> 05:04.230
It's high risk, low difficulty.

05:04.230 --> 05:06.450
We can make pretty meaningful progress

05:06.450 --> 05:09.480
without taking up a ton of people's time.

05:09.480 --> 05:11.340
Now, after that, you're generally gonna want to

05:11.340 --> 05:14.670
focus on things that are still high risk.

05:14.670 --> 05:17.460
Now, since you've knocked some things out,

05:17.460 --> 05:19.530
you're feeling more confident about your project,

05:19.530 --> 05:21.060
you can now start to ask

05:21.060 --> 05:24.030
for larger commitments from your team.

05:24.030 --> 05:25.890
Then after that you're probably gonna wanna

05:25.890 --> 05:28.050
cross your T's and dot your I's.

05:28.050 --> 05:29.760
Why not just swipe through

05:29.760 --> 05:33.360
the other quadrant that's low risk, low effort,

05:33.360 --> 05:35.790
and most likely just end up ignoring

05:35.790 --> 05:38.310
the high effort, low risk,

05:38.310 --> 05:40.470
probably not worth focusing on it all.

05:40.470 --> 05:41.670
Now generally, you're gonna want to

05:41.670 --> 05:43.710
focus your efforts based off of

05:43.710 --> 05:46.500
this graph and always be on the lookout

05:46.500 --> 05:50.820
for low difficulty, simple, easy things

05:50.820 --> 05:52.890
that are not resource intensive ways

05:52.890 --> 05:55.860
of testing out high risk assumptions.

05:55.860 --> 05:57.743
All right, see you in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.000 --> 00:03.250
(upbeat guitar music)

00:04.200 --> 00:05.760
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:05.760 --> 00:08.670
All right, so we have a big bad list of assumptions

00:08.670 --> 00:12.060
that we've made about our proposed feature or product.

00:12.060 --> 00:13.680
And in that big bad list,

00:13.680 --> 00:16.110
we've identified the order and the priority

00:16.110 --> 00:17.730
in which we should figure out,

00:17.730 --> 00:19.500
A, is this true?

00:19.500 --> 00:22.260
Now, your goal at this point is to steadily

00:22.260 --> 00:26.010
and methodically start testing these assumptions

00:26.010 --> 00:28.110
one by one and ruling them out

00:28.110 --> 00:31.170
as potential issues that could blow up down the road.

00:31.170 --> 00:34.290
But we can't do that until we first solve

00:34.290 --> 00:36.720
a new problem we have.

00:36.720 --> 00:39.960
An assumption list is just a raw list of things

00:39.960 --> 00:42.540
we roughly think need to be true

00:42.540 --> 00:44.280
for the success of our product.

00:44.280 --> 00:46.440
These assumptions are not precise

00:46.440 --> 00:48.840
and they're not particularly actionable.

00:48.840 --> 00:52.770
If I said, go build an MVP that tests the assumption

00:52.770 --> 00:57.390
of people are satisfied parking in a garage

00:57.390 --> 00:58.747
you would probably say,

00:58.747 --> 01:00.660
"I don't know what you're talking about."

01:00.660 --> 01:03.810
We need something we can more easily work with.

01:03.810 --> 01:05.670
We need to take our assumptions,

01:05.670 --> 01:07.710
flesh them out a little bit more,

01:07.710 --> 01:11.070
and roll them together into their much more specific

01:11.070 --> 01:14.430
and easier to deal with sibling, the hypothesis.

01:14.430 --> 01:16.680
So what is a hypothesis?

01:16.680 --> 01:19.860
It's a single written, testable statement

01:19.860 --> 01:22.050
of what you believe to be true

01:22.050 --> 01:24.720
with regards to the assumption you've identified.

01:24.720 --> 01:26.640
Now, I know that's a mouthful,

01:26.640 --> 01:29.880
so let's break it down and explain it a little bit more.

01:29.880 --> 01:31.440
Let's say that we had identified

01:31.440 --> 01:34.710
that people are not satisfied with parking in a garage,

01:34.710 --> 01:38.610
as a particularly hairy assumption we were making.

01:38.610 --> 01:41.160
Now, remember, this is from that Zurich's example

01:41.160 --> 01:42.840
that we covered previously.

01:42.840 --> 01:45.000
Check that one out if you're lost.

01:45.000 --> 01:46.980
We would probably think of running some

01:46.980 --> 01:50.310
sort of test to see if that assumption was true.

01:50.310 --> 01:53.130
It could be as simple as doing street surveys

01:53.130 --> 01:55.440
of people leaving garages on a Friday night,

01:55.440 --> 01:57.360
or maybe it's an ad we run

01:57.360 --> 02:00.450
that says parking in garages suck.

02:00.450 --> 02:02.160
And we see who responds to that ad,

02:02.160 --> 02:06.180
or maybe we plan to pitch an alternative

02:06.180 --> 02:08.130
and how many people will then vote

02:08.130 --> 02:11.790
with their feet and start using the parking garages.

02:11.790 --> 02:15.480
The point here though is that it doesn't matter yet

02:15.480 --> 02:19.290
how we get our data and how we run our test.

02:19.290 --> 02:22.380
The simple fact is that we cannot construct

02:22.380 --> 02:26.280
a test around people are not satisfied with parking.

02:26.280 --> 02:28.080
We need to get more specific.

02:28.080 --> 02:31.800
We need to identify who exactly we think are unsatisfied,

02:31.800 --> 02:34.920
how unsatisfied they are, and potentially things

02:34.920 --> 02:37.680
like why we think they're unsatisfied.

02:37.680 --> 02:41.250
All of this information is crucial for you to understand.

02:41.250 --> 02:44.220
Now, there's a reason why MVPs are more correctly

02:44.220 --> 02:46.320
called MVP experiments.

02:46.320 --> 02:49.710
And yes, if you say, "Oh my God, Evan, look at my MVP,"

02:49.710 --> 02:51.870
I'm gonna correct you, because, come on.

02:51.870 --> 02:55.530
Now because we don't wanna waste any of our resources

02:55.530 --> 02:57.900
pursuing our new product or feature,

02:57.900 --> 03:00.360
we need to treat this entire process

03:00.360 --> 03:02.040
like a science experiment.

03:02.040 --> 03:04.530
And the best way to focus our efforts is on one

03:04.530 --> 03:09.530
singular hypothesis, something we can try to prove true.

03:10.020 --> 03:12.150
Hypothesis brings clarity not only to you

03:12.150 --> 03:13.680
but the rest of your team.

03:13.680 --> 03:16.290
If you skip this stage, you're on the risk

03:16.290 --> 03:18.210
of down the road for getting what exactly

03:18.210 --> 03:20.400
you're trying to do in the first place.

03:20.400 --> 03:21.690
So in the next couple lectures,

03:21.690 --> 03:24.810
we're gonna try to cover how to put these together.

03:24.810 --> 03:26.670
As a matter of strategy,

03:26.670 --> 03:28.710
it's gonna be up to you whether or not

03:28.710 --> 03:30.210
you wanna build a hypothesis

03:30.210 --> 03:32.820
for every single assumption you have.

03:32.820 --> 03:34.530
Or you wanna build a hypothesis

03:34.530 --> 03:38.100
that if proven true could rule out several assumptions

03:38.100 --> 03:43.080
killing multiple assumption birds with one MVP stone.

03:43.080 --> 03:45.063
We'll cover both, stay tuned.

WEBVTT

00:00.240 --> 00:01.800
-: Hey guys, welcome back.

00:01.800 --> 00:05.700
Let's dive into creating some hypotheses

00:05.700 --> 00:10.380
based off of the assumptions we previously listed out.

00:10.380 --> 00:13.320
Remember what goes into one of these right?

00:13.320 --> 00:15.150
No? You don't actually remember?

00:15.150 --> 00:16.590
Well, let me tell you

00:16.590 --> 00:20.610
because I only alluded to it before in the previous lecture

00:20.610 --> 00:21.720
and you probably only heard it

00:21.720 --> 00:24.150
if you were paying really close attention.

00:24.150 --> 00:27.000
Pat yourself on the back if you know what I'm about to say.

00:27.000 --> 00:28.770
The biggest difference between an assumption

00:28.770 --> 00:32.850
and a hypothesis is that a hypothesis is actionable.

00:32.850 --> 00:34.770
How exactly is it actionable?

00:34.770 --> 00:36.540
Well, because it has specifics.

00:36.540 --> 00:38.460
We define a target group.

00:38.460 --> 00:41.400
We're gonna define an expected outcome of our test

00:41.400 --> 00:43.290
and we might even dabble with details

00:43.290 --> 00:46.080
like why we expect our subjects to do something

00:46.080 --> 00:49.410
or how we plan to get them to do it.

00:49.410 --> 00:51.840
So target group, potential problem,

00:51.840 --> 00:56.250
an action and potentially even an expected outcome.

00:56.250 --> 00:58.020
So we'll try writing out some of these formulas

00:58.020 --> 00:59.850
starting with the most basic and then building

00:59.850 --> 01:02.880
up to more and more sophisticated versions.

01:02.880 --> 01:05.160
As a product manager, you're probably going to run tests

01:05.160 --> 01:07.080
for a variety of scenarios.

01:07.080 --> 01:09.090
You're thinking of launching a new product.

01:09.090 --> 01:10.500
You're thinking of adding

01:10.500 --> 01:13.980
or removing a new feature on your existing product.

01:13.980 --> 01:16.260
Or you're looking to make a significant change

01:16.260 --> 01:18.510
to the product features you already have.

01:18.510 --> 01:20.160
We'll cover three different ways

01:20.160 --> 01:23.310
and you can choose whichever one fits your needs

01:23.310 --> 01:25.380
for your exact situation.

01:25.380 --> 01:28.560
Let's start with the most basic hypothesis.

01:28.560 --> 01:30.390
It goes a little something like this.

01:30.390 --> 01:34.920
We believe blank will blank because blank.

01:34.920 --> 01:36.660
The first blank is the subject.

01:36.660 --> 01:39.390
We believe some group of people,

01:39.390 --> 01:43.140
some target group of customers out there.

01:43.140 --> 01:47.400
The second blank will is the expected action we

01:47.400 --> 01:48.300
think they're going to do.

01:48.300 --> 01:50.220
Maybe they'll buy something from you.

01:50.220 --> 01:52.290
Maybe they'll flock to some new service.

01:52.290 --> 01:54.210
Maybe they'll sign up for something.

01:54.210 --> 01:55.110
And then the because

01:55.110 --> 01:58.920
is the reason why we're predicting they do it.

01:58.920 --> 02:00.600
Maybe it's cause they wanna save money.

02:00.600 --> 02:02.970
Maybe they don't like the competition.

02:02.970 --> 02:05.280
Whatever it is we think, that's what we're gonna put there.

02:05.280 --> 02:06.464
There you go.

02:06.464 --> 02:07.297
That's a hypothesis.

02:07.297 --> 02:08.340
This one works great.

02:08.340 --> 02:12.030
If you're building a new product or you're an entrepreneur

02:12.030 --> 02:16.860
you wanna establish, let's say, women ages 40 to 45

02:16.860 --> 02:21.060
will sign up for weekly events newsletters

02:21.060 --> 02:25.410
because they would like to stay informed about their city.

02:25.410 --> 02:27.480
Yeah, I know it's nonsense, but if one

02:27.480 --> 02:31.770
of your riskiest assumptions was older women are interested

02:31.770 --> 02:35.940
in being informed about news in their city, close quotes,

02:35.940 --> 02:39.270
this would pretty much nail it for you as a hypothesis.

02:39.270 --> 02:41.460
Now let's address some concerns.

02:41.460 --> 02:45.180
Do we have to include or address a problem

02:45.180 --> 02:46.320
in our hypothesis?

02:46.320 --> 02:48.690
Well, this is a bit complicated.

02:48.690 --> 02:52.110
At its core, all products and all features stem

02:52.110 --> 02:53.220
from problems.

02:53.220 --> 02:55.620
You're gonna hear me saying this throughout the course.

02:55.620 --> 02:56.453
Whatever it is,

02:56.453 --> 02:58.890
if you think long enough about it, you can probably

02:58.890 --> 03:01.680
pinpoint some problem that it solves.

03:01.680 --> 03:03.930
The problem with Google Glass, yes,

03:03.930 --> 03:07.620
is that you have to reach for your phone, right?

03:07.620 --> 03:09.870
As opposed to reaching for my phone, I can have it up here.

03:09.870 --> 03:12.540
I know very trying times we live in

03:12.540 --> 03:14.730
but it is still a problem

03:14.730 --> 03:17.700
regardless of the severity or the degree of the problem.

03:17.700 --> 03:20.400
Whatever you think your users are going to do

03:20.400 --> 03:24.210
it's probably because you're going to solve

03:24.210 --> 03:28.050
or alleviate some problem they have in some degree.

03:28.050 --> 03:30.030
So if you wanna be more accurate

03:30.030 --> 03:34.200
in your hypothesis and do a better job, add a problem.

03:34.200 --> 03:38.040
And if you can't think of a problem, it's okay.

03:38.040 --> 03:41.160
Instead you can describe a benefit.

03:41.160 --> 03:44.790
Blank group would benefit by blank.

03:44.790 --> 03:45.840
That doesn't necessarily get

03:45.840 --> 03:48.720
at the core reason why they would benefit

03:48.720 --> 03:51.780
because it's solving a problem at its core,

03:51.780 --> 03:54.630
but sometimes that's something you really don't care about.

03:54.630 --> 03:57.090
That extra bit of learning is really helpful.

03:57.090 --> 03:59.520
But is it crucial at this stage?

03:59.520 --> 04:00.720
Not necessarily.

04:00.720 --> 04:03.270
Okay, now that you have a hypothesis

04:03.270 --> 04:05.820
you would then go out and test that.

04:05.820 --> 04:08.400
And this form is fine for this stage

04:08.400 --> 04:10.980
because at this stage you're just establishing

04:10.980 --> 04:13.470
that people are even interested at all.

04:13.470 --> 04:16.980
You are completely wading out from shore on this one

04:16.980 --> 04:21.060
and you're just looking for a heartbeat of a demand.

04:21.060 --> 04:22.050
This is what you might do

04:22.050 --> 04:24.750
if you were doing exploratory tests

04:24.750 --> 04:27.030
that entrepreneurs are so familiar with.

04:27.030 --> 04:29.340
But product managers might be managing an already

04:29.340 --> 04:32.460
established customer base that has already established

04:32.460 --> 04:34.290
that they want your product.

04:34.290 --> 04:36.300
So we need to get more specific.

04:36.300 --> 04:38.790
Product managers are often going to use MVP tests

04:38.790 --> 04:43.200
and hypotheses to make changes to their existing product

04:43.200 --> 04:45.420
in order to create a desired outcome.

04:45.420 --> 04:47.610
In this use case, we're not just tacking on

04:47.610 --> 04:52.080
more product to our product or creating a new product.

04:52.080 --> 04:54.810
We're tinkering with what we already have built.

04:54.810 --> 04:57.270
Let's try adding some criteria to accommodate

04:57.270 --> 04:58.770
for this type of scenario.

04:58.770 --> 05:01.170
All right, new style of hypotheses.

05:01.170 --> 05:06.170
If we blank, we believe blank will blank because blank.

05:08.430 --> 05:10.530
Now we have an action.

05:10.530 --> 05:14.070
If we change the color of the buy button

05:14.070 --> 05:17.490
we believe all users will buy more

05:17.490 --> 05:22.230
because they are busy and often get distracted like I do.

05:22.230 --> 05:25.620
Now, that is something we can test.

05:25.620 --> 05:28.890
But what about that word we used in there, more?

05:28.890 --> 05:31.530
Well, you could actually use that word

05:31.530 --> 05:35.970
and it's unideal, but it can actually work technically.

05:35.970 --> 05:38.250
Technically more means more.

05:38.250 --> 05:40.500
A larger number in some regards.

05:40.500 --> 05:43.470
So it either goes up or it goes down

05:43.470 --> 05:45.210
which right now is fine.

05:45.210 --> 05:46.860
In the next lectures we're gonna talk

05:46.860 --> 05:50.280
about how you define what more means

05:50.280 --> 05:53.460
and it's called a minimum criteria for success.

05:53.460 --> 05:54.510
I don't wanna spoil it for you

05:54.510 --> 05:56.850
but it's in the next couple lectures.

05:56.850 --> 05:59.940
But if we wanted to make it more specific at this point

05:59.940 --> 06:01.290
well, let's do that

06:01.290 --> 06:05.700
and let's actually try to define what buy more means.

06:05.700 --> 06:08.550
And let's just reorganize our hypothesis to

06:08.550 --> 06:11.520
avoid making a nasty runoff sentence.

06:11.520 --> 06:13.590
Now, buy more could mean a lot of different things.

06:13.590 --> 06:15.420
It could mean increased cart size

06:15.420 --> 06:16.800
if you're an e-commerce store.

06:16.800 --> 06:18.720
It could mean they buy more often.

06:18.720 --> 06:21.030
They return to purchase more often.

06:21.030 --> 06:23.880
It could also mean that they buy the same amount of items

06:23.880 --> 06:25.320
but they're more expensive.

06:25.320 --> 06:27.930
So in this hypothesis, let's actually try to address that.

06:27.930 --> 06:32.250
So let's say we believe blank has a blank

06:32.250 --> 06:34.110
because blank.

06:34.110 --> 06:39.110
If we do blank, this blank metric will be affected.

06:39.930 --> 06:41.490
I know this is kind of crazy

06:41.490 --> 06:43.230
but all you have to do is just fill

06:43.230 --> 06:45.870
in the blanks with what should go there.

06:45.870 --> 06:48.810
And now you have a very easy template to use to

06:48.810 --> 06:52.290
make a very specific and very actionable hypothesis.

06:52.290 --> 06:54.990
Okay, now in this scenario, we now have an action

06:54.990 --> 06:58.140
we can take to solve a user problem that happens

06:58.140 --> 07:02.340
to a specific group because of a reason we think.

07:02.340 --> 07:03.420
If we do this,

07:03.420 --> 07:07.050
we assume this metric will then be affected.

07:07.050 --> 07:07.890
And keep in mind

07:07.890 --> 07:10.560
this is the product management way of thinking.

07:10.560 --> 07:14.850
More specificity equals more focus.

07:14.850 --> 07:18.150
Also, product managers love metrics

07:18.150 --> 07:19.950
as you'll find out more later.

07:19.950 --> 07:20.880
So there you have it.

07:20.880 --> 07:23.010
Three different ways of running a hypothesis

07:23.010 --> 07:25.020
with varying degrees of complexity.

07:25.020 --> 07:27.780
If you've established basic demand and basic validation

07:27.780 --> 07:31.080
i.e. new products or entrepreneurs

07:31.080 --> 07:33.780
stick with the short version of the hypothesis

07:33.780 --> 07:35.220
and just keep it simple.

07:35.220 --> 07:36.540
If you're merely speculating

07:36.540 --> 07:40.410
on a way to change user behavior and you're exploring this

07:40.410 --> 07:42.240
then try the second type.

07:42.240 --> 07:45.030
But if you want to manage a product

07:45.030 --> 07:47.310
you gotta get used to using the most specific one

07:47.310 --> 07:48.690
which was the third type.

07:48.690 --> 07:51.930
All right guys, you now know how to make a hypothesis.

07:51.930 --> 07:53.550
Next lecture, we're gonna do it

07:53.550 --> 07:56.100
for our example business Zerx.

07:56.100 --> 07:56.933
See you then.

WEBVTT

00:00.180 --> 00:01.440
-: Hey guys, welcome back.

00:01.440 --> 00:04.800
So last lecture, we covered how to take your assumptions

00:04.800 --> 00:05.760
and then convert them

00:05.760 --> 00:10.760
into testable and easy to understand hypotheses.

00:10.770 --> 00:14.850
And since we've been using that company called Zirx before

00:14.850 --> 00:19.623
as an example, let's go ahead and try out making a for Zirx.

00:21.030 --> 00:23.880
Let's first go back a little bit and refresh ourselves

00:23.880 --> 00:27.570
on what some of the assumptions we made about Zirx were.

00:27.570 --> 00:31.020
Well, we were assuming a lot of different things.

00:31.020 --> 00:32.400
We were assuming that parking

00:32.400 --> 00:34.470
in cities was gonna be limited.

00:34.470 --> 00:35.303
We were assuming

00:35.303 --> 00:38.220
that people thought garages were too expensive.

00:38.220 --> 00:42.240
Maybe they also thought that garages were inconvenient

00:42.240 --> 00:43.620
and we even had assumptions that were

00:43.620 --> 00:46.770
on the supply side where we assumed

00:46.770 --> 00:49.800
that people would even want to park cars

00:49.800 --> 00:51.510
as part of our service.

00:51.510 --> 00:53.430
Now, out of all of those ones we went over

00:53.430 --> 00:55.950
we didn't cover all of them in just that second

00:55.950 --> 00:57.630
but do you remember the biggest one?

00:57.630 --> 01:00.450
The one we were concerned about the most, the riskiest one.

01:00.450 --> 01:02.610
The riskiest one was that people will be

01:02.610 --> 01:06.840
okay letting an on demand valet park their car.

01:06.840 --> 01:08.370
Well, let's try to think

01:08.370 --> 01:11.642
about how we would build a hypothesis that turns

01:11.642 --> 01:16.642
this assumption into an actionable version of itself.

01:16.920 --> 01:19.290
Well, we first need to define out the pieces

01:19.290 --> 01:22.740
of the hypothesis and draw out our handy dandy template.

01:22.740 --> 01:24.240
I'm using the shortened version

01:24.240 --> 01:26.250
because we're testing a new service

01:26.250 --> 01:28.650
so we're not really trying to tie this

01:28.650 --> 01:31.530
to things like internal metrics or say, you know

01:31.530 --> 01:34.950
expecting this behavioral change because,

01:34.950 --> 01:38.640
well we don't have any data to compare the past behavior.

01:38.640 --> 01:39.660
We're gonna keep it simple.

01:39.660 --> 01:41.370
Now, what are we to put in for the who?

01:41.370 --> 01:45.510
Well, I'm guessing it's gonna be people that have cars

01:45.510 --> 01:49.256
and are probably in the age range of 30 to 50

01:49.256 --> 01:54.256
potentially with incomes over, let's say $80,000 a year.

01:54.930 --> 01:56.807
Really, I'm just spit balling.

01:56.807 --> 02:00.540
Realistically, you would probably do some research here

02:00.540 --> 02:04.650
but this is a pretty plausible subject target.

02:04.650 --> 02:07.350
Now, what's the action we're expecting them to do?

02:07.350 --> 02:11.550
Well, it's let an on demand valet park their car.

02:11.550 --> 02:15.060
Now what's the problem we think we are solving?

02:15.060 --> 02:17.280
Remember, again, we don't have to define the problem

02:17.280 --> 02:18.450
but I think it's helpful.

02:18.450 --> 02:21.960
Well, you know, there could be a lot of different reasons

02:21.960 --> 02:25.170
and we highlighted them before in past lectures.

02:25.170 --> 02:28.410
Let's just say that it's because garages are too expensive

02:28.410 --> 02:29.490
and that's their problem.

02:29.490 --> 02:32.220
We could even add a location if we wanted to add even

02:32.220 --> 02:36.090
more specificity to this, but that's completely optional.

02:36.090 --> 02:38.730
They themselves actually launched in San Francisco

02:38.730 --> 02:41.700
so we could say San Francisco, but really it'd be

02:41.700 --> 02:44.910
any city that has bad downtown parking.

02:44.910 --> 02:45.900
Well, there you go.

02:45.900 --> 02:49.650
We've pieced together a one sentence testable hypothesis

02:49.650 --> 02:50.675
and guess what?

02:50.675 --> 02:53.700
We got to wrap into that hypothesis

02:53.700 --> 02:57.120
several other assumptions that were on our list.

02:57.120 --> 02:58.260
See how that works.

02:58.260 --> 03:02.550
If this hypothesis turns out to be true, in its entirety,

03:02.550 --> 03:06.090
then people do think garages are too expensive.

03:06.090 --> 03:07.230
That was one of our assumptions.

03:07.230 --> 03:09.330
If they end up completing a transaction where

03:09.330 --> 03:11.610
they use an MVP

03:11.610 --> 03:16.290
to book their first Zirx pickup,

03:16.290 --> 03:18.810
well then that also means that it's probably

03:18.810 --> 03:22.200
not too difficult to use the service while driving.

03:22.200 --> 03:24.840
Remember, that was also one of our assumptions.

03:24.840 --> 03:26.220
Now, realistically, we could go

03:26.220 --> 03:28.530
at this in a hundred different ways.

03:28.530 --> 03:31.561
We could build a separate hypothesis for each assumption

03:31.561 --> 03:34.920
and then consolidate them down when we got

03:34.920 --> 03:39.060
to the stage of deciding how we need to test each one.

03:39.060 --> 03:41.520
Or we could just do the biggest assumptions

03:41.520 --> 03:43.680
in one big test and come back

03:43.680 --> 03:46.530
to the later ones after we've done our first round

03:46.530 --> 03:48.990
of testing and if we think it's absolutely necessary.

03:48.990 --> 03:51.270
Like I said, many, many different ways of doing this.

03:51.270 --> 03:53.430
We, in this case, started off with an assumption

03:53.430 --> 03:56.040
that happened to be the riskiest, but it also

03:56.040 --> 03:58.500
happened to be something that's pretty low difficulty.

03:58.500 --> 04:01.064
So according to our quadrant system

04:01.064 --> 04:03.120
that is where we would start anyway.

04:03.120 --> 04:06.000
In the next lecture, we're gonna talk about how you measure

04:06.000 --> 04:08.940
whether or not your test end up being true.

04:08.940 --> 04:11.310
It's a really, really important part

04:11.310 --> 04:13.650
of this entire MVP process.

04:13.650 --> 04:14.800
See ya then.

response.title: What's a minimum criteria for success?

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.000 --> 00:02.583
(upbeat music)

00:04.320 --> 00:06.690
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:06.690 --> 00:07.530
In this lecture,

00:07.530 --> 00:10.920
we're moving on to the next part of the process

00:10.920 --> 00:12.196
of running an MVP.

00:12.196 --> 00:15.600
Okay, so we know what a hypothesis is

00:15.600 --> 00:18.720
and we know how to make one on our own, great.

00:18.720 --> 00:20.550
We now have our sights set

00:20.550 --> 00:23.880
on what exactly we're setting out to prove.

00:23.880 --> 00:26.370
But you may or may not have noticed this,

00:26.370 --> 00:29.939
but did we ever define what exactly proof`

00:29.939 --> 00:34.590
or define what exactly success in this experiment means?

00:34.590 --> 00:37.350
In one hypothesis, we said buy more,

00:37.350 --> 00:38.730
so is that it?

00:38.730 --> 00:40.590
If they buy more, we win?

00:40.590 --> 00:43.320
And the other said X metric improves.

00:43.320 --> 00:46.020
There are three different outcomes to an MVP test

00:46.020 --> 00:47.298
you need to be aware of.

00:47.298 --> 00:49.140
One is you find out

00:49.140 --> 00:52.020
that your hypothesis is categorically false

00:52.020 --> 00:55.470
and overwhelmingly not worth doing.

00:55.470 --> 00:56.430
Great, you dump it.

00:56.430 --> 00:58.530
It's not really a hard call.

00:58.530 --> 01:00.776
Two, you find out that within a wide margin,

01:00.776 --> 01:02.597
that your hypothesis is true,

01:02.597 --> 01:04.590
no question about it.

01:04.590 --> 01:06.488
And three, you're somewhere in the middle.

01:06.488 --> 01:08.399
Now, here's the rub.

01:08.399 --> 01:12.750
90% of the MVP experiments you're gonna run

01:12.750 --> 01:15.210
as a product manager or as an entrepreneur

01:15.210 --> 01:18.030
are gonna end up in situation three,

01:18.030 --> 01:19.740
somewhere in the middle.

01:19.740 --> 01:22.560
It's gonna look like some people like it and some don't.

01:22.560 --> 01:25.830
And the numbers are gonna be all over the board.

01:25.830 --> 01:27.570
It's probably gonna be a mess.

01:27.570 --> 01:31.410
So in our pursuit to see if our users buy more,

01:31.410 --> 01:33.253
what exactly do we mean?

01:33.253 --> 01:35.925
If 2% of our users buy more,

01:35.925 --> 01:39.240
or let's say our users don't even buy more

01:39.240 --> 01:41.310
but they buy twice as frequently,

01:41.310 --> 01:43.020
is that worth it to, say,

01:43.020 --> 01:45.390
build something that takes three months

01:45.390 --> 01:47.310
and four people to do it?

01:47.310 --> 01:49.440
Well, it depends on the size of your company

01:49.440 --> 01:53.310
and it depends on an internal calculation you have to make.

01:53.310 --> 01:54.719
We need to establish a concept

01:54.719 --> 01:57.810
called minimum criteria for success.

01:57.810 --> 01:59.250
We need to draw a line in the sand

01:59.250 --> 02:02.700
to definitively distinguish between worth it,

02:02.700 --> 02:04.093
all systems are a-go,

02:04.093 --> 02:05.680
and not worth it.

02:05.680 --> 02:06.840
Now, why is setting

02:06.840 --> 02:08.760
a minimum criteria for success important?

02:08.760 --> 02:11.460
Well, because if you forget to set your MCS,

02:11.460 --> 02:13.710
which is what I'm gonna start calling it from now on,

02:13.710 --> 02:15.450
your definition of success,

02:15.450 --> 02:18.965
you might end up conflating a validated hypothesis

02:18.965 --> 02:22.290
with a clear signal to proceed.

02:22.290 --> 02:23.766
They could be two different things.

02:23.766 --> 02:27.240
You can validate your hypothesis for example

02:27.240 --> 02:29.130
in a way that wouldn't make it worth it

02:29.130 --> 02:31.770
to go out and start building everything.

02:31.770 --> 02:34.710
Now, what if you just barely validate it?

02:34.710 --> 02:37.320
Well, you gotta proceed with caution,

02:37.320 --> 02:39.180
my young product manager friend.

02:39.180 --> 02:42.420
The one last thing I'll say about why we use the MCS

02:42.420 --> 02:46.230
is that it gives your experiments clarity and meaning.

02:46.230 --> 02:48.814
We aren't just trying to improve stuff,

02:48.814 --> 02:52.530
we're trying to improve stuff by X amount.

02:52.530 --> 02:53.790
So how do we figure out

02:53.790 --> 02:56.430
where our line in the sand should be?

02:56.430 --> 02:58.620
Well, creating a minimum criteria for success

02:58.620 --> 02:59.970
is relatively simple.

02:59.970 --> 03:02.020
And like the hypothesis exercise,

03:02.020 --> 03:06.000
there are roughly about a million different ways to do it.

03:06.000 --> 03:08.161
We're gonna take into consideration and outline

03:08.161 --> 03:11.280
two sections in this process.

03:11.280 --> 03:12.240
On the left, we're gonna put

03:12.240 --> 03:15.233
the cost of building your product feature

03:15.233 --> 03:17.610
or the cost of making the change

03:17.610 --> 03:20.310
to your prior existing product.

03:20.310 --> 03:21.390
And on the right,

03:21.390 --> 03:22.530
the second section,

03:22.530 --> 03:25.170
we're gonna put the expected reward.

03:25.170 --> 03:28.050
What do you expect to see in return?

03:28.050 --> 03:28.883
Now the next step

03:28.883 --> 03:31.560
is to fill both of these sections with criteria.

03:31.560 --> 03:33.240
Whatever criteria you choose,

03:33.240 --> 03:35.460
it should probably be criteria

03:35.460 --> 03:38.102
that clearly signals not only success,

03:38.102 --> 03:43.048
but that your idea is a viable option for your company.

03:43.048 --> 03:46.801
Popular ideas that are monetarily unsustainable

03:46.801 --> 03:49.620
are not generally great ideas.

03:49.620 --> 03:52.170
I know someone need to tell that to Silicon Valley.

03:52.170 --> 03:53.670
So starting in reverse order,

03:53.670 --> 03:54.753
let's start on the right

03:54.753 --> 03:59.010
with our expected reward section of this calculation.

03:59.010 --> 04:01.891
And we're gonna pick a metric that we hope will improve

04:01.891 --> 04:05.610
as a result of building our product feature

04:05.610 --> 04:06.780
or making our change.

04:06.780 --> 04:10.260
If we're for instance adding additional sharing features

04:10.260 --> 04:11.794
to a social network,

04:11.794 --> 04:15.420
then we're probably targeting engagement, right,

04:15.420 --> 04:17.850
or some other aspect we've identified

04:17.850 --> 04:21.030
as being crucial to our specific business.

04:21.030 --> 04:23.250
So that could be time spent on page,

04:23.250 --> 04:25.478
it could be actions per session,

04:25.478 --> 04:27.570
it could be number of likes,

04:27.570 --> 04:31.080
or something very literal like number of shares.

04:31.080 --> 04:33.180
Every experiment you run as a product manager

04:33.180 --> 04:35.220
is gonna be pointed at something.

04:35.220 --> 04:38.010
When we talk later in depth about metrics,

04:38.010 --> 04:39.990
we have an entire section on it,

04:39.990 --> 04:41.820
you'll realize that all metrics

04:41.820 --> 04:44.520
in some way or other are interconnected.

04:44.520 --> 04:46.800
And you as a product manager,

04:46.800 --> 04:50.130
you'll have to have your eye on a couple or a handful

04:50.130 --> 04:54.750
that you associate with product growth or product success.

04:54.750 --> 04:56.220
Whatever metric you put here

04:56.220 --> 04:59.910
needs to either be directly or indirectly related

04:59.910 --> 05:02.520
to one of those crucial metrics.

05:02.520 --> 05:06.690
Otherwise this is an entirely pointless endeavor

05:06.690 --> 05:10.372
and you're trying to inflate what we call false metrics.

05:10.372 --> 05:12.420
So put down the potential metrics

05:12.420 --> 05:15.960
you hope are going to be moved by your experiment.

05:15.960 --> 05:16.924
If you're building a new product,

05:16.924 --> 05:18.270
it'll probably be things

05:18.270 --> 05:20.700
like percentage of people signing up,

05:20.700 --> 05:21.990
conversion rate,

05:21.990 --> 05:25.286
open rate if you use email as a distribution channel,

05:25.286 --> 05:28.290
percentage of positive reactions, even.

05:28.290 --> 05:31.620
Things that indicate interest from a potential customer.

05:31.620 --> 05:33.294
Or it could be literally

05:33.294 --> 05:37.350
like how much money does each customer make us?

05:37.350 --> 05:39.060
We'll talk in one of the next lectures

05:39.060 --> 05:41.250
about pre-product scenarios specifically

05:41.250 --> 05:44.997
and how to tweak this for that type of situation.

05:44.997 --> 05:47.649
For now, we're gonna focus more on adding features

05:47.649 --> 05:51.660
and, or trying to improve user behavior.

05:51.660 --> 05:54.593
Okay, once you're done writing out on the right side,

05:54.593 --> 05:56.400
let's go to the left side.

05:56.400 --> 05:57.233
On the left side,

05:57.233 --> 05:59.640
we need to brainstorm the costs

05:59.640 --> 06:02.820
of doing what we're proposing to do.

06:02.820 --> 06:05.160
Every experiment is gonna cost something

06:05.160 --> 06:07.650
and you need to have a decent understanding

06:07.650 --> 06:08.880
of what's at stake.

06:08.880 --> 06:11.100
So let's say you're a company

06:11.100 --> 06:13.830
and you're going to add X feature.

06:13.830 --> 06:17.640
What does X feature end up costing your company?

06:17.640 --> 06:19.080
Well, in this hypothetical,

06:19.080 --> 06:22.710
it's probably going to take developers' time, your time,

06:22.710 --> 06:24.030
other people's time.

06:24.030 --> 06:27.330
It's probably going to take up some sweet hot cash.

06:27.330 --> 06:30.240
Keep in mind also that time is money,

06:30.240 --> 06:32.280
even outside of what you buy

06:32.280 --> 06:34.560
or what you contract to get this done.

06:34.560 --> 06:37.560
So we can reasonably assume no matter what

06:37.560 --> 06:40.380
that there's always gonna be some form of a dollar amount

06:40.380 --> 06:43.020
to building what it is that we're proposing.

06:43.020 --> 06:44.022
Well, let's try to brainstorm

06:44.022 --> 06:48.033
of what could we possibly have to end up buying

06:48.033 --> 06:49.560
in order to do this?

06:49.560 --> 06:54.330
Well, we probably need to promote the feature in some way.

06:54.330 --> 06:56.770
And even if we don't pay money necessarily for ads,

06:56.770 --> 07:01.350
there's also displacement of other ad inventory

07:01.350 --> 07:02.241
you could have used,

07:02.241 --> 07:04.110
or lost opportunity

07:04.110 --> 07:06.000
because you just took up an email

07:06.000 --> 07:07.841
that you could have spent on something else.

07:07.841 --> 07:08.849
There are other things

07:08.849 --> 07:10.710
you need to take into consideration as well

07:10.710 --> 07:12.150
like opportunity cost,

07:12.150 --> 07:13.550
which is a little abstract,

07:13.550 --> 07:15.900
but yes, it is a thing.

07:15.900 --> 07:17.940
If you pursue X and fail

07:17.940 --> 07:20.908
then you could have pursued Y and succeeded.

07:20.908 --> 07:24.810
You are one successful feature shorter.

07:24.810 --> 07:28.470
There are other things like pride, brand, morale,

07:28.470 --> 07:30.090
but these things are really up to you

07:30.090 --> 07:34.590
to self-identify as important to you and your company

07:34.590 --> 07:35.423
and put them down

07:35.423 --> 07:37.590
because it's not necessarily true for every single company.

07:37.590 --> 07:39.000
So think about what the effects

07:39.000 --> 07:41.250
of running the experiment are going to be.

07:41.250 --> 07:44.910
Take into consideration your organizational size as well.

07:44.910 --> 07:47.760
Large organizations, as we previously learned,

07:47.760 --> 07:50.250
are not gonna run scrappy experiments.

07:50.250 --> 07:52.830
The calculus for them is just different.

07:52.830 --> 07:55.200
They're probably gonna go with something a little meatier

07:55.200 --> 07:57.289
because they have to put their name on it.

07:57.289 --> 08:00.597
So factor that brand aspect

08:00.597 --> 08:01.650
and that aspect

08:01.650 --> 08:04.251
that they're gonna be around after this MVP experiment

08:04.251 --> 08:06.060
into your calculation.

08:06.060 --> 08:06.990
Okay, now that we've talked

08:06.990 --> 08:10.950
about how there are basically two signs to every calculation

08:10.950 --> 08:12.990
for a minimum criteria for success

08:12.990 --> 08:13.884
and we've discussed roughly

08:13.884 --> 08:18.780
what are potential metrics you could put in each section,

08:18.780 --> 08:20.040
in the next lecture,

08:20.040 --> 08:21.510
we're going to work

08:21.510 --> 08:24.570
on coming up with a minimum criteria of success

08:24.570 --> 08:28.203
that works for a theoretical position, see you then.

response.title: Creating a formula for your MCS

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.270 --> 00:02.580
-: All right, so we have both sides

00:02.580 --> 00:05.370
of the equation outlined and we've identified

00:05.370 --> 00:07.800
both the things we are trying to effect.

00:07.800 --> 00:10.770
We've also identified the things that it will cost

00:10.770 --> 00:13.710
if we actually decide to go full steam ahead

00:13.710 --> 00:17.730
and build out whatever our wildest dreams have in store.

00:17.730 --> 00:20.640
Now, you at home that are bright probably already know

00:20.640 --> 00:22.440
that whatever experiment that we run,

00:22.440 --> 00:26.520
we need the potential upside probably to outweigh the cost.

00:26.520 --> 00:30.570
And since we're setting our line in the sand of success

00:30.570 --> 00:33.450
in one specific spot, we need to identify

00:33.450 --> 00:37.320
at what point does the benefit outweigh the cost.

00:37.320 --> 00:39.240
And that's how we figure out that line.

00:39.240 --> 00:40.980
So, let's just do that.

00:40.980 --> 00:44.670
I suggest starting on the left-hand side first

00:44.670 --> 00:48.600
so we know exactly what we're spending to make this happen.

00:48.600 --> 00:50.850
The left side also is in general easier

00:50.850 --> 00:53.940
to calculate because it's based off of cold, hard facts,

00:53.940 --> 00:57.090
whereas the right side is a little more speculative.

00:57.090 --> 00:59.340
Now, for demonstration purposes, let's say we work

00:59.340 --> 01:02.850
at our favorite big, bloated bluebird, the Twitter bird.

01:02.850 --> 01:05.340
Let's say for some reason we wanted to add a feature

01:05.340 --> 01:08.610
that allowed us to hover over any link

01:08.610 --> 01:11.520
that someone shared or tweeted and see a pop-up

01:11.520 --> 01:14.430
of whatever that link was referencing.

01:14.430 --> 01:16.800
It's clunky. It's unnecessary.

01:16.800 --> 01:18.120
But hey, it's Twitter.

01:18.120 --> 01:21.240
It's not like they ever said no to a feature request.

01:21.240 --> 01:23.940
Let's try to figure out a rough cost associated

01:23.940 --> 01:26.520
if we pursued this new feature.

01:26.520 --> 01:30.090
Let's start with development time because there will have

01:30.090 --> 01:34.530
to be people programming this to make it actually happen.

01:34.530 --> 01:37.470
If you work in tech, this is frequently going

01:37.470 --> 01:39.420
to be one of the most relative things you have

01:39.420 --> 01:40.980
to take into consideration.

01:40.980 --> 01:43.830
Now, you don't need to be a developer to calculate this,

01:43.830 --> 01:47.100
but you will have to do a little bit of outside research.

01:47.100 --> 01:49.290
You might have to talk to your developers.

01:49.290 --> 01:51.060
And if you're using a contractor,

01:51.060 --> 01:52.860
well, they're generally required

01:52.860 --> 01:55.260
to give you this information upfront anyway.

01:55.260 --> 01:57.240
Let's make up some fake details.

01:57.240 --> 01:58.740
Let's say our feature edition,

01:58.740 --> 02:00.690
which is massively scaled back

02:00.690 --> 02:04.290
in its first iteration will take two developers

02:04.290 --> 02:06.030
two weeks to develop.

02:06.030 --> 02:08.100
So now, we've established what the loss

02:08.100 --> 02:09.900
of time is going to be.

02:09.900 --> 02:13.080
These developers, let's say they make $100,000 a year.

02:13.080 --> 02:14.880
They probably make more, to be honest,

02:14.880 --> 02:17.340
but we'll say $100,000, make it simple.

02:17.340 --> 02:22.340
So, that means roughly rounded down to $8,000 a month,

02:22.470 --> 02:25.650
two weeks inside of a month, 4K for each of them.

02:25.650 --> 02:30.210
You're spending $8,000 to try this, just in labor costs.

02:30.210 --> 02:33.600
It's also gonna take your time and other people's time.

02:33.600 --> 02:37.620
So, let's just for giggles assume it takes part-time effort

02:37.620 --> 02:40.260
for you for two weeks, as well.

02:40.260 --> 02:43.260
So, let's add in another $2,000.

02:43.260 --> 02:47.100
You generally will get paid roughly the same as developers.

02:47.100 --> 02:50.730
That makes the grand total of labor time

02:50.730 --> 02:55.320
at $10,000 just to try out our pop-up.

02:55.320 --> 02:57.930
Now, let's say that you're gonna buy some ads

02:57.930 --> 03:00.570
to announce the launch of this feature

03:00.570 --> 03:04.350
and also to drive traffic to test it in its first version.

03:04.350 --> 03:07.980
So, maybe add in an extra $3,000.

03:07.980 --> 03:11.760
That means our total cost could theoretically be $13,000.

03:11.760 --> 03:15.480
That is a finite number that we can now work with.

03:15.480 --> 03:20.220
Fantastic, now we have a cost baseline, really helpful.

03:20.220 --> 03:22.650
Now, does this number have to be exact?

03:22.650 --> 03:26.490
Well, no, not necessarily, but you should be in a position

03:26.490 --> 03:31.050
to gather information you need to make a decent estimate.

03:31.050 --> 03:32.640
If you're using too fuzzy of math,

03:32.640 --> 03:34.830
that probably just means that you were lazy.

03:34.830 --> 03:37.290
At the same time, don't try to spend your entire life trying

03:37.290 --> 03:40.290
to calculate this down to the penny because

03:40.290 --> 03:43.680
if it's worth it, it's going to obviously be worth it.

03:43.680 --> 03:47.310
It's not gonna be something where you only make it worth it

03:47.310 --> 03:50.610
by $10 or $5 or some thin margin.

03:50.610 --> 03:52.740
Now that we know the theoretical cost,

03:52.740 --> 03:56.190
let's think what else could be built in that time.

03:56.190 --> 03:58.080
And that brings us to the other thing

03:58.080 --> 04:00.960
that it's gonnna cost us, opportunity costs.

04:00.960 --> 04:02.820
Opportunity costs, specifically in this case,

04:02.820 --> 04:07.230
is probably small because, hey, it's not a huge feature.

04:07.230 --> 04:09.660
But that still is time those developers

04:09.660 --> 04:14.660
could have spent fixing bugs or building something else,

04:14.820 --> 04:16.860
or even time spent collaborating

04:16.860 --> 04:19.530
on a newer, much larger feature.

04:19.530 --> 04:22.200
Regardless, we now have a cost baseline.

04:22.200 --> 04:24.480
We've satisfied the requirements

04:24.480 --> 04:27.270
for the left side of our calculation.

04:27.270 --> 04:29.070
From the looks of it, depending on how large

04:29.070 --> 04:32.640
your organization is, this is probably not a huge risk.

04:32.640 --> 04:36.570
So, we probably have some flexibility on the right side,

04:36.570 --> 04:39.030
and we start talking about what we need in return.

04:39.030 --> 04:40.650
Let's now try to think critically

04:40.650 --> 04:43.560
about what metric has to move what amount

04:43.560 --> 04:46.830
to justify the cost we've just established.

04:46.830 --> 04:49.200
Now, big risks need big rewards

04:49.200 --> 04:52.710
and small risks are okay with small rewards.

04:52.710 --> 04:54.240
That's just generally how this works.

04:54.240 --> 04:57.360
So, if we're pulling two guys off the development team

04:57.360 --> 05:01.050
and taking 50% of our time, plus some ad money,

05:01.050 --> 05:03.420
and the fact that we could be building something else,

05:03.420 --> 05:05.730
what's it gonna make to make this worth it?

05:05.730 --> 05:09.150
This is generally just up to you and your team.

05:09.150 --> 05:13.500
You will know what's a big deal and what's not.

05:13.500 --> 05:16.200
Now, outside of that internal decision you guys make,

05:16.200 --> 05:18.540
the best way and the best practice for going

05:18.540 --> 05:23.490
about this is to just settle on one specific metric.

05:23.490 --> 05:26.970
Two metrics becomes too confusing and it makes it hard

05:26.970 --> 05:29.640
to really verify success, so just pick one.

05:29.640 --> 05:32.310
Second, try to think past these metrics

05:32.310 --> 05:34.590
and what exactly they mean.

05:34.590 --> 05:38.160
If you have an app that makes money off ads, let's say,

05:38.160 --> 05:40.680
well then, increasing the time a user stays

05:40.680 --> 05:44.430
on your site will increase revenue, that's important.

05:44.430 --> 05:48.690
If you're a startup and you increase user growth by 15%,

05:48.690 --> 05:52.620
could that then affect your ability to raise money?

05:52.620 --> 05:56.850
Think through what your organization needs as a whole,

05:56.850 --> 05:59.790
and if you can't think of anything with regards

05:59.790 --> 06:01.770
to business requirements, well,

06:01.770 --> 06:03.570
that's a great sign that you're out of touch

06:03.570 --> 06:06.990
and you probably need a refresher on your company's vision.

06:06.990 --> 06:09.750
Let's try some scenarios for the reward side

06:09.750 --> 06:10.920
of our calculation.

06:10.920 --> 06:13.170
Maybe we calculate a money effect,

06:13.170 --> 06:15.720
a direct effect on revenue.

06:15.720 --> 06:18.450
Let's say we're thinking that the addition of this feature,

06:18.450 --> 06:22.410
although limited, will in our task group raise conversion

06:22.410 --> 06:24.030
to a paid program, let's say it's

06:24.030 --> 06:27.540
from free program to paid, by some amount.

06:27.540 --> 06:30.870
That's easy because then we just have to figure out

06:30.870 --> 06:34.110
what is more worth it, $13,000

06:34.110 --> 06:37.590
or that increase which then gives us more money.

06:37.590 --> 06:38.730
Which number's larger?

06:38.730 --> 06:42.390
Or maybe you can say, "Well, we have a year-long goal

06:42.390 --> 06:46.350
of raising our average minutes consumed by 40%.

06:46.350 --> 06:50.280
To keep us on that 40% path, we need to see an improvement

06:50.280 --> 06:54.960
and at least 4% since that's roughly the equivalent

06:54.960 --> 06:57.960
of a month that it's gonna take us a month worth

06:57.960 --> 07:00.990
of time labor-wise to develop this."

07:00.990 --> 07:03.930
Now, I said a month because opportunity costs.

07:03.930 --> 07:05.700
It's two weeks working on this feature,

07:05.700 --> 07:06.930
but if it doesn't work out,

07:06.930 --> 07:09.540
you could've spent two weeks on a different one.

07:09.540 --> 07:12.420
So, it's kind of a double jeopardy situation.

07:12.420 --> 07:13.890
The point is, try to align this

07:13.890 --> 07:16.350
as some bigger goal if you can.

07:16.350 --> 07:19.710
Now, guys, be aware, this is napkin math

07:19.710 --> 07:21.450
when you're a product manager.

07:21.450 --> 07:25.620
You're not always going to have the actual numbers available

07:25.620 --> 07:27.180
most times when you do this.

07:27.180 --> 07:30.990
Sometimes, you're going to be adding numbers to potatoes,

07:30.990 --> 07:33.150
then squaring them by smiles,

07:33.150 --> 07:35.430
and then dividing them by green.

07:35.430 --> 07:39.000
It can oftentimes be more abstraction than accounting.

07:39.000 --> 07:40.350
At the end of this calculation,

07:40.350 --> 07:42.810
you should know what metric you want to improve.

07:42.810 --> 07:45.510
How big of a cost is it going to take,

07:45.510 --> 07:49.560
and then in your head make a basic ---for-tat statement

07:49.560 --> 07:52.170
that says, if it's gonna cost X,

07:52.170 --> 07:56.550
we need to see Y improvement, or Y metrics

07:56.550 --> 08:00.360
to justify giving the thumbs-up and going ahead with this.

08:00.360 --> 08:02.850
All right, so you now know how to think about

08:02.850 --> 08:06.300
and put together a minimum criteria for success.

08:06.300 --> 08:08.580
The next lecture, we're gonna talk specifically

08:08.580 --> 08:12.180
about pre-product scenarios and startups.

08:12.180 --> 08:14.520
How do you do it if you don't

08:14.520 --> 08:17.520
already have internal established metrics?

08:17.520 --> 08:18.353
See you then.

response.title: OPTIONAL: Making the calculation for startups

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.210 --> 00:01.043
-: Hey guys.

00:01.043 --> 00:02.130
Welcome back to the course.

00:02.130 --> 00:04.860
Let's talk a little bit about startups

00:04.860 --> 00:09.720
or pre-product situations and how this process defers

00:09.720 --> 00:13.320
for calculating your minimum criteria for success.

00:13.320 --> 00:16.020
Now, when you're running the show with an existing product,

00:16.020 --> 00:19.287
an existing user base, and potentially existing revenue

00:19.287 --> 00:22.860
you're going to be, one, more concerned

00:22.860 --> 00:25.907
with certain metrics and, two, less sensitive

00:25.907 --> 00:30.510
to certain types of risks and certain types of costs.

00:30.510 --> 00:33.150
PM's at a big company generally care

00:33.150 --> 00:37.680
about metrics that are just not of concern to entrepreneurs

00:37.680 --> 00:39.810
or people who are trying to figure out whether

00:39.810 --> 00:42.600
or not their product should exist in the first place.

00:42.600 --> 00:45.810
Average minutes consumed is not very important

00:45.810 --> 00:47.880
if you have zero customers

00:47.880 --> 00:50.970
and you're only getting 1% of your people to even sign

00:50.970 --> 00:53.610
up for your service, two different worlds.

00:53.610 --> 00:56.160
Specifically in the case of a startup

00:56.160 --> 00:59.820
you're looking for what we call validation metrics.

00:59.820 --> 01:01.560
Is my product a good idea?

01:01.560 --> 01:03.690
Does it meet a user need?

01:03.690 --> 01:07.200
Does my identified problem really exist?

01:07.200 --> 01:09.330
What exactly is a validation metric?

01:09.330 --> 01:12.205
Well, it's something that demonstrates real interest

01:12.205 --> 01:14.970
from your potential customers:

01:14.970 --> 01:16.320
percentage of people that sign up,

01:16.320 --> 01:18.450
percentage of people that share your post,

01:18.450 --> 01:20.040
average purchase price,

01:20.040 --> 01:21.900
how many people open your email?

01:21.900 --> 01:22.904
Those are examples.

01:22.904 --> 01:24.391
You're also more concerned

01:24.391 --> 01:28.560
with the economic viability of the project as a whole.

01:28.560 --> 01:31.890
It's hard to justify how much money exactly

01:31.890 --> 01:36.720
a feature will make, but a product is relatively easy.

01:36.720 --> 01:38.730
So we're probably going to look

01:38.730 --> 01:43.110
at things like lifetime value and things like gross margins.

01:43.110 --> 01:44.760
So the biggest difference when you're

01:44.760 --> 01:45.960
doing this pre-product

01:45.960 --> 01:50.790
it's really going to be the right side of our equation.

01:50.790 --> 01:53.452
Rewards are just gonna be different for you.

01:53.452 --> 01:54.870
You're gonna be looking

01:54.870 --> 01:58.290
for specific things that signal interest

01:58.290 --> 02:01.957
and not necessarily some behavioral change you expected

02:01.957 --> 02:02.820
to happen.

02:02.820 --> 02:06.600
You're also not gonna be as concerned with opportunity costs

02:06.600 --> 02:10.860
because if it fails and you flop, you're probably looking

02:10.860 --> 02:12.000
for a new job.

02:12.000 --> 02:15.450
So make sure that you understand the nuance between the two,

02:15.450 --> 02:17.670
because as a product manager, you might find yourself

02:17.670 --> 02:19.500
in both situations.

02:19.500 --> 02:21.150
All right, so at this point in the process

02:21.150 --> 02:22.320
we know what we wanna build.

02:22.320 --> 02:25.770
Ideally, it's whether it's a product or a feature

02:25.770 --> 02:29.610
or a big change to the product you already have.

02:29.610 --> 02:32.790
We know what we need to test assumption wise

02:32.790 --> 02:37.260
and we know what outcome we want to get out of these tests.

02:37.260 --> 02:40.314
All right, now let's shift our focus to the ways

02:40.314 --> 02:43.050
and the tools that are available to us

02:43.050 --> 02:45.960
to get the actual testing job done.

02:45.960 --> 02:49.560
When you run MVP experiments, you're always going to present

02:49.560 --> 02:51.430
in some way, shape, or form

02:51.430 --> 02:54.000
something that implies your new feature

02:54.000 --> 02:58.590
or product is already real and or coming very soon.

02:58.590 --> 02:59.674
This, obviously is not the case

02:59.674 --> 03:02.578
because internally we actually haven't decided yet

03:02.578 --> 03:04.380
whether or not we wanna build it.

03:04.380 --> 03:08.550
Now, the extent to which you need to fake this

03:08.550 --> 03:10.710
in order to get the information you need is

03:10.710 --> 03:14.760
what's going to determine what type of MVP you need.

03:14.760 --> 03:18.243
So let's list them out and briefly discuss them.

WEBVTT

00:00.150 --> 00:00.983
-: Hey guys.

00:00.983 --> 00:01.860
Welcome back to the course.

00:01.860 --> 00:04.620
All right, so at this stage in the game

00:04.620 --> 00:06.780
we know what we want to build ideally

00:06.780 --> 00:08.730
whether it's a product, feature,

00:08.730 --> 00:12.000
or a large change to the product we already have.

00:12.000 --> 00:13.290
We also know what types

00:13.290 --> 00:16.620
of assumptions we need to test to get to the truth,

00:16.620 --> 00:19.260
and we also know roughly what outcome

00:19.260 --> 00:22.860
we want to see out of these MVP tests.

00:22.860 --> 00:25.410
Let's now take a slight turn and start

00:25.410 --> 00:28.740
talking about the ways and the tools

00:28.740 --> 00:32.670
that are available to us to get this testing job done.

00:32.670 --> 00:34.740
First off, when you run MVP experiments,

00:34.740 --> 00:38.190
you're always going to present in some way, shape, or form,

00:38.190 --> 00:41.100
something that implies your new feature or product

00:41.100 --> 00:45.720
is already real and/or coming very soon.

00:45.720 --> 00:47.430
The reason why we do this, if you remember

00:47.430 --> 00:49.470
is because we need validated learning.

00:49.470 --> 00:52.830
We need our potential customers, or users,

00:52.830 --> 00:57.360
to be in real live situations where they don't know

00:57.360 --> 01:01.020
they're being tested to see how they actually behave.

01:01.020 --> 01:02.880
That is the data we crave,

01:02.880 --> 01:04.950
and that is the data that we have to rely on.

01:04.950 --> 01:06.030
Now, it's obviously not the case that

01:06.030 --> 01:09.630
you have built what you are saying you have

01:09.630 --> 01:12.660
because internally we haven't yet decided that.

01:12.660 --> 01:15.660
Now, the extent to which you need to fake it

01:15.660 --> 01:17.640
in order to get the information you need is

01:17.640 --> 01:21.660
what's going to determine what type of MVP you need.

01:21.660 --> 01:25.020
So let's list them out and briefly discuss them.

01:25.020 --> 01:26.790
In the following lectures,

01:26.790 --> 01:29.280
we'll cover each one a little bit more in depth,

01:29.280 --> 01:32.280
talk about pros and cons and when to use them,

01:32.280 --> 01:35.700
and in the spirit of faking it, let's start,

01:35.700 --> 01:39.420
and let's start with the ones that are the most fake,

01:39.420 --> 01:40.950
and we'll get towards the end where

01:40.950 --> 01:44.370
we talk about the ones that require less fakery.

01:44.370 --> 01:46.200
All right, so the first MVP technique

01:46.200 --> 01:49.080
is what we call the email MVP.

01:49.080 --> 01:51.150
It's the simplest one out there.

01:51.150 --> 01:55.620
All you need to run an email MVP is an email client,

01:55.620 --> 01:57.750
some of your email list, user base,

01:57.750 --> 02:01.890
and maybe some basic wordsmithing skills.

02:01.890 --> 02:04.980
As an established company, you already have

02:04.980 --> 02:07.700
a list of users, and most likely

02:07.700 --> 02:10.830
you communicate with them on a frequent basis.

02:10.830 --> 02:12.930
I can't really think of any company

02:12.930 --> 02:17.930
that doesn't use email marketing in and at least some way.

02:18.030 --> 02:19.860
So your users are already used to

02:19.860 --> 02:22.320
buying things, or signing up for things,

02:22.320 --> 02:26.100
or engaging with the content and the emails you send them.

02:26.100 --> 02:29.940
They're already ready to have a test run on them.

02:29.940 --> 02:32.940
By taking a small segment of your user population

02:32.940 --> 02:37.110
and emailing them a pitch for a new feature or new product,

02:37.110 --> 02:40.597
you can see exactly how they'll react in a simulated,

02:40.597 --> 02:43.200
"This is real." quote unquote scenario.

02:43.200 --> 02:45.990
Whether or not this is a good idea as a PM

02:45.990 --> 02:48.420
is a different question, and we'll cover that

02:48.420 --> 02:51.660
in the lectures where we go more into depth,

02:51.660 --> 02:54.150
but right now we're just covering what they are.

02:54.150 --> 02:56.430
Product managers and our pre-product,

02:56.430 --> 02:58.890
also known as early founders,

02:58.890 --> 03:01.620
can use this one quite effectively.

03:01.620 --> 03:03.900
Even if you don't have an email list already,

03:03.900 --> 03:07.440
you can gather a list of emails from potential customers

03:07.440 --> 03:10.440
i.e. people that fit your target demographics,

03:10.440 --> 03:13.110
or your target customer segmentation,

03:13.110 --> 03:16.200
and send this out in much of the same way.

03:16.200 --> 03:20.130
You'll probably take a much more personal tone

03:20.130 --> 03:23.190
to avoid getting flagged as spam,

03:23.190 --> 03:26.520
but it's a very viable way of testing,

03:26.520 --> 03:30.630
and it is literally the simplest way of doing this.

03:30.630 --> 03:32.280
If anyone's familiar with AppSumo,

03:32.280 --> 03:36.690
they sell new products all the time in this way.

03:36.690 --> 03:40.050
In fact, they're kind of like a daily deals website,

03:40.050 --> 03:42.690
but for marketing and entrepreneurial tools.

03:42.690 --> 03:45.810
They routinely test their new products

03:45.810 --> 03:50.810
by sending out email MVPs before they acquire or build them.

03:50.820 --> 03:53.400
They generally just say, "Hey, this is real.

03:53.400 --> 03:56.790
Come sign up." and if they're even more brazen,

03:56.790 --> 04:00.210
and I've seen this, they'll put an order button in the email

04:00.210 --> 04:03.960
and see if people actually buy right out of the email.

04:03.960 --> 04:05.910
All right, so that's an email MVP.

04:05.910 --> 04:07.320
The next one we're gonna talk about

04:07.320 --> 04:10.140
is called the shadow button MVP,

04:10.140 --> 04:14.430
and this is one that's very specific to product managers.

04:14.430 --> 04:16.320
A shadow button MVP generally requires

04:16.320 --> 04:19.740
more resources to do than an email MVP,

04:19.740 --> 04:23.460
but it's still on the whole very easy to pull off.

04:23.460 --> 04:26.460
Now, what companies will do instead of building

04:26.460 --> 04:28.740
out a new feature is they'll put a button

04:28.740 --> 04:31.170
within their already made product

04:31.170 --> 04:35.130
that supposedly links the person to this new feature.

04:35.130 --> 04:36.750
When the person clicks that button,

04:36.750 --> 04:38.790
it registers that they did it,

04:38.790 --> 04:42.180
and either one does nothing and just looks broken

04:42.180 --> 04:46.680
or two shows texts saying, "Sorry, this is coming soon.".

04:46.680 --> 04:48.900
That is in essence a shadow button.

04:48.900 --> 04:51.810
Again, people don't know if it's real,

04:51.810 --> 04:54.360
so when we observe their behavior in this scenario,

04:54.360 --> 04:56.730
we can generally consider it valid.

04:56.730 --> 04:58.680
People act entirely differently when they

04:58.680 --> 05:00.420
think something is real versus

05:00.420 --> 05:03.270
if they don't have any reason to think it's not real.

05:03.270 --> 05:05.250
You'll hear this over and over again.

05:05.250 --> 05:07.260
Oftentimes you'll see shadow buttons be used

05:07.260 --> 05:09.450
in midsize startups that are really trying

05:09.450 --> 05:13.080
to triage their nice to have features.

05:13.080 --> 05:16.020
Which login would you offer at first?

05:16.020 --> 05:17.520
Let's say you're a startup deciding

05:17.520 --> 05:21.630
between email login, Facebook login, Twitter, Google

05:21.630 --> 05:24.540
and let's say LinkedIn login.

05:24.540 --> 05:27.240
Well, instead of implementing all of those,

05:27.240 --> 05:30.570
which is a decently large task,

05:30.570 --> 05:32.610
you just put fake buttons for them

05:32.610 --> 05:35.880
and record if people actually try to use them.

05:35.880 --> 05:36.960
If they don't use them,

05:36.960 --> 05:40.200
then you know it's not worth putting in just yet.

05:40.200 --> 05:42.870
Now is the shadow button MVP a good idea?

05:42.870 --> 05:45.000
Anytime I say this, Cole just cringes.

05:45.000 --> 05:48.540
Again, we'll talk about that in another lecture.

05:48.540 --> 05:51.780
All MVPs depend on your organization,

05:51.780 --> 05:54.480
your size and some other factors we'll discuss.

05:54.480 --> 05:58.050
The next two types of MVPs I've actually lumped together.

05:58.050 --> 06:00.297
There's one, it's called the 404 page,

06:00.297 --> 06:03.390
and the two which is called the Coming Soon page.

06:03.390 --> 06:05.250
They're really similar to each other,

06:05.250 --> 06:07.020
but they're just slight variations,

06:07.020 --> 06:08.850
and that's why we'll discuss them together.

06:08.850 --> 06:11.700
In this MVP tactic, what you do is again

06:11.700 --> 06:14.610
act like you're adding a new feature or a product,

06:14.610 --> 06:18.720
and when the user navigates to the page that is designated

06:18.720 --> 06:22.050
as the new feature, or product, or section of your site,

06:22.050 --> 06:24.454
it either displays a 404 message,

06:24.454 --> 06:27.390
404 meaning server cannot find this page,

06:27.390 --> 06:29.970
you'll find us any time a server's not working,

06:29.970 --> 06:32.640
or you click to a a page that's old

06:32.640 --> 06:35.827
and it's no longer there, or a page that says,

06:35.827 --> 06:38.460
"Thank you for your interest. The product is coming soon,"

06:38.460 --> 06:40.590
and then often we'll ask them to

06:40.590 --> 06:43.410
sign up or display interest some way.

06:43.410 --> 06:44.467
Now, you might be thinking to yourself,

06:44.467 --> 06:46.890
"Hey this is way too scrappy.

06:46.890 --> 06:50.190
My big company in our beautiful brand and blah

06:50.190 --> 06:51.270
blah, blah, blah, blah.

06:51.270 --> 06:53.400
We can't use this possibly."

06:53.400 --> 06:54.780
Well, guess what?

06:54.780 --> 06:57.960
Guess who uses this technique every single day

06:57.960 --> 07:00.450
to test demand for new products?

07:00.450 --> 07:01.320
Amazon.

07:01.320 --> 07:05.429
Amazon.com is the king of using the 404 page

07:05.429 --> 07:08.700
or the coming soon signup page.

07:08.700 --> 07:10.770
Amazon uses this extensively to see

07:10.770 --> 07:13.050
if their existing user base is interested

07:13.050 --> 07:17.190
in a new side project or even a new category of goods.

07:17.190 --> 07:18.840
They track the interest against

07:18.840 --> 07:21.360
their criteria of success and there you go.

07:21.360 --> 07:23.790
That's how they know where to put their resources

07:23.790 --> 07:26.940
and where to expand the Amazon empire.

07:26.940 --> 07:27.930
It also works great for them

07:27.930 --> 07:30.990
because they have so many random things

07:30.990 --> 07:33.810
attached to their brand that have nothing to do

07:33.810 --> 07:37.740
with the core value proposition, so it kind of makes sense.

07:37.740 --> 07:39.570
Oculus Rift, if you guys ever seen

07:39.570 --> 07:41.970
that virtual reality goggle system,

07:41.970 --> 07:44.040
we have an activity on it actually,

07:44.040 --> 07:45.450
is another great example of a company

07:45.450 --> 07:47.490
that launched in a very similar way.

07:47.490 --> 07:49.950
They just put up a pre-order page.

07:49.950 --> 07:51.810
I'm sure you've seen companies have launched

07:51.810 --> 07:53.850
with Kickstarter pages.

07:53.850 --> 07:54.930
They did the same thing.

07:54.930 --> 07:57.120
It's the same thing as a coming soon page.

07:57.120 --> 08:00.120
We're coming soon, sign up or pre-order,

08:00.120 --> 08:02.490
or get your foot in the door by paying us money.

08:02.490 --> 08:05.610
The difference with Oculus Rift was that when they did that,

08:05.610 --> 08:09.450
all they had was a very rough prototype,

08:09.450 --> 08:12.840
