and I don't honestly know if it even worked,

08:12.840 --> 08:15.630
and the only other thing was a a way to pay in advance,

08:15.630 --> 08:18.600
so that pretty much is an MVP.

08:18.600 --> 08:19.433
Whew.

08:19.433 --> 08:20.790
All right, guys, let's take a break.

08:20.790 --> 08:22.530
Now that we've learned three of them,

08:22.530 --> 08:24.300
try to digest that one.

08:24.300 --> 08:25.650
Don't worry if you feel

08:25.650 --> 08:27.300
like you're a little bit lost on some of these,

08:27.300 --> 08:30.300
we're gonna cover them in more depth later.

08:30.300 --> 08:33.183
Feel free to pick which ones you want to dive into.

08:34.350 --> 08:35.190
Take a break.

08:35.190 --> 08:38.220
We'll start with the next batch in the next lecture.

08:38.220 --> 08:39.053
See you then.

response.title: More MVP techniques: walk me throughs & pitch experiments

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.000 --> 00:00.833
-: Hey guys.

00:00.833 --> 00:02.340
Welcome back to the course.

00:02.340 --> 00:04.080
Let's dive right back in

00:04.080 --> 00:07.380
and start talking about more MVP techniques.

00:07.380 --> 00:08.400
Now we're gonna start talking

00:08.400 --> 00:12.150
about ones that require more resource investment

00:12.150 --> 00:16.080
look a little more real, less scrappy

00:16.080 --> 00:18.480
but still pretty scrappy.

00:18.480 --> 00:19.470
All right, so the next one we're gonna talk

00:19.470 --> 00:22.260
about is called the Explainer MVP.

00:22.260 --> 00:24.030
This is a little bit harder to do

00:24.030 --> 00:27.120
because it requires some resources to make.

00:27.120 --> 00:29.340
Explainer, if you don't know what I'm talking about

00:29.340 --> 00:33.000
or those videos that explain what an app does

00:33.000 --> 00:36.360
or what a new feature or feature set will do

00:36.360 --> 00:37.650
they come in two different types.

00:37.650 --> 00:40.440
You have tutorial style explainer

00:40.440 --> 00:43.200
and sales styled explainer.

00:43.200 --> 00:45.720
In a tutorial style, you'll see someone manually

00:45.720 --> 00:48.420
explaining over a screen cast, much in the same

00:48.420 --> 00:51.320
way that companies use Tutorial Screen Casts to

00:51.320 --> 00:54.300
explain how to do certain tasks in their product.

00:54.300 --> 00:57.270
In this version, someone will pretend to use a new feature

00:57.270 --> 01:01.590
or product and explain what it does and why they made it

01:01.590 --> 01:04.260
but they haven't actually built it yet.

01:04.260 --> 01:06.240
It's all smoke and mirrors

01:06.240 --> 01:10.050
and actually the way it looks real is in editing.

01:10.050 --> 01:10.883
In editing

01:10.883 --> 01:14.730
you create this illusion that you have a working product

01:14.730 --> 01:18.060
by animating certain things and having them, for instance

01:18.060 --> 01:23.060
navigate from one static page to another static page.

01:23.250 --> 01:24.690
They're not connected, they don't work

01:24.690 --> 01:27.540
but you'll just cut the video, so it looks like they do.

01:27.540 --> 01:29.400
Sounds a little nutty.

01:29.400 --> 01:30.294
Yeah, it does.

01:30.294 --> 01:34.260
But this is actually a fairly common way

01:34.260 --> 01:37.350
of re-validating before you build.

01:37.350 --> 01:40.590
True story, this is actually how Dropbox started.

01:40.590 --> 01:43.350
Dropbox, not familiar multi-billion dollar

01:43.350 --> 01:45.180
company that lets you store anything

01:45.180 --> 01:48.870
in their data servers through a desktop and browser client.

01:48.870 --> 01:51.270
When the founder first started Dropbox

01:51.270 --> 01:52.320
he wanted to test to see

01:52.320 --> 01:57.240
if people wanted this idea, this cloud storage idea

01:57.240 --> 02:00.420
where you could sync your files very seamlessly

02:00.420 --> 02:03.060
to servers somewhere else

02:03.060 --> 02:05.070
and have access to your files anywhere you go.

02:05.070 --> 02:07.260
So what he did is he just made a fake video

02:07.260 --> 02:10.830
of him using his product and put it on YouTube.

02:10.830 --> 02:15.090
The response was huge, so he knew it was worth building.

02:15.090 --> 02:18.300
The other type of explainer is the Sales related explainer

02:18.300 --> 02:22.140
and this is just more when you make more like a fake promo

02:22.140 --> 02:25.440
video that pitches the product or the feature using

02:25.440 --> 02:26.273
Who knows?

02:26.273 --> 02:29.010
It could be one of those, you know, whiteboard videos

02:29.010 --> 02:32.220
with a super fake looking hand that comes up in the middle

02:32.220 --> 02:35.520
and right, or you could use an animated one or even

02:35.520 --> 02:38.790
a talking head video like this as your promo.

02:38.790 --> 02:41.310
A decent example I actually saw the other day was a company

02:41.310 --> 02:45.210
called Framework that makes a collaborative web app

02:45.210 --> 02:46.920
for video editors and producers.

02:46.920 --> 02:49.560
We actually use it to make our courses.

02:49.560 --> 02:52.230
They made a video pitching a new set of features

02:52.230 --> 02:55.050
and added all sorts of crazy animations

02:55.050 --> 02:58.710
and cg, but actually those features had not

02:58.710 --> 03:00.720
been added yet at all.

03:00.720 --> 03:03.840
-: They just said it was launching very soon

03:03.840 --> 03:06.090
and to sign up for a notification.

03:06.090 --> 03:09.660
Presumably, if not enough people signed up, then who knows?

03:09.660 --> 03:11.460
Maybe they just wouldn't launch the feature

03:11.460 --> 03:13.410
and maybe it just disappear.

03:13.410 --> 03:15.720
Either way, no one is the wiser.

03:15.720 --> 03:17.460
The point is that this MVP works

03:17.460 --> 03:21.720
because real products generally will have promotion videos

03:21.720 --> 03:24.420
of some kind, and real features will often

03:24.420 --> 03:28.470
have tutorial videos showing how to use them.

03:28.470 --> 03:30.660
So you just kind of slip

03:30.660 --> 03:32.820
in and explainer video and there you go.

03:32.820 --> 03:34.050
That's your validation.

03:34.050 --> 03:36.150
The next MVP we're gonna discuss is called the

03:36.150 --> 03:39.510
Fake Landing Page/Pitch Experiment MVP.

03:39.510 --> 03:43.350
This is when a business will create one singular

03:43.350 --> 03:46.950
page that pitches all of the benefits of a new product

03:46.950 --> 03:50.430
and then has a call to action somewhere on the page.

03:50.430 --> 03:51.870
So you might not know what some

03:51.870 --> 03:53.490
of these terms are that I just used.

03:53.490 --> 03:55.620
Let's break it down just a little bit.

03:55.620 --> 03:57.480
Landing pages are a term used

03:57.480 --> 04:00.570
for webpages that are just one page.

04:00.570 --> 04:04.290
They're a marketing tool so that companies can run ads

04:04.290 --> 04:06.330
and have a very controlled experience.

04:06.330 --> 04:09.254
When people move from that ad to that site.

04:09.254 --> 04:12.960
It doesn't generally make sense to have an ad for, let's say

04:12.960 --> 04:17.460
an E-commerce store that just added the new iPhone SE.

04:17.460 --> 04:18.480
And when people click

04:18.480 --> 04:20.730
on the ad that says, We have the iPhone

04:20.730 --> 04:23.910
they go to the homepage of your E-commerce store.

04:23.910 --> 04:27.480
Where nowhere on the page are you talking about the iPhone.

04:27.480 --> 04:30.480
The person generally will get confused

04:30.480 --> 04:34.200
and will probably leave, so that's one of the primary uses

04:34.200 --> 04:38.130
of landing pages, although they are pretty versatile

04:38.130 --> 04:39.960
and essentially, if you want your users to

04:39.960 --> 04:44.280
specifically do something like sign up or pay or fill

04:44.280 --> 04:47.190
out a form, you're probably gonna use a landing page.

04:47.190 --> 04:50.040
Now, the other term I used was pitch experiment.

04:50.040 --> 04:52.590
Now, this is just a nifty little term worth knowing

04:52.590 --> 04:56.430
although you might not ever actually use it in conversation.

04:56.430 --> 04:59.280
It's a term from the Lean Startup Framework.

04:59.280 --> 05:02.610
A pitch experiment is basically when you have

05:02.610 --> 05:06.960
an online experiment where you pitch for a new product

05:06.960 --> 05:10.290
or a new feature to an online audience

05:10.290 --> 05:12.420
and you see what they do.

05:12.420 --> 05:16.561
Landing pages are a natural fit for this because they're one

05:16.561 --> 05:21.561
completely focused page that you can deliver a product

05:21.660 --> 05:24.649
or feature pitch directly to the user.

05:24.649 --> 05:27.300
Whether they like it or not, they can't ignore it.

05:27.300 --> 05:30.480
As far as MVPs go, the landing page pitch experiment

05:30.480 --> 05:32.959
it's kind of the gold standard, at least

05:32.959 --> 05:36.540
for entrepreneurial MVP test.

05:36.540 --> 05:40.230
Got an idea for a product, create a fake product page

05:40.230 --> 05:43.170
and drive traffic to it, See how they react.

05:43.170 --> 05:46.650
Product managers with existing user bases can also

05:46.650 --> 05:48.660
use this technique quite well.

05:48.660 --> 05:50.580
Instead of getting external traffic, you're just

05:50.580 --> 05:52.650
gonna drive your internal traffic

05:52.650 --> 05:54.566
to a page through an email.

05:54.566 --> 05:58.890
Internal ads, push notifications, whatever.

05:58.890 --> 06:00.360
Now, two classic examples

06:00.360 --> 06:02.970
of a landing page pitch experiment are one

06:02.970 --> 06:06.870
a company called Buffer and two, a company called Basecamp.

06:06.870 --> 06:07.920
Let's talk about them.

06:07.920 --> 06:10.491
Buffer. Buffer is an online service.

06:10.491 --> 06:12.120
You haven't actually heard of it.

06:12.120 --> 06:15.240
That helps you manage your company's social media

06:15.240 --> 06:16.770
messaging campaigns.

06:16.770 --> 06:18.510
What are the main things it'll do is help you

06:18.510 --> 06:21.720
schedule when your messages go out in advance

06:21.720 --> 06:22.830
so you don't have to have someone sitting

06:22.830 --> 06:25.770
at the computer writing all of your Facebook posts.

06:25.770 --> 06:28.800
Now, Buffer started off by creating a fake page

06:28.800 --> 06:30.797
pitching what their service would do

06:30.797 --> 06:33.900
and then just drove traffic to the page.

06:33.900 --> 06:37.110
They actually set it up in a way where they put

06:37.110 --> 06:39.403
up pieces of what they thought about adding

06:39.403 --> 06:42.900
and then when they saw what things people interacted with

06:42.900 --> 06:44.040
they kind of just use it as

06:44.040 --> 06:46.770
like a puzzle piece, Jigsaw puzzle put together

06:46.770 --> 06:49.800
what their product should actually end up being.

06:49.800 --> 06:51.120
Now, Basecamp, on the other hand

06:51.120 --> 06:53.160
is an online project management tool

06:53.160 --> 06:55.891
something that's often compared to Asana

06:55.891 --> 07:00.300
or Trello or any other popular project management tools.

07:00.300 --> 07:03.780
-: They didn't necessarily start with a landing page.

07:03.780 --> 07:05.683
They actually came out

07:05.683 --> 07:08.820
of a a web development studio called 37 Signals

07:08.820 --> 07:11.931
and it was a side project, but they are very well known

07:11.931 --> 07:16.931
for using landing pages to pre validate any feature

07:17.070 --> 07:18.750
or new side project.

07:18.750 --> 07:20.430
Any time they have a new side project

07:20.430 --> 07:22.590
they make Landing pages that say

07:22.590 --> 07:24.384
This is what it does, this is why it's awesome.

07:24.384 --> 07:27.390
Here's some more information, some photos

07:27.390 --> 07:29.463
and you should buy it or sign up for it.

response.title: Even more MVP techniques: bellhops, Dorothy, & Frankensteins

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.150 --> 00:01.920
-: All right, so the next MVP we're gonna cover

00:01.920 --> 00:04.590
is the concierge service MVP.

00:04.590 --> 00:05.834
Concierge is for those of you

00:05.834 --> 00:08.451
that didn't grow up in a mansion.

00:08.451 --> 00:11.430
It's a service where you get one-on-one support

00:11.430 --> 00:15.030
from someone who manually walks you through some task.

00:15.030 --> 00:18.060
It's like a concierge desk at a fancy hotel.

00:18.060 --> 00:20.400
If you call the concierge, you can ask them to do

00:20.400 --> 00:22.680
pretty much anything, and they'll try

00:22.680 --> 00:25.020
to personally help you do it.

00:25.020 --> 00:28.230
So in this MVP, instead of making the feature or product,

00:28.230 --> 00:30.982
what you can do is launch an informal offering

00:30.982 --> 00:35.040
to a small subset of users or potential users.

00:35.040 --> 00:36.960
Tell them it's a beta program,

00:36.960 --> 00:40.560
or just tell them it's a new thing that you're planning out

00:40.560 --> 00:42.450
and just starting to do.

00:42.450 --> 00:45.480
Then you can manually help them accomplish the task

00:45.480 --> 00:47.490
your proposed feature or product would do.

00:47.490 --> 00:49.980
The idea here is that if you are manually there

00:49.980 --> 00:53.278
to help them accomplish their task, you can see firsthand

00:53.278 --> 00:56.910
whether or not what you are doing is helpful and necessary.

00:56.910 --> 00:58.080
Let's give an example.

00:58.080 --> 01:00.732
Let's say Amazon wanted to build a bot,

01:00.732 --> 01:03.340
and that bot could take written request and then go

01:03.340 --> 01:06.570
and fetch things it thought the customer might want.

01:06.570 --> 01:09.660
Sounds like a really expensive thing to build, right?

01:09.660 --> 01:11.903
Well, what you can do instead of building

01:11.903 --> 01:15.672
that complex algorithm is just find some power users

01:15.672 --> 01:18.540
and say, "Hey, we're launching a new service.

01:18.540 --> 01:20.907
Tell us what you're looking for and we'll come back

01:20.907 --> 01:23.280
with personalized recommendations."

01:23.280 --> 01:26.610
And then through email, just help them fetch the items.

01:26.610 --> 01:27.630
See if they like it,

01:27.630 --> 01:30.000
and see if they continue to ask for more things.

01:30.000 --> 01:34.920
And also, see exactly what they plan to use the service for.

01:34.920 --> 01:37.098
That then tells you who to focus on

01:37.098 --> 01:40.350
and what areas to prioritize when you launch.

01:40.350 --> 01:43.020
A great example of a concierge MVP in action

01:43.020 --> 01:45.960
was a company called Rent the Runway.

01:45.960 --> 01:48.210
Startup a long time ago, very successful now.

01:48.210 --> 01:49.890
They'd rent out really fancy dresses

01:49.890 --> 01:52.182
to women who want really fancy dresses

01:52.182 --> 01:54.121
but don't wanna buy them forever.

01:54.121 --> 01:56.130
They just wanna wear then once.

01:56.130 --> 01:58.920
They launched with an in-person concierge service

01:58.920 --> 02:02.370
on college campuses where they physically helped women

02:02.370 --> 02:04.113
pick dresses, get them,

02:05.160 --> 02:06.690
stock them, organize them,

02:06.690 --> 02:08.850
return them, and then charge them.

02:08.850 --> 02:09.930
It was a hit.

02:09.930 --> 02:11.522
It was easy to figure out what people wanted.

02:11.522 --> 02:13.647
They built the product around their feedback,

02:13.647 --> 02:15.420
and the rest is history.

02:15.420 --> 02:16.879
And the next MVP we're gonna talk about

02:16.879 --> 02:19.650
is the piecemeal MVP.

02:19.650 --> 02:23.043
A piecemeal MVP is where, instead of building out

02:23.043 --> 02:25.159
your product, you take what's available

02:25.159 --> 02:27.800
in the form of out-of-the box softwares,

02:27.800 --> 02:30.720
and by piecing those softwares together,

02:30.720 --> 02:32.527
you can match the functionality you need

02:32.527 --> 02:37.320
to test the basic version of what you're tryna build.

02:37.320 --> 02:39.932
Now, there are tons and tons of platforms out there,

02:39.932 --> 02:41.682
and that's why this works.

02:41.682 --> 02:44.597
You can use a Weebly, or a Wix, or a Squarespace

02:44.597 --> 02:46.873
to create a page that can take orders,

02:46.873 --> 02:49.385
send emails, send text messages.

02:49.385 --> 02:50.744
Really do a lot.

02:50.744 --> 02:53.585
You can stick softwares like Formstack, or Typeform,

02:53.585 --> 02:56.610
or Jotform ,to create forms that can do

02:56.610 --> 03:00.300
advanced calculations and have advanced features.

03:00.300 --> 03:01.373
You wanna send text messages

03:01.373 --> 03:04.135
and have entire systems built off that?

03:04.135 --> 03:05.880
You can use Twilio.

03:05.880 --> 03:08.490
You can use Recurly to manage subscriptions.

03:08.490 --> 03:11.760
There are just tons and tons of services out there

03:11.760 --> 03:15.228
for almost anything you can think of doing.

03:15.228 --> 03:16.752
Now, with some creativity,

03:16.752 --> 03:19.200
you can take these things and stick them together

03:19.200 --> 03:20.350
into a Frankenstein.

03:20.350 --> 03:21.908
(thrilling music)

03:21.908 --> 03:23.700
Actor: It's alive.

03:23.700 --> 03:26.367
-: That gets the job done until you can then justify

03:26.367 --> 03:28.290
using real resources.

03:28.290 --> 03:30.769
Groupon's a great example of a company that uses MVP.

03:30.769 --> 03:32.470
Groupon's that company that for so long

03:32.470 --> 03:35.670
just bombarded your email inbox with flash deals

03:35.670 --> 03:38.715
on things from spa packages, the skydiving,

03:38.715 --> 03:42.030
to that restaurant you've vaguely heard of.

03:42.030 --> 03:45.300
Well, they didn't want to build out the complex system

03:45.300 --> 03:49.138
to take orders, manage their clientele,

03:49.138 --> 03:51.330
the actual stores, and generate the coupons,

03:51.330 --> 03:54.000
so they instead created their site on WordPress,

03:54.000 --> 03:56.100
and this is back when they're called The Point.

03:56.100 --> 03:58.836
The WordPress site then would email

03:58.836 --> 04:02.460
every time there was an order, and then they would use

04:02.460 --> 04:04.620
Apple Mail in a thing called AppleScript.

04:04.620 --> 04:06.227
So that when they receive an email,

04:06.227 --> 04:08.923
it could just run some basic logic on it

04:08.923 --> 04:12.939
and generate a coupon that would then auto send back

04:12.939 --> 04:15.240
to the person who needed it.

04:15.240 --> 04:17.670
All right, so we're at the last MVP tactic

04:17.670 --> 04:18.990
that we're going to cover.

04:18.990 --> 04:21.182
This one's called the Wizard of Oz MVP.

04:21.182 --> 04:22.853
♪ We're off to see the Wizard ♪

04:22.853 --> 04:24.780
♪ The wonderful Wizard of Oz ♪

04:24.780 --> 04:28.530
-: A Wizard of Oz MVP is an MVP that from the front

04:28.530 --> 04:33.411
looks to be completely made, but all the task that computers

04:33.411 --> 04:36.107
and automated systems would normally be doing,

04:36.107 --> 04:38.190
it's actually carried out manually

04:38.190 --> 04:40.710
behind the scenes by an individual.

04:40.710 --> 04:42.026
It's just a fact of engineering

04:42.026 --> 04:44.760
that when you build a web or mobile product,

04:44.760 --> 04:47.028
the largest portion of your effort is going to be dedicated

04:47.028 --> 04:50.340
towards making the server-side logic,

04:50.340 --> 04:53.550
i.e., the parts of the website that make it work

04:53.550 --> 04:55.620
but no one ever physically sees.

04:55.620 --> 04:58.560
So in this MVP, you take advantage of that.

04:58.560 --> 05:00.780
You develop a front-end design

05:00.780 --> 05:02.610
that looks entirely functional,

05:02.610 --> 05:05.040
but you just have someone ready on standby to fulfill

05:05.040 --> 05:09.990
whatever the user thinks should be happening in the app.

05:09.990 --> 05:12.030
So let's say you had a social network,

05:12.030 --> 05:14.770
and you wanted to start a feature that matched people

05:14.770 --> 05:18.000
based off of their interest and their profile information.

05:18.000 --> 05:19.954
Instead of building out the complex algorithm

05:19.954 --> 05:21.480
for matching people

05:21.480 --> 05:24.420
and then seeing if people want to use it after the fact,

05:24.420 --> 05:27.111
you could build out the button that says "Match me."

05:27.111 --> 05:28.565
And then every time someone clicks that,

05:28.565 --> 05:31.718
you manually had someone look through their profile

05:31.718 --> 05:35.160
and match them to someone, email them the results.

05:35.160 --> 05:37.950
Voila! Now, why do we call it Wizard of Oz?

05:37.950 --> 05:40.422
Well, did you watch The Wizard of Oz?

05:40.422 --> 05:41.640
I mean, it's a classic,

05:41.640 --> 05:42.771
but remember when they get

05:42.771 --> 05:45.023
through the entire Yellow Brick Road,

05:45.023 --> 05:48.531
only to find out the great Wizard of Oz was actually a man

05:48.531 --> 05:52.936
behind a curtain pulling levers to make special effects

05:52.936 --> 05:56.343
to create that giant emerald face that was so scary.

05:56.343 --> 05:57.176
-: I am Oz.

05:58.989 --> 06:01.239
The greatest Oz has spoken.

06:02.566 --> 06:03.787
-: Who are you?

06:03.787 --> 06:04.745
-: Who are...

06:04.745 --> 06:08.553
I-I am the great and powerful Wizard of Oz.

06:08.553 --> 06:11.250
-: So this is the software version of that.

06:11.250 --> 06:13.590
The classic example of this was Zappos.

06:13.590 --> 06:16.470
They created a online shoe store,

06:16.470 --> 06:18.143
but they didn't stock any shoes.

06:18.143 --> 06:20.912
When people ordered through their online store,

06:20.912 --> 06:23.700
they walked across the street and bought it

06:23.700 --> 06:28.700
from an actual brick and mortar store then mailed it.

06:28.800 --> 06:30.998
That way, they didn't have to build any server-side logic,

06:30.998 --> 06:33.836
they didn't have to buy any inventory,

06:33.836 --> 06:36.131
and they could pretty much say they offered

06:36.131 --> 06:38.640
whatever the people down the street offered.

06:38.640 --> 06:39.929
All right, guys, that's the whirlwind tour

06:39.929 --> 06:43.170
of all the MVPs that you need to know about.

06:43.170 --> 06:44.473
The following lectures, we're gonna talk

06:44.473 --> 06:46.388
about each one in depth.

06:46.388 --> 06:48.840
You can skip some of them if you're not interested

06:48.840 --> 06:50.520
in a specific one.

06:50.520 --> 06:52.073
Otherwise, I'll see you then.

06:52.073 --> 06:53.853
Witch: I'm melting! Melting!

06:54.900 --> 06:57.697
Oh, what a world! What a world!

WEBVTT

00:00.270 --> 00:01.350
-: Hey guys. Welcome back.

00:01.350 --> 00:03.570
Let's talk a little bit more in detail

00:03.570 --> 00:05.253
on the concept of that first MVP we talked

00:05.253 --> 00:08.880
about called the email based MVP.

00:08.880 --> 00:11.220
Let's cover what the pros and cons are.

00:11.220 --> 00:13.198
You are after all going to have to decide

00:13.198 --> 00:16.290
whether or not this is a good idea given your situation

00:16.290 --> 00:17.498
as a product manager.

00:17.498 --> 00:20.730
You could have guessed it, but this technique is best suited

00:20.730 --> 00:23.858
for smaller organizations that do not yet have

00:23.858 --> 00:27.270
much brand anxiety, as I call it.

00:27.270 --> 00:28.560
The pros of this are simple.

00:28.560 --> 00:29.910
You can do it very quickly.

00:29.910 --> 00:32.103
You can limit your testing group to a small

00:32.103 --> 00:35.232
but statistically significant group of people easily,

00:35.232 --> 00:37.950
and you can segment your users as long

00:37.950 --> 00:40.410
as you've collected secondary data on them.

00:40.410 --> 00:43.650
What I mean is if you collect, say, usage statistics

00:43.650 --> 00:46.770
or basic profile information, you can easily tie that

00:46.770 --> 00:49.050
to their email address and then when you need to say

00:49.050 --> 00:51.390
launch a test to your power users

00:51.390 --> 00:52.893
you can then pick only the email addresses

00:52.893 --> 00:57.360
that fit that specific group based off of their usage stats.

00:57.360 --> 00:59.793
Okay, so it's cheap, it's fast, it's targeted

00:59.793 --> 01:02.760
and you can limit the damage.

01:02.760 --> 01:05.190
Oh, and also anyone can do it.

01:05.190 --> 01:08.790
No bugging your developers to run this one pretty much ever.

01:08.790 --> 01:11.490
Now the cons are that they can come off sloppy.

01:11.490 --> 01:14.250
It's not standard practice to ask people

01:14.250 --> 01:17.550
to, say, buy something straight out of an email,

01:17.550 --> 01:19.619
so you might run the risk of having it look

01:19.619 --> 01:21.750
a little bit odd.

01:21.750 --> 01:23.340
You also might dent your brand

01:23.340 --> 01:26.173
if you're not careful to match the general tone

01:26.173 --> 01:29.640
of how your normal email marketing campaigns

01:29.640 --> 01:31.680
and conversations happen.

01:31.680 --> 01:35.075
If you do decide to go down this path and try it out

01:35.075 --> 01:39.150
here are some tips to make it worth your while.

01:39.150 --> 01:42.518
Number one is be aware of what your audience expects of you.

01:42.518 --> 01:45.815
Are you a luxury brand or a bargain outlet?

01:45.815 --> 01:48.630
Different audiences have different tolerances

01:48.630 --> 01:49.890
for this technique.

01:49.890 --> 01:51.980
The example we used before was AppSumo.

01:51.980 --> 01:54.603
They can get away with it because they sell flash deals,

01:54.603 --> 01:57.930
so their normal product offering often looks

01:57.930 --> 01:59.730
like it's rushed anyway.

01:59.730 --> 02:03.360
They also sell to entrepreneurs who are used to being

02:03.360 --> 02:05.963
in beta groups and they're used to seeing pre-launched

02:05.963 --> 02:07.860
products and MVPs.

02:07.860 --> 02:11.430
Number two, try your best to match the production value

02:11.430 --> 02:14.400
of the emails your company usually uses.

02:14.400 --> 02:15.900
In your normal email campaigns.

02:15.900 --> 02:19.410
You probably have templates, designs, and probably

02:19.410 --> 02:21.870
some embedded images or videos.

02:21.870 --> 02:25.770
If that's the case, try to match the same, add some images

02:25.770 --> 02:29.010
add some video, and by all means, try to use the

02:29.010 --> 02:30.300
exact same design.

02:30.300 --> 02:32.900
I'm sure your designers will lend you the template file.

02:32.900 --> 02:36.540
As for images, you don't need to bug anyone.

02:36.540 --> 02:39.272
There are a slew of easy to use tools that can help you

02:39.272 --> 02:41.610
crank these out really easily.

02:41.610 --> 02:43.590
My favorite by far is Canva.

02:43.590 --> 02:44.423
Check it out.

02:44.423 --> 02:45.630
I put it in the resources.

02:45.630 --> 02:48.330
Number three is try to at a minimum, pair this

02:48.330 --> 02:51.390
with a landing page or another MVP technique.

02:51.390 --> 02:53.130
For instance, a concierge service.

02:53.130 --> 02:55.260
You can pitch the idea in the email and then have

02:55.260 --> 02:58.530
a link that goes to the landing page for more information,

02:58.530 --> 03:03.027
or in the email ask them to respond, state their interest,

03:03.027 --> 03:04.860
and then run them like they're

03:04.860 --> 03:07.800
in a beta program through a concierge service.

03:07.800 --> 03:10.080
Email by itself is kind of tacky

03:10.080 --> 03:12.930
so you can optimize it by adding in other techniques.

03:12.930 --> 03:14.670
If you think in the email, MVP will fly

03:14.670 --> 03:17.280
with your crowd and your company will go for it,

03:17.280 --> 03:18.780
well then try it out,

03:18.780 --> 03:23.040
but be careful as to not make it look too cavalier.

03:23.040 --> 03:23.873
All right guys,

03:23.873 --> 03:26.161
in the next lecture, let's talk more about shadow buttons.

03:26.161 --> 03:27.153
See you then.

WEBVTT

00:00.360 --> 00:02.790
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:02.790 --> 00:06.180
Let's talk a little bit more in depth about that other MVP

00:06.180 --> 00:08.160
that I called the shadow button.

00:08.160 --> 00:10.860
All right, now shadow buttons are fake buttons

00:10.860 --> 00:13.950
put on an app that just track when they're clicked

00:13.950 --> 00:15.600
and that's literally it.

00:15.600 --> 00:17.730
If you're scratching your head wondering where

00:17.730 --> 00:20.580
you've seen this before, you might not remember.

00:20.580 --> 00:22.170
They're pretty subtle.

00:22.170 --> 00:23.970
Here's one I actually found the other day.

00:23.970 --> 00:26.580
If you guys ever go to uncommongoods.com,

00:26.580 --> 00:31.110
try to log in in the sales page with Facebook.

00:31.110 --> 00:33.900
What you'll see is an image pop up or text pop up

00:33.900 --> 00:37.830
and say, "Thank you for voting to add Facebook login."

00:37.830 --> 00:39.720
Great example of a shadow button.

00:39.720 --> 00:42.270
Shadow buttons are good because they give you great data.

00:42.270 --> 00:43.440
That is pretty much as close

00:43.440 --> 00:44.520
as you're going to get to showing

00:44.520 --> 00:47.400
that someone is in fact interested in your new feature.

00:47.400 --> 00:48.930
It's also pretty easy to do.

00:48.930 --> 00:51.960
Your developer can probably do it over lunch.

00:51.960 --> 00:53.400
The downside though, is obvious.

00:53.400 --> 00:55.170
It doesn't look good.

00:55.170 --> 00:56.400
No one who's ever interacted

00:56.400 --> 00:59.130
with a shadow button said, "Wow, that was awesome."

00:59.130 --> 01:01.440
They probably think, "What was that?

01:01.440 --> 01:02.790
Is this broken?"

01:02.790 --> 01:04.170
Because that's what it looks like.

01:04.170 --> 01:06.000
It looks like your site is broken.

01:06.000 --> 01:08.640
Now, that's okay if you're maintaining a product

01:08.640 --> 01:11.130
that has a reputation for having bugs

01:11.130 --> 01:14.400
and has a user population that's just gotten used to it.

01:14.400 --> 01:16.710
I'm not saying be complacent about it.

01:16.710 --> 01:17.910
You should fix your bugs.

01:17.910 --> 01:20.220
But let's just say if you, to me, for example

01:20.220 --> 01:23.280
had a new feature when I clicked on it, it just did nothing.

01:23.280 --> 01:26.610
Well, I wouldn't even notice because let's be honest,

01:26.610 --> 01:29.040
I probably ran into 15 different bugs

01:29.040 --> 01:32.040
and broken things before I even got to the shadow button.

01:32.040 --> 01:34.140
Shadow buttons are sneaky.

01:34.140 --> 01:38.100
Great data, but they create a universally negative response

01:38.100 --> 01:39.330
from your users.

01:39.330 --> 01:40.770
It's just a question of whether or not

01:40.770 --> 01:43.800
the user cares about the polish of your product.

01:43.800 --> 01:45.690
If you do decide to go this route,

01:45.690 --> 01:47.640
let's cover some best practices.

01:47.640 --> 01:50.490
Number one, do it just like Uncommon Goods

01:50.490 --> 01:51.960
did it like you saw.

01:51.960 --> 01:54.060
Put a message that says, Thank you for clicking

01:54.060 --> 01:55.860
and acknowledge what it is.

01:55.860 --> 01:57.060
That softens the blow

01:57.060 --> 02:00.360
because intentional bug is way better than a bug

02:00.360 --> 02:02.670
that they aren't aware of and simply can't fix.

02:02.670 --> 02:04.830
Number two is limit the amount of users

02:04.830 --> 02:07.080
that see this as much as possible.

02:07.080 --> 02:09.510
You can calculate what number of people need to see it

02:09.510 --> 02:11.370
to be statistically significant

02:11.370 --> 02:14.610
be as close to that line as possible.

02:14.610 --> 02:17.070
In summary, it's easy to do.

02:17.070 --> 02:20.850
It's very valid data, generally not a good thing

02:20.850 --> 02:24.150
for your brand, so have a hard think about

02:24.150 --> 02:28.590
whether or not you think your users are tolerant enough

02:28.590 --> 02:31.443
to be okay with this type of testing.

WEBVTT

00:00.180 --> 00:02.160
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:02.160 --> 00:04.410
Let's talk about the best practices

00:04.410 --> 00:06.480
of using the 404 page

00:06.480 --> 00:08.402
or the Coming Soon page

00:08.402 --> 00:12.750
type MVP to test out your new feature product idea.

00:12.750 --> 00:15.090
Chances are you've seen these before,

00:15.090 --> 00:17.070
but you probably don't remember them.

00:17.070 --> 00:17.903
Why?

00:17.903 --> 00:19.590
Well, because it generally just feels

00:19.590 --> 00:22.290
like nothing out of the ordinary.

00:22.290 --> 00:24.240
As for the 404 MVPs

00:24.240 --> 00:27.014
these are some of the hardest MVPs to spot

00:27.014 --> 00:30.180
because how do you even know if it was a test or not?

00:30.180 --> 00:32.550
It could just be that the page was broken.

00:32.550 --> 00:35.430
And Coming Soon pages are nowadays everywhere,

00:35.430 --> 00:39.660
half of which never materialize into anything at all.

00:39.660 --> 00:42.150
Now, how do we choose between running a 404

00:42.150 --> 00:44.070
or a Coming Soon page?

00:44.070 --> 00:46.440
Well just ask yourself this question.

00:46.440 --> 00:50.640
If I'm a user, would I rather think the page is broken

00:50.640 --> 00:53.400
or that the page was misleading?

00:53.400 --> 00:56.910
Which of these two options is least evil for you?

00:56.910 --> 00:59.880
Whatever you say, that's probably the way you should go.

00:59.880 --> 01:02.340
The companies that can get away with 404s are the ones

01:02.340 --> 01:05.310
that generally have tons and tons of pages.

01:05.310 --> 01:06.143
Why?

01:06.143 --> 01:07.920
Because if you see a 404,

01:07.920 --> 01:10.950
you can still jump to another product and keep browsing.

01:10.950 --> 01:12.840
Not a lot of damage done.

01:12.840 --> 01:14.700
Amazon does this all the time

01:14.700 --> 01:17.940
because they have 10 bajillion roughly

01:17.940 --> 01:19.980
other pages you can look at.

01:19.980 --> 01:23.370
Clothing companies run variations of this all the time.

01:23.370 --> 01:24.960
They'll add new items,

01:24.960 --> 01:28.200
but make them out of stock, and that's totally normal.

01:28.200 --> 01:29.910
No one notices this.

01:29.910 --> 01:31.170
The companies that generally get away

01:31.170 --> 01:34.110
with Coming Soon page MVPs are often companies

01:34.110 --> 01:36.840
that are smaller and where the audience is aware

01:36.840 --> 01:39.030
that they are in fact new.

01:39.030 --> 01:40.890
Those companies are already working

01:40.890 --> 01:42.870
with their early adopters.

01:42.870 --> 01:45.990
And those of you guys that completed my MVP course

01:45.990 --> 01:47.460
know that early adopters

01:47.460 --> 01:49.350
generally have very tough skin

01:49.350 --> 01:50.760
when it comes to this stuff.

01:50.760 --> 01:52.590
They generally revel in the fact

01:52.590 --> 01:55.560
that they are cutting edge so in a weird way,

01:55.560 --> 01:58.920
Coming Soon pages actually don't phase them at all,

01:58.920 --> 02:00.630
they feel better about them.

02:00.630 --> 02:03.900
Now, if walmart.com did this,

02:03.900 --> 02:07.890
it would be considered universally tacky on the other hand.

02:07.890 --> 02:09.720
So think about your user expectations

02:09.720 --> 02:13.350
and which of these result in the least negative outcome.

02:13.350 --> 02:15.300
Can they get over the broken page

02:15.300 --> 02:18.420
and will this Coming Soon page peak their interest

02:18.420 --> 02:19.680
or deflate it?

02:19.680 --> 02:21.180
If you decide to go this route,

02:21.180 --> 02:23.820
let's talk about some tips and best practices.

02:23.820 --> 02:25.290
Number one is design it.

02:25.290 --> 02:26.250
What do I mean?

02:26.250 --> 02:28.590
Well, there is a world of difference between

02:28.590 --> 02:32.130
a bland colorless page that really does look like

02:32.130 --> 02:33.390
your site is broken,

02:33.390 --> 02:35.940
and a clever, well designed 404 page

02:35.940 --> 02:38.100
that actually might enhance your brand.

02:38.100 --> 02:39.330
Check out the resources.

02:39.330 --> 02:42.000
There are actually lists of 404 sites

02:42.000 --> 02:43.950
that have done this really well.

02:43.950 --> 02:46.230
Some of these 404s are actually really funny

02:46.230 --> 02:48.690
and it endears you to the company

02:48.690 --> 02:51.990
and allows the company to continue a conversation

02:51.990 --> 02:54.240
with you that they otherwise wouldn't be able to.

02:54.240 --> 02:56.160
The same goes for Coming Soon pages.

02:56.160 --> 02:57.930
Put some effort into them.

02:57.930 --> 02:59.400
Don't just use generic templates

02:59.400 --> 03:02.520
and don't forget to proofread whatever you put up.

03:02.520 --> 03:04.320
My best suggestion here is to use a tool

03:04.320 --> 03:07.680
that is designed specifically for Coming Soon pages.

03:07.680 --> 03:11.670
The two main ones being LaunchRock and KickoffLabs.

03:11.670 --> 03:15.150
Those two sites will help you pull one together very quickly

03:15.150 --> 03:17.310
and even let you import your own design assets

03:17.310 --> 03:20.430
and make it look like a seamless continuation

03:20.430 --> 03:21.480
of your product.

03:21.480 --> 03:23.460
Sometimes you can't even tell

03:23.460 --> 03:25.260
that you used a platform for it.

03:25.260 --> 03:27.210
Number two is shorten the horizon

03:27.210 --> 03:29.460
on these as much as possible.

03:29.460 --> 03:31.200
You don't wanna see the same user

03:31.200 --> 03:34.500
seeing the same 404 or Coming Soon page.

03:34.500 --> 03:36.090
That's when that's annoying

03:36.090 --> 03:37.980
turns into I'm angry,

03:37.980 --> 03:41.190
so try to limit the length of these tests.

03:41.190 --> 03:43.133
All right guys, see you in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.180 --> 00:01.650
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:01.650 --> 00:06.480
Let's talk about explainer videos and how best to use 'em.

00:06.480 --> 00:08.040
First off, let's answer the question

00:08.040 --> 00:11.220
why do people use this technique versus others?

00:11.220 --> 00:12.750
Well, it's simple actually.

00:12.750 --> 00:16.530
Video converts much better than just text pages.

00:16.530 --> 00:19.160
This is what every company on the planet is figuring out

00:19.160 --> 00:21.720
and it's the reason you're seeing more and more

00:21.720 --> 00:26.010
videos embedded everywhere including your Facebook newsfeed.

00:26.010 --> 00:27.390
Another reason we might choose this is

00:27.390 --> 00:30.570
because an explainer video can do a really good job

00:30.570 --> 00:34.740
of getting across a product that needs in depth explaining.

00:34.740 --> 00:37.020
Some products are just inherently complicated

00:37.020 --> 00:39.090
which makes pitching them difficult.

00:39.090 --> 00:42.030
You can cover way more ground in a shorter time

00:42.030 --> 00:44.040
through a shnazzy video.

00:44.040 --> 00:46.980
The next question is, when should we use these?

00:46.980 --> 00:49.611
Well, an explainer video, as you remember me saying

00:49.611 --> 00:52.740
can be a lot of work to create.

00:52.740 --> 00:55.590
They often need videographers, editors

00:55.590 --> 00:57.960
and potentially custom graphics.

00:57.960 --> 01:01.140
On the flip side they can really excite your user base

01:01.140 --> 01:02.040
if done well.

01:02.040 --> 01:04.230
Let's look at a couple examples.

01:04.230 --> 01:06.060
Narrator: Hello, this is Joe.

01:06.060 --> 01:08.640
Joe runs his own business selling local goods.

01:08.640 --> 01:11.040
But Joe has a big problem,

01:11.040 --> 01:14.490
he's got too much to do and he can't find qualified help.

01:14.490 --> 01:16.500
Everywhere Joe has looked he's only found

01:16.500 --> 01:18.600
unqualified employees.

01:18.600 --> 01:22.110
Then one day Joe called the solution company.

01:22.110 --> 01:24.810
Over the phone the solution company was able to analyze

01:24.810 --> 01:27.810
Joe's needs and match him with the qualified candidates.

01:27.810 --> 01:30.570
Within days, Joe had a wide selection of qualified

01:30.570 --> 01:32.670
pre-screened workers to choose from.

01:32.670 --> 01:35.533
Thanks to the solution company, Joe can now sit back

01:35.533 --> 01:38.400
and relax knowing he's got the right people

01:38.400 --> 01:39.960
for the right job.

01:39.960 --> 01:42.360
Do you want to find the right people for your business?

01:42.360 --> 01:44.670
Call the solution company today.

01:44.670 --> 01:47.430
Let the solution company provide you with pre-screened

01:47.430 --> 01:50.653
and qualified candidates to grow your business.

01:50.653 --> 01:53.236
(upbeat music)

01:56.460 --> 01:59.490
-: So, how do you balance this exactly?

01:59.490 --> 02:02.010
Well, like anything, it's gonna come down to your

02:02.010 --> 02:03.690
organizational profile.

02:03.690 --> 02:06.840
Companies that rely heavily on video already use

02:06.840 --> 02:09.930
video frequently with their users,

02:09.930 --> 02:12.450
and they're gonna be much more likely candidates

02:12.450 --> 02:14.340
to use this type of test.

02:14.340 --> 02:17.670
Why? Well, for one, you probably already have

02:17.670 --> 02:20.610
the infrastructure to create half decent videos

02:20.610 --> 02:22.650
without a significant amount of work

02:22.650 --> 02:24.390
and outside recruitment.

02:24.390 --> 02:26.520
Another reason is that if your users are used

02:26.520 --> 02:29.340
to seeing video then they're more likely to respond

02:29.340 --> 02:30.173
to video.

02:30.173 --> 02:32.910
If you use a service like Wistia, you can see how many

02:32.910 --> 02:35.880
of your users watch the entirety of your video.

02:35.880 --> 02:38.250
Wistia, by the way is just a platform that lets you embed

02:38.250 --> 02:41.280
videos in webpages, emails really anywhere,

02:41.280 --> 02:42.690
and gives you detailed analytics

02:42.690 --> 02:44.820
about how people engage with it.

02:44.820 --> 02:47.280
Very similar to the YouTube analytics system.

02:47.280 --> 02:51.930
If you run a content website that rarely uses video content

02:51.930 --> 02:54.300
throwing an explainer video for a new feature

02:54.300 --> 02:55.710
is probably going to get ignored

02:55.710 --> 02:57.630
and skew the results of your test.

02:57.630 --> 03:01.500
Same goes for websites that simply have older demographics.

03:01.500 --> 03:04.890
Old people on video are just not really a thing.

03:04.890 --> 03:06.270
My grandma, for the most part,

03:06.270 --> 03:08.610
thinks the iPad is the work of the devil

03:08.610 --> 03:11.910
and videos just don't really register with her.

03:11.910 --> 03:14.880
As a funny aside, check out our Oculus Rift activity

03:14.880 --> 03:16.530
if you haven't already.

03:16.530 --> 03:19.650
There's a video of old people using Oculus Rift and it's

03:19.650 --> 03:20.851
pretty hilarious.

03:20.851 --> 03:23.280
-: My God, I'm not gonna electrocute myself, am I?

03:23.280 --> 03:24.630
-: Snorkeling mask?

03:24.630 --> 03:28.800
-: Something you would have in war time situation.

03:28.800 --> 03:30.870
-: It's a space mask, I don't know.

03:30.870 --> 03:32.970
Or something you wear for the Ebola crisis.

03:32.970 --> 03:36.570
-: This could be some kind of mind control hypnotic thing

03:36.570 --> 03:38.130
you're gonna try to pull on me.

03:38.130 --> 03:41.100
-: Oh my, walking without using my feet.

03:41.100 --> 03:42.510
-: It's very pretty out here.

03:42.510 --> 03:45.480
I mean, it's all very fake looking, but it's beautiful.

03:45.480 --> 03:47.580
-: Oh. Let's not do this.

03:47.580 --> 03:48.413
-: Oh (beep).

03:50.100 --> 03:51.450
-: What happened to the tracks?

03:51.450 --> 03:54.270
(laughing)

03:54.270 --> 03:56.430
We just jumped off the tracks.

03:56.430 --> 03:57.300
There were no tracks.

03:57.300 --> 03:59.910
-: Other things you take into consideration are your size

03:59.910 --> 04:01.920
and your user's expectations.

04:01.920 --> 04:04.530
If you have no online presence at all, i.e.

04:04.530 --> 04:07.530
you're in that entrepreneurial pre-product scenario

04:07.530 --> 04:09.330
and you can be as scrappy as you want.

04:09.330 --> 04:11.880
My favorite way of making explainers in this case

04:11.880 --> 04:15.900
is you just cheap software is like Powtoon, Go Animate

04:15.900 --> 04:18.060
or even just hiring someone on Fiverr

04:18.060 --> 04:20.070
to make a video for me.

04:20.070 --> 04:22.702
If you haven't set the bar for quality yet,

04:22.702 --> 04:25.470
you can get away with whatever quality you can afford

04:25.470 --> 04:26.400
at the moment.

04:26.400 --> 04:29.340
That flexibility is fantastic and embrace it.

04:29.340 --> 04:32.640
If you're larger, however and you have already dabbled

04:32.640 --> 04:36.720
in video in some way be aware that you do have a quality bar

04:36.720 --> 04:37.710
to get over.

04:37.710 --> 04:41.130
If you make slick fun videos that your users are used to

04:41.130 --> 04:44.610
then out of nowhere you create a poorly produced screen cast

04:44.610 --> 04:46.641
with low quality voiceover.

04:46.641 --> 04:48.720
The difference in what users expect

04:48.720 --> 04:50.790
and what they find will taint their perception

04:50.790 --> 04:52.800
of whatever you're trying to pitch.

04:52.800 --> 04:57.180
The key here is to try to be as ordinary as possible.

04:57.180 --> 05:00.060
You don't wanna stick out either way

05:00.060 --> 05:02.790
relative to the other video content you use.

05:02.790 --> 05:04.890
Explainers are an interesting way to go

05:04.890 --> 05:06.420
but they're definitely something you need to

05:06.420 --> 05:08.700
internally assess before you try it.

05:08.700 --> 05:10.950
The bonus of using explainers is that in general

05:10.950 --> 05:14.190
video is just more engaging with your users

05:14.190 --> 05:17.280
and this increases the likelihood of being shared,

05:17.280 --> 05:18.390
which is great.

05:18.390 --> 05:20.580
Downside obviously though, is that it could just

05:20.580 --> 05:22.533
simply not be your demographic.

WEBVTT

00:00.240 --> 00:02.100
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:02.100 --> 00:04.020
Let's talk about one of the last types

00:04.020 --> 00:07.920
of MVPs that we covered, the Piecemeal MVP.

00:07.920 --> 00:09.240
Now piecemeal, if you remember,

00:09.240 --> 00:11.580
is when you piece together other softwares

00:11.580 --> 00:14.340
to create the functionality that you need.

00:14.340 --> 00:17.250
To better help you visualize what this concept is,

00:17.250 --> 00:19.320
because it's kind of confusing,

00:19.320 --> 00:20.550
let's go over an example.

00:20.550 --> 00:23.250
Let's say you had an online course like Udemy.

00:23.250 --> 00:25.890
You're on it right now, watching the course.

00:25.890 --> 00:28.800
Let's say you wanted to add a certificate of completion

00:28.800 --> 00:32.040
so that anyone who finishes a course would get emailed a PDF

00:32.040 --> 00:34.621
that says, "Yay, you've finished the course."

00:34.621 --> 00:36.690
(children cheering)

00:36.690 --> 00:40.675
Well, instead of building an auto PDF generator

00:40.675 --> 00:44.400
and setting it up to know when the user finished the course,

00:44.400 --> 00:48.030
maybe you just add a link to the end of the course

00:48.030 --> 00:49.620
that takes them to a form.

00:49.620 --> 00:52.080
They can then input their name into that form

00:52.080 --> 00:55.920
with their email, and then they can get a PDF certificate

00:55.920 --> 00:57.480
sent to them that way.

00:57.480 --> 01:01.200
How you can make this work is by, let's say using Typeform,

01:01.200 --> 01:05.040
which is a form software to create an online form

01:05.040 --> 01:07.050
with a public access link.

01:07.050 --> 01:09.330
Then you could have Typeform submit that information

01:09.330 --> 01:10.748
into a Google Sheet.

01:10.748 --> 01:11.640
If you've never used Google Sheet,

01:11.640 --> 01:13.320
it's just Excel, but online,

01:13.320 --> 01:16.710
which can then trigger something like Zoho Invoice

01:16.710 --> 01:18.720
to create an invoice with the information

01:18.720 --> 01:20.400
and send out an email.

01:20.400 --> 01:22.830
There you go, it was 1, 2, 3 steps,

01:22.830 --> 01:24.330
and you've accomplished your goals.

01:24.330 --> 01:25.830
If you wanted to pursue this option,

01:25.830 --> 01:29.100
the best way would probably be to physically write out

01:29.100 --> 01:32.040
what your feature or product needs to do,

01:32.040 --> 01:34.530
and then use those functions as, just,

01:34.530 --> 01:37.980
search terms in Google by adding things like software

01:37.980 --> 01:41.220
or online at the end of your search query.

01:41.220 --> 01:44.910
You would be amazed at just how much you can get done

01:44.910 --> 01:47.460
with available softwares that are out there.

01:47.460 --> 01:50.610
Once you mark down what you think will do it,

01:50.610 --> 01:52.650
then you just need to find what combination

01:52.650 --> 01:55.530
is gonna work the best and cooperate together.

01:55.530 --> 01:57.828
Typically, Piecemeal MVPs are best suited

01:57.828 --> 02:01.380
for when you want to add some functionality to your product

02:01.380 --> 02:03.420
that's relatively common.

02:03.420 --> 02:06.420
If enough products and people start using, let's say,

02:06.420 --> 02:08.700
text message reminders in their app,

02:08.700 --> 02:11.460
eventually there's gonna be an online software

02:11.460 --> 02:14.940
that tries to simplify adding that process,

02:14.940 --> 02:17.910
and then makes money by saving companies the hassle

02:17.910 --> 02:20.610
and the money of doing it from scratch themselves.

02:20.610 --> 02:24.270
That's great, and can be a seamless integration for you.

02:24.270 --> 02:28.110
When a Piecemeal MVP is not generally a good idea to pursue

02:28.110 --> 02:29.460
is if you have, let's say,

02:29.460 --> 02:31.980
multiple functionalities you need to implement,

02:31.980 --> 02:35.400
because in general, it's kind of a pain

02:35.400 --> 02:40.400
to get off-the-shelf software to cooperate with each other.

02:40.440 --> 02:41.700
They can do it.

02:41.700 --> 02:43.440
That's the hard part though,

02:43.440 --> 02:45.990
especially when you're chaining them together.

02:45.990 --> 02:47.520
It can get really complicated.

02:47.520 --> 02:49.920
Oftentimes, you'll just end up running into dead ends

02:49.920 --> 02:52.350
trying to find some obscure software

02:52.350 --> 02:54.930
that needs to send some obscure information

02:54.930 --> 02:57.180
to another obscure software.

02:57.180 --> 02:59.790
It can become a hairball really quickly,

02:59.790 --> 03:01.920
and you'll wish you didn't get into that.

03:01.920 --> 03:03.600
If you do, for whatever reason,

03:03.600 --> 03:07.080
decide that this is what you want to do, here are some tips.

03:07.080 --> 03:11.460
Number one, Piecemeal MVPs are becoming quite popular

03:11.460 --> 03:14.010
and online softwares are so common

03:14.010 --> 03:15.720
that there are systems cropping up

03:15.720 --> 03:18.150
that can actually help you facilitate interactions

03:18.150 --> 03:20.130
between all of these softwares.

03:20.130 --> 03:22.950
The two that are worth checking out are Zapier,

03:22.950 --> 03:25.860
which I can never pronounce, it's Zapier or Zapier,

03:25.860 --> 03:28.650
and the other one is IFTTT,

03:28.650 --> 03:31.530
which stands for If This, Then That.

03:31.530 --> 03:34.560
In both of these, you can tell it to say, you know,

03:34.560 --> 03:38.610
every time a new note with this text is created in Evernote,

03:38.610 --> 03:40.800
send information to MailChimp,

03:40.800 --> 03:43.680
and then it can trigger MailChimp to do something else.

03:43.680 --> 03:46.830
With some persistence, you can actually daisy chain together

03:46.830 --> 03:49.650
a whole list of these different things

03:49.650 --> 03:51.180
and you can get quite a bit done,

03:51.180 --> 03:54.150
and these softwares will make it a lot easier for you.

03:54.150 --> 03:55.950
Number two, as a product manager,

03:55.950 --> 03:58.530
it's rarely the case that you're gonna get away with

03:58.530 --> 04:03.360
sending people from one external app to another and another.

04:03.360 --> 04:06.270
Look for softwares that allow white labeling, as in,

04:06.270 --> 04:08.820
look for ones that allow you to put in your own images,

04:08.820 --> 04:11.910
put in your own colors, and put in your own fonts,

04:11.910 --> 04:14.820
or even better, look for the softwares that can integrate

04:14.820 --> 04:16.500
into your existing product,

04:16.500 --> 04:18.780
so it looks like it's part of your product.

04:18.780 --> 04:21.510
That lowers the chance your users abandon the process,

04:21.510 --> 04:24.360
and in general it just looks a lot more professional,

04:24.360 --> 04:26.850
which is a problem with Piecemeal MVPs.

04:26.850 --> 04:29.940
Sometimes they just don't look that great,

04:29.940 --> 04:31.020
and people can tell

04:31.020 --> 04:33.900
that you're just sticking a bunch of services together.

04:33.900 --> 04:35.670
All right guys, so that's Piecemeal MVPs.

04:35.670 --> 04:38.850
Any questions, post in the Q&A and we'll jump on it.

04:38.850 --> 04:40.620
Otherwise, moving on to the next lecture,

04:40.620 --> 04:42.520
we're gonna talk about Concierge MVPs.

WEBVTT

00:00.270 --> 00:01.860
-: Hey guys. Welcome back to the course.

00:01.860 --> 00:05.070
Let's talk about concierge MVPs.

00:05.070 --> 00:06.090
As a refresher,

00:06.090 --> 00:09.630
concierge style MVPs are where you manually help your users

00:09.630 --> 00:12.360
accomplish their goals as a means of validating

00:12.360 --> 00:13.950
whether or not they have a need

00:13.950 --> 00:16.110
for what you're doing in the first place.

00:16.110 --> 00:17.850
This is different than the Wizard of Oz MVP

00:17.850 --> 00:20.550
and that you don't actually build out anything,

00:20.550 --> 00:24.630
just some way of connecting your users to you

00:24.630 --> 00:27.630
to get their participation and then secondly,

00:27.630 --> 00:30.300
you are upfront in this scenario

00:30.300 --> 00:33.600
about the fact that you don't yet have this feature.

00:33.600 --> 00:36.090
Those are two key distinctions between the two.

00:36.090 --> 00:38.880
Concierge MVPs are very common ways

00:38.880 --> 00:41.220
to test the needs of your users

00:41.220 --> 00:42.960
even in big companies.

00:42.960 --> 00:46.110
The advantages are that you can run them almost in secret.

00:46.110 --> 00:49.440
All you have to do is email a batch of users

00:49.440 --> 00:51.510
and manage those relationships directly.

00:51.510 --> 00:55.440
You don't even have to add any fake buttons to your website

00:55.440 --> 00:57.570
or build any fake pages.

00:57.570 --> 00:58.770
Another advantage is that

00:58.770 --> 01:01.710
a concierge service is kind of like a hybrid

01:01.710 --> 01:04.710
between customer development and MVP testing.

01:04.710 --> 01:06.960
You do get to see how they react

01:06.960 --> 01:09.090
and whether or not they're gonna use your service

01:09.090 --> 01:10.320
in a meaningful way

01:10.320 --> 01:14.190
but you also get the opportunity to ask why

01:14.190 --> 01:16.470
and see what their internal reasoning is.

01:16.470 --> 01:18.240
The cons of this type of experiment

01:18.240 --> 01:20.400
are that they're really management intensive

01:20.400 --> 01:23.100
and they can beat really time absorbing.

01:23.100 --> 01:24.090
In most cases,

01:24.090 --> 01:26.340
you yourself will wanna run the task

01:26.340 --> 01:27.240
because you know,

01:27.240 --> 01:30.090
you're the one who knows what questions to ask,

01:30.090 --> 01:32.340
which then can add some serious

01:32.340 --> 01:34.710
logistical issues for everyone

01:34.710 --> 01:36.630
'cause you have other responsibilities.

01:36.630 --> 01:40.920
You're also going to burn a lot of resources on one user.

01:40.920 --> 01:45.120
You are just going to have to swallow the extra cost because

01:45.120 --> 01:47.790
concierge services and concierge MVPs

01:47.790 --> 01:52.500
are just very rarely profitable in and of themselves.

01:52.500 --> 01:53.880
The only cases where a concierge

01:53.880 --> 01:55.710
might actually make you money

01:55.710 --> 01:58.020
is if you're selling luxury items,

01:58.020 --> 01:59.790
in which case you can justify

01:59.790 --> 02:02.190
spending a lot of time on one person.

02:02.190 --> 02:06.003
So, how exactly do you run these experiments?

02:07.820 --> 02:10.590
Let's try an example to help conceptualize this.

02:10.590 --> 02:13.110
Let's say, you're a product manager for Uber.

02:13.110 --> 02:14.280
You know what Uber is, right?

02:14.280 --> 02:16.500
And then I probably don't have to tell you what it is.

02:16.500 --> 02:17.730
You wanna know if adding,

02:17.730 --> 02:21.390
let's say, a wheelchair accessible car option to your app

02:21.390 --> 02:22.680
is worth pursuing.

02:22.680 --> 02:25.530
You're not sure how many people exactly need it

02:25.530 --> 02:27.930
and whether the drivers will get enough money

02:27.930 --> 02:30.300
to stay on the system

02:30.300 --> 02:34.560
if they're solely focused on serving that one type.

02:34.560 --> 02:37.560
So, what you could do is pick a city,

02:37.560 --> 02:39.810
take a handful of users and email them

02:39.810 --> 02:44.430
talking about a beta program for handicap accessible cars.

02:44.430 --> 02:47.220
You could also tell your regional managers

02:47.220 --> 02:50.640
to ask around and forward any accounts they can find

02:50.640 --> 02:52.800
that might benefit from the service.

02:52.800 --> 02:55.290
The users that sign up then get a phone number

02:55.290 --> 02:57.360
to text whenever they need pickup.

02:57.360 --> 02:59.430
It's really that simple.

02:59.430 --> 03:00.660
You could drive the car

03:00.660 --> 03:02.460
or have one person drive the car

03:02.460 --> 03:04.140
and you operate the text message.

03:04.140 --> 03:06.570
You didn't actually have to develop anything to do this.

03:06.570 --> 03:07.830
Then it's up to you to just track

03:07.830 --> 03:10.230
how often people end up using the car,

03:10.230 --> 03:13.860
whether or not they decide the weight is even worth it,

03:13.860 --> 03:16.770
and whether or not they just end up switching

03:16.770 --> 03:18.750
and using regular cars.

03:18.750 --> 03:20.610
Also, the plus side is you get to talk to them

03:20.610 --> 03:21.450
during the ride

03:21.450 --> 03:23.850
and hear what the pros and cons of the setup are.

03:23.850 --> 03:25.620
That's a realistic scenario

03:25.620 --> 03:27.390
in which you could pull this off.

03:27.390 --> 03:28.223
If you wanna assess

03:28.223 --> 03:30.420
whether or not this is gonna work for your organization,

03:30.420 --> 03:34.170
you need to think about the value of time.

03:34.170 --> 03:35.760
If you're too in demand

03:35.760 --> 03:38.310
and you simply can't be a hundred percent committed

03:38.310 --> 03:40.290
to a concierge test,

03:40.290 --> 03:42.390
then it might be the case that you try

03:42.390 --> 03:45.270
some different way of MVP testing.

03:45.270 --> 03:47.760
Similarly, if you need bigger data sets

03:47.760 --> 03:49.560
to justify your decision

03:49.560 --> 03:51.360
you're just not gonna get that with this technique

03:51.360 --> 03:55.677
unless you're planning a giant expensive operation.

03:55.677 --> 03:58.110
So, that is a concierge service.

03:58.110 --> 04:02.640
It's cheap to run, but it takes a ton of your time.

04:02.640 --> 04:04.920
Think deeply about whether or not

04:04.920 --> 04:07.530
this is something that is gonna fit for you.

04:07.530 --> 04:10.860
Generally, earlier in your company's history

04:10.860 --> 04:12.060
if you're a smaller group,

04:12.060 --> 04:14.640
that's generally where that's the sweet spot

04:14.640 --> 04:15.840
for concierge tests.

04:15.840 --> 04:18.660
But big organizations use them all the time.

04:18.660 --> 04:20.693
All right, guys. See you the next lecture.

response.title: OPTIONAL: How do big companies think about MVP experiments?

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.330 --> 00:02.400
-: Hey guys, and welcome back to the course.

00:02.400 --> 00:04.890
Now, I know after watching the lectures

00:04.890 --> 00:07.050
and all the different techniques you can use

00:07.050 --> 00:09.540
to MVP your feature or product,

00:09.540 --> 00:10.710
you might have been repeatedly

00:10.710 --> 00:13.020
asking the question to yourself,

00:13.020 --> 00:18.020
can I do any of these in a big well known organization?

00:18.690 --> 00:20.250
What about our brand?

00:20.250 --> 00:21.930
Well, it's a great question,

00:21.930 --> 00:25.380
and so, in this lecture we're gonna talk about the answer.

00:25.380 --> 00:29.850
So, how do big organizations run MVP tests?

00:29.850 --> 00:32.070
Well, remember how we talked about the relationship

00:32.070 --> 00:35.430
between risk and the size of your company?

00:35.430 --> 00:38.910
Well, if you fell asleep, let me just recap it for you.

00:38.910 --> 00:40.710
This is the gist, the gist is,

00:40.710 --> 00:43.260
larger organizations have the resources

00:43.260 --> 00:45.570
to weather failed products.

00:45.570 --> 00:47.820
It doesn't mean that they want their products

00:47.820 --> 00:50.850
to fail necessarily, but if they do,

00:50.850 --> 00:53.190
it's not the end of the world.

00:53.190 --> 00:56.100
So big organizations generally have a different

00:56.100 --> 00:59.220
risk to reward calculus when it comes to

00:59.220 --> 01:01.770
launching new products or new features.

01:01.770 --> 01:04.980
A startup's main risk with a new product launch is

01:04.980 --> 01:07.560
that they use up all of their resources.

01:07.560 --> 01:08.850
That's the biggest risk for them.

01:08.850 --> 01:11.970
But the risk big organizations care about

01:11.970 --> 01:15.750
is comprised of a lot of other things,

01:15.750 --> 01:18.750
one of which is the risk to their brand.

01:18.750 --> 01:20.550
So when you've brought an MVP test,

01:20.550 --> 01:22.410
you're going to have to decide

01:22.410 --> 01:24.900
how much risk you can tolerate.

01:24.900 --> 01:28.110
A general rule of thumb is that the larger the organization

01:28.110 --> 01:31.350
the more concerned they will be with brand

01:31.350 --> 01:35.100
and the less concerned they will be with resources.

01:35.100 --> 01:38.730
Pre-product startups remember, care the least about brand

01:38.730 --> 01:41.550
because what does it matter how strong your brand is

01:41.550 --> 01:43.920
if you're dead and you don't exist anymore?

01:43.920 --> 01:46.140
Now remember, when you run an MVP test

01:46.140 --> 01:49.290
there are numerous ways of tackling this

01:49.290 --> 01:52.020
and they all exist on a spectrum.

01:52.020 --> 01:55.410
An email MVP is probably the easiest on resources

01:55.410 --> 01:57.780
but the most reckless with your brand.

01:57.780 --> 02:01.920
A Wizard of Oz MVP probably is the most resource intensive.

02:01.920 --> 02:04.530
After all, you have to actually make the front end

02:04.530 --> 02:06.390
of your supposed product,

02:06.390 --> 02:08.550
but it's the most protective of your brand.

02:08.550 --> 02:09.690
People don't even notice

02:09.690 --> 02:12.450
that it's not officially your version one.

02:12.450 --> 02:15.540
If done well, it won't look to the user

02:15.540 --> 02:17.070
like anything is different.

02:17.070 --> 02:19.710
But generally, companies are gonna reach a certain size

02:19.710 --> 02:22.380
where they're beyond the tactics.

02:22.380 --> 02:25.080
In those cases, as a product manager, you'll be expected

02:25.080 --> 02:28.770
to build an MVP that is really more like what most people

02:28.770 --> 02:31.740
incorrectly assume an MVP is.

02:31.740 --> 02:35.340
It's a really, really basic version of your product.

02:35.340 --> 02:37.890
And product managers in that type of situation

02:37.890 --> 02:39.870
are calling it an MVP

02:39.870 --> 02:41.880
just because they're used to calling it that

02:41.880 --> 02:44.250
and everyone else knows what you're talking about.

02:44.250 --> 02:47.340
Even if it's the case that you make Eric Reese cry.

02:47.340 --> 02:49.800
Your job in these roles is to think about

02:49.800 --> 02:52.470
how exactly you can build out your features,

02:52.470 --> 02:56.760
but in a way that doesn't expend any unnecessary resources.

02:56.760 --> 02:58.770
You can't rely on parlour tricks anymore.

02:58.770 --> 03:01.590
You really have to build at least something,

03:01.590 --> 03:04.080
you can't really half-step it.

03:04.080 --> 03:05.610
The most common way that product managers

03:05.610 --> 03:08.460
tackle this issue is through a type of MVP

03:08.460 --> 03:10.110
we didn't actually cover.

03:10.110 --> 03:13.740
This type of MVP is called the One Feature app.

03:13.740 --> 03:17.160
A one feature MVP is exactly what it sounds like,

03:17.160 --> 03:20.250
when you build out literally only one core function

03:20.250 --> 03:21.960
of your product or feature

03:21.960 --> 03:26.160
and then test its reception before slowly adding on more.

03:26.160 --> 03:28.650
To do this well you need to take every function

03:28.650 --> 03:31.860
in your feature, or every feature in your product,

03:31.860 --> 03:33.420
and list them out.

03:33.420 --> 03:36.930
After that, you rank it based on the value to your user.

03:36.930 --> 03:40.260
In 99% of the cases, one function

03:40.260 --> 03:42.630
or one feature is going to stand out

03:42.630 --> 03:46.320
way more than the others in terms of value.

03:46.320 --> 03:48.090
So you just build that.

03:48.090 --> 03:49.320
Remember Foursquare?

03:49.320 --> 03:51.090
It was this app where you could check

03:51.090 --> 03:55.560
into local businesses or locations, you could win prizes,

03:55.560 --> 03:59.250
you could network with other people, gather badges.

03:59.250 --> 04:00.900
You could do about a million different things

04:00.900 --> 04:02.670
that were all location based.

04:02.670 --> 04:05.940
The core of Foursquare's idea though was,

04:05.940 --> 04:10.260
can you guess it, to let people check into their locations

04:10.260 --> 04:11.940
and let their friends know this.

04:11.940 --> 04:14.280
So when they built their MVP

04:14.280 --> 04:15.780
to see if anyone wanted to use this,

04:15.780 --> 04:18.150
they just built literally that function.

04:18.150 --> 04:19.860
You could connect your social networks

04:19.860 --> 04:21.180
and then you could check in.

04:21.180 --> 04:24.300
The check in would send out a text to your social networks.

04:24.300 --> 04:26.610
They didn't add photos, they didn't add hashtags,

04:26.610 --> 04:28.080
they didn't add badges,

04:28.080 --> 04:31.650
because all of these things were really secondary in value

04:31.650 --> 04:33.600
to the checking-in process,

04:33.600 --> 04:36.180
which was the number one thing they cared about.

04:36.180 --> 04:37.110
As a product manager,

04:37.110 --> 04:39.870
you're gonna be constantly making hard decisions

04:39.870 --> 04:42.060
about what to build and whatnot.

04:42.060 --> 04:46.050
It's really no exception in the land of MVPs.

04:46.050 --> 04:48.810
You'll have to gauge your company's tolerance for risk.

04:48.810 --> 04:52.170
Do they care more about resources or more about brand?

04:52.170 --> 04:53.970
And then figure out the best way

04:53.970 --> 04:56.550
to optimize your resource expenditure

04:56.550 --> 04:59.640
with the information that you need to gather.

04:59.640 --> 05:01.770
It's exhausting, but I know you can do it.

05:01.770 --> 05:03.000
So cheer up.

05:03.000 --> 05:04.740
All right, guys, questions or concerns,

05:04.740 --> 05:06.840
just post in the Q&A and we'll get to those.

05:06.840 --> 05:08.790
Otherwise, see you in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.233 --> 00:02.816
(upbeat music)

00:04.260 --> 00:06.120
-: Hey guys, Welcome back to the course.

00:06.120 --> 00:08.910
I hope you didn't run out of gas in this section

00:08.910 --> 00:12.051
because knowing how to design and put together

00:12.051 --> 00:15.960
an effective MVP experiment is gonna be crucial

00:15.960 --> 00:18.360
to being a successful product manager.

00:18.360 --> 00:21.240
As a product manager, you're gonna hear the term MVP

00:21.240 --> 00:23.160
and you're gonna be expected to follow

00:23.160 --> 00:26.430
roughly the same process that we followed in this section.

00:26.430 --> 00:29.130
Coming up with an idea, designing an experiment,

00:29.130 --> 00:31.830
setting up your expectations, and then running a test

00:31.830 --> 00:35.400
is how product managers decide what to add

00:35.400 --> 00:37.140
and what to go forward with.

00:37.140 --> 00:39.990
It's your flashlight in the dark, so to speak.

00:39.990 --> 00:43.170
When you run your experiment and gather your data,

00:43.170 --> 00:45.330
you're going to then take what you have

00:45.330 --> 00:48.480
and compare it to your minimum criteria for success.

00:48.480 --> 00:51.420
If it passes your bar, whoo, congratulations.

00:51.420 --> 00:53.580
If it doesn't, then you need to see

00:53.580 --> 00:56.730
if you can figure out why it didn't work.

00:56.730 --> 00:59.190
Regardless of whether it worked or didn't work,

00:59.190 --> 01:00.900
this is the time to sit down

01:00.900 --> 01:03.300
and evaluate what you need to do next.

01:03.300 --> 01:04.680
One thing that has to be understood

01:04.680 --> 01:06.990
by any entrepreneur or product manager

01:06.990 --> 01:08.580
is that MVP experiments

01:08.580 --> 01:12.690
will primarily return quantitative data.

01:12.690 --> 01:15.480
Quantitative data, if you're not familiar with the term,

01:15.480 --> 01:17.520
is kind of the opposite of what we were doing

01:17.520 --> 01:19.050
when we were interviewing customers

01:19.050 --> 01:20.940
and doing customer development.

01:20.940 --> 01:23.670
Quantitative data is going to be data

01:23.670 --> 01:27.000
that has numbers, numbers that indicate behavior,

01:27.000 --> 01:30.450
maybe percentages of users that did this,

01:30.450 --> 01:33.210
how much time someone spent on your page,

01:33.210 --> 01:35.340
total signups, et cetera, et cetera.

01:35.340 --> 01:37.230
When we were interviewing customers

01:37.230 --> 01:40.380
we were getting what's called qualitative information.

01:40.380 --> 01:43.620
Qualitative information and qualitative data

01:43.620 --> 01:46.110
cannot be expressed in numbers, it's verbal,

01:46.110 --> 01:48.870
it's not a scale from one to 10,

01:48.870 --> 01:52.200
it's a measurement of feeling strongly about something

01:52.200 --> 01:54.240
or who is this user.

01:54.240 --> 01:57.750
And here is a crucial lesson you have to learn.

01:57.750 --> 01:59.850
If you wanna make the right call

01:59.850 --> 02:04.470
you need to make sure you're collecting both types of data.

02:04.470 --> 02:06.287
Numbers will tell you what happened

02:06.287 --> 02:08.700
and it will give you unbiased records

02:08.700 --> 02:11.160
of what your users did or did not do,

02:11.160 --> 02:14.670
but you can and will not know why they did what they did

02:14.670 --> 02:16.710
without also speaking to them.

02:16.710 --> 02:20.400
The what without the why can be incredibly misleading.

02:20.400 --> 02:21.510
Let's say in a hypothetical,

02:21.510 --> 02:23.340
we created a landing page experiment

02:23.340 --> 02:26.280
that targeted hardcore marathon runners

02:26.280 --> 02:29.760
that displayed a brand new shoe

02:29.760 --> 02:32.370
that was designed for running long distances.

02:32.370 --> 02:34.170
When you put it out there into the world

02:34.170 --> 02:35.700
and drove traffic to it,

02:35.700 --> 02:39.540
you see that 14% of users signed up to be notified

02:39.540 --> 02:41.040
of when it gets released.

02:41.040 --> 02:42.960
In fact, some of them on a separate page

02:42.960 --> 02:44.580
actually paid for pre-orders.

02:44.580 --> 02:47.730
Both of those cases you decide those are great numbers

02:47.730 --> 02:49.860
and you give the project a green light.

02:49.860 --> 02:50.880
You build out the shoe,

02:50.880 --> 02:52.710
you build out an advertising campaign

02:52.710 --> 02:54.480
to market it for the first time.

02:54.480 --> 02:57.720
Let's say that you advertise in a marathon running forum,

02:57.720 --> 02:59.880
and of all the people that clicked your page

02:59.880 --> 03:03.120
only 1% ended up buying the shoe.

03:03.120 --> 03:05.220
What exactly happened?

03:05.220 --> 03:07.680
Now, you might think this is an exaggerated case

03:07.680 --> 03:10.020
but it actually happens all the time,

03:10.020 --> 03:13.350
because you've never collected qualitative data

03:13.350 --> 03:16.620
on those 14% of users that signed up

03:16.620 --> 03:19.020
you never found out that the users that signed up

03:19.020 --> 03:21.750
were actually marathon runners at all.

03:21.750 --> 03:23.580
They were casual runners and they bought it

03:23.580 --> 03:25.830
because, hey, they like the design of the shoe.

03:25.830 --> 03:27.240
So, as a product manager,

03:27.240 --> 03:30.600
if you want your MVP experiment to bear fruitful results

03:30.600 --> 03:32.310
you can make sense of,

03:32.310 --> 03:35.310
make sure you incorporate regular conversations

03:35.310 --> 03:38.910
with your users that are involved in your test.

03:38.910 --> 03:40.980
Incentivize them to talk on the phone,

03:40.980 --> 03:42.720
install live chat boxes,

03:42.720 --> 03:44.700
you know now how to get this done

03:44.700 --> 03:46.800
'cause we had a section on customer development.

03:46.800 --> 03:49.530
This is primarily a discovery process

03:49.530 --> 03:51.450
and throughout your experiments

03:51.450 --> 03:54.810
you're gonna find out things that will surprise you.

03:54.810 --> 03:57.120
Maybe your feature ended up being popular,

03:57.120 --> 03:59.160
but not at all by the people

03:59.160 --> 04:00.960
you thought would originally like it,

04:00.960 --> 04:04.950
or maybe your user behavior didn't change the ideal metric

04:04.950 --> 04:08.070
but it did change an entirely different metric.

04:08.070 --> 04:09.750
All of this is gold to you

04:09.750 --> 04:11.520
because you're finding out the truth

04:11.520 --> 04:12.930
about your competitors' needs

04:12.930 --> 04:14.940
and your competitors' preferences.

04:14.940 --> 04:18.540
At the end of this process, though, you need to make a call.

04:18.540 --> 04:21.030
Do you make the feature and give it the green light?

04:21.030 --> 04:22.560
Do you scrap the whole idea

04:22.560 --> 04:24.420
and forget that you ever thought about it?

04:24.420 --> 04:26.670
Or, do you think you should rerun the test

04:26.670 --> 04:29.940
but on a slightly different hypothesis?

04:29.940 --> 04:31.200
These are the decisions you have to make

04:31.200 --> 04:32.490
as a product manager,

04:32.490 --> 04:36.000
but now you know how to run MVP test

04:36.000 --> 04:38.160
which are the primary way

04:38.160 --> 04:40.770
you're gonna get the information you need

04:40.770 --> 04:43.470
to guide you in making those decisions.

04:43.470 --> 04:44.670
All right, guys, congratulations

04:44.670 --> 04:46.950
on finishing the MVP section.

04:46.950 --> 04:49.500
If you guys wanna get more on MVPs,

04:49.500 --> 04:53.250
we have my MVP course that's attached to this.

04:53.250 --> 04:56.550
There's a coupon and lecture for, I think,

04:56.550 --> 04:58.530
go back, check that out.

04:58.530 --> 05:03.530
The MVP course that I teach is a lot longer

05:03.900 --> 05:05.880
and it's a lot more in-depth.

05:05.880 --> 05:08.190
It's focused more on entrepreneurs

05:08.190 --> 05:10.980
but you can still learn a lot

05:10.980 --> 05:14.850
about things that can apply to basic product management.

05:14.850 --> 05:16.500
Like always, guys, questions, concerns,

05:16.500 --> 05:19.533
post in the Q&A, otherwise, see in the next section.

WEBVTT

00:00.180 --> 00:02.370
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course and welcome

00:02.370 --> 00:06.300
to the new section on conceptualizing your ideas.

00:06.300 --> 00:07.650
If you wanna build a great product

00:07.650 --> 00:11.790
you first have to conceptualize it and any great product

00:11.790 --> 00:16.790
is a result of a long process of development and refinement.

00:17.160 --> 00:19.170
No product can stand on its own

00:19.170 --> 00:22.320
without a well thought out and well built foundation.

00:22.320 --> 00:24.869
And with that in mind we're gonna learn

00:24.869 --> 00:27.720
one of the crucial skills for conceptualizing your idea

00:27.720 --> 00:29.070
and communicating it.

00:29.070 --> 00:30.090
Wireframing.

00:30.090 --> 00:31.650
Now, what is a wireframe?

00:31.650 --> 00:33.690
Some of you guys have heard of this before

00:33.690 --> 00:36.300
feel free to skip this lecture, but for those of you

00:36.300 --> 00:38.700
who do not know what it is, a wireframe is

00:38.700 --> 00:42.870
a visual guide for a website, web app,

00:42.870 --> 00:46.770
or mobile app that basically lays out

00:46.770 --> 00:50.940
a rough structure for where the content is gonna go.

00:50.940 --> 00:53.790
When you're making a web app or a mobile app

00:53.790 --> 00:56.430
or just a regular old website

00:56.430 --> 00:59.040
what is the first thing you do

00:59.040 --> 01:02.880
long before you have anything actually built

01:02.880 --> 01:05.310
and anything to actually play with?

01:05.310 --> 01:08.880
Well, the first thing you usually do is you wireframe it.

01:08.880 --> 01:11.160
Now, why do we make wireframes?

01:11.160 --> 01:13.950
Well, when you first come up with your product idea

01:13.950 --> 01:15.420
or your feature idea,

01:15.420 --> 01:18.420
you're in what we call the design phase.

01:18.420 --> 01:22.020
In the design phase, everything's kind of up in the air.

01:22.020 --> 01:24.900
Feature ideas are thrown around.

01:24.900 --> 01:27.180
You don't know exactly what's gonna go in it.

01:27.180 --> 01:30.420
It's very abstract, it's very amorphous.

01:30.420 --> 01:33.900
Wireframing is usually the first step you will take

01:33.900 --> 01:37.470
in corralling that information that's in everyone's head

01:37.470 --> 01:39.660
and what's been communicated only verbally

01:39.660 --> 01:40.860
up until this point,

01:40.860 --> 01:44.040
onto something that is definite and precise.

01:44.040 --> 01:47.070
Think of it as the first rough translation

01:47.070 --> 01:48.480
of a product idea.

01:48.480 --> 01:50.430
And specifically when you start wireframing

01:50.430 --> 01:52.320
