you're gonna start wireframing with what we call

01:52.320 --> 01:54.660
a low fidelity wireframe.

01:54.660 --> 01:58.770
Low fidelity in this case, just meaning not very exact,

01:58.770 --> 02:02.310
not a lot of detail, not very precise.

02:02.310 --> 02:06.330
So it's fidelity being accuracy, low, low accuracy.

02:06.330 --> 02:08.040
These versions are very basic.

02:08.040 --> 02:10.740
They're created really quickly and they're really just

02:10.740 --> 02:15.740
meant to test broad concepts and define the overall vision

02:15.840 --> 02:17.130
for what we're trying to build.

02:17.130 --> 02:19.830
The idea here is that we're gonna paint with broad strokes

02:19.830 --> 02:21.900
and this is gonna allow us to then go

02:21.900 --> 02:25.800
and do some user research, collect some feedback internally,

02:25.800 --> 02:29.220
as well as from potential users.

02:29.220 --> 02:33.600
And then over time we can slowly define more and more

02:33.600 --> 02:35.310
of the product or feature.

02:35.310 --> 02:38.310
We can really start to figure out what it does,

02:38.310 --> 02:40.560
how it does it, and what it looks like.

02:40.560 --> 02:43.060
Generally, teams have a very iterative process

02:44.048 --> 02:47.100
with wireframing, and as they gather more and more feedback,

02:47.100 --> 02:49.380
they're gonna add more and more fidelity.

02:49.380 --> 02:53.130
They're gonna add more detail to the actual mockup.

02:53.130 --> 02:56.670
We'll go from things like broad sketches of a circle

02:56.670 --> 03:01.080
that just says button, to circle that actually has shape

03:01.080 --> 03:03.660
that says sign up, or whatever the button's actually

03:03.660 --> 03:07.980
supposed to do, to a button that is actually designed.

03:07.980 --> 03:11.580
You'll go from things like writing a scribble text

03:11.580 --> 03:14.070
like Lorem Ipsum or just gibberish,

03:14.070 --> 03:18.270
to writing out a rough outline of what the content

03:18.270 --> 03:21.720
should say, to writing out the actual finished copywriting

03:21.720 --> 03:23.070
that should go on the website.

03:23.070 --> 03:24.999
You'll go through that process

03:24.999 --> 03:27.240
and eventually you'll land at the top of the high fidelity

03:27.240 --> 03:31.140
curve and your mockups at that point,

03:31.140 --> 03:33.150
if you took a glance at them you might not know

03:33.150 --> 03:35.250
that they were not functional.

03:35.250 --> 03:38.070
They'll have graphic design, they'll have the layout

03:38.070 --> 03:41.280
and the information pretty much decided.

03:41.280 --> 03:44.940
Now, do product managers themselves wireframe?

03:44.940 --> 03:47.280
Well, it actually just depends.

03:47.280 --> 03:50.610
If you're on a smaller team like a startup,

03:50.610 --> 03:54.630
you probably will be required to do some wireframing

03:54.630 --> 03:56.430
and that's just because small teams are gonna wear

03:56.430 --> 03:58.950
a lot more hats and it's kind of

03:58.950 --> 04:01.470
an all hands on deck type situation.

04:01.470 --> 04:03.570
If you can do it, they're gonna ask you to do it.

04:03.570 --> 04:06.300
Now, as you move up organizations and they get larger

04:06.300 --> 04:09.600
and larger and larger, your role's gonna become more defined

04:09.600 --> 04:11.730
and it's gonna become more narrow.

04:11.730 --> 04:14.580
In those types situations, you might not be asked

04:14.580 --> 04:17.790
to wireframe, it kind of just depends on the company

04:17.790 --> 04:21.150
but you will need familiarity with wireframing.

04:21.150 --> 04:23.070
Typically what's gonna happen with a product manager

04:23.070 --> 04:25.620
in a larger company is that you will be asked

04:25.620 --> 04:28.530
to sit down with graphic designers

04:28.530 --> 04:32.100
and together create wireframes and you will have

04:32.100 --> 04:34.889
to contribute to it but you won't have to be an expert

04:34.889 --> 04:38.700
or be super polished with UX skill.

04:38.700 --> 04:41.640
Another common scenario is that you might yourself

04:41.640 --> 04:45.300
have to sketch out an idea that you wanna propose

04:45.300 --> 04:47.340
because you don't want to take up resources

04:47.340 --> 04:50.970
from other people who can make it in a more fidelity way.

04:50.970 --> 04:53.640
So if you wanna be effective as a product manager

04:53.640 --> 04:57.930
or as an entrepreneur in web or mobile technology

04:57.930 --> 05:02.444
you need to know how to communicate complex feature

05:02.444 --> 05:04.140
and product ideas.

05:04.140 --> 05:06.120
And wireframing is the way we do that.

05:06.120 --> 05:08.340
You don't have to be a UX guru

05:08.340 --> 05:10.860
you don't have to grow a goatee or anything

05:10.860 --> 05:13.470
but you will be expected to know the basics

05:13.470 --> 05:16.230
and maybe in a pinch, whip up some wireframes yourself.

05:16.230 --> 05:18.210
So in the first half of the section, we're gonna focus

05:18.210 --> 05:20.970
on getting you up to speed with wireframes.

05:20.970 --> 05:24.540
Show you how with a very little time investment,

05:24.540 --> 05:26.850
get in, get out, and learn to be dangerous.

05:26.850 --> 05:29.130
And a worst case scenario, you now have a skill

05:29.130 --> 05:31.440
that you can impress your coworkers with

05:31.440 --> 05:33.570
and you don't have to bug people every time

05:33.570 --> 05:35.160
you wanna draft something up.

05:35.160 --> 05:36.923
All right, see you in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.210 --> 00:02.370
-: Hey guys. Welcome back to the course.

00:02.370 --> 00:04.440
So last lecture, I introduced you

00:04.440 --> 00:06.690
to the concept of wireframing.

00:06.690 --> 00:09.480
Why we do it, what it is,

00:09.480 --> 00:12.570
and how often are you going to use it

00:12.570 --> 00:15.990
as a product manager, and under what circumstances.

00:15.990 --> 00:18.000
Now that you're comfortable with wireframes,

00:18.000 --> 00:21.000
we can actually introduce more concepts now

00:21.000 --> 00:23.400
that are related to wireframes.

00:23.400 --> 00:26.670
wireframes are kind of the entry point

00:26.670 --> 00:29.520
if you're wanting to conceptualize your product idea

00:29.520 --> 00:30.540
or communicate it.

00:30.540 --> 00:34.500
It's the most ubiquitous way of communicating.

00:34.500 --> 00:38.790
But wireframes can have more detail,

00:38.790 --> 00:41.220
they can have more interaction,

00:41.220 --> 00:44.070
and they can do all sorts of different things.

00:44.070 --> 00:47.280
At which point we actually call them something else?

00:47.280 --> 00:49.230
And that's the title of this lecture,

00:49.230 --> 00:53.040
which is wireframe, mockup and prototype.

00:53.040 --> 00:53.873
As a product manager

00:53.873 --> 00:55.590
you need to understand what makes them different,

00:55.590 --> 00:58.440
what tools you'll use for each one,

00:58.440 --> 01:00.900
and in general, how do they relate to each other?

01:00.900 --> 01:03.180
And now let's first talk about wireframes,

01:03.180 --> 01:07.470
and I'm gonna explain this on a scale of fidelity.

01:07.470 --> 01:09.360
Remember the concept of fidelity.

01:09.360 --> 01:11.820
Fidelity meaning accuracy.

01:11.820 --> 01:14.520
Wireframes are at the far left

01:14.520 --> 01:16.650
and they're the first thing we start with,

01:16.650 --> 01:17.820
because they're the simplest,

01:17.820 --> 01:19.140
they're also called skeletons

01:19.140 --> 01:21.420
because they're very, very simple.

01:21.420 --> 01:22.980
The way I like to think of wireframes

01:22.980 --> 01:26.220
is that they're like the blueprint of your design.

01:26.220 --> 01:28.950
You're almost always gonna make these first,

01:28.950 --> 01:30.210
and the reason why you do it

01:30.210 --> 01:33.060
is because you wanna tackle some basic questions

01:33.060 --> 01:35.700
around structure, layout, and organization

01:35.700 --> 01:39.480
before your team iterates on visual details.

01:39.480 --> 01:42.240
Wireframes are on the low fidelity side,

01:42.240 --> 01:46.950
we are gonna worry about technical and visual detail later,

01:46.950 --> 01:49.860
there's a time and place for it, and it is not now.

01:49.860 --> 01:52.920
Visuals and specifications and technical details

01:52.920 --> 01:54.810
are very important,

01:54.810 --> 01:56.880
but you wanna make sure that you have

01:56.880 --> 02:00.420
the structure and the layout hammered down first.

02:00.420 --> 02:03.150
Some of the tools we'll use in wireframing

02:03.150 --> 02:04.410
might be Balsamic,

02:04.410 --> 02:07.500
and we're gonna use Balsamic in this course.

02:07.500 --> 02:12.210
Balsamic is incredibly popular for low fidelity wireframes,

02:12.210 --> 02:15.540
because it allows you to make them very quickly

02:15.540 --> 02:19.710
in a mock kind of sketch fashion that looks cool,

02:19.710 --> 02:21.120
it's fun to play with,

02:21.120 --> 02:23.820
and people generally respond positively to it.

02:23.820 --> 02:26.910
Another popular tool used for wireframing is Axure.

02:26.910 --> 02:30.270
If I can remember how to draw the logo.

02:30.270 --> 02:33.720
Sometimes I get confused with the Windows Azure,

02:33.720 --> 02:35.460
this is Axure with an X.

02:35.460 --> 02:37.890
Axure is a big application.

02:37.890 --> 02:41.010
Oftentimes you have it at the enterprise level

02:41.010 --> 02:43.710
because it allows teams to collaborate,

02:43.710 --> 02:46.590
and it has a lot of features baked into it.

02:46.590 --> 02:48.990
It's by no means a lightweight solution.

02:48.990 --> 02:51.390
Another one is OmniGraffle

02:51.390 --> 02:52.800
That one is still used,

02:52.800 --> 02:57.090
I'm not entirely sure why, it's kind of clunky,

02:57.090 --> 02:58.710
but it has some interesting features.

02:58.710 --> 03:00.030
Just usually skip that one.

03:00.030 --> 03:02.940
HotGloo is another example of a popular wireframing tool.

03:02.940 --> 03:03.773
I mentioned this one

03:03.773 --> 03:07.080
because this one is actually browser-based only.

03:07.080 --> 03:10.350
You'll notice this with a lot of tools

03:10.350 --> 03:12.720
for conceptualizing your product.

03:12.720 --> 03:14.580
A lot of them are browser-based, why?

03:14.580 --> 03:16.470
Well, it makes it more convenient

03:16.470 --> 03:18.450
for you to store your information with them

03:18.450 --> 03:21.660
and not on your hard drive where something can go wrong,

03:21.660 --> 03:23.910
and also you can then access that information

03:23.910 --> 03:25.680
anywhere you are.

03:25.680 --> 03:28.680
It's also much easier to share that way,

03:28.680 --> 03:31.440
share your wireframes with your teammates

03:31.440 --> 03:34.020
without having to send them an email attachment,

03:34.020 --> 03:36.270
which are notorious for just sucking.

03:36.270 --> 03:37.830
And last one I'm gonna mention is POP,

03:37.830 --> 03:39.840
which we're actually gonna use in this course.

03:39.840 --> 03:41.280
It's really, really cool.

03:41.280 --> 03:42.810
Check out that lecture.

03:42.810 --> 03:46.170
POP allows you to take sketches that you've made

03:46.170 --> 03:49.680
and turn them into digital copies,

03:49.680 --> 03:51.210
and then you can manipulate them.

03:51.210 --> 03:53.280
All right, now, what generally happens after

03:53.280 --> 03:55.200
we have wireframes.

03:55.200 --> 03:58.470
Well, that's the concept of a mockup.

03:58.470 --> 04:01.380
Now, mockups can confuse all the time

04:01.380 --> 04:04.440
with wireframes, with prototypes.

04:04.440 --> 04:05.580
A lot of people don't even know

04:05.580 --> 04:07.290
what a mockup necessarily is.

04:07.290 --> 04:09.810
Mockups are actually static displays

04:09.810 --> 04:13.650
of what the final product should visually look like.

04:13.650 --> 04:14.850
The reason why we use mockups

04:14.850 --> 04:17.910
is because it gives you the opportunity to make decisions

04:17.910 --> 04:22.560
about things like colors, typography, and in general style.

04:22.560 --> 04:23.910
To give you visual representation

04:23.910 --> 04:26.490
I'll draw the same version of the wireframe,

04:26.490 --> 04:28.110
but now I'm gonna add in color,

04:28.110 --> 04:30.360
and I'm gonna add in some people

04:30.360 --> 04:32.520
that we can just imagine or photos.

04:32.520 --> 04:35.220
I'm able to fill in the buttons and what they look like.

04:35.220 --> 04:36.960
And we already know the structure,

04:36.960 --> 04:40.440
now I'm just filling in kind of the pixel specific details.

04:40.440 --> 04:41.273
Now, if you ever have trouble

04:41.273 --> 04:43.320
remembering what tools to use for mockups,

04:43.320 --> 04:46.380
well, remember who generally does a mockup.

04:46.380 --> 04:49.800
Generally, the mockups are gonna be done by the designers.

04:49.800 --> 04:53.220
Designers are the ones that are in charge of visual detail,

04:53.220 --> 04:56.190
and so this portion of the process makes sense for them

04:56.190 --> 04:58.230
to be the ones kind of at the helm.

04:58.230 --> 04:59.430
And what a designers use?

04:59.430 --> 05:01.650
Typically, they're gonna use Photoshop.

05:01.650 --> 05:04.980
Photoshop is incredibly powerful,

05:04.980 --> 05:09.840
kind of clunky when it comes to making mockups quickly,

05:09.840 --> 05:13.800
but the vast majority of designers still use that.

05:13.800 --> 05:15.150
Another option is Sketch.

05:15.150 --> 05:17.760
Something that's been gaining a lot of popularity,

05:17.760 --> 05:20.700
kind of as a competitor to Photoshop,

05:20.700 --> 05:24.090
allows you to draw things faster.

05:24.090 --> 05:25.380
That's kind of their focus.

05:25.380 --> 05:28.110
It's the final cut pro to the Adobe Premier,

05:28.110 --> 05:30.300
if you guys are in video, and that means anything to you.

05:30.300 --> 05:31.500
Also not to be left out,

05:31.500 --> 05:34.470
a lot of designers will do their mockups in Illustrator.

05:34.470 --> 05:37.140
That is a legitimate way of doing it,

05:37.140 --> 05:39.420
kind of clunky because Illustrator forces you

05:39.420 --> 05:41.760
to make your designs in vector,

05:41.760 --> 05:43.080
which basically means

05:43.080 --> 05:46.110
that you're gonna be drawing your designs

05:46.110 --> 05:51.110
and the designs are only going to be held in space

05:51.540 --> 05:55.530
relative to the other points of the design.

05:55.530 --> 05:58.320
I know that's kind of confusing what Vector means,

05:58.320 --> 05:59.850
but think of it like this.

05:59.850 --> 06:02.790
Photoshop is pixel based, Illustrator is vector.

06:02.790 --> 06:06.090
Photoshop, when you draw something, you draw something,

06:06.090 --> 06:08.880
and when you stretch an image it stretches out

06:08.880 --> 06:10.470
and sometimes it gets blurry.

06:10.470 --> 06:13.230
But with Illustrator, because it's vector,

06:13.230 --> 06:16.320
there are no hard to find dots in the image,

06:16.320 --> 06:18.510
it redefines the image as you stretch it,

06:18.510 --> 06:21.390
because the image is only concerned with the relationship

06:21.390 --> 06:24.450
between where this point is and where that point is.

06:24.450 --> 06:25.980
That was totally extra, guys.

06:25.980 --> 06:27.330
I'll put some stuff in the resources

06:27.330 --> 06:29.100
if you wanna read more about that.

06:29.100 --> 06:32.850
Another tool you can use and back around to is Axure, again.

06:32.850 --> 06:34.380
And another one called UXPin.

06:34.380 --> 06:38.710
Now, I added these ones because they allow you to wireframe,

06:38.710 --> 06:40.290
at least Axure does,

06:40.290 --> 06:43.320
but Axure also has an image library and templates

06:43.320 --> 06:45.210
that you can easily drag and drop

06:45.210 --> 06:49.230
into your wireframe to convert them into mockups.

06:49.230 --> 06:52.620
All right, and then once you have the design hammered out,

06:52.620 --> 06:55.110
what do you usually do then afterwards?

06:55.110 --> 06:58.770
Well, now that you know the visual design looks like this,

06:58.770 --> 07:00.570
the structure looks like this,

07:00.570 --> 07:05.250
the layout is gonna be exactly the way you have it on paper,

07:05.250 --> 07:06.990
now you add interaction.

07:06.990 --> 07:09.360
Now, if wireframes handle structure,

07:09.360 --> 07:11.280
and markups handle visuals,

07:11.280 --> 07:12.810
what do prototypes handle?

07:12.810 --> 07:14.910
They handle usability.

07:14.910 --> 07:16.920
Prototyping is actually the first phase

07:16.920 --> 07:20.040
where you can play with what you've created,

07:20.040 --> 07:22.920
even if it's in a very limited regard.

07:22.920 --> 07:26.130
Prototypes allow you to explore the user interface

07:26.130 --> 07:27.750
that you've put together,

07:27.750 --> 07:32.160
start looking for potential problems in the user flow,

07:32.160 --> 07:35.910
and get a decent idea of what you think is gonna work best.

07:35.910 --> 07:38.490
Now, for a product manager, prototypes are essential

07:38.490 --> 07:41.310
if you wanna do things like usability research.

07:41.310 --> 07:44.430
It's one thing to show a potential customer,

07:44.430 --> 07:46.680
or someone you're doing a user test

07:46.680 --> 07:49.830
with a mockup or a wireframe,

07:49.830 --> 07:51.600
but it's an entirely different thing

07:51.600 --> 07:53.100
if you show them a prototype,

07:53.100 --> 07:55.260
a prototype can show you what they think of it

07:55.260 --> 07:56.610
and how they interact with it,

07:56.610 --> 07:57.670
with the other two

07:59.280 --> 08:01.680
you just only get their first impressions.

08:01.680 --> 08:03.780
The best part of prototypes is it allows teams

08:03.780 --> 08:05.460
to do usability research.

08:05.460 --> 08:08.010
If you're an entrepreneur, prototypes are pretty cool

08:08.010 --> 08:10.140
because a lot of times that's all you need

08:10.140 --> 08:12.420
to recruit people and raise money.

08:12.420 --> 08:14.220
But keep in mind guys, prototypes,

08:14.220 --> 08:17.610
and when I say prototype in the web and mobile world,

08:17.610 --> 08:19.920
I mean something that I can interact with,

08:19.920 --> 08:22.680
but that interaction is gonna be very limited,

08:22.680 --> 08:25.440
it's basically going to be hot spots

08:25.440 --> 08:28.560
where I press something and it takes me to a new page

08:28.560 --> 08:33.150
or maybe, maybe, I can do some basic gestures

08:33.150 --> 08:35.010
if it's a mobile prototype.

08:35.010 --> 08:37.230
Now, a very popular tool for making prototypes,

08:37.230 --> 08:39.270
believe it or not, it's Keynote.

08:39.270 --> 08:40.890
If you use Keynote,

08:40.890 --> 08:44.790
which is the Mac comparable to PowerPoint,

08:44.790 --> 08:48.300
and you can use templates and libraries like Keynotopia.

08:48.300 --> 08:49.590
What you can do is you can make

08:49.590 --> 08:52.320
a lot of images on the slides

08:52.320 --> 08:54.570
and then connect the slides to other slides,

08:54.570 --> 08:57.210
so if I click a button on the Keynote page

08:57.210 --> 08:59.550
it links to another Keynote page.

08:59.550 --> 09:03.000
And with Keynote you can load that onto a mobile phone

09:03.000 --> 09:05.490
or onto a web browser.

09:05.490 --> 09:07.380
Pretty crafty I might add,

09:07.380 --> 09:10.590
but keep in mind that you're gonna be very limited

09:10.590 --> 09:12.660
by how much interaction you can add with Keynote.

09:12.660 --> 09:14.100
Another one is a POP.

09:14.100 --> 09:16.320
Now look at POP, how it was a wireframe tool,

09:16.320 --> 09:18.030
and it's a prototyping tool.

09:18.030 --> 09:20.340
Well, because it allows us to add interactions

09:20.340 --> 09:21.750
to our wireframes.

09:21.750 --> 09:23.520
It skips the visual step,

09:23.520 --> 09:27.060
so we're just working with skeletons, literal wireframes,

09:27.060 --> 09:30.600
and moving them around and adding basic interactions.

09:30.600 --> 09:33.210
Other ones to be aware of again, our Axure.

09:33.210 --> 09:35.130
Axure is a massive product,

09:35.130 --> 09:38.250
it handles all three of these stages.

09:38.250 --> 09:40.890
No wonder it's decently popular.

09:40.890 --> 09:43.560
Proto.io is one that's quite popular.

09:43.560 --> 09:44.393
It's worth mentioning

09:44.393 --> 09:47.160
because it focuses solely on mobile apps.

09:47.160 --> 09:49.980
If you're doing mobile apps, look into Proto.io.

09:49.980 --> 09:51.570
And the last one is Envision.

09:51.570 --> 09:55.620
And Envision is another massive product, very popular,

09:55.620 --> 09:59.160
that allows you to create multiple layers of interactions

09:59.160 --> 10:02.760
into a page that you can import mockups into.

10:02.760 --> 10:04.980
Envision gets my vote for prototyping

10:04.980 --> 10:07.440
because it's probably the best I've used.

10:07.440 --> 10:09.810
All right, you now know what's a wireframe,

10:09.810 --> 10:11.730
what's a mockup and what's a prototype.

10:11.730 --> 10:14.430
You know that they're related to each other,

10:14.430 --> 10:16.740
and that wireframes are the least accurate,

10:16.740 --> 10:17.880
the lowest fidelity,

10:17.880 --> 10:19.440
mockups are in the middle,

10:19.440 --> 10:21.450
and prototypes have the most fidelity,

10:21.450 --> 10:24.120
because they have interaction

10:24.120 --> 10:26.940
and they look exactly what the finished product

10:26.940 --> 10:28.440
is probably gonna be.

10:28.440 --> 10:32.070
Wireframes are structure, mockups are for visuals,

10:32.070 --> 10:36.060
and prototypes are for usability and interactions.

10:36.060 --> 10:37.890
Keep this in mind 'cause I just saved you

10:37.890 --> 10:40.260
a whole lot of hurt for your first meeting

10:40.260 --> 10:43.800
when you walk in and you call a wireframe a mockup,

10:43.800 --> 10:45.540
and someone just slaps you.

10:45.540 --> 10:46.830
All right, guys, in the next section,

10:46.830 --> 10:49.950
we're actually gonna get started with sketching,

10:49.950 --> 10:51.570
so we're gonna jump right into it.

10:51.570 --> 10:54.720
I'm gonna talk to you about what sketching is,

10:54.720 --> 10:56.003
how does it relate to wireframes,

10:56.003 --> 10:57.690
it is a wireframe,

10:57.690 --> 11:01.102
and how you can do it now easily in five minutes.

11:01.102 --> 11:02.302
All right, see you then.

WEBVTT

00:00.120 --> 00:02.460
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:02.460 --> 00:05.130
So in the last lecture we learned what wire framing is

00:05.130 --> 00:06.690
and hopefully you remember what that is.

00:06.690 --> 00:08.220
If you don't remember, well,

00:08.220 --> 00:11.670
they're visual mock-ups of a potential website,

00:11.670 --> 00:14.190
mobile app, or even just a feature.

00:14.190 --> 00:16.020
And they're these things that we sketch out

00:16.020 --> 00:17.730
and they look like wires

00:17.730 --> 00:21.270
and we do them because it helps us communicate the

00:21.270 --> 00:23.100
vision of the product.

00:23.100 --> 00:25.890
It helps us make sure that everyone's on the same page,

00:25.890 --> 00:28.440
and it allows us to get feedback internally

00:28.440 --> 00:30.300
and from potential users.

00:30.300 --> 00:32.340
But how do you start wire framing?

00:32.340 --> 00:33.960
Let's say you wanted to make a wire frame.

00:33.960 --> 00:36.300
How would you actually start? Do you need

00:36.300 --> 00:39.300
thousands of dollars of software? No.

00:39.300 --> 00:41.610
Do you need thousands of dollars of equipment?

00:41.610 --> 00:42.870
No, actually, you don't.

00:42.870 --> 00:45.450
The easiest way to start wire framing is available

00:45.450 --> 00:48.570
to literally anyone, it's a lot more simple than you think.

00:48.570 --> 00:52.170
It's literally just pen and paper. That's what we do.

00:52.170 --> 00:55.140
I'm not actually joking when product managers,

00:55.140 --> 00:57.870
when UX specialist graphic designers,

00:57.870 --> 00:59.670
project managers, you name it.

00:59.670 --> 01:02.700
When they wanna start, and actually draft out

01:02.700 --> 01:04.540
their first wire frame, they reach for

01:04.540 --> 01:07.710
a pencil, or pen, and paper.

01:07.710 --> 01:09.900
Google does this, they sketch.

01:09.900 --> 01:11.940
Microsoft, they do it as well, they sketch.

01:11.940 --> 01:14.310
Facebook does the same thing. Pretty much

01:14.310 --> 01:17.100
everyone starts by drawing it out.

01:17.100 --> 01:20.370
Now, why is that? Well, because

01:20.370 --> 01:21.420
started wire framing,

01:21.420 --> 01:24.960
the idea here is we wanna do it very quickly.

01:24.960 --> 01:28.860
Supposed to be a rapid task. We don't want it to take

01:28.860 --> 01:31.350
a lotta time, we don't want people to

01:31.350 --> 01:35.190
think it's real, or think that it's well defined,

01:35.190 --> 01:36.480
or something that we've been working on

01:36.480 --> 01:38.280
for a long period of time. We also

01:38.280 --> 01:40.410
really don't wanna get attached to it,

01:40.410 --> 01:42.450
so when you write it out on paper,

01:42.450 --> 01:44.550
you're more likely to be able to let it go

01:44.550 --> 01:47.040
and throw it away, if you need to pivot

01:47.040 --> 01:47.970
or try something else.

01:47.970 --> 01:50.250
At this stage of the game, your idea is the loosest,

01:50.250 --> 01:52.920
it's the least defined, and we wanna stay flexible,

01:52.920 --> 01:55.620
so we use something that's very temporary.

01:55.620 --> 01:58.440
So, you guys at home, grab a pen or pencil,

01:58.440 --> 02:01.770
and piece of paper, and let's draw some wire frames.

02:01.770 --> 02:04.020
Can't draw? Well, guess what, neither can I.

02:04.020 --> 02:05.940
I've never been able to draw anything

02:05.940 --> 02:08.460
more complicated than a stick figure. True story.

02:08.460 --> 02:11.640
So, we're standing here, looking at a blank sheet of paper.

02:11.640 --> 02:13.800
We know what wire frames look like, right?

02:13.800 --> 02:16.980
We also know what websites look like,

02:16.980 --> 02:19.470
but that doesn't stop us from getting this kinda

02:19.470 --> 02:23.010
deer in the headlights effect. The possibilities

02:23.010 --> 02:25.740
are almost too infinite, what do I even start with?

02:25.740 --> 02:27.660
Don't worry, I'm gonna walk you through

02:27.660 --> 02:31.830
a bulletproof way of getting out of that first

02:31.830 --> 02:35.910
kind of analysis paralysis, and it's really quite easy.

02:35.910 --> 02:38.070
So ask yourself the question, what is something that,

02:38.070 --> 02:41.280
no matter what you draw, you're gonna have to

02:41.280 --> 02:43.710
actually put down on the paper?

02:43.710 --> 02:45.660
If you guessed browser, you are correct.

02:45.660 --> 02:48.720
So, actually, let's just draw out a browser,

02:48.720 --> 02:51.150
'cause that'll help us get something on the paper,

02:51.150 --> 02:53.610
and all of a sudden the paper's went from this big,

02:53.610 --> 02:57.420
to a very smaller section, much more manageable.

02:57.420 --> 02:59.880
So, draw out your browser, you guys can pick

02:59.880 --> 03:02.730
whatever browser you want. I'm gonna pick Chrome,

03:02.730 --> 03:04.680
and I'm gonna put the three little colored dots

03:04.680 --> 03:07.710
in the top left corner, put some tabs, and put,

03:07.710 --> 03:09.480
you know, a search box and pretty much

03:09.480 --> 03:10.950
everything else you see on Chrome.

03:10.950 --> 03:15.000
Doesn't it feel, all of a sudden, kind of less overwhelming?

03:15.000 --> 03:17.269
I've been doing this for years and for some reason,

03:17.269 --> 03:20.040
it always feels better after I draw the browser.

03:20.040 --> 03:22.380
So, again, now what do we do? We have a browser,

03:22.380 --> 03:24.540
but we have to put stuff inside the browser.

03:24.540 --> 03:26.220
Well, what's another thing that you

03:26.220 --> 03:29.430
probably will have to draw out regardless?

03:29.430 --> 03:32.550
Well, I'll give you a hint. Most websites are gonna have

03:32.550 --> 03:35.910
a logo of some sort, and they're probably gonna have

03:35.910 --> 03:38.250
a navigation bar. So, draw those out,

03:38.250 --> 03:40.100
just put the logo in the top left corner,

03:40.100 --> 03:41.100
or top right corner,

03:41.100 --> 03:43.140
and draw a little navigation bar.

03:43.140 --> 03:44.610
And the navigation bar, you can say, like,

03:44.610 --> 03:46.140
one of them's home, and then just leave

03:46.140 --> 03:47.970
empty spaces for other things.

03:47.970 --> 03:49.470
Now, I know this is a little traditional,

03:49.470 --> 03:51.630
a lot of websites now don't have navigation bars,

03:51.630 --> 03:56.310
or they're floating text, but for the most part,

03:56.310 --> 03:59.040
we all have some form of this.

03:59.040 --> 04:01.470
Alright, so it's starting to look like something.

04:01.470 --> 04:04.020
Now, at this point, you just need to close your eyes,

04:04.020 --> 04:09.020
and try to envision what you imagine it looking like.

04:09.150 --> 04:12.540
Open your eyes, look back at the wire frame,

04:12.540 --> 04:14.940
and you probably still don't have any idea,

04:14.940 --> 04:16.680
so we're gonna move on to the next thing.

04:16.680 --> 04:18.210
Alright, the next thing you need to do,

04:18.210 --> 04:19.860
and this is probably something you've answered

04:19.860 --> 04:23.610
in your head, but you haven't yet written it out

04:23.610 --> 04:27.300
or consciously done this, is write out the question,

04:27.300 --> 04:31.740
and answer the question, what is the point of this page?

04:31.740 --> 04:35.520
Why are users on this page? What is their goal

04:35.520 --> 04:39.180
and what do you think their outcome of being here is?

04:39.180 --> 04:42.180
Try not to overthink this. Write it out in

04:42.180 --> 04:43.950
the simplest way you can.

04:43.950 --> 04:45.420
It's probably gonna be something along the lines of,

04:45.420 --> 04:48.150
it's gonna be to let users do

04:48.150 --> 04:49.200
something, something, something.

04:49.200 --> 04:50.250
Alright, so that was easy,

04:50.250 --> 04:52.830
so let's move on to the next exercise.

04:52.830 --> 04:56.700
Write out users should be able to do,

04:56.700 --> 04:58.350
and answer that question to yourself.

04:58.350 --> 05:00.240
Once you've figured this part out,

05:00.240 --> 05:01.980
you're pretty much halfway there.

05:01.980 --> 05:04.890
So, try to think about what are all the things

05:04.890 --> 05:08.610
that your user should be able to do on this page.

05:08.610 --> 05:12.270
It's different than the primary function of the page,

05:12.270 --> 05:14.250
which is the main point of why they're there,

05:14.250 --> 05:16.020
but there're also secondary things

05:16.020 --> 05:17.370
they should be able to do.

05:17.370 --> 05:19.440
Let's call these functions and write them out.

05:19.440 --> 05:21.540
Don't worry if you miss something,

05:21.540 --> 05:23.040
you're always gonna miss something,

05:23.040 --> 05:26.220
and later it'll come to you. These are sketches,

05:26.220 --> 05:28.260
they're not supposed to be taken that seriously.

05:28.260 --> 05:31.740
Alright, so let's say that we're building a page that

05:31.740 --> 05:35.670
shows users a lot of different videos that they can watch.

05:35.670 --> 05:37.650
So, let's answer the question, what's the actual

05:37.650 --> 05:40.770
point of that. Well, the point of that would be to let

05:40.770 --> 05:45.180
users see a thumbnail of all of the available videos,

05:45.180 --> 05:47.640
such that they can choose which video they wanna watch.

05:47.640 --> 05:49.890
So, I would just draw that out.

05:49.890 --> 05:52.380
I'm just gonna draw some boxes, that says video,

05:52.380 --> 05:55.020
I'm gonna put video, video, video on it.

05:55.020 --> 05:57.000
And that satisfies the first question.

05:57.000 --> 05:59.460
I now have established visually

05:59.460 --> 06:01.320
what the point of the page is.

06:01.320 --> 06:03.930
Then move on to the next one, function one.

06:03.930 --> 06:06.360
What was the first function, well,

06:06.360 --> 06:09.600
let's just take a guess. I would guess that

06:09.600 --> 06:13.800
maybe users need to like certain videos.

06:13.800 --> 06:15.990
And some way of communicating that,

06:15.990 --> 06:17.370
you know, they like it, so

06:17.370 --> 06:21.990
let's draw little hearts onto the bottoms of the thumbnails.

06:21.990 --> 06:24.360
Alright, good, so let's check off that function.

06:24.360 --> 06:26.940
Now let's do function number two.

06:26.940 --> 06:29.940
Let's say users probably should be able search.

06:29.940 --> 06:31.890
They should probably be able to search that list,

06:31.890 --> 06:34.080
it shouldn't just be a random list

06:34.080 --> 06:36.780
that they don't get to curate or play with,

06:36.780 --> 06:38.880
so let's put a search box. It's really simple,

06:38.880 --> 06:42.120
it's just an oval with a little symbol in the middle.

06:42.120 --> 06:43.770
Great, we satisfied function number two.

06:43.770 --> 06:44.850
Function number three,

06:44.850 --> 06:47.850
let's say that we need to add pagination, as in

06:47.850 --> 06:49.620
there can be a lotta search results, so

06:49.620 --> 06:51.900
we probably assume users might look at

06:51.900 --> 06:53.640
page two and page three and page four

06:53.640 --> 06:57.210
or decide how many of the thumbnails to display in a page.

06:57.210 --> 06:58.980
So, I'll put up at the top right,

06:58.980 --> 07:01.500
same way that you see it on all websites,

07:01.500 --> 07:03.630
show, you know, ten results,

07:03.630 --> 07:05.700
twenty results, or thirty results, et cetera.

07:05.700 --> 07:08.460
Now, if you get stuck at any point in this,

07:08.460 --> 07:11.610
you're thinking, my familiarity with the web or mobile

07:11.610 --> 07:14.520
is just not good enough to do this, my suggestion is to

07:14.520 --> 07:16.650
always just hop online and start

07:16.650 --> 07:18.540
playing around with some major websites.

07:18.540 --> 07:20.760
Go to the websites that you go to all the time,

07:20.760 --> 07:22.830
play around for a long enough time

07:22.830 --> 07:24.990
and you'll probably run into a function

07:24.990 --> 07:27.360
or a page that has similarities.

07:27.360 --> 07:30.420
That'll get the creative juices going and a lot of times

07:30.420 --> 07:31.860
you can just outright copy them.

07:31.860 --> 07:33.210
If that doesn't seem to work for you,

07:33.210 --> 07:36.390
try to think really hard of a website that you know of

07:36.390 --> 07:39.570
that has the same solution or problem

07:39.570 --> 07:42.600
that you're trying to solve, and if you can think of that

07:42.600 --> 07:45.720
then maybe you can find a website that might have

07:45.720 --> 07:48.600
that function, and worse case scenario,

07:48.600 --> 07:50.940
I always just go to my favorite website Dribbble,

07:50.940 --> 07:55.050
D R I, three Bs, L E dot com, and it's just a website

07:55.050 --> 07:58.320
where graphic designers put up really

07:58.320 --> 08:01.350
good designs of web layouts,

08:01.350 --> 08:02.520
buttons,

08:02.520 --> 08:03.870
forums,

08:03.870 --> 08:04.703
everything.

08:04.703 --> 08:07.680
And it just kinda helps you get in a creative mood,

08:07.680 --> 08:09.840
reminds you of what good webpages

08:09.840 --> 08:12.030
or simple webpages should look like.

08:12.030 --> 08:13.380
So now that you know the process,

08:13.380 --> 08:15.990
I need to instill one last thing in your head,

08:15.990 --> 08:18.210
this is the most important thing you need to know,

08:18.210 --> 08:21.240
as a product manager doing wire frames.

08:21.240 --> 08:23.160
And that is,

08:23.160 --> 08:26.190
it's function, not form,

08:26.190 --> 08:27.150
okay?

08:27.150 --> 08:32.010
You are not the UX person, you're not the designer,

08:32.010 --> 08:35.280
even as an entrepreneur, no one is gonna judge you

08:35.280 --> 08:39.210
because the user flow of your website is a little off,

08:39.210 --> 08:42.930
or you sketched out something that doesn't have a comparable

08:42.930 --> 08:44.610
and it might look weird,

08:44.610 --> 08:46.890
that's not the point, the point is to get

08:46.890 --> 08:50.580
the function on the page, the form is something that

08:50.580 --> 08:52.140
someone else will figure out later.

08:52.140 --> 08:53.070
Alright guys, in the next lecture,

08:53.070 --> 08:56.910
I'm gonna practice, in front of you, making more sketches.

08:56.910 --> 08:59.760
If you don't want that, skip on to the next lecture,

08:59.760 --> 09:00.840
where we'll talk about pop.

09:00.840 --> 09:01.940
Alright, see you then.

WEBVTT

00:00.300 --> 00:01.740
-: Hey, guys, welcome back to the course.

00:01.740 --> 00:04.590
Last lecture we got introduced to sketching.

00:04.590 --> 00:08.010
Sketching is just a simpler form of wire framing.

00:08.010 --> 00:10.650
It's actually the first form you will use

00:10.650 --> 00:14.070
in the vast majority of organizations and teams out there.

00:14.070 --> 00:16.710
First, start by taking pen to paper.

00:16.710 --> 00:18.990
So we covered a pretty good way

00:18.990 --> 00:22.380
of going about making your first sketches,

00:22.380 --> 00:25.230
but in this lecture, let's get some practice.

00:25.230 --> 00:28.530
So this time, I'm gonna sketch out some wire frames,

00:28.530 --> 00:30.090
and if you guys wanna follow along,

00:30.090 --> 00:31.650
continue the rest of the lecture.

00:31.650 --> 00:33.240
If you think you got it,

00:33.240 --> 00:36.240
skip to the next so I don't hear you complain.

00:36.240 --> 00:39.840
All right, this time, let's not do a website or web app.

00:39.840 --> 00:42.390
Let's do a mobile application.

00:42.390 --> 00:44.730
So, let's draw out an iPhone.

00:44.730 --> 00:46.380
All right, so what kind of app

00:46.380 --> 00:50.040
do you think we should try to make?

00:50.040 --> 00:52.920
I think what I'm gonna try to do is

00:52.920 --> 00:54.600
let's make a mobile application

00:54.600 --> 00:57.090
for ordering groceries, okay?

00:57.090 --> 01:01.650
Grocery delivery, that's gonna be the topic of the app.

01:01.650 --> 01:04.890
The idea, kind of like a web van,

01:04.890 --> 01:06.090
where you can get on your phone

01:06.090 --> 01:09.270
and order groceries from nearby stores

01:09.270 --> 01:10.560
to where you live.

01:10.560 --> 01:12.120
All right, now that we have our iPhone template,

01:12.120 --> 01:13.230
what should we start with?

01:13.230 --> 01:16.980
Well, I'd say an easy one to start with is a navigation bar.

01:16.980 --> 01:18.870
I'm assuming that we're gonna need a navigation bar,

01:18.870 --> 01:21.390
so I can put some space down at the bottom

01:21.390 --> 01:22.950
where I can click some buttons.

01:22.950 --> 01:24.330
Later on, we'll fill that out.

01:24.330 --> 01:27.240
Another thing that we could put instead,

01:27.240 --> 01:30.360
we could actually instead make a login screen.

01:30.360 --> 01:33.000
Pretty much any app that's not a demo,

01:33.000 --> 01:34.815
or any app that is complete

01:34.815 --> 01:38.280
and something you take seriously, has a login screen,

01:38.280 --> 01:40.662
and login screens are also called splash pages.

01:40.662 --> 01:44.700
So let's make that splash page and that login page,

01:44.700 --> 01:46.890
and I like doing this sometimes first,

01:46.890 --> 01:48.570
because it gets the juices flowing.

01:48.570 --> 01:50.610
All right, so let's draw out the login screen.

01:50.610 --> 01:54.750
This is a pretty standard template I'm gonna draw out.

01:54.750 --> 01:56.640
If you guys wanna use something else,

01:56.640 --> 01:58.860
again, just go out and find your favorite app.

01:58.860 --> 02:00.540
It literally takes four seconds.

02:00.540 --> 02:02.040
You just hit the app icon,

02:02.040 --> 02:04.230
and see what the first thing you see is.

02:04.230 --> 02:05.310
What's the login screen?

02:05.310 --> 02:06.750
I'm gonna put the name in the middle,

02:06.750 --> 02:10.530
and the logo up the top left like we always do.

02:10.530 --> 02:13.380
And then, how do you think they should be able to log in?

02:13.380 --> 02:15.780
Well, I think it would make sense to log in

02:15.780 --> 02:18.720
with Facebook login and email.

02:18.720 --> 02:21.630
Cool, now we have a finished screen.

02:21.630 --> 02:22.650
Now we should ask the question,

02:22.650 --> 02:25.320
should we link these two screens in any way,

02:25.320 --> 02:28.410
or at least draw some form of relationship between the two?

02:28.410 --> 02:29.340
Yeah, probably.

02:29.340 --> 02:31.110
So, I'm just gonna draw an arrow

02:31.110 --> 02:34.578
from the login screen to the main screen,

02:34.578 --> 02:38.010
and that just shows that the login screen is first,

02:38.010 --> 02:40.740
and it's a linear process going from left to right.

02:40.740 --> 02:42.690
This is actually how most people

02:42.690 --> 02:43.650
when they're sketching will do it.

02:43.650 --> 02:44.910
They'll just use arrows,

02:44.910 --> 02:47.430
and then often right next to the arrows,

02:47.430 --> 02:48.660
what button they're clicking,

02:48.660 --> 02:52.230
or a description of the interaction, or maybe an animation.

02:52.230 --> 02:53.430
We don't need to do that.

02:53.430 --> 02:56.520
And also, guys, keep in mind this is supposed to be rapid.

02:56.520 --> 02:58.260
That's why we do sketching.

02:58.260 --> 03:00.600
No one's gonna gonna judge you based

03:00.600 --> 03:02.670
off of your ability to draw,

03:02.670 --> 03:07.620
or your ability to make something that looks crisp

03:07.620 --> 03:09.900
and clean and makes sense immediately.

03:09.900 --> 03:11.850
Remember, function, not form.

03:11.850 --> 03:13.530
All right, great, now we have them linked,

03:13.530 --> 03:16.800
and for our purposes of showing other people,

03:16.800 --> 03:18.210
that's perfect, 'cause I can just show them,

03:18.210 --> 03:20.880
and it makes a lot more sense to them

03:20.880 --> 03:22.230
pretty much immediately.

03:22.230 --> 03:26.160
Okay, what should we do on the first page?

03:26.160 --> 03:28.470
Again, we're gonna have to answer those questions

03:28.470 --> 03:30.870
that we asked ourselves as part

03:30.870 --> 03:32.910
of the exercise in the last lecture.

03:32.910 --> 03:33.990
Remember the first question?

03:33.990 --> 03:37.590
It is, what is the point of this screen?

03:37.590 --> 03:41.670
Well, the point of the main screen, I would assume,

03:41.670 --> 03:46.050
is to let people actually find a grocery store,

03:46.050 --> 03:47.910
and start picking items.

03:47.910 --> 03:50.670
I think that speed is probably important,

03:50.670 --> 03:52.080
so in the first screen,

03:52.080 --> 03:54.390
they should be able to do both those things.

03:54.390 --> 03:56.070
All right, so let's draw it out.

03:56.070 --> 04:01.070
Let's make it so that we can select a store that's near us,

04:01.470 --> 04:02.640
'cause we obviously don't wanna order

04:02.640 --> 04:04.590
from the same store over and over again.

04:04.590 --> 04:07.590
That's part of the appeal of the app.

04:07.590 --> 04:08.640
Then we'll put a little box

04:08.640 --> 04:10.590
where we can display the store names,

04:10.590 --> 04:13.680
just so you know which one you are actually operating,

04:13.680 --> 04:15.744
and then let's just draw in some boxes

04:15.744 --> 04:19.920
to show where the favorite items and most common items

04:19.920 --> 04:21.510
for that store would display.

04:21.510 --> 04:22.343
All right, awesome.

04:22.343 --> 04:25.530
So, we actually have a decent wire frame right there,

04:25.530 --> 04:28.260
but let's put some more detail into it.

04:28.260 --> 04:29.850
Now, let's answer the second question.

04:29.850 --> 04:32.850
What should users be able to do?

04:32.850 --> 04:34.470
Well, I think that users should be able

04:34.470 --> 04:37.860
to visually see the grocery items,

04:37.860 --> 04:41.550
just so they can really quickly see what's available,

04:41.550 --> 04:43.080
and that's easier for them.

04:43.080 --> 04:48.080
So, let's draw in some actual apples and bananas and grapes

04:48.450 --> 04:51.120
and that little circular oranges.

04:51.120 --> 04:52.410
You got it in orange.

04:52.410 --> 04:54.630
I think users should also be able to see

04:54.630 --> 04:57.690
what the price of the items are,

04:57.690 --> 05:01.470
and I don't need to put a range or anything,

05:01.470 --> 05:04.050
so I'm just gonna put dollar sign, dollar sign,

05:04.050 --> 05:05.610
because I think that the price,

05:05.610 --> 05:07.860
let's just put it right underneath the image.

05:07.860 --> 05:11.640
I also think that since grocery stores have so many items,

05:11.640 --> 05:13.290
and I'm on this screen,

05:13.290 --> 05:17.430
it wouldn't make sense to just show me the popular items,

05:17.430 --> 05:19.380
regardless of whether or not the user asks for it,

05:19.380 --> 05:20.820
so let's put a search box.

05:20.820 --> 05:23.640
So, I'll just draw out a little search box.

05:23.640 --> 05:25.170
Cool, check, check, and check.

05:25.170 --> 05:28.380
The point is to be able to find a grocery store,

05:28.380 --> 05:32.400
and start the process of ordering items.

05:32.400 --> 05:33.480
Other things they should be able to do,

05:33.480 --> 05:35.130
they should be able to search for their groceries,

05:35.130 --> 05:37.260
see them, and know what they cost.

05:37.260 --> 05:39.480
Okay, so now let's say if I clicked on

05:39.480 --> 05:42.090
one of the grocery items, what do you think would happen?

05:42.090 --> 05:43.620
Well, it'd probably go to a page

05:43.620 --> 05:45.330
where gives more information, why?

05:45.330 --> 05:48.690
Because a user needs more information about an apple

05:48.690 --> 05:53.010
or a pear or whatever they're buying in order to buy it.

05:53.010 --> 05:54.180
They're not all commodities.

05:54.180 --> 05:55.570
We need to give more information.

05:55.570 --> 05:57.720
All right, so let's make a new screen,

05:57.720 --> 05:58.980
and we can connect these

05:58.980 --> 06:01.530
just like we connected the other ones.

06:01.530 --> 06:04.350
So this one, we'll just call this the detail page.

06:04.350 --> 06:06.030
And then the detail page,

06:06.030 --> 06:08.610
we'll just draw a big image of whatever it is.

06:08.610 --> 06:09.630
We'll just draw on apple,

06:09.630 --> 06:11.520
'cause I'm great at drawing apples,

06:11.520 --> 06:14.280
and now the user gets a big picture of what it is.

06:14.280 --> 06:16.530
And so, that saves us the time of answering the question of,

06:16.530 --> 06:17.730
what's the point of this screen?

06:17.730 --> 06:18.563
Well, the point of this screen

06:18.563 --> 06:21.660
is to display the items so they can see it,

06:21.660 --> 06:25.080
and give them information so that they can purchase it.

06:25.080 --> 06:26.850
What should they also be able to do?

06:26.850 --> 06:30.240
Well, they should probably also be able to read about it,

06:30.240 --> 06:32.431
so put some squiggly lines for text.

06:32.431 --> 06:35.760
They should probably also be able to buy it,

06:35.760 --> 06:37.290
so I'll put a buy button,

06:37.290 --> 06:38.760
and I'm gonna guess that you should probably be able

06:38.760 --> 06:41.220
to choose how many of them they're gonna buy,

06:41.220 --> 06:44.310
so I'll put a little number box

06:44.310 --> 06:46.020
where they can manipulate that.

06:46.020 --> 06:47.880
And there you go, now you have it.

06:47.880 --> 06:51.630
That is a pretty decent wire frame right there.

06:51.630 --> 06:53.580
There's an enormous amount of screens

06:53.580 --> 06:57.390
I could make after that, but I would bore you guys to tears.

06:57.390 --> 07:01.320
Notice how though we went about it in a very systematic way,

07:01.320 --> 07:02.970
very logical about it.

07:02.970 --> 07:04.770
Always ask yourselves the questions

07:04.770 --> 07:06.840
that we went over in the last lecture,

07:06.840 --> 07:08.880
that'll get you out of a rut.

07:08.880 --> 07:09.990
I always suggest starting

07:09.990 --> 07:12.390
with really easy intermediary screens,

07:12.390 --> 07:14.670
or elements that are always on the page

07:14.670 --> 07:17.460
to get the juices flowing, to get you comfortable.

07:17.460 --> 07:19.620
All right guys, in the next lecture I have a treat for you,

07:19.620 --> 07:21.773
'cause we're gonna use a system called POP,

07:21.773 --> 07:24.300
which is a mobile application that allow us

07:24.300 --> 07:27.900
to take sketches and turn them into prototypes.

07:27.900 --> 07:29.190
Interesting, right?

07:29.190 --> 07:30.803
All right, see you in the next lecture.

response.title: OPTIONAL: Using POP features in Marvel

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.210 --> 00:02.580
-: Hey guys, welcome back to the course.

00:02.580 --> 00:06.900
Welcome to this bonus slash optional lecture where

00:06.900 --> 00:09.930
I'm gonna talk about how you can take your sketches,

00:09.930 --> 00:14.464
add interaction to them, get them on your phone,

00:14.464 --> 00:19.110
and take them to the next level using a system called Pop.

00:19.110 --> 00:20.820
As always, if you're not interested

00:20.820 --> 00:22.470
you're too busy for this stuff.

00:22.470 --> 00:23.940
This is optional.

00:23.940 --> 00:25.230
Skip the lecture.

00:25.230 --> 00:26.340
I will see you later.

00:26.340 --> 00:28.260
Alright, so in the last two lectures

00:28.260 --> 00:31.650
we got into sketching, showed you how to do it,

00:31.650 --> 00:34.650
how to think through it, and then we practiced

00:34.650 --> 00:37.830
by creating a wireframe set called InstaFood.

00:37.830 --> 00:41.070
Now, the idea behind sketches are that they're rapid,

00:41.070 --> 00:44.490
they're very easy to do pen and paper.

00:44.490 --> 00:45.323
That's it.

00:45.323 --> 00:47.760
And you can do them really, really quickly.

00:47.760 --> 00:50.730
So if you have an idea, you wanna communicate it

00:50.730 --> 00:52.380
you can sketch it out and then show it

00:52.380 --> 00:55.290
to someone that instantly bring your communication

00:55.290 --> 00:57.810
and your conversation to the next level.

00:57.810 --> 01:00.570
It's also incredible useful for things like validation

01:00.570 --> 01:04.530
or user testing, because on the fly, if you want to ask

01:04.530 --> 01:07.170
about something or get someone's feedback on it

01:07.170 --> 01:09.510
you can just whip it up and show it to them.

01:09.510 --> 01:12.993
But one of the limits of being able to do that quickly is

01:12.993 --> 01:14.820
that you have to hold the piece of paper

01:14.820 --> 01:17.880
and explain to them, oh, this button goes over here

01:17.880 --> 01:20.820
and trust me, it's gonna have like a thing that does this.

01:20.820 --> 01:22.020
And it, it makes sense.

01:22.020 --> 01:23.730
It's especially troublesome when you're trying to

01:23.730 --> 01:25.320
convince someone of mobile apps

01:25.320 --> 01:27.780
because you're holding a piece of paper and you know

01:27.780 --> 01:28.727
if you draw an iPhone

01:28.727 --> 01:31.463
like the way I draw an iPhone, people think you're crazy.

01:31.463 --> 01:33.360
Alright, so enter Pop,

01:33.360 --> 01:37.440
one of my favorite apps for sketching and collaborating.

01:37.440 --> 01:38.273
Alright?

01:38.273 --> 01:39.600
So you can check it out online if you want to.

01:39.600 --> 01:41.700
It's popapp.in.

01:41.700 --> 01:44.204
I don't know what the .in suffix is,

01:44.204 --> 01:47.550
but they'll talk you through it, show you how it works.

01:47.550 --> 01:51.420
It's free to use, and you only pay if you start using it

01:51.420 --> 01:53.760
for tons and tons of projects, at which point you

01:53.760 --> 01:55.380
should probably pay anyway.

01:55.380 --> 01:57.840
It works for iOS, Android, and Windows phone.

01:57.840 --> 01:59.880
So it pretty much covers everybody.

01:59.880 --> 02:02.790
Sorry if you have a Blackberry, but let's be honest

02:02.790 --> 02:04.860
if you have a Blackberry, you have bigger problems.

02:04.860 --> 02:08.146
Now, the idea behind Pop is that we're gonna use our phones

02:08.146 --> 02:11.310
to take a photo of our sketches,

02:11.310 --> 02:14.250
then it's gonna import it into the Pop system,

02:14.250 --> 02:18.450
and we can add basic user flow and interactions.

02:18.450 --> 02:20.850
One thing that does make this easier for you guys though

02:20.850 --> 02:25.110
is to go to the Pop.in/sketchpad

02:25.110 --> 02:28.107
and you can download these sketchpads they have

02:28.107 --> 02:32.405
that are really clean, really two spec,

02:32.405 --> 02:35.190
and for all the major phones,

02:35.190 --> 02:37.200
it'll make this process a lot easier.

02:37.200 --> 02:38.820
At least look a lot better.

02:38.820 --> 02:40.050
All right, you ready?

02:40.050 --> 02:42.780
Grab a piece of paper, draw out your wireframes

02:42.780 --> 02:43.860
and your sketches.

02:43.860 --> 02:46.080
Grab your phone, and let's use some Pop.

02:46.080 --> 02:49.170
So you'll open Pop, and then it's pretty simple.

02:49.170 --> 02:51.480
You just want to create a project,

02:51.480 --> 02:53.670
and I'll just talk you through this as we go.

02:53.670 --> 02:54.503
We'll name it.

02:54.503 --> 02:55.893
Remember ours was InstaFood.

02:57.390 --> 02:58.260
Okay?

02:58.260 --> 02:59.880
Now what it wants me to do is

02:59.880 --> 03:04.140
say what device that my designs are for.

03:04.140 --> 03:06.540
If I wanna do landscape or portrait.

03:06.540 --> 03:08.520
I wanna do portrait with an iPhone five.

03:08.520 --> 03:10.620
Actually drew an iPhone five, at least that's what was

03:10.620 --> 03:12.150
in my head when I was using it.

03:12.150 --> 03:15.300
Yes, this is an iPhone five, and it is yellow.

03:15.300 --> 03:18.120
Create your project and then just hit the plus button here.

03:18.120 --> 03:21.480
'Cause hitting the plus button will ask us to add screens.

03:21.480 --> 03:24.210
So the way we do this is we take photos of them.

03:24.210 --> 03:25.350
If you've taken photos before,

03:25.350 --> 03:28.980
you can throw them into Dropbox or your creative cloud

03:28.980 --> 03:30.609
or it's already stored on your phone

03:30.609 --> 03:32.730
or you can just take them right now.

03:32.730 --> 03:35.051
Alright, so then all I have to do is take the photo

03:35.051 --> 03:38.280
of the app that we just drew before,

03:38.280 --> 03:40.320
actually drew out a couple more screens so

03:40.320 --> 03:44.310
that you guys can see the interaction a little bit better.

03:44.310 --> 03:46.320
And then what you want to do is just place it

03:46.320 --> 03:51.320
on the image and line up the boxes and take the photo.

03:52.020 --> 03:52.853
Alright?

03:52.853 --> 03:54.450
And then all you have to do is just keep taking photos.

03:54.450 --> 03:55.470
You don't have to hit next

03:55.470 --> 03:58.004
and save and move on or anything like that.

03:58.004 --> 04:01.053
And so we can just keep moving on.

04:04.020 --> 04:07.803
Then the third screen that we drew right there.

04:08.820 --> 04:09.653
Alright, cool.

04:09.653 --> 04:13.071
Now I have a couple other screens that I'm gonna add.

04:13.071 --> 04:15.180
I'm gonna call in some extra light

04:15.180 --> 04:19.170
because we're in the learning den, also known as a cave.

04:19.170 --> 04:20.520
And let's add some more screens.

04:20.520 --> 04:24.180
I made a screen for what happens when you hit email log in.

04:24.180 --> 04:27.270
Obviously, it didn't just automatically send you

04:27.270 --> 04:29.040
to the storefront.

04:29.040 --> 04:31.170
Facebook log in would do that though.

04:31.170 --> 04:33.690
So we need to take a photo of that.

04:33.690 --> 04:36.690
And then I made another screen for checking out.

04:36.690 --> 04:38.280
So after you hit buy, it should add it

04:38.280 --> 04:40.590
to a screen so you can see your subtotal and whatever,

04:40.590 --> 04:43.793
whatever made a very basic screen for that there.

04:47.970 --> 04:49.361
Okay, when you're done, hit next

04:49.361 --> 04:52.620
you can view what you've done.

04:52.620 --> 04:54.900
Great, looks great, looks great.

04:54.900 --> 04:56.370
You have the option to crop

04:56.370 --> 04:58.830
if you shot it off a little bit wrong,

04:58.830 --> 05:00.750
like see how it's misaligned

05:00.750 --> 05:03.570
so I can put it right back on where it should be.

05:03.570 --> 05:05.430
And I could also just, you know

05:05.430 --> 05:06.930
I could cut it so it's like this.

05:06.930 --> 05:09.652
If I wanted to be shorter or taller or whatever, done.

05:09.652 --> 05:13.980
Okay, I can add brightness too as well, if you guys are also

05:13.980 --> 05:16.890
in a cave and you might wanna add some brightness.

05:16.890 --> 05:19.020
Okay, now I see my five screens.

05:19.020 --> 05:22.050
Now I just need to add in relationships between the screens.

05:22.050 --> 05:23.580
We established this before, right?

05:23.580 --> 05:25.830
You have a log in screen and you click Facebook log in,

05:25.830 --> 05:27.090
takes you to the storefront.

05:27.090 --> 05:28.770
If you click on one of the items you wanna buy

05:28.770 --> 05:31.470
takes you to the detail page, and then if you click buy

05:31.470 --> 05:33.090
it's just gonna take you to the shopping cart.

05:33.090 --> 05:35.220
So I'm gonna reflect that in the app.

05:35.220 --> 05:37.020
I'm gonna go do the first one and just click it.

05:37.020 --> 05:39.960
And all you wanna do is click on the button

05:39.960 --> 05:42.680
or the area that you want to be tappable.

05:42.680 --> 05:45.510
So I'll just click on the Facebook log in

05:45.510 --> 05:47.160
and then I'll draw this little box

05:47.160 --> 05:50.070
so it has a wider area that I can touch with my finger.

05:50.070 --> 05:53.790
Great, now I link it to link it to the store front.

05:53.790 --> 05:55.800
Great, I'll add one more.

05:55.800 --> 06:00.003
This email log in will go to the email log in screen I made.

06:00.900 --> 06:02.160
There we go.

06:02.160 --> 06:05.070
I realize now that I didn't put a log in button

06:05.070 --> 06:07.560
for the log in button, so we'll make that a dead end

06:07.560 --> 06:09.360
but we'll move on to the next screen.

06:09.360 --> 06:12.600
So this one, I would label this the main screen up here just

06:12.600 --> 06:14.340
by tapping the title.

06:14.340 --> 06:17.754
I could call it the main screen, done,

06:17.754 --> 06:19.530
and then I can add in the buttons

06:19.530 --> 06:21.990
that we know should work.

06:21.990 --> 06:25.860
So I'll just do the apple for our sake,

06:25.860 --> 06:27.750
link to the detail page.

06:27.750 --> 06:31.320
Great, moving over the detail page, see how fast this is.

06:31.320 --> 06:33.188
It's really, really simple.

06:33.188 --> 06:36.210
Double tap the buy button, make it a little bit larger.

06:36.210 --> 06:38.700
That's gonna link to the cart page.

06:38.700 --> 06:40.200
Great, okay.

06:40.200 --> 06:43.440
Now I have a project that's set up.

06:43.440 --> 06:45.030
It's it's ready to go.

06:45.030 --> 06:47.910
Now what I can do is when I'm talking to someone, say, hey

06:47.910 --> 06:49.860
I have this idea for InstaFood,

06:49.860 --> 06:52.230
or at least this is the way I envision

06:52.230 --> 06:53.640
a grocery delivery app.

06:53.640 --> 06:57.090
Maybe I work at a startup that is making something

06:57.090 --> 06:58.780
like that, like Instacart.

06:58.780 --> 07:01.200
And then what I would do is I'd show this to them

07:01.200 --> 07:02.070
but not like this.

07:02.070 --> 07:04.380
I'd show it to 'em by hitting play.

07:04.380 --> 07:09.330
And now play basically makes a fake version of our app.

07:09.330 --> 07:12.780
Now, the sketches look like something on your phone.

07:12.780 --> 07:15.087
So if I wanted to log in, I would hit log in

07:15.087 --> 07:17.040
in. Oh, and it takes me to the storefront

07:17.040 --> 07:18.810
and I would be talking them through it, right?

07:18.810 --> 07:21.150
I'd explain what the top button does

07:21.150 --> 07:23.250
and I'd explain what these images are.

07:23.250 --> 07:24.750
Not everyone's gonna be doing this

07:24.750 --> 07:26.695
with a chisel tip sharpie

07:26.695 --> 07:29.413
so there's gonna be a lot more detail to this.

07:29.413 --> 07:31.260
I'd do it with those sharpies

07:31.260 --> 07:33.030
because I like to, they're fun.

07:33.030 --> 07:35.910
I'll click on the apple, explain to them how that works.

07:35.910 --> 07:38.550
Oh, I click buy and buy, and I go to the cart page.

07:38.550 --> 07:40.970
Great, that is the gist of Pop.

07:40.970 --> 07:44.340
What you can also do is you can add in comments.

07:44.340 --> 07:46.110
So what you can do is you can share it with people

07:46.110 --> 07:47.880
and then if you want to add in commenting

07:47.880 --> 07:50.307
you just double tap in one area, leave a comment

07:50.307 --> 07:53.820
this is great, and then someone can read that.

07:53.820 --> 07:56.040
And then when I want to share it

07:56.040 --> 07:59.157
I just go back up here, hit share,

07:59.157 --> 08:01.110
and I can send out a link.

08:01.110 --> 08:03.580
So I will text it to myself

08:06.780 --> 08:08.220
and we can see it on our browser.

08:08.220 --> 08:09.053
There you go.

08:09.053 --> 08:09.886
It works the same.

08:09.886 --> 08:12.780
I can read an introduction that describes what it is

08:12.780 --> 08:14.100
or I can just play right through it.

08:14.100 --> 08:16.170
It works these same exact way.

08:16.170 --> 08:17.003
Check it out.

08:17.003 --> 08:19.830
Cool, then I give descriptions, yada, yada, yada.

08:19.830 --> 08:22.080
Anyway, so that's one way you can take your sketches

08:22.080 --> 08:23.640
to the next level.

08:23.640 --> 08:27.060
Awesome, awesome tool for rapidly on the go

08:27.060 --> 08:29.700
taking two dimensional sketches

08:29.700 --> 08:31.200
and then putting 'em on your phone

08:31.200 --> 08:34.440
and putting them in a way that you can explain them,

08:34.440 --> 08:36.840
share them, collaborate on them,

08:36.840 --> 08:41.130
and instantly connect in a better more meaningful way

08:41.130 --> 08:43.140
with whoever you're trying to explain this to.

08:43.140 --> 08:44.520
Alright, hope you enjoyed that one.

08:44.520 --> 08:46.800
We have plenty other tools in this course.

08:46.800 --> 08:48.030
At the end of this course

08:48.030 --> 08:50.370
you'll be a master of so many different things.

08:50.370 --> 08:51.750
I'm kind of envious,

08:51.750 --> 08:53.340
even though I guess I'm teaching the course

08:53.340 --> 08:54.630
so it doesn't really make sense.

08:54.630 --> 08:55.740
Whatever, don't think about it.

08:55.740 --> 08:56.790
See you next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.210 --> 00:01.043
-: Hey guys.

00:01.043 --> 00:02.490
Welcome back to the course.

00:02.490 --> 00:05.581
So in the last lectures, we learned how to sketch.

00:05.581 --> 00:09.930
We learned how to think our way through basic wireframes

00:09.930 --> 00:11.820
and we even learned how to use Pop

00:11.820 --> 00:14.160
so that we can add some basic interaction

00:14.160 --> 00:16.920
to the sketches we had before.

00:16.920 --> 00:21.360
Now it's time to upgrade and add a little bit of fidelity.

00:21.360 --> 00:22.410
Tiny bit of fidelity.

00:22.410 --> 00:24.201
We're not gonna make mock-ups yet.

00:24.201 --> 00:26.460
What we're gonna do is we're gonna learn

00:26.460 --> 00:27.660
how to use Balsamiq.

00:27.660 --> 00:30.240
I think as a prospective product manager

00:30.240 --> 00:31.830
it really helps to have one

00:31.830 --> 00:34.440
of the wire framing tools under your belt.

00:34.440 --> 00:37.800
So I picked the tool that is probably the most popular

00:37.800 --> 00:40.650
for low fidelity wireframes

00:40.650 --> 00:45.120
and also happens to be probably the fastest as well.

00:45.120 --> 00:47.310
That's the reason why I like it.

00:47.310 --> 00:48.300
At the end of this lecture

00:48.300 --> 00:51.840
you'll know how to get into Balsamiq and be dangerous,

00:51.840 --> 00:54.720
make pretty much whatever you want.

00:54.720 --> 00:56.790
It's a very simple application.

00:56.790 --> 00:57.930
It's not gonna take us a ton

00:57.930 --> 01:00.030
of time to get up and running in it.

01:00.030 --> 01:03.270
If you're the type that does not like tutorials

01:03.270 --> 01:05.840
of softwares and someone showing you

01:05.840 --> 01:08.850
what you should care about and think about first,

01:08.850 --> 01:09.870
skip the lecture.

01:09.870 --> 01:10.830
See you in the next lecture.

01:10.830 --> 01:12.960
For the rest of you guys, let's jump in.

01:12.960 --> 01:15.750
Alright, so this is what Balsamiq's website looks like.

01:15.750 --> 01:18.300
They offer their service in two different ways.

01:18.300 --> 01:22.230
One is a web version and one is a desktop version.

01:22.230 --> 01:24.176
Now, there's pros and cons to both.

01:24.176 --> 01:26.920
The web version is cheaper

01:28.050 --> 01:30.780
and the desktop version is more expensive

01:30.780 --> 01:33.000
but the web version is a subscription

01:33.000 --> 01:36.180
where as a desktop app is one payment, one time.

01:36.180 --> 01:37.530
For these guys out there that don't know

01:37.530 --> 01:39.270
if you wanna buy this stuff, don't worry

01:39.270 --> 01:40.260
they have a free trial.

01:40.260 --> 01:42.240
You can try either one you want.

01:42.240 --> 01:43.710
If you don't wanna save something on your computer

01:43.710 --> 01:44.910
go for the web app.

01:44.910 --> 01:46.650
If you're fine saving stuff on your computer

01:46.650 --> 01:48.900
I prefer using the desktop app.

01:48.900 --> 01:52.350
It's faster, it's easier to use in my opinion

01:52.350 --> 01:53.520
and you don't have to wait

01:53.520 --> 01:55.729
for your browser to constantly update.

01:55.729 --> 01:56.562
Alright?

01:56.562 --> 01:59.550
So take your pick, download whichever one you want to do.

01:59.550 --> 02:02.308
I'll see you as soon as you get into it.

02:02.308 --> 02:04.410
We fast forward to the future.

02:04.410 --> 02:05.243
Okay, alright

02:05.243 --> 02:08.250
so this is what the desktop application looks like.

02:08.250 --> 02:11.850
It's very similar to the web application.

02:11.850 --> 02:14.002
In fact, I'm not even sure there's anything really

02:14.002 --> 02:15.810
that much different about it.

02:15.810 --> 02:19.560
Now, Balsamiq is very, very simple.

02:19.560 --> 02:22.770
There is just this screen.

02:22.770 --> 02:26.070
Not only is this application fast, it is very, very simple.

02:26.070 --> 02:29.400
Another one of the good things about it

02:29.400 --> 02:32.190
people often fault Balsamiq for being simple

02:32.190 --> 02:36.210
but I challenge you to find something you can't wireframe

02:36.210 --> 02:39.270
with Balsamiq, they are simple, but they've really

02:39.270 --> 02:41.520
thought out pretty much everything in their app.

02:41.520 --> 02:43.320
Alright, so the way that this works is pretty much

02:43.320 --> 02:46.470
like any editing tool that uses a canvas

02:46.470 --> 02:50.066
and then has tools like Photoshop, like a sketch.

02:50.066 --> 02:51.938
If you're not familiar with those, don't worry.

02:51.938 --> 02:54.480
Right here is the canvas, okay?

02:54.480 --> 02:56.970
This is where we're gonna take elements and add it

02:56.970 --> 03:00.780
to the canvas, manipulate them, and create our wireframe.

03:00.780 --> 03:03.210
Now, this canvas here is not defined

03:03.210 --> 03:05.610
as in there are no boundaries to it.

03:05.610 --> 03:09.030
I can scroll up and down and just go on forever.

03:09.030 --> 03:10.200
There's really no limit

03:10.200 --> 03:12.810
to where I can place things in this area.

03:12.810 --> 03:13.650
Alright, so at the top

03:13.650 --> 03:16.650
you can see all of the elements that you can use.

03:16.650 --> 03:21.150
They have them sectioned off into little groups here

03:21.150 --> 03:23.460
which is the way that you find them.

03:23.460 --> 03:25.470
There is no search for some reason.

03:25.470 --> 03:28.398
I believe they removed it from a previous version

03:28.398 --> 03:31.687
so you're gonna have to get used to these categories.

03:31.687 --> 03:33.420
Alright, so they have categories

03:33.420 --> 03:35.820
for pretty much everything you would want.

03:35.820 --> 03:37.350
Now, if you wanna add something to the canvas

03:37.350 --> 03:40.590
you can drag and drop or just double click.

03:40.590 --> 03:42.000
If you wanna do add an iPhone

03:42.000 --> 03:43.470
we go over to the iOS section

03:43.470 --> 03:46.800
which holds a bunch of elements for mobile applications

03:46.800 --> 03:48.510
and I can just double click an iPhone.

03:48.510 --> 03:52.110
All right, so now that it's in the canvas, it's really easy.

03:52.110 --> 03:53.400
I have boundary boxes up here

03:53.400 --> 03:55.950
if I wanna shrink it or grow it in any way.

03:55.950 --> 03:58.080
It'll tell me how many pixels it is

03:58.080 --> 03:59.884
if you want to get exact.

03:59.884 --> 04:02.790
Over here, what we have is called the inspector

04:02.790 --> 04:06.390
and the inspector allows us to add modifications

04:06.390 --> 04:08.070
to our elements.

04:08.070 --> 04:11.400
I can manually pick the size of it or the positioning.

04:11.400 --> 04:13.980
Notice how as I move this, the position changes.

04:13.980 --> 04:15.452
It's an absolute position.

04:15.452 --> 04:18.210
I can change it between things like the iPhone four,

04:18.210 --> 04:22.200
iPhone five, I can make it landscape versus portrait.

04:22.200 --> 04:26.670
I can even add in some really helpful stuff like top bars

04:26.670 --> 04:29.370
and lower bars, which are really helpful because remember

04:29.370 --> 04:31.380
the first thing we do with iOS

04:31.380 --> 04:33.240
at least we always add a navigation bar

04:33.240 --> 04:34.500
so that saves us time.

04:34.500 --> 04:36.780
Also gives you the option to change the layering

04:36.780 --> 04:39.330
of your canvas and your wireframing

04:39.330 --> 04:41.520
and let me show you what that means.

04:41.520 --> 04:45.600
Let's say I wanted to add this iOS keyboard onto my iPhone.

04:45.600 --> 04:47.160
Let's make it line up.

04:47.160 --> 04:49.560
Great, now, you have to keep in mind is

04:49.560 --> 04:53.490
that these two elements are quite literally stacked

04:53.490 --> 04:54.390
on top of each other.

04:54.390 --> 04:55.470
It's like paper.

04:55.470 --> 04:56.940
That's the easiest way of thinking about it.

04:56.940 --> 04:58.410
Right now, the keyboard is

04:58.410 --> 05:00.180
on top because I created it second, but

05:00.180 --> 05:02.040
if I wanted the keyboard to be behind the iPhone

05:02.040 --> 05:05.580
for whatever reason, I would just send it backward, right?

05:05.580 --> 05:06.690
So when I hit send it backward

05:06.690 --> 05:10.500
it goes back one layer behind whatever's directly behind it

05:10.500 --> 05:11.460
or I can send it to the bottom

05:11.460 --> 05:13.200
or I can send it to the front with these buttons.

05:13.200 --> 05:14.460
Alright, pretty simple, right?

05:14.460 --> 05:17.610
So let's start throwing some more elements on the page.

05:17.610 --> 05:19.980
We have the option of adding things like forms.

05:19.980 --> 05:21.510
These are incredibly helpful

05:21.510 --> 05:24.660
if you ever need to create a form, incredibly tedious

05:24.660 --> 05:28.080
but with Balsamiq, you can do it pretty easily.

05:28.080 --> 05:29.430
Buttons are over here.

05:29.430 --> 05:33.120
Very easy to change the text just by double clicking them

05:33.120 --> 05:36.030
so you can change it to buy now or whatever it is.

05:36.030 --> 05:37.980
You can change the color.

05:37.980 --> 05:39.540
You can add icons

05:39.540 --> 05:41.640
and I'll talk about icons in just a second.

05:41.640 --> 05:44.220
You can create a form then by just throwing down a bunch

05:44.220 --> 05:47.850
of stuff that you would normally see into, in a form.

05:47.850 --> 05:51.960
And notice this one thing as I move elements on the canvas

05:51.960 --> 05:55.680
it will bring up guidelines, little grids

05:55.680 --> 05:59.070
and this is incredibly helpful for lining things.

05:59.070 --> 06:00.990
Specifically, if I was making a form

06:00.990 --> 06:03.242
I would want everything to line up on the left, right?

06:03.242 --> 06:05.220
Well, I can do that really easily

06:05.220 --> 06:09.060
without having to zoom in by clicking the second element

06:09.060 --> 06:12.120
and then just lining it up right there with the left end.

06:12.120 --> 06:15.720
I can also line it up with the middle of the element.

06:15.720 --> 06:16.980
It's tricky, but there it goes.

06:16.980 --> 06:19.860
If I want to do center alignment or with the middle

06:19.860 --> 06:23.400
of the design, which actually happens to be right there

06:23.400 --> 06:25.170
cause it's the middle of the canvas.

06:25.170 --> 06:27.990
Alright, let's see what else we can add in it.

06:27.990 --> 06:30.000
Let's throw in some text right there.

06:30.000 --> 06:31.530
We'll call it a form.

06:31.530 --> 06:33.360
Let's see what else we can add in.

06:33.360 --> 06:36.163
Let's say we want to grab some numbers,

06:36.163 --> 06:40.560
maybe turn something on and off right there,

06:40.560 --> 06:41.970
line 'em up, and there you go.

06:41.970 --> 06:44.460
You have the most basic form ever made.

06:44.460 --> 06:46.950
Now I also have the option of adding icons.

06:46.950 --> 06:48.450
Now, I said I'd get back to these.

06:48.450 --> 06:49.440
What an icon is

06:49.440 --> 06:52.380
it's just gonna be a blank space whenever you add it

06:52.380 --> 06:55.320
but if you double click this magic thing it gives you

06:55.320 --> 06:58.849
an enormous option of icons you can choose from.

06:58.849 --> 06:59.682
Now, don't worry

06:59.682 --> 07:02.160
it's not gonna send these icons into a box.

07:02.160 --> 07:04.440
It's gonna take the place of the box.

07:04.440 --> 07:07.350
We have hundreds and hundreds of icons here.

07:07.350 --> 07:09.540
You can pick the size that you want

07:09.540 --> 07:11.190
and it'll give you a preview.

07:11.190 --> 07:12.090
You can also search

07:12.090 --> 07:16.170
over here for whatever specifically you wanted.

07:16.170 --> 07:17.460
If I want to get really specific,

07:17.460 --> 07:18.570
I'm looking for a rupee.

07:18.570 --> 07:19.403
There we go.

07:19.403 --> 07:21.210
That's actually the symbol.

07:21.210 --> 07:22.530
Great, now I can use that

07:22.530 --> 07:25.380
for whatever I was planning to use it for.

07:25.380 --> 07:29.070
Similarly, I can add icons to things like buttons

07:29.070 --> 07:32.430
so in the button right here, over here, I can add an icon.

07:32.430 --> 07:35.460
Okay, so if I wanted to add rupee to that, there we go.

07:35.460 --> 07:36.420
Now we added a rupee

07:36.420 --> 07:38.970
and I can also change the size to make it fit

07:38.970 --> 07:42.000
so it doesn't stick out so much, and there you go.

07:42.000 --> 07:45.210
Alright, so that is the basics of Balsamiq.

07:45.210 --> 07:49.260
There's not that much left to go over.

07:49.260 --> 07:52.680
You can add certain things like states to buttons.

07:52.680 --> 07:53.670
See this button.

07:53.670 --> 07:57.390
When I say a state, a state could be clicked or unclicked

07:57.390 --> 08:00.330
for instance, or grayed out, I could put the state.

08:00.330 --> 08:03.840
It could be selected and focused or disabled, say selected.

08:03.840 --> 08:05.460
See how it changes the color.

08:05.460 --> 08:06.420
That's helpful.

08:06.420 --> 08:10.110
If I'm putting wireframes in a row, showing a sequence,

08:10.110 --> 08:12.990
or if I wanna to get really specific saying when

08:12.990 --> 08:14.550
this button is depressed,

08:14.550 --> 08:17.760
then you see these other elements on the screen.

08:17.760 --> 08:19.350
Over here in the common section

08:19.350 --> 08:22.418
you'll see all the common things that you would see.

08:22.418 --> 08:24.750
Image boxes are right here.

08:24.750 --> 08:26.550
Notice how before in wireframes

08:26.550 --> 08:30.030
typically you won't have the image or sketch out the image.

08:30.030 --> 08:33.360
You'll put a box and write image, but if you wanted to

08:33.360 --> 08:34.800
you could put something in this.

08:34.800 --> 08:38.460
I like doing it like this, clicking it and hitting enter

08:38.460 --> 08:42.000
and then putting image in the middle

08:42.000 --> 08:43.770
so you can see that, hey, this is an image.

08:43.770 --> 08:47.040
You can also just double tap on it

08:47.040 --> 08:49.680
if you wanted to add an image in that container

08:49.680 --> 08:52.800
it'll insert an image into the container and constrict it

08:52.800 --> 08:56.940
and then you can expand it to your heart's content.

08:56.940 --> 08:59.490
If you ever want to duplicate something

08:59.490 --> 09:01.200
it's relatively easy.

09:01.200 --> 09:02.640
I just use copy and paste.

09:02.640 --> 09:05.910
You can do it up here, edit copy, and then you know

09:05.910 --> 09:08.160
control V, and you can copy it right there

09:08.160 --> 09:11.880
and then you can use grids for alignment, yada, yada, yada.

09:11.880 --> 09:14.310
The last two things I wanna show you are one

09:14.310 --> 09:15.570
annotations and two,

09:15.570 --> 09:17.070
then how do we export?

09:17.070 --> 09:20.850
Now, annotations in Balsamiq are controlled

09:20.850 --> 09:22.440
in kind of a goofy way.

09:22.440 --> 09:24.288
We just use it through stickies.

09:24.288 --> 09:27.030
Now, go over to the markup right here.

09:27.030 --> 09:30.360
If I wanted to add something onto the page

09:30.360 --> 09:33.150
like a comment for someone else reading this right here

09:33.150 --> 09:36.790
I could put a sticky and then put a description saying

09:36.790 --> 09:39.330
you know, this is the screen that does this

09:39.330 --> 09:43.350
or some comment that I want other people to notice

09:43.350 --> 09:45.270
and then I can use arrows right here.

09:45.270 --> 09:49.590
If I want to change the arrows I have in the inspector ways

09:49.590 --> 09:51.990
of changing it, changing the size, the length

09:51.990 --> 09:54.911
which way it points, in which direction

09:54.911 --> 09:58.563
in case I wanted to say point the sticky in that direction.

10:00.450 --> 10:02.583
Sorry to go like this, and there you go.

10:10.170 --> 10:13.327
When you want to save your creations

10:13.327 --> 10:15.300
you have to export them

10:15.300 --> 10:19.065
and you'll have to export them either as an image or a pdf

10:19.065 --> 10:23.820
or you can share it and use a share link online.

10:23.820 --> 10:26.310
That's great if you wanna share it with other people.
