10:26.310 --> 10:28.200
Otherwise, you'll have to export it and send it to them.

10:28.200 --> 10:30.660
If you wanna create multiple mock-ups, by the way

10:30.660 --> 10:32.130
you just hit, click this

10:32.130 --> 10:34.440
and then you can create another mock-up

10:34.440 --> 10:35.273
and if you click here

10:35.273 --> 10:37.710
it'll show you all the mock-ups you have made.

10:37.710 --> 10:40.835
When you then export, it'll put all of them together

10:40.835 --> 10:44.160
into one long PDF and it, if you have a lot of 'em

10:44.160 --> 10:46.380
it'll create a table of contents at the top.

10:46.380 --> 10:48.090
Alright, guys, that is for the most part

10:48.090 --> 10:51.240
all you need to know to get into Balsamiq and get dangerous.

10:51.240 --> 10:53.190
There's a lot of other things obviously I didn't cover

10:53.190 --> 10:56.130
but you'll figure them out pretty quickly.

10:56.130 --> 10:58.399
That is 90% of it.

10:58.399 --> 10:59.232
Alright, guys.

10:59.232 --> 11:00.065
In the next lecture

11:00.065 --> 11:01.650
we're gonna practice this just a little bit.

11:01.650 --> 11:02.970
If you don't feel like you need practice

11:02.970 --> 11:04.710
then just skip on to the next lecture.

11:04.710 --> 11:06.060
Won't hurt my feelings.

11:06.060 --> 11:08.423
For the rest of you guys, I'll see you in the next lecture.

And, there we go.

05:26.130 --> 05:27.780
Those are our suggested videos.

05:27.780 --> 05:30.330
Now, maybe I wanted to put a container around that.

05:30.330 --> 05:32.100
Something light.

05:32.100 --> 05:33.810
Just go to Shape, again.

05:33.810 --> 05:34.830
Now a shape.

05:34.830 --> 05:37.803
I am just gonna pick a rectangle right here.

05:39.210 --> 05:40.203
Put it right here.

05:42.270 --> 05:44.400
And I want to expand it over.

05:44.400 --> 05:46.770
See, notice how it covered it.

05:46.770 --> 05:50.340
Now, they, Balsamiq has a layering, kind of like Photoshop.

05:50.340 --> 05:52.140
If you use Photoshop before, it has layers.

05:52.140 --> 05:54.540
The way that you would want this to fade to the back,

05:54.540 --> 05:57.720
is you would use this little subgroup.

05:57.720 --> 06:00.515
This will send it back one, this will send it.

06:00.515 --> 06:01.620
Oh. this'll send it all the way to the back.

06:01.620 --> 06:03.150
So, this will be the bottom of your,

06:03.150 --> 06:04.350
imagine, this is kinda like paper,

06:04.350 --> 06:05.700
laying on top of each other.

06:05.700 --> 06:06.900
This would be the bottom piece of paper.

06:06.900 --> 06:08.160
If you click this.

06:08.160 --> 06:09.750
If you click this, it's gonna bring it

06:09.750 --> 06:10.650
all the way to the top.

06:10.650 --> 06:12.030
So, it's gonna cover anything behind it.

06:12.030 --> 06:13.440
I just wanna send it back,

06:13.440 --> 06:15.633
until it's behind the cat videos.

06:16.680 --> 06:17.610
All right, So the rest of it, really,

06:17.610 --> 06:19.440
is just, kind of, tiny little details.

06:19.440 --> 06:20.730
It's up to you how much detail,

06:20.730 --> 06:22.200
you honestly wanna put in here.

06:22.200 --> 06:24.810
We can tend, like, this is a,

06:24.810 --> 06:26.460
here's the placeholder for the image.

06:26.460 --> 06:31.083
Whoever made the video, we can add in some text again.

06:33.480 --> 06:35.673
This'll be the name of the video,

06:36.889 --> 06:40.433
My Super Secret Webcam Video.

06:41.610 --> 06:43.230
Cause, they want it to be a secret,

06:43.230 --> 06:45.150
but, they also want the entire world to see it.

06:45.150 --> 06:47.880
And, let's put this realistically much larger.

06:47.880 --> 06:50.850
Okay, let's move this down, add some space.

06:50.850 --> 06:55.410
You can add those thumbs up and thumbs down items.

06:55.410 --> 06:56.493
Just pick an icon.

06:57.426 --> 06:58.384
Let's put it over here.

06:58.384 --> 06:59.460
You can even just search for thumbs.

06:59.460 --> 07:00.690
Thumbs is one.

07:00.690 --> 07:01.920
There you go.

07:01.920 --> 07:03.510
Run it as small.

07:03.510 --> 07:05.460
That makes more sense, right?

07:05.460 --> 07:08.610
And, you can, you can even copy it.

07:08.610 --> 07:12.030
Put it right next to it, and then just find another icon.

07:12.030 --> 07:12.990
Thumbs, again.

07:12.990 --> 07:15.043
This one's thumbs down, like the video.

07:15.043 --> 07:17.580
Oh, then just stick someone's face on it,

07:17.580 --> 07:19.053
cause it's a webcam, right?

07:20.190 --> 07:21.603
You get the drift now.

07:22.860 --> 07:26.340
You can put an enormous amount of detail into Balsamiq,

07:26.340 --> 07:28.800
if you want to, but as you've noticed,

07:28.800 --> 07:30.840
that really did not take me a lot of time.

07:30.840 --> 07:33.690
And, I have a wireframe of a video.

07:33.690 --> 07:35.970
You're obviously gonna wanna put more time and effort,

07:35.970 --> 07:38.160
into whatever you actually build yourself.

07:38.160 --> 07:40.410
But, once you actually get acquainted with it,

07:40.410 --> 07:42.390
hopefully, by now, you are acquainted,

07:42.390 --> 07:44.430
you can really move at lightning speed.

07:44.430 --> 07:46.920
And, you can really produce these very, very quickly.

07:46.920 --> 07:49.860
Now, if I wanted to make the next screen at YouTube,

07:49.860 --> 07:53.250
I'll could just go up to file, hit Clone Current Mockup,

07:53.250 --> 07:54.630
and look, I have the same exact thing,

07:54.630 --> 07:55.950
and I can just move from here,

07:55.950 --> 07:57.840
and start deleting things wherever I want,

07:57.840 --> 07:59.310
and changing things around.

07:59.310 --> 08:00.390
Now, when you're done,

08:00.390 --> 08:02.310
and you're happy with what you've got,

08:02.310 --> 08:04.500
all you gotta do, is just go up to file,

08:04.500 --> 08:07.620
and you can save it as whatever you want it to be.

08:07.620 --> 08:09.510
Make sure to back it up.

08:09.510 --> 08:11.280
If you guys are on an older computer,

08:11.280 --> 08:12.600
make sure that you don't have tons and tons

08:12.600 --> 08:14.020
and tons of tabs open. It can really, kind of,

08:14.020 --> 08:15.960
slow down your computer.

08:15.960 --> 08:17.700
But otherwise, just export it to whatever you want.

08:17.700 --> 08:21.060
I like PDF, that's one of the easiest of view.

08:21.060 --> 08:22.230
Or, you can just do it as an image.

08:22.230 --> 08:23.760
That's what you end up sending to your graphic designer

08:23.760 --> 08:25.343
or your developer, and they will thank you,

08:25.343 --> 08:27.003
a thousand times over.

WEBVTT

00:00.270 --> 00:01.103
-: Hey, everyone.

00:01.103 --> 00:01.936
Welcome back.

00:01.936 --> 00:04.230
This section of the course is about metrics,

00:04.230 --> 00:06.840
and this is one of the most important topics

00:06.840 --> 00:08.550
that we will be covering in the course

00:08.550 --> 00:09.990
for a product manager.

00:09.990 --> 00:12.870
As a product manager, basically your entire day,

00:12.870 --> 00:15.660
your entire role actually revolves around metrics

00:15.660 --> 00:16.710
in some way or another.

00:16.710 --> 00:19.410
So we will cover metrics in general,

00:19.410 --> 00:20.340
like what are they?

00:20.340 --> 00:21.780
Why are they important?

00:21.780 --> 00:24.030
Then how do you pick them?

00:24.030 --> 00:26.280
We're gonna go over some real life examples of metrics

00:26.280 --> 00:28.350
at real tech companies,

00:28.350 --> 00:32.310
and then we're gonna talk about some metrics frameworks

00:32.310 --> 00:35.910
that will help you pick and understand your metrics.

00:35.910 --> 00:39.780
So pay close attention, because this is a big section.

00:39.780 --> 00:43.590
So there's a very famous saying that goes,

00:43.590 --> 00:46.470
what gets measured gets managed,

00:46.470 --> 00:48.090
and that is by Peter Drucker,

00:48.090 --> 00:53.090
who is a very famous old school business consultant,

00:53.850 --> 00:55.650
management consultant, that sort of thing.

00:55.650 --> 00:57.900
And he was actually most active

00:57.900 --> 01:00.360
in earlier parts of the 1900s.

01:00.360 --> 01:04.380
That is so true, and metrics are really all about that.

01:04.380 --> 01:06.990
So have you ever been driving down the road

01:06.990 --> 01:11.990
and seen one of these radar automated speed limit signs?

01:12.180 --> 01:13.950
The ones where it says the speed limit,

01:13.950 --> 01:16.680
but then it also uses radar to show you

01:16.680 --> 01:19.260
exactly how fast you are currently going.

01:19.260 --> 01:24.026
These signs have been proven to reduce speed for people

01:24.026 --> 01:25.685
even more effectively

01:25.685 --> 01:29.790
than under threat of getting a ticket from a police officer.

01:29.790 --> 01:32.850
The reason is because the feedback is instantaneous.

01:32.850 --> 01:35.760
So when you get tickets from the police,

01:35.760 --> 01:39.060
you're probably only getting one every few times

01:39.060 --> 01:41.490
that you actually speed and a police officer catches you

01:41.490 --> 01:43.080
and then decides to give you a ticket.

01:43.080 --> 01:45.373
So, you know, depending on how much you speed,

01:45.373 --> 01:48.690
it's however many times you speed

01:48.690 --> 01:50.580
and getting a ticket in that period of time.

01:50.580 --> 01:51.630
But with these signs,

01:51.630 --> 01:54.660
every single time you go past a speed limit sign,

01:54.660 --> 01:55.980
you're getting direct feedback

01:55.980 --> 01:57.870
on here's how fast you're going,

01:57.870 --> 02:00.600
and this has been proven, again, to be way more effective

02:00.600 --> 02:03.510
than just being ticketed for reducing speed.

02:03.510 --> 02:08.010
The reason that this happens is because of human nature,

02:08.010 --> 02:10.230
of a science called feedback loops,

02:10.230 --> 02:13.080
which means that the more frequently

02:13.080 --> 02:15.360
you're getting accurate feedback about something,

02:15.360 --> 02:19.170
the more effectively you are going to manage that.

02:19.170 --> 02:22.800
This same exact principle is behind the success

02:22.800 --> 02:25.500
of the fitness tracker industry.

02:25.500 --> 02:29.550
So people that wear fitness tracking arm bands

02:29.550 --> 02:33.030
or watches or heart rate monitors or whatever,

02:33.030 --> 02:35.430
they're getting instantaneous feedback all the time

02:35.430 --> 02:37.530
about their calories or the way they slept

02:37.530 --> 02:39.030
or what they've been eating,

02:39.030 --> 02:43.650
and this helps people lose weight or become healthier.

02:43.650 --> 02:46.020
And this is the exact same reason,

02:46.020 --> 02:48.810
because they're getting really quick feedback,

02:48.810 --> 02:51.060
every single day or even every hour,

02:51.060 --> 02:53.340
if they're looking at their device pretty frequently,

02:53.340 --> 02:57.030
and this is that science at work.

02:57.030 --> 03:01.440
As a product manager, we can use the same science

03:01.440 --> 03:04.710
to really help our products be successful,

03:04.710 --> 03:09.600
and this whole feedback loop process through metrics

03:09.600 --> 03:12.540
is really the core of Agile development

03:12.540 --> 03:14.550
and your role as a product manager.

03:14.550 --> 03:15.840
So what are metrics?

03:15.840 --> 03:18.570
Well, metrics is just a fancy term for a number

03:18.570 --> 03:21.210
that describes what's going on with your product.

03:21.210 --> 03:23.460
It's a measurement of something,

03:23.460 --> 03:25.830
and sometimes you'll hear the word KPIs,

03:25.830 --> 03:28.680
which stands for key performance indicator,

03:28.680 --> 03:30.720
and this is whatever.

03:30.720 --> 03:33.180
You can use these two terms interchangeably, to be honest.

03:33.180 --> 03:34.440
You'll hear both of them

03:34.440 --> 03:36.240
depending on the company you're working at.

03:36.240 --> 03:38.220
I think KPI sounds a little bit corporate,

03:38.220 --> 03:39.750
so we just use the word metrics.

03:39.750 --> 03:40.583
No big deal.

03:40.583 --> 03:42.180
Some common examples of metrics

03:42.180 --> 03:46.800
are things like monthly active users, returning users,

03:46.800 --> 03:50.613
churned users, things like even app store reviews,

03:51.810 --> 03:55.020
maybe things that people do on certain platforms

03:55.020 --> 03:56.310
like Facebook or Twitter.

03:56.310 --> 03:58.650
It's how many posts have they made.

03:58.650 --> 03:59.940
These are all metrics.

03:59.940 --> 04:00.870
It's just a number

04:00.870 --> 04:03.300
saying how many times someone has done something,

04:03.300 --> 04:07.260
or even in terms of let's say a Netflix

04:07.260 --> 04:09.600
or a streaming music company,

04:09.600 --> 04:11.940
they have metrics for how fast

04:11.940 --> 04:14.850
between the time that the user hits the play button

04:14.850 --> 04:17.370
to when the video starts,

04:17.370 --> 04:18.330
and in this case,

04:18.330 --> 04:21.210
that measurement they're trying to decrease

04:21.210 --> 04:22.860
to be as small as possible,

04:22.860 --> 04:25.020
to have the fastest service, basically.

04:25.020 --> 04:26.790
So metrics are different

04:26.790 --> 04:29.250
depending on the type of the product manager you are,

04:29.250 --> 04:30.660
the industry you're working in,

04:30.660 --> 04:32.160
the company you're working at,

04:32.160 --> 04:34.830
and we're gonna talk a little bit more about that.

04:34.830 --> 04:35.700
As I keep saying,

04:35.700 --> 04:38.610
metrics are a huge part of your life as a product manager,

04:38.610 --> 04:39.930
and as you know,

04:39.930 --> 04:41.790
product managers do a lot of different things

04:41.790 --> 04:43.230
that we've already covered,

04:43.230 --> 04:45.060
but one of the most important things

04:45.060 --> 04:46.950
is that you must accomplish a goal,

04:46.950 --> 04:51.281
and in order to basically accomplish that goal,

04:51.281 --> 04:54.330
you have to first say, what are you accomplishing?

04:54.330 --> 04:57.300
You have to do what we call defining success,

04:57.300 --> 04:59.100
and you do that through metrics.

04:59.100 --> 05:00.570
So you basically say,

05:00.570 --> 05:02.670
hey, these are the numbers we're gonna try to hit

05:02.670 --> 05:05.610
in this metric, by building this thing,

05:05.610 --> 05:08.100
and if we do it, we're successful,

05:08.100 --> 05:11.130
and if we don't, then something's wrong.

05:11.130 --> 05:12.000
If we're getting close,

05:12.000 --> 05:14.280
then we know we're moving in the right direction,

05:14.280 --> 05:17.160
but this is our feedback loop as a product manager.

05:17.160 --> 05:20.640
So back to the real world example on a fitness tracker,

05:20.640 --> 05:22.200
if a person is trying to lose weight,

05:22.200 --> 05:24.510
then they're going to have a goal

05:24.510 --> 05:28.080
of eating under a certain amount of calories

05:28.080 --> 05:31.200
or burning a certain amount of calories per day.

05:31.200 --> 05:32.640
Exact same thing for us.

05:32.640 --> 05:35.250
We have to say, here's what we wanna hit,

05:35.250 --> 05:38.010
and when we get there, that means we're successful.

05:38.010 --> 05:39.960
Again, this is called defining success,

05:39.960 --> 05:41.340
and this is something you've gotta do

05:41.340 --> 05:44.940
before every single endeavor you or your team does

05:44.940 --> 05:46.290
or even your company does.

05:46.290 --> 05:48.990
You have to know what are you actually trying to do here

05:48.990 --> 05:50.130
before you start doing it.

05:50.130 --> 05:52.740
So here's a real world quick example

05:52.740 --> 05:55.200
from like let's say Facebook as an example.

05:55.200 --> 05:57.630
Let's say that Facebook is trying to increase

05:57.630 --> 06:00.750
the amount of time that people spend on Facebook.

06:00.750 --> 06:03.210
Okay, well that's like a company-level goal,

06:03.210 --> 06:07.740
and now let's also say that from this,

06:07.740 --> 06:10.770
we know we want to build a feature

06:10.770 --> 06:14.341
that's going to basically encourage people to stay longer.

06:14.341 --> 06:18.810
But let's say we are a product manager on a smaller team,

06:18.810 --> 06:23.810
and we know that people who comment more on Facebook,

06:24.150 --> 06:27.540
or they make comments on, you know, posts and stuff,

06:27.540 --> 06:30.810
they tend to stay longer than the average person.

06:30.810 --> 06:33.270
So they looked into the data, and we know that.

06:33.270 --> 06:34.710
What we want to do in that case

06:34.710 --> 06:37.740
is look at building a product that,

06:37.740 --> 06:40.530
or building a feature, rather, or improving that product

06:40.530 --> 06:44.190
to basically encourage people to comment more.

06:44.190 --> 06:47.730
And we would say, okay, we're gonna go for a 5% increase

06:47.730 --> 06:51.570
in comments per person on average,

06:51.570 --> 06:55.260
which will in turn affect the average amount of time

06:55.260 --> 06:59.400
that everyone is spending on Facebook in general,

06:59.400 --> 07:01.590
on that top company-level metric.

07:01.590 --> 07:02.940
So next we're gonna take a look

07:02.940 --> 07:05.796
at some real life examples of metrics

07:05.796 --> 07:08.610
at some companies you may have heard of before,

07:08.610 --> 07:10.277
and I'll see you in the next lecture.

07:10.277 --> 07:12.860
(upbeat music)

WEBVTT

00:00.093 --> 00:01.560
-: Hey, so welcome back.

00:01.560 --> 00:03.960
And last lecture we talked about

00:03.960 --> 00:05.640
just the basic intro to metrics.

00:05.640 --> 00:07.170
So what metrics are?

00:07.170 --> 00:10.290
They're just a way to measure stuff.

00:10.290 --> 00:12.210
It's a number that is a measurement

00:12.210 --> 00:13.740
that tells you something about your product.

00:13.740 --> 00:17.640
And it's a huge integral part of product management

00:17.640 --> 00:21.240
because it's the way that we are basically knowing

00:21.240 --> 00:22.530
if we're doing things right or not.

00:22.530 --> 00:26.190
So in this lecture, we are going to chat a little bit

00:26.190 --> 00:28.860
about some real world examples.

00:28.860 --> 00:31.260
And it's basically just going to be me talking about

00:31.260 --> 00:32.130
a couple of companies

00:32.130 --> 00:34.020
and some of the metrics that they track.

00:34.020 --> 00:36.960
And then afterwards, we'll talk about a couple similarities

00:36.960 --> 00:38.820
between these companies.

00:38.820 --> 00:40.440
So the first example is Twitter.

00:40.440 --> 00:43.170
And they have some growth metrics

00:43.170 --> 00:45.360
that a few other companies do as well

00:45.360 --> 00:50.360
which are total new users per month or per week or however.

00:50.730 --> 00:52.710
They can slice it up however they want.

00:52.710 --> 00:54.840
Then you have monthly active users,

00:54.840 --> 00:56.880
probably also daily active users,

00:56.880 --> 01:00.180
and then probably activated users as well per month.

01:00.180 --> 01:03.300
And we'll talk a little bit more about that

01:03.300 --> 01:05.610
a little bit later in this section.

01:05.610 --> 01:08.940
So then they have some engagement metrics as well.

01:08.940 --> 01:13.940
Things like the multiple logins per day.

01:14.160 --> 01:17.130
Probably time spent looking at Twitter,

01:17.130 --> 01:18.690
either the app or the website.

01:18.690 --> 01:21.340
Then they have things like number of tweets sent

01:22.246 --> 01:24.060
per user on average.

01:24.060 --> 01:28.320
And then they have probably the average number of likes.

01:28.320 --> 01:30.000
And the average number of retweets.

01:30.000 --> 01:33.750
And then probably also the average number of follows.

01:33.750 --> 01:36.960
So they have a good idea of the social activity going on.

01:36.960 --> 01:38.460
And then they would also have

01:38.460 --> 01:42.090
probably the number of messages sent,

01:42.090 --> 01:45.330
the private messages in their direct messaging portion

01:45.330 --> 01:46.620
of the site in the app.

01:46.620 --> 01:49.800
So the second example would be YouTube.

01:49.800 --> 01:52.680
And YouTube and many other companies

01:52.680 --> 01:54.750
are going to have the same type of growth metrics

01:54.750 --> 01:55.950
that we already talked about with Twitter.

01:55.950 --> 01:59.550
So you have your monthly active users.

01:59.550 --> 02:02.220
You've got your new users. Your activated users.

02:02.220 --> 02:04.080
Your daily active users.

02:04.080 --> 02:08.340
But where some a lot of tech companies end up differing is

02:08.340 --> 02:09.447
their engagement metrics.

02:09.447 --> 02:10.620
And again, we're going to talk

02:10.620 --> 02:12.720
about the difference between growth and engagement

02:12.720 --> 02:14.040
in another lecture.

02:14.040 --> 02:18.210
But for now, just understand that most companies

02:18.210 --> 02:20.340
will have similar metrics

02:20.340 --> 02:22.050
and they'll have some that are completely different

02:22.050 --> 02:24.060
from any other company.

02:24.060 --> 02:27.780
So on the YouTube example, they probably have stuff

02:27.780 --> 02:31.890
that Twitter doesn't like video views.

02:31.890 --> 02:34.440
First of all, per user, like how many videos

02:34.440 --> 02:37.260
did each user watch on average?

02:37.260 --> 02:40.560
And then they would have some criteria inside of that

02:40.560 --> 02:43.080
like, okay, they must have watched

02:43.080 --> 02:47.100
at least 30 seconds of a video in order to call it a view.

02:47.100 --> 02:49.980
And that's an indicator of quality

02:49.980 --> 02:52.230
or a conservative indicator for them.

02:52.230 --> 02:53.250
Basically letting them know

02:53.250 --> 02:55.680
that this person's actually watching this video.

02:55.680 --> 02:58.380
Secondly, for YouTube, they will probably have

02:58.380 --> 03:03.060
a average viewing time overall per user.

03:03.060 --> 03:07.530
So you could have someone that watched 20 videos,

03:07.530 --> 03:10.800
but if each one of those videos was 1 1/2 minutes long,

03:10.800 --> 03:12.990
that's not as valuable as having someone

03:12.990 --> 03:17.100
that watched one video which was three hours long,

03:17.100 --> 03:19.020
like a documentary or something.

03:19.020 --> 03:22.500
And notice that these are specific to YouTube.

03:22.500 --> 03:24.900
Okay, so then we have our third example

03:24.900 --> 03:27.750
which is the favorite example, Facebook.

03:27.750 --> 03:29.040
And Facebook's got some

03:29.040 --> 03:31.200
really interesting engagement metrics.

03:31.200 --> 03:34.320
Again, on the other metrics, the growth ones,

03:34.320 --> 03:35.580
they're going to have the same sort of stuff

03:35.580 --> 03:37.650
that most people do, most companies

03:37.650 --> 03:42.180
like new users and monthly actives and so on.

03:42.180 --> 03:45.000
But engagement wise, they have some pretty interesting ones.

03:45.000 --> 03:47.250
I've seen some stuff online that talks about them

03:47.250 --> 03:51.180
having metrics like position clicks on the newsfeed.

03:51.180 --> 03:55.080
And that basically means they are tracking

03:55.080 --> 03:59.820
which position on average did a person engage with

03:59.820 --> 04:00.720
in their newsfeed.

04:00.720 --> 04:03.420
So let's say, you open the Facebook app

04:03.420 --> 04:05.700
and you have like something at the top of your newsfeed

04:05.700 --> 04:06.570
from one of your friends

04:06.570 --> 04:09.150
and you have 10 other things under that.

04:09.150 --> 04:11.760
And you engage or you click on or you like,

04:11.760 --> 04:14.310
or you comment on the one

04:14.310 --> 04:16.890
that's like the fifth position down.

04:16.890 --> 04:19.110
Well they actually think that's bad.

04:19.110 --> 04:22.680
They want you to engage with the one that's the first.

04:22.680 --> 04:24.330
And the reason they do that is because

04:24.330 --> 04:26.640
it's a measurement for them of relevancy.

04:26.640 --> 04:30.271
So they basically in their mind,

04:30.271 --> 04:33.390
they want you to see the very most relevant thing,

04:33.390 --> 04:36.570
the thing you wanna engage with or comment on or like

04:36.570 --> 04:38.730
at the very top of your newsfeed.

04:38.730 --> 04:40.800
So of course they track where you're clicking,

04:40.800 --> 04:41.760
or where you're engaging

04:41.760 --> 04:43.680
or where you're watching the videos at

04:43.680 --> 04:46.290
because if it's too far down the page

04:46.290 --> 04:48.810
then they're not doing a good enough job

04:48.810 --> 04:52.950
of ranking your newsfeed based on your interests.

04:52.950 --> 04:56.820
So other than that, they're going to have some metrics

04:56.820 --> 04:59.610
like of obviously the message is sent,

04:59.610 --> 05:02.520
and then time spent on site.

05:02.520 --> 05:05.100
And the interesting thing about Facebook is

05:05.100 --> 05:09.870
that they also own WhatsApp and Instagram, things like that.

05:09.870 --> 05:12.690
So if you leave Facebook and go to WhatsApp

05:12.690 --> 05:15.000
or leave Facebook and go to Instagram,

05:15.000 --> 05:15.930
that's okay with them

05:15.930 --> 05:18.090
because they own those properties as well.

05:18.090 --> 05:19.680
And you're still basically inside

05:19.680 --> 05:23.520
of their universe of companies.

05:23.520 --> 05:28.140
So then they're also going to have the number of likes

05:28.140 --> 05:33.140
that you make on average per day on other people's items

05:33.630 --> 05:35.550
in the newsfeed or wherever.

05:35.550 --> 05:39.960
And then of course they are also going to be tracking things

05:39.960 --> 05:42.990
like the average number of likes

05:42.990 --> 05:47.370
that you as a user get per post.

05:47.370 --> 05:52.370
So on average some people have a higher average

05:53.250 --> 05:55.710
like count per post, obviously,

05:55.710 --> 05:57.390
if they're more popular or whatever,

05:57.390 --> 06:00.390
more famous or celebrities, that sort of thing.

06:00.390 --> 06:04.080
And then the interesting thing about that

06:04.080 --> 06:05.760
is from what I've read,

06:05.760 --> 06:10.760
they actually try to keep these numbers relatively stable.

06:10.920 --> 06:15.920
And they also don't want anyone to not have any likes at all

06:16.440 --> 06:19.620
or any comments at all because if you post something

06:19.620 --> 06:21.570
and you're sharing it with your network

06:21.570 --> 06:24.540
and then no one likes it or comments on it,

06:24.540 --> 06:28.320
then you are less likely to come back and post again.

06:28.320 --> 06:30.150
And of course they want you to do that.

06:30.150 --> 06:34.440
So what they'll do is apparently sort of hold your item

06:34.440 --> 06:37.530
in the newsfeed in front of people longer

06:37.530 --> 06:40.260
so that you know people will see it

06:40.260 --> 06:42.180
and maybe actually interact with it

06:42.180 --> 06:44.400
until you get to some minimum threshold.

06:44.400 --> 06:48.750
And at that point, then it'll start moving down the newsfeed

06:48.750 --> 06:49.830
as a normal item.

06:49.830 --> 06:51.870
But that way you're like, "Oh hey, I got some likes.

06:51.870 --> 06:53.520
Like, I'll come and do this again."

06:53.520 --> 06:55.110
So that's pretty interesting.

06:55.110 --> 06:57.540
Okay, so just to recap real quick.

06:57.540 --> 07:00.540
Each company is going to have a lot of the same metrics

07:00.540 --> 07:03.180
as others on things like new users

07:03.180 --> 07:05.220
and active users and all of that.

07:05.220 --> 07:06.690
But when it comes to engagement,

07:06.690 --> 07:09.150
they're probably going to have some metrics

07:09.150 --> 07:12.360
that are specific to their company, their industry

07:12.360 --> 07:14.190
or what they're trying to get you to do.

07:14.190 --> 07:16.740
And they're really tracking those things closely.

07:16.740 --> 07:19.110
So I know I've been talking a lot

07:19.110 --> 07:21.930
about growth versus engagement metrics

07:21.930 --> 07:23.850
and those are different categories.

07:23.850 --> 07:24.930
And in the next lecture,

07:24.930 --> 07:27.570
we're going to talk about these categories.

07:27.570 --> 07:30.150
So I will see there and pay attention

07:30.150 --> 07:31.650
because it's an important one.

07:32.607 --> 07:35.357
(cheerful music)

WEBVTT

00:00.150 --> 00:02.070
-: All right everyone, welcome back.

00:02.070 --> 00:04.080
And the last lecture,

00:04.080 --> 00:08.400
we talked about some real world examples

00:08.400 --> 00:11.040
of metrics from companies you've probably heard of.

00:11.040 --> 00:14.280
It was YouTube and Twitter and Facebook.

00:14.280 --> 00:15.612
And you might have noticed that,

00:15.612 --> 00:17.880
when I was talking about some of these metrics,

00:17.880 --> 00:21.000
I said that here are some growth metrics,

00:21.000 --> 00:22.740
you know, the new users

00:22.740 --> 00:25.170
and the monthly actives and that sort of thing.

00:25.170 --> 00:27.750
And then, here's the engagement metrics

00:27.750 --> 00:31.360
which are things that were a little bit more specific

00:31.360 --> 00:32.580
to each company.

00:32.580 --> 00:35.304
A lot of times the growth ones were the same

00:35.304 --> 00:39.330
because they were, you know, the more generic metrics.

00:39.330 --> 00:41.280
And then, the engagement ones were very specific

00:41.280 --> 00:42.577
to the platform.

00:42.577 --> 00:44.160
In the Facebook example,

00:44.160 --> 00:48.509
we had the newsfeed position engagement number.

00:48.509 --> 00:52.231
And on YouTube we had the, you know,

00:52.231 --> 00:56.880
number of views that were over 30 seconds

00:56.880 --> 00:58.470
on average per person.

00:58.470 --> 01:01.410
In this section, we're gonna be talking

01:01.410 --> 01:05.130
about these different types, these categories of metrics.

01:05.130 --> 01:09.390
And there are a number of categories of metrics out there.

01:09.390 --> 01:12.780
And they are not, there's no official definition

01:12.780 --> 01:14.130
for any of these,

01:14.130 --> 01:16.500
but this is, in my experience,

01:16.500 --> 01:18.840
how they're usually sectioned off.

01:18.840 --> 01:20.460
So, in practice there's a whole bunch

01:20.460 --> 01:23.100
of these different categories of metrics,

01:23.100 --> 01:25.530
and these are not,

01:25.530 --> 01:27.660
there's no dictionary definition for this

01:27.660 --> 01:29.730
and you're never gonna have the same ones across companies.

01:29.730 --> 01:32.100
But there's sort of general categories that,

01:32.100 --> 01:34.020
in my experience I've seen

01:34.020 --> 01:37.320
at the companies I've either worked with or worked for.

01:37.320 --> 01:38.610
Some of the most common

01:38.610 --> 01:41.340
and the ones we're gonna talk about here are growth

01:41.340 --> 01:43.950
and activation as one category.

01:43.950 --> 01:47.232
Engagement, retention,

01:47.232 --> 01:50.820
then user happiness, and then revenue.

01:50.820 --> 01:54.060
And those are really the big buckets.

01:54.060 --> 01:56.130
So, we'll talk a little bit about each one of these.

01:56.130 --> 01:57.810
We'll give some real life examples

01:57.810 --> 02:00.012
and define a little bit further

02:00.012 --> 02:00.930
what they mean.

02:00.930 --> 02:03.930
The first category is growth and activation.

02:03.930 --> 02:05.370
This is pretty self-explanatory.

02:05.370 --> 02:06.203
This is a bucket

02:06.203 --> 02:09.960
of metrics that basically tracks and measures

02:09.960 --> 02:14.130
and forms you how your company or product is growing.

02:14.130 --> 02:16.830
So, some examples of some growth

02:16.830 --> 02:20.370
and acquisition metrics are gonna be total new users

02:20.370 --> 02:21.720
per month or per week.

02:21.720 --> 02:23.820
They're gonna have new users by source

02:23.820 --> 02:25.260
and we'll talk a little bit about that.

02:25.260 --> 02:27.300
And then activated users.

02:27.300 --> 02:29.490
So, if you're working as a product manager

02:29.490 --> 02:31.770
either on a growth team inside a company

02:31.770 --> 02:33.930
or you are a product manager

02:33.930 --> 02:34.763
at a smaller company,

02:34.763 --> 02:36.870
that's also responsible for growth.

02:36.870 --> 02:39.030
Then you want to know obviously,

02:39.030 --> 02:40.290
how many new users you have.

02:40.290 --> 02:44.520
But you really want to know where these users coming from.

02:44.520 --> 02:48.900
Are they coming from the SEO optimizations you did

02:48.900 --> 02:52.132
which is search engine optimizations,

02:52.132 --> 02:54.030
so that people can find you

02:54.030 --> 02:56.820
via Google and search engines and stuff like that.

02:56.820 --> 02:59.670
Or is it that they're finding you on an app store

02:59.670 --> 03:04.410
or is it that they're finding you on maybe a blog somewhere

03:04.410 --> 03:06.210
or perhaps they're finding you

03:06.210 --> 03:09.090
because one of your users shared some content

03:09.090 --> 03:09.960
from your platform

03:09.960 --> 03:12.852
or your app out to another platform like Twitter

03:12.852 --> 03:14.610
and they click the link and like,

03:14.610 --> 03:15.690
hey, what is this?

03:15.690 --> 03:17.220
These are all very important to track

03:17.220 --> 03:20.280
and that would be new users by source.

03:20.280 --> 03:22.230
So, we're tracking where they're coming from.

03:22.230 --> 03:24.000
So, activation is a big one

03:24.000 --> 03:26.670
because there's a really big difference

03:26.670 --> 03:31.424
between a person that has been considered

03:31.424 --> 03:33.090
someone who's found

03:33.090 --> 03:37.350
and maybe installed your app and an activated user.

03:37.350 --> 03:39.240
So, what usually happens is a company

03:39.240 --> 03:41.280
or product manager will say that

03:41.280 --> 03:45.788
an activated user is someone that has not only downloaded

03:45.788 --> 03:47.970
the app or gone to the website,

03:47.970 --> 03:49.560
but they've actually signed up

03:49.560 --> 03:52.560
and they've performed a particular type of action

03:52.560 --> 03:55.020
like maybe, you know, making their first tweet

03:55.020 --> 03:57.270
in Twitter's example or something like that.

03:57.270 --> 04:00.307
This is hugely important because if you get a million people

04:00.307 --> 04:01.980
to download your app,

04:01.980 --> 04:06.852
but only 500 of them actually get logged in and activated,

04:06.852 --> 04:09.222
well, that's not so useful.

04:09.222 --> 04:11.464
The second one is retention

04:11.464 --> 04:14.694
and retention is tied very closely

04:14.694 --> 04:17.340
to the growth and activation metrics.

04:17.340 --> 04:21.270
It's simply a way to find out who's coming back.

04:21.270 --> 04:23.730
It's just how many people were here last month using

04:23.730 --> 04:27.840
our product that are now here this month using it again.

04:27.840 --> 04:31.290
Some examples of retention metrics are things

04:31.290 --> 04:34.260
like a metric called actually retained users

04:34.260 --> 04:37.950
and one other one is called resurrected users.

04:37.950 --> 04:41.460
Again, returning users is the number

04:41.460 --> 04:44.250
of users that are returning that are here again.

04:44.250 --> 04:47.190
They are continually either week, over week

04:47.190 --> 04:49.290
or day over day or month over month

04:49.290 --> 04:53.730
using your service or your app, your product.

04:53.730 --> 04:58.730
And then, resurrected users is basically something that

04:59.400 --> 05:04.020
we use to describe people that we've gotten back.

05:04.020 --> 05:05.309
So let's say that,

05:05.309 --> 05:09.120
there was a user that was using our product last month

05:09.120 --> 05:11.250
and they're not here this month.

05:11.250 --> 05:12.720
They haven't been using our product this month.

05:12.720 --> 05:15.450
But next month they do come back

05:15.450 --> 05:16.800
because we sent them

05:16.800 --> 05:19.200
either push notification on their mobile phone

05:19.200 --> 05:20.550
or we sent them an email and said,

05:20.550 --> 05:22.650
hey, did you know we have these features?

05:22.650 --> 05:23.700
Or something like that.

05:23.700 --> 05:25.560
Then if they do come back, we can say,

05:25.560 --> 05:27.529
hey, that's a resurrected user.

05:27.529 --> 05:30.360
The implication there is that they were, you know

05:30.360 --> 05:32.190
dead or they were just gone.

05:32.190 --> 05:33.840
So, we are now resurrecting them

05:33.840 --> 05:36.000
and bringing them back into our product.

05:36.000 --> 05:38.310
So, in terms of how those might actually be measured

05:38.310 --> 05:39.720
in number sense,

05:39.720 --> 05:44.720
let's say that a hundred users downloaded our app last month

05:44.940 --> 05:49.620
and got activated, then we see this month that,

05:49.620 --> 05:54.086
60 of them are actually here again this month.

05:54.086 --> 05:55.886
Using the app, we would say that,

05:55.886 --> 05:58.050
our user retention rate is a 60%.

05:58.050 --> 05:59.880
We know that there's something wrong

05:59.880 --> 06:02.190
that 40% of people did not come back.

06:02.190 --> 06:05.130
Those people did not find it a good enough product

06:05.130 --> 06:06.690
and that's how we'd measure it.

06:06.690 --> 06:09.658
Then for the resurrected users,

06:09.658 --> 06:13.890
let's say that those 40 we're now considering gone

06:13.890 --> 06:15.060
like we've sort of lost them

06:15.060 --> 06:16.380
because they're not here this month,

06:16.380 --> 06:20.160
but next month we sent them an email or a push notification,

06:20.160 --> 06:22.170
and let's say all 40 of those people come

06:22.170 --> 06:24.480
back into our app, we would say that

06:24.480 --> 06:28.710
our resurrected user rate was a hundred percent.

06:28.710 --> 06:32.070
Because remember, we have 40 people that are considered

06:32.070 --> 06:33.360
dead or gone.

06:33.360 --> 06:37.500
And now, we are resurrecting all 40 of them means

06:37.500 --> 06:39.510
our resurrection rate is a hundred percent.

06:39.510 --> 06:40.860
Hey, by the way,

06:40.860 --> 06:42.570
you might be wondering about these time periods

06:42.570 --> 06:46.230
the one month or the two months or that sort of thing.

06:46.230 --> 06:47.755
All of these time periods

06:47.755 --> 06:52.410
and actual definitions are really up to each company

06:52.410 --> 06:55.440
and, or product team to decide

06:55.440 --> 06:57.775
the definitions on.

06:57.775 --> 06:59.490
Basically, the only thing that really matters

06:59.490 --> 07:02.010
about your metrics is that you're keeping them consistent.

07:02.010 --> 07:05.130
So, if you're measuring something one way in one period

07:05.130 --> 07:07.380
of time, you have to keep measuring it that way,

07:07.380 --> 07:10.521
in the future in order to be able to compare

07:10.521 --> 07:13.034
to the past and you want to keep these things the same

07:13.034 --> 07:15.390
for as long as possible.

07:15.390 --> 07:19.380
All right, so the next bucket of metrics is engagement.

07:19.380 --> 07:23.940
Engagement is gonna be one of the most common things

07:23.940 --> 07:26.730
for product managers to be tracking that work

07:26.730 --> 07:29.220
at consumer product companies.

07:29.220 --> 07:31.470
Engagement metrics we've already talked about a little bit.

07:31.470 --> 07:32.970
It's things like Twitter.

07:32.970 --> 07:36.364
How many times did a user tweet on average per day?

07:36.364 --> 07:39.240
How many times did they like something, et cetera.

07:39.240 --> 07:41.460
Engagement metrics are usually tailored

07:41.460 --> 07:44.727
per company to encourage a specific type

07:44.727 --> 07:48.720
of behavior depending on the goals

07:48.720 --> 07:51.120
of the company or your industry, basically.

07:51.120 --> 07:53.310
And I'll give you a real world example really quick.

07:53.310 --> 07:56.250
I know that YouTube tracks views, they count someone

07:56.250 --> 08:00.090
as viewed if they've watched 30 seconds of video,

08:00.090 --> 08:01.830
30 seconds of a YouTube video.

08:01.830 --> 08:06.270
Facebook on the other hand, tracks their video views,

08:06.270 --> 08:08.190
I think, based off of three seconds.

08:08.190 --> 08:09.023
So, if someone's watched

08:09.023 --> 08:12.840
for at least three seconds, these are different.

08:12.840 --> 08:14.670
Of course, Facebook is going to report

08:14.670 --> 08:16.260
way more views that way

08:16.260 --> 08:18.180
because it's pretty hard to shut off a video

08:18.180 --> 08:20.902
in three seconds if you're just scrolling past it.

08:20.902 --> 08:23.850
But these metrics, as you can see, are tailored

08:23.850 --> 08:27.750
to the types of metrics that each company wants to report

08:27.750 --> 08:31.560
both externally and internally for reasons of quality

08:31.560 --> 08:36.315
and accuracy, and also being able to be easily compared

08:36.315 --> 08:39.300
to stuff they've tracked in the past.

08:39.300 --> 08:41.370
The next bucket is user happiness.

08:41.370 --> 08:43.680
This is pretty self-explanatory.

08:43.680 --> 08:45.990
It's just a way for us to quantitatively say

08:45.990 --> 08:48.570
how happy are our users.

08:48.570 --> 08:53.570
So, examples of that would be something called NPS scores

08:54.090 --> 08:58.524
or number of customers that have written a complaint

08:58.524 --> 09:00.960
to the customer service department.

09:00.960 --> 09:05.490
And then, even something like the app store rating

09:05.490 --> 09:06.750
for your particular app

09:06.750 --> 09:09.240
and like the Android or iOS app store.

09:09.240 --> 09:12.060
NPS means net promoter score, by the way.

09:12.060 --> 09:13.950
And it's something that was developed a long time ago.

09:13.950 --> 09:16.860
That's pretty ubiquitous from company to company.

09:16.860 --> 09:18.660
It's basically a survey that goes

09:18.660 --> 09:21.570
out to users that tracks how likely they are

09:21.570 --> 09:26.570
to recommend that service or product to other people.

09:26.580 --> 09:28.320
You might have actually seen these before,

09:28.320 --> 09:30.060
if you've ever gotten a survey that says,

09:30.060 --> 09:32.730
will you rate us on a scale of 1 to 10

09:32.730 --> 09:36.340
of how likely they are to recommend our product?

09:36.340 --> 09:39.690
The secret behind this though, by the way, is that,

09:39.690 --> 09:42.600
I think, anything under a seven is basically counted

09:42.600 --> 09:43.590
as a zero.

09:43.590 --> 09:45.750
So, if you can't get a user to at least say,

09:45.750 --> 09:48.243
I'm at least like a seven, eight, or nine or 10,

09:49.740 --> 09:52.710
in terms of a scale of likelihood to recommend this

09:52.710 --> 09:56.220
to other people, then you're basically, you know,

09:56.220 --> 09:59.070
losing out and it's gonna throw off the average.

09:59.070 --> 10:02.543
This is because companies want to kind of set a high bar

10:02.543 --> 10:04.410
for customer happiness.

10:04.410 --> 10:06.330
Overall, happiness metrics are some

10:06.330 --> 10:09.300
of the most difficult to get because it's not as easy

10:09.300 --> 10:11.143
as just looking at a database for, you know,

10:11.143 --> 10:13.530
a number of times someone clicks something,

10:13.530 --> 10:18.530
but they are very important and they are even more relevant

10:19.200 --> 10:24.200
in industries or companies where there's little competition

10:25.080 --> 10:28.650
or where people don't have other options

10:28.650 --> 10:30.360
or it's a total necessity.

10:30.360 --> 10:34.980
Some examples for that would be a cable provider

10:34.980 --> 10:36.630
or an airline.

10:36.630 --> 10:38.610
I know most people out there hate

10:38.610 --> 10:40.200
their cable internet service

10:40.200 --> 10:42.360
or their internet service at home,

10:42.360 --> 10:43.530
but they don't really have a choice

10:43.530 --> 10:46.140
because there's not very many choices available.

10:46.140 --> 10:51.140
So, the cable companies, they see a lot of use, obviously,

10:51.360 --> 10:53.130
but it doesn't mean the customer's happy.

10:53.130 --> 10:54.000
And when they say,

10:54.000 --> 10:55.590
how happy are you to the customers,

10:55.590 --> 10:56.880
they're gonna say, not very.

10:56.880 --> 10:57.930
You guys suck.

10:57.930 --> 10:59.040
All right, so the last category

10:59.040 --> 11:01.770
of metrics we're gonna talk about is revenue metrics.

11:01.770 --> 11:03.780
Revenue metrics, pretty self-explanatory.

11:03.780 --> 11:05.460
How much revenue are we making?

11:05.460 --> 11:07.470
But there's a lot of different ways we can look at this

11:07.470 --> 11:09.870
and these are very important

11:09.870 --> 11:12.813
for product managers that are working

11:12.813 --> 11:15.480
at companies where revenue

11:15.480 --> 11:17.961
from customers is a really big deal.

11:17.961 --> 11:20.820
Or product managers that are working inside

11:20.820 --> 11:24.660
of consumer companies where the consumers are not

11:24.660 --> 11:27.510
paying anything but maybe they're being advertised to,

11:27.510 --> 11:29.490
and that's how you generate revenue.

11:29.490 --> 11:33.576
An example of a revenue metric is LTV,

11:33.576 --> 11:36.120
which stands for lifetime value.

11:36.120 --> 11:39.150
And that basically means we pick a period,

11:39.150 --> 11:41.520
let's say a long period of time, like one year,

11:41.520 --> 11:43.410
and we say that's a customer lifetime.

11:43.410 --> 11:47.580
And then we say over one year, on average,

11:47.580 --> 11:52.580
how much revenue does each customer generate us?

11:52.950 --> 11:54.810
So, within that space of one year,

11:54.810 --> 11:58.170
we're getting, let's say 300 bucks

11:58.170 --> 12:01.260
from each user on average.

12:01.260 --> 12:03.720
That would be our LTV.

12:03.720 --> 12:07.380
Another example would be a metric called CAC,

12:07.380 --> 12:10.950
which is cost of acquisition of customer.

12:10.950 --> 12:15.600
Sometimes it's CCA, which is cost of customer acquisition.

12:15.600 --> 12:16.830
It's all the same thing.

12:16.830 --> 12:20.250
It's basically how much money do we have to spend

12:20.250 --> 12:25.250
in marketing or advertising or even salaries

12:25.890 --> 12:30.690
for salespeople to on average acquire a customer.

12:30.690 --> 12:32.730
This is very important to track

12:32.730 --> 12:36.030
because we want to keep this number as low as possible.

12:36.030 --> 12:37.980
One other example that a lot

12:37.980 --> 12:40.200
of business to business companies put a lot

12:40.200 --> 12:44.790
of value into is a metric called MRR,

12:44.790 --> 12:47.989
which is monthly recurring revenue, and it's counterpart

12:47.989 --> 12:51.480
which is ARR, annual recurring revenue.

12:51.480 --> 12:55.470
This is basically, how much revenue are we making

12:55.470 --> 12:57.630
per month or per year.

12:57.630 --> 13:00.150
And this is all of our customers combined,

13:00.150 --> 13:01.770
all of our users combined.

13:01.770 --> 13:04.920
So, this is for a subscription service.

13:04.920 --> 13:08.700
Let's say Dropbox, for example, they have a hundred users

13:08.700 --> 13:12.690
and each one of those users is paying $10 a month.

13:12.690 --> 13:15.724
Well, then their monthly recurring revenue is gonna be

13:15.724 --> 13:17.760
a thousand bucks.

13:17.760 --> 13:19.080
So, you can see how that works.

13:19.080 --> 13:21.450
This is, of course, a huge indicator of value

13:21.450 --> 13:24.630
for subscription businesses like Dropbox.

13:24.630 --> 13:26.460
All right, so let's recap really quick.

13:26.460 --> 13:28.470
There's a ton of metrics out there

13:28.470 --> 13:31.080
and some of them are gonna be the same across companies.

13:31.080 --> 13:33.120
A lot of them are gonna be different.

13:33.120 --> 13:35.730
But the important thing to know here is that,

13:35.730 --> 13:38.100
there's different categories of metrics.

13:38.100 --> 13:42.060
And the purpose of these categories is to ensure that,

13:42.060 --> 13:43.110
we are covering what's called

13:43.110 --> 13:46.590
the customer life cycle journey, or the customer life cycle.

13:46.590 --> 13:50.040
So, I have metrics to know how many people are finding us.

13:50.040 --> 13:53.010
And then, signing up and then using us

13:53.010 --> 13:56.400
and then I have a metric to measure how happy they are.

13:56.400 --> 13:58.530
And then, I have a metric to measure

13:58.530 --> 14:00.810
how much money are they making us?

14:00.810 --> 14:03.940
And finally, sometimes people even add

14:05.040 --> 14:07.920
how much are they referring us to others to account

14:07.920 --> 14:11.730
for sort of a growth, like a viral growth.

14:11.730 --> 14:14.880
But yeah, we wanna cover basically

14:14.880 --> 14:19.110
the entire life cycle for each individual user.

14:19.110 --> 14:20.760
Now, if you're at a larger company,

14:20.760 --> 14:23.981
you might work on a team that is only focused

14:23.981 --> 14:25.950
on really one of these categories

14:25.950 --> 14:28.950
or one of the metrics inside of that category.

14:28.950 --> 14:31.260
But if you're at a much smaller company,

14:31.260 --> 14:33.300
you might be a product manager that's responsible

14:33.300 --> 14:38.100
for a growth metric and a retention metric

14:38.100 --> 14:42.030
and an engagement metric, and so on and so forth.

14:42.030 --> 14:43.440
All right, so in the next lecture we're gonna get

14:43.440 --> 14:44.505
even further into metrics.

14:44.505 --> 14:46.413
We'll see you there.

14:48.796 --> 14:51.379
(gentle music)

WEBVTT

00:00.180 --> 00:01.320
-: Welcome back, everyone.

00:01.320 --> 00:04.080
In this lecture, we're gonna talk more about metrics.

00:04.080 --> 00:06.540
We're gonna talk about how to pick good metrics,

00:06.540 --> 00:09.000
what makes a good metric in the first place,

00:09.000 --> 00:10.800
and then we'll go into a couple other details

00:10.800 --> 00:14.460
about how metrics roll up into other top-level metrics.

00:14.460 --> 00:16.890
So firstly, let's talk about how metrics

00:16.890 --> 00:18.900
roll up into other metrics.

00:18.900 --> 00:21.720
Let's say that you're working at Facebook

00:21.720 --> 00:26.220
and Facebook's overall metric is to get people

00:26.220 --> 00:29.250
on the site and leave them there for a long time.

00:29.250 --> 00:31.710
You want people to spend as much time

00:31.710 --> 00:33.660
on Facebook as possible.

00:33.660 --> 00:36.330
Well, that doesn't mean that if you're working

00:36.330 --> 00:40.860
on say the photos part of Facebook or the newsfeed part,

00:40.860 --> 00:44.640
that your metric is going to be the amount

00:44.640 --> 00:46.920
of time spent on Facebook

00:46.920 --> 00:49.650
or maybe even on that page, it's likely that it would,

00:49.650 --> 00:52.650
but sometimes it's not the most important metric.

00:52.650 --> 00:54.420
Like I've mentioned before,

00:54.420 --> 00:59.420
there are sometimes things that we know that we can track

00:59.670 --> 01:04.170
on our individual team that makes the overall metric

01:04.170 --> 01:06.390
for the company increase.

01:06.390 --> 01:10.590
The example that we've used is the comments on Facebook.

01:10.590 --> 01:13.170
If we know that people that leave more comments

01:13.170 --> 01:17.709
on Facebook are more likely to spend more time on Facebook

01:17.709 --> 01:21.270
or one of its related apps, then we might be working

01:21.270 --> 01:23.670
on a team that just tries to increase the number

01:23.670 --> 01:26.550
of comments on average per person.

01:26.550 --> 01:27.690
This is because we know

01:27.690 --> 01:30.330
that it will increase the overall metric

01:30.330 --> 01:33.090
which is the metric that the company has a goal

01:33.090 --> 01:34.560
for as a whole.

01:34.560 --> 01:35.610
So, that's it for that part.

01:35.610 --> 01:37.860
I just want you to understand that even

01:37.860 --> 01:40.331
though a company might have an overall metric,

01:40.331 --> 01:44.490
that you as a product manager might not always

01:44.490 --> 01:46.980
be tracking that exact metric.

01:46.980 --> 01:49.020
You might be tracking something for your team.

01:49.020 --> 01:51.270
The second thing I wanna mention is the difference

01:51.270 --> 01:55.650
between exploratory metrics and reporting metrics.

01:55.650 --> 01:58.770
Exploratory metrics are things that you're not

01:58.770 --> 02:03.270
always tracking, or always really telling investors

02:03.270 --> 02:05.790
or your boss or other people

02:05.790 --> 02:08.610
about how they're doing, but they're there.

02:08.610 --> 02:12.240
It's tracking an analysis available to us just

02:12.240 --> 02:15.690
to go poke around and see what user behavior is like.

02:15.690 --> 02:18.690
This contrasts with reporting metrics,

02:18.690 --> 02:21.180
because reporting metrics are the things

02:21.180 --> 02:25.050
that we are tracking over long periods of time to ensure

02:25.050 --> 02:26.970
that our product is doing well

02:26.970 --> 02:28.200
and heading in the right direction.

02:28.200 --> 02:30.630
So, an example of an exploratory metric

02:30.630 --> 02:33.510
would be something where

02:33.510 --> 02:36.060
let's say I'm working at Twitter,

02:36.060 --> 02:39.090
and the overall goal is to increase the amount

02:39.090 --> 02:42.690
of time spent again, on the website or in the app.

02:42.690 --> 02:45.690
An exploratory metric might be the number

02:45.690 --> 02:48.990
of times someone clicks a certain button somewhere,

02:48.990 --> 02:50.460
because we're not really interested

02:50.460 --> 02:52.860
in keeping track of this over time,

02:52.860 --> 02:56.370
but if we as product managers with our team, our engineers

02:56.370 --> 02:59.460
and our designers want to kind of try to figure

02:59.460 --> 03:03.240
out a solution in order to keep people on the site longer,

03:03.240 --> 03:05.460
we might go look at the buttons they're clicking

03:05.460 --> 03:07.470
and the pages they're going to so

03:07.470 --> 03:10.860
that we can tell when they go to this particular page,

03:10.860 --> 03:12.330
all of a sudden they drop off

03:12.330 --> 03:14.550
and they don't come back.

03:14.550 --> 03:18.090
So maybe something about that page is bad and these sorts

03:18.090 --> 03:21.359
of things are just metrics and just data we look

03:21.359 --> 03:24.900
at to help us, inform us

03:24.900 --> 03:26.940
on other decisions.

03:26.940 --> 03:29.850
These exploratory metrics are basically just

03:29.850 --> 03:33.930
data points that we go in and look at kind of hunting

03:33.930 --> 03:38.640
for a little bit of a clue so that we can know how

03:38.640 --> 03:43.440
to potentially build a feature to hit the reporting metric

03:43.440 --> 03:44.850
and make that increase.

03:44.850 --> 03:47.040
So for the purposes of this course

03:47.040 --> 03:49.350
and for the rest of this lecture,

03:49.350 --> 03:51.660
we're gonna be talking about reporting metrics

03:51.660 --> 03:52.980
and these are the metrics that you're tracking

03:52.980 --> 03:55.200
over time to make sure your product is

03:55.200 --> 03:57.900
either getting better or getting worse.

03:57.900 --> 03:59.940
All right, so what makes a good metric?

03:59.940 --> 04:02.370
Well, let me first say that if you are working

04:02.370 --> 04:04.590
at a company or you join a larger company,

04:04.590 --> 04:07.380
I bet they're probably already going to have a bunch

04:07.380 --> 04:10.650
of metrics per team that they are tracking

04:10.650 --> 04:14.580
and that you can already begin to try to optimize

04:14.580 --> 04:17.940
with features and changes and fixes and that sort of thing.

04:17.940 --> 04:21.120
But let's say just for the thought exercise,

04:21.120 --> 04:24.600
that you don't have any metrics to track

04:24.600 --> 04:26.310
and this feature that you've been brought

04:26.310 --> 04:29.790
in to work on with your team is completely new,

04:29.790 --> 04:31.650
so you have to come up with some metrics.

04:31.650 --> 04:33.600
Well, what makes the metrics good?

04:33.600 --> 04:35.820
If I pick some metrics, how do I know if they're good?

04:35.820 --> 04:38.850
The first thing a metric should be is understandable,

04:38.850 --> 04:40.740
and that means you don't need to go

04:40.740 --> 04:43.770
and create some crazy metric like the number

04:43.770 --> 04:45.960
of times someone clicked this button twice

04:45.960 --> 04:46.793
and then went back

04:46.793 --> 04:48.780
and clicked this other thing and did this thing

04:48.780 --> 04:51.510
and then call that a particular metric that you're tracking.

04:51.510 --> 04:54.049
It needs to be something that's relatively simple

04:54.049 --> 04:56.190
that you can explain just

04:56.190 --> 04:58.380
by telling them the name of that metric.

04:58.380 --> 05:00.630
It's usually like something divided

05:00.630 --> 05:03.600
by something else to get a percentage or the number

05:03.600 --> 05:07.530
of times this person or this group of people did this thing.

05:07.530 --> 05:08.820
It's usually pretty simple,

05:08.820 --> 05:11.160
so keep your metrics understandable.

05:11.160 --> 05:12.900
That brings me to the second point,

05:12.900 --> 05:17.130
which is metrics should usually be a rate or ratio,

05:17.130 --> 05:20.220
and this goes back to what we were talking about in regards

05:20.220 --> 05:25.170
to monthly active users versus just total number of users.

05:25.170 --> 05:28.530
You wanna pick a metric that is not something

05:28.530 --> 05:30.210
that's going to look like a hockey stick

05:30.210 --> 05:31.500
no matter what you do.

05:31.500 --> 05:34.710
Imagine if say Twitter or Facebook or something,

05:34.710 --> 05:37.830
just basically tracked the number

05:37.830 --> 05:39.840
of users on their platform or the number

05:39.840 --> 05:42.930
of people that have ever signed up, that's pretty silly

05:42.930 --> 05:45.840
because maybe some spam accounts sign up,

05:45.840 --> 05:48.570
maybe some people sign up and hate it and leave.

05:48.570 --> 05:52.740
What really matters is total number of users.

05:52.740 --> 05:56.040
And then from there, you take the total number

05:56.040 --> 06:00.240
of users that have actually logged in in the last 30 days

06:00.240 --> 06:03.090
or something like that, and we call that monthly active.

06:03.090 --> 06:06.450
So it's a portion of a larger number

06:06.450 --> 06:09.720
and this is the sort of stuff that you want to be doing.

06:09.720 --> 06:10.860
Let's give another example.

06:10.860 --> 06:13.110
Let's say we're working at Airbnb

06:13.110 --> 06:14.580
and for those of you that don't know,

06:14.580 --> 06:18.690
Airbnb is a company that allows you to basically rent

06:18.690 --> 06:22.440
out your house as a sort of independent hotel

06:22.440 --> 06:25.320
to people around the world using their platform.

06:25.320 --> 06:27.741
Would you want, if you're a product manager

06:27.741 --> 06:30.690
at Airbnb, to be tracking

06:30.690 --> 06:33.600
a metric like total number of nights booked?

06:33.600 --> 06:36.690
Well, actually, you would want to know that number

06:36.690 --> 06:39.960
but that number is not going to be very effective in knowing

06:39.960 --> 06:44.010
whether or not you and your team are doing a very good job.

06:44.010 --> 06:46.440
This is because that can be affected

06:46.440 --> 06:48.000
by a whole bunch of stuff.

06:48.000 --> 06:50.511
It could be affected by publicity and marketing.

06:50.511 --> 06:51.750
It could be affected

06:51.750 --> 06:55.215
by any number of other things, maybe the economy, et cetera.

06:55.215 --> 06:59.160
But what we could track is the average number

06:59.160 --> 07:02.730
of nights booked per person in a month

07:02.730 --> 07:04.350
or let's say six months,

07:04.350 --> 07:07.080
whatever might work for the data that you have.

07:07.080 --> 07:09.750
This is basically measuring a ratio

07:09.750 --> 07:11.040
which is super important

07:11.040 --> 07:13.410
because the numbers are much smaller,

07:13.410 --> 07:16.140
and they are more descriptive

07:16.140 --> 07:18.690
of whether or not your product is good.

07:18.690 --> 07:21.270
The third thing I want to point out is correlation.

07:21.270 --> 07:24.990
You don't want to look at two metrics that are correlated

07:24.990 --> 07:28.530
and then assume something from those that isn't true.

07:28.530 --> 07:29.550
I'll give you an example.

07:29.550 --> 07:33.119
So there's an old sort of metrics joke that basically says,

07:33.119 --> 07:37.170
if you look at revenue for ice cream sales and then you look

07:37.170 --> 07:42.060
at a metric for the number of people that drown

07:42.060 --> 07:46.230
in a pool and die, those numbers,

07:46.230 --> 07:48.750
those metrics, if you looked at them on a graph,

07:48.750 --> 07:52.470
they would track each other pretty closely.

07:52.470 --> 07:53.910
And do you know why that is?

07:53.910 --> 07:55.980
This is because people eat ice cream

07:55.980 --> 07:58.950
in the summer obviously, and they swim also

07:58.950 --> 08:00.900
in the summer because it's hot out.

08:00.900 --> 08:03.120
But you might be led to believe

08:03.120 --> 08:04.350
if you're not paying attention,

08:04.350 --> 08:07.860
that when people eat more ice cream, more people drown,

08:07.860 --> 08:09.480
which makes no sense at all.

08:09.480 --> 08:11.550
So you have to be careful about these things.

08:11.550 --> 08:13.860
So the next point I wanna bring out is

08:13.860 --> 08:16.230
that you should pick a metric that is actually

08:16.230 --> 08:20.160
able to be changed and with relative ease

08:20.160 --> 08:21.810
or you should position your metric

08:21.810 --> 08:25.050
so that when you do make changes, the metric is changing,

08:25.050 --> 08:26.550
either going up or down.

08:26.550 --> 08:27.840
Here's an example of that.

08:27.840 --> 08:31.170
Let's say that you have a particular type

08:31.170 --> 08:34.710
of user that let's just say,

08:34.710 --> 08:36.210
only has like one

08:36.210 --> 08:41.210
or two hours available at all during the average day

08:41.250 --> 08:43.020
and there is no more time

08:43.020 --> 08:45.510
than just those two hours in the day.

08:45.510 --> 08:46.410
In that case,

08:46.410 --> 08:51.410
you probably wouldn't want to try to maximize the time spent

08:52.560 --> 08:55.290
in your product or in your app

08:55.290 --> 09:00.090
beyond the two-hour mark or maybe not even measure it all.

09:00.090 --> 09:02.670
This is because there's some external force, again,

09:02.670 --> 09:05.490
in our thought experiment, where the users only

09:05.490 --> 09:08.310
have that amount of time per day.

09:08.310 --> 09:11.040
In that case, you may want to measure something

09:11.040 --> 09:12.960
like the number of times per week

09:12.960 --> 09:14.310
that they're using your product

09:14.310 --> 09:16.740
or maybe the number of times that they log in.

09:16.740 --> 09:18.840
In that case, you might want to measure the number

09:18.840 --> 09:22.950
of times per week that the users are using your product.

09:22.950 --> 09:25.290
Are they coming back really frequently?

09:25.290 --> 09:27.390
We're not gonna care about how much time they're spending

09:27.390 --> 09:29.310
per day because we assume

09:29.310 --> 09:31.560
once we've got them near the two-hour mark,

09:31.560 --> 09:32.940
they're not gonna be able to spend more time

09:32.940 --> 09:34.530
in there anyways depending on sort of

09:34.530 --> 09:35.640
the nature of the product.

09:35.640 --> 09:37.680
One more quick example for that is,

09:37.680 --> 09:39.960
let's say you're running an e-commerce business

09:39.960 --> 09:44.730
and you sell something that people only buy,

09:44.730 --> 09:47.730
like they just only buy once per month.

09:47.730 --> 09:50.730
Like 90% of people only buy once per month,

09:50.730 --> 09:53.160
let's say some sort of fancy clothes.

09:53.160 --> 09:55.530
In that case, if the users are coming in once

09:55.530 --> 09:57.930
per month and it's very unlikely that they're gonna come

09:57.930 --> 10:00.090
in more than once per month to use your product

10:00.090 --> 10:02.910
or buy something from your e-commerce platform,

10:02.910 --> 10:05.910
then it's gonna be very difficult to get them to come

10:05.910 --> 10:07.470
in more than that.

10:07.470 --> 10:10.110
And of course, we would want to try,

10:10.110 --> 10:13.050
but it's going to be something that involves a lot more

10:13.050 --> 10:14.550
than just our product team.

10:14.550 --> 10:16.170
It's probably gonna be marketing

10:16.170 --> 10:17.700
and all sorts of other stuff.

10:17.700 --> 10:19.830
So what might we track instead?

10:19.830 --> 10:20.663
Well, in that case,

10:20.663 --> 10:23.280
we might just try to get them to spend more money

10:23.280 --> 10:27.380
for that one time that they do shop with us once per month.

10:27.380 --> 10:29.670
So let's do a really quick recap.

10:29.670 --> 10:34.560
We know that metrics should be easily understandable.

10:34.560 --> 10:37.680
We know that people should be able to know

10:37.680 --> 10:40.080
what they're about as soon as they read

10:40.080 --> 10:42.210
the sort of description of that metric.

10:42.210 --> 10:44.670
You shouldn't pick metrics based on

10:44.670 --> 10:47.730
an erroneous correlation like the ice cream

10:47.730 --> 10:49.380
and drowning deaths example.

10:49.380 --> 10:53.520
You want to pick metrics that actually do impact each other

10:53.520 --> 10:57.120
and are not just tracking each other due to circumstance.

10:57.120 --> 10:59.100
You also want to have a ratio

10:59.100 --> 11:02.670
or a rate rather than just a number that might go

11:02.670 --> 11:04.890
up when marketing does some sales,

11:04.890 --> 11:07.530
or when your company gets a lot of publicity.

11:07.530 --> 11:09.854
And we want to make sure our metrics are

11:09.854 --> 11:12.870
with relative ease, able to be affected.

11:12.870 --> 11:16.980
So we don't want to really try to change a number

11:16.980 --> 11:20.850
or metric that our users are very unlikely

11:20.850 --> 11:23.370
to behave differently within.

11:23.370 --> 11:25.080
All right, so that's it for this one

11:25.080 --> 11:27.090
and next, we're gonna talk about

11:27.090 --> 11:30.690
some metrics frameworks to actually help you

11:30.690 --> 11:32.310
think through the process

11:32.310 --> 11:35.460
of choosing metrics and brainstorm a little bit.

11:35.460 --> 11:36.638
See you there.

11:36.638 --> 11:39.638
(soft upbeat music)

response.title: Using the HEART Metrics Framework - Part 1

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.180 --> 00:02.070
-: Hey everyone, welcome back.

00:02.070 --> 00:03.960
So in the last lecture we talked

00:03.960 --> 00:06.210
about what makes a good metric,

00:06.210 --> 00:08.310
and all of that and the talks before that,

00:08.310 --> 00:11.310
very useful in terms of understanding metrics.

00:11.310 --> 00:13.830
But now you might be wondering, gosh,

00:13.830 --> 00:16.770
there are so many things that I can pick from.

00:16.770 --> 00:18.690
How do I even get started thinking

00:18.690 --> 00:21.660
about what metrics are important to me

00:21.660 --> 00:25.590
as a product manager at a company or me as an entrepreneur?

00:25.590 --> 00:29.280
Which ones should I even think about tracking,

00:29.280 --> 00:33.240
and is there any sort of cheat sheet for common metrics?

00:33.240 --> 00:35.310
What we're gonna talk about in this lecture is

00:35.310 --> 00:39.150
a metrics framework called the HEART metrics framework.

00:39.150 --> 00:42.060
And this isn't exactly a cheat sheet

00:42.060 --> 00:44.250
but it does help you think

00:44.250 --> 00:47.220
through the process of which sorts

00:47.220 --> 00:49.980
of metrics you should be thinking about to make sure

00:49.980 --> 00:51.990
that you know what's going on in your product.

00:51.990 --> 00:55.670
So this metrics framework was originally popularized

00:55.670 --> 00:59.430
by a girl named Kerry Rodden at Google Ventures

00:59.430 --> 01:01.170
and she's a UX researcher

01:01.170 --> 01:02.970
and does some really fantastic work.

01:02.970 --> 01:05.790
So why should we use the HEART framework?

01:05.790 --> 01:07.530
Well, it's an acronym,

01:07.530 --> 01:10.770
so if you know it's an acronym, you know it's good.

01:10.770 --> 01:12.870
Executives love acronyms.

01:12.870 --> 01:16.050
Secondly, metrics are harder than you think.

01:16.050 --> 01:18.120
We've been talking about a lot of stuff.

01:18.120 --> 01:20.550
So it's nice to have a little bit of a framework

01:20.550 --> 01:22.590
to help you come up with the metrics

01:22.590 --> 01:23.580
you should be thinking about.

01:23.580 --> 01:26.760
So this again, is just a really simple way to think

01:26.760 --> 01:28.560
about the customer journey.

01:28.560 --> 01:30.810
And by that I mean, what I've mentioned before,

01:30.810 --> 01:33.180
which is, the user has to first of all

01:33.180 --> 01:34.200
be aware of your product,

01:34.200 --> 01:35.910
then they have to come in

01:35.910 --> 01:38.940
and download it, maybe if it's an app,

01:38.940 --> 01:40.890
then they actually have to sign in

01:40.890 --> 01:42.450
if you need them to sign in.

01:42.450 --> 01:44.340
And there's just this whole journey

01:44.340 --> 01:47.400
that you have to get them activated, they have to come back.

01:47.400 --> 01:49.650
And then from there, there's a whole bunch

01:49.650 --> 01:51.360
of different things you should be thinking about.

01:51.360 --> 01:53.970
Like how much money am I making off of these users?

01:53.970 --> 01:56.070
Are these users actually liking it?

01:56.070 --> 01:57.960
Do they like it as much as they use it?

01:57.960 --> 01:58.793
And so forth.

01:58.793 --> 01:59.850
So what we're doing here

01:59.850 --> 02:03.240
with the screen is I'm gonna show you a table.

02:03.240 --> 02:05.730
And the reason I'm going to show you a table

02:05.730 --> 02:08.700
and a real life example of this,

02:08.700 --> 02:12.450
is because if you look up this HEART metrics framework

02:12.450 --> 02:16.590
on the internet, there's actually not too many good sources.

02:16.590 --> 02:19.987
In fact, the first Google result is this one,

02:19.987 --> 02:22.680
"How to choose the right UX metric for your product,"

02:22.680 --> 02:25.740
which is the original post that Kerry made

02:25.740 --> 02:27.390
describing this framework.

02:27.390 --> 02:30.870
However, there are no graphics associated with it,

02:30.870 --> 02:33.030
so that's why we're doing this

02:33.030 --> 02:35.580
because I wanna show you a real-life example.

02:35.580 --> 02:37.830
So let's come up with a fictional company

02:37.830 --> 02:39.540
and what is really hot right now?

02:39.540 --> 02:42.810
Well, we know Uber, the car ride

02:42.810 --> 02:46.950
or ride sharing service is a really hot topic right now.

02:46.950 --> 02:50.250
Everyone loves Uber and maybe some people don't

02:50.250 --> 02:51.810
but anyways, everyone knows about it.

02:51.810 --> 02:53.490
How about Uber for X?

02:53.490 --> 02:54.930
If you don't know what I'm talking about

02:54.930 --> 02:59.220
there's this trend recently where, ever since Uber came out,

02:59.220 --> 03:03.990
people have been having these on-demand location based apps

03:03.990 --> 03:05.460
for pretty much everything

03:05.460 --> 03:09.090
like dog grooming and all sorts of weird stuff.

03:09.090 --> 03:10.770
So it's pretty funny that there's an Uber

03:10.770 --> 03:12.630
for pretty much everything.

03:12.630 --> 03:15.060
What are two other things people like?

03:15.060 --> 03:16.620
How about alcohol?

03:16.620 --> 03:17.760
Everyone loves alcohol.

03:17.760 --> 03:19.650
Then how about lemonade?

03:19.650 --> 03:21.510
Everyone loves lemonade, and if you don't,

03:21.510 --> 03:24.060
you should get into lemonade, it's pretty good.

03:24.060 --> 03:26.970
And I say that coming from the Southern United States

03:26.970 --> 03:29.880
originally, where the lemonade is really good.

03:29.880 --> 03:33.060
So let's imagine that our fictional company

03:33.060 --> 03:36.180
or product here is Uber for spiked lemonade.

03:36.180 --> 03:37.590
And for those of you that don't know,

03:37.590 --> 03:39.330
spiked means alcoholic.

03:39.330 --> 03:44.220
So basically this is going to be an on-demand location app

03:44.220 --> 03:49.220
for the delivery of alcoholic lemonade anywhere you are.

03:49.470 --> 03:50.550
Sounds pretty good.

03:50.550 --> 03:55.550
So the HEART framework works off of the acronym HEART,

03:56.280 --> 03:59.010
and they stand for these five things,

03:59.010 --> 04:03.480
happiness, engagement, adoption and retention.

04:03.480 --> 04:06.120
And then finally we have task success.

04:06.120 --> 04:07.830
We're gonna talk a little bit about each of those.

04:07.830 --> 04:10.770
So happiness is how happy is your user?

04:10.770 --> 04:13.020
Remember when we were talking about the fact

04:13.020 --> 04:16.380
that you may have a ton of usage from your product?

04:16.380 --> 04:19.410
Let's say if you are a cable company,

04:19.410 --> 04:21.240
you know cable companies are notorious

04:21.240 --> 04:23.820
for having unhappy customers, but sometimes

04:23.820 --> 04:26.550
there's no other choices, there's no other options.

04:26.550 --> 04:29.700
So if you were working at a cable company for internet

04:29.700 --> 04:32.940
for instance, then you might have a ton of usage

04:32.940 --> 04:36.060
but that's no indicator of how happy your customers are.

04:36.060 --> 04:39.150
This is why we need to talk about customer happiness.

04:39.150 --> 04:42.090
Then we have engagement, and we've talked about this before.

04:42.090 --> 04:44.520
How engaged is your user in the short term?

04:44.520 --> 04:47.160
This is different than over the very long-term.

04:47.160 --> 04:49.110
It's not if they're coming back month over month

04:49.110 --> 04:52.290
but are they coming back within short time spans?

04:52.290 --> 04:53.730
The A stands for adoption.

04:53.730 --> 04:57.360
So how many users have actually tried your product?

04:57.360 --> 04:58.920
Have they even logged in?

04:58.920 --> 04:59.850
Maybe they've heard about it.

04:59.850 --> 05:01.440
Yeah, but have they tried it?

05:01.440 --> 05:03.510
Have they gotten in there and placed an order?

05:03.510 --> 05:06.180
The R is for retention, which we've talked about before

05:06.180 --> 05:08.220
when we talked about growth metrics.

05:08.220 --> 05:10.980
So retention is, by the way,

05:10.980 --> 05:12.960
it could be considered an engagement metric,

05:12.960 --> 05:15.720
depends on where you're working or how things are organized.

05:15.720 --> 05:19.410
But retention is basically are your users returning

05:19.410 --> 05:20.730
over a long period of time?

05:20.730 --> 05:24.180
Most commonly it's, are they returning every single month?

05:24.180 --> 05:27.690
Then we have this concept of task success

05:27.690 --> 05:30.300
which is the final letter in this framework.

05:30.300 --> 05:34.200
And this is all about you saying, okay,

05:34.200 --> 05:37.440
there's one thing at least, the most important thing

05:37.440 --> 05:40.080
that someone should be able to do with my product.

05:40.080 --> 05:41.490
Are users doing it?

05:41.490 --> 05:43.560
Let's pick a metric for it and find out.

05:43.560 --> 05:45.660
So that forms the left side of the table.

05:45.660 --> 05:47.850
So then across the top we have three things.

05:47.850 --> 05:49.860
So there's three columns in our table.

05:49.860 --> 05:53.100
It's goals, signals, and metrics.

05:53.100 --> 05:55.650
Goals is just, okay, what do you want to happen?

05:55.650 --> 05:56.820
What is our goal?

05:56.820 --> 06:00.150
Do we want to maximize or minimize the number

06:00.150 --> 06:03.330
of times a user does a particular thing?

06:03.330 --> 06:06.630
Or another example is, do we want to minimize the amount

06:06.630 --> 06:07.980
of time they wait?

06:07.980 --> 06:09.720
There's something that we're trying to get better

06:09.720 --> 06:11.340
and better up to

06:11.340 --> 06:14.730
as close to absolutely optimum as possible.

06:14.730 --> 06:16.290
That's what goals is.

06:16.290 --> 06:18.840
The signals column is really simple.

06:18.840 --> 06:22.170
It doesn't seem so, but it's literally just,

06:22.170 --> 06:25.710
what is the actual thing that we need to measure

06:25.710 --> 06:29.550
in order to know if we are getting closer to that goal?

06:29.550 --> 06:30.960
The third column is the easiest

06:30.960 --> 06:32.600
and that is the metrics column.

06:32.600 --> 06:37.140
It is basically, how do we take the goal and the signal

06:37.140 --> 06:41.130
and express it as an actual metric over time?

06:41.130 --> 06:44.280
And that would be the thing that we're actually looking at

06:44.280 --> 06:45.990
as a number at the end of the day

06:45.990 --> 06:47.760
to know if our product is doing well or not.

06:47.760 --> 06:50.910
So together, the left side and the top look like this.

06:50.910 --> 06:53.010
But really quick, I wanted to mention something

06:53.010 --> 06:55.740
about this framework since it is an acronym,

06:55.740 --> 07:00.740
and that is that as you can see, the HEART, the word heart,

07:00.840 --> 07:04.440
spelled out, leads it to be spelled out

07:04.440 --> 07:06.450
in terms of happiness and then engagement

07:06.450 --> 07:09.240
and then adoption, then retention.

07:09.240 --> 07:12.390
And in fact, if we were to order it

07:12.390 --> 07:15.390
in terms of how a customer actually uses our product,

07:15.390 --> 07:18.180
it would look a little bit something more like this,

07:18.180 --> 07:20.400
which is first, they adopt something,

07:20.400 --> 07:23.970
then they have a successful task that they've done,

07:23.970 --> 07:25.650
that thing we want them to do.

07:25.650 --> 07:28.350
Then they have other signs of engagement.

07:28.350 --> 07:31.050
Finally, they are either being retained

07:31.050 --> 07:33.540
over the long period of time or not.

07:33.540 --> 07:36.780
And then the one on the side, at the bottom,

07:36.780 --> 07:40.020
is the happiness, which is just,

07:40.020 --> 07:41.070
is the customer happy?

07:41.070 --> 07:42.360
And the reason this is the last thing

07:42.360 --> 07:45.030
is because you can gauge this at any point in time,

07:45.030 --> 07:47.910
you could do it the first time they've ordered something

07:47.910 --> 07:50.250
or you could do it six months later, whatever.

07:50.250 --> 07:51.360
It's really up to you.

07:51.360 --> 07:53.520
It's a little bit separate, but in any case,

07:53.520 --> 07:54.990
I wanted to call your attention to this
