07:54.990 --> 07:56.793
just because it's an acronym.

07:57.930 --> 07:59.520
It may be a little bit confusing

07:59.520 --> 08:00.990
but they just rearranged the letters

08:00.990 --> 08:02.310
so that it spells out HEART.

08:02.310 --> 08:03.960
Okay, so let's take

08:03.960 --> 08:07.530
our Uber for Spiked Lemonade company example,

08:07.530 --> 08:10.140
and let's go down the goals column first

08:10.140 --> 08:12.030
and list out what our goals might be.

08:12.030 --> 08:13.710
Okay, starting with happiness.

08:13.710 --> 08:17.850
What is happiness for the user of our app here?

08:17.850 --> 08:21.630
Well, we want to maximize the drinker satisfaction

08:21.630 --> 08:22.800
with our spiked lemonade

08:22.800 --> 08:25.800
and the delivery service that brings it to them.

08:25.800 --> 08:28.620
So, for the engagement part of the framework here

08:28.620 --> 08:31.800
for our product, we're going to try to maximize

08:31.800 --> 08:34.860
the order value, or the number of orders

08:34.860 --> 08:36.660
in a shorter period of time.

08:36.660 --> 08:39.450
And the way we might maximize the order value

08:39.450 --> 08:43.380
for instance, is eventually doing some add-on stuff

08:43.380 --> 08:45.330
like little umbrellas in your glass

08:45.330 --> 08:47.700
or extra-fast delivery or something like that.

08:47.700 --> 08:51.270
So for adoption, we want to maximize the number

08:51.270 --> 08:53.160
of people that have adopted the product.

08:53.160 --> 08:55.380
And for us, that's basically going to be

08:55.380 --> 08:57.030
the number of people who have ordered

08:57.030 --> 09:00.570
at least once, successfully, with our product.

09:00.570 --> 09:04.500
For the retention part we are going to do the standard,

09:04.500 --> 09:08.100
okay, every month we want to have users come back.

09:08.100 --> 09:09.750
So out of our total user base

09:09.750 --> 09:12.330
we want the highest percentage possible

09:12.330 --> 09:14.250
to come back month after month.

09:14.250 --> 09:16.140
If we were able to get 100%

09:16.140 --> 09:19.560
of everyone that's ever used our app to come back

09:19.560 --> 09:21.660
and do it again and again every single month,

09:21.660 --> 09:23.130
that would be ideal.

09:23.130 --> 09:24.690
So that's what we're maximizing here.

09:24.690 --> 09:26.970
So task success could be a couple things.

09:26.970 --> 09:30.810
We could minimize the number of abandoned carts,

09:30.810 --> 09:33.150
and that's the people who put stuff

09:33.150 --> 09:34.890
into the ordering cart

09:34.890 --> 09:38.250
and then you know, delete the app or turn it off

09:38.250 --> 09:39.750
or just exit or whatever,

09:39.750 --> 09:41.640
and they decide they don't want any spiked lemonade.

09:41.640 --> 09:43.020
We wanna minimize that.

09:43.020 --> 09:45.450
And then we might even go so far as to say,

09:45.450 --> 09:48.600
we take pride in how fast you can get this spike lemonade,

09:48.600 --> 09:52.410
not only to the delivery, but how fast you can do any app.

09:52.410 --> 09:56.700
So let's say that we want people to be able to get that

09:56.700 --> 09:58.920
in under five minutes from the app.

09:58.920 --> 10:02.010
So let's maximize the number of people

10:02.010 --> 10:04.610
who complete their order in that five minute window.

response.title: Using the HEART Metrics Framework - Part 2

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.120 --> 00:02.280
-: So let's go onto the signals column.

00:02.280 --> 00:06.240
So what signals, what things are we going to track

00:06.240 --> 00:08.580
in order to know about happiness?

00:08.580 --> 00:11.100
Well, how about the app store rating?

00:11.100 --> 00:12.750
That's a pretty good signal

00:12.750 --> 00:15.630
for the happiness of people using the app.

00:15.630 --> 00:17.790
If you don't believe me, just go to any app,

00:17.790 --> 00:19.800
any popular app, look at all the ratings

00:19.800 --> 00:21.750
and people are saying, "Oh, I love this thing."

00:21.750 --> 00:23.070
Or, "Oh, I hate this thing."

00:23.070 --> 00:26.100
This is a really good number to judge happiness from.

00:26.100 --> 00:29.460
Secondly, we could track these scores from the NPS survey.

00:29.460 --> 00:30.690
Do you remember what that was?

00:30.690 --> 00:32.010
Net promoter score.

00:32.010 --> 00:34.680
That's just kind of a industry standard way

00:34.680 --> 00:38.850
of measuring user happiness through a score

00:38.850 --> 00:43.620
through one to 10 that they say, I am very likely

00:43.620 --> 00:46.230
or not very likely to recommend this to a friend.

00:46.230 --> 00:47.760
So how about for engagement?

00:47.760 --> 00:49.260
What signal should we track there?

00:49.260 --> 00:51.480
Well, how about the number of orders per customer,

00:51.480 --> 00:54.000
let's say in a period of a day or something?

00:54.000 --> 00:56.940
And then how about the order value?

00:56.940 --> 01:01.080
So we wanna get them to order as much stuff in one,

01:01.080 --> 01:03.060
basically, order as possible,

01:03.060 --> 01:05.400
as many dollars worth of product

01:05.400 --> 01:07.260
as we can possibly get them to do.

01:07.260 --> 01:10.170
So what do we need to track for adoption as a signal?

01:10.170 --> 01:13.170
What is that thing that we need to be able to extract

01:13.170 --> 01:14.670
from a database or whatever?

01:14.670 --> 01:19.620
Well, we know that we need to know the number of people

01:19.620 --> 01:22.920
who have ordered at least one glass of lemonade

01:22.920 --> 01:24.210
using our app.

01:24.210 --> 01:26.400
That's what we are calling adopted.

01:26.400 --> 01:30.030
These people have adopted our product

01:30.030 --> 01:32.190
because they've tried it at least once.

01:32.190 --> 01:33.570
That's a thing we need to track,

01:33.570 --> 01:36.060
who has done this at least once.

01:36.060 --> 01:38.790
Now, on the other hand we need to be able to find out

01:38.790 --> 01:42.600
how that compares to people who have not adopted the product

01:42.600 --> 01:44.280
but they have downloaded the app

01:44.280 --> 01:47.280
or downloaded and then uninstalled it, for instance.

01:47.280 --> 01:50.010
So we also need to know the number of people

01:50.010 --> 01:52.080
that have downloaded it in total

01:52.080 --> 01:54.090
because we wanna be able to compare these two, remember?

01:54.090 --> 01:57.060
So the signal for retention is just gonna be,

01:57.060 --> 02:00.030
we just need to know who has ordered spike lemonade

02:00.030 --> 02:03.630
and we actually also need to know the time periods

02:03.630 --> 02:05.640
when they have done that so that we can say,

02:05.640 --> 02:07.200
okay this was in one month

02:07.200 --> 02:10.590
and then this other time they did it in the next month.

02:10.590 --> 02:13.050
But we're assuming because this is signals,

02:13.050 --> 02:14.730
this is the thing we're tracking,

02:14.730 --> 02:16.590
this stuff is in a database somewhere,

02:16.590 --> 02:19.740
databases always carry what's called a timestamp.

02:19.740 --> 02:22.590
So we always know when a particular thing happened

02:22.590 --> 02:23.460
in a database.

02:23.460 --> 02:27.510
So for task success, what should our signals be there?

02:27.510 --> 02:30.540
Well, how about the number of people

02:30.540 --> 02:32.880
who did not complete an order?

02:32.880 --> 02:34.230
What do we need from that?

02:34.230 --> 02:36.630
Like what in the database should we track?

02:36.630 --> 02:39.390
How about just the people that have added something

02:39.390 --> 02:43.140
to their their cart and they never actually went and clicked

02:43.140 --> 02:45.900
on the, you know, finalize purchase button?

02:45.900 --> 02:46.860
And if you remember,

02:46.860 --> 02:51.120
we were concerned with the people that are ordering

02:51.120 --> 02:52.410
within that five minute window

02:52.410 --> 02:55.230
because we want our app to be super quick to order stuff.

02:55.230 --> 02:58.380
So we need to know the amount of time

02:58.380 --> 03:00.750
that people are spending from opening the app and saying,

03:00.750 --> 03:02.370
yeah I want to buy lemonade,

03:02.370 --> 03:04.770
all the way to the time where they have successfully clicked

03:04.770 --> 03:07.170
that sort of finalize purchase button.

03:07.170 --> 03:09.960
So, the last column metrics, this is the easy one.

03:09.960 --> 03:12.750
We just take our goal and we take our signal,

03:12.750 --> 03:14.760
which is a thing that we were actually

03:14.760 --> 03:16.890
physically going to track in the database

03:16.890 --> 03:21.000
and we just say over a period of time this is the metric.

03:21.000 --> 03:22.920
So for the happiness one,

03:22.920 --> 03:26.280
we said that we were going to track the app store rating.

03:26.280 --> 03:29.220
So for happiness as a metric over time,

03:29.220 --> 03:31.710
we just say app store rating over time.

03:31.710 --> 03:34.200
What's the app store rating from month to month?

03:34.200 --> 03:35.130
That's our metric.

03:35.130 --> 03:38.010
Then because we're also sending out those NPS surveys

03:38.010 --> 03:41.010
like I mentioned, we may also want to say,

03:41.010 --> 03:44.430
okay how many people have given us a perfect 10 score?

03:44.430 --> 03:46.620
What percentage of total users

03:46.620 --> 03:49.350
that have actually filled out the survey

03:49.350 --> 03:51.450
have given us a perfect score?

03:51.450 --> 03:53.790
Those are two really good indicators of happiness.

03:53.790 --> 03:54.990
So for the engagement,

03:54.990 --> 03:57.900
as a product manager at this company or a CEO,

03:57.900 --> 04:00.510
we're gonna wanna know what is the average order value

04:00.510 --> 04:03.480
of all of our customers per day?

04:03.480 --> 04:06.060
By that I mean, let's say we have a hundred customers

04:06.060 --> 04:11.060
and all of them are placing orders in between $1 and $10,

04:11.070 --> 04:12.930
if the average is $5

04:12.930 --> 04:14.670
then that's the number we're seeing

04:14.670 --> 04:16.620
when we pull up our metric screen

04:16.620 --> 04:20.580
and we want to basically move that closer and closer

04:20.580 --> 04:22.860
to 10 or further than that

04:22.860 --> 04:24.990
if we're shooting for a much larger number.

04:24.990 --> 04:28.050
Then we want to know the average number of orders

04:28.050 --> 04:31.350
that the users make per day.

04:31.350 --> 04:34.440
So, let's say again, we have a hundred users

04:34.440 --> 04:39.440
and on average like 90 of them are placing one order a day,

04:40.230 --> 04:41.550
but there's some power users,

04:41.550 --> 04:43.440
there's 10 people that order like five

04:43.440 --> 04:45.090
or six of these per day.

04:45.090 --> 04:47.070
We take all of that and we average it out,

04:47.070 --> 04:49.620
that's the number that we'll be tracking over time.

04:49.620 --> 04:53.160
So then for adoption, again, we want to know some sort

04:53.160 --> 04:56.040
of metric coming from the goal we have

04:56.040 --> 04:56.873
and then the signal

04:56.873 --> 04:59.040
which is a thing in the database we're tracking.

04:59.040 --> 05:03.480
What do we know about how many people have adopted this?

05:03.480 --> 05:04.890
What does adoption mean?

05:04.890 --> 05:07.140
Well, we already said that it is people

05:07.140 --> 05:11.340
that have ordered at least one glass of lemonade.

05:11.340 --> 05:14.940
So what we're gonna do is we're going to take the number

05:14.940 --> 05:17.520
of people that have downloaded the app in general

05:17.520 --> 05:19.740
and we're gonna find out what percentage of them

05:19.740 --> 05:22.200
have actually ordered one successfully.

05:22.200 --> 05:24.720
Notice how we divided those two things.

05:24.720 --> 05:28.320
We basically say people that have made successful orders

05:28.320 --> 05:30.660
versus people in general.

05:30.660 --> 05:32.710
And we're gonna call this metric

05:33.743 --> 05:37.860
the new users to customers rate, or let's say adoption rate.

05:37.860 --> 05:39.510
So then for retention,

05:39.510 --> 05:41.547
we're going to want to know our retention rate

05:41.547 --> 05:45.120
and that's gonna be our metric because we know the, again,

05:45.120 --> 05:46.530
the only thing we were tracking

05:46.530 --> 05:50.430
with the signals column was the, you know, for retention

05:50.430 --> 05:52.710
it was just, okay, who is ordering?

05:52.710 --> 05:55.200
So what people, maybe we sign them an ID

05:55.200 --> 05:56.430
in the database or something.

05:56.430 --> 05:57.810
And again, we know that the database

05:57.810 --> 05:59.640
has a time associated with it.

05:59.640 --> 06:04.640
So, we want to know of the people that were here last month,

06:04.980 --> 06:06.930
did they return this month?

06:06.930 --> 06:08.010
We're gonna divide those two

06:08.010 --> 06:11.070
and we're going to basically call them the retained users.

06:11.070 --> 06:13.860
And that will be our retention rate, that's our metric.

06:13.860 --> 06:16.020
Okay, so task success.

06:16.020 --> 06:19.140
If you remember on the goals part of the task success

06:19.140 --> 06:22.260
for this app, we said that users should be able

06:22.260 --> 06:23.670
to check out really quickly

06:23.670 --> 06:26.430
because our app is really easy to use.

06:26.430 --> 06:28.920
And if you remember on the signals part,

06:28.920 --> 06:30.060
we said in the database,

06:30.060 --> 06:33.660
we're gonna track how long people spend in the app

06:33.660 --> 06:35.820
and specifically how long they spend

06:35.820 --> 06:37.860
between the time they basically open it

06:37.860 --> 06:39.780
and indicate they wanna place an order

06:39.780 --> 06:42.750
to the time that they actually complete that order.

06:42.750 --> 06:45.750
So, for the metric that we're gonna track here,

06:45.750 --> 06:48.870
it's just going to be the average time it takes

06:48.870 --> 06:51.930
everyone that uses our app to complete their order.

06:51.930 --> 06:53.460
So if we have a hundred users

06:53.460 --> 06:57.570
and they're taking, you know, three minutes, four minutes,

06:57.570 --> 07:00.060
four and a half, some are taking six or seven.

07:00.060 --> 07:03.450
We take the average of that and we use that as our metric

07:03.450 --> 07:06.450
and we try to get that average as low as possible.

07:06.450 --> 07:09.360
And then for minimizing abandoned carts,

07:09.360 --> 07:12.510
we wanted to know, remember in the signals column,

07:12.510 --> 07:14.700
we wanted to know the number of people

07:14.700 --> 07:17.490
that indicated they wanted to start an order

07:17.490 --> 07:20.850
and then the number that actually completed the order.

07:20.850 --> 07:22.470
And we just do the math there,

07:22.470 --> 07:25.980
and then we have a ratio of completed orders

07:25.980 --> 07:28.950
and we have a ratio also of incomplete orders.

07:28.950 --> 07:31.290
And we said we were gonna try to minimize that.

07:31.290 --> 07:34.110
So that's it, that's all of the stuff.

07:34.110 --> 07:35.520
And if you put all this together,

07:35.520 --> 07:37.260
it's just a really big table.

07:37.260 --> 07:39.210
So that's why I showed it to you, sort of,

07:39.210 --> 07:41.250
one column one road of time

07:41.250 --> 07:43.890
because it can look a little daunting at first.

07:43.890 --> 07:46.500
So once you've got this all sort of filled out,

07:46.500 --> 07:47.460
you can just take a look at it

07:47.460 --> 07:49.800
and it's a really good guide to thinking about

07:49.800 --> 07:52.950
the kinds of stuff that you should be potentially tracking.

07:52.950 --> 07:54.630
However, there are some tips I wanna give you

07:54.630 --> 07:55.710
on using this framework

07:55.710 --> 07:58.440
because there are a lot of open questions I find

07:58.440 --> 08:00.240
when people use this for the first time.

08:00.240 --> 08:01.980
First of all, people always ask

08:01.980 --> 08:04.650
whether or not you can use this for a product

08:04.650 --> 08:07.530
or an entire company or just a small feature.

08:07.530 --> 08:09.210
Well, you can use this for anything.

08:09.210 --> 08:10.980
This framework, the HEART Metrics Framework

08:10.980 --> 08:12.300
is very flexible.

08:12.300 --> 08:15.360
So you could use it at a small company

08:15.360 --> 08:19.200
in order to think about the customer lifecycle journey

08:19.200 --> 08:22.140
through the entire product that the company has,

08:22.140 --> 08:24.060
and you can also use it at a huge company

08:24.060 --> 08:26.250
where you're a product manager on a team

08:26.250 --> 08:29.610
that is only working on a small feature

08:29.610 --> 08:33.090
because even features have adoption rates

08:33.090 --> 08:33.923
and this sort of thing,

08:33.923 --> 08:36.960
and happiness with particular feature, et cetera.

08:36.960 --> 08:40.410
So you can use this for either, it's useful for both.

08:40.410 --> 08:43.980
Second, this metric framework is used for reporting metrics,

08:43.980 --> 08:45.870
not exploratory metrics.

08:45.870 --> 08:48.720
Remember, exploratory metrics were just data points we have

08:48.720 --> 08:52.200
in our database that we can go query, like whenever we want,

08:52.200 --> 08:55.200
to basically find out a little bit about user behavior

08:55.200 --> 08:57.120
but it's not stuff that we're saying to ourselves,

08:57.120 --> 08:59.310
this needs to be on a dashboard.

08:59.310 --> 09:02.250
So this framework is for the stuff that is on a dashboard.

09:02.250 --> 09:04.350
It's the stuff that you do report up.

09:04.350 --> 09:05.730
You report it to your investors,

09:05.730 --> 09:08.340
you report it to your executives or your boss.

09:08.340 --> 09:11.160
That's what this framework is for, the reporting metrics.

09:11.160 --> 09:12.570
Thirdly, it's important to know

09:12.570 --> 09:14.910
that you don't have to use all of the metrics

09:14.910 --> 09:17.310
that you've come up with when you are doing this exercise

09:17.310 --> 09:19.830
and putting together this table.

09:19.830 --> 09:21.750
The reason is because it's pretty overwhelming.

09:21.750 --> 09:23.520
There's a bunch of stuff you'll end up with

09:23.520 --> 09:25.660
in the metrics column where you're just saying,

09:25.660 --> 09:28.020
well, I have to track all of these.

09:28.020 --> 09:30.390
Well, it's not always the case that you need to track

09:30.390 --> 09:31.980
every single one of these,

09:31.980 --> 09:34.620
but it's a good exercise to, sort of,

09:34.620 --> 09:36.300
list out the things you might track.

09:36.300 --> 09:37.830
And then you may only say,

09:37.830 --> 09:40.260
oh, I only want to track engagement

09:40.260 --> 09:42.090
and I only wanna track happiness.

09:42.090 --> 09:46.560
But the other stuff like task success or adoption,

09:46.560 --> 09:48.390
I'm not gonna track that, someone else will

09:48.390 --> 09:50.940
or it's not important to our company at this time.

09:50.940 --> 09:52.830
So don't think you have to use everything,

09:52.830 --> 09:56.010
just pick the ones that you really think are very important

09:56.010 --> 09:57.930
for your business or your product.

09:57.930 --> 09:59.940
So the fourth thing is actually really cool

09:59.940 --> 10:02.670
and that is that these signals column can actually be used

10:02.670 --> 10:05.250
as a sort of guide to what you need to do

10:05.250 --> 10:08.490
on the engineering side to get ahold of these metrics

10:08.490 --> 10:11.670
and put them in the database so that you can track them.

10:11.670 --> 10:15.090
Let's say, again, that you don't have metrics being tracked

10:15.090 --> 10:16.500
and you've got a new product

10:16.500 --> 10:18.840
or you're working on a new feature at a company

10:18.840 --> 10:21.060
and you say, all right we need some metrics

10:21.060 --> 10:23.100
'cause that's how we're gonna define success

10:23.100 --> 10:25.080
and that's how we're gonna know if we succeeded

10:25.080 --> 10:26.430
and you don't have anything.

10:26.430 --> 10:28.440
Well basically you can just do this exercise,

10:28.440 --> 10:29.790
you can take the signals column

10:29.790 --> 10:33.690
because remember that's the stuff that we want to track,

10:33.690 --> 10:35.910
that's the actual data we need.

10:35.910 --> 10:37.350
And you can just hand that,

10:37.350 --> 10:39.630
basically that column to your engineers

10:39.630 --> 10:41.040
and talk it over with them and be like,

10:41.040 --> 10:44.040
here's the signals that I need from the database

10:44.040 --> 10:46.680
and then we need to basically turn them into a ratio

10:46.680 --> 10:48.300
and express them as a metric.

10:48.300 --> 10:51.510
But it's a really handy way to show engineers

10:51.510 --> 10:54.630
what kind of data do you need from the backend

10:54.630 --> 10:57.570
in order to know whether or not you're doing well.

10:57.570 --> 11:00.150
So that's it for the HEART Metrics Framework.

11:00.150 --> 11:03.660
It's a really handy tool, more like an exercise actually,

11:03.660 --> 11:05.810
to get you thinking about metrics

11:05.810 --> 11:08.940
in a sort of a way that follows the customer journey.

11:08.940 --> 11:12.600
And on that note, the next lecture also does the same thing

11:12.600 --> 11:14.910
in a little bit of a different way.

11:14.910 --> 11:16.830
It's another metrics framework

11:16.830 --> 11:19.770
and it's called AARRR metrics, Pirate Metrics.

11:19.770 --> 11:22.590
It's pretty common in Silicon Valley.

11:22.590 --> 11:25.244
So, check it out and I'll see you there.

11:25.244 --> 11:28.244
(soft upbeat music)

WEBVTT

00:00.090 --> 00:01.740
-: Hey, and welcome back.

00:01.740 --> 00:03.900
In this lecture we're gonna be talking about

00:03.900 --> 00:07.350
one more metrics framework that will help you out

00:07.350 --> 00:11.430
with deciding on which metrics you should look at tracking

00:11.430 --> 00:13.590
for a new product or business.

00:13.590 --> 00:16.560
This framework is a little bit lighter than the last one,

00:16.560 --> 00:18.060
a little bit lighter than the HEART framework

00:18.060 --> 00:19.440
we just talked about.

00:19.440 --> 00:21.180
It's pretty short and sweet

00:21.180 --> 00:23.490
and it's pretty popular in the Silicon Valley.

00:23.490 --> 00:26.250
This framework is also really commonly used

00:26.250 --> 00:28.290
with software as service businesses.

00:28.290 --> 00:31.350
So the framework is called AARRR,

00:31.350 --> 00:33.810
as in like, "AARRR, I'm a pirate."

00:33.810 --> 00:35.595
Kind of weird, right?

00:35.595 --> 00:37.830
But the guy that came up with it, Dave McClure,

00:37.830 --> 00:41.310
he's a venture capital guy out in Silicon Valley,

00:41.310 --> 00:43.680
who is actually a partner in 500 Startups,

00:43.680 --> 00:45.780
which is a venture capital firm.

00:45.780 --> 00:48.240
So he knows a little bit about what he's talking about,

00:48.240 --> 00:49.848
and he came up with this framework himself,

00:49.848 --> 00:52.140
because he is investing in companies.

00:52.140 --> 00:54.480
And he said like, "These are the metrics that I like to see.

00:54.480 --> 00:56.430
These are the ones that you should focus on."

00:56.430 --> 00:58.620
The reason it has that weird name is because

00:58.620 --> 01:02.940
it's A-A-R-R-R, a five step process,

01:02.940 --> 01:06.600
acquisition, activation, retention,

01:06.600 --> 01:09.090
referral, and then revenue.

01:09.090 --> 01:12.390
This framework is pretty popular with entrepreneurs

01:12.390 --> 01:14.730
or people doing much smaller businesses.

01:14.730 --> 01:16.590
So the general idea here is that

01:16.590 --> 01:18.210
for each of these five stages

01:18.210 --> 01:21.180
you pick a metric that you want to measure.

01:21.180 --> 01:23.490
And again, just like the HEART framework,

01:23.490 --> 01:26.280
this is a way of getting us to think about

01:26.280 --> 01:29.160
the life cycle of our user.

01:29.160 --> 01:31.380
We'll go over each one of these really quickly,

01:31.380 --> 01:34.560
because we've talked a lot about all of this so far.

01:34.560 --> 01:37.020
Just want to make sure that you're aware of this one.

01:37.020 --> 01:39.660
The first one, acquisition, is just like it sounds.

01:39.660 --> 01:42.720
We need a metric for us acquiring a user.

01:42.720 --> 01:44.430
That means the user went to our website

01:44.430 --> 01:46.500
or downloaded our app.

01:46.500 --> 01:48.706
One of these, they have arrived.

01:48.706 --> 01:50.160
They know about you

01:50.160 --> 01:52.590
and now they are indicating their interest

01:52.590 --> 01:54.810
to begin using you by taking that step.

01:54.810 --> 01:56.280
The second one, activation.

01:56.280 --> 01:57.932
I've talked a little bit about it already.

01:57.932 --> 01:59.850
It just means that the customer

01:59.850 --> 02:01.650
that we've acquired is now activated.

02:01.650 --> 02:04.320
So they've signed up or they've entered their info

02:04.320 --> 02:06.970
on our mailing list or they have

02:07.830 --> 02:10.872
integrated the app with another app

02:10.872 --> 02:14.100
that you're considering to be an important step.

02:14.100 --> 02:16.890
It just means that they are now active.

02:16.890 --> 02:19.440
And you can define that however you want.

02:19.440 --> 02:20.610
Some people just say, "Okay,

02:20.610 --> 02:22.710
if they've made an account, they're activated."

02:22.710 --> 02:25.560
Other people will say, "Okay, they have to make an account

02:25.560 --> 02:27.360
and then they have to do one small thing

02:27.360 --> 02:30.060
so that we know that they are now officially active."

02:30.060 --> 02:31.860
Let's say adding a profile picture

02:31.860 --> 02:35.580
or connecting their phone book contact list

02:35.580 --> 02:38.130
to find their friends on your app or something like that.

02:38.130 --> 02:38.963
Pretty easy.

02:38.963 --> 02:40.740
The third one is retention.

02:40.740 --> 02:42.840
It means just like we've talked about before,

02:42.840 --> 02:45.390
they come back, they're repeat users.

02:45.390 --> 02:49.350
Notice how there's not so much emphasis on engagement here.

02:49.350 --> 02:51.360
This is because the framework just assumes

02:51.360 --> 02:53.910
that you're maximizing engagement in the first place

02:53.910 --> 02:56.730
and that if you're having someone being retained,

02:56.730 --> 02:58.140
well, they're gonna be engaged,

02:58.140 --> 03:01.800
otherwise they wouldn't have come back to be retained.

03:01.800 --> 03:05.670
So an example of trying to optimize your retention rate

03:05.670 --> 03:08.854
would be to send the user a notification to their phone

03:08.854 --> 03:11.937
if they haven't logged in in like a week or two weeks,

03:11.937 --> 03:15.000
or send them an email drip campaign,

03:15.000 --> 03:16.620
when you sign up for those emails

03:16.620 --> 03:19.110
and then they email you like once a week

03:19.110 --> 03:21.840
and then they email you like once every two weeks

03:21.840 --> 03:23.010
and then once a month,

03:23.010 --> 03:24.927
they're just trying to get you to come back.

03:24.927 --> 03:27.150
And the fourth one is referral,

03:27.150 --> 03:29.250
which means are users happy enough with your app

03:29.250 --> 03:31.230
that they're going to refer it to others?

03:31.230 --> 03:33.840
And this doesn't just mean user happiness

03:33.840 --> 03:36.840
in fact, it's focused, the way this thing is built,

03:36.840 --> 03:40.500
or the way that it's framed, from Dave McClure's mind,

03:40.500 --> 03:42.780
is that you can optimize it beyond

03:42.780 --> 03:44.400
just making the users happy enough

03:44.400 --> 03:47.340
to talk to their friends in real life about it.

03:47.340 --> 03:49.170
In fact, you could put a feature in

03:49.170 --> 03:50.550
where they could share it with their friends

03:50.550 --> 03:52.571
and maybe get some credit for doing that.

03:52.571 --> 03:54.736
You might have seen this with Dropbox,

03:54.736 --> 03:57.690
where when you sign up and you invite a friend

03:57.690 --> 04:00.630
you get some free storage, this sort of thing.

04:00.630 --> 04:02.430
So referral, that's easy.

04:02.430 --> 04:03.900
They're gonna share it with others.

04:03.900 --> 04:05.490
And the last one is revenue.

04:05.490 --> 04:08.400
That means you are making revenue from this user,

04:08.400 --> 04:10.650
and that is the furthest step down the funnel.

04:10.650 --> 04:12.330
So if you're a consumer company,

04:12.330 --> 04:14.640
that means eventually that they're maybe

04:14.640 --> 04:18.030
buying a subscription or they've bought like a pro version

04:18.030 --> 04:21.330
or a full fledged version of your program or your app.

04:21.330 --> 04:23.910
Or if you're a company that's free to use

04:23.910 --> 04:27.420
then maybe they are seeing advertisements

04:27.420 --> 04:29.280
and you can gauge that per user,

04:29.280 --> 04:31.680
you're making this much money off of them.

04:31.680 --> 04:34.680
The goal here is just to quantify

04:34.680 --> 04:36.960
the revenue per user that you're getting.

04:36.960 --> 04:37.793
So that's it.

04:37.793 --> 04:38.910
Just those five stages.

04:38.910 --> 04:41.562
And the way this framework is meant to be used is,

04:41.562 --> 04:44.310
you just use those five stages as a guide.

04:44.310 --> 04:46.140
So for each one of the five,

04:46.140 --> 04:48.180
those five steps down the funnel,

04:48.180 --> 04:51.030
you just think of a metric that you can measure

04:51.030 --> 04:52.590
that will tell you how you're doing

04:52.590 --> 04:54.150
on each one of those steps.

04:54.150 --> 04:57.030
Because each one leads to the other.

04:57.030 --> 04:58.890
You're not gonna get revenue from someone

04:58.890 --> 05:01.590
unless they are first activated.

05:01.590 --> 05:04.380
So you have to get a lot of people acquired

05:04.380 --> 05:06.330
and a lot of people activated,

05:06.330 --> 05:08.070
and then go all the way down the funnel

05:08.070 --> 05:10.530
and you want to get that bottom bucket revenue

05:10.530 --> 05:12.090
as big as possible.

05:12.090 --> 05:14.700
So you optimize the metrics all the way down.

05:14.700 --> 05:16.890
It's just an easy way of thinking about

05:16.890 --> 05:18.930
that journey for the user.

05:18.930 --> 05:20.490
So if you want to learn more about

05:20.490 --> 05:22.590
the AARR metrics framework,

05:22.590 --> 05:24.770
or Pirate Metrics Framework from Dave McClure,

05:24.770 --> 05:26.550
just Google it.

05:26.550 --> 05:29.400
There's a slide deck and a presentation he gave

05:29.400 --> 05:31.290
that sort of put him on the map for this.

05:31.290 --> 05:33.750
He became very popular because of this.

05:33.750 --> 05:36.289
It was meant originally as his suggestion

05:36.289 --> 05:39.150
to companies that he's investing in

05:39.150 --> 05:42.180
or people that want him to invest in them,

05:42.180 --> 05:44.490
because he feels these are the most important

05:44.490 --> 05:47.310
to venture capitalists and those types of people.

05:47.310 --> 05:49.620
So if you're in Silicon Valley or making a startup

05:49.620 --> 05:51.870
and you want to get some investment money or something,

05:51.870 --> 05:54.150
this is a great place to start,

05:54.150 --> 05:56.700
because these things do matter a lot

05:56.700 --> 06:00.330
to venture capital investors and other investors in general.

06:00.330 --> 06:01.350
That's it for this lecture.

06:01.350 --> 06:02.760
It wasn't very difficult.

06:02.760 --> 06:06.690
AARR, Pirate Metrics, Dave McClure, good stuff to know.

06:06.690 --> 06:09.125
I promise you, if you know these two metrics frameworks

06:09.125 --> 06:11.370
and you tell people about them

06:11.370 --> 06:13.890
or you're in a job interview and you mention them

06:13.890 --> 06:16.920
or anything like that, they're gonna be impressed.

06:16.920 --> 06:19.293
And I'll see you in the next lecture.

06:19.293 --> 06:21.876
(upbeat music)

WEBVTT

00:00.210 --> 00:01.830
-: Welcome back.

00:01.830 --> 00:06.000
So by now you should feel pretty confident about metrics,

00:06.000 --> 00:09.060
about what they are, why they're important,

00:09.060 --> 00:13.470
and a few ways you can go about choosing your own metrics.

00:13.470 --> 00:17.850
And likely if you're joining an established company

00:17.850 --> 00:20.190
on a team or something like this,

00:20.190 --> 00:23.640
you'll probably already have metrics to track

00:23.640 --> 00:26.520
and they'll probably already be actively tracked

00:26.520 --> 00:27.480
in the back end.

00:27.480 --> 00:29.100
So in the case that you're working

00:29.100 --> 00:30.000
for one of these companies

00:30.000 --> 00:31.440
and the metrics are already there,

00:31.440 --> 00:33.720
you'll probably be working with an insights team,

00:33.720 --> 00:37.500
or analytics team, or even a data science team

00:37.500 --> 00:41.010
to get the metrics that you're looking for to report.

00:41.010 --> 00:42.120
But let's imagine for a moment

00:42.120 --> 00:44.550
that you actually have no metrics

00:44.550 --> 00:46.020
and you've decided what you want

00:46.020 --> 00:48.270
to track using the earlier lectures,

00:48.270 --> 00:49.980
but you have no idea.

00:49.980 --> 00:52.260
Well, in this lecture I wanted to talk about a couple

00:52.260 --> 00:54.660
of my favorite tools to track metrics

00:54.660 --> 00:56.640
and that are really easy to use.

00:56.640 --> 00:58.440
So the first one is probably one

00:58.440 --> 01:01.530
of the most popular analytics tools out there.

01:01.530 --> 01:03.210
It's Google Analytics,

01:03.210 --> 01:06.360
and this is a really easy thing to install

01:06.360 --> 01:07.560
and will give you all sorts

01:07.560 --> 01:10.800
of tracking information on your website.

01:10.800 --> 01:12.930
And they even have a component

01:12.930 --> 01:15.300
that delivers on mobile tracking.

01:15.300 --> 01:18.600
So five stars for Google Analytics,

01:18.600 --> 01:21.000
it is awesome, I highly recommend it.

01:21.000 --> 01:23.400
Secondly, there's a really cool tool

01:23.400 --> 01:25.920
for websites called Crazy Egg,

01:25.920 --> 01:28.590
and this gives you all sorts of click data

01:28.590 --> 01:30.930
on where people are clicking and that sort of thing,

01:30.930 --> 01:32.790
but also gives you these really cool,

01:32.790 --> 01:36.570
like, heat map things where you can see with colors,

01:36.570 --> 01:39.480
like, a heat map of where people have been clicking

01:39.480 --> 01:42.480
and moving their mouse, and scrolling.

01:42.480 --> 01:46.260
It is pretty cool and gives you a ton of insight.

01:46.260 --> 01:48.300
The next one is KISSmetrics,

01:48.300 --> 01:52.530
and it is an awesome solution for both web and mobile,

01:52.530 --> 01:55.230
and it provides a ton of data,

01:55.230 --> 01:58.380
and you can set up custom metrics,

01:58.380 --> 02:01.140
and do calculations on the fly,

02:01.140 --> 02:03.330
and look at all sorts of crazy graphs.

02:03.330 --> 02:06.300
It's really good, but it's not cheap.

02:06.300 --> 02:09.390
So the fourth one is similar to KISSmetrics,

02:09.390 --> 02:11.970
and in fact it's a big competitor.

02:11.970 --> 02:13.830
I think it's probably about 50/50 split

02:13.830 --> 02:18.270
between the people who like KISSmetrics versus Mixpanel,

02:18.270 --> 02:19.680
but it's also not cheap,

02:19.680 --> 02:24.680
but it's extremely good to use for both web and mobile,

02:24.870 --> 02:29.010
and has some really cool features like a calculator,

02:29.010 --> 02:33.150
and you can look up individual user behavior.

02:33.150 --> 02:34.650
You could even hook it up to stuff

02:34.650 --> 02:36.960
like email and push notifications

02:36.960 --> 02:41.760
so you can send users messages to try to retain them.

02:41.760 --> 02:44.760
So the fifth one I'll recommend is Optimizely.

02:44.760 --> 02:48.600
And Optimizely is actually more of an A/B testing tool,

02:48.600 --> 02:52.800
but it also provides some really good click tracking

02:52.800 --> 02:54.150
and that sort of thing that you can set up

02:54.150 --> 02:58.350
to see how many people click where and where they're going.

02:58.350 --> 03:02.130
It is really good and it is super easy to use

03:02.130 --> 03:04.830
compared to a lot of other A/B testing tools out there.

03:04.830 --> 03:07.830
I highly recommend it and you can actually just go demo it

03:07.830 --> 03:10.050
for free on their website.

03:10.050 --> 03:12.930
So the last one I wanna mention is one of my favorites,

03:12.930 --> 03:17.430
and that's because it's not a tool itself for metrics,

03:17.430 --> 03:19.410
but it's a metrics hub.

03:19.410 --> 03:24.120
So if you imagine you have all these metrics tools out there

03:24.120 --> 03:27.960
and they all integrate with either your mobile app

03:27.960 --> 03:30.630
or your website through an API,

03:30.630 --> 03:31.860
which we'll talk about later,

03:31.860 --> 03:35.700
but you usually have to put a piece of code on your site

03:35.700 --> 03:39.360
or in your app in order to track stuff.

03:39.360 --> 03:42.810
And the problem is if you switch metrics tools,

03:42.810 --> 03:46.290
then you will usually have to switch out that code,

03:46.290 --> 03:48.870
and then to make it even worse,

03:48.870 --> 03:50.520
let's say you have one metrics tool

03:50.520 --> 03:52.320
and you have a year of using that,

03:52.320 --> 03:54.000
and you have all this data in there,

03:54.000 --> 03:56.850
and then you switch to another one to try it out.

03:56.850 --> 03:58.920
Well now you've lost all the historical data,

03:58.920 --> 04:01.350
because you're switching to a new tool.

04:01.350 --> 04:05.550
Well Segment solves this problem because it's like a hub.

04:05.550 --> 04:09.090
So you connect other services to it,

04:09.090 --> 04:13.500
and then you connect your app or your website to Segment,

04:13.500 --> 04:16.170
and this way you can basically plug

04:16.170 --> 04:18.990
and unplug any other solution,

04:18.990 --> 04:21.270
and it will just automatically send the data

04:21.270 --> 04:23.400
to the tool that you would like.

04:23.400 --> 04:26.760
So it's really good if you consider

04:26.760 --> 04:28.710
setting up with Segment first

04:28.710 --> 04:32.220
and then trying out a whole bunch of other tools.

04:32.220 --> 04:35.280
It even keeps a backup of your data.

04:35.280 --> 04:37.980
All right, so that's it for the metric section

04:37.980 --> 04:39.750
and I hope you paid close attention,

04:39.750 --> 04:42.150
because this stuff is super important

04:42.150 --> 04:45.510
as a product manager or an entrepreneur.

04:45.510 --> 04:47.070
Everyone needs to know this stuff.

04:47.070 --> 04:49.230
So in the next sections we're gonna get even deeper

04:49.230 --> 04:51.420
into the world of product management.

04:51.420 --> 04:53.730
It's time to get our hands dirty.

04:53.730 --> 04:54.813
I'll see you there.

04:56.605 --> 05:00.105
(upbeat electronic music)

WEBVTT

00:00.000 --> 00:02.583
(upbeat music)

00:04.320 --> 00:06.390
-: What's up everyone? Welcome back.

00:06.390 --> 00:08.310
So, in this section, we're gonna get into

00:08.310 --> 00:11.190
the real nuts and bolts of product management

00:11.190 --> 00:15.180
and managing a project day-to-day as a PM.

00:15.180 --> 00:17.070
This lecture is a little bit longer,

00:17.070 --> 00:19.050
but it's extremely important,

00:19.050 --> 00:21.480
so I really recommend you pay attention here.

00:21.480 --> 00:24.240
So before we start talking about building,

00:24.240 --> 00:27.510
we need to understand that the things companies build

00:27.510 --> 00:29.700
come from an overall vision

00:29.700 --> 00:32.790
that's established by the CEO or founder.

00:32.790 --> 00:33.870
So the question is,

00:33.870 --> 00:37.560
how does this vision turn into things that get built?

00:37.560 --> 00:41.340
Okay, so we know that the CEO and founder or executives,

00:41.340 --> 00:43.530
they have this vision that they want to achieve

00:43.530 --> 00:45.570
about where the company is going

00:45.570 --> 00:49.620
and really what the purpose is in two, three, or five years.

00:49.620 --> 00:51.360
In order to achieve the vision,

00:51.360 --> 00:54.720
a company has to have goals that they want to reach

00:54.720 --> 00:57.150
to achieve it, to get there to that vision.

00:57.150 --> 01:00.540
Like vision, the goals are decided by the executives,

01:00.540 --> 01:03.810
but then they're defined further into metrics usually,

01:03.810 --> 01:06.990
like increase new users by 5% this year.

01:06.990 --> 01:09.090
So once these goals are established,

01:09.090 --> 01:10.470
the management teams come up with

01:10.470 --> 01:12.060
what are called initiatives.

01:12.060 --> 01:14.190
These are things that the whole company

01:14.190 --> 01:15.300
is gonna do and build

01:15.300 --> 01:17.160
in order to reach those goals.

01:17.160 --> 01:18.960
An example of initiative would be

01:18.960 --> 01:22.680
translate the product into three other languages this year.

01:22.680 --> 01:26.850
Underneath initiatives, we have what are called releases.

01:26.850 --> 01:30.030
Now, don't worry too much about releases right now,

01:30.030 --> 01:33.660
because it's not really the same at every single company,

01:33.660 --> 01:35.160
but really what it is,

01:35.160 --> 01:38.610
is just a grouping of new features or functionality

01:38.610 --> 01:41.640
that's released to the users, to the public,

01:41.640 --> 01:44.070
on one particular date or time period.

01:44.070 --> 01:45.750
We can continue our example

01:45.750 --> 01:47.730
and say that if we translated the product

01:47.730 --> 01:49.470
into three additional languages,

01:49.470 --> 01:53.190
we might decide that we have one release in February

01:53.190 --> 01:55.860
where all three languages are available to the public.

01:55.860 --> 01:57.030
That would be our release.

01:57.030 --> 01:58.500
But underneath releases,

01:58.500 --> 02:01.800
there are features and functionality that get released,

02:01.800 --> 02:05.040
the product management world calls these epics.

02:05.040 --> 02:06.270
Underneath epics,

02:06.270 --> 02:08.970
there are things called stories and requirements,

02:08.970 --> 02:11.790
but we're gonna talk about that part in another lecture.

02:11.790 --> 02:13.530
Let's finish off this lecture

02:13.530 --> 02:17.310
by diving deeper into this concept of what epics are.

02:17.310 --> 02:20.010
First, we must start with a statement of things

02:20.010 --> 02:22.500
that we're building to solve a problem

02:22.500 --> 02:25.800
and clearly show what features or functionality

02:25.800 --> 02:28.740
we intend to build in order to do that.

02:28.740 --> 02:30.870
This is what we call an epic.

02:30.870 --> 02:33.090
We learned in previous lectures

02:33.090 --> 02:36.780
about the concepts behind minimum viable products,

02:36.780 --> 02:39.660
customer interviews, and wireframing.

02:39.660 --> 02:42.870
We use these tools to help us make the right decisions

02:42.870 --> 02:45.990
in solving user problems with features.

02:45.990 --> 02:50.220
But once we know what we want to actually build,

02:50.220 --> 02:53.100
how do we actually get it built?

02:53.100 --> 02:55.140
Well, that's what all this stuff is gonna be about,

02:55.140 --> 02:57.960
and that in particular is where epics come in.

02:57.960 --> 03:00.720
An epic is really just product management lingo

03:00.720 --> 03:05.460
for a grouping of one or more features or functionalities

03:05.460 --> 03:07.110
that we want to build.

03:07.110 --> 03:10.020
It's the fundamental brick that we build with

03:10.020 --> 03:13.380
as a product manager and a product team.

03:13.380 --> 03:14.550
Just so you know,

03:14.550 --> 03:18.030
usually a product team will build out three to five epics

03:18.030 --> 03:20.550
during a quarter, like a quarter of a year.

03:20.550 --> 03:22.530
This depends on the company size and all of that,

03:22.530 --> 03:23.670
but in general.

03:23.670 --> 03:24.750
This really just depends

03:24.750 --> 03:27.090
on how much effort the epic's gonna take

03:27.090 --> 03:28.350
and that sort of thing.

03:28.350 --> 03:30.540
In real life, an epic example

03:30.540 --> 03:34.620
would be something like translate the app to Spanish,

03:34.620 --> 03:38.010
or implement photo sharing in direct messages,

03:38.010 --> 03:41.790
or allow users to upgrade to a pro membership.

03:41.790 --> 03:45.810
This stuff might sound a lot to you like a feature,

03:45.810 --> 03:47.520
and that's because it is.

03:47.520 --> 03:49.800
One of the reasons that we use the term epic

03:49.800 --> 03:51.330
instead of feature

03:51.330 --> 03:55.770
is because not all things we do as a product team result

03:55.770 --> 03:59.370
in a new feature for an outside user,

03:59.370 --> 04:01.860
so the term reduces confusion.

04:01.860 --> 04:03.180
There are some other reasons as well,

04:03.180 --> 04:05.700
but we're not gonna really get into it here.

04:05.700 --> 04:08.130
It's something that will become apparent as you continue.

04:08.130 --> 04:10.080
For instance, we could have an epic

04:10.080 --> 04:15.080
that is called migrate our data to a new database.

04:15.120 --> 04:17.940
This isn't really a feature for end users,

04:17.940 --> 04:19.830
like the people out there using our product,

04:19.830 --> 04:22.170
but it's something that, internally,

04:22.170 --> 04:24.510
our engineers might need to do,

04:24.510 --> 04:27.630
and we might need to do it to make sure we can scale

04:27.630 --> 04:29.970
or that we can support more users in the future,

04:29.970 --> 04:31.290
so we still gotta do it,

04:31.290 --> 04:33.060
and it would still be called an epic.

04:33.060 --> 04:35.730
Epics are also defined as a piece of work

04:35.730 --> 04:38.490
that takes longer than one sprint to build.

04:38.490 --> 04:41.760
If there's something smaller that you want to build,

04:41.760 --> 04:44.820
and it's gonna take less time than one sprint,

04:44.820 --> 04:47.550
then it wouldn't really need its own epic,

04:47.550 --> 04:49.590
it would just need a user story.

04:49.590 --> 04:52.500
Now, don't worry quite yet about user stories

04:52.500 --> 04:55.170
because we're gonna talk about that in another lecture,

04:55.170 --> 04:58.320
but just know that epics are above user stories,

04:58.320 --> 05:00.780
they contain features and functionalities

05:00.780 --> 05:02.460
and user stories inside of them.

05:02.460 --> 05:05.490
So epics are the fundamental groupings

05:05.490 --> 05:08.940
of features or functionalities that teams build.

05:08.940 --> 05:11.130
In the next lecture, we're gonna talk about

05:11.130 --> 05:13.650
what they actually look like in real life

05:13.650 --> 05:15.573
and how you might go about making one.

WEBVTT

00:00.210 --> 00:03.720
-: So now that we understand what epics are in concept,

00:03.720 --> 00:06.930
let's talk a little bit about how they work in reality.

00:06.930 --> 00:08.730
Most project management software

00:08.730 --> 00:11.130
gives you the ability to create an epic

00:11.130 --> 00:13.380
and then add features to that epic.

00:13.380 --> 00:16.200
This really depends on the project management software

00:16.200 --> 00:18.240
you're using but in any case,

00:18.240 --> 00:21.360
epics always have documentation with them

00:21.360 --> 00:24.810
and describes what it is you're building and then why.

00:24.810 --> 00:27.690
This documentation is called an Epic Spec Sheet,

00:27.690 --> 00:30.840
and it's sometimes referred to as a Requirements Document.

00:30.840 --> 00:32.580
You can make one pretty much anywhere,

00:32.580 --> 00:35.490
like Google Docs, your project management tool,

00:35.490 --> 00:37.230
or anything else that you want,

00:37.230 --> 00:39.360
it's just gotta kind of be out there

00:39.360 --> 00:42.600
in the public for people at your own company to look at.

00:42.600 --> 00:44.370
The purpose of an epic spec sheet

00:44.370 --> 00:47.100
is to allow anyone in your company to read it

00:47.100 --> 00:49.680
and understand exactly what you're building.

00:49.680 --> 00:51.930
It also serves as a guide

00:51.930 --> 00:54.300
for your own team as you build things.

00:54.300 --> 00:56.010
So what will these epic specs look like?

00:56.010 --> 00:58.560
Well, they usually have four main areas,

00:58.560 --> 01:01.260
an introduction, the product requirements,

01:01.260 --> 01:02.910
the design requirements,

01:02.910 --> 01:05.220
and then the engineering requirements.

01:05.220 --> 01:07.530
As a product manager, you're in charge of creating

01:07.530 --> 01:09.870
and maintaining the whole epic spec sheet

01:09.870 --> 01:11.040
and the epic itself

01:11.040 --> 01:12.900
but you're especially responsible

01:12.900 --> 01:14.670
for the first two sections,

01:14.670 --> 01:16.212
which again are the introduction

01:16.212 --> 01:18.450
and the product requirement section.

01:18.450 --> 01:20.370
Let's go through each section

01:20.370 --> 01:23.220
of a typical epic spec sheet individually.

01:23.220 --> 01:25.080
So first we've got the introduction.

01:25.080 --> 01:27.960
This is really just a summary of what the feature

01:27.960 --> 01:30.630
or features you're building are for

01:30.630 --> 01:32.580
and why you're building it.

01:32.580 --> 01:35.070
What metrics are you trying to improve?

01:35.070 --> 01:36.690
And you can also put in any links

01:36.690 --> 01:40.110
to supporting documentation or other miscellaneous things

01:40.110 --> 01:44.010
like marketing plans, legal requirements,

01:44.010 --> 01:44.940
and that kind of thing.

01:44.940 --> 01:46.943
This is also where you can write down

01:46.943 --> 01:49.890
what a feature or functionality looks like,

01:49.890 --> 01:51.456
some early wire frames

01:51.456 --> 01:55.500
and what it looks like in an ideal state down the road

01:55.500 --> 01:58.110
versus what you're going to start out building

01:58.110 --> 02:01.380
for your MVP and then maybe what it looks like

02:01.380 --> 02:03.210
for a V2, et cetera.

02:03.210 --> 02:06.090
So the second section is the product requirements.

02:06.090 --> 02:08.490
And here we're talking about the features

02:08.490 --> 02:11.430
or functionality that we're developing in detail.

02:11.430 --> 02:14.010
We go deeper on what is required.

02:14.010 --> 02:17.310
For instance, we might say that the feature has to be fast

02:17.310 --> 02:19.740
or it has to be available in a certain language

02:19.740 --> 02:22.068
or it must generate a certain type of data

02:22.068 --> 02:24.750
and then store it for later use.

02:24.750 --> 02:27.990
So the next section is the design requirements.

02:27.990 --> 02:30.900
This section is to be filled out by both you

02:30.900 --> 02:34.650
and the designer together, but a little bit more emphasis

02:34.650 --> 02:36.420
needs to come from the designer here

02:36.420 --> 02:37.770
since they're the experts.

02:37.770 --> 02:40.230
Let's imagine that for a particular feature

02:40.230 --> 02:44.040
the designer wants to display the user profile picture

02:44.040 --> 02:46.200
with a really high resolution

02:46.200 --> 02:48.030
because they think that's the best design,

02:48.030 --> 02:50.010
this is where that would be stated.

02:50.010 --> 02:52.426
This is also where you and your designer will attach

02:52.426 --> 02:55.230
any early wire frames and sketches

02:55.230 --> 02:58.920
or high fidelity prototypes to help the engineers

02:58.920 --> 03:00.990
better understand the requirements

03:00.990 --> 03:02.220
and what's gotta be done here.

03:02.220 --> 03:04.650
So now onto the engineering requirement section

03:04.650 --> 03:05.790
of the spec sheet.

03:05.790 --> 03:08.400
This is the portion that is mostly filled out

03:08.400 --> 03:12.150
by your engineers after you discuss the product

03:12.150 --> 03:13.920
and design requirements with them.

03:13.920 --> 03:15.630
Here is where the engineers can write out

03:15.630 --> 03:18.150
database and technology requirements

03:18.150 --> 03:21.570
or outline the series of things that must be done

03:21.570 --> 03:25.590
on the technology side in order to support the new feature.

03:25.590 --> 03:27.600
An example here would be developing

03:27.600 --> 03:31.860
a certain API endpoint in order to get the high resolution

03:31.860 --> 03:34.980
picture stated in the design requirements.

03:34.980 --> 03:37.200
Or they could note that they need to

03:37.200 --> 03:41.280
work with another team to access their database

03:41.280 --> 03:44.160
in order to get the information that's required

03:44.160 --> 03:45.180
to make this feature.

03:45.180 --> 03:46.380
So let's recap here.

03:46.380 --> 03:49.470
Epics always have accompanying documentation

03:49.470 --> 03:52.680
or spec sheets to inform other teams

03:52.680 --> 03:55.140
or guide ours as we build.

03:55.140 --> 03:57.180
In most cases this epic spec sheet

03:57.180 --> 04:01.050
will have a few major sections including an introduction,

04:01.050 --> 04:03.420
product requirements, design requirements,

04:03.420 --> 04:05.190
and engineering requirements.

04:05.190 --> 04:07.530
It's important that your epic spec sheet

04:07.530 --> 04:09.330
is available to anyone in the company

04:09.330 --> 04:12.510
so that they can easily understand who is building what

04:12.510 --> 04:14.608
like which teams are working on what things

04:14.608 --> 04:16.470
and why they're working on them.

04:16.470 --> 04:18.180
I also wanna mention that

04:18.180 --> 04:20.722
the way that companies do epic spec sheets

04:20.722 --> 04:24.630
is not the exact same from company to company.

04:24.630 --> 04:28.170
Some companies will say our epic specs or requirements docs

04:28.170 --> 04:30.750
need to have more information or less

04:30.750 --> 04:32.940
and some companies don't even use them at all.

04:32.940 --> 04:35.220
But this is the standard practice

04:35.220 --> 04:37.230
and you absolutely need to be familiar

04:37.230 --> 04:38.460
with it as a product manager.

04:38.460 --> 04:40.200
So we've got the basic foundation

04:40.200 --> 04:41.760
of a new feature or functionality

04:41.760 --> 04:46.230
or set of functionalities with epics and epic spec sheets.

04:46.230 --> 04:49.263
Let's find out in the next lecture where we go after.

WEBVTT

00:00.270 --> 00:01.290
-: Hey, welcome back.

00:01.290 --> 00:03.180
So now we're gonna talk about user stories

00:03.180 --> 00:06.570
and acceptance criteria, which are underneath Epics.

00:06.570 --> 00:07.770
And we already know that an Epic

00:07.770 --> 00:10.200
has an Epic spec sheet associated with it

00:10.200 --> 00:11.910
to describe what we're doing here

00:11.910 --> 00:14.760
and what sorts of functionalities we need and all of that.

00:14.760 --> 00:17.910
But we're gonna talk about now how we actually get that

00:17.910 --> 00:20.250
into the developer's hands and turn it into work.

00:20.250 --> 00:22.530
So here we're gonna assume we've made a decision

00:22.530 --> 00:24.150
on what we want to build.

00:24.150 --> 00:26.640
We've outlined the phases that we're gonna build it in

00:26.640 --> 00:28.950
and we've put all that in the Epic spec sheet.

00:28.950 --> 00:31.530
Now, the task is to put this work

00:31.530 --> 00:33.840
into our project management software,

00:33.840 --> 00:36.660
so that the engineers can start working on it.

00:36.660 --> 00:40.530
This is where user stories and acceptance criteria come in.

00:40.530 --> 00:44.370
So a user story is just a way to describe a thing

00:44.370 --> 00:47.070
we're going to build that delivers some type

00:47.070 --> 00:49.470
of functionality to the end user.

00:49.470 --> 00:51.660
An example of a user story is,

00:51.660 --> 00:54.600
as a user I want to send pictures

00:54.600 --> 00:56.820
in a direct message to my friends,

00:56.820 --> 00:59.730
so that I can share my favorite photos with them.

00:59.730 --> 01:01.140
That's kind of a weird thing, right?

01:01.140 --> 01:02.220
It's a weird format.

01:02.220 --> 01:05.190
User stories always follow that format.

01:05.190 --> 01:10.190
As an X, I want to do Y, so that I can Z.

01:10.770 --> 01:13.260
So why do we do this whole weird formatting thing?

01:13.260 --> 01:16.680
Why are we describing work to be done in this weird way?

01:16.680 --> 01:18.990
Do you remember how product managers are responsible

01:18.990 --> 01:21.120
for the what and the why?

01:21.120 --> 01:24.510
And the engineers and designers are responsible for the how?

01:24.510 --> 01:28.140
That's the reason that user stories follow this weird format

01:28.140 --> 01:30.360
because the product manager is usually the person

01:30.360 --> 01:33.360
that writes them and puts them into the product management

01:33.360 --> 01:35.250
or the project management software.

01:35.250 --> 01:37.920
By writing them this way, we can avoid saying

01:37.920 --> 01:41.190
how something should be done on a technical level.

01:41.190 --> 01:43.680
In other words, by writing in this format,

01:43.680 --> 01:46.325
we're not telling engineers how to do their jobs,

01:46.325 --> 01:49.740
we're just telling them functionally as a user

01:49.740 --> 01:52.230
what we want the user to be able to do.

01:52.230 --> 01:54.510
Okay, so where do we write user stories

01:54.510 --> 01:56.970
and how do they get to your engineer?

01:56.970 --> 02:01.350
Well, they belong inside your project management tool.

02:01.350 --> 02:03.750
And no matter which software you're using

02:03.750 --> 02:07.620
for project management, there's probably some kind of place

02:07.620 --> 02:12.180
or card or something where you can write the work to be done

02:12.180 --> 02:14.456
and then you can change that card or that place

02:14.456 --> 02:18.630
into different statuses, like to do, in progress,

02:18.630 --> 02:20.790
and then done, these little cards,

02:20.790 --> 02:21.990
or whatever you want to call 'em.

02:21.990 --> 02:23.970
They're commonly called tickets

02:23.970 --> 02:25.590
in the product management world,

02:25.590 --> 02:28.140
and it's where your user stories belong,

02:28.140 --> 02:30.930
along with proper designs from the designer.

02:30.930 --> 02:34.350
So engineers take the user stories, they complete them,

02:34.350 --> 02:36.300
and then they move on to the next one.

02:36.300 --> 02:40.110
However, before we can say that a user story is complete,

02:40.110 --> 02:41.940
we have to make sure that the functionality

02:41.940 --> 02:44.520
does what we intended it to do.

02:44.520 --> 02:47.580
This is where acceptance criteria comes in.

02:47.580 --> 02:50.576
Acceptance criteria is just a set of conditions

02:50.576 --> 02:55.290
that software must satisfy to be considered complete.

02:55.290 --> 02:58.800
Typically, acceptance criteria is written

02:58.800 --> 03:02.430
inside of the ticket with the user story

03:02.430 --> 03:04.920
and sounds a little bit something like this.

03:04.920 --> 03:06.570
Given I am a user

03:06.570 --> 03:10.770
and I click the add picture button in the direct message,

03:10.770 --> 03:14.130
I am presented with a pop-up window to choose the file

03:14.130 --> 03:17.340
I can upload, submit it with the upload button

03:17.340 --> 03:20.010
and see a preview of the uploaded image.

03:20.010 --> 03:21.600
And then here's another one.

03:21.600 --> 03:25.530
Given I am a user who has successfully uploaded a photo

03:25.530 --> 03:28.110
from my computer, when I click send,

03:28.110 --> 03:30.180
the image is sent to my friend

03:30.180 --> 03:33.660
through the direct message and it appears in the chat.

03:33.660 --> 03:35.700
This sounds really specific, right?

03:35.700 --> 03:37.680
Well, the purpose of acceptance criteria

03:37.680 --> 03:42.030
is to be very specific on how a feature should function.

03:42.030 --> 03:46.110
It provides a list of things that you can test

03:46.110 --> 03:50.190
to make sure it works before you release it to the public.

03:50.190 --> 03:53.010
This is also extremely helpful for engineers

03:53.010 --> 03:55.590
who like everything to be very specific

03:55.590 --> 03:57.120
so that they can think through

03:57.120 --> 03:59.730
the process of engineering this stuff correctly.

03:59.730 --> 04:02.730
As a product manager, you are usually responsible

04:02.730 --> 04:05.783
for testing completed tickets and stories

04:05.783 --> 04:08.610
before approving them to go to the public

04:08.610 --> 04:10.680
or to go out live in your product.

04:10.680 --> 04:11.760
So that's it.

04:11.760 --> 04:14.760
Tickets inside of project management software

04:14.760 --> 04:17.280
have user stories that describe

04:17.280 --> 04:19.860
what a user should be able to do,

04:19.860 --> 04:22.080
followed by some acceptance criteria

04:22.080 --> 04:25.770
that really spell out exactly how it should work.

04:25.770 --> 04:26.880
Okay look, if all of this

04:26.880 --> 04:29.430
is a little bit confusing to you, don't worry.

04:29.430 --> 04:32.430
I'm gonna show you a real life example coming up.

04:32.430 --> 04:33.633
I will see you in there.

response.title: Real Life Example of Epics, Specs, User Stories, and the Backlog

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.240 --> 00:01.650
-: Hey everyone, welcome back.

00:01.650 --> 00:03.630
So if you were left a little bit confused

00:03.630 --> 00:05.220
from the last things we talked about

00:05.220 --> 00:06.990
with the epics and spec sheets,

00:06.990 --> 00:09.090
user stories and acceptance criteria,

00:09.090 --> 00:11.070
I'm gonna show you a real life example here

00:11.070 --> 00:14.640
inside a tool called Jira, J-I-R-A.

00:14.640 --> 00:17.910
And this is not the only tool you can use.

00:17.910 --> 00:19.410
In fact, I don't recommend it

00:19.410 --> 00:21.330
unless you work at a very large company

00:21.330 --> 00:23.790
but it is very common at large companies.

00:23.790 --> 00:24.900
You can also use a whole bunch

00:24.900 --> 00:27.660
of other tools we'll talk about, but I'll show you

00:27.660 --> 00:30.390
in JIRA because this is kind of an industry standard.

00:30.390 --> 00:31.320
So let's get into it.

00:31.320 --> 00:32.400
Let's go down my screen

00:32.400 --> 00:34.530
and I'll show you the real life example.

00:34.530 --> 00:37.547
All right, so what we have here is a screen

00:37.547 --> 00:41.130
that is inside of a project I've created.

00:41.130 --> 00:44.763
And I'm gonna pretend that this project inside of JIRA

00:44.763 --> 00:49.560
is basically the project that my team owns.

00:49.560 --> 00:52.020
I'm a product manager and I lead a product team.

00:52.020 --> 00:53.872
And this is the project my team owns.

00:53.872 --> 00:57.990
And so what you can see here is, this is what is called

00:57.990 --> 01:00.270
I'll expand this so you can see this better,

01:00.270 --> 01:02.100
This is called the backlog.

01:02.100 --> 01:04.960
And a backlog is merely just a place

01:04.960 --> 01:08.576
that we hold things that we plan to do later

01:08.576 --> 01:10.936
but we're not working on quite yet.

01:10.936 --> 01:14.356
If you remember, we do sprints in the scrum model.

01:14.356 --> 01:16.830
So the stuff that we're working on right now

01:16.830 --> 01:19.500
it goes into the sprint and we start working on it.

01:19.500 --> 01:21.240
And then when the sprint is over,

01:21.240 --> 01:23.862
we see what we got done and what we didn't

01:23.862 --> 01:28.614
and we add more stuff from the backlog to the next sprint.

01:28.614 --> 01:30.270
And that's how we work.

01:30.270 --> 01:32.520
The backlog is just a place to hold stuff

01:32.520 --> 01:33.813
that you plan to do.

01:33.813 --> 01:38.082
So what I've got here is an empty sprint at the top.

01:38.082 --> 01:40.566
And then here I've got my backlog.

01:40.566 --> 01:43.787
And over here on the left side, you see the epics.

01:43.787 --> 01:47.520
So like I said, most project management softwares

01:47.520 --> 01:50.623
like Pivotal Tracker or JIRA.

01:50.623 --> 01:52.830
They're gonna have places for epics.

01:52.830 --> 01:54.960
And in other ones you might just have to create

01:54.960 --> 01:56.760
some sort of structure yourself.

01:56.760 --> 01:59.790
But here you can see that I've selected all issues.

01:59.790 --> 02:03.460
And that's basically all of the things in my backlog,

02:03.460 --> 02:07.860
all the user stories basically that I want to do

02:07.860 --> 02:11.288
but may or may not already be inside of an epic.

02:11.288 --> 02:13.920
And then here we have one epic

02:13.920 --> 02:17.528
which we've called the direct message photos version one.

02:17.528 --> 02:19.290
And then I've got another one here

02:19.290 --> 02:22.080
that's a technical epic that's not user facing

02:22.080 --> 02:24.300
and it's My great user database.

02:24.300 --> 02:28.200
We can see here in the backlog we've got four tickets here

02:28.200 --> 02:30.030
that have user stories inside of them

02:30.030 --> 02:32.400
that belong to this epic.

02:32.400 --> 02:34.830
And I can click on this and it will only show those.

02:34.830 --> 02:38.490
And then below those, I've got the stories and the tickets

02:38.490 --> 02:41.043
for migrating the user database.

02:41.043 --> 02:43.950
So let's go ahead and add a couple

02:43.950 --> 02:45.930
of these things to our sprint

02:45.930 --> 02:48.600
or pretend like we're gonna start a sprint basically.

02:48.600 --> 02:50.640
So we know that we're gonna work on this epic.

02:50.640 --> 02:53.250
We're gonna try to implement direct messaging and photos.

02:53.250 --> 02:55.123
We're gonna do that.

02:55.123 --> 03:00.060
And we're going to also be able,

03:00.060 --> 03:03.599
we also want users to be able to cancel a photo being sent

03:03.599 --> 03:04.941
in mid-transfer.

03:04.941 --> 03:07.163
And then there's some other things here where, okay

03:07.163 --> 03:08.970
users can maybe upload the photos

03:08.970 --> 03:12.720
from a URL and they can also upload from copying pasting

03:12.720 --> 03:14.040
but that's gonna take some more effort.

03:14.040 --> 03:16.920
So we're actually considering moving that

03:16.920 --> 03:20.070
to maybe another epic where it's the direct message

03:20.070 --> 03:22.920
photos version two, or it's our second phase.

03:22.920 --> 03:26.010
But for our minimum viable product, we just

03:26.010 --> 03:30.360
want to allow people to upload photos from their computer.

03:30.360 --> 03:31.740
So that's what we'll start with.

03:31.740 --> 03:35.190
I'm also going to add maybe the first phase

03:35.190 --> 03:36.720
or the first ticket here

03:36.720 --> 03:41.100
from our migrating the user database Epic into our sprint

03:41.100 --> 03:43.560
because maybe we have two engineers that are working

03:43.560 --> 03:46.560
on the photo indirect messages

03:46.560 --> 03:49.650
and another engineer is working on migrating this database.

03:49.650 --> 03:51.480
So what happens is we've got our sprint loaded

03:51.480 --> 03:54.660
up here and we click Start sprint.

03:54.660 --> 03:56.460
And it says, "How long is the sprint?"

03:56.460 --> 03:58.350
Because not all companies use two week sprints

03:58.350 --> 04:00.450
but it does pre-fill with two weeks.

04:00.450 --> 04:02.400
You can choose some other options here too.

04:02.400 --> 04:03.976
We'll click Start.

04:03.976 --> 04:07.980
And this brings us to the active sprints area.

04:07.980 --> 04:10.773
So you can see we have items here that are to do

04:10.773 --> 04:13.560
then we have in progress, then we have done.

04:13.560 --> 04:16.380
And we're gonna start, let's pretend like we're an engineer

04:16.380 --> 04:18.150
and we're working on stuff.

04:18.150 --> 04:19.297
What's gonna happen is they're gonna say,

04:19.297 --> 04:21.600
"All right, I'm working on this."

04:21.600 --> 04:24.517
Now it's in progress and eventually we move it to done.

04:24.517 --> 04:26.670
But what are they actually working on?

04:26.670 --> 04:28.650
Let's move this back here

04:28.650 --> 04:32.973
and let's click on this first item.

04:33.810 --> 04:36.323
You can see that it's part of an epic here

04:36.323 --> 04:39.900
which is the direct messages with photos.

04:39.900 --> 04:43.560
And I will just go in, JIRA's a little complex

04:43.560 --> 04:45.283
so bear with me here.

04:45.283 --> 04:46.770
This is why not many people like it.

04:46.770 --> 04:51.770
But we'll go into the more details for this actual ticket.

04:52.618 --> 04:56.563
So inside of it, we can see that this is the ticket.

04:56.563 --> 04:59.700
And the ticket is send photos through direct message.

04:59.700 --> 05:01.763
Inside we've got a user story.

05:01.763 --> 05:05.760
So as a user, I want to be able to send pictures

05:05.760 --> 05:08.517
in a direct message by clicking the photo sharing button

05:08.517 --> 05:11.822
so that I can share my favorite photos with friends.

05:11.822 --> 05:14.700
And we have those acceptance criteria I talked about.

05:14.700 --> 05:15.960
So given I'm a user

05:15.960 --> 05:18.900
and I click the add picture button in the direct message

05:18.900 --> 05:21.390
I'm presented with a pop-up window to choose the file

05:21.390 --> 05:24.360
I can upload, I can submit it with the upload button.

05:24.360 --> 05:27.660
And then I see a preview of the uploaded image.

05:27.660 --> 05:30.680
This is also a place you can see where we've got a couple,

05:30.680 --> 05:32.880
we've got a wire frame in here, right?

05:32.880 --> 05:35.820
So this is where we would implement

05:35.820 --> 05:37.440
or put in the wire frames

05:37.440 --> 05:41.280
or mocks where it shows the designer has said, "Okay

05:41.280 --> 05:43.843
this thing is this many pixels wide

05:43.843 --> 05:44.676
and this many pixels tall."

05:44.676 --> 05:48.420
So those go into the actual user stories themselves.

05:48.420 --> 05:49.830
But I wanna show you one more thing

05:49.830 --> 05:52.050
and that is, because this is a ticket

05:52.050 --> 05:54.930
has a user story in it, it's part of an epic.

05:54.930 --> 05:57.000
So what does that epic look like?

05:57.000 --> 05:59.040
Well, we can click here, we can go

05:59.040 --> 06:01.077
to the actual epic itself.

06:01.077 --> 06:04.650
And this is just the inside

06:04.650 --> 06:07.100
of that epic that we saw on the backlog screen earlier.

06:07.100 --> 06:10.320
Basically it has those sections that I was talking about.

06:10.320 --> 06:12.963
So we have an introduction and it's like, okay

06:12.963 --> 06:15.544
this feature's gonna allow users to send photos

06:15.544 --> 06:17.730
to each other through the direct message.

06:17.730 --> 06:19.920
It says, "Our research shows

06:19.920 --> 06:21.930
that 80% of people would use this.

06:21.930 --> 06:23.400
And we tested it with people

06:23.400 --> 06:26.437
in user tests and they really liked our designs."

06:26.437 --> 06:30.120
And basically we say, "Okay, ideally

06:30.120 --> 06:33.780
in the future we want to be able to share animated gifts

06:33.780 --> 06:35.370
as photos as well

06:35.370 --> 06:37.410
and maybe paste photos from the clipboard.

06:37.410 --> 06:41.520
But for this MVP, we're only going to allow uploading

06:41.520 --> 06:45.330
from a device that's just to kind of get the base level

06:45.330 --> 06:47.430
in there without doing too much work yet

06:47.430 --> 06:49.890
still providing some functionality for the users.

06:49.890 --> 06:51.960
Notice that we also are saying we expect this

06:51.960 --> 06:54.264
functionality to increase the number

06:54.264 --> 06:56.839
of messages sent per user by 15% on average.

06:56.839 --> 07:01.200
And you know, this results in a 4% time in app

07:01.200 --> 07:05.502
for each user over the course of a week, it increases by 4%.

07:05.502 --> 07:07.080
This is all stuff that you

07:07.080 --> 07:09.720
as a product manager are responsible for figuring out ahead

07:09.720 --> 07:13.224
of time using data and then writing in here in the epics.

07:13.224 --> 07:16.424
So then we've got the product requirements, okay.

07:16.424 --> 07:20.220
User can initiate a photo message from the message window.

07:20.220 --> 07:22.680
User can select a photo from their own device

07:22.680 --> 07:25.480
or a connected device, user can preview an image

07:25.480 --> 07:27.000
before sending.

07:27.000 --> 07:28.530
We want users to be able to send very

07:28.530 --> 07:30.504
high resolution images.

07:30.504 --> 07:32.647
This is all stuff that you're gonna think,

07:32.647 --> 07:35.190
"Okay, as a product manager, according to data

07:35.190 --> 07:36.883
this is what we're gonna need

07:36.883 --> 07:39.683
in in terms of functionality here

07:39.683 --> 07:43.200
for this to be successful through all the testing

07:43.200 --> 07:45.560
and user interviews we've done and that sort of thing."

07:45.560 --> 07:47.580
And then you and the designer work

07:47.580 --> 07:48.900
on the design requirements

07:48.900 --> 07:50.904
but a lot of it comes from the designer.

07:50.904 --> 07:54.510
They're saying, "we should store the photos on our servers

07:54.510 --> 07:56.970
so that the users can always see these pictures

07:56.970 --> 07:58.890
even if they go to like another computer

07:58.890 --> 08:01.170
they wanna look at the old pictures they sent."

08:01.170 --> 08:03.944
And this is something that UI designers and UX designers

08:03.944 --> 08:06.630
they're thinking about the user experience.

08:06.630 --> 08:09.840
So they're going to say things like this

08:09.840 --> 08:12.480
like it's important for them to see it in multiple locations

08:12.480 --> 08:15.443
because they think that is important as a UX designer.

08:15.443 --> 08:17.400
And that is up to them.

08:17.400 --> 08:18.930
So then we have, okay,

08:18.930 --> 08:22.410
maximum photo size should be 5,000 by 5,000 pixels.

08:22.410 --> 08:24.342
The designer thinks that's very important

08:24.342 --> 08:26.501
previews should be this size.

08:26.501 --> 08:29.480
And then also they're thinking about that actual experience.

08:29.480 --> 08:32.024
So once the user successfully uploads

08:32.024 --> 08:34.980
then we show a success indicator.

08:34.980 --> 08:37.080
And then we have the engineering requirements.

08:37.080 --> 08:38.730
So the engineers have looked

08:38.730 --> 08:41.640
at this with us at the Epic spec

08:41.640 --> 08:43.680
and we've talked to them about all this functionality.

08:43.680 --> 08:45.539
They've read the design requirements

08:45.539 --> 08:48.000
and they come in and they write some additions.

08:48.000 --> 08:49.170
They're like, "Okay, for this

08:49.170 --> 08:50.730
we're gonna need a photo database,

08:50.730 --> 08:52.883
'cause we don't have one yet.

08:52.883 --> 08:54.517
It's gonna be able to need to scale

08:54.517 --> 08:55.350
to a hundred million images

08:55.350 --> 09:00.270
with a maximum file size of 10 MB per image."

09:00.270 --> 09:01.740
So they're thinking ahead in terms

09:01.740 --> 09:04.339
of how much traffic they can support, and if it's scalable

09:04.339 --> 09:07.140
we're gonna need to pull user IDs

09:07.140 --> 09:10.080
from the user profile table in order to associate

09:10.080 --> 09:13.059
that message and that photo and that user together.

09:13.059 --> 09:15.540
And then they're gonna ensure

09:15.540 --> 09:18.540
that the content delivery network is optimized for this.

09:18.540 --> 09:19.440
So we can get pictures

09:19.440 --> 09:21.750
to people over in Europe really quickly.

09:21.750 --> 09:23.340
So, so on and so forth.

09:23.340 --> 09:25.200
You understand just by going through this.

09:25.200 --> 09:27.779
And then you see we also have some wires in here

09:27.779 --> 09:31.778
and usually this is some of the earlier wires.

09:31.778 --> 09:34.590
And then in the actual tickets themselves

09:34.590 --> 09:36.990
we've got the very specific wires around, okay

09:36.990 --> 09:39.535
the pixel widths and all that, like I mentioned before.

09:39.535 --> 09:42.510
And then JIRA allows you to comment down here

09:42.510 --> 09:44.838
and it shows you the issues they call 'em issues

09:44.838 --> 09:47.102
or tickets in the Epic.

09:47.102 --> 09:49.860
Okay, so really quick, let's recap on this.

09:49.860 --> 09:53.730
I'm gonna go back to the work board.

09:53.730 --> 09:56.380
This is where in a sprint and this is what's going on

09:57.444 --> 09:58.583
but we'll start off in the backlog.

09:59.443 --> 10:00.840
We've added stuff to this sprint.

10:00.840 --> 10:03.270
The sprint is right here, the backlog is right here.

10:03.270 --> 10:05.550
This is stuff we're doing in the next sprint

10:05.550 --> 10:08.520
or the one after, or just down the way.

10:08.520 --> 10:10.680
And then you can also reprioritize stuff

10:10.680 --> 10:15.030
on the backlog based on, you know, things that happen.

10:15.030 --> 10:17.370
Like is this now more important than this?

10:17.370 --> 10:18.360
And this is something that you

10:18.360 --> 10:21.120
as a product manager are also responsible

10:21.120 --> 10:22.800
for as the prioritization.

10:22.800 --> 10:24.090
We're gonna talk about that later.

10:24.090 --> 10:27.054
But you can do that in most project management softwares.

10:27.054 --> 10:29.779
So then once we're in the sprint

10:29.779 --> 10:33.219
we can go to the sprint that is currently active

10:33.219 --> 10:36.078
and we've got to do, in progress and done.

10:36.078 --> 10:37.710
The engineers come in here

10:37.710 --> 10:39.939
they click this, they're like, "Okay, what is this?"

10:39.939 --> 10:43.182
Okay, here's the user story, here's the acceptance criteria.

10:43.182 --> 10:45.240
And they're like, "All right."

10:45.240 --> 10:46.170
And they put it in progress

10:46.170 --> 10:49.438
and they start coding and then they go to done.

10:49.438 --> 10:52.020
And in some cases, in some companies

10:52.020 --> 10:56.370
you'll even have a step before done that is called QA

10:56.370 --> 10:57.270
or testing.

10:57.270 --> 10:59.370
QA stands for quality assurance.

10:59.370 --> 11:01.939
And this is sometimes where engineers move stuff

11:01.939 --> 11:04.558
before it is sort of approved.

11:04.558 --> 11:08.190
And that's usually the phase when product managers

11:08.190 --> 11:10.819
and designers will go through the acceptance criteria

11:10.819 --> 11:14.820
and literally use a test version on a test device

11:14.820 --> 11:18.090
or a test website and say, "Okay, is all this stuff working

11:18.090 --> 11:19.260
like we said it would?"

11:19.260 --> 11:20.970
And then they go, "Okay, it is approved."

11:20.970 --> 11:22.530
