And then they move it to done.

11:22.530 --> 11:26.090
And then after the two weeks, you should be done with all

11:26.090 --> 11:28.560
of the things that you have in your sprint.

11:28.560 --> 11:30.360
So that is really it.

11:30.360 --> 11:34.260
That's an overview of typically what an epic looks like.

11:34.260 --> 11:36.960
It's just kind of a container, a grouping of stuff

11:36.960 --> 11:40.050
but you have an epic spec with it or requirements

11:40.050 --> 11:43.200
document that tells everybody about why you're doing this

11:43.200 --> 11:44.760
what you're expecting.

11:44.760 --> 11:47.130
And then you've got the requirements from the engineers

11:47.130 --> 11:49.880
and designers, and then you've got

11:49.880 --> 11:52.382
the tickets that contain those user stories

11:52.382 --> 11:54.240
and acceptance criteria.

11:54.240 --> 11:56.430
All of this stuff combined together.

11:56.430 --> 11:59.370
It makes the actual day to day workflow

11:59.370 --> 12:01.920
of engineering and product management together

12:01.920 --> 12:04.470
in a product team at a tech company.

12:04.470 --> 12:07.220
All right everyone, I will see you in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.301 --> 00:02.884
(upbeat music)

00:04.470 --> 00:05.670
-: What's up, everyone?

00:05.670 --> 00:08.070
So I want to talk really quick

00:08.070 --> 00:12.300
about how we estimate the time it's gonna take

00:12.300 --> 00:15.210
to do a particular software project

00:15.210 --> 00:17.850
or how to figure out how long it's gonna take

00:17.850 --> 00:22.087
to have us build something so that we can say,

00:22.087 --> 00:24.900
"Okay, at this point in time, we're gonna have this."

00:24.900 --> 00:27.420
This is very much related to road mapping,

00:27.420 --> 00:30.600
so I wanna bring it up before we talk about road mapping.

00:30.600 --> 00:33.630
First, let's talk about a real world situation.

00:33.630 --> 00:36.030
And this is gonna help you understand software estimation

00:36.030 --> 00:37.200
a little bit more.

00:37.200 --> 00:42.200
Okay, imagine that you're taking your car to a mechanic

00:42.600 --> 00:44.677
and you say to the mechanic,

00:44.677 --> 00:47.430
"I would like you to replace my engine.

00:47.430 --> 00:50.130
I just want you to take it out, put another one in."

00:50.130 --> 00:53.670
Well, if your car is, let's say, a particular brand,

00:53.670 --> 00:56.580
and let's say it's a BMW, 'cause you're awesome

00:56.580 --> 01:00.090
and BMW's the best car out there, I think,

01:00.090 --> 01:03.810
and the mechanic, he's a BMW mechanic.

01:03.810 --> 01:08.610
So odds are that it's a BMW car, BMW mechanic,

01:08.610 --> 01:12.720
he's probably done this 100 or 1,000 times in his career.

01:12.720 --> 01:14.370
He's gonna be able to say, "All right,

01:14.370 --> 01:18.150
well, that will take me six hours or maybe one day

01:18.150 --> 01:20.343
and I'll have it ready by tomorrow."

01:20.343 --> 01:23.580
This is something that's probably very true.

01:23.580 --> 01:24.810
It's very easy for someone

01:24.810 --> 01:26.850
who's done something 1,000 times

01:26.850 --> 01:28.890
to predict how long it's gonna take.

01:28.890 --> 01:32.550
Now imagine that everyone on the entire planet

01:32.550 --> 01:36.330
has a different car and that all the mechanics

01:36.330 --> 01:41.250
are not specialists in any one particular car brand

01:41.250 --> 01:44.670
because everyone's building the cars themselves

01:44.670 --> 01:46.170
out of stuff they found at home.

01:46.170 --> 01:48.480
It's like everyone has a custom car.

01:48.480 --> 01:52.230
Now, if you bring your car into a mechanic,

01:52.230 --> 01:54.000
do you think they're gonna be able to give you

01:54.000 --> 01:57.480
an accurate estimate on how long that's gonna take?

01:57.480 --> 02:01.380
All right, well the answer there is no because, okay,

02:01.380 --> 02:05.040
they've never seen your car before, let alone worked on it.

02:05.040 --> 02:08.490
And it's going to take them a while to get in there

02:08.490 --> 02:10.470
and see how you built your car.

02:10.470 --> 02:13.260
Is your engine like a steam engine or a gas engine

02:13.260 --> 02:16.320
or an electric engine or like what is it?

02:16.320 --> 02:18.427
And if they try to give you an estimation like,

02:18.427 --> 02:20.580
"Okay, this is gonna take six hours,"

02:20.580 --> 02:22.800
they're probably going to be wrong.

02:22.800 --> 02:25.530
The best they could do is give a really

02:25.530 --> 02:27.547
conservative estimate and just say like,

02:27.547 --> 02:30.120
"This is maybe gonna be like a week

02:30.120 --> 02:31.740
and then I'll look into it

02:31.740 --> 02:34.350
and if it looks like it's gonna be easier,

02:34.350 --> 02:36.600
then maybe it's gonna be a little bit before then

02:36.600 --> 02:38.130
and I'll call you and let you know."

02:38.130 --> 02:42.600
So why is this analogy appropriate for software development?

02:42.600 --> 02:44.730
It's appropriate because this is the way

02:44.730 --> 02:46.410
software actually works.

02:46.410 --> 02:48.000
You have the engineers,

02:48.000 --> 02:50.430
who are pretty much the mechanics in this situation,

02:50.430 --> 02:52.920
and then you have the things they work on,

02:52.920 --> 02:57.030
the systems and databases and code bases that they work on,

02:57.030 --> 03:01.260
which are almost always completely unique.

03:01.260 --> 03:03.150
From company to company,

03:03.150 --> 03:06.510
the engineers are building things in different ways,

03:06.510 --> 03:08.730
in different languages and styles

03:08.730 --> 03:10.350
depending on the needs of the company.

03:10.350 --> 03:13.440
And to make it even worse, that stuff,

03:13.440 --> 03:16.980
those code bases, is changing all the time.

03:16.980 --> 03:20.850
So one day I could be an engineer working on a code base

03:20.850 --> 03:22.800
and then I come in next week

03:22.800 --> 03:25.140
and try to work on the same code

03:25.140 --> 03:28.650
and four engineers have edited it in between that time,

03:28.650 --> 03:30.600
working on some other feature.

03:30.600 --> 03:32.760
So things change and I have to get used to it.

03:32.760 --> 03:33.810
This, in a nutshell,

03:33.810 --> 03:36.450
is why software estimation is very hard.

03:36.450 --> 03:38.820
A lot of people say, "Oh, engineers are terrible

03:38.820 --> 03:41.517
at estimating software like deadlines."

03:42.659 --> 03:44.100
It couldn't be further from the truth.

03:44.100 --> 03:45.510
They're actually quite good,

03:45.510 --> 03:47.760
given the complexity of software.

03:47.760 --> 03:49.470
But the fact is, here,

03:49.470 --> 03:53.640
that you really can't really say that, "Okay,

03:53.640 --> 03:57.150
we know for sure that from one estimation,

03:57.150 --> 03:59.070
from one engineer, that this thing

03:59.070 --> 04:00.480
is gonna be done on this date."

04:00.480 --> 04:01.800
So how do we get around that?

04:01.800 --> 04:05.370
The way we accurately, or at least semi-accurately

04:05.370 --> 04:08.340
estimate software is by figuring out

04:08.340 --> 04:10.950
what is called a velocity.

04:10.950 --> 04:12.360
So this goes directly

04:12.360 --> 04:14.580
into what we were talking about with sprints.

04:14.580 --> 04:15.810
Now I'm not showing you this

04:15.810 --> 04:17.910
inside a project management software

04:17.910 --> 04:20.850
because the way that velocities and all of this stuff works

04:20.850 --> 04:23.610
in different project management softwares,

04:23.610 --> 04:25.650
it differs from software to software.

04:25.650 --> 04:28.530
So instead, I'm gonna explain the concept to you

04:28.530 --> 04:32.040
so that you more clearly understand the theory behind this.

04:32.040 --> 04:33.390
So let's say we have a sprint

04:33.390 --> 04:37.020
and we are doing five things in this sprint.

04:37.020 --> 04:40.590
We're doing five user stories that are a part of an epic.

04:40.590 --> 04:43.440
What happens is, before we start a sprint,

04:43.440 --> 04:45.990
we do something called a sprint planning meeting

04:45.990 --> 04:49.117
or a scoping meeting, and we ask the engineers,

04:49.117 --> 04:53.280
"How long is this gonna take for this thing and this thing?"

04:53.280 --> 04:55.020
And we ask a few of them, not just one.

04:55.020 --> 04:57.180
We ask a couple engineers on the team.

04:57.180 --> 04:58.897
And they come to some conclusion, they say,

04:58.897 --> 05:01.140
"It's gonna take this amount of time."

05:01.140 --> 05:03.900
The trouble is if we ask them how long it's gonna take,

05:03.900 --> 05:05.490
it's still a little bit inaccurate.

05:05.490 --> 05:10.260
So the trick is we say, "How hard is it to do this?"

05:10.260 --> 05:11.550
And the way we measure

05:11.550 --> 05:14.160
or the way we quantify how hard something is

05:14.160 --> 05:15.630
is through numbers.

05:15.630 --> 05:18.480
And these are called story points.

05:18.480 --> 05:21.630
So each story has some number assigned to it

05:21.630 --> 05:24.450
by an engineer or a couple engineers

05:24.450 --> 05:26.970
and they represent these story points,

05:26.970 --> 05:28.680
they represent the difficulty.

05:28.680 --> 05:30.630
Now, some teams and some companies

05:30.630 --> 05:33.120
use different scales for story points.

05:33.120 --> 05:36.720
You could have a scale like one, two, three, four, five,

05:36.720 --> 05:40.320
where five is the hardest and one is the easiest.

05:40.320 --> 05:42.570
Or you could have like a Fibonacci sequence,

05:42.570 --> 05:45.000
where it's like one, two and three,

05:45.000 --> 05:46.710
all the way up to like 21.

05:46.710 --> 05:49.050
You could even have like one through 100.

05:49.050 --> 05:52.350
It doesn't matter which scale you use.

05:52.350 --> 05:55.860
What matters is that you're consistent with these scales,

05:55.860 --> 05:57.840
so you're always using the same scale

05:57.840 --> 06:00.240
and all of the engineers understand that,

06:00.240 --> 06:02.100
okay, a five means super, super hard

06:02.100 --> 06:03.780
and a one means very easy.

06:03.780 --> 06:06.690
So what you do here is you estimate,

06:06.690 --> 06:10.230
you give all of these things in your sprint a story,

06:10.230 --> 06:12.270
an amount of story points, basically,

06:12.270 --> 06:14.940
and then your sprint has what is called

06:14.940 --> 06:17.220
like the total story points for that sprint.

06:17.220 --> 06:18.990
Okay, so at the end of the sprint,

06:18.990 --> 06:22.200
we see how many things we actually did get done,

06:22.200 --> 06:25.050
how many story points were the developers

06:25.050 --> 06:26.970
able to actually complete.

06:26.970 --> 06:31.830
So if we had five items and we only got three done,

06:31.830 --> 06:35.160
but those things had to combine story point value

06:35.160 --> 06:37.890
of, let's say, 15, because they were,

06:37.890 --> 06:40.230
there was only three things we did, but they were very hard,

06:40.230 --> 06:42.180
so it was five points each,

06:42.180 --> 06:44.280
and we're using the one-to-five scale,

06:44.280 --> 06:46.560
then what we have at the end of the sprint

06:46.560 --> 06:48.540
is what's called a velocity.

06:48.540 --> 06:51.060
And that velocity now is 15.

06:51.060 --> 06:54.900
It's the number of story points we were able to accomplish

06:54.900 --> 06:56.850
in a one period of sprint,

06:56.850 --> 07:00.120
like a one two-week sprint period of time.

07:00.120 --> 07:01.507
All right, so now you're wondering like,

07:01.507 --> 07:03.210
"Okay, well, how does that help me?

07:03.210 --> 07:06.930
Does that mean that I can do 15 points in the next sprint?"

07:06.930 --> 07:10.200
Well kind of, but I'll tell you how we do it

07:10.200 --> 07:11.520
even more accurately.

07:11.520 --> 07:15.600
What we do is this process over many, many sprints.

07:15.600 --> 07:19.020
So back to the analogy of the mechanic, that first mechanic,

07:19.020 --> 07:21.840
he was able to make a accurate estimation

07:21.840 --> 07:25.380
because he had worked on like 100 or 1,000

07:25.380 --> 07:27.900
of these exact types of projects before.

07:27.900 --> 07:31.260
Well, if we take our sprint velocity from one sprint

07:31.260 --> 07:32.640
and then we go through, let's say,

07:32.640 --> 07:36.690
five more sprints in the future, we take those velocities,

07:36.690 --> 07:39.780
we take all these velocities and we average them out,

07:39.780 --> 07:43.860
then you have like a, basically, a roundabout way

07:43.860 --> 07:48.860
of seeing, on average, how much work can you get done

07:49.140 --> 07:51.300
over a particular period of time.

07:51.300 --> 07:52.890
So you have an average sprint velocity

07:52.890 --> 07:55.830
of, let's say, like 12 or 16.

07:55.830 --> 07:58.680
And then what you can do is say, "All right,

07:58.680 --> 08:00.540
in the future, we wanna do this thing

08:00.540 --> 08:01.710
and we talked to the engineers

08:01.710 --> 08:03.570
and they think it's this many story points

08:03.570 --> 08:05.280
in order to do these things."

08:05.280 --> 08:07.020
Well, we can extrapolate out

08:07.020 --> 08:11.370
and say that according to our past average velocity,

08:11.370 --> 08:14.580
this is gonna take us one month or two months.

08:14.580 --> 08:17.820
And in the end, this ends up being pretty accurate.

08:17.820 --> 08:21.960
Not 100% accurate, but it's the most accurate you can get

08:21.960 --> 08:23.790
for software development.

08:23.790 --> 08:26.340
One more thing to mention here is about that scoping

08:26.340 --> 08:28.980
and that estimation from the engineers.

08:28.980 --> 08:32.550
Usually this happens earlier, before you start a project,

08:32.550 --> 08:36.510
and we like to do it before we start a sprint

08:36.510 --> 08:38.550
in either a sprint planning session,

08:38.550 --> 08:41.940
or even in the middle of the previous sprint,

08:41.940 --> 08:44.850
we'll talk about what's coming up in the sprint after that

08:44.850 --> 08:46.230
and we will have the engineers

08:46.230 --> 08:48.930
and the engineering manager talk about, okay,

08:48.930 --> 08:49.770
how many story points

08:49.770 --> 08:52.500
are each one of these tickets and user stories?

08:52.500 --> 08:53.400
All right, so that's it.

08:53.400 --> 08:57.810
That is really engineering software estimation

08:57.810 --> 09:00.900
in a nutshell, and now you've learned about velocity.

09:00.900 --> 09:03.510
And this, again, is why Scrum is pretty useful,

09:03.510 --> 09:07.230
because on Kanban, it's a little bit more difficult

09:07.230 --> 09:09.000
to predict this sort of stuff

09:09.000 --> 09:12.660
'cause you don't timebox your work like we mentioned before.

09:12.660 --> 09:15.630
That's why Scrum basically has these timeboxes

09:15.630 --> 09:18.360
so that we can say, "Okay, over a period of time,

09:18.360 --> 09:20.430
we're able to get this amount of work done,"

09:20.430 --> 09:22.950
and it contributes to accuracy in the long term.

09:22.950 --> 09:24.983
All right, I'll see you guys in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.090 --> 00:01.830
-: Hey, everyone, what's up?

00:01.830 --> 00:04.830
Now we're going to talk about roadmapping.

00:04.830 --> 00:06.270
Okay, I'm sorry in advance,

00:06.270 --> 00:08.341
but I'm not going to actually show you

00:08.341 --> 00:12.750
a real life roadmap right now,

00:12.750 --> 00:14.850
and there's a good reason behind it.

00:14.850 --> 00:17.598
This is because every company you go to,

00:17.598 --> 00:20.940
every single company out there does roadmapping differently.

00:20.940 --> 00:24.240
Furthermore, roadmaps are always,

00:24.240 --> 00:27.480
if not mostly always, just inaccurate.

00:27.480 --> 00:29.880
So we know about how Agile works,

00:29.880 --> 00:31.560
so from what we know there,

00:31.560 --> 00:34.050
can we think about why roadmaps are inaccurate?

00:34.050 --> 00:37.080
Because if you're doing things in an agile fashion,

00:37.080 --> 00:40.710
do you think you're going to be able to predict

00:40.710 --> 00:42.540
three to four months out

00:42.540 --> 00:44.820
what we're gonna be doing at that point?

00:44.820 --> 00:47.520
The whole purpose of Agile is to say

00:47.520 --> 00:49.350
that we're gonna try some stuff

00:49.350 --> 00:51.210
right around this period of time,

00:51.210 --> 00:52.890
this is what's coming next for sure

00:52.890 --> 00:54.750
because it's a big user problem

00:54.750 --> 00:55.830
and we're gonna try to solve it,

00:55.830 --> 00:58.740
but we may solve it now with this initiative

00:58.740 --> 01:00.690
or maybe we don't, and we get rid of that,

01:00.690 --> 01:03.180
we do something else to solve that.

01:03.180 --> 01:05.160
So these things can shift around.

01:05.160 --> 01:08.220
So if you ever see roadmaps that say

01:08.220 --> 01:10.080
in quarter one we're doing this,

01:10.080 --> 01:11.460
in quarter two we're doing this,

01:11.460 --> 01:13.828
in quarter three, this is what we're doing,

01:13.828 --> 01:17.920
it's okay to have those things as a general guide

01:17.920 --> 01:22.770
in terms of what might be coming in what parts of the year,

01:22.770 --> 01:24.960
the first half, the second half of the year,

01:24.960 --> 01:27.000
as a general guide.

01:27.000 --> 01:29.130
But it's not going to be accurate

01:29.130 --> 01:31.470
down to the date that you say it is.

01:31.470 --> 01:34.500
And if it is, then you are not being agile.

01:34.500 --> 01:36.810
So why do you ever see roadmaps that are like,

01:36.810 --> 01:39.300
in Q1, we're doing this, in Q2, we're doing this,

01:39.300 --> 01:40.920
in Q3, we're doing this.

01:40.920 --> 01:43.380
It's because, well, for a couple reasons.

01:43.380 --> 01:46.158
One, executives and other departments and investors,

01:46.158 --> 01:48.690
they like to see these sorts of things.

01:48.690 --> 01:50.970
But the good ones will realize that stuff

01:50.970 --> 01:53.100
that's like three quarters out there

01:53.100 --> 01:55.566
is not gonna be accurate if you're being agile.

01:55.566 --> 01:58.350
It could be that it takes us a whole quarter

01:58.350 --> 01:59.970
to figure out one problem through

01:59.970 --> 02:02.580
a bunch of different user tests and solutions,

02:02.580 --> 02:04.260
and it moves stuff back.

02:04.260 --> 02:08.070
Or it could be that something becomes a non problem.

02:08.070 --> 02:10.380
Maybe we were gonna build something to compete

02:10.380 --> 02:13.170
with another company and that company goes away

02:13.170 --> 02:14.430
or they go bankrupt.

02:14.430 --> 02:16.320
Oh, okay, now we don't have to do that anymore.

02:16.320 --> 02:19.260
The second reason you sometimes see these roadmaps

02:19.260 --> 02:22.980
that are Q1, Q2, Q3, that sort of thing,

02:22.980 --> 02:25.170
is because sometimes,

02:25.170 --> 02:27.420
and especially in business to business companies,

02:27.420 --> 02:29.280
you actually are going to be

02:29.280 --> 02:32.130
against an actual real world deadline.

02:32.130 --> 02:34.140
So large companies will do this a lot

02:34.140 --> 02:37.470
because they have investors that say

02:37.470 --> 02:39.630
that they have to have a certain thing,

02:39.630 --> 02:40.650
a certain functionality,

02:40.650 --> 02:43.380
to compete with somebody done by a certain date.

02:43.380 --> 02:47.310
Or maybe you are at a software as a service company

02:47.310 --> 02:50.310
and your sales people sell a new feature

02:50.310 --> 02:52.650
that you haven't built yet to a customer.

02:52.650 --> 02:55.560
And the customer's like, "We need this by February."

02:55.560 --> 02:58.540
So at that point, you can put something like that

02:59.526 --> 03:02.370
on a time-based roadmap and you gotta stick to it.

03:02.370 --> 03:05.220
You gotta figure out how you can do it before that time.

03:05.220 --> 03:06.270
But I just wanna let you know that

03:06.270 --> 03:09.780
these types of a roadmaps are generally inaccurate,

03:09.780 --> 03:11.760
but it's okay to still look at them.

03:11.760 --> 03:13.470
Just keep in mind that they're inaccurate.

03:13.470 --> 03:14.820
So what's the alternative?

03:14.820 --> 03:16.380
What do we do instead?

03:16.380 --> 03:18.810
Well, what do a lot of product managers do,

03:18.810 --> 03:21.390
and a lot of just product development teams do out there,

03:21.390 --> 03:25.520
is they just basically sort things into kind of priorities.

03:25.520 --> 03:27.330
Here's what we're doing now.

03:27.330 --> 03:29.668
They'll have like a column, and then, okay, this is now,

03:29.668 --> 03:31.170
this is what we're doing immediately,

03:31.170 --> 03:33.360
in the next few weeks, the next month.

03:33.360 --> 03:36.030
And then they have another column that's like midterm.

03:36.030 --> 03:38.580
So here's the stuff we're gonna do after that.

03:38.580 --> 03:39.780
And then they'll have another bucket

03:39.780 --> 03:41.370
that's called long term.

03:41.370 --> 03:43.770
And this is stuff that we want to get to eventually

03:43.770 --> 03:46.050
but it's not a huge priority right now.

03:46.050 --> 03:51.050
So those three columns, the near term, midterm, long term,

03:51.067 --> 03:53.040
are usually pretty helpful because

03:53.040 --> 03:56.580
intentionally it doesn't say any particular dates on there

03:56.580 --> 03:58.830
and it keeps everyone in line

03:58.830 --> 04:01.680
by not expecting things by a certain date.

04:01.680 --> 04:03.990
This three bucket model is something

04:03.990 --> 04:06.780
you're gonna find a lot at consumer tech companies,

04:06.780 --> 04:10.833
especially ones that have the luxury of building products

04:10.833 --> 04:13.650
that, well, they don't really have any deadlines,

04:13.650 --> 04:18.330
like Google and Facebook, and even like Twitter.

04:18.330 --> 04:21.090
They're not really having any financial trouble,

04:21.090 --> 04:23.280
so they can really take as long

04:23.280 --> 04:26.340
or as little time as they want to do something.

04:26.340 --> 04:29.550
They don't really have to put it into a certain quarter,

04:29.550 --> 04:31.530
a certain date, or a certain month.

04:31.530 --> 04:33.150
So you'll see a lot of these things

04:33.150 --> 04:36.960
at the really big money, high tech companies.

04:36.960 --> 04:38.880
So that's what I want to mention about roadmapping.

04:38.880 --> 04:41.940
Don't worry, we're gonna put plenty of resource guides

04:41.940 --> 04:44.640
and articles to read about roadmapping,

04:44.640 --> 04:46.500
because you do need to understand

04:46.500 --> 04:47.760
the ins and outs around it.

04:47.760 --> 04:51.510
But those are the two major things about roadmapping.

04:51.510 --> 04:56.510
One, the quarterly or monthly roadmaps are okay

04:56.670 --> 04:58.731
to make and show as long as you are

04:58.731 --> 05:00.990
on the same page as everyone else,

05:00.990 --> 05:02.167
and you're telling people like,

05:02.167 --> 05:03.870
"Look, this stuff may change."

05:03.870 --> 05:07.530
And then two, the more appropriate way

05:07.530 --> 05:10.770
or the more product management or lean way or agile way

05:10.770 --> 05:15.720
is to do sort of a near term, midterm, and long term bucket.

05:15.720 --> 05:18.776
All right, everyone, I'll catch you in the next lecture.

05:18.776 --> 05:21.359
(upbeat music)

WEBVTT

00:00.270 --> 00:02.130
-: Hey everybody, what is up?

00:02.130 --> 00:06.570
So we know already about epics and epic spec sheets

00:06.570 --> 00:08.430
and we know about user stories.

00:08.430 --> 00:12.090
So how does all this stuff get prioritized?

00:12.090 --> 00:14.970
As a product manager, like we've already talked about,

00:14.970 --> 00:18.240
prioritizing things is a major part of your role.

00:18.240 --> 00:19.290
But here's the thing.

00:19.290 --> 00:22.530
You don't just prioritize user stories

00:22.530 --> 00:25.380
like a lot of people would have you believe.

00:25.380 --> 00:28.050
You're in charge of prioritizing things

00:28.050 --> 00:30.300
all the way from the user stories,

00:30.300 --> 00:32.310
up to bigger things like epics,

00:32.310 --> 00:36.810
all the way to even you can help marketing prioritize,

00:36.810 --> 00:38.700
marketing and branding campaigns

00:38.700 --> 00:40.920
based on product launches.

00:40.920 --> 00:43.590
There's a whole bunch of stuff you got to consider

00:43.590 --> 00:45.960
and it's more than just the value

00:45.960 --> 00:50.610
of that particular thing that helps you prioritize it,

00:50.610 --> 00:52.590
because sometimes you're working in companies

00:52.590 --> 00:56.070
with other teams and different technologies

00:56.070 --> 00:59.400
and other times the competitors will do something.

00:59.400 --> 01:02.550
All of these things have a big effect on how you prioritize.

01:02.550 --> 01:05.160
That being said, I want to talk about three quick methods.

01:05.160 --> 01:07.440
They're very basic academic methods

01:07.440 --> 01:08.580
that a lot of people know about

01:08.580 --> 01:09.990
in the product management world

01:09.990 --> 01:14.400
to prioritize either user stories or epics

01:14.400 --> 01:16.380
or just initiatives that you're doing.

01:16.380 --> 01:18.000
The first thing I want to mention is

01:18.000 --> 01:20.070
there's something called assumption testing,

01:20.070 --> 01:22.380
prioritizing by the assumptions.

01:22.380 --> 01:26.460
The goal here is to pretty much de-risk what you're doing

01:26.460 --> 01:30.720
by saying, Okay, this epic or this story, this user story,

01:30.720 --> 01:33.330
we're assuming a few things here.

01:33.330 --> 01:36.210
We've tried to remove the risky assumptions

01:36.210 --> 01:38.760
through user studies and tests and data

01:38.760 --> 01:41.400
and that sort of thing, but you can't remove them all.

01:41.400 --> 01:43.710
So what are the remaining assumptions

01:43.710 --> 01:46.170
for any given epic or story

01:46.170 --> 01:50.070
and how big is the assumption that you're making?

01:50.070 --> 01:51.960
Or if you're making a few assumptions,

01:51.960 --> 01:54.990
let's see what the biggest assumption is.

01:54.990 --> 01:57.090
What you do here is you just take that

01:57.090 --> 02:01.380
biggest, riskiest assumption and you assign it a value

02:01.380 --> 02:03.540
from like 1 to 5, or 1 to 10.

02:03.540 --> 02:08.540
And you say that 10 is really, really assuming something

02:08.670 --> 02:09.870
as quite risky.

02:09.870 --> 02:12.750
Then you take a score called an important score

02:12.750 --> 02:17.040
and you also score it on the importance of doing that thing

02:17.040 --> 02:18.630
or the impact you think it's going to make.

02:18.630 --> 02:20.820
You just add these two values together

02:20.820 --> 02:23.460
and then you sort based on that.

02:23.460 --> 02:24.990
So you do that for everything.

02:24.990 --> 02:26.370
And you just sort based on that.

02:26.370 --> 02:30.240
This works particularly well on smaller stories

02:30.240 --> 02:33.090
inside of a bigger epic you're doing

02:33.090 --> 02:34.927
because what you're doing basically is saying,

02:34.927 --> 02:37.710
"All right if we're completely wrong about this one thing,

02:37.710 --> 02:39.690
we want to try it first."

02:39.690 --> 02:40.650
We can't get any more data.

02:40.650 --> 02:43.200
We can't figure out how wrong we are until we do it.

02:43.200 --> 02:44.430
So we're going to try it first

02:44.430 --> 02:45.990
to know if we're right or wrong.

02:45.990 --> 02:47.400
and might get us some more information

02:47.400 --> 02:49.710
on the next things we were thinking about doing.

02:49.710 --> 02:53.430
All right. The second method is called the buck method.

02:53.430 --> 02:57.570
This is the business benefits, the user benefits,

02:57.570 --> 02:59.130
and then the cost.

02:59.130 --> 03:01.050
Basically, you want to come up with a value

03:01.050 --> 03:04.080
from like 1 to 5, or 1 to 10 for each of these.

03:04.080 --> 03:05.760
Score them appropriately.

03:05.760 --> 03:07.470
Add up these scores.

03:07.470 --> 03:11.460
And then you basically take the benefits part,

03:11.460 --> 03:13.530
the business benefits and user benefits,

03:13.530 --> 03:17.640
subtract the cost score from the sum of those

03:17.640 --> 03:19.260
and you get a final score.

03:19.260 --> 03:21.480
And then you prioritize all of your stuff

03:21.480 --> 03:23.490
based on those final scores

03:23.490 --> 03:25.710
with the highest being up at the top.

03:25.710 --> 03:27.360
There is a problem with this though

03:27.360 --> 03:29.850
and that is that it's pretty challenging

03:29.850 --> 03:33.390
to accurately like score things based on these sometimes.

03:33.390 --> 03:36.330
That sometimes there's just not enough data available

03:36.330 --> 03:38.820
to make an accurate number out of it, right?

03:38.820 --> 03:40.857
So there's one last method I want to talk about.

03:40.857 --> 03:44.730
And this one's quite common called the Moscow Method.

03:44.730 --> 03:47.700
It's the M-O-S-C-O-W.

03:47.700 --> 03:49.110
You might have seen it spelled like that.

03:49.110 --> 03:52.590
This stands for must, could, should, and would.

03:52.590 --> 03:54.570
Basically, take everything you've got,

03:54.570 --> 03:56.100
everything you intend to do.

03:56.100 --> 03:59.527
All these stories or epics, and you just say,

03:59.527 --> 04:01.140
"Okay we're going to imagine

04:01.140 --> 04:03.000
we're not going to do any of these.

04:03.000 --> 04:05.190
What is the worst thing that happens?"

04:05.190 --> 04:06.570
Like what's going to happen?

04:06.570 --> 04:07.500
We do none of this.

04:07.500 --> 04:10.590
So from there you find out things that, okay,

04:10.590 --> 04:14.340
we absolutely cannot live without building this

04:14.340 --> 04:15.900
for a number of reasons,

04:15.900 --> 04:18.600
and you can call those things the musts.

04:18.600 --> 04:21.577
Then you go over all of the other ones and you just say,

04:21.577 --> 04:26.577
"Is this something that we should have or is it a must?

04:26.640 --> 04:28.380
Or is it something we could do

04:28.380 --> 04:31.020
or something that we would do if we got the opportunity?"

04:31.020 --> 04:33.420
This is really not science.

04:33.420 --> 04:35.070
It's just going through things

04:35.070 --> 04:38.737
and then thinking very deeply about each one and saying,

04:38.737 --> 04:41.970
"Is this something we absolutely must have?"

04:41.970 --> 04:44.190
Or things are going to get really bad

04:44.190 --> 04:48.510
either with the business in terms of revenue or survival

04:48.510 --> 04:49.680
and that kind of thing.

04:49.680 --> 04:53.850
Last thing to mention about that Moscow method is that

04:53.850 --> 04:57.690
if you have something that is a must have,

04:57.690 --> 04:59.370
you can obviously not have that

04:59.370 --> 05:01.350
depend on you doing something else

05:01.350 --> 05:02.520
that you haven't done yet.

05:02.520 --> 05:03.930
So if there's one feature

05:03.930 --> 05:06.450
that depends on another feature being there

05:06.450 --> 05:08.820
and that first feature is a must,

05:08.820 --> 05:12.030
well then it makes sense that the other feature also

05:12.030 --> 05:14.130
must be a must have.

05:14.130 --> 05:16.770
All right, so that's it for those three methods.

05:16.770 --> 05:20.070
We've got Buck, we've got assumption testing

05:20.070 --> 05:22.260
and we've got the Moscow method.

05:22.260 --> 05:25.590
Those are useful, but they're not going to be applicable

05:25.590 --> 05:28.920
to everything you're prioritizing as a product manager,

05:28.920 --> 05:32.250
because like I said, there's all sorts of crazy things

05:32.250 --> 05:34.170
happening out there in the market.

05:34.170 --> 05:35.640
There's companies that go bankrupt

05:35.640 --> 05:37.380
and companies that get acquired.

05:37.380 --> 05:39.900
And then you've got other teams

05:39.900 --> 05:42.810
that are working on things that you didn't know about.

05:42.810 --> 05:46.770
Or maybe there's a new technology available

05:46.770 --> 05:48.660
all of a sudden that allows you to do something.

05:48.660 --> 05:51.510
All of these things affect priorities everywhere

05:51.510 --> 05:55.620
in the business, not just on small user stories or epics,

05:55.620 --> 06:00.030
but on the direction and the vision and the direction

06:00.030 --> 06:03.540
that the entire product team and the entire product team

06:03.540 --> 06:05.910
all over the company heads towards.

06:05.910 --> 06:09.120
Keep these methods in mind, but just remember

06:09.120 --> 06:13.050
they're not the only thing you can use to prioritize things.

06:13.050 --> 06:14.640
You can also think for yourself

06:14.640 --> 06:17.610
and go by your instinct with the data you got.

06:17.610 --> 06:20.760
I'll make one last note by the way, and say that

06:20.760 --> 06:25.760
some of the biggest successes out there in terms of products

06:25.980 --> 06:30.960
were not decided on some sort of fancy,

06:30.960 --> 06:35.010
logical, quantitative decision,

06:35.010 --> 06:36.300
but they come from companies

06:36.300 --> 06:40.230
that engender a risk taking attitude

06:40.230 --> 06:42.210
or have structures in place

06:42.210 --> 06:44.400
to let the employees take some risk.

06:44.400 --> 06:47.700
If you work at a company that is not going to go bankrupt

06:47.700 --> 06:49.200
if you screw up a little bit,

06:49.200 --> 06:51.810
work with your manager, and your bosses,

06:51.810 --> 06:54.090
and the CEO and the executives,

06:54.090 --> 06:56.010
and just think about ways

06:56.010 --> 07:00.300
that you could be a little bit more creative with your team

07:00.300 --> 07:03.000
or even with some of the other teams

07:03.000 --> 07:06.210
to come out with ideas for products

07:06.210 --> 07:08.310
that have never been created before.

07:08.310 --> 07:10.440
Go a step further.

07:10.440 --> 07:11.970
It's okay to do that,

07:11.970 --> 07:15.060
but the main goal is not to bankrupt your company

07:15.060 --> 07:18.210
by screwing up on a really important feature.

07:18.210 --> 07:20.453
All right, everybody. See you in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.240 --> 00:01.500
-: Hey, what's up?

00:01.500 --> 00:04.020
This section is an important one.

00:04.020 --> 00:08.040
This is all about working with people and stakeholders.

00:08.040 --> 00:10.710
It's communication skills and that sort of thing.

00:10.710 --> 00:12.030
So let's get started.

00:12.030 --> 00:15.120
By far, one of the most important skills to master

00:15.120 --> 00:17.430
as a product manager is communication.

00:17.430 --> 00:18.780
And you already know why.

00:18.780 --> 00:20.850
Because being a communication hub

00:20.850 --> 00:22.890
is one of the biggest parts of our job.

00:22.890 --> 00:25.260
Communication happens in a lot of different channels,

00:25.260 --> 00:27.270
so let's talk about a few here.

00:27.270 --> 00:29.070
So first, we've got meetings.

00:29.070 --> 00:31.110
This is among the most important areas

00:31.110 --> 00:34.320
a product manager needs to be a great communicator in,

00:34.320 --> 00:36.900
just these company and one to one meetings.

00:36.900 --> 00:39.750
So much of what affects our product occurs in meetings

00:39.750 --> 00:41.730
and knowing just how to take advantage

00:41.730 --> 00:43.700
of the face to face and internet meetings

00:43.700 --> 00:46.230
is a key portion of the product management job.

00:46.230 --> 00:48.240
We also have to learn how to make sure

00:48.240 --> 00:52.710
that activities that come from meetings are followed up on.

00:52.710 --> 00:55.680
If you send a recap email or notes after a meeting

00:55.680 --> 00:57.660
and you ensure everything gets followed up on

00:57.660 --> 00:59.040
from all the people in the meeting,

00:59.040 --> 01:01.380
you're gonna score major points as a product manager.

01:01.380 --> 01:03.540
Number two, conference calls,

01:03.540 --> 01:05.550
and this includes video meetings as well.

01:05.550 --> 01:07.230
If you've got offices all around the world

01:07.230 --> 01:09.030
these are gonna be very frequent.

01:09.030 --> 01:13.140
Communicating here very well is dependent on the intonation

01:13.140 --> 01:16.020
and the clarity of your verbal message.

01:16.020 --> 01:18.630
Ensure that people are following along with you.

01:18.630 --> 01:20.280
And if you have any resources,

01:20.280 --> 01:23.280
or you're sharing some slide decks or stats,

01:23.280 --> 01:26.010
make sure there's links to all of that in the meeting invite

01:26.010 --> 01:28.440
or in an email that's associated with the meeting.

01:28.440 --> 01:30.180
All right, number three, emails.

01:30.180 --> 01:33.000
This is gonna be the bulk of communication

01:33.000 --> 01:35.730
as a product manager, your primary method.

01:35.730 --> 01:38.910
Ensure that what you're writing is clear, it's concise,

01:38.910 --> 01:41.550
and you have links to all of the data and resources

01:41.550 --> 01:43.290
that you refer to in the email.

01:43.290 --> 01:46.920
Number five, informal meetings, like lunch meetings.

01:46.920 --> 01:48.510
These are casual environments.

01:48.510 --> 01:51.960
It can be great for connecting, getting feedback, ideas,

01:51.960 --> 01:53.850
and working to build support.

01:53.850 --> 01:55.260
Don't underestimate the power

01:55.260 --> 01:56.700
of having lunch with a coworker

01:56.700 --> 01:58.980
and to tell them about what you're working on

01:58.980 --> 02:00.240
and get their input.

02:00.240 --> 02:01.073
So in general,

02:01.073 --> 02:03.510
there's a lot of ways we communicate as a product manager,

02:03.510 --> 02:05.820
but to become a truly stellar product manager,

02:05.820 --> 02:08.400
you must learn the art of communicating differently

02:08.400 --> 02:10.080
with different types of people.

02:10.080 --> 02:13.140
There's an old saying that goes six different audiences,

02:13.140 --> 02:14.880
six different slide decks.

02:14.880 --> 02:16.710
We're gonna talk more about that next.

02:16.710 --> 02:19.013
I'll see you in the next lecture.

02:19.013 --> 02:22.513
(upbeat electronic music)

WEBVTT

00:00.210 --> 00:02.010
-: Hey there. Good to see you again.

00:02.010 --> 00:03.270
Let's get into the details

00:03.270 --> 00:05.970
on how to communicate with different types of people

00:05.970 --> 00:08.370
and we'll start first with engineers.

00:08.370 --> 00:11.160
So engineers are lovers of detail.

00:11.160 --> 00:13.950
When talking to them, emailing them, or anything else,

00:13.950 --> 00:16.440
be very detailed about the thing you're describing.

00:16.440 --> 00:18.510
This is because engineers are always thinking

00:18.510 --> 00:21.000
about software and how what you're saying may

00:21.000 --> 00:23.550
or may not work in a technical sense.

00:23.550 --> 00:25.770
The clearer you are here, the better.

00:25.770 --> 00:27.780
I can't tell you how many times early in my career

00:27.780 --> 00:30.485
I was not detailed enough in my talks with engineers

00:30.485 --> 00:33.600
or while sending them feature specifications.

00:33.600 --> 00:35.490
In order to develop efficiently,

00:35.490 --> 00:37.980
they need to know things that other people don't.

00:37.980 --> 00:39.630
Things like what happens when the screen

00:39.630 --> 00:41.280
is displayed in another language?

00:41.280 --> 00:42.510
What is the error state read

00:42.510 --> 00:44.310
when something goes wrong on this page?

00:44.310 --> 00:47.190
What happens when there's no data to see on this page?

00:47.190 --> 00:49.530
This is often referred to as an empty state.

00:49.530 --> 00:51.630
As time goes on, you'll get better at predicting

00:51.630 --> 00:54.750
what types of information the engineers need from you.

00:54.750 --> 00:56.850
So now I'm going to list off some miscellaneous tips

00:56.850 --> 00:59.730
for working with engineers as a product manager.

00:59.730 --> 01:02.400
Number one, if something goes wrong, it's your fault.

01:02.400 --> 01:04.950
This is not just being nice, this is the truth.

01:04.950 --> 01:07.740
You're responsible for the success of the product.

01:07.740 --> 01:09.060
If something goes wrong

01:09.060 --> 01:10.800
or an engineer develops something incorrectly

01:10.800 --> 01:12.870
because you gave them the wrong specs,

01:12.870 --> 01:15.180
that is your fault, not theirs.

01:15.180 --> 01:17.430
Number two, when you're pitching a feature,

01:17.430 --> 01:19.620
be prepared and have really good idea

01:19.620 --> 01:22.080
of where are the features going to go in the future.

01:22.080 --> 01:24.540
When we do Agile development, we think of a feature

01:24.540 --> 01:26.550
and we think about how it's going to look down the road

01:26.550 --> 01:27.630
in an ideal state,

01:27.630 --> 01:30.690
but also what the minimum viable product will be.

01:30.690 --> 01:32.040
Telling developers, where you think

01:32.040 --> 01:33.510
something's going to go down the road

01:33.510 --> 01:35.160
is going to help them plan out

01:35.160 --> 01:38.010
how we're going to do this technically in the future.

01:38.010 --> 01:39.480
And that matters a lot.

01:39.480 --> 01:43.500
Number three, when possible, you should do the work upfront

01:43.500 --> 01:45.510
on things like checking commit logs

01:45.510 --> 01:49.560
or looking up data before you ask engineers to do it.

01:49.560 --> 01:52.530
Trying yourself before you ask for someone else's time

01:52.530 --> 01:54.060
is a big sign of respect.

01:54.060 --> 01:55.200
And developers like to know

01:55.200 --> 01:57.630
that they aren't the only ones doing the work.

01:57.630 --> 01:59.700
This will also score you major points.

01:59.700 --> 02:01.830
Number four, watch out for tech debt.

02:01.830 --> 02:03.180
So what is tech debt?

02:03.180 --> 02:05.160
It's a term we use to describe something

02:05.160 --> 02:06.360
in the code or technology

02:06.360 --> 02:08.820
which is going to have to be dealt with later

02:08.820 --> 02:11.160
as the result of not doing something correctly

02:11.160 --> 02:12.480
the first time.

02:12.480 --> 02:13.950
Let me give you an example here.

02:13.950 --> 02:16.560
If we're building a new feature that requires a database,

02:16.560 --> 02:18.690
maybe we learn that one way to save time

02:18.690 --> 02:21.450
is to utilize a database that already exists.

02:21.450 --> 02:23.790
However, if the feature becomes very popular

02:23.790 --> 02:25.200
and it needs more space,

02:25.200 --> 02:27.390
then eventually it needs its own database.

02:27.390 --> 02:30.180
This impending work is what we call tech debt.

02:30.180 --> 02:32.040
We're going to have to do it at some point.

02:32.040 --> 02:33.810
Engineers hate tech debt

02:33.810 --> 02:36.210
because they're the ones that have to deal with it.

02:36.210 --> 02:38.820
Be cognizant of this and trust their judgment

02:38.820 --> 02:41.250
on the right way to do things first.

02:41.250 --> 02:43.080
You'll maybe find that a lot of them

02:43.080 --> 02:46.140
want to do something scaled out the first time

02:46.140 --> 02:47.910
to make sure we don't have to come back

02:47.910 --> 02:49.080
and deal with it later

02:49.080 --> 02:52.020
which will stop us from developing features at that point.

02:52.020 --> 02:54.180
Lastly, this is a big one.

02:54.180 --> 02:57.150
Do not treat engineers like an agency.

02:57.150 --> 02:59.610
What I mean by this is don't design

02:59.610 --> 03:01.980
and come up with all of the concepts yourself

03:01.980 --> 03:03.480
or with your designer

03:03.480 --> 03:05.460
and then just hand the completed requirements

03:05.460 --> 03:07.620
to the engineers to code.

03:07.620 --> 03:09.600
Always give engineers an opportunity

03:09.600 --> 03:12.780
to provide you with feedback and product ideas.

03:12.780 --> 03:15.060
Just because they spend their days coding

03:15.060 --> 03:17.130
doesn't mean that they don't have any good ideas

03:17.130 --> 03:19.590
or opinions of their own on the product.

03:19.590 --> 03:22.140
The way I give them the opportunity to provide feedback

03:22.140 --> 03:25.200
is actively involving them with day-to-day discussion.

03:25.200 --> 03:27.420
But also during our planning meetings,

03:27.420 --> 03:29.580
I make it a point to have a part

03:29.580 --> 03:32.700
where we tell the engineers what we think is coming up next

03:32.700 --> 03:35.220
and we give them the opportunity to tell us

03:35.220 --> 03:37.410
what they think about that from the product perspective,

03:37.410 --> 03:38.790
not even the engineering perspective,

03:38.790 --> 03:40.380
but is this a good idea?

03:40.380 --> 03:41.670
Would users use this?

03:41.670 --> 03:43.530
It's very important that you bring them in

03:43.530 --> 03:44.730
on these discussions.

03:44.730 --> 03:47.190
That is one of the best tips I can give you

03:47.190 --> 03:48.780
for working with engineers.

03:48.780 --> 03:52.380
Very important, and engineers will respect to a lot more.

03:52.380 --> 03:53.490
All right, so now you've got a good handle

03:53.490 --> 03:56.280
on how to interact and communicate with engineers.

03:56.280 --> 03:57.540
Let's go onto the next lecture

03:57.540 --> 03:59.640
and talk a little bit about designers.

03:59.640 --> 04:00.473
See you there.

04:01.393 --> 04:04.143
(cheerful music)

WEBVTT

00:00.000 --> 00:03.030
-: Hey, everyone, so now we have a really good idea

00:03.030 --> 00:05.550
of how to work and communicate with engineers.

00:05.550 --> 00:07.350
Let's talk about designers.

00:07.350 --> 00:10.350
Designers, just like developers, often have certain ways

00:10.350 --> 00:12.510
of working that you should be aware of.

00:12.510 --> 00:14.730
Here are some tips on working with them.

00:14.730 --> 00:17.130
Number one, designers are very creative

00:17.130 --> 00:19.470
and you want them to take a lot of creative freedom

00:19.470 --> 00:20.550
in the work that they do.

00:20.550 --> 00:24.570
You want them to feel free to really stretch the limits

00:24.570 --> 00:25.620
of their imagination.

00:25.620 --> 00:27.540
Because of this, it's really important

00:27.540 --> 00:29.700
that you're clear about any limitations.

00:29.700 --> 00:31.650
Let's say, for instance, that we're designing

00:31.650 --> 00:34.530
a new profile page for users and the designer wants

00:34.530 --> 00:37.920
to put in some data that we don't have accessible

00:37.920 --> 00:39.270
at the moment from the database,

00:39.270 --> 00:40.890
and it would take a lot of work to get.

00:40.890 --> 00:43.590
It's important that we tell them about this early,

00:43.590 --> 00:47.850
because one slight change could change their entire design.

00:47.850 --> 00:48.780
Keep this in mind.

00:48.780 --> 00:51.060
Number two, like developers,

00:51.060 --> 00:53.790
you should not be treating designers as an agency

00:53.790 --> 00:56.250
or someone who just makes things pretty.

00:56.250 --> 00:59.880
This is a big mistake I see tons of product managers make.

00:59.880 --> 01:03.570
90% of the time, designers are going to know much more

01:03.570 --> 01:06.090
about user interactions than you.

01:06.090 --> 01:08.280
Not only that, but it's their entire job

01:08.280 --> 01:11.100
to make sure that what they are making fits well

01:11.100 --> 01:13.080
within the other areas of the product

01:13.080 --> 01:14.610
and the other designers

01:14.610 --> 01:16.830
and the stuff that they're doing as well.

01:16.830 --> 01:19.260
Always provide designers ample opportunity

01:19.260 --> 01:21.630
to give you feedback on the core of the idea

01:21.630 --> 01:23.640
or the product that you're working on,

01:23.640 --> 01:27.150
and I mean from the very product and idea perspective,

01:27.150 --> 01:29.250
not just the wire framing and design part.

01:29.250 --> 01:31.470
Number three, you and your designer

01:31.470 --> 01:33.240
should function as a team.

01:33.240 --> 01:35.220
Again, this means that you should be giving them

01:35.220 --> 01:37.140
the opportunity to tell you their opinion

01:37.140 --> 01:38.520
on the product in the future,

01:38.520 --> 01:41.460
but it also means that you should be giving them data,

01:41.460 --> 01:43.020
showing them user feedback,

01:43.020 --> 01:45.720
and not just saying that this is a thing we're developing

01:45.720 --> 01:48.150
or building because I said so.

01:48.150 --> 01:49.350
That's really bad.

01:49.350 --> 01:51.960
You need to basically be showing them

01:51.960 --> 01:54.450
the reasons that you came up with this

01:54.450 --> 01:56.130
being the thing that we're doing next.

01:56.130 --> 01:59.070
Number four, don't ever tell the designer what to do.

01:59.070 --> 02:01.890
Remember, as a PM, you're responsible for the what

02:01.890 --> 02:03.570
and the why, but not the how.

02:03.570 --> 02:05.220
If you dislike something in particular

02:05.220 --> 02:08.430
about the work, feel free to highlight it in broad terms,

02:08.430 --> 02:10.770
but don't tell them what needs to be changed.

02:10.770 --> 02:12.900
For instance, if you think that something is confusing

02:12.900 --> 02:15.960
about their design, remind them that it's important

02:15.960 --> 02:18.780
that the interface is as clear as possible.

02:18.780 --> 02:20.700
Maybe user research shows

02:20.700 --> 02:23.940
that the least technologically adept people

02:23.940 --> 02:26.340
are a big part of our user base, for instance.

02:26.340 --> 02:28.500
At the end of the day, you have the final say

02:28.500 --> 02:29.850
on the product you release,

02:29.850 --> 02:33.330
but you should never ever have to exercise that power.

02:33.330 --> 02:36.210
Okay, number five, and the last one here.

02:36.210 --> 02:38.100
This is one of the best tips I can offer

02:38.100 --> 02:39.540
for working with designers,

02:39.540 --> 02:43.890
and it's to always talk about user problems first

02:43.890 --> 02:45.810
and solutions second.

02:45.810 --> 02:47.490
In other words, don't say to them

02:47.490 --> 02:49.530
that a user should be able to send photos

02:49.530 --> 02:51.300
in a message to another user,

02:51.300 --> 02:53.940
because then you're just describing the solution.

02:53.940 --> 02:56.670
Instead, talk about the fact that the problem

02:56.670 --> 02:58.350
that users can't express themselves

02:58.350 --> 03:01.200
to one another with photos or media in a message

03:01.200 --> 03:02.730
is the real issue.

03:02.730 --> 03:05.010
You would be really surprised at how helpful

03:05.010 --> 03:07.590
this technique is in finding the best solutions

03:07.590 --> 03:09.240
to actual user problems

03:09.240 --> 03:12.300
and designers really appreciate this approach.

03:12.300 --> 03:14.100
Okay, so now that we've got a good idea

03:14.100 --> 03:15.750
of engineers and designers,

03:15.750 --> 03:17.760
let's talk in the next lecture

03:17.760 --> 03:21.185
about communicating with executives and other stakeholders.

03:21.185 --> 03:24.102
(energizing music)

WEBVTT

00:00.240 --> 00:01.073
-: Hey there.

00:01.073 --> 00:02.250
So we've learned already

00:02.250 --> 00:05.370
about how to communicate with engineers and designers,

00:05.370 --> 00:08.220
but how about the rest of the groups

00:08.220 --> 00:12.180
like let's say executives, sales, legal, and marketing?

00:12.180 --> 00:14.220
Well, let's talk about executives first

00:14.220 --> 00:15.720
and then we'll do a few tips

00:15.720 --> 00:18.420
on communicating with the other types of stakeholders.

00:18.420 --> 00:20.340
In my early days of being a product manager,

00:20.340 --> 00:23.220
I always struggled communicating with executives.

00:23.220 --> 00:24.330
It was really tough.

00:24.330 --> 00:27.540
I would send them really big emails with tons of detail

00:27.540 --> 00:30.030
and they would almost never get back to me.

00:30.030 --> 00:31.890
Later, I found out that the secret

00:31.890 --> 00:34.756
to communicating with executives is being brief.

00:34.756 --> 00:37.920
Executives are the busiest people at the company

00:37.920 --> 00:39.690
so sending them compact

00:39.690 --> 00:43.528
but meaningful messages shows that you value their time.

00:43.528 --> 00:46.050
So instead of writing two paragraphs,

00:46.050 --> 00:47.610
write two bullet points.

00:47.610 --> 00:49.050
But Cole, you say,

00:49.050 --> 00:51.000
I won't be able to give them enough information

00:51.000 --> 00:52.069
in two bullet points.

00:52.069 --> 00:53.220
That's right,

00:53.220 --> 00:55.620
and they don't need all the information in one email.

00:55.620 --> 00:58.020
If they want more data or information from you,

00:58.020 --> 00:59.910
they know who to ask, you.

00:59.910 --> 01:03.150
Secondly, when talking to executives and other roles

01:03.150 --> 01:06.660
like sales, always speak in terms of business effect.

01:06.660 --> 01:08.580
By this, I mean, don't tell them

01:08.580 --> 01:10.740
that the user will be able to do a certain thing

01:10.740 --> 01:12.390
in your product more frequently,

01:12.390 --> 01:14.730
but tell them about what it means.

01:14.730 --> 01:17.160
Does it mean more or less revenue?

01:17.160 --> 01:20.880
Does it mean a change in a specific company metric?

01:20.880 --> 01:22.500
If so, tell them that.

01:22.500 --> 01:25.957
Thirdly, communicate in a style that they like best.

01:25.957 --> 01:29.040
Some executives don't like email

01:29.040 --> 01:31.290
as it consumes so much time for them

01:31.290 --> 01:34.110
and maybe even their assistant reads it for them.

01:34.110 --> 01:35.700
In this case, a better approach

01:35.700 --> 01:38.850
might be to give them a really quick update in the hallway

01:38.850 --> 01:40.680
or at lunch or at your next meeting.

01:40.680 --> 01:44.490
Okay, so here's another couple tips for not only executives

01:44.490 --> 01:46.800
but all sorts of stakeholders in general.

01:46.800 --> 01:50.790
Number one, it pays off to talk to people on each team

01:50.790 --> 01:52.080
as much as possible.

01:52.080 --> 01:54.334
Whenever you get a chance, hold a meeting,

01:54.334 --> 01:58.710
take them to lunch, or just finding excuse to talk to people

01:58.710 --> 02:01.710
from different teams like marketing or sales.

02:01.710 --> 02:05.010
Number two, ensure that other teams and people

02:05.010 --> 02:09.090
understand that you know the user base, the technology

02:09.090 --> 02:11.100
and the business very well.

02:11.100 --> 02:13.350
This makes them much more confident

02:13.350 --> 02:16.200
about the decisions you are making in the product.

02:16.200 --> 02:19.110
Number three, update them on the latest developments

02:19.110 --> 02:20.418
as frequently as possible,

02:20.418 --> 02:23.957
but make it clear whether or not they should be replying

02:23.957 --> 02:26.610
or if you just meant to send them an update.

02:26.610 --> 02:28.020
One really good way to do this

02:28.020 --> 02:31.050
is if you have an internal company blog or wiki,

02:31.050 --> 02:32.400
you can just post things there

02:32.400 --> 02:34.170
and they can read it if they want to.

02:34.170 --> 02:35.250
But the important thing here

02:35.250 --> 02:37.410
is to be communicating frequently.

02:37.410 --> 02:40.196
Number four, whenever you're updating outside stakeholders

02:40.196 --> 02:43.020
on progress or the completion of a feature,

02:43.020 --> 02:44.070
be sure to tell 'em

02:44.070 --> 02:47.257
about the reason the solution is the way it is.

02:47.257 --> 02:50.668
Tell them a story about the other options you considered

02:50.668 --> 02:54.810
and why maybe those ones did not turn out to be ideal.

02:54.810 --> 02:58.440
So tell them that story about how you got to the product

02:58.440 --> 03:01.770
or solution that ended up being the one that you built.

03:01.770 --> 03:02.603
All right, everyone.

03:02.603 --> 03:04.770
With those tips, you are really well armed

03:04.770 --> 03:05.880
to communicate effectively

03:05.880 --> 03:07.950
with a broad range of stakeholders.

03:07.950 --> 03:10.080
This puts you way ahead in the game,

03:10.080 --> 03:12.060
so be sure to keep this section in mind

03:12.060 --> 03:14.580
and frequently come back if you need to.

03:14.580 --> 03:15.930
It's gonna take a little bit of time

03:15.930 --> 03:17.850
to get this like really ingrained in your head,

03:17.850 --> 03:19.200
but you'll learn over time.

03:19.200 --> 03:21.550
I will see you all in the next lecture.

03:21.550 --> 03:23.967
(soft music)

WEBVTT

00:00.580 --> 00:03.163
(bright music)

00:04.350 --> 00:05.850
-: Hey everyone. What is up?

00:05.850 --> 00:07.590
This is one of the most

00:07.590 --> 00:11.160
universally useful sections of the course.

00:11.160 --> 00:15.510
We're gonna learn about the common technologies and terms

00:15.510 --> 00:18.390
used in most software companies today

00:18.390 --> 00:20.910
but that you're gonna hear a lot in your day-to-day life

00:20.910 --> 00:22.080
as a product manager.

00:22.080 --> 00:23.700
A large number of product managers

00:23.700 --> 00:25.560
start out as engineers first

00:25.560 --> 00:27.570
or have some sort of technical background

00:27.570 --> 00:28.800
like computer science.

00:28.800 --> 00:31.620
However, you don't absolutely need to be

00:31.620 --> 00:33.990
an X engineer to become a product manager.

00:33.990 --> 00:36.000
You can come from all sorts of backgrounds.

00:36.000 --> 00:38.220
You don't even need to know how to code,

00:38.220 --> 00:40.380
but you do need to understand

00:40.380 --> 00:42.870
how the most common technologies work.

00:42.870 --> 00:45.330
In other words, you don't need to be an engineer

00:45.330 --> 00:47.910
but you need to know how to speak like one.

00:47.910 --> 00:50.550
All right. So why should you learn about technology?

00:50.550 --> 00:53.760
Well, having a foundational understanding of technology

00:53.760 --> 00:55.830
behind the products that you're building,

00:55.830 --> 00:58.620
helps you out in three really big ways.

00:58.620 --> 01:00.720
Number one, it gives you an understanding

01:00.720 --> 01:03.390
of what can be realistically built.

01:03.390 --> 01:06.150
You won't have to ask engineers what is possible

01:06.150 --> 01:10.230
every time you talk about solutions or features for users

01:10.230 --> 01:13.830
or whatever other departments ask you technical questions,

01:13.830 --> 01:16.110
you don't have to go bother engineers and say,

01:16.110 --> 01:18.360
oh, yeah, I'll check with the engineers on that one.

01:18.360 --> 01:20.820
Number two, it helps you build a great relationship

01:20.820 --> 01:21.960
with engineers.

01:21.960 --> 01:23.430
If you're leading a product team

01:23.430 --> 01:25.890
with a group of smart engineers, they want to know

01:25.890 --> 01:28.380
that the person making the product decisions

01:28.380 --> 01:31.080
has both a respect for the work they do

01:31.080 --> 01:33.000
and knowledge of how it works.

01:33.000 --> 01:34.890
Being technically incompetent

01:34.890 --> 01:39.510
and unwilling to learn is a really easy and fast way

01:39.510 --> 01:42.750
to losing respect from your engineers and your team.

01:42.750 --> 01:44.490
All right. So, number three.

01:44.490 --> 01:47.790
Knowing about technology, helps you understand the impact

01:47.790 --> 01:49.710
of the decisions that you're making.

01:49.710 --> 01:51.840
Every decision you make about the product

01:51.840 --> 01:54.450
has a long lasting technical implication.

01:54.450 --> 01:58.050
If you build a product that needs a new database

01:58.050 --> 02:00.570
or a feature that needs a new database,

02:00.570 --> 02:02.910
then that means that some engineer

02:02.910 --> 02:05.280
has to maintain that database

02:05.280 --> 02:06.960
and they have to build it in the first place.

02:06.960 --> 02:09.660
If you later decide as a product team

02:09.660 --> 02:12.900
with everyone else to get rid of that feature,

02:12.900 --> 02:15.720
then guess who's gonna have to get rid of that database

02:15.720 --> 02:17.790
and figure out all the stuff around

02:17.790 --> 02:20.190
what happens when you remove it from the system?

02:20.190 --> 02:21.690
Does it affect anything else?

02:21.690 --> 02:23.700
Obviously, the engineers have to do that.

02:23.700 --> 02:27.000
So, this creates a lot of work for the engineers.

02:27.000 --> 02:29.940
That's why it's important to ahead of time

02:29.940 --> 02:31.410
understand technology

02:31.410 --> 02:33.180
so that when you're thinking about features

02:33.180 --> 02:34.380
and product decisions,

02:34.380 --> 02:36.480
you know in the back of your head,

02:36.480 --> 02:38.220
okay, what type of work

02:38.220 --> 02:40.980
is this going to make the engineers do?

02:40.980 --> 02:42.540
And then what is the risk here

02:42.540 --> 02:44.130
if we remove this in the future?

02:44.130 --> 02:46.440
Anyways, I'm really excited to talk about this section.

02:46.440 --> 02:49.590
This is one of my most favorite sections of the course

02:49.590 --> 02:52.680
and this being technically adapt

02:52.680 --> 02:55.080
and familiar with technology has been,

02:55.080 --> 02:58.500
I would say, by far one of the biggest benefits

02:58.500 --> 03:01.140
to my career as a product manager.

03:01.140 --> 03:02.400
All right. Let's get started.

03:02.400 --> 03:04.083
I'll see all in the next lecture.

response.title: "The Cloud", Servers, Clients, and the Inner Workings of the Internet

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.210 --> 00:01.590
-: What's up everybody?

00:01.590 --> 00:05.820
We're gonna start by talking about the very basics

00:05.820 --> 00:07.950
and this is the internet.

00:07.950 --> 00:10.770
I know that seems silly, but you gotta understand

00:10.770 --> 00:13.380
how the internet works on a technical level to

00:13.380 --> 00:15.180
understand what we're gonna talk about

00:15.180 --> 00:16.890
in the future lectures.

00:16.890 --> 00:19.110
We're also gonna talk about the cloud

00:19.110 --> 00:20.250
and what does that mean

00:20.250 --> 00:23.640
and then also we're gonna talk about what a tech stack is.

00:23.640 --> 00:26.193
All right, so what is cloud computing?

00:27.496 --> 00:29.790
(sighs) Buzz words?

00:29.790 --> 00:31.920
Look, there's this really funny video

00:31.920 --> 00:34.658
of Larry Ellison online.

00:34.658 --> 00:36.180
Take a look.

00:36.180 --> 00:38.250
-: I mean, the guys say, "Oh, it's in the cloud."

00:38.250 --> 00:39.480
Well, what is that?

00:39.480 --> 00:41.100
Then you say, "Are we dead?"

00:41.100 --> 00:41.933
But yeah, we're dead.

00:41.933 --> 00:42.900
If there's no hardware

00:42.900 --> 00:45.630
or software in the cloud, we are so screwed.

00:45.630 --> 00:47.400
But it's not water vapor.

00:47.400 --> 00:49.980
All it is is a computer attached to a network.

00:49.980 --> 00:51.810
What are you talking about?

00:51.810 --> 00:53.040
I mean, what do you think?

00:53.040 --> 00:54.733
What do you think Google runs on?

00:54.733 --> 00:55.566
-: (man offscreen) I'll ask you.

00:55.566 --> 00:57.360
-: They run on water vapor.

00:57.360 --> 01:01.611
I mean cloud, they have, it's databases.

01:01.611 --> 01:02.444
-: (man off screen) How about the next question?

01:02.444 --> 01:05.400
-: and operating systems and memory

01:05.400 --> 01:09.000
and microprocessors and the internet.

01:09.000 --> 01:10.710
-: So the context around that is

01:10.710 --> 01:13.320
that Larry Ellison is a billionaire.

01:13.320 --> 01:16.110
He started Oracle, which is a database company

01:16.110 --> 01:19.020
and he started it back in the day when databases

01:19.020 --> 01:21.600
and servers were physically on the premises

01:21.600 --> 01:23.700
of companies that'd actually buy one

01:23.700 --> 01:25.590
and put it in your office

01:25.590 --> 01:27.660
in order to have like a website and that sort of thing.

01:27.660 --> 01:29.707
The question was about,

01:29.707 --> 01:31.890
"Oh everything's moving to the cloud, Larry,

01:31.890 --> 01:33.870
do you think your business is in danger?"

01:33.870 --> 01:35.370
And that's where you saw the rant.

01:35.370 --> 01:38.340
He's just like, "What a ridiculous question.

01:38.340 --> 01:40.827
the cloud is just the internet."

01:41.670 --> 01:43.057
Basically, he's just saying like,

01:43.057 --> 01:45.960
"Look, you just put servers on the internet,

01:45.960 --> 01:47.640
you make them accessible through the internet,

01:47.640 --> 01:48.900
and there's no problem."

01:48.900 --> 01:50.550
So anyways, that's pretty funny.

01:50.550 --> 01:52.440
But what you gotta know here is that the cloud

01:52.440 --> 01:56.220
is a buzzword, is was created by some marketing pros.

01:56.220 --> 01:57.120
It's just the internet.

01:57.120 --> 02:00.660
All right, so speaking of the internet, how does it work?

02:00.660 --> 02:02.610
Well, okay, first of all, how the internet

02:02.610 --> 02:05.130
works is a really big topic that includes a lot

02:05.130 --> 02:08.400
of weird things like packets and internet service providers

02:08.400 --> 02:10.440
and it would take me a long time to explain that to you.

02:10.440 --> 02:12.420
So we're just gonna talk about the basics here.

02:12.420 --> 02:15.360
So the internet consists of a big network.

02:15.360 --> 02:16.230
You can just think of it

02:16.230 --> 02:19.470
as one really long cord that connects everything.

02:19.470 --> 02:21.720
On this network, there's two primary types

02:21.720 --> 02:24.630
of computers; servers, and clients.

02:24.630 --> 02:27.240
A server is something that stores data

02:27.240 --> 02:30.030
in a database and sends it to the clients.

02:30.030 --> 02:31.620
A client is just a fancy term

02:31.620 --> 02:36.030
for something that people use to access these servers

02:36.030 --> 02:38.100
and that data across the internet.

02:38.100 --> 02:40.230
This would be things like your laptop.

02:40.230 --> 02:41.520
You're probably watching this

02:41.520 --> 02:43.570
on a laptop right now, or even a phone.

02:43.570 --> 02:45.750
Maybe you're watching this on a phone.

02:45.750 --> 02:49.140
And if you're not watching this on a laptop or phone...

02:49.140 --> 02:52.830
A client can also be other internet connected devices

02:52.830 --> 02:56.280
like printers or Apple TV or geez,

02:56.280 --> 02:58.470
everything these days is like internet enabled,

02:58.470 --> 02:59.670
internet of things like,

02:59.670 --> 03:02.171
refrigerators connected to the internet.

03:02.171 --> 03:03.600
Well, your refrigerator's not a server.

03:03.600 --> 03:05.490
If you have an internet connected fridge

03:05.490 --> 03:08.880
it's a client cause it's accessing data from somewhere else.

03:08.880 --> 03:12.090
So websites and services all have servers

03:12.090 --> 03:15.330
behind them that you've gotta access to use them.

03:15.330 --> 03:16.410
That makes sense right?

03:16.410 --> 03:18.900
Now, each server has an address,

03:18.900 --> 03:22.320
like the address of your apartment or house.

03:22.320 --> 03:24.690
It's a long and complex number.

03:24.690 --> 03:26.460
It's good for machines to read

03:26.460 --> 03:29.220
but hard for humans to remember.

03:29.220 --> 03:32.130
This number is called an IP address

03:32.130 --> 03:35.460
and if you've ever seen one, it's like 1 9 2 dot

03:35.460 --> 03:39.000
1 6 8 dot something, something, something, something.

03:39.000 --> 03:40.800
You're not gonna remember that if you want to

03:40.800 --> 03:42.210
access like a website.

03:42.210 --> 03:43.590
All right, so to solve this

03:43.590 --> 03:47.970
there's something called the domain named system or DNS

03:47.970 --> 03:50.850
and that basically just translates simple names

03:50.850 --> 03:53.970
into IP addresses and back again.

03:53.970 --> 03:57.060
Human language to computer language and back.

03:57.060 --> 03:59.220
This allows people to access services

03:59.220 --> 04:03.240
like Google by typing in google.com instead

04:03.240 --> 04:06.270
of whatever IP address Google has for their servers

04:06.270 --> 04:08.430
which is probably, I don't know, pretty long.

04:08.430 --> 04:10.230
All right, so when I go on my computer

04:10.230 --> 04:12.600
and I go to google.com or any other website

04:12.600 --> 04:16.350
my computer sends a request through my internet at home

04:16.350 --> 04:19.181
then through my internet service provider

04:19.181 --> 04:21.560
and they are all crap, right?

04:21.560 --> 04:22.581
Am I right?

04:22.581 --> 04:25.830
Am I right fricking, ugh, terrible, terrible companies.

04:25.830 --> 04:28.950
Anyways, then it goes through the DNS

04:28.950 --> 04:33.240
which translates my request for google.com

04:33.240 --> 04:37.350
into the proper IP address for Google's servers.

04:37.350 --> 04:40.680
Then Google sends the information I requested back

04:40.680 --> 04:43.620
through that same path back from Google servers

04:43.620 --> 04:45.570
through the Internets, and

04:45.570 --> 04:49.323
through my crappy internet service provider,

04:49.323 --> 04:53.790
back to my laptop or my internet of things connected fridge.

04:53.790 --> 04:55.260
Sounds pretty simple, right?

04:55.260 --> 04:56.093
Let's recap.

04:56.093 --> 04:57.570
The cloud is a buzzword

04:57.570 --> 05:00.120
it's a term for the internet and servers.

05:00.120 --> 05:02.970
The internet itself is just a bunch

05:02.970 --> 05:07.550
of connected computers and devices with some of them acting

05:07.550 --> 05:11.160
as servers and the other ones acting as clients.

05:11.160 --> 05:15.180
And humans use the clients to access the servers.

05:15.180 --> 05:17.550
In between, there are some stuff in there

05:17.550 --> 05:20.700
like the DNS that translates human speak

05:20.700 --> 05:23.430
like google.com into computer speak,

05:23.430 --> 05:25.740
the IP address and then back again

05:25.740 --> 05:27.360
when it when the request comes back.

05:27.360 --> 05:28.193
All right?

05:28.193 --> 05:29.733
I will see all in the next lecture.

response.title: Understanding the Front End, Back End, and Tech Stacks

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.150 --> 00:01.228
-: What's up?

00:01.228 --> 00:04.650
You've probably heard of the terms

00:04.650 --> 00:08.220
front end, back end, and tech stack before,

00:08.220 --> 00:11.340
so let's demystify these real quick.

00:11.340 --> 00:12.173
It's not hard.

00:12.173 --> 00:13.290
It won't take us very long.

00:13.290 --> 00:16.620
So we already talked about the internet and how it works.

00:16.620 --> 00:19.110
We know that when I type in google.com,

00:19.110 --> 00:24.110
my laptop or phone or smart internet fridge,

00:24.339 --> 00:27.870
or whatever else's internet of things these days, yeah

00:27.870 --> 00:30.690
it connects to the Google servers through the internet.

00:30.690 --> 00:33.930
If I use a web browser like Google Chrome

00:33.930 --> 00:36.120
and I go to google.com and do a search,

00:36.120 --> 00:38.940
the web browser on my laptop asks Google

00:38.940 --> 00:40.251
for the data I requested

00:40.251 --> 00:43.890
and shows me the results that Google sends back.

00:43.890 --> 00:46.920
Okay, in all of this, there's a couple things

00:46.920 --> 00:49.710
going on here from the technology perspective

00:49.710 --> 00:52.140
and I don't mean the internet, I mean the code.

00:52.140 --> 00:54.870
Google is figuring out what I'm requesting from them.

00:54.870 --> 00:56.940
Whatever I typed into the search bar,

00:56.940 --> 00:59.070
they're getting that information from their database

00:59.070 --> 01:02.341
and then when they send it back to my web browser

01:02.341 --> 01:04.350
they're showing me the results.

01:04.350 --> 01:07.860
Each of these levels has programming behind it

01:07.860 --> 01:10.740
that is often referred to as front end,

01:10.740 --> 01:13.290
back end, and application layer.

01:13.290 --> 01:16.710
So look, front end is just a fancy term for

01:16.710 --> 01:19.890
what the users see and interact with.

01:19.890 --> 01:23.850
This is colors, text, and interactions

01:23.850 --> 01:25.380
I see on the web page.

01:25.380 --> 01:27.750
So there are programming languages that make up

01:27.750 --> 01:29.760
the front end as most commonly used

01:29.760 --> 01:32.430
for the front end, especially on the internet.

01:32.430 --> 01:36.210
Things like HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

01:36.210 --> 01:37.620
We're not gonna go into those too much

01:37.620 --> 01:39.780
but I'll give you a super brief primer.

01:39.780 --> 01:41.310
HTML, you can think of like,

01:41.310 --> 01:45.120
the structure and content of a website.

01:45.120 --> 01:47.760
So it's the actual text.

01:47.760 --> 01:50.190
CSS is a language that is used for

01:50.190 --> 01:52.860
styling that text and that structure.

01:52.860 --> 01:55.770
So, okay, this font should be this color and this big,

01:55.770 --> 01:58.260
and this is how pictures should be stretched

01:58.260 --> 01:59.730
across the page.

01:59.730 --> 02:01.080
And then you've got JavaScript,

02:01.080 --> 02:03.240
and that one is basically

02:03.240 --> 02:06.870
in charge of the interactivity on the website.

02:06.870 --> 02:08.880
So, okay, when I click this button,

02:08.880 --> 02:12.893
it does this little animation, or when a user types this,

02:12.893 --> 02:16.860
this happens over here before it goes to the servers.

02:16.860 --> 02:18.330
That's what JavaScript is doing.

02:18.330 --> 02:19.920
Anyways, those are the three that really

02:19.920 --> 02:22.740
make up most web front ends.

02:22.740 --> 02:23.573
All right?

02:23.573 --> 02:26.730
So back end is a fancy term for information servers

02:26.730 --> 02:29.714
and databases that are located somewhere else

02:29.714 --> 02:31.080
in the back end.

02:31.080 --> 02:33.480
So the technology that is used in the backend

02:33.480 --> 02:35.310
is things like MySQL,

02:35.310 --> 02:37.733
which is a database language combined with

02:37.733 --> 02:41.790
technology that hosts that database on the internet

02:41.790 --> 02:43.680
like Amazon S3.

02:43.680 --> 02:48.680
Amazon S3 is basically servers for rent,

02:48.750 --> 02:50.490
basically that Amazon hosts,

02:50.490 --> 02:53.040
and anyone can really just go in and sort of

02:53.040 --> 02:55.890
rent a server from Amazon on the internet.

02:55.890 --> 02:58.830
And this has, by the way, contributed to how

02:58.830 --> 03:01.500
quickly internet businesses have been able to grow

03:01.500 --> 03:04.200
or test things without, you know, spending too much money

03:04.200 --> 03:06.120
because in the old days you used to have to

03:06.120 --> 03:08.310
buy that server yourself, install it,

03:08.310 --> 03:10.740
and put code on it and all this sort of thing.

03:10.740 --> 03:14.721
Now you just go to like Amazon AWS, Amazon Web Services,

03:14.721 --> 03:17.160
and you just type in, okay,

03:17.160 --> 03:20.340
I want an S3 server on the internet.

03:20.340 --> 03:22.980
I can put my code on it and I can run a website

03:22.980 --> 03:26.070
without actually ever owning a server myself.

03:26.070 --> 03:28.140
Anyways, you might have heard that first term

03:28.140 --> 03:30.870
I mentioned SQL, I mentioned mySQL,

03:30.870 --> 03:34.590
but it's spelled M Y S Q L

03:34.590 --> 03:39.590
and the term SQL is also talked about a lot

03:39.810 --> 03:41.550
as a query language.

03:41.550 --> 03:45.060
That's because SQL itself is a query language.

03:45.060 --> 03:47.154
So if you hear SQL or MySQL

03:47.154 --> 03:50.250
they could be talking about a query language.

03:50.250 --> 03:54.180
This is just a way that we can structure a request

03:54.180 --> 03:58.590
to a MySQL database to get information back.

03:58.590 --> 04:01.980
By that I mean, quite literally, a lot of people

04:01.980 --> 04:05.010
at tech companies know how to query SQL.

04:05.010 --> 04:05.843
They will say,

04:05.843 --> 04:09.480
"Hmm, I wonder how many users we have as of today."

04:09.480 --> 04:12.450
And they'll just enter in a SQL query

04:12.450 --> 04:15.383
and something like select all from

04:15.383 --> 04:19.620
the user database, count number of users,

04:19.620 --> 04:21.997
and that will just spit out a number and it says,

04:21.997 --> 04:23.880
"Well here's all the users in the database."

04:23.880 --> 04:25.950
It's relatively simple to learn

04:25.950 --> 04:27.960
so I do recommend you check it out.

04:27.960 --> 04:30.150
Okay, so what if there's five different screens

04:30.150 --> 04:34.410
on an app that all pulled data from one database?

04:34.410 --> 04:37.980
How does the front end know where to get that data?

04:37.980 --> 04:39.540
Or what if it needs to do some sort of

04:39.540 --> 04:41.610
logic or calculation on it?

04:41.610 --> 04:44.820
This is where the application layer comes in.

04:44.820 --> 04:48.480
So the application layer is just a term for

04:48.480 --> 04:52.140
some code or programming that does calculations

04:52.140 --> 04:56.310
to the data from the front end or the back end

04:56.310 --> 04:58.350
and it sends it to the right place.

04:58.350 --> 05:00.300
You can think of it as being

05:00.300 --> 05:02.850
in between the front end and the back end,

05:02.850 --> 05:04.170
but in reality you just have

05:04.170 --> 05:05.670
the front end and the back end

05:05.670 --> 05:07.463
but the application layer is the code

05:07.463 --> 05:11.100
and it usually lives on the front end or the back end

05:11.100 --> 05:14.190
but it's not always consistent, you know, where it lives.

05:14.190 --> 05:15.943
All of this stuff really just has to do with

05:15.943 --> 05:18.780
how the product or the app is programmed, right?

05:18.780 --> 05:20.340
Let's go a little bit deeper into that.

05:20.340 --> 05:22.950
If I'm using a website like Amazon and I do a search

05:22.950 --> 05:26.490
for products from highest price to lowest price,

05:26.490 --> 05:29.430
the application layer takes my search,

05:29.430 --> 05:30.750
it talks to the database,

05:30.750 --> 05:33.810
it gets the items I'm interested in from the database,

05:33.810 --> 05:36.690
and then sorts them in the correct order

05:36.690 --> 05:40.980
for the front end to display to me in my web browser.

05:40.980 --> 05:44.550
The web browser is interpreting the front end code

05:44.550 --> 05:47.220
that gets generated and showing it to me.

05:47.220 --> 05:49.920
So there's a lot of programming languages out there

05:49.920 --> 05:51.240
and there's a lot of 'em that are used for

05:51.240 --> 05:53.070
this application layer.

05:53.070 --> 05:58.070
Some examples though are things like Ruby, Python, or PHP.

05:58.620 --> 06:00.540
