You've probably heard of Ruby by the way

06:00.540 --> 06:04.740
because you might have heard the the phrase Ruby Unrails.

06:04.740 --> 06:06.390
Well, that's not the same as Ruby,

06:06.390 --> 06:09.630
but Rails is a framework for Ruby.

06:09.630 --> 06:11.550
Anyways, I'm not gonna talk about frameworks right now

06:11.550 --> 06:13.020
but we will talk about 'em.

06:13.020 --> 06:14.160
I just wanted you to know

06:14.160 --> 06:15.990
that if you've heard it, that's why.

06:15.990 --> 06:18.330
Okay, so we've got a good overview of a front end,

06:18.330 --> 06:21.990
the back end, and the middle part, or the the calculations,

06:21.990 --> 06:23.760
which is called the application layer.

06:23.760 --> 06:25.800
So what is a tech stack?

06:25.800 --> 06:28.783
All right, a tech stack is just a way of saying

06:28.783 --> 06:31.320
the programming languages or technology

06:31.320 --> 06:33.930
that make up a website or app

06:33.930 --> 06:36.540
from the front end all the way to the back end.

06:36.540 --> 06:39.390
Some products use different databases

06:39.390 --> 06:42.300
or languages to program the product

06:42.300 --> 06:45.240
and all the stuff that happens there than others

06:45.240 --> 06:48.960
because some languages have advantages over others

06:48.960 --> 06:52.410
for certain types of work or services.

06:52.410 --> 06:56.010
If you applied this whole tech stack concept to cars

06:56.010 --> 06:58.440
it would basically be the same as describing

06:58.440 --> 07:01.623
which type of engine, tires, transmission,

07:01.623 --> 07:05.490
and what kind of metal your car is built out of.

07:05.490 --> 07:06.990
It's basically the same concept.

07:06.990 --> 07:08.160
All right, easy stuff.

07:08.160 --> 07:11.325
But one thing I want to mention lastly here is that

07:11.325 --> 07:13.903
all of the stuff I've been talking about

07:13.903 --> 07:18.210
is the same with mobile devices and mobile apps

07:18.210 --> 07:21.510
except that sometimes instead of a web browser

07:21.510 --> 07:23.631
on a mobile phone, like an iPhone or an Android,

07:23.631 --> 07:26.820
you're using a native mobile app.

07:26.820 --> 07:30.180
And instead of those native mobile apps using HTML

07:30.180 --> 07:33.480
and CSS and JavaScript in a web browser,

07:33.480 --> 07:38.480
the app likely uses a language like Swift, or-

07:38.910 --> 07:41.940
yeah, Swift is for iPhones, or Java for Android.

07:41.940 --> 07:44.160
So those in the case of mobile,

07:44.160 --> 07:47.310
serve as the front ends for mobile devices.

07:47.310 --> 07:48.570
All right, quick recap here.

07:48.570 --> 07:51.150
The front end is what users see and interact with,

07:51.150 --> 07:53.803
with buttons, text, and colors.

07:53.803 --> 07:56.250
The back end holds all the information

07:56.250 --> 07:58.800
in a database on the Internets,

07:58.800 --> 08:01.260
and somewhere in between there's some code

08:01.260 --> 08:02.940
that basically does some calculations

08:02.940 --> 08:06.150
and sends things back and forth to the right places.

08:06.150 --> 08:09.480
And this is called the application layer.

08:09.480 --> 08:10.313
All right.

08:10.313 --> 08:14.610
The next logical topic to talk about is APIs

08:14.610 --> 08:16.200
and you're gonna be using a lot of that

08:16.200 --> 08:17.130
in product management.

08:17.130 --> 08:18.330
You'll hear it all the time.

08:18.330 --> 08:19.590
So let's get to it.

08:19.590 --> 08:21.233
I'll see you in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.160 --> 00:01.440
-: Hello my friends.

00:01.440 --> 00:05.340
So welcome back and we're gonna talk here about APIs.

00:05.340 --> 00:07.200
Look, in your work as a product manager

00:07.200 --> 00:10.170
and in many other roles in the technology industry,

00:10.170 --> 00:13.950
you're gonna hear this term API a lot.

00:13.950 --> 00:17.040
Usually you're gonna hear some engineers talking about it

00:17.040 --> 00:18.277
and sometimes they'll be like,

00:18.277 --> 00:19.290
"Oh, well, we can't do that

00:19.290 --> 00:21.930
because the API doesn't allow us."

00:21.930 --> 00:23.760
So let's demystify that.

00:23.760 --> 00:25.020
What is an API?

00:25.020 --> 00:29.580
API stands for Application Programming Interface.

00:29.580 --> 00:31.920
The easiest way to explain it is

00:31.920 --> 00:35.340
that it's the messenger that takes requests

00:35.340 --> 00:38.310
and tells the system what you want to do.

00:38.310 --> 00:40.590
Then it returns what you requested,

00:40.590 --> 00:42.870
the information, back to you.

00:42.870 --> 00:44.550
Well, let's make it even more simple.

00:44.550 --> 00:46.140
Imagine you're at a restaurant

00:46.140 --> 00:47.580
and you're looking at the menu,

00:47.580 --> 00:50.250
and the kitchen has some delicious food,

00:50.250 --> 00:53.070
probably some barbecue, if it were me,

00:53.070 --> 00:55.350
and I'm like, I want that barbecue,

00:55.350 --> 00:58.410
but I need a way to get that food from the kitchen

00:58.410 --> 01:00.600
because they've gotta make it for me,

01:00.600 --> 01:02.520
and then they've gotta send me the food,

01:02.520 --> 01:03.480
so I gotta request it.

01:03.480 --> 01:05.010
They gotta make it. They gotta send it.

01:05.010 --> 01:06.540
And what I need in this case,

01:06.540 --> 01:08.700
like the missing link here, is a waiter.

01:08.700 --> 01:09.997
The waiter comes to me and says,

01:09.997 --> 01:14.220
"Hello, how many pounds of barbecue would you like, Cole?"

01:14.220 --> 01:17.070
And I'm like, "10 or 15 pounds."

01:17.070 --> 01:18.420
And then he goes back to the kitchen

01:18.420 --> 01:20.970
and he gets me 15 pounds of barbecue,

01:20.970 --> 01:23.340
brings it back to my fat self.

01:23.340 --> 01:26.940
So he's done all of this translating to kitchen language

01:26.940 --> 01:28.770
and bringing me the food.

01:28.770 --> 01:31.683
And that waiter is like an API.

01:31.683 --> 01:33.240
(bell dings)
Also, in this case,

01:33.240 --> 01:36.300
the kitchen was a database,

01:36.300 --> 01:40.380
a database full of delicious barbecue, in our example.

01:40.380 --> 01:43.410
Okay, so there's two types of APIs.

01:43.410 --> 01:45.540
I know, it seems like it's getting more complex,

01:45.540 --> 01:47.400
but I promise it's not that hard.

01:47.400 --> 01:50.250
There's a public API and a private API.

01:50.250 --> 01:52.560
Public APIs are provided by companies

01:52.560 --> 01:55.140
to allow products or services

01:55.140 --> 01:57.390
to get data from their system.

01:57.390 --> 01:59.550
A great example of this would be

01:59.550 --> 02:03.570
on a travel search website like kayak.com

02:03.570 --> 02:05.040
where you can search for a flight

02:05.040 --> 02:08.010
across different airlines for the cheapest price.

02:08.010 --> 02:09.720
Kayak takes what you type in

02:09.720 --> 02:11.520
about what dates you wanna travel,

02:11.520 --> 02:15.660
and then using the public API of every single airline,

02:15.660 --> 02:17.820
or at least most of 'em, it returns to you

02:17.820 --> 02:21.180
the prices and availability for all of the trips.

02:21.180 --> 02:24.030
That's because Kayak does not have the information

02:24.030 --> 02:27.720
on all airlines at all times and all flights

02:27.720 --> 02:31.920
but they contact the airlines' databases through the API.

02:31.920 --> 02:35.460
They did this because the APIs for the airlines are public,

02:35.460 --> 02:37.410
which means that they let anyone,

02:37.410 --> 02:40.140
like a developer like Kayak and the engineers there

02:40.140 --> 02:42.990
just dial in and get that data

02:42.990 --> 02:45.150
and provide some services based on it.

02:45.150 --> 02:47.970
Why? Because now they're making more money.

02:47.970 --> 02:50.310
Because if Kayak does a good job of marketing,

02:50.310 --> 02:51.210
people are gonna come there

02:51.210 --> 02:52.680
and they're gonna search for flights

02:52.680 --> 02:55.620
and they're gonna buy more stuff from the airlines.

02:55.620 --> 02:58.620
So a lot of tech companies out there have public APIs

02:58.620 --> 03:01.410
to encourage developers to build products

03:01.410 --> 03:02.910
using their information.

03:02.910 --> 03:06.750
Here, you can see a documentation page

03:06.750 --> 03:09.420
for Twitter's public API.

03:09.420 --> 03:11.460
All right, so what's a private API?

03:11.460 --> 03:13.980
Well, these are APIs that are available only

03:13.980 --> 03:16.380
to the developers inside of a company

03:16.380 --> 03:19.890
to get information from different internal systems.

03:19.890 --> 03:21.090
It's example time again.

03:21.090 --> 03:24.390
At Facebook, there's probably a database

03:24.390 --> 03:26.730
that holds the pictures,

03:26.730 --> 03:30.120
and another database that holds the messages,

03:30.120 --> 03:31.770
and there's probably a lot of databases

03:31.770 --> 03:34.080
for a lot of different things at Facebook.

03:34.080 --> 03:35.970
So if a developer on one team,

03:35.970 --> 03:38.190
let's say this developer doesn't work

03:38.190 --> 03:40.800
on the photos team or the pictures team,

03:40.800 --> 03:42.990
but they want to code a new feature

03:42.990 --> 03:45.510
and they need access to the pictures database

03:45.510 --> 03:47.970
somewhere inside of Facebook servers,

03:47.970 --> 03:51.330
then they will write what is called an API call

03:51.330 --> 03:55.080
in their code, and they will write this API call

03:55.080 --> 03:58.110
to what is called an API endpoint.

03:58.110 --> 04:01.350
The database then sends back the pictures

04:01.350 --> 04:02.280
that they requested.

04:02.280 --> 04:05.970
By the way, this API endpoint is just a fancy way

04:05.970 --> 04:09.300
of saying access window or something like that.

04:09.300 --> 04:11.790
It's an address that allows developers

04:11.790 --> 04:14.100
to get a certain type of data.

04:14.100 --> 04:18.210
And sometimes the endpoints have limitations

04:18.210 --> 04:19.680
to prevent problems.

04:19.680 --> 04:22.110
Like maybe they'll say, okay, you can only

04:22.110 --> 04:25.320
send 100 requests per minute

04:25.320 --> 04:26.520
because if you go above that,

04:26.520 --> 04:27.930
it's gonna overload our systems,

04:27.930 --> 04:31.362
and things will crash and things blow up.

04:31.362 --> 04:34.445
(computer exploding)

04:35.400 --> 04:37.410
Anyways, yeah, don't worry about that.

04:37.410 --> 04:39.330
I just wanted to let you know that that exists.

04:39.330 --> 04:40.950
All right, I told you that was not very hard,

04:40.950 --> 04:43.350
but we're gonna recap anyways.

04:43.350 --> 04:46.410
Basically, APIs are just ways that developers

04:46.410 --> 04:49.410
can write code to other systems and get data from them.

04:49.410 --> 04:51.330
There are two major types of APIs,

04:51.330 --> 04:54.330
public APIs and private APIs.

04:54.330 --> 04:56.490
Public ones are available to anybody,

04:56.490 --> 04:58.650
including you, if you're a programmer.

04:58.650 --> 05:02.130
And then private APIs are available only to people

05:02.130 --> 05:04.320
inside of that company, which means not you,

05:04.320 --> 05:05.400
unless you work at that company.

05:05.400 --> 05:06.330
All right, everyone.

05:06.330 --> 05:08.040
I hope you're learning some stuff here.

05:08.040 --> 05:09.940
I'm gonna see you in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.210 --> 00:01.860
-: Hey everybody, what is up?

00:01.860 --> 00:04.530
I wanted to take a moment to let you know what

00:04.530 --> 00:06.450
I would recommend for you to look

00:06.450 --> 00:10.920
into further for technology to really get

00:10.920 --> 00:13.080
the most you can out of product management role

00:13.080 --> 00:15.030
and really impress the people you're working

00:15.030 --> 00:19.080
with, with your technology knowledge and just

00:19.080 --> 00:21.150
the breadth of stuff you're able to understand.

00:21.150 --> 00:22.588
So here's my recommendations.

00:22.588 --> 00:25.171
(bouncy music)

00:26.490 --> 00:29.250
You should really look into

00:29.250 --> 00:32.880
some different programming languages and then the types

00:32.880 --> 00:35.943
of frameworks that are available around those.

00:38.640 --> 00:42.450
I already mentioned before how Ruby is different from

00:42.450 --> 00:47.450
Ruby on Rails because Ruby on Rails is a framework for Ruby.

00:47.670 --> 00:51.270
Really briefly on that, a framework is, well

00:51.270 --> 00:55.530
you can think of it like speed dial for programmers.

00:55.530 --> 01:00.530
So instead of me having to remember which numbers to type

01:00.960 --> 01:03.930
for calling a friend, I could just hit a button

01:03.930 --> 01:07.260
and speed dial them with one key press.

01:07.260 --> 01:11.880
In a way, this is what frameworks do for programmers.

01:11.880 --> 01:14.880
So imagine that you are going to use a language

01:14.880 --> 01:18.900
like Ruby to make a website where people can log in

01:18.900 --> 01:21.780
and you're going to store their login information

01:21.780 --> 01:24.420
and their passwords and all of that stuff in a database.

01:24.420 --> 01:26.730
This kind of thing has been done so many times

01:26.730 --> 01:30.060
and there are so many good practices to do that,

01:30.060 --> 01:32.130
that it's pretty safe to say that

01:32.130 --> 01:35.370
if someone just did it really well one time

01:35.370 --> 01:38.580
and just provided an easy way to kind of replicate it

01:38.580 --> 01:40.080
that it would be a benefit.

01:40.080 --> 01:43.260
This is exactly what frameworks do and in this case

01:43.260 --> 01:46.170
if I wanted to do that, I could use Ruby on Rails

01:46.170 --> 01:48.780
and use a few simple commands basically

01:48.780 --> 01:50.850
and it's just gonna auto-generate a database

01:50.850 --> 01:52.740
for me hosted somewhere

01:52.740 --> 01:56.070
and it's gonna generate all I need to basically

01:56.070 --> 01:58.320
have users be able to log in and you know

01:58.320 --> 02:00.840
put in a password and all that sort of thing.

02:00.840 --> 02:04.380
So frameworks are things that are structured

02:04.380 --> 02:05.880
around programming languages.

02:05.880 --> 02:07.890
It's like commonly done things

02:07.890 --> 02:11.460
in programming that allow developers to work faster.

02:11.460 --> 02:13.320
The more knowledgeable you are

02:13.320 --> 02:16.290
about these different languages like PHP

02:16.290 --> 02:20.280
or Ruby or Scala or Java, it's gonna help you.

02:20.280 --> 02:22.470
You don't have to learn them, you just should look

02:22.470 --> 02:25.980
up about them and see what they're best used for.

02:25.980 --> 02:28.230
It's gonna help you out a ton.

02:28.230 --> 02:30.780
And then if you understand the frameworks on top of that

02:30.780 --> 02:32.550
it's gonna be really helpful.

02:32.550 --> 02:36.540
Secondly, I already mentioned MySQL and SQL

02:36.540 --> 02:40.680
or S Q L, where MySQL is the database technology

02:40.680 --> 02:43.980
and S Q L is the query language

02:43.980 --> 02:46.530
for querying those databases.

02:46.530 --> 02:48.270
If I was gonna recommend you

02:48.270 --> 02:49.980
one thing that you should really

02:49.980 --> 02:52.620
really look into as a product manager

02:52.620 --> 02:56.220
it's gonna be to learn how to query SQL databases.

02:56.220 --> 02:59.043
Using SQL pretty proficiently.

03:00.630 --> 03:03.870
Most companies are going to have SQL databases

03:03.870 --> 03:06.840
or MySQL databases, it's gonna be huge

03:06.840 --> 03:10.740
on your resume to be able to make a query

03:10.740 --> 03:12.390
and get some data by yourself

03:12.390 --> 03:16.470
without having to bother engineers or data science people.

03:16.470 --> 03:19.320
So highly recommend looking into that.

03:19.320 --> 03:22.230
Check out codeacademy.com

03:22.230 --> 03:25.200
because they have a SQL course that is really good.

03:25.200 --> 03:27.420
It will get you to understand the fundamentals

03:27.420 --> 03:28.590
and it's gonna be super helpful.

03:28.590 --> 03:29.910
The third thing I wanna say is

03:29.910 --> 03:32.850
that if you're really serious about getting into data

03:32.850 --> 03:35.670
and looking and analyzing data, which you should be

03:35.670 --> 03:39.210
if you're a product manager, then I recommend you check

03:39.210 --> 03:43.233
out some data parsing and analysis languages.

03:46.110 --> 03:51.110
Some examples would be things like Python, and Python is

03:51.270 --> 03:54.330
both a language that has been adapted to actually

03:54.330 --> 03:57.240
run programs on different clients,

03:57.240 --> 04:01.140
like the web or on your computer, but it's also a

04:01.140 --> 04:03.780
what is called a scripting language, which means

04:03.780 --> 04:06.360
that you can basically take a set of data

04:06.360 --> 04:10.110
and use Python to script, or basically write out,

04:10.110 --> 04:11.940
what you want to do with that data

04:11.940 --> 04:15.720
and parse it out, or pull pieces of it out, and analyze it

04:15.720 --> 04:18.240
and then send it to something like Excel

04:18.240 --> 04:22.350
or some other type of visualization software for data.

04:22.350 --> 04:24.780
This is gonna be super helpful.

04:24.780 --> 04:27.510
It's definitely an advanced thing as a product manager

04:27.510 --> 04:29.040
but if you really want to get into it

04:29.040 --> 04:31.950
recommend checking out some scripting languages.

04:31.950 --> 04:34.110
And I would probably start with Python, right?

04:34.110 --> 04:36.300
So the fourth thing I'm gonna mention here is

04:36.300 --> 04:38.370
that if you want to go even further

04:38.370 --> 04:40.200
and you want to get more technical

04:40.200 --> 04:42.330
than you should look at the different types

04:42.330 --> 04:44.883
of databases out there or how databases work.

04:47.400 --> 04:49.350
So there are some things

04:49.350 --> 04:53.970
besides MySQL and there's different really categories

04:53.970 --> 04:56.130
of databases that are things

04:56.130 --> 05:00.390
like relational databases versus non-relational databases.

05:00.390 --> 05:02.490
And all that means is basically it's just

05:02.490 --> 05:05.910
the way that the databases store information.

05:05.910 --> 05:08.670
And some of these databases are newer than others.

05:08.670 --> 05:11.610
Some of them are older, but more proven.

05:11.610 --> 05:13.710
Some of them are faster, but you know

05:13.710 --> 05:15.480
have some things they're not so good at.

05:15.480 --> 05:18.960
So if you want some extra learning, go check out, you know

05:18.960 --> 05:20.400
the differences between relational

05:20.400 --> 05:25.140
and non-relational databases or things like MongoDB

05:25.140 --> 05:29.520
for instance versus MySQL and just get a good amount

05:29.520 --> 05:31.380
of information and knowledge around that.

05:31.380 --> 05:33.810
It's gonna help you out a ton when you're talking

05:33.810 --> 05:35.910
to engineers and data scientists.

05:35.910 --> 05:37.440
All right, so that's it for that part.

05:37.440 --> 05:39.540
I just wanted to let you know what you could look into

05:39.540 --> 05:42.660
if you're really interested in really, really

05:42.660 --> 05:45.870
getting to be a really great technical product manager.

05:45.870 --> 05:47.790
It's gonna help you out a lot when you're talking

05:47.790 --> 05:49.410
with technical people in your company.

05:49.410 --> 05:50.880
Got it? Good.

05:50.880 --> 05:52.480
I'll see it in the next lecture.

WEBVTT

00:00.330 --> 00:01.163
-: Hey, everyone.

00:01.163 --> 00:05.430
So, this is a section all about preparing for a job

00:05.430 --> 00:08.820
in product management, in order to maximize your chances

00:08.820 --> 00:10.050
at getting a job.

00:10.050 --> 00:13.350
There's one big problem that's also kind of a benefit

00:13.350 --> 00:14.910
about being a product manager.

00:14.910 --> 00:18.510
The problem is that it's pretty hard to get the job at first

00:18.510 --> 00:20.820
because it requires prior experience.

00:20.820 --> 00:22.260
But on the positive side,

00:22.260 --> 00:24.630
once you have that sort of experience

00:24.630 --> 00:26.370
or you've done it at least once,

00:26.370 --> 00:28.290
you have a lot of career security,

00:28.290 --> 00:31.230
because everyone else has trouble getting that experience.

00:31.230 --> 00:33.330
So, it's really easy to switch jobs

00:33.330 --> 00:35.370
and get promoted and that sort of thing.

00:35.370 --> 00:36.990
There's also just a low number

00:36.990 --> 00:39.510
of really qualified product managers out there,

00:39.510 --> 00:42.720
and the demand for them is really increasing lately.

00:42.720 --> 00:45.090
Unlike programming and computer science,

00:45.090 --> 00:48.330
there's no way to major in product management in college

00:48.330 --> 00:51.030
and say that you have prior experience from that.

00:51.030 --> 00:54.510
So, how can you best position yourself

00:54.510 --> 00:58.200
to get the first associate product manager job

00:58.200 --> 01:00.030
or junior product manager job?

01:00.030 --> 01:01.890
What you really need to do is demonstrate

01:01.890 --> 01:04.230
that you understand the concept and role

01:04.230 --> 01:06.390
of a product manager thoroughly,

01:06.390 --> 01:08.370
but just talking about product management

01:08.370 --> 01:10.260
is not enough to get a job.

01:10.260 --> 01:13.710
You need to have the experiences to point to and discuss

01:13.710 --> 01:15.330
with people that are interviewing you.

01:15.330 --> 01:17.610
All right, so where can we get these experiences?

01:17.610 --> 01:19.950
Well, we can find them in two major places,

01:19.950 --> 01:23.430
either in your current job or with a side project.

01:23.430 --> 01:24.750
In this lecture, we're gonna talk

01:24.750 --> 01:28.050
about finding relevant experience in your current job,

01:28.050 --> 01:30.090
and then we'll talk about a side project.

01:30.090 --> 01:33.540
Since what you do as a product manager is so broad

01:33.540 --> 01:35.700
and we just do so many things,

01:35.700 --> 01:39.900
I guarantee you that some sort of stuff you're doing

01:39.900 --> 01:43.500
at your current job is related to product management.

01:43.500 --> 01:45.960
In other words, you're probably already doing it,

01:45.960 --> 01:47.100
and you just don't know.

01:47.100 --> 01:48.690
Think about all of the stuff we've learned

01:48.690 --> 01:50.040
in this course so far,

01:50.040 --> 01:51.930
and then think about the things that you do

01:51.930 --> 01:55.560
in your current job that involve these concepts.

01:55.560 --> 01:57.210
I'll give you some examples here.

01:57.210 --> 01:59.640
So, write down any experience

01:59.640 --> 02:01.200
that you have with the following.

02:01.200 --> 02:03.330
Number one, think about any time

02:03.330 --> 02:05.340
you've had multiple things to do,

02:05.340 --> 02:07.320
either by yourself or with a team,

02:07.320 --> 02:10.560
and you have to prioritize what you do first.

02:10.560 --> 02:13.830
You made the priority decision based on information,

02:13.830 --> 02:16.470
and that's what we do all the time as PMs.

02:16.470 --> 02:19.590
Number two, think about all the times you've talked

02:19.590 --> 02:22.950
to any users of a product or a tool,

02:22.950 --> 02:24.750
internally or externally.

02:24.750 --> 02:26.850
If you work in marketing, for example,

02:26.850 --> 02:29.970
then talking to the people you are marketing to

02:29.970 --> 02:32.940
in focus groups or that sort of thing is very similar

02:32.940 --> 02:35.220
to talking to the users of a product.

02:35.220 --> 02:36.780
If you work in customer service,

02:36.780 --> 02:39.180
then you're definitely talking to users of a product

02:39.180 --> 02:40.440
on a regular basis.

02:40.440 --> 02:43.530
Number three, think about any times you've given feedback

02:43.530 --> 02:46.230
or reported bugs on the product of your company

02:46.230 --> 02:47.610
or even another one.

02:47.610 --> 02:50.730
Number four, if the company you're with now

02:50.730 --> 02:54.090
has a product team, then recall all of the interactions

02:54.090 --> 02:56.610
you've had with them and how you may have helped them

02:56.610 --> 02:59.070
acquire data to make decisions

02:59.070 --> 03:01.170
or gave them feedback on the product.

03:01.170 --> 03:03.840
All right, number five, think about any times

03:03.840 --> 03:05.880
you have worked on something at your job

03:05.880 --> 03:09.630
with multiple other teams or groups of people.

03:09.630 --> 03:11.820
Have you ever worked with the legal team,

03:11.820 --> 03:15.900
the engineering team, sales, marketing, or customer service?

03:15.900 --> 03:19.500
If you've helped teams communicate something between them,

03:19.500 --> 03:22.950
this is very relevant to being a product manager.

03:22.950 --> 03:24.180
The last thing I want you to consider

03:24.180 --> 03:26.220
is any work you've done to improve

03:26.220 --> 03:27.990
the efficiency of a process.

03:27.990 --> 03:30.960
Product managers work a lot with different processes,

03:30.960 --> 03:33.900
so any efficiency you have made better in your job

03:33.900 --> 03:36.450
by finding more efficient ways to do things

03:36.450 --> 03:39.390
or improving the way things are done,

03:39.390 --> 03:41.340
from either stuff you do every day

03:41.340 --> 03:43.080
or stuff you and your teammates do,

03:43.080 --> 03:44.910
is gonna be very relevant to talk about.

03:44.910 --> 03:47.430
So, after you've written all of these things down,

03:47.430 --> 03:49.710
think very deeply about each one,

03:49.710 --> 03:52.560
and then detail each one of them even more.

03:52.560 --> 03:55.140
These experiences can be put on your resume

03:55.140 --> 03:56.580
and then they can be talked about

03:56.580 --> 03:58.530
during product management interviews.

03:58.530 --> 04:01.050
One other thing you can do is email product managers

04:01.050 --> 04:02.100
that you find online

04:02.100 --> 04:05.430
and ask if you can ask them a few questions.

04:05.430 --> 04:07.440
Use what you learn from them to inform

04:07.440 --> 04:09.840
your job applications and interviews.

04:09.840 --> 04:13.440
I recommend browsing for product managers in industries

04:13.440 --> 04:14.490
you're interested in,

04:14.490 --> 04:17.040
in places like, let's say, Product Hunt,

04:17.040 --> 04:19.020
LinkedIn, and Twitter.

04:19.020 --> 04:21.180
But if you can't think of anything related

04:21.180 --> 04:23.670
in your current job to product management,

04:23.670 --> 04:27.240
then doing a side project is gonna be the most helpful.

04:27.240 --> 04:29.880
We're gonna talk about that in the next lecture.

04:29.880 --> 04:31.550
All right, I'll see you there.

04:31.550 --> 04:34.467
(energizing music)

response.title: Building a Portfolio with a Side Project

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.270 --> 00:01.140
-: Hey, what's up?

00:01.140 --> 00:02.250
Welcome back.

00:02.250 --> 00:03.180
So as I mentioned,

00:03.180 --> 00:04.860
the key to getting a product management job

00:04.860 --> 00:06.990
is having some sort of relevant experience

00:06.990 --> 00:09.990
to talk about and point to on your resume.

00:09.990 --> 00:12.780
If you have no experience at your current job,

00:12.780 --> 00:14.160
and just can't think of anything

00:14.160 --> 00:16.980
you've done related to product management at all,

00:16.980 --> 00:19.440
then a side project is the best option.

00:19.440 --> 00:20.730
So if you've been following along

00:20.730 --> 00:21.563
with this course,

00:21.563 --> 00:24.270
especially to the sections that Evan has taught,

00:24.270 --> 00:26.100
then you already have a really good idea

00:26.100 --> 00:28.410
of how to do a side project

00:28.410 --> 00:30.360
on a small product or feature.

00:30.360 --> 00:33.090
What you need to do is document your experience

00:33.090 --> 00:36.390
of coming up with ideas to solve user problems

00:36.390 --> 00:38.310
through a product or feature,

00:38.310 --> 00:39.720
and put it together somewhere

00:39.720 --> 00:42.570
like a blog post on your own website,

00:42.570 --> 00:43.650
a slide deck,

00:43.650 --> 00:46.200
or you can actually make your own prototypes

00:46.200 --> 00:49.050
really easily and put that online.

00:49.050 --> 00:51.240
These projects can be very detailed

00:51.240 --> 00:53.130
or really just high-level.

00:53.130 --> 00:55.290
You can even take a product used daily,

00:55.290 --> 00:57.810
and describe in writing or graphics

00:57.810 --> 00:58.920
how you would change it

00:58.920 --> 01:01.320
in order to solve user needs better.

01:01.320 --> 01:03.270
This is a pretty common tactic

01:03.270 --> 01:05.310
in the world of design actually.

01:05.310 --> 01:07.890
You've probably seen a lot of designers

01:07.890 --> 01:10.290
post their own like redesign versions

01:10.290 --> 01:13.290
of popular websites or apps or product,

01:13.290 --> 01:16.170
and a lot of designers get their jobs this way.

01:16.170 --> 01:17.490
You can actually do the same,

01:17.490 --> 01:19.020
but for product management,

01:19.020 --> 01:21.990
and with features and that sort of thing.

01:21.990 --> 01:24.577
But if you want to do a deep dive into the topic

01:24.577 --> 01:26.310
of doing your own project,

01:26.310 --> 01:28.770
I recommend two great resources.

01:28.770 --> 01:30.711
Firstly, check out the other courses

01:30.711 --> 01:34.380
that Evan has taught on coming up with ideas,

01:34.380 --> 01:35.640
creating an MVP

01:35.640 --> 01:38.070
or getting started as an entrepreneur.

01:38.070 --> 01:40.530
These will set you up with everything you need.

01:40.530 --> 01:42.150
Another option if you want to do

01:42.150 --> 01:44.280
a step-by-step project

01:44.280 --> 01:47.070
while also learning about product management,

01:47.070 --> 01:48.930
I recommend you check out my friend,

01:48.930 --> 01:50.910
Ellen Chisa's course called,

01:50.910 --> 01:53.550
The Fundamentals of Product Management.

01:53.550 --> 01:55.830
You can find a link on her website at

01:55.830 --> 01:59.370
www.EllenChisa.com.

01:59.370 --> 02:02.460
Ellen also writes some really good blog posts

02:02.460 --> 02:04.710
on her experience as a product manager,

02:04.710 --> 02:06.030
and she's got a lot.

02:06.030 --> 02:07.980
So also be sure to check those out.

02:07.980 --> 02:09.420
So to recap here,

02:09.420 --> 02:12.480
doing a side project is a huge benefit.

02:12.480 --> 02:14.100
If you don't have any

02:14.100 --> 02:16.350
sort of relevant experience at all,

02:16.350 --> 02:19.080
and can't think of anything in your current role

02:19.080 --> 02:20.310
that qualifies as

02:20.310 --> 02:22.320
product management style experience

02:22.320 --> 02:23.460
in the least,

02:23.460 --> 02:24.900
then you should start thinking about

02:24.900 --> 02:25.950
doing a side project.

02:25.950 --> 02:27.210
All right, in the next lecture,

02:27.210 --> 02:29.160
we're gonna talk about some other things

02:29.160 --> 02:30.840
you can do to really prep,

02:30.840 --> 02:32.070
and increase your chances

02:32.070 --> 02:34.080
of getting a job as a product manager.

02:34.080 --> 02:35.635
I'll see you there.

02:35.635 --> 02:39.302
(upbeat instrumental music)

WEBVTT

00:00.240 --> 00:01.073
-: Hey, what's up?

00:01.073 --> 00:01.906
Welcome back.

00:01.906 --> 00:04.200
The other thing that's gonna really help you out

00:04.200 --> 00:06.060
immensely in getting a job

00:06.060 --> 00:07.680
as a product manager

00:07.680 --> 00:10.470
is having a good personal brand online.

00:10.470 --> 00:11.910
What you want here is basically

00:11.910 --> 00:15.030
an online record that demonstrates your knowledge

00:15.030 --> 00:17.700
of product management and the skills required.

00:17.700 --> 00:19.373
Building credibility before you walk

00:19.373 --> 00:21.540
through the door to your interviews

00:21.540 --> 00:23.550
is worth its weight in gold.

00:23.550 --> 00:25.770
The best way to demonstrate credibility

00:25.770 --> 00:28.020
is to have a website or a blog

00:28.020 --> 00:29.880
where you talk about things related to

00:29.880 --> 00:32.490
product management and have those projects

00:32.490 --> 00:34.680
we talked about available to the public.

00:34.680 --> 00:36.390
Recruiters will often look at

00:36.390 --> 00:37.770
your social media profiles,

00:37.770 --> 00:39.150
and website to understand

00:39.150 --> 00:41.100
if you're qualified or not for the role.

00:41.100 --> 00:43.470
Since there's not as much information out there

00:43.470 --> 00:44.580
on product management

00:44.580 --> 00:46.080
as compared to other topics,

00:46.080 --> 00:47.820
it's actually pretty easy to stand out.

00:47.820 --> 00:49.890
Here's a couple example topics

00:49.890 --> 00:51.720
that you can consider writing about

00:51.720 --> 00:52.553
to get started.

00:52.553 --> 00:53.386
All right.

00:53.386 --> 00:54.540
Number one, you could write about

00:54.540 --> 00:57.630
why a particular product you use is poor,

00:57.630 --> 00:59.670
and then how it could be improved.

00:59.670 --> 01:01.110
This is actually a pretty common

01:01.110 --> 01:02.280
interview question

01:02.280 --> 01:04.200
that they'll ask you as a PM.

01:04.200 --> 01:05.497
They'll say you know,

01:05.497 --> 01:07.530
"What is a favorite product of yours,

01:07.530 --> 01:09.510
and how could it be improved?"

01:09.510 --> 01:11.220
Number two, you could say,

01:11.220 --> 01:13.170
the top five mistakes you've made

01:13.170 --> 01:15.480
in your current role and what you've learned.

01:15.480 --> 01:16.980
Number three, you could read

01:16.980 --> 01:18.540
some product management books,

01:18.540 --> 01:20.580
which we will have in the resources,

01:20.580 --> 01:22.170
some recommendations for those,

01:22.170 --> 01:24.840
and then you could write some blog posts

01:24.840 --> 01:27.000
about your reviews of those books,

01:27.000 --> 01:29.340
and what you agree or disagree with.

01:29.340 --> 01:31.245
Number four, you could write a post

01:31.245 --> 01:34.500
on your thoughts on recent technology trends

01:34.500 --> 01:35.940
or industry events,

01:35.940 --> 01:38.130
and how you see them developing further

01:38.130 --> 01:40.140
or changing the market, all right.

01:40.140 --> 01:43.140
So besides a blog, it's also very helpful

01:43.140 --> 01:45.120
to have a public social media account

01:45.120 --> 01:47.310
like Twitter where you participate

01:47.310 --> 01:48.630
in product discussions

01:48.630 --> 01:50.910
or talk about relevant things

01:50.910 --> 01:52.170
to product management.

01:52.170 --> 01:55.382
I also recommend you go on Quora,

01:55.382 --> 02:00.300
Quora.com and ask or answer questions

02:00.300 --> 02:03.330
related to products and product strategy.

02:03.330 --> 02:05.010
All right, so a quick recap.

02:05.010 --> 02:06.690
One of the best things you can do

02:06.690 --> 02:09.390
to make sure you have a great online brand

02:09.390 --> 02:12.570
is to write or just have a public presence.

02:12.570 --> 02:14.760
If you don't have a website or a blog,

02:14.760 --> 02:16.170
you should make one.

02:16.170 --> 02:17.160
You should start one.

02:17.160 --> 02:18.750
You should start writing,

02:18.750 --> 02:20.820
and you should include some topics

02:20.820 --> 02:22.170
related to product management,

02:22.170 --> 02:23.820
and product strategy in there.

02:23.820 --> 02:25.830
Also make sure you have a publicly

02:25.830 --> 02:28.380
available social media account like Twitter

02:28.380 --> 02:29.430
where you talk about things

02:29.430 --> 02:30.750
that are also on topic

02:30.750 --> 02:33.630
with product management and UX design,

02:33.630 --> 02:34.890
and user experience.

02:34.890 --> 02:35.723
All right, I'll see you

02:35.723 --> 02:38.280
in the next section where we'll be talking about

02:38.280 --> 02:40.560
looking for product management jobs

02:40.560 --> 02:44.054
once you feel that you are sufficiently prepared.

02:44.054 --> 02:47.721
(upbeat instrumental music)

response.title: Where to Look and What to Look For

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.270 --> 00:02.430
-: Hey, guys, welcome back.

00:02.430 --> 00:05.700
Once you've prepared to look for product management jobs,

00:05.700 --> 00:08.040
and you've got that good online brand,

00:08.040 --> 00:11.340
and you think you can point to some relevant experience,

00:11.340 --> 00:14.400
you have to know where to go to look for the jobs

00:14.400 --> 00:16.590
and really what you want to be looking for.

00:16.590 --> 00:19.140
In this lecture, we're gonna talk about three things.

00:19.140 --> 00:22.230
One, looking at your own company,

00:22.230 --> 00:25.620
two, networking, and three, looking online.

00:25.620 --> 00:27.300
Firstly, it's important to understand

00:27.300 --> 00:28.530
that if you're really serious

00:28.530 --> 00:30.720
about getting into product management,

00:30.720 --> 00:34.800
then the best place, by far, that you can look for a job

00:34.800 --> 00:36.450
is in your own company.

00:36.450 --> 00:37.380
Trust me on that.

00:37.380 --> 00:40.050
It's so much easier to move internally

00:40.050 --> 00:43.260
than it is to get a new job as a product manager

00:43.260 --> 00:44.730
with no previous experience.

00:44.730 --> 00:46.770
If you already have a product management team

00:46.770 --> 00:48.660
at your office, your company,

00:48.660 --> 00:52.020
ask to shadow some product managers in meetings

00:52.020 --> 00:54.510
to learn more about what they're working on.

00:54.510 --> 00:56.700
What you should do here is demonstrate

00:56.700 --> 00:59.010
that you're interested in helping them out,

00:59.010 --> 01:02.940
and offer to do some work in your spare time for their team.

01:02.940 --> 01:05.700
The goal here is to learn as much as you can.

01:05.700 --> 01:09.180
If you don't have a product team already at your company,

01:09.180 --> 01:12.870
then you can still start offering to do tasks and projects

01:12.870 --> 01:15.720
that will add to your product manager resume,

01:15.720 --> 01:18.750
things like prioritization of initiatives,

01:18.750 --> 01:21.540
technical implementations, user interviews,

01:21.540 --> 01:23.520
and research and that kind of thing.

01:23.520 --> 01:26.670
Secondly, it's very common for people to get jobs

01:26.670 --> 01:30.600
based off of references rather than just applying to them.

01:30.600 --> 01:32.910
This is the case with every type of job,

01:32.910 --> 01:35.640
but so much more so for product management.

01:35.640 --> 01:37.410
But in order to get references,

01:37.410 --> 01:40.590
you need to meet people in the product management industry.

01:40.590 --> 01:44.130
For that, I recommend you go to websites like meetup.com

01:44.130 --> 01:45.750
and look for product management

01:45.750 --> 01:48.600
or product strategy meetups in your area.

01:48.600 --> 01:49.830
You can go to these events.

01:49.830 --> 01:52.320
You can talk to people that are product managers.

01:52.320 --> 01:55.680
You can find out who's hiring and maybe even meet somebody

01:55.680 --> 01:58.860
who would be willing to provide you a reference for a job

01:58.860 --> 02:00.270
or tell you about an opening.

02:00.270 --> 02:02.430
Outside of finding product management jobs

02:02.430 --> 02:04.320
through networking, there's a few places

02:04.320 --> 02:06.270
I recommend you look online.

02:06.270 --> 02:09.360
Number one, go to angellist.com,

02:09.360 --> 02:12.330
which has a big focus on Silicon Valley tech companies

02:12.330 --> 02:13.980
and look for product management jobs.

02:13.980 --> 02:18.150
Number two, go to Hacker News, and they have a,

02:18.150 --> 02:20.340
it's a technology message board, basically,

02:20.340 --> 02:22.770
and you can Google it or you can search on their site,

02:22.770 --> 02:25.237
because they have a monthly thread called,

02:25.237 --> 02:27.720
"Ask HN Who is Hiring?"

02:27.720 --> 02:29.280
Just Google it and you'll find the threads

02:29.280 --> 02:30.113
that I'm talking about.

02:30.113 --> 02:34.140
They do this every month, and it's just full of openings

02:34.140 --> 02:35.640
for product management positions.

02:35.640 --> 02:37.260
The third place I would recommend

02:37.260 --> 02:41.370
is do a search on Facebook for any local Facebook groups

02:41.370 --> 02:45.060
around your city that involve any type of job,

02:45.060 --> 02:48.030
but especially product or technology jobs.

02:48.030 --> 02:49.950
These are really good to browse through

02:49.950 --> 02:51.660
and something good to keep your eye on

02:51.660 --> 02:53.370
in case some listings come up.

02:53.370 --> 02:55.440
I've seen a lot of product jobs go through

02:55.440 --> 02:56.610
these types of groups.

02:56.610 --> 02:58.350
Number four is mailing lists,

02:58.350 --> 03:00.000
and there are some good ones out there.

03:00.000 --> 03:03.840
I recommend a mailing list by a guy named Ken Norton.

03:03.840 --> 03:06.900
You can go to kennorton.com.

03:06.900 --> 03:10.470
It's K-E-N-N-O-R-T-O-N,

03:10.470 --> 03:12.750
and he's a really well-known product manager.

03:12.750 --> 03:14.340
He has a great little newsletter,

03:14.340 --> 03:17.940
but he always has job postings at the end of each email.

03:17.940 --> 03:21.570
All right, so those are some tips on where to look

03:21.570 --> 03:23.760
for your product management job

03:23.760 --> 03:25.650
if you're looking to get one.

03:25.650 --> 03:27.000
And in the next lecture,

03:27.000 --> 03:29.700
I'm gonna give you a little bit more inside advice

03:29.700 --> 03:32.670
on how to go about your product management job hunt.

03:32.670 --> 03:33.620
I'll see you there.

response.title: Inside Advice on Your PM Job Hunt

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.000 --> 00:01.110
-: Hey, everyone.

00:01.110 --> 00:04.740
Before we move on to the actual resume,

00:04.740 --> 00:06.270
and interview process,

00:06.270 --> 00:07.890
I wanted to give you a couple

00:07.890 --> 00:09.900
of sort of insider tips

00:09.900 --> 00:11.970
on looking for a product management job.

00:11.970 --> 00:14.370
These are kind of miscellaneous tips

00:14.370 --> 00:16.590
that I just thought that I should tell you about,

00:16.590 --> 00:17.640
and pay attention

00:17.640 --> 00:19.230
because they will help you in the long run.

00:19.230 --> 00:20.640
The first tip is for

00:20.640 --> 00:22.920
any product management job opportunity,

00:22.920 --> 00:25.410
you should make sure that the company,

00:25.410 --> 00:28.980
that is looking to hire has a good understanding

00:28.980 --> 00:31.170
of the product management role.

00:31.170 --> 00:32.610
and can clearly illustrate

00:32.610 --> 00:34.020
why they're hiring one.

00:34.020 --> 00:35.190
I say this because

00:35.190 --> 00:36.540
as an observer of the industry,

00:36.540 --> 00:38.040
I've seen a lot of companies

00:38.040 --> 00:39.690
that don't see the difference

00:39.690 --> 00:42.750
between product and project management.

00:42.750 --> 00:44.250
Sometimes these companies will end up

00:44.250 --> 00:47.310
hiring somebody merely to keep engineers on task,

00:47.310 --> 00:48.510
and if you can,

00:48.510 --> 00:50.250
you want to avoid this

00:50.250 --> 00:51.720
to maximize your experience

00:51.720 --> 00:53.460
for actual product management.

00:53.460 --> 00:56.310
The second tip I have for you is to do

00:56.310 --> 00:59.370
a lot of research on the current product team

00:59.370 --> 01:01.950
or product management leader at the company.

01:01.950 --> 01:02.880
Find them on Twitter.

01:02.880 --> 01:04.140
Look at their blogs.

01:04.140 --> 01:05.910
Check out their job history on LinkedIn,

01:05.910 --> 01:06.990
and see what you think

01:06.990 --> 01:08.490
about the things that they say.

01:08.490 --> 01:09.570
What you're looking for here

01:09.570 --> 01:11.280
is someone that you think you can learn

01:11.280 --> 01:12.750
a lot from that has had

01:12.750 --> 01:15.120
product management experience before.

01:15.120 --> 01:16.890
Sometimes someone will be named

01:16.890 --> 01:18.030
director of product

01:18.030 --> 01:19.620
at small growing companies,

01:19.620 --> 01:21.810
but they'll have no product management experience

01:21.810 --> 01:24.300
before that and this is something that trust me,

01:24.300 --> 01:25.770
you really want to avoid.

01:25.770 --> 01:26.603
On the other hand,

01:26.603 --> 01:27.630
if you're interested in working

01:27.630 --> 01:29.730
for a particular industry or company

01:29.730 --> 01:30.750
down the road,

01:30.750 --> 01:32.820
then working for somebody that has worked

01:32.820 --> 01:34.800
in that industry in the past

01:34.800 --> 01:36.450
is a great thing to know.

01:36.450 --> 01:37.867
It's a great thing to be able to say,

01:37.867 --> 01:39.240
"Oh, I'd like them to be my boss

01:39.240 --> 01:40.470
so I can learn from them.

01:40.470 --> 01:41.790
So pay attention to that."

01:41.790 --> 01:42.960
All right so recap here.

01:42.960 --> 01:45.090
Do extensive research on the people

01:45.090 --> 01:46.530
that you'll be working with,

01:46.530 --> 01:48.750
and use it to your advantage.

01:48.750 --> 01:51.360
Also, make sure that the company

01:51.360 --> 01:53.310
that's hiring you knows why they're hiring

01:53.310 --> 01:56.790
a product manager so that you get a good role

01:56.790 --> 01:59.460
at a good product team where you can learn a lot.

01:59.460 --> 02:00.592
I'll see y'all in the next lecture.

02:00.592 --> 02:03.600
We're gonna move into the actual resume,

02:03.600 --> 02:04.830
and interview process.

02:04.830 --> 02:06.306
Catch you there.

02:06.306 --> 02:09.973
(upbeat instrumental music)

WEBVTT

00:00.300 --> 00:01.530
-: What's up everybody?

00:01.530 --> 00:06.060
So this section is all about your resume

00:06.060 --> 00:08.970
and interviewing as a product manager.

00:08.970 --> 00:12.120
And this lecture is about your resume

00:12.120 --> 00:14.310
and how we're going to optimize it

00:14.310 --> 00:18.450
to have a better chance at getting a product management job.

00:18.450 --> 00:20.520
So resumes in general, it's true,

00:20.520 --> 00:22.650
not as important as they used to be,

00:22.650 --> 00:25.230
but you still gotta have one, unfortunately,

00:25.230 --> 00:27.540
and you need to make it the best you can

00:27.540 --> 00:30.690
to optimize your chances for getting a job.

00:30.690 --> 00:31.860
Especially in product management

00:31.860 --> 00:33.750
because it's pretty hard to get a job as one.

00:33.750 --> 00:36.600
If you don't have any prior product management experience,

00:36.600 --> 00:39.930
what's important here is to frame the experience you do have

00:39.930 --> 00:43.350
in a way that relates to getting that PM job.

00:43.350 --> 00:44.910
So we're gonna talk about two ways

00:44.910 --> 00:48.990
to optimize your resume here, and they're pretty simple.

00:48.990 --> 00:51.330
All right, step one: If you haven't already,

00:51.330 --> 00:52.860
go back and watch that lecture

00:52.860 --> 00:57.210
on preparing for the PM role with relevant experience

00:57.210 --> 01:00.480
and think through all of the times you've done something

01:00.480 --> 01:03.930
at your current job, even if you're not a product manager,

01:03.930 --> 01:06.180
because it's probably got some stuff in it

01:06.180 --> 01:09.060
that does relate to product management.

01:09.060 --> 01:12.420
Once you've done that, go through and edit the subtext

01:12.420 --> 01:15.870
of each position you've held on your resume

01:15.870 --> 01:19.860
and make it reflect that product management experience

01:19.860 --> 01:23.940
that you've found using the exercise we did in that lecture.

01:23.940 --> 01:28.380
So step two: Make sure that all of the descriptions

01:28.380 --> 01:31.500
in your previous positions, and even the current one,

01:31.500 --> 01:35.010
just make sure your whole resume is like this: quantitative.

01:35.010 --> 01:39.180
All the examples you give on the things you did,

01:39.180 --> 01:40.620
they need to be quantitative.

01:40.620 --> 01:44.010
What I mean by that is we need to say,

01:44.010 --> 01:46.350
instead of describing what your job was,

01:46.350 --> 01:49.590
we need to say, "How did you do at your job?"

01:49.590 --> 01:51.060
This is because as we know,

01:51.060 --> 01:54.210
product management is all about improving metrics.

01:54.210 --> 01:57.330
So what we're gonna try to do is change-

01:57.330 --> 01:59.790
Instead of describing your job, we're gonna change it into

01:59.790 --> 02:02.700
describing how you, in a quantitative way,

02:02.700 --> 02:05.400
improved things at your job.

02:05.400 --> 02:07.380
Here's an example: If you're a marketer,

02:07.380 --> 02:10.080
instead of saying that you managed marketing campaigns,

02:10.080 --> 02:12.750
say how many marketing campaigns you managed,

02:12.750 --> 02:16.050
and how much revenue it generated for the company.

02:16.050 --> 02:18.540
Or do something like "Increased number of

02:18.540 --> 02:23.010
simultaneous campaigns managed at once by 20%

02:23.010 --> 02:24.210
over the course of the year."

02:24.210 --> 02:26.390
You just gotta make it quantitative.

02:26.390 --> 02:27.900
So what we're gonna do now

02:27.900 --> 02:32.550
is we're going to put some fake resumes on the screen.

02:32.550 --> 02:34.980
All right, so this is totally fake. I made these up.

02:34.980 --> 02:38.130
But it's for someone that works in marketing currently

02:38.130 --> 02:40.350
and wants to go into product management.

02:40.350 --> 02:42.000
So you can see the difference

02:42.000 --> 02:46.110
between these two is that we've changed, first of all,

02:46.110 --> 02:48.840
everything to be more quantitative

02:48.840 --> 02:52.230
in terms of what were the things that we accomplished

02:52.230 --> 02:56.250
and then we have also put in more experience

02:56.250 --> 03:00.540
that's relevant to product management role,

03:00.540 --> 03:03.810
rather than talking about stuff that's not relevant

03:03.810 --> 03:06.990
to what product managers do on a day-to-day basis.

03:06.990 --> 03:09.720
So I'll include these in the resources section,

03:09.720 --> 03:11.310
along with some other guides,

03:11.310 --> 03:14.220
on how to improve your resume in general,

03:14.220 --> 03:16.680
but I really wanna just tell you about that.

03:16.680 --> 03:18.840
Product managers all about those metrics

03:18.840 --> 03:21.030
so make sure stuff is quantitative.

03:21.030 --> 03:22.800
And secondly, product managers

03:22.800 --> 03:25.380
do so much stuff that it's pretty easy to think of stuff

03:25.380 --> 03:27.630
that you did that's related to product management,

03:27.630 --> 03:31.170
like prioritization or communicating between departments.

03:31.170 --> 03:33.420
Just make sure that's reflected in your resume

03:33.420 --> 03:35.820
and even if you don't have prior product experience,

03:35.820 --> 03:38.730
it's gonna give you a much better chance

03:38.730 --> 03:41.400
at not being passed over by the recruiter

03:41.400 --> 03:44.520
for an associate or junior product manager position.

03:44.520 --> 03:47.085
All right everyone, I will see ya in the next lecture.

03:47.085 --> 03:49.668
(upbeat music)

WEBVTT

00:00.300 --> 00:03.600
-: All right, so you've got your resume optimized,

00:03.600 --> 00:06.420
you've got your online brand perfected,

00:06.420 --> 00:08.730
and you're going to interview with a company.

00:08.730 --> 00:11.460
Congrats on getting that first interview.

00:11.460 --> 00:13.110
In this lecture, we're gonna talk

00:13.110 --> 00:15.060
about some tips to help you get

00:15.060 --> 00:17.670
through the product management interview.

00:17.670 --> 00:20.880
These interviews are tough.

00:20.880 --> 00:23.130
And that's because it's not all cut and dry.

00:23.130 --> 00:25.470
You gotta demonstrate some skills,

00:25.470 --> 00:28.260
usually on a whiteboard, for talking through problems.

00:28.260 --> 00:29.862
So what I'm gonna do

00:29.862 --> 00:31.290
is give you three big tips

00:31.290 --> 00:33.420
on how to nail these interviews.

00:33.420 --> 00:35.880
The first two we're gonna talk about here

00:35.880 --> 00:37.920
and then the most important one

00:37.920 --> 00:40.740
has its own lecture coming up next.

00:40.740 --> 00:43.020
All right, tip number one.

00:43.020 --> 00:47.490
Use every interview to make the next one even better.

00:47.490 --> 00:50.460
Each interview you do is gonna help you a lot

00:50.460 --> 00:53.010
in making the next interview even better.

00:53.010 --> 00:56.070
Whether it's another interview at the same company

00:56.070 --> 00:58.290
or an interview at a different company,

00:58.290 --> 00:59.940
you should pay very close attention

00:59.940 --> 01:01.560
to the questions they ask you

01:01.560 --> 01:03.360
for that product management role.

01:03.360 --> 01:05.550
Then, think about whether or not you are able

01:05.550 --> 01:08.610
to get a good answer to them or not.

01:08.610 --> 01:11.040
For every question, take mental notes

01:11.040 --> 01:13.260
or even physical notes if they let you.

01:13.260 --> 01:16.230
When you go home, start researching those things

01:16.230 --> 01:17.063
immediately.

01:17.063 --> 01:18.210
If you didn't understand something,

01:18.210 --> 01:20.460
you didn't have a good answer, go look it up.

01:20.460 --> 01:22.320
So if you make it to the next round of interviews

01:22.320 --> 01:25.650
for that company, then you have more relevant knowledge

01:25.650 --> 01:27.120
to discuss.

01:27.120 --> 01:29.010
Even if you don't get that job

01:29.010 --> 01:32.700
and you are going to an interview at another company,

01:32.700 --> 01:35.160
this is going to be super helpful

01:35.160 --> 01:37.260
for prepping you for these types of questions

01:37.260 --> 01:38.093
in the future.

01:38.093 --> 01:39.630
You gotta be optimistic here.

01:39.630 --> 01:41.250
These are tough interviews

01:41.250 --> 01:44.820
but each failure is an increased chance

01:44.820 --> 01:46.320
at the next opportunity

01:46.320 --> 01:47.730
so that's how you should look at it.

01:47.730 --> 01:50.820
Tip number two, ask them good questions.

01:50.820 --> 01:53.850
The more research you do before they interview

01:53.850 --> 01:56.370
about the company and the industry and the product,

01:56.370 --> 01:57.510
the better.

01:57.510 --> 01:59.190
You can't do enough, to be honest.

01:59.190 --> 02:01.830
Most interviewers will give you a chance

02:01.830 --> 02:05.220
to ask them questions at the end of the interview

02:05.220 --> 02:07.710
so you should have some really good questions in mind.

02:07.710 --> 02:10.140
Before you go into your first interview,

02:10.140 --> 02:13.080
do that research on those latest initiatives

02:13.080 --> 02:15.780
and industry trends and even what their competitors

02:15.780 --> 02:18.660
are doing, what rounds of funding other people

02:18.660 --> 02:20.970
have raised that are in the same industry.

02:20.970 --> 02:24.420
Use this information to ask them thoughtful questions.

02:24.420 --> 02:25.650
I'll give you an example here.

02:25.650 --> 02:26.730
Let's say I'm interviewing

02:26.730 --> 02:29.820
for a product management position at a messaging app

02:29.820 --> 02:31.800
and they compete with WhatsApp,

02:31.800 --> 02:34.470
which is a really popular messaging app

02:34.470 --> 02:35.490
owned by Facebook.

02:35.490 --> 02:37.200
I'd probably ask them questions

02:37.200 --> 02:40.170
about how they tried to differentiate themselves

02:40.170 --> 02:42.720
for the market and then what they think

02:42.720 --> 02:44.550
the biggest obstacles for them

02:44.550 --> 02:46.290
are in the next year.

02:46.290 --> 02:49.140
If WhatsApp releases a feature in, you know,

02:49.140 --> 02:50.850
a month before you do that interview

02:50.850 --> 02:53.160
or any time in the last six months,

02:53.160 --> 02:53.993
you need to say,

02:53.993 --> 02:55.957
"Hey, what are your thoughts on that latest feature?

02:55.957 --> 02:59.100
"Are you guys gonna try to compete with that or what?"

02:59.100 --> 03:00.840
And this really just shows

03:00.840 --> 03:02.490
that you're interested in their product,

03:02.490 --> 03:05.400
you understand what's going on in this industry.

03:05.400 --> 03:08.850
And I guarantee that if you ask good questions like this,

03:08.850 --> 03:10.980
you're gonna stand out from the rest of the candidates.

03:10.980 --> 03:12.930
All right, let's recap here.

03:12.930 --> 03:15.150
Use every interview as an opportunity

03:15.150 --> 03:16.740
to make the next one better.

03:16.740 --> 03:19.050
Don't cheap out on this, you gotta do it.

03:19.050 --> 03:21.870
And then two, ask insightful questions

03:21.870 --> 03:23.280
at the end of your interview

03:23.280 --> 03:26.160
that are relevant to the product management role,

03:26.160 --> 03:27.990
the industry, and the product.

03:27.990 --> 03:29.250
If you use these tips,

03:29.250 --> 03:31.080
your interviews are gonna be really good

03:31.080 --> 03:33.060
and you're gonna be way ahead of the competition.

03:33.060 --> 03:34.800
You're gonna be even better off

03:34.800 --> 03:37.200
if you watch the next lecture

03:37.200 --> 03:39.181
'cause it's a really good tip.

03:39.181 --> 03:41.681
(jolly music)

response.title: How to Answer Interview Questions the Right Way

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.330 --> 00:01.890
-: Welcome back, everybody.

00:01.890 --> 00:03.900
Like I said, this lecture is gonna be one

00:03.900 --> 00:05.790
of the most important tips I can offer you

00:05.790 --> 00:08.220
for interviewing as a product manager.

00:08.220 --> 00:10.770
As you already know, if you've been watching

00:10.770 --> 00:13.470
this course so far and I hope you have,

00:13.470 --> 00:16.380
because what are you doing here if not?

00:16.380 --> 00:21.000
Product management positions are based on making

00:21.000 --> 00:22.770
good decisions based on data.

00:22.770 --> 00:25.740
If someone asks you what you would do for a product

00:25.740 --> 00:29.400
in a particular situation, you never just give them

00:29.400 --> 00:33.113
an answer without mentioning how you came to that conclusion

00:33.113 --> 00:38.113
and then why, and then what are the alternatives?

00:38.250 --> 00:41.640
Here's a real life example from my actual career.

00:41.640 --> 00:44.940
I was once interviewing a candidate for a product management

00:44.940 --> 00:46.560
role at an e-commerce company.

00:46.560 --> 00:48.967
Everyone's like, "Oh, you gotta interview this person.

00:48.967 --> 00:50.077
"She's gonna be, she's great.

00:50.077 --> 00:52.350
"She's got a great resume, great product manager."

00:52.350 --> 00:54.037
So I'm like, "Oh, okay, I'll interview her.

00:54.037 --> 00:54.870
"We'll see."

00:55.830 --> 00:59.670
I asked her if she thought we should develop

00:59.670 --> 01:04.670
a native mobile app, or just continue focusing on just doing

01:04.830 --> 01:08.280
a normal mobile website, like we had been doing.

01:08.280 --> 01:10.380
The problem is without even thinking,

01:10.380 --> 01:12.210
I mean she she had like a three-second delay.

01:12.210 --> 01:14.407
She goes, "Yeah, you should do a mobile app,

01:14.407 --> 01:15.780
"native mobile app."

01:15.780 --> 01:17.550
She didn't give any other details.

01:17.550 --> 01:21.000
So whether or not she was correct with her answer

01:21.000 --> 01:24.000
is not the issue, and in fact it didn't even matter.

01:24.000 --> 01:26.880
The problem is that she just gave an answer

01:26.880 --> 01:28.860
without asking for more information

01:28.860 --> 01:31.230
to help her make the decision, and she didn't

01:31.230 --> 01:33.420
follow it up with any reasoning behind

01:33.420 --> 01:37.020
how she arrived at that conclusion or how her answer

01:37.020 --> 01:39.870
may change based on data.

01:39.870 --> 01:42.630
So you're thinking, "Well, Cole, what was the right answer?"

01:42.630 --> 01:45.600
Look, the answer I was looking for in that situation

01:45.600 --> 01:49.147
was for her to say, "Okay, I need more information

01:49.147 --> 01:50.497
"to help make this decision.

01:50.497 --> 01:53.677
"How many of your customers shop for mobile devices?

01:53.677 --> 01:55.837
"What volume of traffic do you have?

01:55.837 --> 01:59.077
"What sorts of engineering knowledge does your team have?

01:59.077 --> 02:00.540
"And what's your budget?"

02:00.540 --> 02:02.730
These things help make better decisions.

02:02.730 --> 02:05.490
I would've also loved to see her follow-up her answer

02:05.490 --> 02:08.190
by telling me the reasoning behind it.

02:08.190 --> 02:11.580
And then most importantly, tell me what other options

02:11.580 --> 02:14.343
were on the table besides just,

02:16.237 --> 02:18.420
a mobile website or a native mobile app.

02:18.420 --> 02:21.711
Because there are things you can do besides that.

02:21.711 --> 02:23.130
There's an in-between.

02:23.130 --> 02:27.000
You can do a hybrid app, there's other options.

02:27.000 --> 02:30.150
So you need to mention these types of things

02:30.150 --> 02:31.440
when you're answering questions.

02:31.440 --> 02:33.930
As a product manager, there is never

02:33.930 --> 02:36.750
a single correct answer to a question.

02:36.750 --> 02:40.560
There are always multiple possibilities, and it's your job

02:40.560 --> 02:44.520
to synthesize information, to come up with a good approach

02:44.520 --> 02:48.000
and be clear about why this is the best option.

02:48.000 --> 02:50.580
We work on data, not feelings.

02:50.580 --> 02:52.860
Just like if someone in your company asks you

02:52.860 --> 02:54.840
why you're doing a particular initiative,

02:54.840 --> 02:56.760
or adding a certain feature.

02:56.760 --> 02:58.740
You can't just say, "Because."

02:58.740 --> 03:01.537
No, you gotta say, "Because the data says this.

03:01.537 --> 03:03.697
"The user interviews say this.

03:03.697 --> 03:05.227
"The market is going this way,

03:05.227 --> 03:07.057
"and our competitors are doing this.

03:07.057 --> 03:09.097
"but here's the other options we considered.

03:09.097 --> 03:12.570
"This one seems to be the best approach for this reason."

03:12.570 --> 03:16.950
That is how you think and act like a product manager.

03:16.950 --> 03:19.890
So in interviews, always ask clarifying questions

03:19.890 --> 03:21.240
when possible.

03:21.240 --> 03:23.760
It shows that you're being thoughtful and thinking

03:23.760 --> 03:24.870
like a product manager.

03:24.870 --> 03:28.080
By the way, I don't mean to start asking them questions

03:28.080 --> 03:29.287
if they ask you simple things like,

03:29.287 --> 03:30.330
"Where did you go to school?"

03:30.330 --> 03:33.180
Or, "Where did you work before here?"

03:33.180 --> 03:34.177
What are you gonna ask 'em?

03:34.177 --> 03:35.880
"What do you mean by that question?"

03:35.880 --> 03:37.620
No, I mean, if you get to the point where

03:37.620 --> 03:41.730
they're asking you about your opinions on their product

03:41.730 --> 03:44.250
or "What would you do in this situation?"

03:44.250 --> 03:47.160
There are a lot of tech companies out there

03:47.160 --> 03:48.660
Google and Facebook and Twitter

03:48.660 --> 03:50.790
and a lot of the big ones out there,

03:50.790 --> 03:53.490
where the entire product management interview process

03:53.490 --> 03:57.360
is just hypothetical situations.

03:57.360 --> 04:00.037
They're like, "Hey, what do you think about this?

04:00.037 --> 04:01.027
"What if this happened?

04:01.027 --> 04:02.040
"What would you do?"

04:02.040 --> 04:03.750
You got a reason through it.

04:03.750 --> 04:06.840
It's like showing your work on a math test in high school.

04:06.840 --> 04:09.660
Man, I sucked at math in high school.

04:09.660 --> 04:11.130
I don't know why I like this job though.

04:11.130 --> 04:12.780
Anyways, that's a really big tip.

04:12.780 --> 04:15.270
If you can internalize that, and you can start thinking

04:15.270 --> 04:17.760
about these things behind the questions,

04:17.760 --> 04:21.000
and asking for more information to make the correct

04:21.000 --> 04:24.660
or at least most correct decision on the answer

04:24.660 --> 04:26.040
then you're gonna go far.

04:26.040 --> 04:28.230
I'll see you guys in the next lecture for some

04:28.230 --> 04:31.683
real insider tips on actually getting that job.

04:33.221 --> 04:35.804
(upbeat music)

response.title: Insider Tips for Getting the Job

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.240 --> 00:02.220
-: What's up, everybody?

00:02.220 --> 00:03.540
All right, fair warning.

00:03.540 --> 00:07.620
This is a longer lecture, but I promise you it is worth it.

00:07.620 --> 00:10.530
I'm gonna let you in on a little bit of a secret here.

00:10.530 --> 00:14.730
This is one of the best ways for getting any job,

00:14.730 --> 00:18.480
but it works especially well for a product management role

00:18.480 --> 00:21.120
and works especially, especially well

00:21.120 --> 00:23.700
for getting your first product management role

00:23.700 --> 00:26.880
in a junior position, which is what you gotta do

00:26.880 --> 00:28.890
if you don't have any experience prior.

00:28.890 --> 00:30.870
We've already talked about how it's very important

00:30.870 --> 00:33.450
to demonstrate knowledge of product management

00:33.450 --> 00:35.340
in order to land the job.

00:35.340 --> 00:37.980
This is why interviews make you demonstrate

00:37.980 --> 00:39.750
this during the interview.

00:39.750 --> 00:41.580
But what about outside of that interview?

00:41.580 --> 00:43.530
Can you do anything to demonstrate

00:43.530 --> 00:46.170
that you really, really know what you're talking about?

00:46.170 --> 00:48.030
Well, if you followed along so far,

00:48.030 --> 00:49.890
you're probably already well underway

00:49.890 --> 00:53.940
to demonstrating knowledge by establishing an online brand

00:53.940 --> 00:55.890
and doing a side project

00:55.890 --> 00:57.930
if you've never had any experience at work before.

00:57.930 --> 01:01.260
But there is one more thing you can do

01:01.260 --> 01:04.350
to maximize your chances at getting the job.

01:04.350 --> 01:07.020
However, I gotta tell you that this is not easy,

01:07.020 --> 01:10.710
this is gonna require some more time and effort from you.

01:10.710 --> 01:14.340
If you aren't serious about getting your dream job

01:14.340 --> 01:17.400
as a product manager and you don't want to go the extra mile

01:17.400 --> 01:20.640
to stand out from the tons of other people

01:20.640 --> 01:22.230
that want that job,

01:22.230 --> 01:24.930
then you don't need to finish this video.

01:24.930 --> 01:27.510
And I am super disappointed in you.

01:27.510 --> 01:29.580
So, go ahead if you're one of those people,

01:29.580 --> 01:30.900
turn off the video.

01:30.900 --> 01:32.700
The rest of us will wait here.

01:32.700 --> 01:33.533
Go ahead.

01:34.800 --> 01:37.110
No? Yeah, go.

01:37.110 --> 01:39.900
Okay, so now that the lazy people are gone,

01:39.900 --> 01:42.180
I'm gonna let the rest of you in on this secret,

01:42.180 --> 01:44.790
but it's not that complex, it's actually pretty simple.

01:44.790 --> 01:47.550
Do a demonstration project for the company

01:47.550 --> 01:49.200
that you're applying to

01:49.200 --> 01:52.500
and send it to the hiring manager and the recruiter.

01:52.500 --> 01:54.630
It's like doing a generic project

01:54.630 --> 01:55.920
that we already talked about,

01:55.920 --> 01:58.590
but it's much more targeted, and that's the key.

01:58.590 --> 02:00.510
You're probably thinking like, "Oh, does this work?

02:00.510 --> 02:02.730
Is this like why am I gonna do this?"

02:02.730 --> 02:03.563
It does work.

02:03.563 --> 02:05.100
In fact, this is how I got

02:05.100 --> 02:07.440
my first few product management jobs

02:07.440 --> 02:11.520
and I have also gotten a ton of offers by doing this

02:11.520 --> 02:14.430
that allowed me to basically choose the company

02:14.430 --> 02:17.070
I wanted to work for instead of having to accept

02:17.070 --> 02:18.750
the first offer that came my way.

02:18.750 --> 02:20.370
So, the basic technique here

02:20.370 --> 02:23.010
is to find out what sorts of things the company

02:23.010 --> 02:26.280
is working on with their product currently

02:26.280 --> 02:29.070
with their strategy and that sort of thing.

02:29.070 --> 02:32.280
Show them how you would approach the problem

02:32.280 --> 02:33.750
if you were to be hired.

02:33.750 --> 02:36.960
You can do this by either doing some generic research

02:36.960 --> 02:38.730
on the company and finding out

02:38.730 --> 02:40.950
through news articles and that sort of thing

02:40.950 --> 02:44.130
what they're trying to build or the challenges they face.

02:44.130 --> 02:46.110
Or you can be even more effective

02:46.110 --> 02:48.150
and ask them some questions

02:48.150 --> 02:50.190
during your first interview with them.

02:50.190 --> 02:52.890
And with all of the work you've done so far in this course

02:52.890 --> 02:56.340
with the online branding and your social media profiles

02:56.340 --> 02:58.080
and just knowing what you're talking about,

02:58.080 --> 03:00.180
it should be pretty easy to get

03:00.180 --> 03:03.330
at least a first round interview or a phone call.

03:03.330 --> 03:04.920
So, here's how I do it,

03:04.920 --> 03:06.180
and you know how I did it

03:06.180 --> 03:07.590
back when I was first starting out.

03:07.590 --> 03:09.420
Let's say that I got a first round interview

03:09.420 --> 03:11.040
for a product management role.

03:11.040 --> 03:13.830
During the first interview, I would answer the questions,

03:13.830 --> 03:17.370
but then I would also ask them why they're hiring,

03:17.370 --> 03:20.070
which features this product position would work on,

03:20.070 --> 03:23.490
and see if they give me any information I could use

03:23.490 --> 03:25.320
on the things that they're trying to build,

03:25.320 --> 03:27.540
or the markets they're trying to get into,

03:27.540 --> 03:30.390
or the clients that they want, that type of thing.

03:30.390 --> 03:31.980
So, what I would do after that,

03:31.980 --> 03:34.500
I would immediately leave, after the interview

03:34.500 --> 03:38.670
I immediately go home and I do research on the product,

03:38.670 --> 03:40.320
the industry, the technology,

03:40.320 --> 03:43.650
and the competition around the stuff that they told me

03:43.650 --> 03:45.030
when I asked them those questions.

03:45.030 --> 03:48.630
Then I would spend at least 24 to 48 hours

03:48.630 --> 03:51.300
developing a comprehensive overview

03:51.300 --> 03:54.060
of how I would approach that problem.

03:54.060 --> 03:56.940
Usually, I would spend an entire weekend doing this,

03:56.940 --> 03:59.790
but, "Cole," you say, "I want to go out with my friends

03:59.790 --> 04:01.830
and drink beer on the weekends,"

04:01.830 --> 04:05.610
or not beer, like I don't know, vodka.

04:05.610 --> 04:07.200
Maybe you should do that instead.

04:07.200 --> 04:10.110
But the other people are going to be doing this extra work

04:10.110 --> 04:13.500
on the weekends in order to put themselves

04:13.500 --> 04:14.940
above the other candidates

04:14.940 --> 04:16.830
and show that they really want that job

04:16.830 --> 04:18.420
and they know what they're talking about.

04:18.420 --> 04:20.760
So, in the past, these projects I've done

04:20.760 --> 04:25.140
have ranged from a small four-page PDF document

04:25.140 --> 04:28.320
all the way to a 60-page slide deck.

04:28.320 --> 04:29.153
I'm not kidding,

04:29.153 --> 04:32.910
I actually put together 60 slides once for a company.

04:32.910 --> 04:34.110
They freaking loved it.

04:34.110 --> 04:36.030
These things usually contain things

04:36.030 --> 04:38.760
like descriptions of the research I did,

04:38.760 --> 04:40.590
the approaches I wouldn't consider,

04:40.590 --> 04:43.050
the technical limitations I've found,

04:43.050 --> 04:45.690
wire frames of potential solutions,

04:45.690 --> 04:49.380
A/B tests I would run, competitive overviews.

04:49.380 --> 04:52.140
and sometimes I even have gone so far

04:52.140 --> 04:55.710
as to find friends of mine that use their product

04:55.710 --> 04:57.600
then I call up those friends and say, "Hey,

04:57.600 --> 04:59.430
can I talk to you for 30 minutes?"

04:59.430 --> 05:01.800
I interview them about their feedback

05:01.800 --> 05:03.990
and their experiences with that product.

05:03.990 --> 05:06.390
I record it, or at least I transcribe it,

05:06.390 --> 05:08.460
and I include those interviews

05:08.460 --> 05:11.430
and the conclusions I drew from those interviews

05:11.430 --> 05:12.990
in that project.

05:12.990 --> 05:15.300
So, once I'm done with the project,

05:15.300 --> 05:18.900
I will usually email the completed document

05:18.900 --> 05:20.610
in like slide deck or PDF

05:20.610 --> 05:22.710
to the recruiter and the hiring manager,

05:22.710 --> 05:24.450
thanking them for the interview,

05:24.450 --> 05:26.880
telling them that I put together some suggestions

05:26.880 --> 05:29.880
to demonstrate my interest in working with them

05:29.880 --> 05:31.380
and what I know about the industry.

05:31.380 --> 05:33.240
So, the silver lining to all of this,

05:33.240 --> 05:35.250
even if it doesn't help you get the job,

05:35.250 --> 05:36.840
is that if you do it

05:36.840 --> 05:40.830
you will get an even better chance at getting the next job.

05:40.830 --> 05:42.570
This is because, first of all,

05:42.570 --> 05:44.550
you've done a lot of product thinking

05:44.550 --> 05:46.200
while you've been putting this thing together.

05:46.200 --> 05:48.690
And trust me, it builds up and it helps,

05:48.690 --> 05:51.750
but mostly because you can just take that case study

05:51.750 --> 05:54.960
and you can just put it on your website or your blog

05:54.960 --> 05:56.790
and then the next recruiter

05:56.790 --> 05:59.640
that talks to you about this position is gonna check it out

05:59.640 --> 06:00.473
and they're gonna say, "Wow

06:00.473 --> 06:02.460
this person really knows their stuff.

06:02.460 --> 06:04.800
Look at all of these case studies they've done

06:04.800 --> 06:06.240
in their free time."

06:06.240 --> 06:07.530
It's gonna be awesome.

06:07.530 --> 06:09.630
On top of that, you can even talk

06:09.630 --> 06:12.510
about these suggestions and case studies

06:12.510 --> 06:14.790
for that company that you didn't get the job for.

06:14.790 --> 06:15.630
You can talk about them

06:15.630 --> 06:18.330
in the next interviews with other companies.

06:18.330 --> 06:20.130
It has so many benefits.

06:20.130 --> 06:23.460
All right, so use this information wisely.

06:23.460 --> 06:26.310
This is really a great technique,

06:26.310 --> 06:28.110
not only for getting a job,

06:28.110 --> 06:31.140
but thinking through that product management mindset.

06:31.140 --> 06:34.290
Like I said, you're gonna have to go through that process

06:34.290 --> 06:37.560
and doing it is just gonna improve your product mind

06:37.560 --> 06:39.840
as you go through it every single time.

06:39.840 --> 06:41.880
Look, if you apply to a company

06:41.880 --> 06:44.700
and you sit back waiting for them to call you

06:44.700 --> 06:45.930
with a job offer

06:45.930 --> 06:49.110
or waiting for them to call you for the next interview,

06:49.110 --> 06:53.490
you're doing the exact same thing as everybody else.

06:53.490 --> 06:56.910
Stand out from the crowd by putting in the effort,

06:56.910 --> 06:59.970
showing them that you really want this position

06:59.970 --> 07:04.170
and demonstrate to them that you've got the skills to do it.

07:04.170 --> 07:07.230
I hope a bunch of you are motivated now with that lecture,

07:07.230 --> 07:08.670
but also the stuff before it.

07:08.670 --> 07:10.650
If you do all of that,

07:10.650 --> 07:13.020
you're gonna be in such a good place

07:13.020 --> 07:17.730
that you're gonna be so far beyond where I was.

07:17.730 --> 07:20.010
I mean, when I first started

07:20.010 --> 07:21.450
I had nearly no experience,

07:21.450 --> 07:25.740
I did not take this course, obviously,

07:25.740 --> 07:26.890
or that would be weird.

07:27.960 --> 07:28.793
At that point,

07:28.793 --> 07:30.960
there were no schools teaching product management.

07:30.960 --> 07:33.300
I had no product management experience,

07:33.300 --> 07:35.820
I kind of had to just figure it out.

07:35.820 --> 07:37.770
But now, you've got someone like me

07:37.770 --> 07:39.930
that's gone through all of this

07:39.930 --> 07:42.090
telling you exactly how to do it.

07:42.090 --> 07:45.900
You are leagues above every single other person

07:45.900 --> 07:47.970
out there trying to get this position.

07:47.970 --> 07:50.640
All right, I hope you enjoyed that lecture.

07:50.640 --> 07:52.980
I know it's a lot of work, but it's totally worth it.

07:52.980 --> 07:55.182
I will see you in the next one.

07:55.182 --> 07:57.765
(upbeat music)

08:14.739 --> 08:16.494
(upbeat music continues)

WEBVTT

00:00.355 --> 00:04.350
(bright upbeat music)

00:04.350 --> 00:05.970
-: What's up everybody?

00:05.970 --> 00:10.970
So this is all about how to excel at the role

00:11.430 --> 00:14.580
once you've already got the product management job.

00:14.580 --> 00:17.010
And because this is all about

00:17.010 --> 00:19.290
how to excel after you've already got the job,

00:19.290 --> 00:21.030
I'm assuming you've got the job,

00:21.030 --> 00:24.450
and in that case, congratulations.

00:24.450 --> 00:26.010
I am so happy for you.

00:26.010 --> 00:29.040
It brings a tear to my eye.

00:29.040 --> 00:31.140
And if only there were a way that I could

00:31.140 --> 00:35.490
through the screen congratulate you or make you happy.

00:35.490 --> 00:37.050
How about this bear suit?

00:37.050 --> 00:41.190
I will teach the rest of this lecture in this bear suit

00:41.190 --> 00:45.660
with these claws because you make me so happy

00:45.660 --> 00:47.850
to be a product management instructor.

00:47.850 --> 00:48.990
You got the job.

00:48.990 --> 00:52.380
So once you've got your first product management job,

00:52.380 --> 00:54.810
it can be a little bit daunting

00:54.810 --> 00:56.760
trying to figure out what the first thing is

00:56.760 --> 00:57.593
that you should do,

00:57.593 --> 00:59.520
but bear with me here.

00:59.520 --> 01:01.860
Product management is notorious
