01:01.860 --> 01:05.160
for having one of the longest ramp up times

01:05.160 --> 01:07.020
for a lot of jobs out there.

01:07.020 --> 01:11.610
By that I mean, it's really usually taking a lot of time

01:11.610 --> 01:15.060
to get completely comfortable with the role.

01:15.060 --> 01:16.710
This is because the work you do

01:16.710 --> 01:19.650
involves so many different teams

01:19.650 --> 01:22.860
and you need to understand the history and goals of each one

01:22.860 --> 01:24.660
to do the best work.

01:24.660 --> 01:27.510
What I'm gonna do here is give you some tips

01:27.510 --> 01:31.920
on things to do as soon as you get the job and start work.

01:31.920 --> 01:35.250
These tips will make you very much more effective

01:35.250 --> 01:36.390
at your role.

01:36.390 --> 01:37.800
All right, so tip number one,

01:37.800 --> 01:40.230
schedule one on ones with everyone in the team.

01:40.230 --> 01:41.460
If you're at a large company,

01:41.460 --> 01:43.860
you don't have to meet with literally everybody,

01:43.860 --> 01:46.200
but be sure that you at least meet

01:46.200 --> 01:48.570
with the leaders of each major team.

01:48.570 --> 01:52.530
Ask them what their goals are and what challenges they have.

01:52.530 --> 01:56.070
Take notes so that you can refer back later.

01:56.070 --> 01:58.920
All right, step two is arrange a meeting

01:58.920 --> 02:01.530
with the lead engineer on your team

02:01.530 --> 02:03.150
and talk about the technical stuff.

02:03.150 --> 02:06.180
Have them explain to you how the architecture looks

02:06.180 --> 02:08.520
on the back end in detail

02:08.520 --> 02:11.100
and what sorts of technologies they use,

02:11.100 --> 02:14.220
like databases and languages and that sort of thing.

02:14.220 --> 02:18.180
Also, ask them what the biggest technical challenges are

02:18.180 --> 02:20.700
and make sure you understand the basics

02:20.700 --> 02:22.500
of how data is stored,

02:22.500 --> 02:25.050
and again, what languages they use

02:25.050 --> 02:26.880
and what the sticking points are,

02:26.880 --> 02:28.200
what they're having trouble with.

02:28.200 --> 02:30.210
This will just help you build a great relationship

02:30.210 --> 02:31.410
with the engineers,

02:31.410 --> 02:34.260
and it will make sure that you know

02:34.260 --> 02:35.640
what you're getting yourself into

02:35.640 --> 02:37.350
if you're gonna try to change something major

02:37.350 --> 02:38.940
on the back end with a feature.

02:38.940 --> 02:42.390
All right, so the next tip is start talking to users.

02:42.390 --> 02:43.920
When you joined a lot of companies,

02:43.920 --> 02:48.270
it's gonna appear as if they know exactly what users want

02:48.270 --> 02:50.370
and even show you user feedback

02:50.370 --> 02:51.930
that they've received in the past

02:51.930 --> 02:53.490
or over the last couple of years.

02:53.490 --> 02:55.890
However, it's extremely important,

02:55.890 --> 03:00.240
or should I say, beary important to talk to your users

03:00.240 --> 03:03.720
and get the best idea of what's important to them.

03:03.720 --> 03:05.820
You wanna see this yourself

03:05.820 --> 03:08.790
because you may draw some different conclusions.

03:08.790 --> 03:11.430
After all, you're a brilliant product thinker.

03:11.430 --> 03:12.750
Some things you can do here

03:12.750 --> 03:16.680
are ask to join in on a sales call, go to sales meetings,

03:16.680 --> 03:19.440
spend some time with the customer service team

03:19.440 --> 03:21.540
helping them answer user questions

03:21.540 --> 03:24.060
and tickets that people send in for support.

03:24.060 --> 03:26.220
Things of this nature is what you're going for.

03:26.220 --> 03:28.140
I also like to do a quick Google search

03:28.140 --> 03:30.600
for things that people say about the company

03:30.600 --> 03:34.920
on the social websites like the Twitter and the Reddit.

03:34.920 --> 03:38.580
Number four, my bear head is falling off,

03:38.580 --> 03:42.570
but read every internal document you can get your hands on.

03:42.570 --> 03:47.070
I recommend, or you ask or find documentation for the epics

03:47.070 --> 03:50.340
and mobile releases that have been made in the past,

03:50.340 --> 03:52.710
as well as things like sales decks

03:52.710 --> 03:54.780
and internal presentations.

03:54.780 --> 03:56.550
All of these things are gonna help you

03:56.550 --> 03:59.580
get a great historical view of the product

03:59.580 --> 04:02.280
and what has happened over time.

04:02.280 --> 04:05.070
Tip number five, look at the data.

04:05.070 --> 04:06.720
As soon as you have the chance,

04:06.720 --> 04:09.150
you should take a look at the existing metrics

04:09.150 --> 04:10.170
for the product.

04:10.170 --> 04:13.770
If there's a data science team or any data analysts,

04:13.770 --> 04:15.690
ask to have a meeting with them

04:15.690 --> 04:19.050
where they describe the types of data that are available

04:19.050 --> 04:20.520
in terms of metrics

04:20.520 --> 04:24.630
and any plans they have around implementing more tracking.

04:24.630 --> 04:27.030
Sometimes you might not think there's enough tracking

04:27.030 --> 04:28.620
to make the best decisions,

04:28.620 --> 04:30.810
and you'll have to make an effort with your team

04:30.810 --> 04:31.860
to track more.

04:31.860 --> 04:35.820
Tip number six, meet with the boss.

04:35.820 --> 04:38.100
You should make it a top priority

04:38.100 --> 04:41.100
to have a meeting with either the head of product

04:41.100 --> 04:44.790
or the CEO to ask them about expectations for your role,

04:44.790 --> 04:47.610
but also to understand their goals.

04:47.610 --> 04:49.620
Make sure what you talk about

04:49.620 --> 04:53.820
is related to what metrics they're trying to optimize for

04:53.820 --> 04:57.030
and what they'd really like for you to do in this role.

04:57.030 --> 04:58.680
What are their biggest problems?

04:58.680 --> 05:01.800
Like what are they fearful of over the next year

05:01.800 --> 05:04.410
in terms of stuff that's gonna be challenging

05:04.410 --> 05:06.480
or maybe what competitors are doing?

05:06.480 --> 05:08.460
You can also ask them about things like

05:08.460 --> 05:11.760
process improvements internally that need to be made

05:11.760 --> 05:14.340
or improving marketing.

05:14.340 --> 05:16.620
These are all things that you can help out with

05:16.620 --> 05:18.360
as a product manager.

05:18.360 --> 05:20.340
All right, with those tips,

05:20.340 --> 05:22.980
you're going to be in a very good position

05:22.980 --> 05:27.980
to do the best work as soon as possible in your new role.

05:28.140 --> 05:30.270
Like my claws are sharp,

05:30.270 --> 05:32.730
and I do mean my claws because I am a bear,

05:32.730 --> 05:36.810
your product management skillsets will be sharp,

05:36.810 --> 05:39.360
your knowledge will be sharp starting this job.

05:39.360 --> 05:41.580
It's super important that you do this.

05:41.580 --> 05:44.760
And I've done this in all of the companies I've worked for.

05:44.760 --> 05:46.800
It's been really huge.

05:46.800 --> 05:49.503
All right, everybody, hope you enjoyed that one.

WEBVTT

00:00.150 --> 00:04.500
-: Hey everybody. This is the end,

00:04.500 --> 00:08.970
but only the end of the course, not the end of your journey

00:08.970 --> 00:11.460
into the world of product management.

00:11.460 --> 00:13.316
Evan and I have included a ton

00:13.316 --> 00:17.940
of reference material and content, and as you know

00:17.940 --> 00:20.730
we've got the interviews available to you

00:20.730 --> 00:23.850
which are very good, but we'd really like

00:23.850 --> 00:26.790
to add a lot more content to this course.

00:26.790 --> 00:31.080
If there's any feedback you have, I would love to see it

00:31.080 --> 00:34.500
in the feedback section or just email me

00:34.500 --> 00:37.512
or Evan and say, "Yo, I wanna learn about this"

00:37.512 --> 00:40.590
and we'll make a lecture and we'll put it up there

00:40.590 --> 00:41.940
and we wanna make sure everyone's got

00:41.940 --> 00:44.190
a really good grasp on this, and we're,

00:44.190 --> 00:46.037
we're totally open to clarifying

00:46.037 --> 00:50.070
or redoing some stuff to make it make sense for you.

00:50.070 --> 00:53.530
The last thing I want to say before we get outta here

00:53.530 --> 00:57.240
is that something really fundamental to the role

00:57.240 --> 01:02.039
of product management is that it is not so much

01:02.039 --> 01:07.039
a set of skills that makes you a product manager,

01:07.350 --> 01:09.810
you do have to have these skills, don't get me wrong

01:09.810 --> 01:11.451
and we've already talked about them,

01:11.451 --> 01:15.300
but you really need to have a mindset.

01:15.300 --> 01:19.522
That mindset is all about proving things through data,

01:19.522 --> 01:22.923
making the best decisions based on the information

01:22.923 --> 01:27.060
available, getting more information if you need it,

01:27.060 --> 01:31.830
and just generally thinking about things in a deeper way

01:31.830 --> 01:34.320
than just through your gut feeling.

01:34.320 --> 01:36.450
Remember, as a product manager,

01:36.450 --> 01:41.450
you are almost always wrong and so is everyone else.

01:41.700 --> 01:45.212
That is until you can prove that your assumptions

01:45.212 --> 01:50.070
are not wrong through data and through talking to users.

01:50.070 --> 01:53.520
Lastly, I wanna say that this is the coolest job ever

01:53.520 --> 01:56.820
and I'm so glad, along with Evan, that you guys have

01:56.820 --> 01:59.578
joined us through this journey and learned so much

01:59.578 --> 02:03.570
about what we really, really like doing.

02:03.570 --> 02:06.850
Please feel free to reach out, to contact us, email us,

02:06.850 --> 02:09.361
write us whatever, Tweet at us.

02:09.361 --> 02:12.917
Twitter's kind of cool, Snapchat us, whatevs,

02:12.917 --> 02:16.230
and yeah, just see how you're doing.

02:16.230 --> 02:18.714
If you get a job or if this was helpful for you at all

02:18.714 --> 02:23.044
let us know and we're there to answer questions for you

02:23.044 --> 02:26.610
in the future. All right, everybody, I'll see you later.

02:26.610 --> 02:29.820
Thanks so much for taking this course.

02:29.820 --> 02:31.500
Alright, the course is over.

02:31.500 --> 02:34.143
Go ahead and turn off the video.

02:35.550 --> 02:36.933
Yep. Nope, stop the video.

02:37.920 --> 02:38.753
I gotta go.

02:38.753 --> 02:42.610
I got places to go and things to do, so

02:44.520 --> 02:45.353
Turn it off.

02:49.170 --> 02:50.313
Turn it off.

02:54.480 --> 02:55.313
Alright.

response.description:
We sat down and talked with Daniel Demetri, the Product Lead at MeetErnest.com, a startup trying to disrupt the student loan industry. He currently leads a team of eight and is in charge of hiring and growing their Product Management team.

Daniel has had a long history in Product Management. Before Ernest he was a Product Manager at Google getting the job through the very competitive Associate Product Manager program.

We talked about all things Product Management.

Topics:

  • APM program at Google (1’)
  • What was your biggest misconceived notion? (2:31)
  • Difference between the types of PMs (4:52)
  • Stories from the trenches of Google (5:43’)
  • Do Users really know what they want? (7’)
  • Stories from the trenches at Ernest (7:45)
  • Fix it or Replace it (10:53)
  • Google vs Apple in Product Management style (13’)
  • Hiring at Ernest (16’)
  • Alternative ways of getting into PM (17’)
  • Getting Executives attention (22’)
  • Building portfolios pre-hire (24')
response.title: Interview with Daniel Demetri, Product Lead @ Earnest, Ex-PM @ Google

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:02.250 --> 00:04.667
(Jazz music)

00:11.101 --> 00:13.477
(laughing)

00:13.477 --> 00:16.767
(indistinct chatter)

00:16.767 --> 00:18.746
-: Yeah. So I'm here with Daniel Dimitri,

00:18.746 --> 00:20.736
the head of product at Earnest.

00:20.736 --> 00:23.158
And could you tell us a little bit about you,

00:23.158 --> 00:24.283
yourself and your background?

00:24.283 --> 00:28.385
-: Yeah, so I was a computer science major at Harvard.

00:28.385 --> 00:31.751
Studied that undergrad in economics.

00:31.751 --> 00:34.163
Worked in private equity for a year and a half,

00:34.163 --> 00:35.818
and then applied to Google's

00:35.818 --> 00:37.772
associate product manager program.

00:37.772 --> 00:39.391
Got in and started in New York,

00:39.391 --> 00:40.891
moved out here and

00:42.549 --> 00:44.041
worked in ads a bit,

00:44.041 --> 00:45.976
and was, sorry in maps.

00:45.976 --> 00:48.460
Was on the maps team for two years at Google.

00:48.460 --> 00:50.518
And then I left and joined Earnest,

00:50.518 --> 00:53.389
and I've been there for a year and a half.

00:53.389 --> 00:54.222
-: Cool.

00:54.222 --> 00:55.181
-: I was their first product manager

00:55.181 --> 00:56.693
and now I lead a team of eight.

00:56.693 --> 00:57.526
-: Yeah.

00:57.526 --> 00:58.359
Tell us about the APM program

00:58.359 --> 01:00.968
because this is something that not everyone knows about,

01:00.968 --> 01:03.827
the associate product manager program at Google.

01:03.827 --> 01:04.660
-: Yeah.

01:04.660 --> 01:06.128
Well, the Associate Product Manager Program at Google

01:06.128 --> 01:08.520
is a amazing two year program.

01:08.520 --> 01:11.172
They take 30 people a year globally.

01:11.172 --> 01:14.150
Typically, well, it's zero to three years out of college,

01:14.150 --> 01:15.747
but it's typically zero.

01:15.747 --> 01:17.765
They take computer scientists.

01:17.765 --> 01:20.672
They train people up into the nebulous craft

01:20.672 --> 01:22.290
of product management.

01:22.290 --> 01:24.064
And most, I think that they had about

01:24.064 --> 01:27.762
50% retention within five years.

01:27.762 --> 01:30.315
So they do a pretty good job retaining talent

01:30.315 --> 01:32.123
for quite a while.

01:32.123 --> 01:34.342
And they expose you to different parts

01:34.342 --> 01:35.578
of the Google ecosystem.

01:35.578 --> 01:37.219
So you can see ads, you can see search,

01:37.219 --> 01:39.962
you can see Google Plus, back in the day.

01:39.962 --> 01:40.903
-: Do you get a choice on like

01:40.903 --> 01:41.907
where you want to go?

01:41.907 --> 01:46.530
-: Yeah, so I remember my first request was like,

01:46.530 --> 01:48.152
"Please don't put me in ads."

01:48.152 --> 01:49.259
-: Why?

01:49.259 --> 01:50.092
-: And they were like,

01:50.092 --> 01:51.222
"Well, we can't promise anything.

01:51.222 --> 01:53.089
We'll see what happens."

01:53.089 --> 01:55.770
And then I showed up on my first day,

01:55.770 --> 01:57.668
and they have this session

01:57.668 --> 02:00.035
where all the trainees or the recruits of the crop

02:00.035 --> 02:02.507
sort of get picked up by their bosses.

02:02.507 --> 02:06.870
And I get basically my first meeting with my first boss.

02:06.870 --> 02:08.987
And I'm like, "So what do you do?"

02:08.987 --> 02:10.042
I mean, what do we do?"

02:10.042 --> 02:10.875
And he's like,

02:10.875 --> 02:12.008
"Well, I'm the director of the ad exchange."

02:12.008 --> 02:13.318
And I'm like, my face fell.

02:13.318 --> 02:15.904
And I was like, "I'm gonna be in ads, this is the worst."

02:15.904 --> 02:17.644
And they like totally disregarded,

02:17.644 --> 02:20.649
voluntarily disregarded my request to not be on ads.

02:20.649 --> 02:21.778
-: You had one job request.

02:21.778 --> 02:22.611
-: Yeah.

02:22.611 --> 02:23.830
And I actually loved being on ads.

02:23.830 --> 02:26.669
Your second year you have a little bit more influence as to

02:26.669 --> 02:27.502
what you do.

02:27.502 --> 02:28.716
And then throughout the rest of your career,

02:28.716 --> 02:30.179
Google has quite a bit of mobility, so.

02:30.179 --> 02:31.421
-: Yeah.

02:31.421 --> 02:35.038
So before you got into product management and you,

02:35.038 --> 02:37.405
before you were in APM program, like what was

02:37.405 --> 02:38.351
your notion,

02:38.351 --> 02:41.244
your preconceived notion on like what product management was

02:41.244 --> 02:43.395
and what was, why were you interested in doing it?

02:43.395 --> 02:46.164
-: Great question. I had no idea what it was.

02:46.164 --> 02:47.000
My friends...

02:47.000 --> 02:48.911
So I had a friend who was a product manager at Google

02:48.911 --> 02:50.690
and I asked him,

02:50.690 --> 02:54.037
what do you learn as a product manager?

02:54.037 --> 02:56.084
Like what do you become?

02:56.084 --> 02:58.586
And he was basically like, well,

02:58.586 --> 03:00.011
you learn how to get things done.

03:00.011 --> 03:02.698
I'm like, that's super vague, come on.

03:02.698 --> 03:06.256
But he didn't really do a better job at explaining it.

03:06.256 --> 03:09.653
I felt that the role was going to be to marry the business

03:09.653 --> 03:12.385
and the tech sides and being a joint kind of computer

03:12.385 --> 03:14.498
science and economics person, that really appealed to me.

03:14.498 --> 03:16.869
I knew that I liked being engaged with technology,

03:16.869 --> 03:21.289
but I also felt that I didn't want to be coding.

03:21.289 --> 03:22.122
-: Right.

03:22.122 --> 03:24.726
-: And so thinking about technology, but also not coding and,

03:24.726 --> 03:27.308
but being engaged with the coding and the coders.

03:27.308 --> 03:28.141
-: Right.

03:28.141 --> 03:28.980
-: That felt really appealing to me.

03:28.980 --> 03:29.813
-: Yeah.

03:29.813 --> 03:30.733
-: I wasn't really sure what it was.

03:30.733 --> 03:31.875
I just knew that Google's a great place to work.

03:31.875 --> 03:32.708
-: Yeah.

03:32.708 --> 03:33.541
-: And it seemed like a great program.

03:33.541 --> 03:34.725
And so I felt like I'd try it out.

03:34.725 --> 03:37.449
-: So now that you have done it for some time and you're head

03:37.449 --> 03:38.930
of product to Earnest,

03:38.930 --> 03:42.578
if you were to summarize it in some way, like a short way,

03:42.578 --> 03:44.020
which we've actually,

03:44.020 --> 03:46.100
we've had like a whole lecture on this in the course as

03:46.100 --> 03:48.557
well, like summarizing product management.

03:48.557 --> 03:49.390
-: Right.

03:49.390 --> 03:50.520
-: How would you summarize it?

03:50.520 --> 03:51.353
-: Right.

03:51.353 --> 03:53.620
I mean, I think that one better way to think

03:53.620 --> 03:55.817
anything's done might be to fill the gaps

03:55.817 --> 03:58.106
between all of the cross-functional teams.

03:58.106 --> 04:02.269
So everyone in the company is working to make the product a

04:02.269 --> 04:03.975
success in some fashion,

04:03.975 --> 04:06.900
but there tends to be a lot of translation that needs to

04:06.900 --> 04:11.900
occur between engineering and in our case, capital markets.

04:12.279 --> 04:15.762
Because we're lending money out and marketing and PR

04:15.762 --> 04:18.892
and like all these functions need to collaborate,

04:18.892 --> 04:22.260
creating a shared sense of timeline,

04:22.260 --> 04:24.473
creating shared sense of what is being delivered

04:24.473 --> 04:25.990
or when or why,

04:25.990 --> 04:28.714
is sort of core to the product role.

04:28.714 --> 04:31.278
And that sort of manifests in product requirements.

04:31.278 --> 04:32.757
It manifests in roadmaps.

04:32.757 --> 04:34.340
And so I view like,

04:36.093 --> 04:38.776
as a simplified version or defining

04:38.776 --> 04:40.458
what product management is,

04:40.458 --> 04:42.422
I'd say it's something about filling the gaps between the

04:42.422 --> 04:43.500
cross-functional roles.

04:43.500 --> 04:44.365
-: Cool.

04:44.365 --> 04:47.316
We actually actually just got done filming a lecture about

04:47.316 --> 04:49.519
the different types of product management roles

04:49.519 --> 04:50.555
in different companies.

04:50.555 --> 04:51.388
-: Yeah.

04:51.388 --> 04:53.040
-: Actually referring to your blog post,

04:53.040 --> 04:54.069
it's a good blog post.

04:54.069 --> 04:54.952
-: Thank you. Thank you.

04:54.952 --> 04:56.358
I'm excited about it, yeah.

04:56.358 --> 04:57.191
-: Yeah.

04:57.191 --> 04:58.605
Except you have like a different,

04:58.605 --> 05:00.951
probably a more thorough labeling, but

05:00.951 --> 05:03.283
we talk basically about internal product management,

05:03.283 --> 05:06.668
where your stakeholders are internal and then, you know,

05:06.668 --> 05:08.489
business to business and enterprise product managers.

05:08.489 --> 05:09.322
-: Yes.

05:09.322 --> 05:10.261
-: And then just consumer product managers.

05:10.261 --> 05:11.094
-: Yes.

05:11.094 --> 05:11.927
They're very different.

05:11.927 --> 05:13.549
And I think in like resume screening,

05:13.549 --> 05:15.364
I think that like hiring managers

05:15.364 --> 05:18.621
definitely are taking a view as to

05:18.621 --> 05:20.778
oh, that they're that type of product manager.

05:20.778 --> 05:21.611
-: Right.

05:21.611 --> 05:23.874
-: And so one of the great things about Google was that it's

05:23.874 --> 05:25.713
such a broad conglomerate at this point.

05:25.713 --> 05:28.575
You can seamlessly transition between the teams.

05:28.575 --> 05:29.408
-: Right.

05:29.408 --> 05:30.616
-: And allow your functional,

05:30.616 --> 05:35.616
or like your core expertise in computer science or just

05:35.618 --> 05:37.743
academic pedigree generally speaking,

05:37.743 --> 05:40.142
to allow you to transition at a junior level between teams

05:40.142 --> 05:41.540
with actually very different styles.

05:41.540 --> 05:42.373
-: Right.

05:42.373 --> 05:43.254
-: So I worked on Double Click,

05:43.254 --> 05:45.571
very B2B sales driven environment,

05:45.571 --> 05:47.796
which is like features prioritized on

05:47.796 --> 05:49.382
what customers were asking for.

05:49.382 --> 05:50.721
But then I switched to maps,

05:50.721 --> 05:51.818
was entirely different.

05:51.818 --> 05:52.924
It's logs analysis,

05:52.924 --> 05:54.892
What are clients...

05:54.892 --> 05:56.003
I'm stuck in the Earnest.

05:56.003 --> 05:56.836
-: Right.

05:56.836 --> 05:58.418
-: What are users of Google Maps really doing?

05:58.418 --> 05:59.251
-: Right.

05:59.251 --> 06:01.862
-: And analyzing that and then prioritizing and

06:01.862 --> 06:05.393
bug closings and feature requests.

06:05.393 --> 06:06.226
-: Yeah.

06:06.226 --> 06:08.649
-: And so then you can go out from Google or from another

06:08.649 --> 06:13.056
program to sort of address kind of any business need.

06:13.056 --> 06:13.889
-: Yeah.

06:13.889 --> 06:14.722
-: And so that's a really,

06:14.722 --> 06:16.260
that's a great reason to start off a product management

06:16.260 --> 06:17.431
career at a big company.

06:17.431 --> 06:18.264
-: Yeah.

06:18.264 --> 06:20.285
-: Because as you were pointing out earlier in the course,

06:20.285 --> 06:21.224
there's so many different types.

06:21.224 --> 06:23.577
They do very different things to have different values.

06:23.577 --> 06:24.410
-: Right.

06:24.410 --> 06:26.268
-: So getting to experience both is very valuable

06:26.268 --> 06:27.101
for one's career.

06:27.101 --> 06:27.934
-: Yeah.

06:27.934 --> 06:31.210
I've talked to a number of Facebook PMs that apparently

06:31.210 --> 06:34.010
Facebook has a rotation program on,

06:34.010 --> 06:36.202
you're an early product manager.

06:36.202 --> 06:39.187
I think you don't even have a choice but to rotate between

06:39.187 --> 06:40.809
departments or products.

06:40.809 --> 06:41.655
-: Yes.

06:41.655 --> 06:43.701
-: I think like you have to go from like timeline to photos

06:43.701 --> 06:46.025
to whatever and get a good handle

06:46.025 --> 06:47.406
on like all of these things.

06:47.406 --> 06:48.239
-: Right.

06:48.239 --> 06:49.933
-: And it keeps the ideas sort of fresh.

06:49.933 --> 06:51.784
You don't get like a tunnel vision on the,

06:51.784 --> 06:52.702
vision for that one product.

06:52.702 --> 06:53.535
-: Yeah.

06:53.535 --> 06:54.398
-: So that's interesting.

06:54.398 --> 06:56.494
-: Yeah. Google's APM program is not dissimilar.

06:56.494 --> 06:57.327
-: Oh. Really?

06:57.327 --> 06:58.160
-: Yeah.

06:58.160 --> 06:59.534
They, you can convince the leadership

06:59.534 --> 07:01.695
to allow you to stay in a similar area.

07:01.695 --> 07:02.528
-: Right.

07:02.528 --> 07:04.172
-: But they do wanna see you switch your perspective.

07:04.172 --> 07:05.005
-: Right.

07:05.005 --> 07:05.838
-: Yeah.

07:05.838 --> 07:08.355
-: So speaking of features, you said feature requests,

07:08.355 --> 07:11.551
we have a whole section in the course as well on,

07:11.551 --> 07:15.676
so you have ideas and you have feature requests and then,

07:15.676 --> 07:18.631
there's this need to get to the core of a user need

07:18.631 --> 07:22.113
rather than just taking feature requests at face value.

07:22.113 --> 07:22.946
-: Yes.

07:22.946 --> 07:23.779
-: So there's a, I mean,

07:23.779 --> 07:25.607
in the course I talk about a number of times where

07:25.607 --> 07:29.617
I've had a feature request and I do two or three levels

07:29.617 --> 07:32.073
of digging and you find out that there's something,

07:32.073 --> 07:33.946
that there's a,

07:33.946 --> 07:35.537
that that was a symptom,

07:35.537 --> 07:36.370
-: Yes.

07:36.370 --> 07:37.701
-: And that there's more of like a disease somewhere else.

07:37.701 --> 07:38.534
-: Yes.

07:38.534 --> 07:39.633
And I can think of a million instances.

07:39.633 --> 07:40.466
Right?

07:40.466 --> 07:41.299
-: Could you...

07:41.299 --> 07:42.132
Yeah.

07:42.132 --> 07:43.941
Could you tell us about any of these, these instances?

07:43.941 --> 07:46.276
Because this practical real world like storytelling is

07:46.276 --> 07:47.429
really useful.

07:47.429 --> 07:48.262
-: Sure. Yeah.

07:48.262 --> 07:51.012
So I mean right now this week we,

07:52.442 --> 07:54.481
so Earnest is a consumer lender.

07:54.481 --> 07:57.538
We do student loan refinancing and personal loans.

07:57.538 --> 08:00.758
We have an internal team that does some of the loan

08:00.758 --> 08:04.412
processing and they have gotten to the habit reviewing raw

08:04.412 --> 08:06.707
documents that have been provided by the client.

08:06.707 --> 08:08.786
Think like a tax form.

08:08.786 --> 08:12.277
And so they've gotten to the habit of, okay,

08:12.277 --> 08:14.826
I'm gonna look at these tax forms and then I'm going to make

08:14.826 --> 08:15.784
a bunch of calculated

08:15.784 --> 08:18.113
subjective judgments based off of that.

08:18.113 --> 08:20.180
And then I'll using those subjective judgment

08:20.180 --> 08:21.363
put in a decision.

08:21.363 --> 08:23.691
And so they ask for features that are like,

08:23.691 --> 08:26.359
I wanna be able to pull all the documents more quickly.

08:26.359 --> 08:28.190
When in reality if you're thinking

08:28.190 --> 08:29.652
kind of a little bit ahead,

08:29.652 --> 08:32.039
what you wanna do is extract the important information off

08:32.039 --> 08:34.619
those documents and then have the calculations run that

08:34.619 --> 08:36.798
judge the applications quality.

08:36.798 --> 08:37.631
-: Right.

08:37.631 --> 08:40.189
-: And so you don't wanna build features that are going to

08:40.189 --> 08:42.946
make it easier to suck all the applic,

08:42.946 --> 08:44.252
like make it easier to review PDFs faster.

08:44.252 --> 08:45.085
-: Right.

08:45.085 --> 08:47.352
-: You wanna build the extraction mechanism and then automate

08:47.352 --> 08:49.314
kind of a lot of the logic that they're doing.

08:49.314 --> 08:50.147
-: Right.

08:50.147 --> 08:51.137
-: It's a classic problem when you're dealing with an

08:51.137 --> 08:52.125
operations team.

08:52.125 --> 08:52.958
-: Yeah.

08:52.958 --> 08:54.453
-: It's like that balance between automating their job and

08:54.453 --> 08:55.912
making their jobs just more efficient.

08:55.912 --> 08:56.745
-: Yeah.

08:56.745 --> 08:59.328
-: In other circumstances, you know,

08:59.328 --> 09:01.914
you think that you can get away with great examples on

09:01.914 --> 09:02.800
Google Maps.

09:02.800 --> 09:05.062
We tried to get away with a removing a feature called

09:05.062 --> 09:06.273
Search Nearby.

09:06.273 --> 09:10.020
So, Search nearby is a feature where you would search for

09:10.020 --> 09:11.759
Gary Danko restaurant,

09:11.759 --> 09:13.607
maybe you're going on a date there,

09:13.607 --> 09:16.706
and then you wanna find a bar next door for afterward

09:16.706 --> 09:17.715
to meet up with friends.

09:17.715 --> 09:19.344
So you would click search for Gary Danko and then click

09:19.344 --> 09:20.917
search nearby and then search for bars.

09:20.917 --> 09:22.608
And we thought, well, can't people just type

09:22.608 --> 09:24.234
in bars near Gary Danko?

09:24.234 --> 09:25.067
-: Right.

09:25.067 --> 09:25.900
-: Or just search for Gary Danko

09:25.900 --> 09:27.147
and then the map will move

09:27.147 --> 09:28.433
and then they can search for bars.

09:28.433 --> 09:29.307
-: Right.

09:29.307 --> 09:30.140
-: But then,

09:30.140 --> 09:34.183
and the opposite to people requesting features is

09:34.183 --> 09:35.716
the opposite would be removing features people

09:35.716 --> 09:36.809
aren't worried about.

09:36.809 --> 09:37.642
-: Right.

09:37.642 --> 09:38.722
-: And fixing what ain't broken kind of can lead to.

09:38.722 --> 09:40.959
We removed this feature search nearby and got,

09:40.959 --> 09:43.082
this became our biggest chunk

09:43.082 --> 09:46.451
of customer feedback at Google.

09:46.451 --> 09:48.077
And the maps team by-

09:48.077 --> 09:48.910
-: By removing that?

09:48.910 --> 09:49.743
-: By removing that.

09:49.743 --> 09:50.576
-: Yeah.

09:50.576 --> 09:52.155
-: The consumer operations team is the team at Google.

09:52.155 --> 09:54.096
That sort aggregates customer feedback.

09:54.096 --> 09:56.674
Our user feedback and-

09:56.674 --> 09:57.912
-: Oh, there's a whole team.

09:57.912 --> 09:58.924
-: There's a whole team.

09:58.924 --> 09:59.757
-: Wow.

09:59.757 --> 10:02.013
-: That clusters the user feedback.

10:02.013 --> 10:04.417
I think very few people outside of Google are even aware

10:04.417 --> 10:05.407
that this happens.

10:05.407 --> 10:06.240
-: Yeah.

10:06.240 --> 10:08.325
-: Because Google has a habit of not really responding

10:08.325 --> 10:09.158
-: Yeah.

10:09.158 --> 10:09.991
-: To user feedback.

10:09.991 --> 10:11.175
In some circumstances they do,

10:11.175 --> 10:14.754
but actually the user feedback that's clustered by a team in

10:14.754 --> 10:17.273
India and then the product managers reviewed the clusters.

10:17.273 --> 10:18.106
-: Right.

10:18.106 --> 10:20.171
-: It's the only way to digest this information on.

10:20.171 --> 10:21.004
-: Sure.

10:21.004 --> 10:23.235
There's still probably just so much, so much information.

10:23.235 --> 10:24.820
-: Exactly. So the top cluster quickly became,

10:24.820 --> 10:26.075
where does search nearby go?

10:26.075 --> 10:26.908
-: Right.

10:26.908 --> 10:27.741
-: And we're like oh shoot.

10:27.741 --> 10:28.574
-: That's awesome.

10:28.574 --> 10:29.407
-: To redo this.

10:29.407 --> 10:30.240
-: Yeah.

10:30.240 --> 10:31.073
This is...

10:31.073 --> 10:33.705
I met a guy that was a PM of bugs

10:33.705 --> 10:34.538
at Facebook.

10:34.538 --> 10:35.458
So there's a person just-

10:35.458 --> 10:36.291
-: Wow.

10:36.291 --> 10:37.169
-: That they have like one person,

10:37.169 --> 10:38.788
or I guess many people in charge of bugs,

10:38.788 --> 10:40.112
but it's the same thing.

10:40.112 --> 10:42.209
They just have such a volume at like one and a half billion

10:42.209 --> 10:45.933
that they, you just can't look at bug reports.

10:45.933 --> 10:46.766
-: Right.

10:46.766 --> 10:48.224
-: Like you have to do some analysis on the bug reports.

10:48.224 --> 10:49.057
-: Right.

10:49.057 --> 10:50.537
-: Because there's hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of

10:50.537 --> 10:51.396
thousands of them coming in.

10:51.396 --> 10:52.312
Right?
-: Yes.

10:52.312 --> 10:53.640
-: There's no way you could ever read them.

10:53.640 --> 10:55.387
-: Which is another classic trade off product managers have

10:55.387 --> 10:57.906
to make is between closing bugs and working on new features.

10:57.906 --> 11:00.168
Especially when those features are going to render obsolete.

11:00.168 --> 11:01.643
Some of the bugs have been been filed.

11:01.643 --> 11:02.476
-: Yeah.

11:02.476 --> 11:03.309
-: And you're-

11:03.309 --> 11:04.392
-: More features for that matter.

11:04.392 --> 11:05.225
Yeah. Feature.

11:05.225 --> 11:06.058
-: That's right.

11:06.058 --> 11:07.044
Or we could add that check box,

11:07.044 --> 11:08.661
but eventually the whole page is gonna go away.

11:08.661 --> 11:09.494
-: Right.

11:09.494 --> 11:10.792
-: So what-

11:10.792 --> 11:13.107
-: I think you're ignoring them.

11:13.107 --> 11:14.386
-: Yes, it's the worst when people,

11:14.386 --> 11:16.453
I mean most product managers are probably hated in some

11:16.453 --> 11:18.261
respect in their companies because they're the

11:18.261 --> 11:20.934
no people or the like not yet people or not now.

11:20.934 --> 11:21.767
-: Always.

11:21.767 --> 11:22.600
-: That's not a priority.

11:22.600 --> 11:23.433
-: Right.

11:23.433 --> 11:24.266
That's the classic.

11:24.266 --> 11:25.099
-: Yeah.

11:25.099 --> 11:25.932
-: We'll prioritize it.

11:25.932 --> 11:26.765
-: Yeah.

11:26.765 --> 11:28.011
Well yeah, that's P four, P three.

11:28.011 --> 11:29.856
Well we'll get, that's a next quarter thing.

11:29.856 --> 11:30.834
-: Right. Right.

11:30.834 --> 11:33.217
-: I think that that's what...

11:33.217 --> 11:36.404
If you generalize the view of

11:36.404 --> 11:37.540
those sorts of decisions,

11:37.540 --> 11:39.781
what you realize is that product management

11:39.781 --> 11:41.434
is a very path dependent exercise.

11:41.434 --> 11:42.267
-: Yeah.

11:42.267 --> 11:44.242
-: Because you might agree on the vision

11:44.242 --> 11:45.103
with a bunch of people,

11:45.103 --> 11:47.248
which is probably the first goal of product management is

11:47.248 --> 11:49.049
just to agree where we head of product.

11:49.049 --> 11:50.339
Like with Spotify,

11:50.339 --> 11:52.711
it's like what do we want people to do with this product?

11:52.711 --> 11:54.135
Is it, do we,

11:54.135 --> 11:56.318
is the vision more like adding videos in this is a

11:56.318 --> 11:59.932
multimedia center or is this more just like a music?

11:59.932 --> 12:00.987
This is your like lay time.

12:00.987 --> 12:01.820
-: Right.

12:01.820 --> 12:03.303
-: This is the background of your whole life.

12:03.303 --> 12:04.136
-: Right.

12:04.136 --> 12:04.969
-: Like, which is not video.

12:04.969 --> 12:05.802
-: Right.

12:05.802 --> 12:07.118
-: Background of your life is probably more like just silent

12:07.118 --> 12:08.437
and responsive and aware of you.

12:08.437 --> 12:09.270
-: Yeah.

12:09.270 --> 12:10.103
-: So then, okay,

12:10.103 --> 12:11.753
we agree on the vision of where this is headed.

12:11.753 --> 12:13.044
Where of course the vision at Google

12:13.044 --> 12:14.333
will just be all things to everybody.

12:14.333 --> 12:15.166
And then-

12:15.166 --> 12:15.999
-: Yeah.

12:15.999 --> 12:17.246
-: Which made it very challenging in fact

12:17.246 --> 12:18.779
to direct priorities.

12:18.779 --> 12:19.612
-: Right.

12:19.612 --> 12:20.637
-: But then, okay, how,

12:20.637 --> 12:21.811
what's the roadmap to getting there?

12:21.811 --> 12:23.636
What are the coins along the playing field that we've,

12:23.636 --> 12:25.969
we're gonna navigate off course to pick up.

12:25.969 --> 12:26.813
-: Right. Right.

12:26.813 --> 12:27.783
-: And so it's like a video game.

12:27.783 --> 12:28.616
-: Right?

12:28.616 --> 12:31.248
-: Like should we pick up the 2 cent piece over there even

12:31.248 --> 12:32.223
though it's kind of further,

12:32.223 --> 12:33.432
because if we do actually it's close,

12:33.432 --> 12:34.704
it's a traveling salesman problem.

12:34.704 --> 12:35.537
-: Yeah. Yeah.

12:35.537 --> 12:36.519
-: Which is why we're not really worried about product

12:36.519 --> 12:40.044
management being obsoleted by technology.

12:40.044 --> 12:40.877
-: Right.

12:40.877 --> 12:42.216
-: Cause we're having to crack that one.

12:42.216 --> 12:43.422
-: Right. Exactly.

12:43.422 --> 12:44.790
Computers can't crack the traveling salesman thing.

12:44.790 --> 12:45.623
-: Exactly.

12:45.623 --> 12:46.456
-: Yeah.

12:46.456 --> 12:47.289
-: And that's so we're safe.

12:47.289 --> 12:48.143
-: Yeah, exactly.

12:48.143 --> 12:52.744
Actually the first thing you mentioned the off

12:52.744 --> 12:55.110
the feature ideas where you have to dig deeper with

12:55.110 --> 12:58.050
the documents being pulled at a faster rate.

12:58.050 --> 13:01.076
That is like the classic example of the,

13:01.076 --> 13:03.737
I think the Steve Jobs quote where if you ask people what

13:03.737 --> 13:05.393
they want, they'll just say like a faster horse.

13:05.393 --> 13:07.683
-: Yeah. I've heard it attributed to Henry Ford even.

13:07.683 --> 13:08.516
-: Oh, okay.

13:08.516 --> 13:09.482
No, it's, it was-

13:09.482 --> 13:11.095
-: Oh, but Steve was probably the one who made it popular.

13:11.095 --> 13:12.291
-: Yeah. Right.

13:12.291 --> 13:13.697
Like everything. Yeah.

13:13.697 --> 13:14.530
Didn't do it first.

13:14.530 --> 13:15.363
-: Certainly no, like,

13:15.363 --> 13:16.701
I think very few people were asking for the iPhone.

13:16.701 --> 13:20.951
I remember I was pretty excited to have a separate iPod from

13:20.951 --> 13:23.226
my phone and that they just seemed like

13:23.226 --> 13:24.666
different functions of my life.

13:24.666 --> 13:27.570
-: Then only later iPhone V3,

13:27.570 --> 13:30.456
gen three was really where kind of came together as this is

13:30.456 --> 13:32.710
gonna offer some pretty compelling new experiences.

13:32.710 --> 13:33.543
-: Yeah

13:33.543 --> 13:34.465
-: And simplicity for one's life.

13:34.465 --> 13:35.298
-: Right.

13:35.298 --> 13:36.131
-: Yeah.

13:36.131 --> 13:36.964
-: There's...

13:36.964 --> 13:37.797
Yeah.

13:37.797 --> 13:39.770
As you know, Apple is,

13:39.770 --> 13:42.570
I guess they're notorious for not doing, like,

13:42.570 --> 13:45.990
I think they do user research but not doing

13:45.990 --> 13:46.926
the broader-

13:46.926 --> 13:47.759
-: That's right.

13:47.759 --> 13:48.592
-: High volume.

13:48.592 --> 13:50.326
-: The vision doesn't come from the bottom sum.

13:50.326 --> 13:51.243
It is top down vision

13:51.243 --> 13:53.716
and then they'll validate the execution,

13:53.716 --> 13:57.690
but they're not deriving strategic insight as much-

13:57.690 --> 13:58.523
-: From people

13:58.523 --> 13:59.356
-: Exactly.

13:59.356 --> 14:00.189
-: From the average consumer.

14:00.189 --> 14:01.022
-: Yeah.

14:01.022 --> 14:01.855
-: Because back in,

14:01.855 --> 14:03.403
I think you can still look at some pictures like in the

14:03.403 --> 14:06.229
early or late 1800's and their version of the future

14:06.229 --> 14:09.217
was like horses and buggies on the moon.

14:09.217 --> 14:10.050
-: Right.

14:10.050 --> 14:11.453
-: You know, so somehow we're gonna get the horses

14:11.453 --> 14:12.286
and buggies up there.

14:12.286 --> 14:13.119
-: Yeah.

14:13.119 --> 14:13.952
-: But the...

14:13.952 --> 14:15.379
But it's still gonna be a horse and a buggy.

14:15.379 --> 14:16.212
-: Yeah.

14:16.212 --> 14:18.439
It's not dissimilar, companies like Google,

14:18.439 --> 14:21.017
Google takes a very supply side driven approach,

14:21.017 --> 14:23.916
which I think makes them differentiated in the market.

14:23.916 --> 14:26.224
They're really focused on what can we do

14:26.224 --> 14:27.855
with all this technology.

14:27.855 --> 14:29.413
So given that we have big table,

14:29.413 --> 14:30.960
like how can we apply big table?

14:30.960 --> 14:31.793
-: Right.

14:31.793 --> 14:33.641
-: Given we have search, how can we apply search?

14:33.641 --> 14:34.606
And-

14:34.606 --> 14:36.711
-: Big Table is their database of everything.

14:36.711 --> 14:37.544
-: That's right.

14:37.544 --> 14:38.393
-: That's the single table.

14:38.393 --> 14:40.248
-: No, that would be, they have the knowledge graph.

14:40.248 --> 14:41.081
-: Okay.

14:41.081 --> 14:41.914
-: That's the database of everything.

14:41.914 --> 14:43.663
Big table is the database, storage technology.

14:43.663 --> 14:44.746
-: Right. Okay.

14:44.746 --> 14:46.944
-: But, okay, so if you do have the knowledge graph

14:46.944 --> 14:48.894
and you do know a bunch of things about things-

14:48.894 --> 14:49.727
-: Right.

14:49.727 --> 14:50.560
-: Then where can we...

14:50.560 --> 14:52.369
And then, so like I integrated that into Google Maps

14:52.369 --> 14:54.029
and other product managers were integrating that into search

14:54.029 --> 14:55.485
as like knowledge panels.

14:55.485 --> 14:56.318
-: Yeah.

14:56.318 --> 14:57.878
-: Other people are integrating that into Android.

14:57.878 --> 15:00.149
So you're just geographically located somewhere.

15:00.149 --> 15:01.724
It's pulling up relevant facts

15:01.724 --> 15:02.966
about your life in Google now.

15:02.966 --> 15:03.799
-: Right.

15:03.799 --> 15:06.390
-: And so these sort core technologies and data assets,

15:06.390 --> 15:09.426
the product management team then translates those into cool

15:09.426 --> 15:11.660
features and ultimately products.

15:11.660 --> 15:15.448
There was a whole product Google had called,

15:15.448 --> 15:16.657
I don't remember.

15:16.657 --> 15:20.370
And as you navigated through a foreign city,

15:20.370 --> 15:23.605
it would sort of craft a story for you to follow along.

15:23.605 --> 15:24.576
It was like guided tours,

15:24.576 --> 15:27.198
I think it was called like Sidekick or something like this.

15:27.198 --> 15:28.067
-: Yeah, yeah.

15:28.067 --> 15:30.340
One of these Google side things that just comes out.

15:30.340 --> 15:31.453
-: There's a million of them.

15:31.453 --> 15:32.286
-: There's a lot, yeah.

15:32.286 --> 15:33.119
-: Yeah.

15:33.119 --> 15:33.952
-: Sometimes they die quietly.

15:33.952 --> 15:34.785
-: Right.

15:34.785 --> 15:35.871
Sometimes they don't get publicized in the first place.

15:35.871 --> 15:36.704
-: Right.

15:36.704 --> 15:38.342
And so at companies like Google and what we're trying to

15:38.342 --> 15:39.371
create at Earnest,

15:39.371 --> 15:42.774
it's awareness of what technology you have that then

15:42.774 --> 15:44.694
translates into products and features,

15:44.694 --> 15:47.759
which is reasonably different from how product

15:47.759 --> 15:50.751
management is approached at other companies that, you know,

15:50.751 --> 15:52.348
might focus more on user research,

15:52.348 --> 15:53.181
-: Right.

15:53.181 --> 15:54.601
-: Or focus more on customer demands.

15:54.601 --> 15:55.434
-: Right.

15:55.434 --> 15:56.584
-: And it's just thinking, what can we do with this?

15:56.584 --> 15:57.878
-: Right.

15:57.878 --> 15:58.711
-: Yeah.

15:58.711 --> 15:59.544
-: Okay.

15:59.544 --> 16:02.301
So if I am not mistaken, you actually have,

16:02.301 --> 16:04.294
you have job listings out

16:04.294 --> 16:06.652
for product management for Earnest?

16:06.652 --> 16:08.335
I mean, it probably will not be the case

16:08.335 --> 16:09.662
when this course is out, but-

16:09.662 --> 16:11.341
-: No we're gonna be constantly hiring folks.

16:11.341 --> 16:12.174
-: Okay. Okay.

16:12.174 --> 16:13.007
-: Don't worry.

16:13.007 --> 16:13.840
-: But-

16:13.840 --> 16:15.240
-: We've had it open for a year and a half a year.

16:15.240 --> 16:16.073
-: Oh really?

16:16.073 --> 16:16.906
-: Yeah.

16:16.906 --> 16:17.739
It's just you're constantly hiring.

16:17.739 --> 16:19.144
It's how the team goes from just me to eight people.

16:19.144 --> 16:20.199
-: Right. Okay. Yeah.

16:20.199 --> 16:21.169
-: By that thing being open.

16:21.169 --> 16:22.002
-: Okay.

16:22.002 --> 16:23.464
But you, so you're still a person

16:23.464 --> 16:25.313
I'd like to ask about,

16:25.313 --> 16:28.147
because this course is about getting into product management

16:28.147 --> 16:30.743
for a number of people, how would you, first of all,

16:30.743 --> 16:34.636
suggest someone goes about learning about the role,

16:34.636 --> 16:36.218
obviously this course,

16:36.218 --> 16:38.536
but ultimately looking to get a job there.

16:38.536 --> 16:39.369
-: Yeah.

16:39.369 --> 16:43.749
-: Techniques of and such for finding them and that

16:43.749 --> 16:44.633
sort of thing.

16:44.633 --> 16:45.466
But then also,

16:45.466 --> 16:47.556
what do you like to see as a-

16:47.556 --> 16:48.389
-: Yes.

16:48.389 --> 16:49.597
Hiring manager.
-: As a hiring manager.

16:49.597 --> 16:51.249
Like what qualities are you looking for?

16:51.249 --> 16:52.082
-: For sure.

16:52.082 --> 16:53.459
So product management,

16:53.459 --> 16:57.135
learning about it is tremendously difficult.

16:57.135 --> 17:00.334
It's very hard to craft a product management internship.

17:00.334 --> 17:02.854
We do do it at Earnest and we are hiring interns.

17:02.854 --> 17:03.687
-: Yeah.

17:03.687 --> 17:05.942
-: Including interns with no product management experience.

17:05.942 --> 17:08.186
-: Oh, that's good then.

17:08.186 --> 17:09.808
-: Yeah. So that's relevant.

17:09.808 --> 17:11.265
-: Product management interns-

17:11.265 --> 17:12.297
-: See what Tiffany wants.

17:12.297 --> 17:13.470
-: Great.

17:13.470 --> 17:16.411
Product management internships are probably a

17:16.411 --> 17:17.646
good exposure.

17:17.646 --> 17:18.954
I didn't do one. Right?

17:18.954 --> 17:21.613
I didn't know about in product management internships when I

17:21.613 --> 17:24.816
was in college and afterward it I didn't need it.

17:24.816 --> 17:27.114
So, but I've heard great things about it

17:27.114 --> 17:28.203
from my friends who've done it.

17:28.203 --> 17:29.036
Okay.

17:29.036 --> 17:30.520
Otherwise, to learn about product management are

17:30.520 --> 17:31.945
to work in a product environment.

17:31.945 --> 17:34.142
So I've definitely had a number of engineers

17:34.142 --> 17:35.728
sort of approach me

17:35.728 --> 17:37.327
and they're interested in product management.

17:37.327 --> 17:40.247
They don't yet have enough experience working

17:40.247 --> 17:42.261
with product engineering.

17:42.261 --> 17:43.094
-: Right.

17:43.094 --> 17:44.214
-: So they might be very infrastructural-

17:44.214 --> 17:45.047
-: Backend.

17:45.047 --> 17:45.880
-: Backend.
-: Engineering.

17:45.880 --> 17:46.713
That sort of thing.

17:46.713 --> 17:49.905
-: And so I encourage them to transition closer to products

17:49.905 --> 17:53.473
prior to jumping onto the product management team so that

17:53.473 --> 17:56.208
they can observe and participate in the product decision

17:56.208 --> 17:58.394
making process before they're, you know,

17:58.394 --> 18:00.624
in a role that is exclusively focused on that.

18:00.624 --> 18:01.457
-: Right.

18:01.457 --> 18:03.854
-: So front end engineering or just product engineering

18:03.854 --> 18:04.893
generally speaking,

18:04.893 --> 18:07.553
is a great way to get closer to product management.

18:07.553 --> 18:10.614
Once one has a sense for what product management is,

18:10.614 --> 18:13.710
the hiring managers are looking for folks, you know,

18:13.710 --> 18:15.697
at Earnest we're looking for four things.

18:15.697 --> 18:18.296
So we're looking for analytical skills, technical skills,

18:18.296 --> 18:20.627
creative skills, and collaborative skills.

18:20.627 --> 18:23.691
So that's like at Earnest, I think other companies,

18:23.691 --> 18:26.395
based on the type of product manager they're looking for,

18:26.395 --> 18:27.579
they might switch it up.

18:27.579 --> 18:29.483
But I can speak to Earnest really well.

18:29.483 --> 18:31.530
And so analytical skills,

18:31.530 --> 18:33.540
we're looking for things like proficiency and SQL.

18:33.540 --> 18:35.404
Do you know your different join types?

18:35.404 --> 18:36.770
Do you know how limit works?

18:36.770 --> 18:39.806
-: Sort of a basic proficiency, but not to-

18:39.806 --> 18:40.639
-: To an extent to...

18:40.639 --> 18:41.708
Up to the point of an advanced proficiency.

18:41.708 --> 18:42.541
-: Okay.

18:42.541 --> 18:43.374
-: Would be even better.

18:43.374 --> 18:44.207
-: So yeah.

18:44.207 --> 18:46.624
SQL is a database technology.

18:47.523 --> 18:50.905
And then you would learn MySQL, which is,

18:50.905 --> 18:52.582
or you'd learn SQL queries,

18:52.582 --> 18:56.021
which is being able to query the database for like-

18:56.021 --> 18:56.854
-: Exactly.

18:56.854 --> 18:57.687
-: Information.

18:57.687 --> 18:58.520
-: Any kind of...

18:58.520 --> 18:59.721
In disparate data sets, you know,

18:59.721 --> 19:01.066
Excel junkies can kind of,

19:01.066 --> 19:04.441
are most apt to be able to transfer into, SQL experts.

19:04.441 --> 19:05.274
-: That's not too different.

19:05.274 --> 19:06.107
-: Exactly.

19:06.107 --> 19:07.812
You're just dealing with understanding data

19:07.812 --> 19:09.147
at the highest level, right?

19:09.147 --> 19:12.449
Maybe you're a R or a state junkie, like wherever data is,

19:12.449 --> 19:15.225
if you're capable of thinking through basic statistics or

19:15.225 --> 19:17.472
thinking about how you can tie data together

19:17.472 --> 19:18.916
to derive insights,

19:18.916 --> 19:21.681
you're on the path to making data driven product decisions,

19:21.681 --> 19:23.948
which are the types of decisions that you'll be able to use

19:23.948 --> 19:26.459
to get the respect of your team.

19:26.459 --> 19:28.009
-: Yeah, that's a big one.

19:28.009 --> 19:28.955
The respect of your team

19:28.955 --> 19:29.788
-: Yes.

19:29.788 --> 19:30.621
-: Sort of thing.

19:30.621 --> 19:31.454
-: Yes.

19:31.454 --> 19:32.627
-: Is being at least, even if you're not technical,

19:32.627 --> 19:35.597
you should, there's a product manager

19:35.597 --> 19:38.930
named Ellen Chisa, who actually went to,

19:41.288 --> 19:43.593
is called Boston near you and I,

19:43.593 --> 19:46.683
and she has a really great article about this.

19:46.683 --> 19:48.388
Like if you're not technical, like

19:48.388 --> 19:50.103
how technical should you become?

19:50.103 --> 19:51.456
And she argues that it should, at minimum

19:51.456 --> 19:54.229
You should be curious all the time

19:54.229 --> 19:56.647
about learning more, right?

19:56.647 --> 19:57.480
-: Yes. Yes.

19:57.480 --> 19:59.521
-: Doesn't mean you have to go be an expert coder

19:59.521 --> 20:00.620
this weekend,

20:00.620 --> 20:02.919
but it means that you should be curious about like every

20:02.919 --> 20:04.143
single new technology

20:04.143 --> 20:04.976
-: Yes.

20:04.976 --> 20:06.416
-: That you're using and always asking, you know,

20:06.416 --> 20:08.968
engineers about things and yeah.

20:08.968 --> 20:11.395
Demonstrating interest and in learning these things rather

20:11.395 --> 20:13.805
than just saying, I don't care about these things,

20:13.805 --> 20:16.228
we'll have an engineer deal with it or whatever.

20:16.228 --> 20:18.329
Or I'll ask an engineer every single time.

20:18.329 --> 20:19.611
-: Yeah, Yeah.

20:19.611 --> 20:22.059
Which is gonna be an expensive conversation to keep having.

20:22.059 --> 20:23.068
Right?

20:23.068 --> 20:23.984
-: They're very expensive people.

20:23.984 --> 20:24.885
-: Yeah. Yeah.

20:24.885 --> 20:26.527
So that describes kind of the second bucket of skills that

20:26.527 --> 20:27.845
we look for as technical skills.

20:27.845 --> 20:28.678
-: Yeah.

20:28.678 --> 20:30.794
-: So we're looking for folks who have done engineering

20:30.794 --> 20:33.543
or have done computer science class or you know,

20:33.543 --> 20:36.712
have learned programming through a general assembly thing,

20:36.712 --> 20:38.826
and then applied that in some respect on side projects.

20:38.826 --> 20:39.659
-: Right.

20:39.659 --> 20:44.659
-: So ultimately your ability to understand how the technical

20:45.548 --> 20:50.548
architecture has implications for product extensibility is

20:50.585 --> 20:54.660
what is core to the technical knowledge, as well as not

20:54.660 --> 20:56.162
getting shown up on the technical side

20:56.162 --> 20:57.458
by folks like your designers.

20:57.458 --> 20:58.291
-: Yeah.

20:58.291 --> 21:02.336
-: So like that's just kind of impairs trust

21:02.336 --> 21:03.482
in a manner of speaking.

21:03.482 --> 21:04.315
-: Right.

21:04.315 --> 21:06.435
-: So I challenge all of my product managers to not get shown

21:06.435 --> 21:07.742
up by our product design team.

21:07.742 --> 21:08.575
-: Yeah.

21:08.575 --> 21:10.247
-: Which is, you know, relatively advanced design team.

21:10.247 --> 21:12.197
They're getting into get,

21:12.197 --> 21:13.648
they're making some copy changes, style changes,

21:13.648 --> 21:14.481
-: Right.

21:14.481 --> 21:15.314
They're publishing those,

21:15.314 --> 21:16.147
-: Right.

21:16.147 --> 21:16.980
They're dealing with unit tests

21:16.980 --> 21:18.829
failing and understanding the build failed.

21:18.829 --> 21:20.495
What does that even mean? The build failed.

21:20.495 --> 21:21.328
-: Right.

21:21.328 --> 21:22.717
-: If you don't know what it means for the build to fail,

21:22.717 --> 21:24.687
you still need to ramp up your technical skills before you

21:24.687 --> 21:26.734
can really get into a technical product management role.

21:26.734 --> 21:27.567
-: Right.

21:27.567 --> 21:28.908
-: Maybe you'd be product manager of like

21:28.908 --> 21:29.866
a toothpaste company,

21:29.866 --> 21:32.260
but of a tech company, you need to understand the,

21:32.260 --> 21:34.969
what's the process of building the right system.

21:34.969 --> 21:35.802
-: Right.

21:35.802 --> 21:38.036
And even if you're not pushing code yourself, you know,

21:38.036 --> 21:41.621
to production, you need to understand how the flow works.

21:41.621 --> 21:42.454
-: Right.

21:42.454 --> 21:44.261
-: In continuous integration and like what get is and what

21:44.261 --> 21:45.542
branches are and what.

21:45.542 --> 21:46.375
-: You need the vocabulary.

21:46.375 --> 21:47.387
-: You need the vocabulary.

21:47.387 --> 21:48.822
-: Totally.

21:48.822 --> 21:50.423
On the creative side, I usually challenge people

21:50.423 --> 21:52.028
to imagine what I mean by that.

21:52.028 --> 21:52.861
-: Yeah.

21:52.861 --> 21:54.116
-: And then on the collaboration skills,

21:54.116 --> 21:54.949
-: That's a good one.

21:54.949 --> 21:56.282
(laughing)

21:56.282 --> 21:59.482
-: On the collaboration skills, that's like,

21:59.482 --> 22:01.121
I think of communication skills,

22:01.121 --> 22:03.612
I think of getting things done

22:03.612 --> 22:05.014
without stepping on too many feet.

22:05.014 --> 22:07.628
Communication skills I've also further broken down

22:07.628 --> 22:12.111
for folks, it's the ability to speak with accuracy.

22:12.111 --> 22:14.449
So you, what you were saying is true,

22:14.449 --> 22:16.038
which is actually sometimes challenging,

22:16.038 --> 22:17.451
especially when you don't know something.

22:17.451 --> 22:21.067
It can be easy to speak about truth with things that are

22:21.067 --> 22:23.798
actually inferences or you're uncertain about them.

22:23.798 --> 22:25.418
So speaking with truth is accuracy,

22:25.418 --> 22:28.281
speaking with precision or communicating with precision.

22:28.281 --> 22:32.088
So that would mean saying things in an unambiguous manner,

22:32.088 --> 22:33.901
they can't be misinterpreted.

22:33.901 --> 22:34.734
-: Right.

22:34.734 --> 22:35.567
-: And then speaking with brevity,

22:35.567 --> 22:37.663
so even if you were to speak with accuracy and precision,

22:37.663 --> 22:39.313
you might still have that coded

22:39.313 --> 22:40.337
in tons of other information.

22:40.337 --> 22:42.512
And really great product managers are able to communicate

22:42.512 --> 22:46.116
with all the certain teams in those three respects.

22:46.116 --> 22:46.949
-: Yeah.

22:46.949 --> 22:48.766
-: Briefly, accurately, precisely.

22:48.766 --> 22:49.599
-: Right.

22:49.599 --> 22:51.059
-: And then additionally they're able to translate,

22:51.059 --> 22:53.808
they use, we use words that sort of communicate

22:53.808 --> 22:54.978
with each of the teams.

22:54.978 --> 22:55.936
-: Right.

22:55.936 --> 22:57.533
-: Like legal is going to have a different perception

22:57.533 --> 22:59.203
on what a policy is than engineering.

22:59.203 --> 23:00.078
-: Yes.

23:00.078 --> 23:01.053
-: Because engineering has a,

23:01.053 --> 23:03.187
like a rounding policy and a data persistence policy.

23:03.187 --> 23:04.020
-: Right.

23:04.020 --> 23:05.457
-: Legal doesn't really care about data persistence policy.

23:05.457 --> 23:06.864
They care more about the products,

23:06.864 --> 23:07.898
implications for users

23:07.898 --> 23:10.217
and how are users being charged things.

23:10.217 --> 23:11.050
-: Yeah.

23:11.050 --> 23:12.535
The communication thing is huge.

23:12.535 --> 23:14.105
You need...

23:14.105 --> 23:16.854
There's a saying which I don't know if it applies anymore,

23:16.854 --> 23:19.195
but for every single group of stakeholders,

23:19.195 --> 23:20.255
so you executives,

23:20.255 --> 23:22.446
your legal and marketing and engineers and design,

23:22.446 --> 23:24.542
there's a different deck, right?

23:24.542 --> 23:25.426
So there's different-

23:25.426 --> 23:26.259
-: Yeah.

23:26.259 --> 23:27.191
-: Different words for different groups.

23:27.191 --> 23:28.024
-: Yeah.

23:28.024 --> 23:28.857
-: Like you say,

23:28.857 --> 23:31.290
they should all still be concise and brief

23:31.290 --> 23:32.976
and true to the point factual.

23:32.976 --> 23:35.654
But then if you're talking to an executive, you,

23:35.654 --> 23:37.071
it's gonna be ultra brief, right?

23:37.071 --> 23:37.904
-: Ultra, yeah.

23:37.904 --> 23:39.664
Elevator at Google, you were known to have to,

23:39.664 --> 23:41.956
especially in the earlier days, to chase around executives.

23:41.956 --> 23:42.789
-: Right.

23:42.789 --> 23:44.593
-: And so that you could catch them at an elevator moment.

23:44.593 --> 23:45.426
-: Yeah.

23:45.426 --> 23:46.841
And then give them your 30 second pitch

23:46.841 --> 23:48.098
and get their verbal approval and

23:48.098 --> 23:49.458
you'd run off and keep executing.

23:49.458 --> 23:50.291
-: Right.

23:50.291 --> 23:51.929
-: That was how brief you had to be

23:51.929 --> 23:55.106
to win as a product manager back in like 2007.

23:55.106 --> 23:55.982
-: That's awesome.

23:55.982 --> 23:56.815
-: Yeah.

23:56.815 --> 23:57.648
And people would stock,

23:57.648 --> 23:58.840
there'd be like a chat group where you're like, oh,

23:58.840 --> 24:01.145
Marissa's in the elevator, like grab her.

24:01.145 --> 24:02.058
Yeah.

24:02.058 --> 24:03.070
-: Yeah.

24:03.070 --> 24:06.742
-: Fortunately we're not like that yet at Earnest.

24:06.742 --> 24:10.081
-: So then, so you mentioned the four things,

24:10.081 --> 24:12.664
and let's say that someone has,

24:13.715 --> 24:16.859
they've learned about what product management is and they

24:16.859 --> 24:20.012
have learned some technical skills and so on and so forth,

24:20.012 --> 24:22.278
and they've applied some technical skills

24:22.278 --> 24:24.224
and made to decide project and that sort of thing.

24:24.224 --> 24:25.057
-: Yeah.

24:25.057 --> 24:26.707
-: And they're looking not even for a job with you,

24:26.707 --> 24:27.664
but just anywhere,

24:27.664 --> 24:30.696
is it vital to have some sort of portfolio thing

24:30.696 --> 24:32.613
project to point at or?

24:33.551 --> 24:34.384
-: Yeah.

24:34.384 --> 24:36.112
That's a great question.

24:36.112 --> 24:40.862
I think that what I've been looking for has been a little

24:41.984 --> 24:44.127
bit less portfolio based

24:44.127 --> 24:46.899
and a little bit more experience based.

24:46.899 --> 24:51.514
We wanna see that people have gone through the trials of

24:51.514 --> 24:54.792
interacting with a really top class product designer and

24:54.792 --> 24:56.416
knows kind of how to communicate.

24:56.416 --> 24:57.510
As we were just talking about,

24:57.510 --> 24:59.755
one of the core competencies is being able to translate with

24:59.755 --> 25:00.902
the different functions.

25:00.902 --> 25:04.434
That's sort of the ultimate purpose of the role.

25:04.434 --> 25:05.267
-: Right.

25:05.267 --> 25:07.912
-: And so if you have created like an independent portfolio

25:07.912 --> 25:10.319
that you've product managed this product on your own,

25:10.319 --> 25:13.008
it doesn't demonstrate that you have the skills to

25:13.008 --> 25:15.356
communicate well with the product design team and understand

25:15.356 --> 25:18.022
what their concerns are gonna be or their considerations or

25:18.022 --> 25:19.642
what you can delegate to them.

25:19.642 --> 25:21.732
Similarly with a great product engineer,

25:21.732 --> 25:23.584
similarly with a great, you know,

25:23.584 --> 25:25.240
maybe even a legal team or marketing team.

25:25.240 --> 25:26.073
-: Right.

25:26.073 --> 25:27.480
-: And so we're looking a little bit less for

25:27.480 --> 25:29.163
independent work, and a little bit more for

25:29.163 --> 25:32.163
experience working with these teams.

25:33.155 --> 25:34.091
-: Right.

25:34.091 --> 25:35.924
So a product internship role and-

25:35.924 --> 25:36.757
-: Yeah.

25:36.757 --> 25:38.205
Or even like, you know, in coursework,

25:38.205 --> 25:43.057
like a cross-functionally collaborative product, sort of.

25:43.057 --> 25:45.542
-: There could be a side of product as long as,

25:45.542 --> 25:47.139
you're actually doing this communication between the

25:47.139 --> 25:48.170
different groups and you're-

25:48.170 --> 25:49.003
-: Yeah, exactly.

25:49.003 --> 25:49.836
-: Right.

25:49.836 --> 25:50.670
-: It just can't be like a tiny group.

25:50.670 --> 25:51.503
-: Right.

25:51.503 --> 25:53.142
-: Like of one you and one other person.

25:53.142 --> 25:53.975
-: Right.

25:53.975 --> 25:55.549
-: You're wearing too many hats.

25:55.549 --> 25:56.632
-: Right.

25:56.632 --> 26:00.322
-: In that role and you're not like practicing

26:00.322 --> 26:03.841
the scaling of those hats being worn by different people,

26:03.841 --> 26:05.494
then you coordinating all of them.

26:05.494 --> 26:06.327
-: Right.

26:06.327 --> 26:08.444
-: So, okay, for entry level product managers,

26:08.444 --> 26:11.501
we're really just looking for basic proficiency in those

26:11.501 --> 26:13.180
skill areas more so than experience.

26:13.180 --> 26:14.385
So we're looking for, you know,

26:14.385 --> 26:16.418
do you know computer science fundamentals already

26:16.418 --> 26:17.372
and do you know SQL

26:17.372 --> 26:19.451
and do you, you know, have a track record

26:19.451 --> 26:22.066
in the creative side of being able to problem solve

26:22.066 --> 26:24.409
interview questions with us in the whiteboard.

26:24.409 --> 26:25.242
-: Right.

26:25.242 --> 26:26.882
-: Or of academic thought,

26:26.882 --> 26:28.698
like do you have a cool thesis?

26:28.698 --> 26:30.646
On the collaboration side you know,

26:30.646 --> 26:33.310
did you participate in any group projects extracurricularly?

26:33.310 --> 26:34.143
So you know,

26:34.143 --> 26:36.252
at Harvard there was the like robotics competition,

26:36.252 --> 26:39.094
you could like jump into that and help build a better robot.

26:39.094 --> 26:42.480
Or did you just show leadership by becoming president

26:42.480 --> 26:43.649
of the school newspaper?

26:43.649 --> 26:44.482
-: Right.

26:44.482 --> 26:47.133
-: These are sort of like the core collaboration skills or

26:47.133 --> 26:49.819
indications of those core collaboration skills that will

26:49.819 --> 26:52.276
give us confidence to bring you in as a product manager at

26:52.276 --> 26:53.310
the entry level.

26:53.310 --> 26:54.480
But beyond the entry level,

26:54.480 --> 26:55.908
going to mid-level and senior level,

26:55.908 --> 26:58.058
we really need to see actual product management experience

26:58.058 --> 27:00.226
with the types of stakeholders that you're gonna be engaging

27:00.226 --> 27:01.305
with at our environment.

27:01.305 --> 27:02.156
-: Cool.

27:02.156 --> 27:02.989
-: Yeah.

27:02.989 --> 27:04.912
-: All right. Well, so I think that about does it.

27:04.912 --> 27:05.745
-: Sweet.

27:05.745 --> 27:06.578
-: But-

27:06.578 --> 27:07.411
-: Thanks for having me.

27:07.411 --> 27:08.244
-: Yeah.

27:08.244 --> 27:09.711
Daniel Dimitri head of product at Earnest

27:09.711 --> 27:12.878
and we'll see you in the next lecture.

27:16.465 --> 27:19.548
(indistinct chatter)

response.title: Interview with David Lifson, VP of Product @ Homepolish

requests.response:
WEBVTT

00:00.000 --> 00:02.583
(upbeat music)

00:04.260 --> 00:08.234
-: Hey, everyone, I'm with Dave Lifson out of New York,

00:08.234 --> 00:10.862
VP of Product for Homepolish.

00:10.862 --> 00:12.630
And we're gonna do a little bit

00:12.630 --> 00:16.023
of interview here on some product management topics.

00:21.450 --> 00:22.890
And Dave has been...

00:22.890 --> 00:25.350
Dave, you were at what?

00:25.350 --> 00:28.230
You were at Etsy before like long ago

00:28.230 --> 00:29.460
and what, Amazon as well?

00:29.460 --> 00:32.250
-: Yeah, so my first job out of school was at Amazon.

00:32.250 --> 00:33.660
I was a software developer

00:33.660 --> 00:36.450
and then realized within probably 15 months

00:36.450 --> 00:38.880
that I was made to be a product manager.

00:38.880 --> 00:40.620
And so, it got to switch

00:40.620 --> 00:43.080
to the recommendations team at Amazon,

00:43.080 --> 00:45.120
which is because you bought these items,

00:45.120 --> 00:46.530
we recommend these others.

00:46.530 --> 00:49.200
And I was at Amazon for a total of two years,

00:49.200 --> 00:51.060
learned a ton, loved it there,

00:51.060 --> 00:53.460
but just had an opportunity I couldn't say no to,

00:53.460 --> 00:55.170
to move to Brooklyn from Seattle

00:55.170 --> 00:57.220
and be the first head of product at Etsy.

00:58.080 --> 01:01.199
So, that was 2008 when Etsy was 50 people

01:01.199 --> 01:05.010
and all three founders were there.

01:05.010 --> 01:08.820
And I then did my own startup for three years

01:08.820 --> 01:11.820
with the technical founders of Etsy.

01:11.820 --> 01:14.490
We sold that company, I joined General Assembly

01:14.490 --> 01:15.700
as employee 25

01:17.310 --> 01:20.070
and grew that from 400 when I left,

01:20.070 --> 01:22.042
over two and a half years.

01:22.042 --> 01:23.940
I started running curriculum,

01:23.940 --> 01:26.850
and then moved to running product design and engineering.

01:26.850 --> 01:29.160
I spent a year at Poppin doing the same thing,

01:29.160 --> 01:30.180
product design and engineering.

01:30.180 --> 01:31.830
And then, now, I just started at Homepolish

01:31.830 --> 01:34.770
about a month ago with the same.

01:34.770 --> 01:35.670
-: Cool.

01:35.670 --> 01:37.583
So, yeah, this course has about,

01:37.583 --> 01:39.880
right now it's over 4,000 students

01:41.190 --> 01:45.423
that are enrolled, and it's only been out for about a month.

01:46.710 --> 01:48.120
And it's focused on, yeah,

01:48.120 --> 01:49.380
people that are new to product management

01:49.380 --> 01:50.610
or people that are in the role

01:50.610 --> 01:52.710
and they're trying to get better.

01:52.710 --> 01:55.560
And yeah, one of the recurring themes

01:55.560 --> 01:58.620
as I know that you know from teaching at General Assembly

01:58.620 --> 02:01.920
and that sort of thing is that product management,

02:01.920 --> 02:03.960
learning about it is quite popular right now,

02:03.960 --> 02:06.090
because it's becoming a more popular role.

02:06.090 --> 02:06.923
People are starting

02:06.923 --> 02:09.900
to learn what product managers are doing,

02:09.900 --> 02:10.920
but they want to find out more,

02:10.920 --> 02:15.210
but there's no good way to learn about it in a formal way,

02:15.210 --> 02:16.803
or you can't get a degree.

02:17.862 --> 02:19.293
You have that.

02:20.280 --> 02:23.630
You and I both just happened upon it.

02:23.630 --> 02:27.030
And I think, most people end up doing that.

02:27.030 --> 02:31.380
And I think, right now, due to things like General Assembly

02:31.380 --> 02:32.730
or like this course,

02:32.730 --> 02:35.310
you can learn about it formally

02:35.310 --> 02:37.140
from an actual product manager.

02:37.140 --> 02:41.700
So, the first question I wanted to ask was, (chuckles)

02:41.700 --> 02:44.130
it's a interesting one, no matter who I ask is like,

02:44.130 --> 02:46.800
if you had to sum up the role of product management

02:46.800 --> 02:49.920
in a sentence or two, what would it be?

02:49.920 --> 02:52.304
What does the product management mean to you?

02:52.304 --> 02:54.973
-: Oh, wow. Yeah, that's really great.

02:54.973 --> 02:57.958
So, what does product management mean to me?

02:57.958 --> 03:01.380
I think, the role of the product manager

03:01.380 --> 03:06.150
is to have a deep understanding of the customer,

03:06.150 --> 03:08.700
a deep understanding of the business,

03:08.700 --> 03:11.850
and marrying those two together

03:11.850 --> 03:15.430
to identify opportunities and problems,

03:15.430 --> 03:17.340
prioritize those opportunities,

03:17.340 --> 03:19.650
and then devise the right solution for the right time.

03:19.650 --> 03:21.600
And so, what's so challenging

03:21.600 --> 03:24.490
I think about product management is that

03:25.500 --> 03:30.500
we don't fit the mold of the individual contributor.

03:30.900 --> 03:33.330
I think, organizations, historically,

03:33.330 --> 03:36.670
have been structured similar to

03:39.180 --> 03:40.964
how they were in the industrial revolution.

03:40.964 --> 03:43.710
With manufacturing where you just,

03:43.710 --> 03:46.890
you have departments that have workers,

03:46.890 --> 03:48.570
and workers create output.

03:48.570 --> 03:52.890
And coders write code and marketers create marketing

03:52.890 --> 03:57.490
and designers create designs and what (audio breaking)

04:02.100 --> 04:05.430
Don't create anything (audio fast-forwards)

04:05.430 --> 04:08.340
for everyone else that we work with in the organization,

04:08.340 --> 04:11.520
so that the quality that is produced is higher

04:11.520 --> 04:15.660
and the solution is more efficient and more effective.

04:15.660 --> 04:16.830
-: Cool.

04:16.830 --> 04:17.663
Okay.

04:17.663 --> 04:19.200
And so, what did you think about the role

04:19.200 --> 04:22.257
before you were actually a product manager?

04:22.257 --> 04:24.240
Did you have any preconceived notions

04:24.240 --> 04:27.000
or did you know anything about it?

04:27.000 --> 04:28.820
-: Well, my first team at Amazon,

04:28.820 --> 04:30.900
I was in the community group,

04:30.900 --> 04:33.000
which did user-generated content.

04:33.000 --> 04:35.250
And so, my little corner of it was this thing

04:35.250 --> 04:36.670
called Listmania where

04:38.220 --> 04:40.230
Amazon customers could create lists

04:40.230 --> 04:42.390
of their favorite products,

04:42.390 --> 04:45.090
like your top 10 things to take with you on a camping trip

04:45.090 --> 04:46.803
or my favorite scary movies.

04:46.803 --> 04:49.800
And we had no product manager.

04:49.800 --> 04:51.067
It was just a bunch of engineers.

04:51.067 --> 04:53.817
And we just built whatever the heck we wanted to build.

04:55.608 --> 04:57.860
And so, I ended up...

04:59.670 --> 05:01.035
Jeff Bezos had this idea.

05:01.035 --> 05:02.813
This was back in 2006,

05:02.813 --> 05:05.760
where Facebook was becoming a thing.

05:05.760 --> 05:08.070
People were talking a lot about networks.

05:08.070 --> 05:08.947
And so, he was like,

05:08.947 --> 05:12.817
"What if we created a network of product SKUs?

05:12.817 --> 05:15.780
If products could have relationships to each other,

05:15.780 --> 05:16.613
what would that look like?"

05:16.613 --> 05:20.553
And somehow that ended up on my desk as a 23-year-old.

05:23.218 --> 05:27.354
And so, we decided to build this hot or not for products.

05:27.354 --> 05:30.408
So, essentially, customers could vote

05:30.408 --> 05:33.870
on which of these two shoes is more stylish

05:33.870 --> 05:38.050
or which of these two electronics (audio breaking)

05:42.990 --> 05:45.120
easier to use.

05:45.120 --> 05:49.210
And as you've voted (audio breaking)

05:51.420 --> 05:52.530
we would use your votes

05:52.530 --> 05:56.320
to generate you a recommendation (audio breaking)

05:58.860 --> 06:00.720
like very expensive.

06:00.720 --> 06:01.553
We built the whole thing.

06:01.553 --> 06:03.735
We launched it and it was a total disaster.

06:03.735 --> 06:06.605
Conversion rate was somewhere around half a percent.

06:06.605 --> 06:11.112
And it was such a clear example

06:11.112 --> 06:14.610
of how product management could have saved the day,

06:14.610 --> 06:15.990
and we just didn't know it.

06:15.990 --> 06:17.520
-: Mm-hmm.

06:17.520 --> 06:18.353
Yeah.

06:18.353 --> 06:20.793
Working on the right thing rather than just something.

06:21.930 --> 06:23.820
-: Yeah, and or testing any assumptions

06:23.820 --> 06:26.227
or doing any sort of user research or prototyping.

06:26.227 --> 06:31.020
And so, when I moved into the recommendations team,

06:31.020 --> 06:32.140
which did have

06:33.958 --> 06:36.693
a track record of having strong product managers,

06:37.620 --> 06:38.670
I was surrounded by people

06:38.670 --> 06:39.900
who could really show me the ropes,

06:39.900 --> 06:42.210
which is how I learned.
-: Cool.

06:42.210 --> 06:45.660
So, something that we talked, 'cause you mentioned,

06:45.660 --> 06:48.000
you mentioned this like talking to users

06:48.000 --> 06:51.989
or validating something and validating your assumptions.

06:51.989 --> 06:54.911
Another thing that happens quite often

06:54.911 --> 06:58.260
to us as product managers is we talk to users

06:58.260 --> 07:01.883
or we get feedback and they send some sort of request

07:01.883 --> 07:04.618
or maybe if you're an internal product manager,

07:04.618 --> 07:07.140
you get basically what amounts

07:07.140 --> 07:10.830
to a feature request or product spec request.

07:10.830 --> 07:14.370
And oftentimes, that thing that's being requested

07:14.370 --> 07:18.063
is not what is actually the solution.

07:20.460 --> 07:21.702
It happens to everyone.

07:21.702 --> 07:23.790
If you look two levels deeper,

07:23.790 --> 07:25.422
there's actually another problem here

07:25.422 --> 07:27.870
that maybe that solution doesn't solve.

07:27.870 --> 07:30.150
And so, when you're receiving feedback from users,

07:30.150 --> 07:31.752
you gotta dig in deeper.

07:31.752 --> 07:35.100
Do you have any war stories

07:35.100 --> 07:38.610
of user requests that are just absolutely

07:38.610 --> 07:42.900
were just not the solution at first that you could share

07:42.900 --> 07:45.480
and maybe what the real solution was?

07:45.480 --> 07:48.233
-: Well, I don't know if I can tell you the real solution,

07:49.671 --> 07:50.504
'cause I don't know,

07:50.504 --> 07:55.271
but I'll tell you a story of this exact thing,

07:55.271 --> 08:00.271
except it wasn't (audio breaking) it was a founder.

08:00.900 --> 08:05.900
So, the end of 2012 and the founders of-

08:06.120 --> 08:07.020
-: Hey, sorry about that.

08:07.020 --> 08:09.780
So, we had slight technical difficulty. (laughs)

08:09.780 --> 08:11.640
-: It's okay. Do you want to ask the question again?

08:11.640 --> 08:13.290
-: No, no, we can just totally edit it in.

08:13.290 --> 08:15.390
So, you said it was the end of 2012

08:15.390 --> 08:16.590
and that's-
-: Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah,

08:16.590 --> 08:17.423
okay, all right,
(Dave drowns out interviewer)

08:17.423 --> 08:18.256
so let's keep going.

08:18.256 --> 08:19.089
Yeah, so it was the end
-: Yeah.

08:19.089 --> 08:21.180
-: of 2012 and the founders were really worried

08:21.180 --> 08:22.350
that the business model

08:22.350 --> 08:25.170
was looking a lot like University of Phoenix

08:25.170 --> 08:28.360
in the sense that our revenue came from

08:30.300 --> 08:34.410
adults coming to a classroom and learning.

08:34.410 --> 08:36.930
And so, revenue was really constrained

08:36.930 --> 08:41.820
by one, our ability to purchase or rent real estate.

08:41.820 --> 08:42.653
And then, secondly,

08:42.653 --> 08:45.810
our sales ability to fill that real estate.

08:45.810 --> 08:47.430
And so, they came to me and they said,

08:47.430 --> 08:51.143
we really need something that is a subscription

08:51.143 --> 08:53.970
and can target customers

08:53.970 --> 08:56.883
who don't live where we have classrooms.

08:57.870 --> 09:00.213
And you have three months, go build it.

09:02.218 --> 09:06.210
And so, this is like, this was a big mistake for me,

09:06.210 --> 09:07.680
because as a product manager,

09:07.680 --> 09:10.080
I should have recognized immediately

09:10.080 --> 09:12.810
that there was no mention of the customer here

09:12.810 --> 09:14.790
or any sort of customer problem.

09:14.790 --> 09:17.223
This was something that came out of a spreadsheet

09:17.223 --> 09:21.210
and in an analysis of market valuations

09:21.210 --> 09:23.820
and what it would be really nice if this cell

09:23.820 --> 09:26.133
in our Excel spreadsheet was twice as big.

09:27.158 --> 09:30.240
And in some cases, it is up to the product manager

09:30.240 --> 09:31.620
to translate that into, okay,

09:31.620 --> 09:33.390
well then, I'm gonna go do some music research

09:33.390 --> 09:37.440
and discover what a customer problem is,

09:37.440 --> 09:39.450
and then work towards product market fit.

09:39.450 --> 09:42.570
But given the three-month timeline on top of that,

09:42.570 --> 09:45.961
we felt really pressured to just look around

09:45.961 --> 09:48.330
to see what assets we had available

09:48.330 --> 09:49.620
and what we could package together.

09:49.620 --> 09:52.872
And so, we did happen to have a library

09:52.872 --> 09:56.250
of live streams that we had recorded and stored away.

09:56.250 --> 09:58.530
And so, we said, okay, how about for 25 bucks a month,

09:58.530 --> 10:01.546
