16:23.490 --> 16:27.360
Hey guys at home, so if you guys wanna send us questions

16:27.360 --> 16:28.800
feel free to.

16:28.800 --> 16:32.700
You can post your questions in the group discussion or what

16:32.700 --> 16:35.100
you can do, if you want us to actually feature the question

16:35.100 --> 16:38.310
send us a video of you asking a question.

16:38.310 --> 16:40.560
And that's what we're gonna go through right now.

16:40.560 --> 16:42.390
All you gotta do is just take your phone

16:42.390 --> 16:44.280
point it at your face and ask us a question

16:44.280 --> 16:46.503
and we will put it up here and address it.

16:47.400 --> 16:48.660
So if you guys wanna do that

16:48.660 --> 16:50.820
just private message one of us

16:50.820 --> 16:52.890
and we'll set it up.

16:52.890 --> 16:54.450
All right, so we got a question

16:54.450 --> 16:56.553
from one of our students here.

16:57.990 --> 17:01.530
-: Hey guys, I'm Jack and I've got a question for you.

17:01.530 --> 17:04.440
From a product management standpoint

17:04.440 --> 17:06.453
what do you think about Twitter?

17:07.560 --> 17:10.290
I like the activity. I never realized just

17:10.290 --> 17:13.530
how much junk there actually is on Twitter

17:13.530 --> 17:15.930
that possibly no one ever uses, you know

17:15.930 --> 17:19.080
all these people contacting you and they just want to

17:19.080 --> 17:22.170
sell things that are a bit sordid that you don't wanna see.

17:22.170 --> 17:23.610
What do you think is going on there?

17:23.610 --> 17:27.855
Maybe the team doesn't agree on anything, you know?

17:27.855 --> 17:29.280
I don't know, but

17:29.280 --> 17:31.630
I'd love to hear your thoughts on that, please.

17:32.520 --> 17:33.353
-: All right, cool.

17:33.353 --> 17:34.910
So we got a Twitter hater. What's up?

17:35.820 --> 17:39.630
-: All right, so Twitter is an interesting company.

17:39.630 --> 17:40.890
I talked about 'em a little bit earlier

17:40.890 --> 17:44.880
but for those of you who don't know where they've started

17:44.880 --> 17:47.670
they were, I believe they were even

17:47.670 --> 17:48.870
they were a different company first

17:48.870 --> 17:50.520
and they pivoted into Twitter.

17:50.520 --> 17:55.520
-: It was Odeo. Yeah, they were a podcast listing service.

17:55.980 --> 17:58.470
-: Yeah. So then actually they became a

17:58.470 --> 18:00.930
like the very first iteration of Twitter itself was like a

18:00.930 --> 18:04.710
text message update thing where you could basically text

18:04.710 --> 18:08.190
message a number and it would set your Twitter status

18:08.190 --> 18:10.560
right? And so there was no web interface

18:10.560 --> 18:12.480
it was just over text message.

18:12.480 --> 18:15.060
-: Was it just for keeping groups of friends updated

18:15.060 --> 18:16.620
with what you're doing?

18:16.620 --> 18:18.180
-: Yeah, exactly.

18:18.180 --> 18:19.860
It was that sort of thing and it then expanded

18:19.860 --> 18:22.980
pretty rapidly, you know, from there.

18:22.980 --> 18:24.960
But, they've come a long way.

18:24.960 --> 18:27.570
But not to be too lengthy with this answer

18:27.570 --> 18:30.210
'cause it could go on forever, obviously but

18:30.210 --> 18:35.130
they have a very useful tool that has turned into

18:35.130 --> 18:37.320
basically one of the most instantaneous sources

18:37.320 --> 18:40.620
of information on the planet basically

18:40.620 --> 18:45.620
and very useful for all sorts of stuff from business to

18:46.350 --> 18:49.140
politics and activism and this sort of thing.

18:49.140 --> 18:51.420
Really, really cool.

18:51.420 --> 18:53.850
But the thing that they have been struggling with

18:53.850 --> 18:56.190
and you might have seen the news in the last couple years

18:56.190 --> 19:00.180
where their user numbers are plateauing and people

19:00.180 --> 19:03.180
aren't using it as much. And that is asymptomatic

19:03.180 --> 19:06.630
of them saturating this market that I already talked about

19:06.630 --> 19:10.020
which is like the really active users

19:10.020 --> 19:14.850
and they need to become more accessible for casual users.

19:14.850 --> 19:16.650
And so they're doing a couple things.

19:16.650 --> 19:18.194
Well first of all

19:18.194 --> 19:22.440
they had their CEO removed, Dick Costello

19:22.440 --> 19:25.290
and then Jack came back.

19:25.290 --> 19:28.183
Jack, one of the original founders, came back.

19:28.183 --> 19:30.052
-: And he came back with the beard.

19:30.052 --> 19:31.423
The beard is back.

19:31.423 --> 19:32.490
What?

19:32.490 --> 19:33.750
Did you see that?

19:33.750 --> 19:37.200
When he came back he just has like a lumberjack beard.

19:37.200 --> 19:38.940
But anyway, what you were saying.

19:38.940 --> 19:41.220
-: Yeah, so Jack came back

19:41.220 --> 19:43.930
and trying to instill some product change and

19:45.300 --> 19:47.750
kind of bringing it back to its roots and make it

19:49.050 --> 19:51.630
more accessible for casual users.

19:51.630 --> 19:54.270
And so they've implemented these features like moments

19:54.270 --> 19:56.880
and they've implemented better direct messaging

19:56.880 --> 20:00.480
and character limits are removed in direct messaging

20:00.480 --> 20:02.067
and they've added, you know, photos and GIFs

20:02.067 --> 20:04.440
and direct messaging and the most recent

20:04.440 --> 20:09.440
controversial change is the ranking of the Twitter timeline

20:09.510 --> 20:10.800
and they're doing it slowly

20:10.800 --> 20:13.350
but this is the same thing that Facebook did and they did it

20:13.350 --> 20:16.110
quite early and they've done a good job of it 'cause they've

20:16.110 --> 20:17.070
done it for so long.

20:17.070 --> 20:19.140
But I don't know if you remember

20:19.140 --> 20:20.310
if you've been on Facebook this long

20:20.310 --> 20:22.680
but there was a time when people were really scared

20:22.680 --> 20:25.980
of Facebook doing ranking to the newsfeed.

20:25.980 --> 20:27.663
In fact, there were people angry about the newsfeed

20:27.663 --> 20:29.430
when it first came out too.

20:29.430 --> 20:31.500
So you're always gonna have your core users that are upset

20:31.500 --> 20:34.200
about changes, but if you do it correctly

20:34.200 --> 20:37.350
you handle it correctly, then they end up saying

20:37.350 --> 20:38.520
oh, well it's not so bad.

20:38.520 --> 20:40.560
Instagram is actually doing the same thing now.

20:40.560 --> 20:44.340
I mean it's getting so popular and so hard to use for casual

20:44.340 --> 20:48.300
people that once you start following tons of people

20:48.300 --> 20:50.190
it's like almost impossible to keep up with it.

20:50.190 --> 20:53.460
So they're trying to show you the best content first

20:53.460 --> 20:57.060
and Twitter's starting to do this and we'll see how it goes.

20:57.060 --> 21:00.750
But overall, I think they have a storied history

21:00.750 --> 21:02.490
a little bit of difficulty

21:02.490 --> 21:05.670
some rocky points there with the executives

21:05.670 --> 21:07.020
and the product management

21:07.020 --> 21:10.503
and now they're kind of trying to get it back in shape.

21:12.000 --> 21:12.833
-: Yeah.

21:12.833 --> 21:15.210
I mean I didn't even know that about the ranking on Twitter.

21:15.210 --> 21:17.883
I don't use Twitter very often at all.

21:19.020 --> 21:20.640
Do they rank it visually?

21:20.640 --> 21:23.670
Like you can see that there's some marker that says it's the

21:23.670 --> 21:26.160
top or you just mean organically

21:26.160 --> 21:28.833
like the top ones just float to the top?

21:28.833 --> 21:30.630
-: It's, yeah, there's no visual.

21:30.630 --> 21:32.970
It's just the same as Facebook so, right.

21:32.970 --> 21:35.220
You know, and it's not even fully implemented.

21:35.220 --> 21:37.350
They're testing it in a lot of areas.

21:37.350 --> 21:39.780
In fact you had to opt in to it at first

21:39.780 --> 21:44.310
but instead of just saying literally the top of your screen

21:44.310 --> 21:47.760
is the last person that tweeted that you follow, now it's

21:47.760 --> 21:52.200
gonna be okay, if I'm gone for a week and I log into Twitter

21:52.200 --> 21:53.200
seven days later

21:54.480 --> 21:57.180
I'm gonna have to scroll back a long way to see who tweeted

21:57.180 --> 21:59.730
something five days ago if I wasn't online.

21:59.730 --> 22:02.310
And that's the kind of friction that casual users are

22:02.310 --> 22:03.150
not really into.

22:03.150 --> 22:05.250
They don't wanna have to be on it every single day.

22:05.250 --> 22:06.810
So what they're doing is saying

22:06.810 --> 22:10.080
oh well, when you log in in seven days

22:10.080 --> 22:12.270
we'll show you this one tweet that was really great

22:12.270 --> 22:14.280
that you missed from five days ago.

22:14.280 --> 22:16.290
We know you're interested in it because you've clicked on

22:16.290 --> 22:17.850
this person's stuff a lot

22:17.850 --> 22:19.620
and you've talked to them through direct message.

22:19.620 --> 22:21.090
And so they rank all of these things

22:21.090 --> 22:22.410
just like Facebook does.

22:22.410 --> 22:24.798
They'll show that to you first so you can feel like you've

22:24.798 --> 22:27.840
not missed out even though you haven't been logging in

22:27.840 --> 22:29.943
you know, every hour for every day.

22:31.170 --> 22:34.500
-: Yeah. So you think that's a good product decision?

22:34.500 --> 22:36.180
-: Yeah, it's an interesting one.

22:36.180 --> 22:38.330
They could do some other things as well but

22:39.690 --> 22:41.100
they have to go that path.

22:41.100 --> 22:44.550
But, the thing here is that you can't

22:44.550 --> 22:48.300
anger the people who want a chronological feed

22:48.300 --> 22:53.300
or instant access to a pure chronological stream of stuff

22:54.000 --> 22:55.380
because some people, you know

22:55.380 --> 22:58.740
news agencies or whoever, they want to get the very latest

22:58.740 --> 23:01.470
and they don't want algorithms like sort of messing that up.

23:01.470 --> 23:03.450
So you always have to be careful with your users in this

23:03.450 --> 23:07.890
sense and either give them that in some other way or

23:07.890 --> 23:09.450
or just, you know

23:09.450 --> 23:11.100
enable them to turn it off or something.

23:11.100 --> 23:13.170
So, you do gotta be careful.

23:13.170 --> 23:14.642
-: Yeah. That's interesting, 'cause it does kind of

23:14.642 --> 23:17.610
grate against the ethos of Twitter

23:17.610 --> 23:19.470
or at least how they came up. Right?

23:19.470 --> 23:21.696
'Cause a lot of people think of it as

23:21.696 --> 23:23.640
democratization.

23:23.640 --> 23:26.070
Any little person, even if you have a hundred followers

23:26.070 --> 23:28.710
has the chance to tweet something that someone can see.

23:28.710 --> 23:31.650
Right? That's why it's so good for social movement.

23:31.650 --> 23:34.380
Tons and tons of people at the bottom saying something

23:34.380 --> 23:35.463
will get attention.

23:36.660 --> 23:37.950
Yeah. So that's interesting.

23:37.950 --> 23:40.560
All right, well, okay, so that wraps it up guys.

23:40.560 --> 23:43.350
If you have questions, shoot us a message.

23:43.350 --> 23:46.710
If you have any feedback or comments on doing these QAs

23:46.710 --> 23:50.670
let us know. Next Saturday we're gonna do section three.

23:50.670 --> 23:54.000
So make sure by then you guys send us what you want

23:54.000 --> 23:55.800
or what you wanna discuss.

23:55.800 --> 23:57.060
-: All right everybody, thanks a bunch.

23:57.060 --> 23:58.160
We'll catch you later.
